topics.title: RC Differentiating and Integrating Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** RC Differentiating and Integrating Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**RC Differentiating and Integrating Circuits** are first-order linear passive networks formed by a single resistor and capacitor that, under specific time-constant conditions, perform the mathematical operations of differentiation and integration on an input time-varying signal. These circuits form the **foundational building blocks** of analog signal processing, waveform shaping, and pulse-conditioning systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly includes the study of linear wave-shaping circuits using RC networks, emphasizing their response to **standard non-sinusoidal inputs** such as **step, pulse, square, and ramp waveforms**. Mastery of time-domain and frequency-domain analysis is mandatory.

### 1.1.1 The Two Canonical Forms

| Circuit Type | Topology | Output Taken Across | Mathematical Operation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RC Differentiator** (High-Pass) | Series R–C | Resistor (R) | $v_o(t) \propto \dfrac{d v_{in}(t)}{dt}$ |
| **RC Integrator** (Low-Pass) | Series R–C | Capacitor (C) | $v_o(t) \propto \int v_{in}(t)\, dt$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Both circuits use the **exact same series R-C topology** — the only difference is **where the output is measured**. The resistor-output configuration differentiates; the capacitor-output configuration integrates.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

### 1.2.1 The Differentiator — "The Speed Camera Analogy"

Imagine a **speed camera on a highway**. It does not care where a car is; it only cares about **how fast the car is changing its position** — i.e., its rate of change. If a car maintains a steady 60 km/h, the camera records no event. But the moment a car **accelerates or brakes** sharply, the camera captures a "spike" of activity.

Similarly, an **RC Differentiator**:
- Ignores the **DC level** (steady speed) of the input.
- Produces a **spike (output) only when the input changes rapidly** (i.e., has a sudden edge).
- A square wave (alternating constant-high and constant-low segments with sharp edges) produces **sharp positive and negative spikes** at every transition.

### 1.2.2 The Integrator — "The Water Bucket Analogy"

Consider a **water bucket collecting rainwater**. The water level in the bucket does not represent the **instantaneous rainfall rate**; rather, it represents the **cumulative total** of all rain that has fallen over time. A steady drizzle raises the level slowly; a heavy downpour raises it fast.

Similarly, an **RC Integrator**:
- Accumulates the **area under the input waveform** over time.
- A constant DC input produces a **linear ramp** (rising water level).
- A square wave input (alternating positive and negative constant levels) produces a **triangular waveform** (water rising then falling).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Time constant of an RC network:** $\tau = R \cdot C$ with units of **seconds**.
> - For a **differentiator** to behave ideally: $\tau \ll T$, where $T$ is the time period of the input. A common rule of thumb is $\tau \le \dfrac{T}{10}$.
> - For an **integrator** to behave ideally: $\tau \gg T$, with a common rule of thumb being $\tau \ge 10\,T$.

Where:
- $\tau$ = time constant in **seconds (s)**
- $R$ = resistance in **ohms ($\Omega$)**
- $C$ = capacitance in **farads (F)**
- $T$ = period of input waveform in **seconds (s)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Operation — Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.1.1 RC Differentiator (High-Pass Filter)

**Topology:** Input $v_{in}$ is applied across the **series combination** of $R$ and $C$. Output $v_o$ is taken across $R$.

**KVL Equation (Time Domain):**
$$v_{in}(t) = i(t) \cdot R + v_C(t)$$

Where the capacitor voltage is:
$$v_C(t) = \frac{1}{C} \int i(t)\, dt$$

**Current through the series branch:**
$$i(t) = C \cdot \frac{d v_C(t)}{dt}$$

Substituting into the KVL equation:
$$v_{in}(t) = R \cdot C \cdot \frac{d v_C(t)}{dt} + v_C(t)$$

**Frequency Domain (s-plane) Analysis:**
Using the voltage divider rule, the transfer function is:
$$H(s) = \frac{V_o(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{R}{R + \frac{1}{sC}} = \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}$$

**Steady-State Sinusoidal (Phasor) Analysis:**
$$H(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

The magnitude response:
$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

The phase response:
$$\angle H(j\omega) = 90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$$

**Key Condition — Differentiator Behavior:**

When $\omega RC \ll 1$ (i.e., $\tau \ll T$), the magnitude of the term $(\omega RC)^2$ becomes negligible compared to 1:
$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert \approx \frac{\omega RC}{1} = \omega RC$$

And the output becomes:
$$V_o(j\omega) \approx j\omega RC \cdot V_{in}(j\omega)$$

Converting back to the time domain (since $j\omega$ corresponds to differentiation):
$$v_o(t) \approx RC \cdot \frac{d v_{in}(t)}{dt}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **mathematical proof** that the circuit acts as a **differentiator** under the small-$\tau$ condition. The output is proportional to the **time derivative** of the input, scaled by the time constant $RC$.

---

### 2.1.2 RC Integrator (Low-Pass Filter)

**Topology:** Input $v_{in}$ is applied across the **series combination** of $R$ and $C$. Output $v_o$ is taken across $C$.

**Transfer Function:**
$$H(s) = \frac{V_o(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{\frac{1}{sC}}{R + \frac{1}{sC}} = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$

**Steady-State Sinusoidal Analysis:**
$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

The magnitude response:
$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

**Key Condition — Integrator Behavior:**

When $\omega RC \gg 1$ (i.e., $\tau \gg T$), the term $(\omega RC)^2$ dominates the denominator:
$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert \approx \frac{1}{\omega RC}$$

And the output becomes:
$$V_o(j\omega) \approx \frac{1}{j\omega RC} \cdot V_{in}(j\omega)$$

Converting back to the time domain (since $\frac{1}{j\omega}$ corresponds to integration):
$$v_o(t) \approx \frac{1}{RC} \int v_{in}(t)\, dt$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Under the large-$\tau$ condition, the circuit acts as a **pure integrator** (technically, an **inverting integrator** in this passive form because the sign convention depends on the initial conditions and the direction of integration limits).

---

## 2.2 The Critical Boundary: From Differentiator to Integrator

The single transfer function for the **capacitor output** (integrator) and the **resistor output** (differentiator) are related by the complement rule:
$$H_{diff}(s) + H_{int}(s) = 1$$

This is because the **sum of voltage across R and C equals the input**, so the two outputs always add to $v_{in}$.

The **cutoff frequency** (or **corner frequency**) is:
$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}, \quad \omega_c = \frac{1}{RC}$$

| Frequency Regime | Differentiator (R-output) | Integrator (C-output) |
|---|---|---|
| $f \ll f_c$ ($\omega RC \ll 1$) | Attenuates ($-20\,\text{dB/dec}$) | Passes (flat, $\approx 0\,\text{dB}$) |
| $f = f_c$ | $-3\,\text{dB}$ point | $-3\,\text{dB}$ point |
| $f \gg f_c$ ($\omega RC \gg 1$) | Passes (flat, $\approx 0\,\text{dB}$) | Attenuates ($-20\,\text{dB/dec}$) |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where are these circuits used in modern engineering?**
> 1. **Pulse Generators:** Converting square waves to trigger pulses (differentiation).
> 2. **Ramp Generators:** Converting square waves to triangular waves in function generators and PWM controllers (integration).
> 3. **Edge Detectors in Digital Systems:** Detecting the rising and falling edges of clock signals.
> 4. **Sample-and-Hold Circuits:** The RC integrator forms the fundamental hold stage in ADC front-ends.
> 5. **Filter Design:** First-order high-pass and low-pass filter realizations for audio and instrumentation.
> 6. **Time-Delay and Phase-Shift Networks:** Used in oscillators (e.g., RC phase-shift oscillators) and feedback compensation.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU Module 1 problem.**

| # | Quantity / Relationship | Formula | Condition / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Time constant of RC network | $\tau = RC$ | Seconds (s) |
| 2 | Differentiator transfer function | $H(s) = \dfrac{sRC}{1 + sRC}$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Integrator transfer function | $H(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + sRC}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Differentiator condition | $\tau \le T/10$ | i.e., $\omega RC \le 0.1$ |
| 5 | Integrator condition | $\tau \ge 10\,T$ | i.e., $\omega RC \ge 10$ |
| 6 | Differentiator output (time domain) | $v_o(t) \approx RC\,\dfrac{dv_{in}}{dt}$ | Volts (V) |
| 7 | Integrator output (time domain) | $v_o(t) \approx \dfrac{1}{RC}\int v_{in}\,dt$ | Volts (V) |
| 8 | Corner frequency | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| 9 | Differentiator square-wave peak | $V_{peak} = V_m$ (full input swing) | Volts (V) |
| 10 | Integrator square-wave peak (at half-period) | $V_{peak} = \dfrac{V_m \cdot T}{2RC}$ | Volts (V) |
| 11 | Capacitor charge equation | $v_C(t) = V_{\infty} + (V_0 - V_{\infty})\,e^{-t/\tau}$ | Volts (V) |
| 12 | Differentiator spike decay | $v_o(t) = V_m\,e^{-t/\tau}$ | Volts (V) |
| 13 | Differentiator frequency response | $\vert H \vert = \dfrac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1+(\omega RC)^2}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 14 | Integrator frequency response | $\vert H \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1+(\omega RC)^2}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 15 | Slope of integrator ramp output | $\dfrac{dv_o}{dt} = \dfrac{V_m}{RC}$ | Volts/sec (V/s) |

---

## 2.5 Response to a Square Wave — The KTU Classic Problem

### 2.5.1 Differentiator Response to a Symmetric Square Wave

When a square wave of amplitude $V_m$ and period $T$ is applied:

- **During the positive half-cycle** ($v_{in} = +V_m$): Capacitor charges through $R$. Output is $v_o(t) = V_m \, e^{-t/\tau}$.
- **At the transition** ($t = T/2$): Input abruptly switches to $-V_m$. The capacitor voltage cannot change instantaneously, so the output jumps to $-V_m$ (since the voltage drop across $R$ reverses).
- **During the negative half-cycle** ($v_{in} = -V_m$): Output decays from $-V_m$ toward zero as $v_o(t) = -V_m\, e^{-t/\tau}$.

**Result:** A series of **sharp positive and negative spikes** separated by flat (zero) regions, with exponential decay tails.

### 2.5.2 Integrator Response to a Symmetric Square Wave

- **During the positive half-cycle** ($v_{in} = +V_m$): Capacitor charges linearly (in approximation). $v_o(t) = \dfrac{V_m}{RC} t$ (linear ramp from 0 to $\frac{V_m T}{2RC}$ at $t = T/2$).
- **At the transition** ($t = T/2$): Input switches to $-V_m$. The ramp reverses direction.
- **During the negative half-cycle:** $v_o(t)$ ramps downward symmetrically.

**Result:** A **triangular waveform** with peak amplitude $V_{peak} = \dfrac{V_m\, T}{2RC}$ and zero average value.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Differentiator Output for a Pulse Input

**Problem Setup:** An RC differentiator has $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$. A rectangular pulse of amplitude $V_m = 5\,\text{V}$ and width $T_p = 50\,\mu\text{s}$ is applied at $t = 0$. The pulse returns to 0 V at $t = T_p$. Assume the capacitor is initially uncharged.

**Step 1 — Compute the time constant.**
$$\tau = R \cdot C = (10 \times 10^3\,\Omega)(0.1 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{F})$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= 10^4 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{s} \\
\tau &= 10^{-3}\,\text{s} \\
\tau &= 1\,\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Check the differentiator condition.**

We need $\tau \le T_p / 10$:
$$\frac{T_p}{10} = \frac{50\,\mu\text{s}}{10} = 5\,\mu\text{s}$$

Since $\tau = 1\,\text{ms} = 1000\,\mu\text{s} \gg 5\,\mu\text{s}$, the **differentiator condition is NOT satisfied**. This is actually an **integrator** condition. We will still solve the problem, but classify the response accordingly.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** KTU problems often give parameters that violate the ideal condition to test whether students can identify the *actual* circuit behavior. Always verify the condition first!

**Step 3 — Solve using the general capacitor charging equation.**

For a step input from 0 to $V_m$ at $t = 0$, the capacitor voltage (and thus the output for an integrator) is:
$$v_o(t) = V_m\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right), \quad 0 \le t \le T_p$$

**Step 4 — Compute output at key instants.**

At $t = T_p = 50\,\mu\text{s}$ (end of pulse), with $\tau = 1000\,\mu\text{s}$:
$$v_o(T_p) = 5\left(1 - e^{-50/1000}\right) = 5\left(1 - e^{-0.05}\right)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{-0.05} &\approx 0.9512 \\
v_o(T_p) &= 5(1 - 0.9512) = 5(0.0488) \\
v_o(T_p) &\approx 0.244\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **integrator output** at the end of the pulse (a small ramp-up because the pulse is short compared to $\tau$).

**Step 5 — What does the differentiator output look like?**

For the differentiator (output across R), $v_o(t) = v_{in}(t) - v_C(t)$:
$$v_o(t) = V_m \cdot e^{-t/\tau}, \quad 0 \le t \le T_p$$

At $t = 0^+$ (just after pulse starts):
$$v_o(0^+) = V_m = 5\,\text{V}$$

At $t = T_p$ (end of pulse):
$$v_o(T_p) = 5 \cdot e^{-0.05} \approx 4.756\,\text{V}$$

**Step 6 — Response after the pulse ends ($t > T_p$).**

When the pulse returns to 0 V at $t = T_p$, the capacitor voltage (which is now $0.244\,\text{V}$) must appear across R with opposite polarity:
$$v_o(T_p^+) = 0 - v_C(T_p) = -0.244\,\text{V}$$

The output then decays toward zero:
$$v_o(t) = -0.244 \cdot e^{-(t - T_p)/\tau}, \quad t > T_p$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Integrator Triangular Output Amplitude

**Problem Setup:** An RC integrator has $R = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 1\,\mu\text{F}$. A symmetric square wave with amplitude $V_m = 10\,\text{V}$ and period $T = 20\,\text{ms}$ is applied.

**Step 1 — Compute the time constant.**
$$\tau = RC = (10^5\,\Omega)(10^{-6}\,\text{F}) = 0.1\,\text{s} = 100\,\text{ms}$$

**Step 2 — Check the integrator condition.**

We need $\tau \ge 10\,T$:
$$10\,T = 10 \times 20\,\text{ms} = 200\,\text{ms}$$

Since $\tau = 100\,\text{ms} < 200\,\text{ms}$, the **ideal integrator condition is not strictly met**, but it is still much larger than $T$ (5 times), so the **triangular approximation is acceptable** for the first half-cycle.

**Step 3 — Output during the positive half-cycle ($0 \le t \le T/2 = 10\,\text{ms}$).**

The capacitor charges from 0 V toward $V_m$ with time constant $\tau$:
$$v_o(t) = V_m\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) = 10\left(1 - e^{-t/0.1}\right)$$

At $t = T/2 = 0.01\,\text{s}$:
$$v_o(T/2) = 10\left(1 - e^{-0.01/0.1}\right) = 10\left(1 - e^{-0.1}\right)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{-0.1} &\approx 0.9048 \\
v_o(T/2) &= 10(1 - 0.9048) = 10(0.0952) \\
v_o(T/2) &\approx 0.952\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Slope-based (ideal) approximation for comparison.**

Using the small-signal linear approximation $v_o(t) \approx \dfrac{V_m}{RC} t$ for $t \ll \tau$:
$$v_o(T/2) \approx \frac{V_m \cdot T/2}{RC} = \frac{10 \times 0.01}{0.1} = 1.0\,\text{V}$$

The exact value (0.952 V) and the linear approximation (1.0 V) agree within $\approx 5\%$, confirming the triangular behavior.

**Step 5 — Steady-state triangular wave amplitude.**

In steady state, the triangular wave is symmetric about zero, with peak amplitude:
$$V_{peak} = \frac{V_m \cdot T}{2RC} = \frac{10 \times 0.02}{2 \times 0.1} = 1.0\,\text{V}$$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: The Frequency-Domain Differentiator Proof

**Goal:** Show that the differentiator output equation $v_o(t) = RC\,\dfrac{dv_{in}}{dt}$ follows directly from the transfer function when $\omega RC \ll 1$.

**Step 1 — Start with the exact transfer function for the differentiator (R-output).**
$$H(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 2 — Express as magnitude and phase.**
$$H(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC} = \frac{(\omega RC)\,e^{j 90^\circ}}{\sqrt{1+(\omega RC)^2}\; e^{j\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the small-argument approximation.**

When $\omega RC \ll 1$:
- The denominator $\sqrt{1+(\omega RC)^2} \approx \sqrt{1+0} = 1$
- The phase $\tan^{-1}(\omega RC) \approx \omega RC \approx 0$, so the denominator's phase term becomes $e^{j \cdot 0} \approx 1$

Therefore:
$$H(j\omega) \approx j\omega RC \cdot 1 = j\omega RC$$

**Step 4 — Write the output phasor.**
$$V_o(j\omega) = H(j\omega) \cdot V_{in}(j\omega) = j\omega RC \cdot V_{in}(j\omega)$$

**Step 5 — Convert from frequency domain to time domain.**

In the time domain, multiplication by $j\omega$ corresponds to the time derivative operator $\dfrac{d}{dt}$:
$$v_o(t) = RC \cdot \frac{d v_{in}(t)}{dt}$$

**Conclusion:** This rigorously proves that the RC circuit with output across R behaves as a differentiator in the small-$\tau$ regime.

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation 4: The Frequency-Domain Integrator Proof

**Goal:** Show that $v_o(t) = \dfrac{1}{RC}\int v_{in}(t)\,dt$ when $\omega RC \gg 1$.

**Step 1 — Start with the exact transfer function for the integrator (C-output).**
$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the large-argument approximation.**

When $\omega RC \gg 1$:
$$H(j\omega) \approx \frac{1}{j\omega RC}$$

**Step 3 — Write the output phasor.**
$$V_o(j\omega) = \frac{1}{j\omega RC} \cdot V_{in}(j\omega)$$

**Step 4 — Convert to the time domain.**

Division by $j\omega$ corresponds to the time integration operator:
$$v_o(t) = \frac{1}{RC} \int v_{in}(t)\, dt$$

**Conclusion:** The RC circuit with output across C behaves as an integrator when $\omega RC \gg 1$.

---

## 3.5 Summary of Switching Conditions

| Parameter | Differentiator | Integrator |
|---|---|---|
| Time constant $\tau = RC$ | **Small** ($\tau \ll T$) | **Large** ($\tau \gg T$) |
| Frequency regime | $\omega RC \ll 1$ | $\omega RC \gg 1$ |
| Filter type | High-pass | Low-pass |
| Output node | Across R | Across C |
| Square-wave output | Sharp spikes (+/-) | Triangular wave |
| Step input response | Spike that decays to 0 | Exponential approach to $V_m$ |
| Pulse input response | Spike at leading edge + spike at trailing edge | Rises during pulse, falls after |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RC Differentiator Circuit Topology (R-Output)

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputNode[Vin: Input Voltage Source] --> Rseries[R: Resistor]
    Rseries --> nodeA[Node A: Vc measured here]
    nodeA --> Cseries[C: Capacitor]
    Cseries --> groundNode[Ground: 0V Reference]
    nodeA --> outputDiff[Vout across R: Differentiator Output]
    Rseries -. output taken across R .-> outputDiff
    style inputNode fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Rseries fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Cseries fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style outputDiff fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 1 — Differentiator Topology:** Input is applied across the series R-C combination. The output $v_o$ is measured across the resistor R. The output is **proportional to the time derivative** of the input when $\tau \ll T$.

---

## 4.2 RC Integrator Circuit Topology (C-Output)

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputNode2[Vin: Input Voltage Source] --> Rseries2[R: Resistor]
    Rseries2 --> nodeB[Node B: Vout measured here]
    nodeB --> Cseries2[C: Capacitor]
    Cseries2 --> groundNode2[Ground: 0V Reference]
    nodeB --> outputInt[Vout across C: Integrator Output]
    Cseries2 -. output taken across C .-> outputInt
    style inputNode2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Rseries2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Cseries2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style outputInt fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeB fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 2 — Integrator Topology:** Identical R-C series combination, but the output $v_o$ is now measured across the capacitor C. The output is **proportional to the time integral** of the input when $\tau \gg T$.

---

## 4.3 Signal Flow: Block-Level Functional Architecture

This block diagram shows the **complete signal-flow architecture** of how an input waveform is transformed by both circuits, including the conditions and the output waveforms for a square-wave input.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Waveform: Square Wave Vin = Vm] --> B{Choose Circuit}
    B -->|Output across R| C[RC Differentiator]
    B -->|Output across C| D[RC Integrator]
    C --> E{Condition Check: tau is small, tau T/10}
    D --> F{Condition Check: tau is large, tau 10T}
    E -->|Satisfied| G[Output: Sharp Positive and Negative Spikes]
    E -->|Not Satisfied| H[Output: Distorted Exponential Edges]
    F -->|Satisfied| I[Output: Pure Triangular Waveform]
    F -->|Not Satisfied| J[Output: Rounded Exponential Trapezoid]
    G --> K[Final Waveform Shape: Spike Train]
    I --> L[Final Waveform Shape: Triangle]
    H --> M[Final Waveform Shape: Clipped Exponential]
    J --> N[Final Waveform Shape: Partial Triangle]
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#F08080,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#F08080,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Figure 3 — Decision Flow for Wave Shaping:** KTU problems often test the student's ability to predict the *output shape* based on the relative magnitudes of $\tau$ and $T$. The **condition check** is the single most important step — always verify it first.

---

## 4.4 Asymptotic Bode-Magnitude Sketch (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    lowF[Low Frequency: omega RC less than 1] --> corner[Corner Frequency: fc equals 1 over 2 pi RC]
    corner --> highF[High Frequency: omega RC greater than 1]
    lowF -->|Differentiator| diffLow[Differentiator Attenuates: Negative 20 dB per decade slope]
    highF -->|Differentiator| diffHigh[Differentiator Passes: Flat 0 dB response]
    lowF -->|Integrator| intLow[Integrator Passes: Flat 0 dB response]
    highF -->|Integrator| intHigh[Integrator Attenuates: Negative 20 dB per decade slope]
    style lowF fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style corner fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style highF fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style diffLow fill:#F08080,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style diffHigh fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style intLow fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style intHigh fill:#F08080,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!TIP]
> **Figure 4 — Complementary Frequency Behavior:** Notice that the differentiator and integrator have **mirror-image** frequency responses around the corner frequency. This is a direct consequence of the fact that $H_{diff}(s) + H_{int}(s) = 1$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q:** Define the time constant of an RC circuit. State the condition for an RC circuit to act as a good differentiator.

**Model Answer:**

The **time constant** $\tau$ of an RC circuit is defined as the time required for the capacitor voltage to reach **63.2%** of its final steady-state value during charging (or to decay to **36.8%** of its initial value during discharging). It is given by:
$$\tau = R \cdot C \quad \text{(in seconds)}$$

**Condition for a good differentiator:** The time constant must be very small compared to the time period of the input signal:
$$\tau \ll T \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \omega RC \ll 1 \quad \text{or} \quad RC \le \frac{T}{10}$$

Under this condition, the output (taken across R) is proportional to the derivative of the input.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Q:** Draw the circuit of an RC integrator and explain its operation with a square wave input.

**Model Answer:**

**Circuit:** A series combination of R and C with the input applied across the series combination and the **output taken across the capacitor C**.

**Operation with a square wave input of amplitude $V_m$ and period $T$:**

1. **During the positive half-cycle** ($v_{in} = +V_m$, for duration $T/2$): The capacitor charges through R. The voltage across C rises **approximately linearly** with slope $\dfrac{V_m}{RC}$, reaching a peak value of $\dfrac{V_m T}{2RC}$ at $t = T/2$.

2. **At $t = T/2$:** The input switches abruptly to $-V_m$.

3. **During the negative half-cycle** ($v_{in} = -V_m$): The capacitor discharges and then charges in the opposite direction, producing a **linear ramp downward**.

**Output waveform:** A **triangular wave** with peak amplitude $V_{peak} = \dfrac{V_m T}{2RC}$ and zero average value.

**Condition:** $\tau = RC \gg T$ (typically $\tau \ge 10T$).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions in Module 1 of Analog Circuits typically carry **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Module-internal choice is provided, so the student must answer either **Question A** *or* **Question B** in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

**An RC differentiator has $R = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.01\,\mu\text{F}$. A square wave of amplitude $10\,\text{V}$ and frequency $10\,\text{kHz}$ is applied.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Verify whether the circuit acts as a good differentiator. Derive the output waveform expression and sketch it.**

**Step 1 — Compute the time constant.**
$$\tau = RC = (1 \times 10^3\,\Omega)(0.01 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{F}) = 10^{-5}\,\text{s} = 10\,\mu\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the period of the input.**
$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{10 \times 10^3} = 10^{-4}\,\text{s} = 100\,\mu\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Check the differentiator condition.**

We need $\tau \le T/10$:
$$\frac{T}{10} = \frac{100\,\mu\text{s}}{10} = 10\,\mu\text{s}$$

Since $\tau = 10\,\mu\text{s} = T/10$, the **condition is exactly at the boundary** and the circuit acts as a good differentiator.

**Step 4 — Output waveform derivation.**

For the differentiator (output across R) with a square wave input switching between $+V_m$ and $-V_m$:

- For $0 \le t \le T/2$ (positive half-cycle): $v_o(t) = V_m \, e^{-t/\tau} = 10\,e^{-t/10\mu s}$ V.
- At $t = T/2 = 50\,\mu\text{s}$: $v_o(50\,\mu\text{s}) = 10\,e^{-5} \approx 10 \times 0.0067 = 0.067\,\text{V} \approx 0$ (decayed to nearly zero).
- For $T/2 \le t \le T$: $v_o(t) = -V_m \, e^{-(t-T/2)/\tau}$.
- **The output consists of sharp positive spikes (at $t = 0, T, 2T, \ldots$) and sharp negative spikes (at $t = T/2, 3T/2, \ldots$)** of amplitude $V_m = 10\,\text{V}$, with exponential decay tails that essentially vanish before the next transition.

**Step 5 — Sketch the output.**

```
Vout (V)
   10 |\                              |                              |
     | \                             |                              |
    0 |--\---------------------------|------------------------------|------> t
     |   \                           |                              |
  -10 |    \                         |                              |
        0   \____  T/2          _____ T _____   3T/2
```

**Spike train at $t = 0, T/2, T, 3T/2, \ldots$ with peak amplitude $V_m = 10\,\text{V}$.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Find the output if the same circuit is used but the input frequency is changed to $100\,\text{Hz}$. What does the circuit behave as now?**

**Step 1 — New period.**
$$T_{new} = \frac{1}{100} = 10\,\text{ms} = 10{,}000\,\mu\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Check the condition.**

Now $\tau = 10\,\mu\text{s}$ and $T_{new} = 10{,}000\,\mu\text{s}$.

The integrator condition is $\tau \ge 10\,T$:
$$10\,T_{new} = 100{,}000\,\mu\text{s}$$

Since $\tau = 10\,\mu\text{s} \ll 10\,T_{new} = 100{,}000\,\mu\text{s}$, the **differentiator condition is overwhelmingly satisfied** — the circuit remains an excellent differentiator.

**Step 3 — Output waveform expression (unchanged form, but timing changes).**

For $0 \le t \le T/2 = 5\,\text{ms}$: $v_o(t) = 10\,e^{-t/10\mu s}$.

Since $\tau = 10\,\mu\text{s}$ is now **500 times smaller** than $T/2 = 5\,\text{ms}$, the spike decays to essentially zero **in less than 0.1%** of the half-period. The output is now a **very clean spike train** with perfectly isolated positive and negative spikes and **flat zero regions** in between.

**Conclusion:** At lower frequency, the **differentiator quality improves dramatically**. The output becomes an almost ideal spike train.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake (loses 1-2 marks):** Students often think that lowering the frequency changes the circuit behavior from differentiator to integrator. **It does NOT.** Lowering the frequency *strengthens* the differentiator action because the condition $\tau \ll T$ becomes more strongly satisfied. Raising the frequency would be the way to make it an integrator.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**An RC integrator has $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 1\,\mu\text{F}$. A square wave of amplitude $5\,\text{V}$ and period $4\,\text{ms}$ is applied. Assume the capacitor is initially uncharged.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Determine the time constant. Verify the integrator condition. Find the peak amplitude of the output triangular wave in steady state.**

**Step 1 — Time constant.**
$$\tau = RC = (10^4)(10^{-6}) = 10^{-2}\,\text{s} = 10\,\text{ms}$$

**Step 2 — Period and integrator condition.**
$$T = 4\,\text{ms}, \quad 10T = 40\,\text{ms}$$

Since $\tau = 10\,\text{ms} < 40\,\text{ms} = 10T$, the **ideal integrator condition is NOT strictly satisfied**.

**Step 3 — Check the more relaxed rule.** However, $\tau / T = 10/4 = 2.5$, which is still greater than 1, so the output will be a **rounded trapezoidal** wave rather than a perfect triangle.

**Step 4 — Exact peak amplitude calculation.**

The output during the first positive half-cycle ($0 \le t \le T/2 = 2\,\text{ms}$):
$$v_o(t) = V_m(1 - e^{-t/\tau}) = 5(1 - e^{-t/0.01})$$

At $t = T/2 = 0.002\,\text{s}$:
$$v_o(T/2) = 5(1 - e^{-0.002/0.01}) = 5(1 - e^{-0.2})$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{-0.2} &\approx 0.8187 \\
v_o(T/2) &= 5(1 - 0.8187) = 5(0.1813) \\
v_o(T/2) &\approx 0.907\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Steady-state behavior.**

After several cycles, the circuit reaches **steady state** where the average value of $v_o$ becomes 0 (the capacitor neither gains nor loses net charge per cycle). The output oscillates between $+V_{peak}$ and $-V_{peak}$:
$$V_{peak} \approx 0.907\,\text{V}$$

**Step 6 — Approximate triangular peak (using the slope method).**

$$V_{peak, approx} = \frac{V_m \cdot T}{2RC} = \frac{5 \times 0.004}{2 \times 0.01} = \frac{0.02}{0.02} = 1.0\,\text{V}$$

The exact value (0.907 V) is **9.3% lower** than the ideal triangular approximation (1.0 V), confirming that the waveform is slightly curved (trapezoidal) rather than a perfect triangle.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**What modification would you make to the circuit to obtain a perfect triangular waveform? Justify with calculations.**

**Step 1 — Identify the issue.**

The current $\tau = 10\,\text{ms}$ is only $2.5 \times T$, which causes noticeable exponential curvature instead of linear ramping. To get a perfect triangle, we need $\tau \ge 10T = 40\,\text{ms}$.

**Step 2 — Possible modifications.**

**Option 1: Increase C.** Keeping $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, choose:
$$C = \frac{\tau}{R} = \frac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{10^4} = 4\,\mu\text{F}$$

**Option 2: Increase R.** Keeping $C = 1\,\mu\text{F}$, choose:
$$R = \frac{\tau}{C} = \frac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-6}} = 40\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Verify with the new time constant.**

With $\tau = 40\,\text{ms}$ and $T = 4\,\text{ms}$:
$$v_o(T/2) = 5(1 - e^{-0.002/0.04}) = 5(1 - e^{-0.05})$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{-0.05} &\approx 0.9512 \\
v_o(T/2) &= 5(0.0488) = 0.244\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hmm, this is **smaller** than before, not larger! This is because increasing $\tau$ slows the charging rate. Let me reconsider.

**Step 4 — Re-analysis (Corrected).**

For a triangular waveform, we want the **linear approximation** $v_o(t) \approx \dfrac{V_m}{RC} \cdot t$ to be valid throughout the half-period. This requires $T/2 \ll \tau$, i.e., $\tau \gg T$. The peak amplitude is:
$$V_{peak} = \frac{V_m \cdot T}{2RC} = \frac{V_m \cdot T}{2\tau}$$

**Larger $\tau$ → smaller $V_{peak}$ (but better linearity).** The triangular shape is "stretched" — smaller in amplitude but more linear in shape.

For the output to be a **good triangular wave** (linear ramps) with a **reasonable amplitude**, we choose $\tau = 10\,T = 40\,\text{ms}$. The new peak amplitude is:
$$V_{peak} = \frac{5 \times 0.004}{2 \times 0.04} = \frac{0.02}{0.08} = 0.25\,\text{V}$$

This is a clean, low-amplitude, highly linear triangular wave — exactly what is required in many instrumentation and signal-processing applications.

**Conclusion:** To convert the trapezoidal output into a perfect triangle, **increase the time constant** (by either raising R or C) so that $\tau \ge 10T$. The amplitude of the triangle will decrease, but its linearity will improve substantially.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in RC Differentiator/Integrator problems (Module 1):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to verify the condition first ($-2$ marks):** Many students jump straight to writing $v_o = RC\, dv_{in}/dt$ without checking if $\tau \ll T$ or $\tau \gg T$. Always verify the **differentiator/integrator condition** at the start of the problem.
>
> 2. **Confusing the output node ($-2$ marks):** Students sometimes write the differentiator formula for an integrator output (or vice versa). **Memory aid:** Differentiator → "R" for "Rate of change" → output across **R**esistor. Integrator → "C" for "Cumulative" → output across **C**apacitor.
>
> 3. **Mixing up the time-constant formulas ($-1$ to $-2$ marks):** The peak of a **differentiator** spike is the full input amplitude $V_m$. The peak of an **integrator** triangle is $\dfrac{V_m T}{2RC}$, **not** $V_m$. Do not confuse them.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the steady-state condition ($-1$ mark):** For integrator problems with continuous input, the output does not reach $V_m$ on the first pulse — it takes several cycles to settle. KTU examiners often test the **steady-state** triangular amplitude, not the first-cycle value.
>
> 5. **Sketching errors ($-1$ mark):** When drawing the differentiator response to a square wave, students sometimes draw **rounded pulses** instead of **sharp spikes**. The spike must start at $\pm V_m$ and decay to 0 with time constant $\tau$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — RC Differentiating & Integrating Circuits**

### Core Concepts
- [x] Both circuits use the **same series R-C topology**; only the **output node** differs.
- [x] **Differentiator:** Output across R; behaves as a **high-pass filter**; $v_o \propto \dfrac{dv_{in}}{dt}$.
- [x] **Integrator:** Output across C; behaves as a **low-pass filter**; $v_o \propto \int v_{in}\,dt$.
- [x] **Differentiator condition:** $\tau = RC \ll T$ (or $\omega RC \ll 1$, or $\tau \le T/10$).
- [x] **Integrator condition:** $\tau = RC \gg T$ (or $\omega RC \gg 1$, or $\tau \ge 10T$).

### Key Formulas (Memorize These!)
- [x] Time constant: $\tau = RC$
- [x] Differentiator TF: $H(s) = \dfrac{sRC}{1 + sRC}$
- [x] Integrator TF: $H(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + sRC}$
- [x] Corner frequency: $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$
- [x] Differentiator output for square wave: sharp spikes of peak $V_m$.
- [x] Integrator output for square wave: triangle of peak $\dfrac{V_m T}{2RC}$.

### Frequency-Domain Insights
- [x] The two transfer functions are **complementary**: $H_{diff} + H_{int} = 1$.
- [x] Bode plot slope: $\pm 20\,\text{dB/decade}$ away from the corner frequency.
- [x] Below $f_c$: integrator passes, differentiator attenuates.
- [x] Above $f_c$: differentiator passes, integrator attenuates.

### Square-Wave Response — Must-Know Shapes
- [x] **Differentiator + square wave →** spike train (alternating +$V_m$ and $-V_m$ spikes with exponential decay).
- [x] **Integrator + square wave →** triangular wave (zero average, peak $\dfrac{V_m T}{2RC}$).

### KTU Module 1 Exam Strategy
- [x] Always **check the time-constant condition first**.
- [x] Always **state the operating regime** (differentiator / integrator / neither) before solving.
- [x] For integrator problems, distinguish between **transient** (first cycle) and **steady-state** responses.
- [x] **Sketch the output** — KTU examiners reward properly labeled sketches.
- [x] Mention the **filter type** (high-pass / low-pass) and **corner frequency** when relevant.

### Real-World Applications to Remember
- [x] **Differentiation:** Edge detection, pulse generation, FM demodulation.
- [x] **Integration:** Ramp generation, ADC sample-and-hold, PWM, function generators.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of First Order RC Low Pass and High Pass Filter for Step Input - Rise Time, Bandwidth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Analysis of First Order RC Low Pass and High Pass Filter for Step Input - Rise Time, Bandwidth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 First-Order RC Filters — Formal Definitions

A **first-order filter** is any filter whose transfer function contains a single energy-storage element, meaning its magnitude response rolls off at **−20 dB/decade** beyond the cutoff frequency. In the case of an RC network, the resistor (R) and capacitor (C) form a single-pole system whose behavior is governed by one time constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **First-Order RC Low Pass Filter (LPF)** is a passive network that passes frequencies from DC (0 Hz) up to its cutoff frequency $f_H$ with minimal attenuation, and attenuates all frequencies above $f_H$ at a rate of **−20 dB/decade**.
> A **First-Order RC High Pass Filter (HPF)** is a passive network that attenuates frequencies below its cutoff frequency $f_L$ and passes frequencies above $f_L$ with minimal attenuation, rolling off at **−20 dB/decade** below cutoff.

A **step input** is an ideal signal that transitions instantaneously from one level to another (e.g., 0 V to V volts). Mathematically, it is modeled as $V_{in}(t) = V \cdot u(t)$, where $u(t)$ is the unit step function.

**Rise Time ($t_r$)** is the time required for the output waveform to transition from **10%** to **90%** of its final steady-state value.

**Bandwidth (BW)** for a low-pass system is defined as the frequency range from **0 Hz** to the upper cutoff frequency $f_H$ (the **−3 dB frequency**).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Water Tank Analogy**
> 
> Imagine a **water tank with a small drain pipe** at the bottom.
> - The **water level** = output voltage
> - The **inflow rate** = input voltage
> - The **pipe diameter (resistance to flow)** = resistor R
> - The **tank size (capacity)** = capacitor C
> 
> When you suddenly pour water in (a step input), the tank does **not** fill up instantly. Water rises **exponentially** because the tank can only accept water as fast as the drain allows. The rate of filling depends on the pipe size (R) and tank size (C). This exponential rise is exactly what happens in an **RC Low Pass Filter** when a step is applied.
> 
> Conversely, an **RC High Pass Filter** behaves like a **sudden pressure surge** in a pipe — initially, water gushes out quickly (large output), but as pressure equalizes, the flow decays to zero. The capacitor "blocks" DC and only "passes" the sudden change.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **−3 dB Cutoff Frequency:** The frequency at which the output power drops to half (or voltage drops to $1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$) of its maximum value.
- **Time Constant ($\tau$):** The product $\tau = RC$, measured in **seconds**.
- **Rise Time Product ($t_r \times BW$):** A fundamental constant for first-order systems: **0.35** (unitless when BW is in Hz and $t_r$ in seconds).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Low Pass Filter (LPF)

The first-order RC LPF consists of a resistor in series with the input, and the output is taken across the capacitor to ground.

**Why it acts as a Low Pass Filter:**
- At **low frequencies (DC):** The capacitor behaves as an **open circuit** (high impedance $X_C = 1/2\pi f C$ becomes very large). Almost the entire input voltage appears at the output. → Signal passes.
- At **high frequencies:** The capacitor behaves as a **short circuit** (low impedance). The output is shunted to ground. → Signal is attenuated.

**Step-by-Step Logic for Step Response:**
1. A step input $V \cdot u(t)$ is applied at $t = 0$.
2. The capacitor cannot change voltage instantaneously → $V_o(0^+) = 0$.
3. The capacitor charges exponentially through R.
4. Final value: $V_o(\infty) = V$ (steady-state).
5. The response follows: $V_o(t) = V(1 - e^{-t/RC})$.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — High Pass Filter (HPF)

The first-order RC HPF has the resistor in series with the input, and the output is taken across the resistor (capacitor to ground).

**Why it acts as a High Pass Filter:**
- At **low frequencies (DC):** The capacitor is open. No current flows. No voltage across R. → Output is zero.
- At **high frequencies:** The capacitor is short. Input passes directly to output. → Signal passes.

**Step Response Logic:**
1. At $t = 0^+$, capacitor acts as a short (cannot change voltage suddenly), so the full input appears at the output: $V_o(0^+) = V$.
2. The capacitor charges through R, gradually blocking the DC component.
3. The response: $V_o(t) = V \cdot e^{-t/RC}$.

### 2.3 Rise Time and Bandwidth — The Core Relationship

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:**
> 
> For a first-order LPF, the **faster** the filter's step response (smaller $t_r$), the **wider** its bandwidth (larger BW). These two parameters are inversely related through the universal constant **0.35**:
> 
> $$t_r \times BW = 2.2RC \times \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{2.2}{2\pi} \approx 0.35$$
> 
> This is one of the most tested relationships in KTU Analog Circuits.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula | Units | Notes / Condition |
|---|---------------------|---------|-------|-------------------|
| 1 | LPF Transfer Function | $H(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + sRC}$ | dimensionless | Single-pole system |
| 2 | LPF Cutoff Frequency | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | **Hz** | −3 dB frequency |
| 3 | LPF Step Response | $V_o(t) = V(1 - e^{-t/RC})$ | **Volts** | $t \geq 0$ |
| 4 | HPF Transfer Function | $H(s) = \dfrac{sRC}{1 + sRC}$ | dimensionless | Single-pole, single-zero |
| 5 | HPF Cutoff Frequency | $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | **Hz** | −3 dB frequency |
| 6 | HPF Step Response | $V_o(t) = V \cdot e^{-t/RC}$ | **Volts** | Decays to zero |
| 7 | Time Constant | $\tau = RC$ | **seconds** | Time to reach 63.2% |
| 8 | Rise Time (10%–90%) | $t_r = 2.2 \, RC$ | **seconds** | First-order LPF |
| 9 | Fall Time (90%–10%) | $t_f = 2.2 \, RC$ | **seconds** | Symmetric for LPF |
| 10 | Rise Time–Bandwidth Product | $t_r \cdot BW = 0.35$ | dimensionless | Universal constant |
| 11 | Time to reach 50% | $t_{50\%} = 0.693 \, RC$ | **seconds** | Half-value point |
| 12 | Time to reach 95% | $t_{95\%} = 3RC$ | **seconds** | 5τ rule approximation |
| 13 | Capacitive Reactance | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | **Ohms** | Frequency dependent |
| 14 | Voltage Divider Ratio (LPF) | $H(j\omega) = \dfrac{1/j\omega C}{R + 1/j\omega C}$ | dimensionless | Complex form |
| 15 | Phase Shift (LPF) | $\phi = -\arctan(2\pi f RC)$ | **radians / degrees** | Negative = lagging |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Audio Systems:** LPFs separate bass from mid-range (crossover networks).
- **Noise Filtering:** LPFs remove high-frequency noise in power supplies and signal lines.
- **Pulse Shaping:** HPFs are used as **differentiators** for sharp pulses (square → spikes).
- **DC Blocking:** HPFs remove DC offset from AC-coupled amplifier stages.
- **Communication Systems:** Channel bandwidth directly limits pulse rise times (digital signal integrity).
- **Oscilloscope Probes:** Compensated probes use RC networks to maintain flat frequency response.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Transfer Function of RC Low Pass Filter

**Circuit Setup:** A series resistor R connected to input $V_{in}$, and a capacitor C to ground. Output $V_o$ is taken across the capacitor.

**Using the Voltage Divider Rule in the s-domain:**

The impedances are $Z_R = R$ and $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{sC}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(s) &= \frac{V_o(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{Z_C}{Z_R + Z_C} \\
&= \frac{\frac{1}{sC}}{R + \frac{1}{sC}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Multiply numerator and denominator by $sC$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(s) &= \frac{1}{sC \cdot R + 1} \\
&= \frac{1}{1 + sRC}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **standard first-order LPF transfer function** with a single pole at $s = -1/RC$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Step Response of RC LPF

**Given:** $V_{in}(t) = V \cdot u(t)$, so $V_{in}(s) = \dfrac{V}{s}$.

**Substitute into the transfer function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_o(s) &= H(s) \cdot V_{in}(s) \\
&= \frac{1}{1 + sRC} \cdot \frac{V}{s} \\
&= \frac{V}{s(1 + sRC)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Perform Partial Fraction Expansion:**

Let $\dfrac{V}{s(1 + sRC)} = \dfrac{A}{s} + \dfrac{B}{1 + sRC}$.

Solving: $A = V$ and $B = -V \cdot RC$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_o(s) &= \frac{V}{s} - \frac{V \cdot RC}{1 + sRC} \\
&= \frac{V}{s} - \frac{V}{s + \frac{1}{RC}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Take the Inverse Laplace Transform** (using $u(t)$ for $t \geq 0$):

$$
\boxed{V_o(t) = V\left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right) \cdot u(t)}
$$

**Verification at boundary conditions:**

- At $t = 0$: $V_o(0) = V(1 - e^0) = V(1 - 1) = 0$ ✓ (capacitor initially uncharged)
- At $t = \infty$: $V_o(\infty) = V(1 - e^{-\infty}) = V(1 - 0) = V$ ✓ (capacitor fully charged)
- At $t = RC = \tau$: $V_o(\tau) = V(1 - e^{-1}) = 0.632V$ (the **63.2% rule**)

### 3.3 Derivation: Rise Time (10% to 90%) of LPF

**Definition:** Rise time $t_r$ is the time taken for the output to rise from 10% of final value to 90% of final value.

**Step 1: Find time $t_1$ when $V_o = 0.1V$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.1V &= V(1 - e^{-t_1/RC}) \\
0.1 &= 1 - e^{-t_1/RC} \\
e^{-t_1/RC} &= 0.9 \\
-\frac{t_1}{RC} &= \ln(0.9) \\
t_1 &= -RC \cdot \ln(0.9) \\
t_1 &= 0.1054 \, RC
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Find time $t_2$ when $V_o = 0.9V$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.9V &= V(1 - e^{-t_2/RC}) \\
0.9 &= 1 - e^{-t_2/RC} \\
e^{-t_2/RC} &= 0.1 \\
-\frac{t_2}{RC} &= \ln(0.1) \\
t_2 &= -RC \cdot \ln(0.1) \\
t_2 &= 2.3026 \, RC
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Rise Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_r &= t_2 - t_1 \\
&= 2.3026 \, RC - 0.1054 \, RC \\
&= 2.1972 \, RC
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_r \approx 2.2 \, RC = 2.2 \, \tau}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Bandwidth (Cutoff Frequency) of LPF

**Magnitude of LPF Transfer Function:**

Substitute $s = j\omega$ in $H(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + sRC}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(j\omega) &= \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC} \\
|H(j\omega)| &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Definition of −3 dB Cutoff:** The frequency at which $\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ of its maximum (DC) value.

At DC ($\omega = 0$): $\vert H(0) \vert = 1$. Maximum value.

**Set the condition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2}} &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\
1 + (\omega_c RC)^2 &= 2 \\
(\omega_c RC)^2 &= 1 \\
\omega_c RC &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solve for cutoff frequency:**

$$
\boxed{f_H = \frac{\omega_c}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}
$$

### 3.5 Derivation: Rise Time–Bandwidth Product

This is the **most important KTU 2024 derivation** combining both concepts.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_r \times BW &= (2.2 \, RC) \times \left(\frac{1}{2\pi RC}\right) \\
&= \frac{2.2}{2\pi} \\
&= \frac{2.2}{6.2832} \\
&= 0.3501
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_r \times BW \approx 0.35}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **universal constant** for any first-order system. It means: if you know the rise time of a circuit's pulse response, you automatically know its bandwidth, and vice versa.

### 3.6 Derivation: Step Response of RC High Pass Filter

**Circuit Setup:** Resistor R in series, capacitor C to ground. Output taken across R.

**Transfer Function (s-domain):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(s) &= \frac{Z_R}{Z_R + Z_C} = \frac{R}{R + \frac{1}{sC}} \\
&= \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step Response (with $V_{in}(s) = V/s$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_o(s) &= \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC} \cdot \frac{V}{s} \\
&= \frac{V \cdot RC}{1 + sRC} \\
&= \frac{V}{s + \frac{1}{RC}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Inverse Laplace Transform:**

$$
\boxed{V_o(t) = V \cdot e^{-t/RC} \cdot u(t)}
$$

**Key Observations:**
- At $t = 0^+$: $V_o(0^+) = V$ (sudden jump)
- At $t = \infty$: $V_o(\infty) = 0$ (DC blocked)
- The output decays exponentially with time constant $\tau = RC$

### 3.7 Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** For an RC low pass filter with $R = 10 \, k\Omega$ and $C = 0.01 \, \mu F$, calculate the cutoff frequency and rise time for a step input of 5 V.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate Time Constant.**

$$
\tau = RC = (10 \times 10^3)(0.01 \times 10^{-6}) = 10^{-4} \, s = 0.1 \, ms
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Cutoff Frequency.**

$$
f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}} = 1591.55 \, Hz \approx 1.59 \, kHz
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Rise Time.**

$$
t_r = 2.2 \, RC = 2.2 \times 10^{-4} \, s = 0.22 \, ms
$$

**Step 4: Verify Rise Time–Bandwidth Product.**

$$
t_r \times f_H = (2.2 \times 10^{-4})(1591.55) = 0.35 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 5: Output at $t = 0.1 \, ms$ (one time constant).**

$$
V_o(0.1 \, ms) = 5(1 - e^{-1}) = 5 \times 0.632 = 3.16 \, V
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> 
> **Q1.** Define rise time and bandwidth of a first-order RC low pass filter. Derive the relationship between them.

**Model Answer:**

**Rise Time ($t_r$):** The time required for the output waveform to rise from **10%** to **90%** of its final steady-state value, in response to a step input.

**Bandwidth (BW):** For a low pass system, the bandwidth is the range of frequencies from **DC (0 Hz) to the upper −3 dB cutoff frequency** $f_H$.

**Derivation of Relationship:**

For a first-order LPF:
- Rise time: $t_r = 2.2 \, RC$
- Cutoff frequency: $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$, so Bandwidth $= f_H$

Multiplying:

$$
t_r \times BW = 2.2 \, RC \times \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{2.2}{2\pi} \approx 0.35
$$

**Conclusion:** The product of rise time and bandwidth is a **universal constant (0.35)** for any first-order system.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> 
> **Q2.** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a first-order RC high pass filter for a step input.

**Model Answer:**

**Circuit:** A series capacitor C and shunt resistor R, with output taken across R.

**Operation for Step Input $V \cdot u(t)$:**

At $t = 0^+$, the capacitor behaves as a **short circuit** (voltage across it cannot change instantaneously). The entire input voltage $V$ appears across the resistor (output). Thus, $V_o(0^+) = V$.

As $t$ increases, the capacitor charges through R, developing a voltage across itself. This reduces the voltage across R. The output decays exponentially:

$$
V_o(t) = V \cdot e^{-t/RC}
$$

At $t = \infty$, the capacitor is fully charged, no current flows, and $V_o(\infty) = 0$.

**Key point:** The HPF passes the **sudden change** (high-frequency component) of the step and **blocks the DC** level. It acts as a **differentiator** for fast pulses.

---

### 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]

> **(a)** [7 Marks] For the first-order RC low pass filter shown, derive the expression for step response. If $R = 4.7 \, k\Omega$ and $C = 1 \, nF$, calculate:
> - (i) Time constant
> - (ii) Cutoff frequency  
> - (iii) Rise time
> - (iv) Output voltage at $t = 2\tau$

> **(b)** [7 Marks] Derive the expression for the rise time of an RC low pass filter. Show that for a first-order system, the product of rise time and bandwidth is approximately 0.35.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Derivation of Step Response:**

For an RC LPF with input $V_{in}(s) = V/s$, the output is:

$$
V_o(s) = H(s) \cdot V_{in}(s) = \frac{1}{1 + sRC} \cdot \frac{V}{s} = \frac{V}{s(1 + sRC)}
$$

By partial fractions: $V_o(s) = \dfrac{V}{s} - \dfrac{V}{s + 1/RC}$

Inverse Laplace transform: $V_o(t) = V(1 - e^{-t/RC})u(t)$

**Calculations:**

**(i) Time Constant:**

$$
\tau = RC = (4.7 \times 10^3)(1 \times 10^{-9}) = 4.7 \times 10^{-6} \, s = 4.7 \, \mu s
$$

**(ii) Cutoff Frequency:**

$$
f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 4.7 \times 10^{-6}} = 33.86 \, kHz
$$

**(iii) Rise Time:**

$$
t_r = 2.2 \, RC = 2.2 \times 4.7 \times 10^{-6} = 10.34 \, \mu s
$$

**(iv) Output at $t = 2\tau$:**

$$
V_o(2\tau) = V(1 - e^{-2}) = V(1 - 0.1353) = 0.8647 \, V
$$

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Rise Time Derivation:**

The output is $V_o(t) = V(1 - e^{-t/RC})$.

**Time $t_1$ when $V_o = 0.1V$:**

$$
0.1 = 1 - e^{-t_1/RC} \implies t_1 = -RC \ln(0.9) = 0.1054 \, RC
$$

**Time $t_2$ when $V_o = 0.9V$:**

$$
0.9 = 1 - e^{-t_2/RC} \implies t_2 = -RC \ln(0.1) = 2.3026 \, RC
$$

**Rise Time:**

$$
t_r = t_2 - t_1 = 2.3026 \, RC - 0.1054 \, RC = 2.1972 \, RC \approx 2.2 \, RC
$$

**Rise Time–Bandwidth Product:**

$$
t_r \times f_H = 2.2 \, RC \times \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{2.2}{2\pi} \approx 0.35
$$

This is a **constant for any first-order system**, proving the inverse relationship between time-domain speed and frequency-domain bandwidth.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]

> **(a)** [7 Marks] Draw the circuit of a first-order RC high pass filter. Derive its transfer function and step response. Hence explain why the HPF acts as a differentiator for high-frequency signals.

> **(b)** [7 Marks] Compare the step responses of first-order RC low pass and high pass filters with the help of neat waveforms. For a filter with $R = 2.2 \, k\Omega$ and $C = 470 \, pF$, determine (i) bandwidth, (ii) rise time, and (iii) time taken for the output to reach 50% of final value for the LPF.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Circuit:** Series capacitor C, shunt resistor R, output across R.

**Transfer Function Derivation:**

$$
H(s) = \frac{R}{R + 1/sC} = \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}
$$

**Step Response Derivation (for input $V/s$):**

$$
V_o(s) = \frac{sRC}{1+sRC} \cdot \frac{V}{s} = \frac{V}{s + 1/RC}
$$

Inverse Laplace: $V_o(t) = V \cdot e^{-t/RC} \cdot u(t)$

**Differentiator Behavior:**

For high frequencies ($sRC \gg 1$), $H(s) \approx sRC$. In time domain, this corresponds to differentiation:

$$
V_o(t) \approx RC \cdot \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}
$$

A step input has a very high $\frac{dV}{dt}$ at $t = 0$, producing a large output spike that then decays. Sharp pulses (square waves) thus produce spike outputs — the differentiator behavior.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Comparison of Step Responses:**

| Feature | LPF | HPF |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Initial Value $V_o(0^+)$ | 0 | V |
| Final Value $V_o(\infty)$ | V | 0 |
| Nature | Exponential rise | Exponential decay |
| Time Constant | $\tau = RC$ | $\tau = RC$ |
| Rise Time | 2.2 RC (10%→90%) | 2.2 RC (90%→10%) |

**Calculations (with $R = 2.2 \, k\Omega$, $C = 470 \, pF$):**

**(i) Bandwidth (= $f_H$ for LPF):**

$$
f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times (2.2 \times 10^3)(470 \times 10^{-12})} = 153.9 \, kHz
$$

**(ii) Rise Time:**

$$
t_r = 2.2 \, RC = 2.2 \times (2.2 \times 10^3)(470 \times 10^{-12}) = 2.274 \, \mu s
$$

**(iii) Time to reach 50% of final value:**

Set $V_o(t) = 0.5V$:

$$
0.5 = 1 - e^{-t/RC} \implies e^{-t/RC} = 0.5 \implies t = RC \ln(2) = 0.693 \, RC
$$

$$
t_{50\%} = 0.693 \times (2.2 \times 10^3)(470 \times 10^{-12}) = 0.716 \, \mu s
$$

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing LPF and HPF outputs:** LPF **rises** from 0 to V; HPF **falls** from V to 0. Many students draw them backwards.
> 
> 2. **Using $\tau = 1/RC$ instead of $\tau = RC$:** This is the most common unit-error trap. Always check: $R$ in Ohms, $C$ in Farads, $\tau$ in seconds.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 2.2 in rise time:** Rise time is **not** equal to $\tau$. The exact value is $2.2\tau$ (between 10% and 90%).
> 
> 4. **Mixing up cutoff frequencies:** For LPF, $f_H$ is the **upper** cutoff. For HPF, $f_L$ is the **lower** cutoff. Bandwidth for LPF = $f_H - 0 = f_H$.
> 
> 5. **Not showing partial fraction expansion:** The KTU board requires full working when deriving step responses from transfer functions. Skipping the PFE step costs 2–3 marks.
> 
> 6. **Incorrect units in final answer:** Always write $Hz$, $s$, or $\mu s$ explicitly. Numerical values without units lose 1 mark.
> 
> 7. **Ignoring boundary conditions:** Examiners expect you to verify $V_o(0) = 0$ and $V_o(\infty) = V$ for the LPF. This is a free 1-mark bonus.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1**

- ✅ **First-order LPF** transfer function: $H(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + sRC}$
- ✅ **First-order HPF** transfer function: $H(s) = \dfrac{sRC}{1 + sRC}$
- ✅ **Time constant:** $\tau = RC$ (always in seconds when R in Ω and C in F)
- ✅ **Cutoff frequency:** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ (same formula for both LPF and HPF)
- ✅ **LPF step response:** $V_o(t) = V(1 - e^{-t/RC})$ — exponential **rise**
- ✅ **HPF step response:** $V_o(t) = V \cdot e^{-t/RC}$ — exponential **decay**
- ✅ **Rise time (10%–90%):** $t_r = 2.2RC$ — applies to LPF
- ✅ **Fall time (90%–10%):** $t_f = 2.2RC$ — applies to HPF step response
- ✅ **63.2% point:** reached at $t = \tau$ (one time constant)
- ✅ **95% point:** reached at $t = 3\tau$ (practical settling)
- ✅ **50% point:** reached at $t = 0.693RC$
- ✅ **Universal relation:** $t_r \times BW = 0.35$ (first-order systems)
- ✅ **−3 dB point:** $|H| = 1/\sqrt{2} = 0.707$ of maximum
- ✅ **Roll-off rate:** −20 dB/decade or −6 dB/octave (LPF/HPF above/below cutoff)
- ✅ **Phase shift (LPF):** $\phi = -\arctan(\omega RC)$ (output lags input)
- ✅ **Differentiator condition (HPF):** $\omega RC \gg 1$
- ✅ **Integrator condition (LPF):** $\omega RC \ll 1$
- ✅ **Energy storage:** Only one element (C) stores energy in first-order RC systems
- ✅ **Memory element:** The capacitor cannot change voltage instantaneously → $V_C(0^+) = V_C(0^-)$

> **Final Tip:** Always **draw the waveform first**, then write the equations. KTU examiners give partial credit for correct sketches even if calculations are incomplete.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diode Clipping and Clamping Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Diode Clipping and Clamping Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Diode Waveform Shaping Circuits — Formal Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCECT303 — Analog Circuits)**, *diode waveform shaping circuits* are non-linear analog networks that exploit the **unidirectional current conduction** property of the **p-n junction diode** to deliberately alter the shape of an input time-varying signal (typically a sinusoidal waveform) into a desired output waveform. They are classified into two principal families:

1. **Clipping Circuits (Limiters):** Circuits that *remove* or *slice off* a portion of the input signal that lies above (or below) a defined reference voltage level. The output is a *truncated* version of the input — the shape is changed, but the **DC average** is shifted.
2. **Clamping Circuits (DC Restorers):** Circuits that *shift* an entire AC waveform vertically (upward or downward) by introducing a DC offset, without altering the peak-to-peak amplitude of the signal. The shape is preserved; only the **DC level** is repositioned.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** *"Diode Clipping and Clamping Circuits"* — students are expected to draw the input-output waveforms, derive the **piecewise-linear transfer characteristic (V₀ vs Vᵢ)** for each topology, and identify the **transfer slope** and **breakpoint voltages**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a waveform as a **river of water** flowing past a barrier:

- **Clipping** is like a **dam with a controlled spillway**: water below the spillway level flows freely, but any water exceeding the spillway's threshold is *spilled away* — it never appears downstream. The river (signal) still arrives, but its "tops" or "bottoms" are sliced off.
- **Clamping** is like a **lock in a canal**: the entire water level is shifted up or down by an equivalent height, but the *fluctuation amplitude* (ripple) remains unchanged. The water still rises and falls by the same amount — it just rides on a different baseline.

In both cases, the **diode acts as an automatic, electronically controlled valve** — it conducts (ON) in one direction and blocks (OFF) in the other, executing the slicing or shifting operation in real time.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Value | Remarks |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Silicon diode forward voltage | $V_\gamma$ | **≈ 0.7 V** | Used for piecewise-linear models |
| Germanium diode forward voltage | $V_\gamma$ | **≈ 0.3 V** | Rare in modern designs |
| Ideal diode forward drop | $V_F$ | **0 V** | Used for ideal-diode analysis |
| Thermal voltage at 300 K | $V_T$ | **≈ 25.85 mV** | Used in Shockley equation |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board examinations, the **ideal diode model ($V_\gamma = 0$ V)** is the default unless the question explicitly specifies a practical silicon diode. Always read the question stem carefully.

---

## 1.4 Standard Notation Adopted Throughout These Notes

- $V_i(t)$ — instantaneous input voltage
- $V_o(t)$ — instantaneous output voltage
- $V_{ref}$ — external reference (bias) voltage
- $V_\gamma$ — diode knee / cut-in voltage
- $R$ — series resistor (current limiter)
- $C$ — coupling / storage capacitor
- **D** — diode, anode → A, cathode → K

The diode conducts when its **anode-to-cathode voltage $V_{AK} \geq V_\gamma$**, and it behaves as an open circuit (OFF) otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-State Operational Logic of Every Diode Clipper

A clipper is fundamentally a **two-state voltage-transfer network**. The output is governed by a **piecewise-linear relationship** that depends on whether the diode is in its **ON** (forward biased, modeled as a $V_\gamma$ battery or short) or **OFF** (reverse biased, open circuit) state.

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic Steps (Generic Procedure)

1. **Identify the diode orientation** — Anode tied to higher potential? Cathode tied to lower potential?
2. **Determine the ON/OFF threshold** — The transition voltage $V_{TH}$ is the value of $V_i$ at which $V_{AK} = V_\gamma$.
3. **Case A: Diode OFF** — Replace diode with **open circuit**. Apply KVL/KCL to find $V_o$ in terms of $V_i$. This defines one linear segment of the transfer curve.
4. **Case B: Diode ON** — Replace diode with a **$V_\gamma$ battery** (or short for ideal). Solve the resulting linear network. This defines the second linear segment.
5. **Concatenate the two segments** at the breakpoint $V_{TH}$ to draw the **complete transfer characteristic** $V_o(V_i)$.
6. **Superimpose the input sinusoid** on the transfer characteristic (graphical construction) to sketch the output waveform.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners award **full marks only** when the student draws *both* the transfer characteristic *and* the output waveform on a *time axis*. Omitting either costs at least 2–3 marks.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Clipping Circuits

| Class | Sub-type | Diode Polarity | Clips Which Half? | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Series Clipper** | Positive half | Cathode → output | Negative half-cycle | Half-wave rectifiers |
| **Series Clipper** | Negative half | Anode → output | Positive half-cycle | Reverse-polarity protection |
| **Shunt (Parallel) Clipper** | Positive half | Cathode → ground | Positive half-cycle | Positive-peak limiter |
| **Shunt (Parallel) Clipper** | Negative half | Anode → ground | Negative half-cycle | Negative-peak limiter |
| **Biased Clipper** | Positive/negative | Either + $V_{ref}$ | Above / below $V_{ref}$ | Adjustable threshold |
| **Combinational Clipper** | Double-diode | Both orientations | Both peaks | Symmetrical limiter |
| **Zener Clipper** | Back-to-back zeners | Both directions | Bidirectional | Precision voltage limiter |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Clippers (Ideal Diode, $V_\gamma = 0$)

| Circuit Topology | ON Condition (Diode Conducts) | OFF Condition | Output $V_o$ when OFF | Output $V_o$ when ON | Breakpoint $V_{TH}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Series Positive Half-Wave Clipper** | $V_i < 0$ | $V_i \geq 0$ | $V_o = V_i$ | $V_o = 0$ | $V_{TH} = 0$ |
| **Series Negative Half-Wave Clipper** | $V_i > 0$ | $V_i \leq 0$ | $V_o = V_i$ | $V_o = 0$ | $V_{TH} = 0$ |
| **Shunt Positive Clipper** | $V_i > 0$ | $V_i \leq 0$ | $V_o = V_i$ | $V_o = 0$ | $V_{TH} = 0$ |
| **Shunt Negative Clipper** | $V_i < 0$ | $V_i \geq 0$ | $V_o = V_i$ | $V_o = 0$ | $V_{TH} = 0$ |
| **Biased Positive Clipper (ref $V_R$)** | $V_i > V_R$ | $V_i \leq V_R$ | $V_o = V_i$ | $V_o = V_R$ | $V_{TH} = V_R$ |
| **Biased Negative Clipper (ref $V_R$)** | $V_i < V_R$ | $V_i \geq V_R$ | $V_o = V_i$ | $V_o = V_R$ | $V_{TH} = V_R$ |
| **Combinational Symmetric Clipper** | $\vert V_i \vert > V_R$ | $\vert V_i \vert \leq V_R$ | $V_o = V_i$ | $V_o = \pm V_R$ | $V_{TH} = \pm V_R$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When using a **practical silicon diode** ($V_\gamma = 0.7$ V), replace every occurrence of `0` with `$V_\gamma$` and `0 V` with `$V_\gamma$` in the output expressions, and shift the breakpoint accordingly. For example, a series positive clipper with $V_\gamma$ clips the negative half-cycle but produces $V_o = +V_\gamma$ during the ON interval — *not zero*.

---

## 2.4 Clamping Circuits — DC Restorers

A clamper is essentially a **clipper preceded by a capacitor**. The capacitor charges during one half-cycle to the peak of the input, then behaves as a DC battery in series with the signal source during the subsequent half-cycle, shifting the entire waveform.

### 2.4.1 Operational Logic Steps (Generic Clamper)

1. During the **first quarter cycle**, the diode conducts heavily, charging the capacitor to the **peak value** of the input through the diode's small forward resistance.
2. After charging, the diode becomes **reverse biased** (OFF), and the capacitor **holds its charge** (assuming $RC \gg T/2$, where $T$ is the signal period).
3. The output voltage is then $V_o(t) = V_i(t) \pm V_C$, where $V_C$ is the steady-state capacitor voltage.
4. The sign of $V_C$ determines whether the waveform is shifted **upward (positive clamper)** or **downward (negative clamper)**.

### 2.4.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Clampers (Ideal Diode)

| Circuit | Capacitor Charges To | Steady-State Capacitor Voltage | Output Waveform Peak | DC Shift |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| **Negative Clamper** | $V_i = +V_m$ (first +ve peak) | $V_C = +V_m$ | Peaks at $0$ and $-2V_m$ | **Down** by $V_m$ |
| **Positive Clamper** | $V_i = -V_m$ (first −ve peak) | $V_C = -V_m$ | Peaks at $0$ and $+2V_m$ | **Up** by $V_m$ |
| **Biased Negative Clamper (ref $V_R$)** | $+V_m - V_R$ | $V_C = V_m - V_R$ | Peaks at $V_R$ and $2V_R - V_m$ | **Down** by $(V_m - V_R)$ |
| **Biased Positive Clamper (ref $V_R$)** | $-V_m - V_R$ | $V_C = V_m + V_R$ | Peaks at $-V_R$ and $2V_R + V_m$ | **Up** by $(V_m + V_R)$ |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility & Engineering Applications

- **Signal conditioning in oscilloscopes and ADC front-ends** — clipping protects downstream low-voltage electronics from over-voltage transients.
- **TV video signal processing** — *DC restoration* (clamping) holds the sync tips at a fixed reference so the brightness information rides on a stable baseline.
- **FM demodulation** — double-diode clippers symmetrize noisy FM signals before they reach the discriminator.
- **Power supply crowbar protection** — zener clippers shunt over-voltage to ground.
- **Communication receivers** — IF-stage limiters remove AM noise by clipping amplitude variations while preserving zero-crossings.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Biased Positive Shunt Clipper with Reference $V_R$

### 3.1.1 Circuit Description

A sinusoidal source $V_i(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ drives a node through series resistor $R$. The shunt branch consists of a diode **D** (anode at node, cathode at reference $V_R$) in parallel with the load. The output is taken across the load (assumed very high impedance).

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Piecewise Solution

**Step 1 — Identify diode state.**
The diode's anode-to-cathode voltage is $V_{AK} = V_o - V_R$.
The diode conducts (ON) when $V_{AK} \geq V_\gamma$, i.e., when $V_o \geq V_R + V_\gamma$.

**Step 2 — Case A: Diode OFF ($V_o < V_R + V_\gamma$).**
With the diode an open circuit, no current flows through $R$ from source to ground, hence $V_o = V_i$.

$$
V_o = V_i \quad \text{(diode OFF, no load current)}
$$

**Step 3 — Case B: Diode ON ($V_o \geq V_R + V_\gamma$).**
With the diode replaced by its constant-voltage drop $V_\gamma$ (idealized), the cathode sits at $V_R$, so the anode (which is $V_o$) is clamped at $V_R + V_\gamma$.

$$
V_o = V_R + V_\gamma \quad \text{(diode ON, output is clamped)}
$$

**Step 4 — Determine breakpoint.**
The transition occurs when both expressions are equal: $V_i = V_R + V_\gamma$.

$$
V_{TH} = V_R + V_\gamma
$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the complete piecewise-linear transfer characteristic.**

$$
V_o(V_i) =
\begin{cases}
V_i, & \text{if } V_i \leq V_R + V_\gamma \\[6pt]
V_R + V_\gamma, & \text{if } V_i > V_R + V_\gamma
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 6 — Graphical construction of output waveform.**
Project the input sinusoid $V_m \sin(\omega t)$ onto the transfer curve. Below $V_{TH}$, the curve is the 45° line $V_o = V_i$ — the sinusoid passes through unchanged. Above $V_{TH}$, the curve flattens to $V_R + V_\gamma$ — the sinusoid is sliced horizontally. The result is a waveform whose **positive peaks are clipped to $V_R + V_\gamma$** while the negative half-cycle is untouched.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Sketch the transfer characteristic and the output waveform."* Always include **both diagrams** in your answer sheet.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Unbiased Negative Clamper (Ideal Diode)

### 3.2.1 Circuit Description

A sinusoid $V_i(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ feeds a series capacitor $C$, followed by a shunt diode whose **cathode is at the input side and anode is at ground** (this is the canonical negative-clamper orientation). Output is taken at the diode anode (which is also the node between $C$ and the diode).

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Initial transient (first quarter cycle, $0 \leq \omega t \leq \pi/2$).**
During the *first positive-going* excursion, the diode's cathode (input side) is driven positive while its anode (output side) is at $0$ V. The diode is therefore **reverse-biased OFF**.

Wait — for a **negative clamper**, the convention is: diode **cathode at output node, anode to ground**. Let us adopt this canonical topology.

With the anode at $0$ and cathode following the input through the capacitor, when $V_i$ first goes negative, the anode ($0$ V) becomes *more positive* than the cathode, so the diode conducts.

**Step 2 — First negative half-cycle ($\pi \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi$).**
$V_i$ reaches its most negative value $-V_m$. The diode turns ON; the capacitor charges rapidly through the diode's small forward resistance $r_F$. The capacitor voltage $V_C$ (measured with + on the left/input side, − on the right/output side) reaches:

$$
V_C(\text{charged}) = V_m
$$

**Step 3 — Subsequent cycles — Diode OFF.**
For all other portions of the waveform, the diode is OFF, so the capacitor **holds its charge** ($V_C = V_m$, constant). The output voltage is given by KVL around the loop:

$$
V_o = V_i + V_C
$$

Wait, careful with sign convention. If the capacitor's positive plate is on the **output side** (right) and negative plate on the **input side** (left), then:

$$
V_o - V_i = V_C \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_o = V_i + V_C
$$

But we found $V_C = V_m$ with the polarity opposite. Let us redefine: take $V_C$ as the magnitude. The DC shift is **downward** by $V_m$:

$$
\boxed{V_o(t) = V_i(t) - V_m = V_m \sin(\omega t) - V_m}
$$

**Step 4 — Locate the new peaks.**

$$
V_{o,\max} = V_m - V_m = 0 \quad (\text{positive peak pinned at } 0)
$$

$$
V_{o,\min} = -V_m - V_m = -2V_m \quad (\text{negative peak at } -2V_m)
$$

**Step 5 — Verify peak-to-peak amplitude is preserved.**

$$
V_{o,pp} = V_{o,\max} - V_{o,\min} = 0 - (-2V_m) = 2V_m = V_{i,pp} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Principle:** A clamper does *not* change the peak-to-peak amplitude. It only shifts the DC level.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Output Waveform Peak Locations

| Circuit | Positive Peak $V_{o,+}$ | Negative Peak $V_{o,-}$ | DC Shift Direction |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Negative Clamper** (unbiased) | $0$ | $-2V_m$ | Down by $V_m$ |
| **Positive Clamper** (unbiased) | $+2V_m$ | $0$ | Up by $V_m$ |
| **Biased Negative Clamper** | $V_R$ | $V_R - 2V_m$ | Down by $(V_m - V_R)$ |
| **Biased Positive Clamper** | $2V_m + V_R$ | $V_R$ | Up by $(V_m + V_R)$ |
| **Biased Negative Clamper (ideal: $V_m = V_R$)** | $V_R$ | $-V_R$ | Symmetric about $0$ |
| **Combinational Clipper** | $+V_R$ | $-V_R$ | Square wave (centered) |

---

## 3.4 Verification — Average (DC) Value of a Clamped Waveform

The **average value of an unbiased negative clamper output** is computed as follows.

$$
V_{o,\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \bigl[V_m \sin(\omega t) - V_m\bigr]\, dt
$$

$$
= \frac{V_m}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \sin(\omega t)\, dt - \frac{V_m}{T} \int_{0}^{T} dt
$$

$$
= V_m \cdot 0 - V_m = -V_m
$$

Thus the **DC component has been shifted from $0$ (input) to $-V_m$ (output)** — a downward shift of $V_m$. This confirms the clamper's role as a **DC level translator**.
