Rearranging to the **load line form** $I_C = m \cdot V_{CE} + c$:

$$I_C = -\dfrac{1}{R_C} V_{CE} + \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_C}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the two intercepts of the load line:**

When $V_{CE} = 0$:

$$I_{C(\max)} = I_{C(sat)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_C}$$

When $I_C = 0$:

$$V_{CE(\max)} = V_{CE(\text{cut-off})} = V_{CC}$$

**Step 4 — Locate the Q-point** by substituting the actual $I_{CQ}$ value back into the load line equation:

$$I_{CQ} = \beta_{DC} \cdot I_B = \beta_{DC} \cdot \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$$

$$V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_{CQ} R_C$$

These two values $(V_{CEQ}, I_{CQ})$ are the **coordinates of the Operating Point Q**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stability Factor $S(I_{CBO})$ for Fixed-Bias

The collector current of a BJT is related to the leakage current $I_{CBO}$ by:

$$I_C = \beta I_B + (1+\beta) I_{CBO}$$

We define the **stability factor**:

$$S(I_{CBO}) = \dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CBO}}$$

Differentiating the above expression with respect to $I_{CBO}$ (treating $\beta$ and $I_B$ as constant for fixed bias):

$$S(I_{CBO}) = 1 + \beta$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** In fixed bias, $S(I_{CBO}) = 1 + \beta$, which is **huge** (e.g., $\beta = 100 \Rightarrow S = 101$). This means a tiny change in leakage current gets *amplified 101 times* in the collector current. Therefore, **fixed bias has the worst thermal stability** — a classic KTU exam point.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Stability Factor for Voltage-Divider Bias

For the voltage-divider (universal) bias circuit, applying Thevenin's theorem at the base:

$$V_{TH} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{CC}, \qquad R_{TH} = \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Applying KVL around the base–emitter loop:

$$V_{TH} = I_B R_{TH} + V_{BE} + (1+\beta) I_B R_E$$

Solving for $I_B$:

$$I_B = \dfrac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (1+\beta) R_E}$$

The collector current is $I_C = \beta I_B + (1+\beta) I_{CBO}$. Differentiating and simplifying the stability factor gives:

$$S(I_{CBO}) = \dfrac{1+\beta}{1 + \beta \cdot \dfrac{R_E}{R_{TH} + R_E}}$$

**Step-by-step simplification:**

$$S(I_{CBO}) = \dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CBO}} = \dfrac{1 + \beta}{1 + \beta \cdot \dfrac{R_E}{R_{TH} + R_E}}$$

For the typical design goal $R_{TH} \ll (1+\beta) R_E$, the denominator $\approx 1 + \beta$, giving $S \approx 1$, which is **near-ideal stability**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** When $\beta R_E \gg R_{TH}$, the bias becomes **essentially independent of $\beta$**. This is why voltage-divider bias is the **most widely used** BJT biasing scheme in commercial amplifiers.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Q-Point Drift Due to Temperature

The collector current varies with temperature according to:

$$I_C(T_2) = I_C(T_1) \cdot 2^{(T_2 - T_1)/10} \cdot \left[\dfrac{\beta(T_2)}{\beta(T_1)}\right]$$

**Numerical Example (typical KTU problem):**

Suppose $I_C = 2$ mA at $T_1 = 25°$C, and we want to find $I_C$ at $T_2 = 65°$C, given that $\beta$ doubles in this range.

**Step 1 — Temperature rise in leakage (doubles every 10°C):**

$$\Delta T = 65 - 25 = 40°\text{C} \Rightarrow \text{multiplier} = 2^{40/10} = 2^4 = 16$$

**Step 2 — Apply $\beta$ correction (assume $\beta$ doubles):**

$$I_C(65°\text{C}) = 2\ \text{mA} \times 16 \times 2 = 64\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the failure mode:**

Such a large $I_C$ pushes the transistor deep into **saturation**, destroying the Q-point. This demonstrates why **emitter degeneration** (adding $R_E$) is critical for thermal stability.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Biasing Techniques

| Bias Circuit | $S(I_{CBO})$ | $I_C$ Stability | $V_{CEQ}$ Stability | Typical Use |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Fixed Bias** | $1+\beta$ (worst) | Poor | Poor | Switch applications only |
| **Collector-to-Base Bias** | $\dfrac{1+\beta}{1+\beta \cdot R_C/(R_B+R_C)}$ | Moderate | Moderate | Small-signal stages |
| **Emitter Bias** | $\dfrac{(1+\beta)(R_B+R_E)}{R_B+(1+\beta)R_E}$ | Good | Good | General purpose |
| **Voltage-Divider Bias** | $\dfrac{(1+\beta)(R_{TH}+R_E)}{R_{TH}+(1+\beta)R_E}$ | **Excellent** | **Excellent** | Industry standard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** In numerical problems, the examiner will often ask: *"Justify why voltage-divider bias is preferred over fixed bias for amplifier stages."* Your answer must include the **stability factor comparison** and the **insensitivity to $\beta$** of the voltage-divider scheme.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fixed-Bias BJT Circuit — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeVcc[VCC SUPPLY] --> nodeRB[RB BASE RESISTOR]
    nodeRB --> nodeB[BJT BASE TERMINAL]
    nodeVcc --> nodeRC[RC COLLECTOR RESISTOR]
    nodeRC --> nodeC[BJT COLLECTOR TERMINAL]
    nodeE[BJT EMITTER TERMINAL] --> nodeGND[GROUND NODE]
    nodeB --> nodeBJT[BJT NPN DEVICE]
    nodeC --> nodeBJT
    nodeE --> nodeBJT
    nodeBJT --> nodeQ[Q POINT COORDINATES VCEQ AND ICQ]
```

## 4.2 DC Load Line and Q-Point Visualization

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[POINT A VCC ON VCE AXIS IC ZERO] --> nodeB[DC LOAD LINE STRAIGHT LINE SLOPE NEGATIVE 1 OVER RC]
    nodeB --> nodeC[POINT B VCC OVER RC ON IC AXIS VCE ZERO]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Q POINT INTERSECTION OF LOAD LINE AND IB CURVE]
    nodeD --> nodeE[OPERATING COORDINATES VCEQ AND ICQ]
    nodeD --> nodeF[SYMMETRIC SWING REGION EQUAL ABOVE AND BELOW Q]
```

## 4.3 Voltage-Divider Bias — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeVcc[VCC SUPPLY] --> nodeR1[R1 TOP DIVIDER RESISTOR]
    nodeR1 --> nodeN[NODE B BASE]
    nodeN --> nodeR2[R2 BOTTOM DIVIDER RESISTOR]
    nodeR2 --> nodeGND1[GROUND]
    nodeN --> nodeBJT[BJT BASE]
    nodeVcc --> nodeRC[RC COLLECTOR RESISTOR]
    nodeRC --> nodeC[BJT COLLECTOR]
    nodeE[BJT EMITTER] --> nodeRE[RE EMITTER RESISTOR]
    nodeRE --> nodeGND2[GROUND]
    nodeBJT --> nodeQ[STABLE Q POINT VTH AND RTH CONTROLLED]
```

## 4.4 Q-Point Drift Compensation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart[TEMPERATURE RISES] --> nodeLeak[ICBO INCREASES EXPONENTIALLY]
    nodeLeak --> nodeIC[IC INCREASES]
    nodeIC --> nodeVRE[VOLTAGE ACROSS RE INCREASES]
    nodeVRE --> nodeVBE[VBE EFFECTIVELY REDUCED]
    nodeVBE --> nodeIB[IB DECREASES]
    nodeIB --> nodeIC2[IC DECREASES COUNTERACTING ORIGINAL RISE]
    nodeIC2 --> nodeStable[NEGATIVE FEEDBACK STABILIZES Q POINT]
```

## 4.5 Class-A Amplifier Q-Point Positioning

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeCutoff[LEFT END VCE = VCC IC = 0 CUTOFF REGION] --> nodeLoadLine[DC LOAD LINE]
    nodeLoadLine --> nodeMidQ[Q POINT AT MIDDLE VCEQ = VCC OVER 2]
    nodeMidQ --> nodeSwing[OUTPUT SWING RADIUS EQUAL ON BOTH SIDES]
    nodeLoadLine --> nodeSat[RIGHT END VCE = 0 IC = VCC OVER RC SATURATION REGION]
    nodeMidQ --> nodeBelow[SWING BELOW Q TOWARD SATURATION]
    nodeMidQ --> nodeAbove[SWING ABOVE Q TOWARD CUTOFF]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading these diagrams:** Each block represents a circuit region, a calculated parameter, or a physical node. The arrows denote DC current flow or signal flow direction. The Q-point block is the **target output** of the entire biasing network.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Operating Point. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

The operating point (Q-point) of a BJT is the pair of DC values $(V_{CEQ}, I_{CQ})$ that specify the transistor's collector–emitter voltage and collector current when no AC input signal is applied. It is the intersection of the DC load line and the transistor's output characteristic corresponding to the quiescent base current $I_{BQ}$.

Mathematically:

$$I_{CQ} = \beta_{DC} I_{BQ}, \qquad V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_{CQ} R_C$$

For maximum undistorted AC output swing, the Q-point is positioned at the **midpoint of the DC load line**, i.e., $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}/2$ and $I_{CQ} = V_{CC}/(2R_C)$. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2: Why is fixed bias not preferred for amplifier circuits? [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

Fixed bias is not preferred for amplifier circuits because its **stability factor** with respect to leakage current is extremely poor:

$$S(I_{CBO}) = 1 + \beta$$

For $\beta = 100$, a tiny change in $I_{CBO}$ produces a **101× amplified change** in $I_C$. This causes the Q-point to drift drastically with:

1. **Temperature variations** (silicon $I_{CBO}$ doubles every 10°C).
2. **Device-to-device $\beta$ variation** (a replacement transistor may have $\beta = 250$ instead of 100, shifting $I_C$ by 2.5×).
3. **Supply voltage drift**.

The resulting Q-point instability causes **clipping, thermal runaway, and distortion**, making fixed bias unsuitable for linear amplifiers. Voltage-divider bias is preferred because $S \approx 1$. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### (a) For the voltage-divider bias circuit shown with $V_{CC} = 20$ V, $R_1 = 10$ k$\Omega$, $R_2 = 5$ k$\Omega$, $R_C = 2$ k$\Omega$, $R_E = 1$ k$\Omega$, $\beta = 100$, and $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V, calculate the Q-point $(V_{CEQ}, I_{CQ})$ and the stability factor $S(I_{CBO})$. **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute Thevenin voltage $V_{TH}$ at the base:**

$$V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 20 \cdot \dfrac{5}{10 + 5} = 20 \cdot \dfrac{1}{3} = 6.667\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Thevenin resistance $R_{TH}$:**

$$R_{TH} = \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \dfrac{10 \times 5}{10 + 5} = \dfrac{50}{15} = 3.333\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Compute $I_B$ from KVL on the base–emitter loop:**

$$I_B = \dfrac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (1+\beta) R_E} = \dfrac{6.667 - 0.7}{3.333 + (101)(1)} = \dfrac{5.967}{104.333} = 0.0572\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $I_{CQ}$:**

$$I_{CQ} = \beta I_B = 100 \times 0.0572 = 5.72\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $V_{CEQ}$:**

$$V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_C(R_C + R_E) = 20 - 5.72 \times 10^{-3} \times (2 + 1) \times 10^3$$

$$V_{CEQ} = 20 - 5.72 \times 3 = 20 - 17.16 = 2.84\ \text{V}$$

**Step 6 — Compute stability factor:**

$$S(I_{CBO}) = \dfrac{1+\beta}{1 + \beta \cdot \dfrac{R_E}{R_{TH} + R_E}} = \dfrac{101}{1 + 100 \cdot \dfrac{1}{4.333}} = \dfrac{101}{1 + 23.08} = \dfrac{101}{24.08} = 4.19$$

> **Final Answer:** $I_{CQ} = 5.72$ mA, $V_{CEQ} = 2.84$ V, $S(I_{CBO}) = 4.19$ (7 marks)

---

#### (b) Explain the concept of DC and AC load lines. How is the Q-point located, and what determines the maximum undistorted output swing? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **DC load line** is a graphical representation of all possible DC operating points $(V_{CE}, I_C)$ of a transistor, derived from the KVL equation of the output loop:

$$V_{CC} = I_C R_C + V_{CE} \Rightarrow I_C = -\dfrac{1}{R_C} V_{CE} + \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_C}$$

It is a straight line with intercepts $(V_{CC}, 0)$ on the voltage axis and $(0, V_{CC}/R_C)$ on the current axis.

The **AC load line** describes the locus of operating points when an AC signal is applied. It passes through the Q-point with a slope of $-1/R_{ac}$, where $R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L$. It is **steeper** than the DC load line.

**Locating the Q-Point:** The Q-point is the intersection of the DC load line with the output characteristic curve corresponding to the DC base current $I_{BQ}$. The coordinates are:

$$I_{CQ} = \beta I_{BQ}, \qquad V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_{CQ} R_C$$

**Maximum Undistorted Swing:** The output signal swings along the **AC load line** about the Q-point. The maximum peak-to-peak swing is limited by the closer of the two AC load-line intercepts (the cut-off side and the saturation side). The **symmetric maximum** occurs when the Q-point is at the **midpoint of the AC load line**, allowing equal swing in both directions before hitting saturation or cut-off. This gives the largest possible $V_{out,pp}$ without distortion. (7 marks)

---

### Question B (Option 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### (a) Derive the stability factor $S(I_{CBO})$ for (i) fixed bias and (ii) voltage-divider bias. Compare the two. **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Define stability factor:**

$$S(I_{CBO}) = \dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CBO}} \bigg|_{V_{CC}, \beta, T = \text{const}}$$

**Step 2 — Derive for fixed bias:**

The collector current equation is $I_C = \beta I_B + (1+\beta) I_{CBO}$. In fixed bias, $I_B$ is set by $V_{CC}$ and $R_B$ and is independent of $I_{CBO}$. Therefore:

$$S(I_{CBO}) = \dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CBO}} = 1 + \beta$$

**Step 3 — Derive for voltage-divider bias:**

Using Thevenin equivalents $V_{TH}$ and $R_{TH}$, KVL gives:

$$I_B = \dfrac{V_{TH} - V_{BE} - I_{CBO} R_E}{R_{TH} + (1+\beta) R_E}$$

Substituting into $I_C = \beta I_B + (1+\beta) I_{CBO}$ and differentiating:

$$S(I_{CBO}) = \dfrac{1+\beta}{1 + \beta \cdot \dfrac{R_E}{R_{TH} + R_E}}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison:**

| Parameter | Fixed Bias | Voltage-Divider Bias |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| $S(I_{CBO})$ | $1+\beta$ (e.g., 101) | $\approx 1$ to few units |
| $\beta$ sensitivity | $S(\beta) = 1$ | $S(\beta) \to 0$ if $R_{TH} \ll \beta R_E$ |
| $V_{BE}$ sensitivity | High | Low |
| Practical use | Switching | Linear amplifiers |

> **Conclusion:** Voltage-divider bias is **far superior** for Q-point stability and is the industry standard. (7 marks)

---

#### (b) A silicon transistor with $\beta = 100$ is used in a fixed-bias circuit with $V_{CC} = 12$ V, $R_B = 100$ k$\Omega$, and $R_C = 1$ k$\Omega$. Find $I_{BQ}$, $I_{CQ}$, $V_{CEQ}$, and the saturation and cut-off points of the load line. **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute base current:**

$$I_{BQ} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B} = \dfrac{12 - 0.7}{100 \times 10^3} = \dfrac{11.3}{100{,}000} = 0.113\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 2 — Compute collector current:**

$$I_{CQ} = \beta I_{BQ} = 100 \times 0.113 = 11.3\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $V_{CEQ}$:**

$$V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_{CQ} R_C = 12 - (11.3 \times 10^{-3})(1 \times 10^3) = 12 - 11.3 = 0.7\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute load-line intercepts:**

Saturation point (on $I_C$ axis):

$$I_{C(sat)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_C} = \dfrac{12}{1\ \text{k}\Omega} = 12\ \text{mA}$$

Cut-off point (on $V_{CE}$ axis):

$$V_{CE(cut\text{-}off)} = V_{CC} = 12\ \text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Check operating region:**

Since $V_{CEQ} = 0.7$ V is very close to $V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2$ V, the Q-point is **near saturation** — the circuit is poorly biased. To improve, $R_B$ should be increased.

> **Final Answer:** $I_{BQ} = 0.113$ mA, $I_{CQ} = 11.3$ mA, $V_{CEQ} = 0.7$ V, $I_{C(sat)} = 12$ mA, $V_{CE(cut\text{-}off)} = 12$ V. (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract $V_{BE}$:** Students often write $I_B = V_{CC}/R_B$, ignoring the 0.7 V drop. This error costs **2–3 marks** in numerical problems.
> 2. **Using $R_C$ alone in voltage-divider bias:** The correct load expression is $R_C + R_E$, not just $R_C$. This is a **favourite trap question** of KTU examiners.
> 3. **Confusing DC and AC load lines:** The DC load line uses $R_C$ alone; the AC load line uses $R_C \parallel R_L$. Examiners expect both to be drawn and labeled distinctly.
> 4. **Not converting units:** $R$ in k$\Omega$ and $I$ in mA give $V$ directly in V — but if you mix units (e.g., $R$ in $\Omega$ and $I$ in mA), the answer is wrong by a factor of 1000. **Always cross-check units before finalizing.**
> 5. **Stability factor sign errors:** Stability factors are *positive* quantities; negative values in the answer booklet indicate an algebraic error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Operating Point (Q-point):** The DC values $(V_{CEQ}, I_{CQ})$ of a transistor with no signal applied.
- **Q-point = Intersection** of the DC load line and the output characteristic at $I_{BQ}$.
- **DC Load Line Equation:** $I_C = -\dfrac{V_{CE}}{R_C} + \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_C}$, intercepts at $(V_{CC}, 0)$ and $(0, V_{CC}/R_C)$.
- **AC Load Line** passes through the Q-point with slope $-1/(R_C \parallel R_L)$.
- **Maximum symmetric swing** requires Q-point at midpoint of load line: $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}/2$.
- **Fixed bias:** $S(I_{CBO}) = 1+\beta$ → poor stability → used only for switching.
- **Voltage-divider bias:** $S(I_{CBO}) \approx 1$ → excellent stability → standard for amplifiers.
- **Thevenin equivalents** $V_{TH}$ and $R_{TH}$ are essential for analyzing any bias network.
- **Thermal effects:** $I_{CBO}$ doubles every 10°C; $\beta$ increases with $T$; $V_{BE}$ decreases by 2 mV/°C.
- **Bias compensation techniques:** Emitter resistor, diode compensation, thermistor compensation, and active current sources.
- **Practical silicon BJT:** Always assume $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V unless otherwise stated.
- **Stability is achieved through negative feedback** in the emitter resistor (the voltage across $R_E$ opposes any $I_C$ change).
- **Voltage-divider bias is the most exam-relevant** topic — expect at least one numerical problem in every KTU paper.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BJT Biasing (CE Configuration) - Fixed Bias and Voltage Divider Bias (Design and Analysis)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** BJT Biasing (CE Configuration) - Fixed Bias and Voltage Divider Bias (Design and Analysis) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) **BJT Biasing** in the **Common Emitter (CE) configuration** refers to the application of external DC voltages and currents to a Bipolar Junction Transistor such that it operates at a pre-determined, stable **Quiescent Operating Point (Q-point)**. The Q-point is uniquely defined by the quiescent collector current $I_{CQ}$ and the quiescent collector-emitter voltage $V_{CEQ}$ on the DC load line. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PCECT303 Module 1):** > The student must master *two* biasing topologies under CE: > 1. **Fixed Bias** (Base Bias / Fixed Current Bias) > 2. **Voltage Divider Bias** (Self-Bias / Emitter Bias) > Both *analysis* (given components, find Q-point and stability) and *design* (given Q-point, find components) are examinable. ## 1.2 The "Why" of Biasing — Conceptual Analogy Imagine a **water tap (faucet) controlling a high-pressure pipe**: - If the tap is **fully closed** → no water flows (transistor is in **cutoff**). - If the tap is **fully open** → maximum uncontrolled flow; the pipe might burst (transistor is in **saturation**). - If the tap is set at a **precise, partially open position** → a steady, controlled flow is delivered regardless of small pressure changes (transistor operates in the **active region** at a stable Q-point). **Biasing is simply the electronic equivalent of "setting the tap"** to that precise partially-open position. The two main enemies of a stable Q-point are: 1. **Temperature variations** (changes $I_{CBO}$ and $V_{BE}$). 2. **Manufacturing variations** in current gain $\beta$ (a transistor labeled "$\beta = 100$" might actually be 80 or 120). A good bias circuit **isolates the Q-point** from these enemies, keeping $I_{CQ}$ and $V_{CEQ}$ rock-steady. ## 1.3 The Operating Point (Q-Point) $$Q\text{-point} = \{I_{BQ}, \; I_{CQ}, \; V_{CEQ}, \; I_{EQ}\}$$ These four DC quantities are linked by the transistor's fundamental DC equations: $$I_{CQ} = \beta_{DC} \cdot I_{BQ} + (\beta_{DC}+1)\,I_{CBO}$$ $$I_{EQ} = I_{CQ} + I_{BQ} \approx I_{CQ} \;\;\; \text{(since } I_{BQ} \ll I_{CQ}\text{)}$$ $$V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_{CQ}R_C - I_{EQ}R_E$$ > [!NOTE] > **Standard Engineering Constants used in BJT biasing analysis:** > - Thermal voltage: $V_T \approx \mathbf{26\;mV}$ (at $27^\circ C / 300\;K$) > - Base-emitter drop (silicon): $V_{BE} \approx \mathbf{0.7\;V}$ (active region) > - Leakage current (silicon, room temp): $I_{CBO} \approx \mathbf{nA}$ (often **neglected** unless thermal stability is the focus) > - Typical BJT $\beta$ range: $\mathbf{50 - 400}$ (so designs must tolerate wide $\beta$ spread). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Bias Circuit 1 — Fixed Bias (Base Bias) ### 2.1.1 Topology and Intuition A single resistor $R_B$ connects $V_{CC}$ directly to the base. The base current is therefore *fixed* by $V_{CC}$ and $R_B$ alone — hence the name. **No emitter resistor** is present. ``` VCC -----+--------+--------+ | | | | RC | | | | | C | | | | +---RB---+---B | | \ | | > NPN | / | | E | | | | | | +--------+---+ | | | +-------+----> Vout (taken from Collector) | GND ``` > [!NOTE] > The base "pulls" current from $V_{CC}$ through $R_B$. Because $I_B$ is set by **Ohm's law alone** (no feedback from collector/emitter), the circuit is the *simplest* but the *least stable*. ### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step DC Analysis (Fixed Bias) **Step 1 — Apply KVL around the Base–Emitter loop** (only $R_B$ and the B-E junction are in this loop, since the emitter is grounded): $$V_{CC} - I_B R_B - V_{BE} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; I_B = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the transistor current equation** to find $I_C$: $$I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta+1) I_{CBO} \approx \beta I_B \;\;\; \text{(if } I_{CBO}\text{ neglected)}$$ **Step 3 — Apply KVL around the Collector–Emitter loop:** $$V_{CC} - I_C R_C - V_{CE} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$$ ### 2.1.3 Stability of Fixed Bias — The Honest Truth If $\beta$ doubles (e.g., due to temperature or unit-to-unit variation), $I_C$ **also doubles**, dragging $V_{CE}$ down toward saturation. **Fixed bias has the worst thermal stability of all standard biasing methods.** This is why it is rarely used in production analog design but appears in textbooks because of its analytical simplicity. ## 2.2 Bias Circuit 2 — Voltage Divider Bias (Self-Bias / Emitter Bias) ### 2.2.1 Topology and Intuition Two resistors $R_1$ (upper) and $R_2$ (lower) form a voltage divider from $V_{CC}$ to ground. The mid-point feeds the base. An emitter resistor $R_E$ (often **bypassed** by a capacitor $C_E$ for AC gain) provides **DC negative feedback** that stabilizes the Q-point. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why it works:** If $I_C$ tries to increase (say, due to $\beta$ rising with temperature), the voltage drop across $R_E$ also increases, which *reduces* $V_{BE}$, which *reduces* $I_B$, which *opposes* the original $I_C$ increase. This self-correcting loop is called **DC negative feedback**. ### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step DC Analysis (Voltage Divider Bias) using Thevenin's Theorem **Step 1 — Replace the $R_1$–$R_2$ divider with its Thevenin equivalent** seen from the base: $$V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$ $$R_{TH} = R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$ **Step 2 — Apply KVL around the new Base–Emitter loop** (which now contains $R_{TH}$, the B-E junction, and $R_E$): $$V_{TH} - I_B R_{TH} - V_{BE} - I_E R_E = 0$$ Using $I_E = (\beta+1) I_B \approx \beta I_B$: $$I_B = \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E}$$ **Step 3 — Compute $I_C$, $I_E$, and $V_{CE}$:** $$I_C = \beta I_B, \quad I_E = (\beta+1) I_B \approx I_C$$ $$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E \approx V_{CC} - I_C (R_C + R_E)$$ ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table.** It contains every equation you will write in the exam hall for Module 1 biasing. | # | Quantity | Fixed Bias | Voltage Divider Bias | |---|----------|-----------|---------------------| | 1 | $V_{TH}$ (Thevenin voltage at base) | **Not applicable** ($V_{TH} = V_{CC}$, $R_{TH} = R_B$) | $V_{TH} = \dfrac{V_{CC} R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | | 2 | $R_{TH}$ (Thevenin resistance at base) | $R_B$ | $R_{TH} = R_1 \parallel R_2$ | | 3 | Base current $I_B$ | $I_B = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$ | $I_B = \dfrac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E}$ | | 4 | Collector current $I_C$ | $I_C = \beta I_B$ | $I_C = \beta I_B$ | | 5 | Collector-emitter voltage $V_{CE}$ | $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$ | $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E$ | | 6 | Stability factor $S$ (w.r.t. $I_{CBO}$) | $S = \beta + 1$ **(worst)** | $S = \dfrac{(\beta+1)\left(1 + R_{TH}/R_E\right)}{1 + \beta + R_{TH}/R_E}$ | | 7 | Approx. $S$ for $R_{TH} \ll \beta R_E$ | — | $S \approx 1 + \dfrac{R_{TH}}{R_E}$ | | 8 | Stability factor $S'$ (w.r.t. $V_{BE}$) | $S' = 0$ | $S' = \dfrac{-\beta}{(\beta+1)R_E + R_{TH}} \approx \dfrac{-1}{R_E}$ | | 9 | Stability factor $S''$ (w.r.t. $\beta$) | $S'' = \dfrac{I_{C1} I_{C2}}{\beta_1 I_{C2} - \beta_2 I_{C1}}$ (large) | $S'' \approx \dfrac{I_{C1} I_{C2}}{\beta_1 I_{C2} - \beta_2 I_{C1}}$ (smaller) | | 10 | Design rule of thumb (good stability) | — | $\beta R_E \ge 10\, R_{TH}$, equivalently $S \le \beta/10$ | | 11 | Self-bias condition (no $I_{CBO}$ drift) | $S = 1$ is **impossible** | Achieved when $R_{TH} \to 0$ and $R_E \to \infty$ (idealized) | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Note on $|x|$ in tables:** Throughout these notes, the absolute value notation uses $\lvert x \rvert$ with `\lvert` and `\rvert` LaTeX commands so that it does **not** break markdown table syntax. Never use a bare pipe symbol inside table cells. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Why Biasing Matters | |-------------|---------------------| | Audio amplifiers (Class A) | Stable Q-point prevents thermal runaway and crossover distortion | | RF front-ends | Bias drift detunes matching networks and degrades noise figure | | Operational amplifier input stages | Long-term DC offset stability depends on bias network symmetry | | Power regulators & references | Bandgap references rely on PTAT/CTAT biasing for temperature independence | | Mixed-signal ICs | Bias networks occupy $\sim 10\text{–}20\%$ of die area; a poor layout = poor PSRR | > [!TIP] > **Industry Note:** The two-transistor **current mirror** (a derivative of basic biasing) is the *single most important* analog sub-circuit — it biases virtually every stage inside an op-amp, a bandgap reference, and a bias generator. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Stability Factor $S$ for Voltage Divider Bias We start from the fundamental current equation of the BJT (with leakage term included): $$I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta+1) I_{CBO}$$ Differentiate **partially** with respect to $I_{CBO}$, treating $I_B$ as a function of $I_C$ (because $I_B$ depends on $V_{BE}$ and $V_{BE}$ depends on $I_C$ in the loop equation): $$S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CBO}}\bigg|_{V_{BE},\,\beta\text{ fixed}}$$ From the base–emitter loop KVL (Voltage Divider case): $$V_{TH} = I_B R_{TH} + V_{BE} + I_E R_E$$ $$V_{TH} = I_B R_{TH} + V_{BE} + (\beta+1) I_B R_E$$ Solving for $I_B$: $$I_B = \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E}$$ Differentiate $I_B$ w.r.t. $I_C$ (treating $V_{BE}$ as fixed for this partial derivative): $$\frac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_C} = -\frac{R_E \frac{\partial \beta}{\partial I_C} R_{TH} + \text{terms} \dots}{\text{kept simple, treat } \beta \text{ fixed}} \Rightarrow \frac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_C} = -\frac{R_E}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E}$$ *(In the full KTU derivation, one differentiates the relation $I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta+1)I_{CBO}$ implicitly w.r.t. $I_{CBO}$ and eliminates $\partial I_B / \partial I_{CBO}$ using the loop equation — see standard textbook treatment.)* The classical result after substitution is: $$\boxed{\,S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CBO}} = \frac{(\beta+1)\left(1 + R_{TH}/R_E\right)}{1 + \beta + R_{TH}/R_E}\,}$$ **Special-case checks:** - If $R_E = 0$ (degenerate to fixed-bias with $R_{TH} = R_B$): $S = \beta + 1$ ✓ - If $R_{TH} \ll \beta R_E$: $S \approx 1 + R_{TH}/R_E$ (small and ≈ 1 — *self-bias* limit) - If $R_{TH} \to 0$: $S \to 1$ (the *ideal* self-bias condition, never reached in practice) ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Fixed Bias Analysis > **Problem:** $V_{CC} = 12\;V$, $R_B = 400\;k\Omega$, $R_C = 2\;k\Omega$, $\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7\;V$. Find $I_B$, $I_C$, $V_{CE}$ and check the region of operation. **Step 1 — Base current:** $$I_B = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B} = \frac{12 - 0.7}{400 \times 10^3} = \frac{11.3}{4 \times 10^5} = 2.825 \times 10^{-5}\;A$$ $$\boxed{\,I_B = 28.25\;\mu A\,}$$ **Step 2 — Collector current:** $$I_C = \beta \cdot I_B = 100 \times 28.25\;\mu A = 2.825\;mA$$ **Step 3 — Collector-emitter voltage:** $$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C = 12 - (2.825 \times 10^{-3})(2 \times 10^3) = 12 - 5.65$$ $$\boxed{\,V_{CE} = 6.35\;V\,}$$ **Step 4 — Check region of operation:** - Saturation voltage $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2\;V$ - Since $V_{CE} = 6.35\;V \gg V_{CE(sat)}$, the transistor is firmly in the **active region** ✓ **Step 5 — Stability factor:** $$S = \beta + 1 = 101$$ This means a $1\;\mu A$ change in $I_{CBO}$ produces a $101\;\mu A$ change in $I_C$ — a 100× amplification of leakage drift. **Not stable.** ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Voltage Divider Bias Analysis > **Problem:** $V_{CC} = 20\;V$, $R_1 = 10\;k\Omega$, $R_2 = 5\;k\Omega$, $R_C = 2\;k\Omega$, $R_E = 1\;k\Omega$, $\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7\;V$. Find $I_B$, $I_C$, $V_{CE}$, $I_E$ and the stability factor $S$. **Step 1 — Thevenin voltage:** $$V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 20 \cdot \frac{5}{10 + 5} = 20 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 6.667\;V$$ **Step 2 — Thevenin resistance:** $$R_{TH} = R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{10 \times 5}{10 + 5} = \frac{50}{15} = 3.333\;k\Omega$$ **Step 3 — Base current:** $$I_B = \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E} = \frac{6.667 - 0.7}{3.333 + (101)(1)} = \frac{5.967}{3.333 + 101}$$ $$I_B = \frac{5.967}{104.333} = 0.0572\;mA = 57.2\;\mu A$$ **Step 4 — Collector and emitter currents:** $$I_C = \beta I_B = 100 \times 0.0572 = 5.72\;mA$$ $$I_E = (\beta+1) I_B = 101 \times 0.0572 = 5.776\;mA \approx 5.78\;mA$$ **Step 5 — Collector-emitter voltage:** $$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E = 20 - (5.72)(2) - (5.78)(1)$$ $$V_{CE} = 20 - 11.44 - 5.78 = 2.78\;V$$ $$\boxed{\,Q\text{-point} = (5.72\;mA,\; 2.78\;V)\,}$$ **Step 6 — Stability factor $S$:** Using the exact formula: $$S = \frac{(\beta+1)(1 + R_{TH}/R_E)}{1 + \beta + R_{TH}/R_E} = \frac{101 \times (1 + 3.333/1)}{1 + 100 + 3.333/1}$$ Wait — careful: the denominator uses $1 + \beta$ in many textbook treatments, but the **most common KTU convention** uses $\beta$ in the denominator constant (with the $\beta+1$ above absorbed). The cleanest canonical form is: $$S = \frac{(\beta+1)(R_E + R_{TH})}{R_E(\beta+1) + R_{TH}} = \frac{(101)(1 + 3.333)}{(101)(1) + 3.333} = \frac{101 \times 4.333}{104.333}$$ $$S = \frac{437.67}{104.333} \approx 4.195$$ Compare this to the fixed-bias case where $S = 101$ — voltage divider bias is **~24× more stable** for the same $\beta$. ✓ **Step 7 — Verify the design rule of thumb:** $$\beta R_E = 100 \times 1\;k\Omega = 100\;k\Omega$$ $$10\, R_{TH} = 10 \times 3.333 = 33.33\;k\Omega$$ $$\beta R_E = 100\;k\Omega \gg 33.33\;k\Omega \;\;\checkmark$$ The design satisfies the "$\beta R_E \ge 10 R_{TH}$" rule, confirming good stability. ## 3.4 Design Procedure — Voltage Divider Bias (Given Q-Point) > **Design problem:** Design a voltage divider bias circuit for $V_{CC} = 15\;V$, $I_{CQ} = 2\;mA$, $V_{CEQ} = 7\;V$, $\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7\;V$, and $V_E = 0.2\,V_{CC}$ (typical "10-V rule" for max AC swing). **Step 1 — Choose $V_E$:** $$V_E = 0.2 \times V_{CC} = 0.2 \times 15 = 3\;V$$ **Step 2 — Compute $R_E$:** $$R_E = \frac{V_E}{I_E} \approx \frac{V_E}{I_C} = \frac{3}{2\;mA} = 1.5\;k\Omega$$ **Step 3 — Compute $R_C$** using KVL on output loop ($V_{RC} = V_{CC} - V_{CE} - V_E$): $$V_{RC} = 15 - 7 - 3 = 5\;V$$ $$R_C = \frac{V_{RC}}{I_C} = \frac{5}{2\;mA} = 2.5\;k\Omega$$ **Step 4 — Compute $V_{TH}$ from the base loop:** $$V_B = V_E + V_{BE} = 3 + 0.7 = 3.7\;V$$ Using the rule $\beta R_E \ge 10 R_{TH}$ to ensure stability, solve for $R_{TH}$: $$R_{TH} \le \frac{\beta R_E}{10} = \frac{100 \times 1.5}{10} = 15\;k\Omega$$ Choose a safe value: $R_{TH} = 5\;k\Omega$ (well below 15 kΩ, so $S$ will be very small). **Step 5 — Compute $R_1$ and $R_2$ from $V_{TH}$ and $R_{TH}$:** From $V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$: $$\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{V_{TH}}{V_{CC}} = \frac{3.7}{15} = 0.2467$$ $$R_1 = R_{TH} \cdot \frac{V_{CC}}{V_{TH}} = 5 \times \frac{15}{3.7} = 20.27\;k\Omega$$ $$R_2 = R_{TH} \cdot \frac{V_{CC}}{V_{CC} - V_{TH}} = 5 \times \frac{15}{15 - 3.7} = 5 \times \frac{15}{11.3} = 6.64\;k\Omega$$ $$\boxed{\,R_1 \approx 20\;k\Omega,\; R_2 \approx 6.8\;k\Omega,\; R_C = 2.5\;k\Omega,\; R_E = 1.5\;k\Omega\,}$$ **Step 6 — Verify stability:** $$S = \frac{(\beta+1)(R_E + R_{TH})}{R_E(\beta+1) + R_{TH}} = \frac{101 \times (1.5 + 5)}{101 \times 1.5 + 5} = \frac{656.5}{156.5} \approx 4.19$$ Excellent — $S \approx 4.2$ means the collector current will vary only $\sim 4\times$ the $I_{CBO}$ variation, instead of $100\times$ in fixed bias. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Bias Circuit Decision Flow The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **decision logic and analytical path** a student must follow when solving any KTU biasing problem. It is *not* a literal circuit drawing (Mermaid cannot draw schematics), but it is the closest faithful top-down representation. ```mermaid flowchart TD StartA([KTU Exam: Given BJT biasing problem]) --> IdCirc{Identify

KVL on B-E loop] FB2 --> FB3[IC equals beta IB] FB3 --> FB4[VCE equals VCC minus IC RC] FB4 --> FB5[Check VCE greater than VCE sat

The following block diagram captures the geometric relationships used in design verification. ```mermaid flowchart TB AxisXY[Cartesian axes
