The Q-point must lie on this line, between saturation and cutoff. The midpoint (i.e., $V_{CEQ} \approx V_{CC}/2$) gives the **largest possible undistorted AC swing** — this is the "Class-A sweet spot" examiners love to test. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Define the stability factor $S$ of a BJT biasing circuit. Why is the voltage divider bias preferred over the fixed bias configuration in most amplifier applications?** **Model Answer:** The stability factor $S$ is defined as the **rate of change of collector current $I_C$ with respect to the reverse-saturation current $I_{CBO}$**, with $V_{BE}$ and $\beta$ held constant: $$S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CBO}}\bigg|_{V_{BE},\;\beta\text{ fixed}}$$ Physically, $S$ measures **how much the Q-point shifts when leakage current drifts** (e.g., with temperature). A small $S$ (close to 1) means a thermally stable Q-point; a large $S$ means a Q-point that wanders with temperature, leading to thermal runaway in extreme cases. **Why voltage divider bias is preferred:** | Parameter | Fixed Bias | Voltage Divider Bias | |-----------|-----------|---------------------| | $S$ | $\beta + 1$ (large, e.g. 101) | $\approx 1 + R_{TH}/R_E$ (e.g. 4) | | Thermal drift of $I_C$ | Severe | Mild | | $\beta$ sensitivity | Very high | Low | | Component count | 2 resistors | 3 resistors (or 4 with $R_E$) | | Application | Switching only | **All linear amplifiers** | Hence voltage divider bias is the **workhorse** of BJT amplifier design. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **For a fixed bias circuit, derive the expression for the Q-point $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ})$ in terms of $V_{CC}, R_B, R_C, \beta$ and $V_{BE}$. Mention its major drawback.** **Model Answer:** **Derivation:** Applying KVL to the base–emitter loop: $$V_{CC} - I_B R_B - V_{BE} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; I_B = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$$ Using $I_C = \beta I_B$: $$\boxed{\,I_{CQ} = \frac{\beta(V_{CC} - V_{BE})}{R_B}\,}$$ Applying KVL to the collector–emitter loop: $$V_{CC} - I_C R_C - V_{CE} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{\,V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C\,}$$ **Major drawback:** Since $I_{CQ}$ is **directly proportional to $\beta$**, any unit-to-unit variation in $\beta$ (typical spread $50$–$400$) causes a large, unpredictable shift in $I_{CQ}$ and hence $V_{CEQ}$. The stability factor $S = \beta + 1$ is large, and the circuit is **highly sensitive to temperature** and **$\beta$ variations** — making it unsuitable for linear amplifier design. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]` **For the voltage divider bias circuit shown (assumed): $V_{CC} = 18\;V$, $R_1 = 15\;k\Omega$, $R_2 = 5\;k\Omega$, $R_C = 2.2\;k\Omega$, $R_E = 1.2\;k\Omega$, $\beta = 120$, $V_{BE} = 0.7\;V$.** **(a) (7 Marks) Calculate the quiescent operating point $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ})$ and verify the region of operation.** **(b) (7 Marks) Calculate the stability factor $S$ and comment on the thermal stability of the circuit. Also find $S'$ w.r.t. $V_{BE}$.** --- #### Solution to A(a) **Step 1 — Thevenin voltage:** $$V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 18 \cdot \frac{5}{15 + 5} = 18 \cdot \frac{5}{20} = 4.5\;V$$ **Step 2 — Thevenin resistance:** $$R_{TH} = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{15 \times 5}{20} = 3.75\;k\Omega$$ **Step 3 — Base current:** $$I_B = \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E} = \frac{4.5 - 0.7}{3.75 + (121)(1.2)}$$ $$I_B = \frac{3.8}{3.75 + 145.2} = \frac{3.8}{148.95} = 0.02551\;mA = 25.51\;\mu A$$ **Step 4 — Collector and emitter currents:** $$I_C = \beta I_B = 120 \times 0.02551 = 3.061\;mA$$ $$I_E = (\beta+1) I_B = 121 \times 0.02551 = 3.087\;mA$$ **Step 5 — Collector-emitter voltage:** $$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E$$ $$= 18 - (3.061)(2.2) - (3.087)(1.2)$$ $$= 18 - 6.734 - 3.704 = 7.562\;V$$ $$\boxed{\,Q\text{-point} = (I_{CQ} = 3.06\;mA,\; V_{CEQ} = 7.56\;V)\,}$$ **Step 6 — Region of operation:** Since $V_{CEQ} = 7.56\;V \gg V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2\;V$, the transistor is in the **active region**. ✓ --- #### Solution to A(b) **Step 1 — Stability factor $S$:** $$S = \frac{(\beta+1)(R_E + R_{TH})}{R_E(\beta+1) + R_{TH}} = \frac{121 \times (1.2 + 3.75)}{1.2 \times 121 + 3.75}$$ $$S = \frac{121 \times 4.95}{145.2 + 3.75} = \frac{598.95}{148.95} = 4.022$$ **Step 2 — Stability factor $S'$ w.r.t. $V_{BE}$:** $$S' = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial V_{BE}} = \frac{-\beta}{(\beta+1) R_E + R_{TH}} = \frac{-120}{148.95}$$ $$S' = -0.806\;mA/V$$ **Step 3 — Comment on thermal stability:** - $S \approx 4.02$ is **very small** compared to fixed bias ($S = 121$). A $1\;\mu A$ rise in $I_{CBO}$ causes only a $4\;\mu A$ rise in $I_C$ — an **~30× improvement** in stability. - The design rule $\beta R_E = 120 \times 1.2 = 144\;k\Omega \ge 10 R_{TH} = 37.5\;k\Omega$ is satisfied, confirming **good bias design**. - $S'$ is small in magnitude, indicating the Q-point is also robust against $V_{BE}$ variations caused by temperature. - **Verdict:** The circuit is **thermally stable** and suitable for linear amplifier applications. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]` **For a self-bias (voltage divider) circuit, derive the expression for stability factor $S = \partial I_C / \partial I_{CBO}$ and show that it reduces to $(\beta+1)$ when $R_E = 0$.** **(a) (7 Marks) Derive the stability factor $S$ for a voltage divider bias circuit from first principles, using the relation $I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta+1) I_{CBO}$.** **(b) (7 Marks) Hence obtain the limiting form of $S$ when (i) $R_E = 0$ and (ii) $R_{TH}/R_E \to 0$. Comment on the practical implications.** --- #### Solution to B(a) — Full Derivation **Step 1 — Start with the BJT current equation:** $$I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta+1) I_{CBO} \quad \text{...(1)}$$ **Step 2 — KVL on the base–emitter loop** (after Thevenin reduction): $$V_{TH} = I_B R_{TH} + V_{BE} + I_E R_E \quad \text{...(2)}$$ Using $I_E = I_C + I_B = (\beta+1) I_B + I_{CBO}$ — *note: this leakage-aware form*: $$V_{TH} = I_B R_{TH} + V_{BE} + R_E \left[(\beta+1) I_B + I_{CBO}\right] \quad \text{...(3)}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $I_B$:** $$I_B = \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE} - R_E I_{CBO}}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E} \quad \text{...(4)}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute (4) into (1):** $$I_C = \beta \cdot \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE} - R_E I_{CBO}}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E} + (\beta+1) I_{CBO}$$ **Step 5 — Take partial derivative** w.r.t. $I_{CBO}$ with $V_{BE}$ and $\beta$ held constant: $$S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CBO}} = \frac{-\beta R_E}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E} + (\beta+1)$$ **Step 6 — Combine over a common denominator:** $$S = \frac{-\beta R_E + (\beta+1)[R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E]}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E}$$ $$S = \frac{(\beta+1) R_{TH} + [(\beta+1)^2 - \beta] R_E}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E}$$ Using $(\beta+1)^2 - \beta = \beta^2 + 2\beta + 1 - \beta = \beta^2 + \beta + 1 \approx \beta(\beta+1)$ for large $\beta$: $$\boxed{\,S = \frac{(\beta+1)(R_E + R_{TH})}{R_E(\beta+1) + R_{TH}}\,}$$ --- #### Solution to B(b) — Limiting Cases **Case (i): $R_E = 0$** Substituting $R_E = 0$: $$S = \frac{(\beta+1)(0 + R_{TH})}{0 + R_{TH}} = \beta + 1$$ This is exactly the **fixed-bias stability factor** — confirming that voltage divider bias with the emitter resistor *shorted out* degenerates into a fixed-bias circuit. ✓ **Case (ii): $R_{TH}/R_E \to 0$** (equivalently $\beta R_E \gg R_{TH}$) $$S = \frac{(\beta+1)(R_E + R_{TH})}{R_E(\beta+1) + R_{TH}} \;\xrightarrow{R_{TH}\to 0}\; \frac{(\beta+1) R_E}{(\beta+1) R_E} = 1$$ This is the **ideal self-bias limit** — the collector current is *completely independent* of $I_{CBO}$. In practice, $S$ is between 1 and $\beta+1$, with $S \le \beta/10$ being the rule-of-thumb target for well-designed amplifiers. **Practical implications:** | Limit | Engineering meaning | |-------|--------------------| | $S = \beta+1$ ($R_E = 0$) | No DC feedback → Q-point drifts maximally with temperature. **Never use this configuration in analog stages.** | | $S \to 1$ ($R_{TH} \ll \beta R_E$) | Strong DC negative feedback → near-ideal Q-point stability. **The goal of every well-designed bias network.** | | Typical $S \approx 4$–$10$ | Achievable with sensible component ratios — the **practical sweet spot** for production designs. | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in BJT biasing problems (Module 1):** > 1. **Forgetting to use $\beta+1$ instead of $\beta$ in the $I_B$ denominator** when computing the base current. The denominator of $I_B$ is $R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E$, **not** $R_{TH} + \beta R_E$. This is a 1–2 mark error. > 2. **Using $I_C$ instead of $I_E$ in the $V_{CE}$ equation.** The correct form is $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E$. Using $I_C$ in place of $I_E$ gives a small but visible error. > 3. **Not verifying the region of operation.** Examiners award marks for showing $V_{CE} > V_{CE(sat)}$ and $V_{CE} < V_{CC}$. Always include a one-line region-of-operation check. > 4. **Mixing up $R_B$ and $R_{TH}$.** In voltage divider bias, $R_{TH} = R_1 \parallel R_2$, **not** $R_1 + R_2$. > 5. **Neglecting to compute the stability factor $S$ when the question says "comment on stability."** $S$ alone (numerically) is worth 2–3 marks in a 14-mark question. > 6. **Rounding too early.** Keep at least **4 significant figures** in intermediate steps; only round the final answer to 3. > 7. **Sign errors in the KVL loop.** When you write $V_{CC} - I_B R_B - V_{BE} - I_E R_E = 0$, every term must have the correct sign. A single sign flip cascades into all subsequent answers being wrong. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "BJT Biasing (CE) — Fixed & Voltage Divider"** - ☐ Fixed bias: $I_B = (V_{CC} - V_{BE})/R_B$, $I_C = \beta I_B$, $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$. - ☐ Fixed bias stability: $S = \beta + 1$ → **very poor**; used mostly in switching. - ☐ Voltage divider: always reduce to **Thevenin** ($V_{TH}, R_{TH}$) before writing the base loop. - ☐ Voltage divider: $I_B = (V_{TH} - V_{BE})/[R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E]$ — denominator uses **$\beta+1$**. - ☐ Voltage divider: $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E$, not just $V_{CC} - I_C(R_C + R_E)$ (small difference, but examiners check). - ☐ Voltage divider stability: $S \approx 1 + R_{TH}/R_E$ when $\beta R_E \gg R_{TH}$ — **small and good**. - ☐ **Design rule of thumb:** $\beta R_E \ge 10 R_{TH}$ (equivalently $S \le \beta/10$). - ☐ **Q-point midpoint rule** for max AC swing: $V_{CEQ} \approx V_{CC}/2$. - ☐ Three stability factors exist: $S$ (w.r.t. $I_{CBO}$), $S'$ (w.r.t. $V_{BE}$), $S''$ (w.r.t. $\beta$). - ☐ Fixed bias: $S' = 0$ (no $V_{BE}$ dependence in $I_B$ formula). - ☐ Always **verify region of operation** (active, saturation, or cutoff) — examiners check. - ☐ For design: choose $V_E \approx 0.2 V_{CC}$, $V_{CEQ} \approx 0.5 V_{CC}$, then solve for $R_E, R_C, R_1, R_2$. - ☐ **Industry reality:** Voltage divider bias is the *default* in discrete BJT amplifier design; fixed bias survives mainly in digital/switching circuits. - ☐ Internal choice in Part B: **always answer both Question A and Question B in rough work** so you can switch seamlessly if your first choice turns out harder than expected.

topics.title: MOSFET Biasing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** MOSFET Biasing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MOSFET Biasing** is the deliberate application of DC voltages and currents to the terminals of a Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor (MOSFET) so as to establish a stable, predefined **Quiescent Operating Point (Q-point)** — defined by the pair $(I_{DQ}, V_{DSQ})$ — in the **saturation (active) region** of the device, independent of variations in device parameters, temperature, and supply voltage.

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, biasing is a foundational pre-requisite for every analog amplifier stage. The MOSFET is biased so that the AC input signal can be amplified linearly about the Q-point without the transistor entering the cutoff or triode (ohmic) regions during signal swings.

The four DC quantities that completely define the bias condition of an n-channel enhancement MOSFET (the most common KTU exam scenario) are:

| DC Quantity | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Gate-to-Source Voltage | $V_{GS}$ | $V_t < V_{GS}$ |
| Drain Current | $I_D$ | mA range |
| Drain-to-Source Voltage | $V_{DS}$ | Few volts |
| Source Voltage | $V_S$ | Reference |

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold Voltage ($V_t$)** is a positive value (typically **1 V to 3 V**) for an n-channel enhancement MOSFET. For conduction, $V_{GS}$ must exceed $V_t$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model

Imagine a **pressurized water tap** connected to a pipe with a flexible nozzle:

- **$V_{GS}$ (Gate-to-Source Voltage)** is like the **pressure you apply with your hand** on the tap handle. If you press too softly, no water flows (cutoff region). If you press reasonably, water flows in a controlled stream (saturation region). If you press so hard that the handle hits the bottom (triode/ohmic region), the flow becomes uncontrolled.
- **$I_D$ (Drain Current)** is the **rate of water flow** out of the tap.
- **$V_{DS}$ (Drain-to-Source Voltage)** is the **pressure of the incoming water main** (V_DD).
- **Biasing** is the act of setting your hand at the *right pressure* (Q-point) so that small variations in hand pressure (the AC input signal) produce clean, proportional changes in water flow (the amplified AC output).

**Why not just connect the gate directly to V_DD?** If you push the handle to the maximum, the water flow becomes a function of every tiny change in main pressure — it is unstable. Biasing creates a *stable resting position* on the curve, and the small AC signal rides on top of that.

## 1.3 The Fundamental Bias Condition

For an n-channel enhancement MOSFET to act as a linear amplifier, the Q-point **must** satisfy:

$$V_{GS} > V_t \quad \text{(MOSFET is ON)}$$

$$V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_t \quad \text{(Saturation / Active region)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The quantity $V_{GS} - V_t$ is called the **Effective Voltage** or **Overdrive Voltage ($V_{OV}$)**. Thus saturation requires $V_{DS} \geq V_{OV}$. This is one of the most heavily tested relations in KTU board examinations.

## 1.4 Why Biasing Is Needed — Three Critical Reasons

1. **Avoid Distortion in the AC Output:** Without a proper Q-point, a sinusoidal input may push the MOSFET into cutoff (clipped bottom) or triode (clipped top), producing a distorted output.
2. **Temperature & Parameter Independence:** MOSFET parameters ($V_t$, $K_n$, $\mu_n C_{ox}$) vary with temperature. A well-designed bias circuit *desensitizes* the Q-point from these variations.
3. **Predictable Small-Signal Gain:** The small-signal transconductance $g_m = 2K_n(V_{GSQ} - V_t) = 2\sqrt{K_n I_{DQ}}$ depends on the Q-point. A stable Q-point guarantees a stable, predictable voltage gain $A_v = -g_m R_D$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The DC Drain Current Equation (Saturation Region)

For an n-channel enhancement MOSFET operating in saturation, the drain current is governed by the **square-law equation**:

$$I_D = K_n (V_{GS} - V_t)^2 = K_n \cdot V_{OV}^2$$

where the conduction parameter $K_n$ is defined as:

$$K_n = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)$$

**Units of $K_n$:** $\text{A/V}^2$ (or mA/V² in practical circuits). The aspect ratio $(W/L)$ is the designer's control knob.

> [!NOTE]
> Some textbooks (and even KTU question papers) write the equation as $I_D = \frac{K_n}{2}(V_{GS} - V_t)^2$, where $K_n$ already includes the factor of ½. **Always check the definition of $K_n$ given in the question paper** — this is a classic source of 1-mark loss in the KTU valuation key.

## 2.2 The Four Standard MOSFET Biasing Topologies

### Topology 1 — Fixed Bias (Gate Bias)

**Circuit Concept:** A single DC supply $V_{GG}$ is applied directly to the gate through a resistor $R_G$. The source is grounded.

**Operating Principle:** Since the gate draws **zero DC current** (MOSFET input is an open circuit), there is no voltage drop across $R_G$. Therefore:

$$V_{GS} = V_{GG}$$

The drain current is then computed directly from the square law:

$$I_{DQ} = K_n (V_{GG} - V_t)^2$$

The drain-to-source voltage follows from KVL around the drain-source loop:

$$V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ} R_D$$

**Critical Evaluation:** This is the **worst possible biasing scheme**. Since $I_{DQ}$ depends purely on $V_{GG}$ and the device parameter $K_n$, any change in $K_n$ (due to temperature or manufacturing tolerances) directly changes $I_{DQ}$. The stability factor $S = \partial I_D / \partial I_D = 1$ is at its theoretical worst.

### Topology 2 — Self-Bias (Source Bias)

**Circuit Concept:** The gate is grounded through a large resistor $R_G$ (typically 1 MΩ to 10 MΩ to keep input impedance high). The source is connected to ground through $R_S$.

**Operating Principle:** Since gate current is zero, $V_G = 0$. KVL from gate to source gives:

$$V_{GS} = V_G - V_S = 0 - I_D R_S = -I_D R_S$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $V_{GS}$ is **negative** in self-bias! This means the circuit only works properly for depletion-type MOSFETs (which can operate with $V_{GS} < 0$) or for very small $V_{GG}$ configurations. For enhancement MOSFETs, a modified version with a positive $V_{GG}$ is required.

Substituting into the square law:

$$I_D = K_n (-I_D R_S - V_t)^2$$

This is a **quadratic equation in $I_D$** which must be solved for the physically meaningful (positive) root. Expanding:

$$I_D = K_n (I_D R_S + V_t)^2 = K_n (I_D^2 R_S^2 + 2 I_D R_S V_t + V_t^2)$$

$$K_n R_S^2 I_D^2 + (2 K_n R_S V_t - 1) I_D + K_n V_t^2 = 0$$

$$I_D = \frac{1 - 2 K_n R_S V_t \pm \sqrt{(1 - 2 K_n R_S V_t)^2 - 4 K_n^2 R_S^2 V_t^2}}{2 K_n R_S^2}$$

The plus sign in front of the square root gives the valid operating point.

**Drain-to-Source Voltage:**

$$V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_D (R_D + R_S)$$

### Topology 3 — Voltage Divider Bias (Most Important for KTU)

**Circuit Concept:** Two resistors $R_1$ (top) and $R_2$ (bottom) form a voltage divider from $V_{DD}$ to ground. The midpoint drives the gate through a high-value $R_G$. The source is grounded through $R_S$ (often bypassed by a capacitor $C_S$ for AC, but for DC analysis $C_S$ is an open).

**Operating Principle:** Because $R_G$ carries zero DC gate current, the gate voltage is set purely by the unloaded voltage divider:

$$V_G = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

KVL from gate to source:

$$V_{GS} = V_G - I_D R_S = \left( \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \right) V_{DD} - I_D R_S$$

> [!NOTE]
> This equation is the **single most-tested relationship** in KTU 2024 scheme MOSFET biasing problems. The minus sign in front of $I_D R_S$ is the source of *negative feedback* that stabilizes the Q-point.

The drain current is found by substituting $V_{GS}$ into the square law:

$$I_D = K_n \left( \frac{R_2 V_{DD}}{R_1 + R_2} - I_D R_S - V_t \right)^2$$

This again yields a quadratic in $I_D$:

$$K_n R_S^2 I_D^2 + \left(2 K_n R_S V_t + 2 K_n R_S \cdot \frac{R_2 V_{DD}}{R_1 + R_2} - 1 \right) I_D + K_n \left( \frac{R_2 V_{DD}}{R_1 + R_2} - V_t \right)^2 = 0$$

**Drain-to-Source Voltage:**

$$V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_D (R_D + R_S)$$

**Why this is the best topology:**
- If $I_D$ tries to increase (due to temperature rise or device variation), $V_S = I_D R_S$ rises, which reduces $V_{GS}$, which in turn reduces $I_D$. This is **negative feedback** — the heart of stable biasing.
- The stability factor approaches zero as $R_S$ becomes large.

### Topology 4 — Drain-Feedback (Drain-to-Gate) Bias

**Circuit Concept:** The gate is connected to the drain through a feedback resistor $R_G$.

**Operating Principle:** Zero gate current means zero drop across $R_G$, so:

$$V_G = V_D \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{GS} = V_D - V_S = V_{DS}$$

Thus the constraint $V_{GS} = V_{DS}$ reduces the two-equation system to one. KVL around the outer loop:

$$V_{DD} = I_D R_D + V_{DS} + I_D R_S = I_D (R_D + R_S) + V_{GS}$$

This gives a linear relation between $V_{GS}$ and $I_D$:

$$V_{GS} = V_{DD} - I_D (R_D + R_S)$$

Combined with $I_D = K_n (V_{GS} - V_t)^2$:

$$I_D = K_n \left[ V_{DD} - I_D (R_D + R_S) - V_t \right]^2$$

This is again a quadratic in $I_D$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield, Board-Exam Critical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE MASTER FORMULA TABLE** — Memorize these before walking into the KTU exam hall. Every MOSFET biasing problem reduces to one of these cases.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Valid When | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Saturation Drain Current | $I_D = K_n (V_{GS} - V_t)^2$ | $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_t$ | A or mA |
| 2 | Overdrive Voltage | $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_t$ | MOSFET is ON | V |
| 3 | Conduction Parameter | $K_n = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)$ | Always | A/V² |
| 4 | Fixed Bias: $V_{GS}$ | $V_{GS} = V_{GG}$ | Always | V |
| 5 | Self-Bias: $V_{GS}$ | $V_{GS} = -I_D R_S$ | Always | V |
| 6 | Voltage Divider: $V_G$ | $V_G = V_{DD} \cdot R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$ | $R_G$ no current | V |
| 7 | Voltage Divider: $V_{GS}$ | $V_{GS} = V_G - I_D R_S$ | Always | V |
| 8 | Drain-Feedback: $V_{GS}$ | $V_{GS} = V_{DS}$ | Zero $I_G$ | V |
| 9 | DC Load Line | $V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D (R_D + R_S)$ | Any bias topology | V |
| 10 | Boundary of Saturation | $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_t = V_{OV}$ | Edge of saturation | V |

> [!WARNING]
> **Equation of the DC Load Line:** $V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D (R_D + R_S)$. The line passes through $(I_D = 0, V_{DS} = V_{DD})$ on the vertical axis and $(I_D = V_{DD}/(R_D + R_S), V_{DS} = 0)$ on the horizontal axis. The Q-point is the *intersection* of this load line with the transistor's $I_D$ vs $V_{GS}$ curve (or equivalently, the curve derived from the square law).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Audio Amplifiers** | Voltage divider bias places Q-point in middle of load line to maximize symmetric swing for music signals. |
| **RF Power Amplifiers** | Drain-feedback bias is used in radio-frequency power MOSFETs to obtain stable bias with fewer components. |
| **Analog IC Design** | The "current-mirror bias" (an advanced voltage divider variant) is used in nearly every op-amp to bias thousands of transistors identically. |
| **Digital CMOS Logic** | In digital circuits, the MOSFET is biased as a switch (between cutoff and triode) — but the analog designer must still understand the bias boundary. |
| **Temperature Sensors** | The deliberate variation of $V_{GS}$ with $I_D$ in self-bias is exploited to build on-chip temperature sensors. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Square-Law $I_D$ Equation from Physics

The drain current in a MOSFET can be derived from first principles by considering the channel charge. For a long-channel n-MOSFET in saturation, the gradual-channel approximation gives:

$$I_D = \frac{W}{L} \mu_n C_{ox} \int_{V_t}^{V_{GS}} (V - V_t) \, dV$$

where $V$ is the local channel potential varying from $V_t$ at the source end to $V_{GS}$ at the drain end (in saturation, the channel is pinched off, so the integration limit at the drain is replaced by $V_{GS}$).

**Step 1 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$I_D = \frac{W \mu_n C_{ox}}{L} \left[ \frac{(V - V_t)^2}{2} \right]_{V_t}^{V_{GS}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the limits:**

$$I_D = \frac{W \mu_n C_{ox}}{L} \cdot \frac{(V_{GS} - V_t)^2 - (V_t - V_t)^2}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$I_D = \frac{W \mu_n C_{ox}}{2 L} (V_{GS} - V_t)^2$$

**Step 4 — Define the conduction parameter:**

$$\boxed{K_n \equiv \frac{W \mu_n C_{ox}}{2L} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)}$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form:**

$$I_D = K_n (V_{GS} - V_t)^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is fundamental. KTU examiners may ask: "Derive the expression for drain current in saturation." The five steps above are the model answer.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Voltage Divider Bias (Full Q-Point Calculation)

> **Given:** n-channel enhancement MOSFET with $K_n = 0.5 \text{ mA/V}^2$ and $V_t = 2 \text{ V}$. The circuit has $V_{DD} = 12 \text{ V}$, $R_1 = 1 \text{ M}\Omega$, $R_2 = 500 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_S = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_D = 4 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**Goal:** Find the Q-point $(I_{DQ}, V_{DSQ})$ and verify saturation.

---

### Step 1 — Compute the gate voltage $V_G$

Since $R_G$ carries no current, the divider is unloaded:

$$V_G = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$V_G = 12 \cdot \frac{500 \text{ k}}{1000 \text{ k} + 500 \text{ k}} = 12 \cdot \frac{500}{1500} = 12 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 4 \text{ V}$$

### Step 2 — Write the $V_{GS}$ equation

$$V_{GS} = V_G - I_D R_S = 4 - I_D \cdot 2 \text{ k}$$

Let $I_D$ be in mA and $R_S$ in kΩ, so $I_D R_S$ is in volts. Therefore:

$$V_{GS} = 4 - 2 I_D \quad \text{...(units: V, with $I_D$ in mA)}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute into the square law

$$I_D = K_n (V_{GS} - V_t)^2 = 0.5 \cdot (4 - 2 I_D - 2)^2 = 0.5 \cdot (2 - 2 I_D)^2$$

$$I_D = 0.5 \cdot 4 (1 - I_D)^2 = 2 (1 - I_D)^2$$

### Step 4 — Expand and form the quadratic

$$I_D = 2 (1 - 2 I_D + I_D^2) = 2 - 4 I_D + 2 I_D^2$$

Rearranging all terms to one side:

$$2 I_D^2 - 5 I_D + 2 = 0$$

### Step 5 — Solve the quadratic

Using the quadratic formula $I_D = \frac{5 \pm \sqrt{25 - 16}}{4} = \frac{5 \pm 3}{4}$:

$$I_D = \frac{5 + 3}{4} = 2 \text{ mA} \quad \text{or} \quad I_D = \frac{5 - 3}{4} = 0.5 \text{ mA}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Root Selection Rule:** Choose the root such that $V_{GS} > V_t$ and $V_{GS}$ is reasonable. For $I_D = 2$ mA: $V_{GS} = 4 - 2(2) = 0$ V, which is less than $V_t = 2$ V. **Reject — MOSFET is OFF.** For $I_D = 0.5$ mA: $V_{GS} = 4 - 2(0.5) = 3$ V, which is greater than $V_t = 2$ V. **Accept.** $V_{OV} = 1$ V.

$$\boxed{I_{DQ} = 0.5 \text{ mA}, \quad V_{GSQ} = 3 \text{ V}}$$

### Step 6 — Compute the drain-to-source voltage

$$V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ} (R_D + R_S) = 12 - 0.5 (4 + 2) = 12 - 3 = 9 \text{ V}$$

### Step 7 — Verify saturation

$$V_{DS,sat} = V_{GSQ} - V_t = 3 - 2 = 1 \text{ V}$$

Since $V_{DSQ} = 9 \text{ V} \geq V_{DS,sat} = 1 \text{ V}$, the MOSFET is **firmly in saturation**. ✅

$$\boxed{\text{Q-point: } (I_{DQ}, V_{DSQ}) = (0.5 \text{ mA}, 9 \text{ V})}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Biasing Topologies

| Property | Fixed Bias | Self-Bias | Voltage Divider | Drain-Feedback |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{GS}$ determined by | External $V_{GG}$ | $-I_D R_S$ | $V_G - I_D R_S$ | Equals $V_{DS}$ |
| Negative feedback | None | Yes (via $R_S$) | Yes (via $R_S$) | Yes (via $R_G$) |
| Stability vs $K_n$ | **Poor** | Moderate | **Excellent** | Good |
| Component count | 2 | 2 | 4 | 3 |
| Preferred for | Switches, references | Depletion MOSFETs | **General-purpose amp** | RF power stages |
| KTU exam frequency | Medium | Medium | **Highest** | Medium |
| Independent of $V_{DD}$? | No (needs $V_{GG}$) | No | Partially (only $V_G$ depends) | No |

## 3.4 Derivation: Stability Factor $S$ for Voltage Divider Bias

The **stability factor** $S_K = \dfrac{\partial I_D / I_D}{\partial K_n / K_n}$ measures how much $I_D$ changes for a fractional change in $K_n$.

Starting from the bias equation:

$$I_D = K_n \left( \frac{R_2 V_{DD}}{R_1 + R_2} - I_D R_S - V_t \right)^2$$

Let $V_{G,th} = \dfrac{R_2 V_{DD}}{R_1 + R_2}$. Define $f = V_{G,th} - I_D R_S - V_t$. Then $I_D = K_n f^2$.

Differentiating with respect to $K_n$ (treating $f$ as depending on $I_D$):

$$\frac{\partial I_D}{\partial K_n} = f^2 + K_n \cdot 2f \cdot \left(-R_S \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial K_n}\right)$$

$$\frac{\partial I_D}{\partial K_n} \left(1 + 2 K_n f R_S \right) = f^2$$

$$\frac{\partial I_D}{\partial K_n} = \frac{f^2}{1 + 2 K_n f R_S}$$

Dividing both sides by $I_D / K_n$:

$$S_K = \frac{K_n}{I_D} \cdot \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial K_n} = \frac{K_n f^2}{I_D (1 + 2 K_n f R_S)} = \frac{I_D / K_n}{I_D (1 + 2 K_n f R_S)}$$

Using $I_D = K_n f^2$ so $K_n f^2 / I_D = 1$:

$$S_K = \frac{1}{1 + 2 K_n f R_S}$$

Since $f = V_{G,th} - I_D R_S - V_t = V_{GS} - V_t = V_{OV}$, and $g_m = 2 K_n V_{OV}$:

$$\boxed{S_K = \frac{1}{1 + g_m R_S}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** As $R_S \to \infty$, $S_K \to 0$. The Q-point becomes completely independent of $K_n$. This is the principle behind the *constant-current bias* (current source) used in IC design, where $R_S$ is replaced by a transistor current source of infinite small-signal output resistance.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — MOSFET Biasing Decision Tree

The following Mermaid flowchart describes the analytical process for solving any MOSFET biasing problem that a KTU examiner may pose.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Circuit and Parameters] --> B{Identify Bias Topology}
    B --> C1[Fixed Bias: V_GS = V_GG]
    B --> C2[Self-Bias: V_GS = -I_D R_S]
    B --> C3[Voltage Divider: V_G from divider]
    B --> C4[Drain-Feedback: V_GS = V_DS]

    C1 --> D1[Compute I_D from Square Law]
    C2 --> D2[Form Quadratic in I_D]
    C3 --> D3[Compute V_G, then V_GS = V_G - I_D R_S]
    C4 --> D4[Use V_GS = V_DS constraint]

    D1 --> E1[Compute V_DS = V_DD - I_D R_D]
    D2 --> E2[Compute V_DS = V_DD - I_D R_D + R_S]
    D3 --> E3[Compute V_DS = V_DD - I_D R_D + R_S]
    D4 --> E4[Compute V_DS from KVL]

    E1 --> F[Check Saturation: V_DS >= V_GS - V_t]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F

    F --> G{Saturation?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Q-Point Valid]
    G -->|No| I[Recompute in Triode or Adjust Design]

    H --> J[Report Q-Point: I_DQ, V_DSQ, V_GSQ]
    I --> K[Redesign: Increase R_D or V_DD]
    K --> A
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Voltage Divider Bias Functional Modules

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SUPPLY[DC Power Domain]
        VDD[V_DD Supply Rail]
    end

    subgraph DIV[Voltage Divider Network]
        R1[Resistor R1]:::resistor
        R2[Resistor R2]:::resistor
    end

    subgraph GATE[Gate Driving Stage]
        RG[Resistor R_G High Value]:::resistor
    end

    subgraph MOSFET[Transistor Core]
        G[Gate]:::node
        D[Drain]:::node
        S[Source]:::node
    end

    subgraph DRAIN[Drain Load]
        RD[Resistor R_D]:::resistor
    end

    subgraph SOURCE[Source Degeneration]
        RS[Resistor R_S]:::resistor
    end

    VDD --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> GND1[Ground]
    R2 --> RG
    RG --> G
    VDD --> RD
    RD --> D
    S --> RS
    RS --> GND2[Ground]
    G --> S

    classDef resistor fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — DC Load Line Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Load Line Equation] --> B["V_DS = V_DD - I_D * R_D + R_S"]
    B --> C[End Point 1: I_D = 0]
    B --> D[End Point 2: V_DS = 0]
    C --> E1[Point A on V_DS axis: 0, V_DD]
    D --> E2[Point B on I_D axis: V_DD / R_D+R_S, 0]
    E1 --> F[DC Load Line Segment AB]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Transistor I_D vs V_GS curve]
    G --> H[Intersection defines Q-point]
    H --> I[Symmetric placement of Q at midpoint<br>maximizes AC swing]
```

## 4.4 Signal-Flow Block — Bias Stabilization Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Perturbation: Temperature rises] --> Q[K_n increases]
    Q --> R[I_D tends to increase]
    R --> S[Source voltage V_S = I_D R_S increases]
    S --> T[V_GS = V_G - V_S decreases]
    T --> U[I_D pushed back down]
    U --> V[Q-point restored]
    V -.->|Negative Feedback| P

    style P fill:#FFB6C1
    style V fill:#90EE90
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagram is the **negative feedback loop** responsible for bias stability in the voltage divider configuration. This is the single most important conceptual diagram to draw if the KTU question asks: "Explain why voltage divider bias is more stable than fixed bias."
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Define the Quiescent Operating Point (Q-point) of a MOSFET. Why is it necessary to bias a MOSFET in the saturation region for linear amplification?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Q-Point Definition (1 Mark):** The Q-point is the DC operating point of the MOSFET, defined by the DC drain current $I_{DQ}$ and the DC drain-to-source voltage $V_{DSQ}$, in the absence of any input AC signal.
> **Necessity of Saturation Bias (2 Marks):** In the saturation region, the drain current $I_D$ is nearly independent of $V_{DS}$ and is controlled only by $V_{GS}$ via the square law $I_D = K_n(V_{GS} - V_t)^2$. This makes the small-signal transconductance $g_m = 2K_n(V_{GSQ} - V_t)$ a well-defined, constant value, which produces linear amplification. If biased in the triode region, $I_D$ becomes a strong function of $V_{DS}$ as well, causing the output to depend nonlinearly on the input, leading to severe signal distortion.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Explain the role of the source resistor $R_S$ in a voltage divider biased MOSFET amplifier.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Role 1 — Sets DC Bias (1 Mark):** The source resistor $R_S$ develops a DC voltage $V_S = I_{DQ} R_S$ that subtracts from the gate voltage $V_G$ to establish the gate-to-source bias voltage $V_{GSQ} = V_G - I_{DQ} R_S$.
> **Role 2 — Negative Feedback for Stability (2 Marks):** If $I_D$ tries to increase (due to a rise in temperature or in $K_n$), $V_S$ increases, which reduces $V_{GS}$, which in turn reduces $I_D$. This automatic correction is called *negative feedback* and is quantified by the stability factor $S_K = 1/(1 + g_m R_S)$, which approaches zero for large $R_S$. Thus $R_S$ desensitizes the Q-point from device parameter variations.

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 scheme regulation, each Part B question carries **14 marks** with a typical split of **7 + 7 marks** across two sub-parts. Two independent choices are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

For the voltage divider bias circuit shown, the n-channel enhancement MOSFET has $K_n = 0.25 \text{ mA/V}^2$ and threshold voltage $V_t = 1.5 \text{ V}$.

**Circuit Parameters:** $V_{DD} = 15 \text{ V}$, $R_1 = 2 \text{ M}\Omega$, $R_2 = 1 \text{ M}\Omega$, $R_D = 6 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_S = 3 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**(a)** Derive the expression for the gate-to-source voltage $V_{GS}$ in terms of circuit parameters. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the Q-point $(I_{DQ}, V_{DSQ})$ and verify that the MOSFET operates in the saturation region. **(7 marks)**

**(c)** Compute the small-signal transconductance $g_m$ of the MOSFET at the Q-point. **(4 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation of $V_{GS}$ (3 marks):**

Since the gate of the MOSFET draws zero DC current, the voltage at the gate is set by the unloaded voltage divider:

$$V_G = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

The source is at a potential $V_S = I_D R_S$ above ground. Applying KVL from gate to source:

$$V_{GS} = V_G - V_S = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} - I_D R_S$$

This is the required expression.

**Part (b) — Q-point Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute $V_G$.**

$$V_G = 15 \cdot \frac{1 \text{ M}\Omega}{2 \text{ M}\Omega + 1 \text{ M}\Omega} = 15 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Express $V_{GS}$ in terms of $I_D$ (mA).**

$$V_{GS} = 5 - I_D \cdot 3 \text{ k}\Omega = 5 - 3 I_D \quad \text{(V, $I_D$ in mA)}$$

**Step 3: Apply the square law.**

$$I_D = K_n (V_{GS} - V_t)^2 = 0.25 \cdot (5 - 3 I_D - 1.5)^2 = 0.25 \cdot (3.5 - 3 I_D)^2$$

**Step 4: Expand.**

$$I_D = 0.25 \cdot (12.25 - 21 I_D + 9 I_D^2) = 3.0625 - 5.25 I_D + 2.25 I_D^2$$

**Step 5: Form the quadratic.**

$$2.25 I_D^2 - 6.25 I_D + 3.0625 = 0$$

Multiply by 4 to clear decimals:

$$9 I_D^2 - 25 I_D + 12.25 = 0$$

**Step 6: Solve using the quadratic formula.**

$$I_D = \frac{25 \pm \sqrt{625 - 441}}{18} = \frac{25 \pm \sqrt{184}}{18} = \frac{25 \pm 13.56}{18}$$

$$I_D = \frac{38.56}{18} = 2.14 \text{ mA} \quad \text{or} \quad I_D = \frac{11.44}{18} = 0.636 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 7: Root selection.** For $I_D = 2.14$ mA: $V_{GS} = 5 - 3(2.14) = -1.42$ V, which is below $V_t = 1.5$ V. **Reject.** For $I_D = 0.636$ mA: $V_{GS} = 5 - 3(0.636) = 3.09$ V, which exceeds $V_t = 1.5$ V. **Accept.**

$$I_{DQ} \approx 0.64 \text{ mA}, \quad V_{GSQ} \approx 3.09 \text{ V}$$

**Step 8: Compute $V_{DSQ}$.**

$$V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ}(R_D + R_S) = 15 - 0.636 \cdot (6 + 3) = 15 - 5.72 = 9.28 \text{ V}$$

**Step 9: Verify saturation.**

$$V_{DS,sat} = V_{GSQ} - V_t = 3.09 - 1.5 = 1.59 \text{ V}$$

Since $V_{DSQ} = 9.28 \text{ V} \gg 1.59 \text{ V}$, the MOSFET is in saturation. ✅

**Part (c) — Transconductance (4 marks):**

$$g_m = 2 K_n (V_{GSQ} - V_t) = 2 \cdot 0.25 \cdot (3.09 - 1.5) = 0.5 \cdot 1.59 = 0.795 \text{ mA/V}$$

Equivalently:

$$g_m = 2 \sqrt{K_n I_{DQ}} = 2 \sqrt{0.25 \cdot 0.636} = 2 \cdot 0.399 = 0.797 \text{ mA/V} \quad \checkmark$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **self-bias (source bias) circuit** for an n-channel enhancement MOSFET. Derive the expression for the drain current $I_{DQ}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the stability of self-bias, fixed bias, and voltage divider bias configurations. Justify your answer with the help of stability factor expressions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Self-Bias Operation and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Circuit Description (2 marks):** In a self-bias circuit, the gate of the n-channel enhancement MOSFET is connected to ground through a high-value resistor $R_G$ (typically 1–10 MΩ). The source is connected to ground through a source resistor $R_S$. The drain is connected to the supply $V_{DD}$ through the drain resistor $R_D$. No separate gate supply $V_{GG}$ is required.

**Operating Principle (1 mark):** Since the gate current of the MOSFET is essentially zero, there is no voltage drop across $R_G$, and therefore $V_G = 0$. The source current $I_D$ flowing through $R_S$ raises the source to a potential $V_S = I_D R_S$. Hence:

$$V_{GS} = V_G - V_S = 0 - I_D R_S = -I_D R_S$$

For an n-channel enhancement MOSFET, $V_t > 0$, and to turn the device ON, $V_{GS} > V_t$. This requires $I_D R_S < -V_t$, which is only possible if the polarity is reversed (i.e., the circuit is more commonly used with **depletion MOSFETs** or a modified version of enhancement MOSFETs with a small positive $V_{GG}$).

**Derivation of $I_{DQ}$ (4 marks):** Substituting $V_{GS} = -I_D R_S$ into the square law:

$$I_D = K_n (-I_D R_S - V_t)^2 = K_n (I_D R_S + V_t)^2$$

Expanding:

$$I_D = K_n (I_D^2 R_S^2 + 2 I_D R_S V_t + V_t^2)$$

$$K_n R_S^2 I_D^2 + (2 K_n R_S V_t - 1) I_D + K_n V_t^2 = 0$$

Applying the quadratic formula:

$$I_{DQ} = \frac{(1 - 2 K_n R_S V_t) \pm \sqrt{(1 - 2 K_n R_S V_t)^2 - 4 K_n^2 R_S^2 V_t^2}}{2 K_n R_S^2}$$

The physically meaningful (positive, smaller) root is selected. The corresponding drain-to-source voltage is:

$$V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ}(R_D + R_S)$$

**Part (b) — Comparison of Stability (7 marks):**

| Topology | Stability Factor $S_K$ | Quality |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Bias** | $S_K = 1$ | **Worst.** $I_D$ changes proportionally with $K_n$; no negative feedback. |
| **Self-Bias** | $S_K = \dfrac{1}{1 + g_m R_S}$ (with $R_G = 0$) | Moderate. Feedback exists via $R_S$. |
| **Voltage Divider** | $S_K = \dfrac{1}{1 + g_m R_S}$ (approximate form) | **Best among discrete designs.** Independent gate voltage plus $R_S$ feedback. |
| **Drain-Feedback** | $S_K = \dfrac{1}{1 + g_m (R_D \vert\vert R_G)}$ | Good. Used in RF. |
| **Current Source Bias** | $S_K \to 0$ (ideal) | **Ideal.** Used in ICs. |

**Justification (3 marks):** The stability factor measures the *sensitivity* of $I_D$ to changes in the conduction parameter $K_n$. A value of $S_K = 1$ in fixed bias means a 10% change in $K_n$ produces a 10% change in $I_D$ — completely unacceptable for precision analog design. In contrast, the voltage divider and current-source topologies achieve $S_K$ values approaching zero, ensuring that the Q-point is rock-stable against temperature and process variations. The presence of the source resistor $R_S$ introduces a *negative feedback* loop: an increase in $I_D$ raises $V_S$, which reduces $V_{GS}$, which reduces $I_D$. The strength of this correction scales with $g_m R_S$, which is why designers prefer large $R_S$ values (often bypassed by $C_S$ at signal frequencies to preserve AC gain).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Root Selection Error (–2 marks):** When solving the quadratic, students often pick the *larger* root, which corresponds to a $V_{GS}$ below $V_t$ (i.e., the MOSFET would be in cutoff). Always verify $V_{GS} > V_t$ before finalizing.
> 2. **Units Mismatch in $K_n$ (–1 mark):** If $K_n$ is given in mA/V² and the equation is computed using $V$ in volts, the result for $I_D$ is in mA. Mixing units is a frequent deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting to Verify Saturation (–1 mark):** Computing $V_{DSQ}$ is meaningless without checking $V_{DSQ} \geq V_{GSQ} - V_t$. KTU examiners specifically look for this line.
> 4. **Source Resistor Sign Error (–1 mark):** In voltage divider bias, $V_{GS} = V_G - I_D R_S$, **not** $V_G + I_D R_S$. A wrong sign propagates into a completely wrong $I_{DQ}$.
> 5. **Ignoring the DC Load Line Geometry (–1 mark):** A Q-point exactly on the saturation boundary $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_t$ is *unstable* — any temperature rise pushes it into triode. Aim for $V_{DSQ} \approx V_{DD}/2$ for maximum symmetric AC swing.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- **MOSFET Biasing = Setting DC Q-point** $(I_{DQ}, V_{DSQ})$ in **saturation** for linear amplification.
- **Saturation condition:** $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_t$ (equivalently $V_{DS} \geq V_{OV}$).
- **Square law (the master equation):** $I_D = K_n (V_{GS} - V_t)^2$, with $K_n = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)$.
- **Transconductance:** $g_m = 2K_n(V_{GS} - V_t) = 2\sqrt{K_n I_D}$.
- **Fixed Bias:** $V_{GS} = V_{GG}$, **no feedback**, $S_K = 1$ (worst).
- **Self-Bias:** $V_{GS} = -I_D R_S$, requires solving a quadratic.
- **Voltage Divider Bias:** $V_G = V_{DD} R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$, then $V_{GS} = V_G - I_D R_S$, then quadratic. **Best discrete topology.**
- **Drain-Feedback Bias:** $V_{GS} = V_{DS}$, KVL gives linear relation between $V_{GS}$ and $I_D$.
- **DC Load Line:** $V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D (R_D + R_S)$; passes through $(0, V_{DD})$ and $(V_{DD}/R_{total}, 0)$.
- **Stability Factor (generic):** $S_K = 1/(1 + g_m R_S)$ — larger $R_S$ → better stability → lower $S_K$.
- **Ideal Q-point for max swing:** $V_{DSQ} \approx V_{DD}/2$ and $I_{DQ} R_S \approx V_{DD}/4$ (rule of thumb).
- **Always verify (in this order):** $V_{GSQ} > V_t$ (MOSFET ON), then $V_{DSQ} \geq V_{GSQ} - V_t$ (saturation).
- **Two unknowns, two equations:** The bias problem is always a $2 \times 2$ system — the $V_{GS}$–$I_D$ loop equation and the square law.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of RC Coupled CE Amplifier - Small Signal Analysis of CE Amplifier Using Hybrid-π Model (Low and Mid Frequency)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of RC Coupled CE Amplifier - Small Signal Analysis of CE Amplifier Using Hybrid-π Model (Low and Mid Frequency)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 The RC Coupled Common Emitter (CE) Amplifier

The **RC Coupled CE Amplifier** is the most widely used configuration in small-signal analog electronics. In this circuit, a **resistor–capacitor (RC) network** is employed to *couple* the input and output of one Common Emitter (CE) stage to the next, or to the source/load. The coupling capacitor blocks the DC bias of one stage from interfering with the DC bias of the adjacent stage, while permitting AC signals to pass unimpeded. The bypass capacitor $C_E$ is placed across the emitter resistor $R_E$ to prevent AC degeneration (negative feedback) that would otherwise reduce the gain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The focus is on the *step-by-step design* of a single-stage RC coupled CE amplifier, followed by *small-signal analysis* using the **Hybrid-π model** for the **low** and **mid** frequency regions.

## 1.2 The Hybrid-π Small-Signal Model

The **Hybrid-π model** is a two-port, small-signal equivalent circuit of a BJT valid at high frequencies (and in fact accurate from low frequencies up to $f_T/3$). It expresses the transistor as a *resistive input* ($r_\pi$), a *voltage-controlled current source* ($g_m v_\pi$), and an *output resistance* ($r_o$).

| Symbol | Parameter | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $r_\pi$ | Base–emitter input resistance | Represents the dynamic resistance of the forward-biased base–emitter junction |
| $g_m$ | Transconductance | Converts input base–emitter voltage $v_\pi$ into collector current $i_c$ |
| $r_o$ | Output resistance | Models the **Early effect** (finite output impedance of the current source) |
| $r_x$ | Base spreading resistance | Parasitic resistance of the base bulk (usually neglected unless $f > f_T/3$) |
| $C_\pi$ | Base–emitter depletion + diffusion capacitance | Low/mid frequency: usually treated as open circuit (neglected) |
| $C_\mu$ | Base–collector (Miller) capacitance | **Neglected in mid-band**, considered in high-frequency analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> For **low and mid frequency analysis** (this module), all internal transistor capacitances ($C_\pi, C_\mu$) are treated as **open circuits**. Only the **external coupling and bypass capacitors** ($C_i, C_o, C_E$) determine the low-frequency response.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Audio Stethoscope

Imagine the BJT as a **stethoscope** used by a doctor:
- The **diaphragm (input)** picks up faint heartbeats (small input signal $v_i$).
- The **rubber tubing (coupling capacitor $C_i$)** transmits only the *vibrations* (AC) to the next chamber — it blocks any *steady pressure* (DC bias).
- The **amplifying chamber ($g_m v_\pi$ current source)** boosts the vibration into a loud, audible signal.
- The **emitter resistor with bypass capacitor ($R_E \parallel C_E$)** is like a *damper* that stabilises the chamber against temperature drift (DC thermal stability) but is **bypassed at signal frequencies** so the amplification is not wasted as heat.

This is exactly the philosophy of the **RC coupled CE amplifier**: stabilise the DC operating point with $R_E$, but never let the AC signal "see" that resistor — bypass it with $C_E$.

## 1.4 Why "Hybrid-π"?

The name originates from the **π-shaped (Greek letter π)** topology of the equivalent circuit (two nodes bridging base to emitter and base to collector, with the current source hanging between collector and emitter) combined with the word **"hybrid"** because the parameters are a *mixture* of $h$-parameter, $y$-parameter, and $z$-parameter concepts. The Greek letter $\pi$ is used to indicate the **shunt-shunt configuration** of the elements.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Exam:** Always start your answer with a labelled circuit diagram of the amplifier **and** the corresponding hybrid-π equivalent. Examiners award 2–3 marks just for a clean, correctly labelled diagram.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 2 — SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Complete Design Philosophy

A single-stage RC coupled CE amplifier consists of the following functional blocks (which you must memorise for KTU):

1. **Biasing Network** ($R_1, R_2, R_E$): Establishes the Q-point using voltage-divider bias.
2. **Coupling Capacitor at Input** ($C_i$): Blocks DC from source, passes AC to base.
3. **Coupling Capacitor at Output** ($C_o$): Blocks DC from collector, passes AC to load.
4. **Bypass Capacitor** ($C_E$): Short-circuits $R_E$ at signal frequencies.
5. **Collector Resistor** ($R_C$): Converts collector current variations into voltage.
6. **Load Resistor** ($R_L$): The external load driven by the amplifier.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step DC Design (Sets the Q-Point)

Using the **voltage-divider bias** approximation (valid when $\beta R_E \gg 10 R_2$):

$$V_{BB} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$V_E = V_{BB} - V_{BE}$$

$$I_E = \frac{V_E}{R_E}, \quad I_C \approx I_E$$

$$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E$$

For maximum symmetrical swing, design for **$V_{CE} = V_{CC}/2$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Q-point (i.e. $I_C, V_{CE}$) directly determines every small-signal parameter: $g_m, r_\pi, r_o$. So **DC design comes first**, then **AC analysis**.

## 2.3 Hybrid-π Small-Signal Parameters

These are the **lifeblood of every KTU small-signal problem**:

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{I_C(\text{mA})}{26 \text{ mV}}$$

$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{\beta V_T}{I_C}$$

$$r_o = \frac{V_A}{I_C} \quad \text{(where } V_A = \text{Early voltage)}$$

For most KTU problems, **$r_o$ is neglected unless explicitly mentioned** (e.g. when asked to calculate $R_{out}$ due to Early effect).

## 2.4 Mid-Frequency Analysis (The Easy Region)

In the **mid-band**, all coupling and bypass capacitors are **AC short circuits**, and all internal transistor capacitances are **AC open circuits**. The hybrid-π circuit reduces to a simple resistive network.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "mid-band"?** At mid frequencies, the coupling/bypass capacitors act as perfect shorts (their reactance is negligible), but the internal capacitances $C_\pi, C_\mu$ are not yet effective. The amplifier exhibits a **flat, constant gain** — this is the region where the amplifier is *designed to operate*.

### Key Mid-Band Formulas

Define the **effective load** at the collector:
$$R_L' = R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{R_C R_L}{R_C + R_L}$$

**Voltage gain (from base to collector):**
$$A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_{be}} = -g_m R_L'$$

**Voltage gain (from source to output, including source resistance $R_S$):**
$$A_{vs} = \frac{R_{in}}{R_S + R_{in}} \cdot (-g_m R_L')$$

**Input resistance (looking into the base):**
$$R_{in(base)} = r_\pi$$

**Input resistance (looking into the amplifier terminals):**
$$R_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$$

**Output resistance:**
$$R_{out} = R_C \parallel r_o \approx R_C$$

**Current gain:**
$$A_i = \frac{i_o}{i_i} = \beta \cdot \frac{R_C}{R_C + R_L}$$

## 2.5 Low-Frequency Analysis (Where the Coupling Capacitors Bite)

Below the **lower cutoff frequency $f_L$**, the reactance of the coupling and bypass capacitors becomes significant, and the gain begins to fall at **+20 dB/decade** per capacitor. The **upper cutoff $f_H$** is determined by $C_\pi, C_\mu$ (not in this module).

### The Three Culprits at Low Frequency

| Capacitor | Function | Frequency It Affects |
|---|---|---|
| $C_i$ (input coupling) | Blocks source DC | $f_{L1}$ |
| $C_o$ (output coupling) | Blocks DC to load | $f_{L2}$ |
| $C_E$ (emitter bypass) | Shorts $R_E$ at high freq | $f_{L3}$ — usually the dominant one |

### Time-Constant (Short-Circuited) Method

For each capacitor, compute its **Thevenin resistance** seen from its terminals, treating all *other* capacitors as short circuits (for low-frequency analysis, the other coupling caps are open). The lower cutoff contributed by each capacitor is:

$$f_{Lk} = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_{eq,k} C_k}$$

**For input coupling capacitor $C_i$:**
$$R_{eq,i} = R_S + (R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi)$$

$$f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2 \pi C_i [R_S + (R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi)]}$$

**For output coupling capacitor $C_o$:**
$$R_{eq,o} = R_C + R_L$$

$$f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2 \pi C_o (R_C + R_L)}$$

**For bypass capacitor $C_E$** (using Miller's Theorem on $R_E$):
The effective resistance seen by $C_E$ is $\dfrac{R_E}{1 + g_m (R_E \parallel R_S' \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2)}$ — a small resistance, so $f_{L3}$ is usually the **highest** and **dominant**.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transconductance | $g_m = I_C / V_T$ | A/V (S) | $V_T = 26$ mV at 300 K |
| 2 | Base–emitter resistance | $r_\pi = \beta / g_m$ | $\Omega$ | Always positive |
| 3 | Output resistance | $r_o = V_A / I_C$ | $\Omega$ | Often neglected |
| 4 | Effective collector load | $R_L' = R_C \parallel R_L$ | $\Omega$ | Mid-band gain uses this |
| 5 | Mid-band voltage gain | $A_v = -g_m R_L'$ | dimensionless | Sign indicates $180°$ phase shift |
| 6 | Source-to-output gain | $A_{vs} = -g_m R_L' \cdot R_{in} / (R_S + R_{in})$ | dimensionless | Accounts for source loading |
| 7 | Input resistance | $R_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$ | $\Omega$ | Seen by the source |
| 8 | Output resistance | $R_{out} = R_C$ | $\Omega$ | If $r_o$ neglected |
| 9 | Lower cutoff (input cap) | $f_{L1} = 1/[2\pi C_i(R_S + R_{in})]$ | Hz | |
| 10 | Lower cutoff (output cap) | $f_{L2} = 1/[2\pi C_o(R_C + R_L)]$ | Hz | |
| 11 | Lower cutoff (bypass cap) | $f_{L3} \approx g_m / (2\pi C_E)$ | Hz | Approximate dominant form |
| 12 | Overall lower cutoff | $f_L \approx f_{L1} + f_{L2} + f_{L3}$ | Hz | Sum of individual cuts |
| 13 | Power gain | $G_P = A_v \cdot A_i$ | dimensionless | dB = $10 \log G_P$ |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

RC coupled CE amplifiers are the **building blocks of every audio pre-amplifier, op-amp input stage, microphone amplifier, instrumentation amplifier front-end, and radio IF strip** in existence. From your **smartphone's microphone pre-amp** to the **first stage of an ECG machine**, the topology is identical. Engineers must design $C_i, C_o, C_E$ to ensure that the amplifier's $f_L$ is below the lowest signal frequency of interest (e.g. **20 Hz for audio**).

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 2 — SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mid-Band Voltage Gain Using Hybrid-π Model

**Given:** A single-stage CE amplifier with the hybrid-π equivalent inserted at the base–emitter port. The collector is loaded by $R_C$ in parallel with $R_L$.

**Step 1 — Replace the transistor with its hybrid-π model.**

The base–emitter port sees $r_\pi$ between base and emitter. The collector–emitter port has the current source $g_m v_\pi$ in parallel with $r_o$, with $R_C \parallel R_L = R_L'$ to AC ground.

**Step 2 — Write KCL at the collector node.**

All collector current flows into $R_L'$ (since the current source pushes current *into* the collector, and assuming conventional flow with $i_c$ flowing **out** of the collector node into the source):

$$i_c = g_m v_\pi$$

$$v_o = -g_m v_\pi \cdot R_L'$$

The negative sign arises because an increase in $v_\pi$ causes the current source to *pull* current *out of* the collector node, thereby *dropping* the collector voltage.

**Step 3 — Define the voltage gain from base to collector.**

$$A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_{be}} = \frac{-g_m v_\pi R_L'}{v_\pi} = -g_m R_L'$$

$$\boxed{A_v = -g_m R_L' = -\frac{\beta R_L'}{r_\pi}}$$

**Step 4 — Include source resistance $R_S$.**

The voltage reaching the base is reduced by the voltage divider formed by $R_S$ and $R_{in}$:

$$v_{be} = v_s \cdot \frac{R_{in}}{R_S + R_{in}}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{A_{vs} = \frac{v_o}{v_s} = -g_m R_L' \cdot \frac{R_{in}}{R_S + R_{in}}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Input Resistance

**Step 1 — Apply a test voltage $v_{be}$ at the base–emitter terminals.**

By the hybrid-π model, the current entering the base is:

$$i_b = \frac{v_{be}}{r_\pi}$$

**Step 2 — The resistance looking into the base alone is:**

$$R_{in(base)} = \frac{v_{be}}{i_b} = r_\pi$$

**Step 3 — Including the bias resistors in parallel:**

$$\boxed{R_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \frac{1}{r_\pi}}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Output Resistance

**Step 1 — Deactivate the input source ($v_s = 0 \Rightarrow v_{be} = 0 \Rightarrow g_m v_\pi = 0$).**

The dependent current source becomes an open circuit.

**Step 2 — Looking back into the output terminals, only $R_C$ and $r_o$ remain in parallel:**

$$\boxed{R_{out} = R_C \parallel r_o \approx R_C \quad (\text{when } r_o \gg R_C)}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Low-Frequency Cutoff Due to $C_i$ (Input Coupling Capacitor)

**Step 1 — Identify the Thevenin resistance seen by $C_i$.**

Looking to the left of $C_i$: the source $v_s$ with internal resistance $R_S$ (an AC source $v_s$ with $R_S$ in series).
Looking to the right of $C_i$: the amplifier input resistance $R_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$.

For AC small-signal, the source is shorted (so we see only $R_S$ to ground), and the input is $R_{in}$ to ground.

$$R_{eq,i} = R_S + R_{in}$$

**Step 2 — Form the high-pass RC network.**

The circuit becomes a simple first-order high-pass filter with time constant $\tau_i = R_{eq,i} C_i$:

$$\frac{A_{vs}(j\omega)}{A_{vs,mid}} = \frac{j\omega \tau_i}{1 + j\omega \tau_i}$$

**Step 3 — The cutoff is the frequency at which $\vert A_{vs} \vert = A_{vs,mid}/\sqrt{2}$:**

$$\boxed{f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2 \pi C_i (R_S + R_{in})}}$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Low-Frequency Cutoff Due to $C_o$ (Output Coupling Capacitor)

**Step 1 — Identify the Thevenin resistance seen by $C_o$.**

To the left: $R_C$ to ground (after the dependent source is opened).
To the right: $R_L$ to ground.

$$R_{eq,o} = R_C + R_L$$

**Step 2 — Apply the same high-pass logic:**

$$\boxed{f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2 \pi C_o (R_C + R_L)}}$$

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Low-Frequency Cutoff Due to $C_E$ (Bypass Capacitor) — Dominant Pole

This is the most frequently examined derivation in KTU module 2.

**Step 1 — Split $R_E$ into a DC-bias part and an AC part.**

The total emitter resistance is $R_E$. The bypass capacitor $C_E$ is in parallel with $R_E$ as seen at the emitter. The collector current source $g_m v_\pi$ is mirrored into the emitter as a current $g_m v_\pi$ flowing *out* of the emitter node (by Ebers–Moll symmetry).

**Step 2 — Write KCL at the emitter node (with $C_i, C_o$ shorted for low-frequency $C_E$ analysis):**

Define $R_S' = R_S \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2$ — the Thevenin resistance seen looking *out* of the base.

The current entering the emitter from the controlled source is $g_m v_\pi$. The current leaving through $R_E \parallel C_E$ is $v_e / (R_E \parallel 1/sC_E)$.

But by KVL around the base–emitter loop:

$$v_\pi = v_b - v_e = v_s' - v_e$$

where $v_s' = v_s \cdot R_1\parallel R_2 / (R_S + R_1 \parallel R_2)$ is the open-circuit source voltage seen at the base.

**Step 3 — A more elegant method — approximate the Thevenin resistance seen by $C_E$.**

From the emitter looking into the transistor, the resistance is approximately:

$$R_{e,in} = \frac{r_\pi + R_S'}{\beta + 1} \approx \frac{1}{g_m} + \frac{R_S'}{\beta}$$

The total resistance seen by $C_E$ is:

$$R_{eq,E} = R_E \parallel R_{e,in} = R_E \parallel \left(\frac{1}{g_m} + \frac{R_S'}{\beta}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Lower cutoff frequency:**

$$f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2 \pi C_E \cdot R_{eq,E}}$$

**Step 5 — Dominant-pole approximation:**

Since $R_{eq,E}$ is very small (often less than $1/g_m$), $f_{L3}$ is usually much larger than $f_{L1}$ and $f_{L2}$, so:

$$\boxed{f_L \approx f_{L3} \approx \frac{1}{2 \pi C_E} \left(g_m + \frac{\beta}{R_S'}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{R_E}}$$

## 3.7 Worked Example — Single-Stage CE Amplifier (Full Design & Analysis)

**Given:** $V_{CC} = 12$ V, $R_1 = 10$ k$\Omega$, $R_2 = 5$ k$\Omega$, $R_E = 1$ k$\Omega$, $R_C = 4.7$ k$\Omega$, $R_L = 4.7$ k$\Omega$, $R_S = 600$ $\Omega$, $\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V, $V_A = 100$ V, $C_i = 1 \mu$F, $C_o = 1 \mu$F, $C_E = 100 \mu$F.

**Step 1 — DC Analysis.**

$$V_{BB} = 12 \cdot \frac{5}{10 + 5} = 4.0 \text{ V}$$

$$V_E = 4.0 - 0.7 = 3.3 \text{ V}$$

$$I_E = \frac{3.3}{1 \text{ k}\Omega} = 3.3 \text{ mA} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_C \approx 3.3 \text{ mA}$$

$$V_C = 12 - 3.3 \text{ mA} \times 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega = 12 - 15.51 = -3.51 \text{ V (wrong! saturation)}$$

**Oops — re-design.** The Q-point is in saturation! Choose $R_C = 2.2$ k$\Omega$ instead.

$$V_C = 12 - 3.3 \text{ mA} \times 2.2 \text{ k}\Omega = 12 - 7.26 = 4.74 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{CE} = V_C - V_E = 4.74 - 3.3 = 1.44 \text{ V} \quad (\text{not centred, but acceptable})$$

**Step 2 — Hybrid-π Parameters.**

$$g_m = \frac{3.3 \text{ mA}}{26 \text{ mV}} = 0.127 \text{ S} = 127 \text{ mA/V}$$

$$r_\pi = \frac{100}{0.127} = 787.4 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$r_o = \frac{100 \text{ V}}{3.3 \text{ mA}} = 30.3 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Mid-Band Analysis.**

$$R_L' = 2.2 \text{ k}\Omega \parallel 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega = \frac{2.2 \times 4.7}{2.2 + 4.7} = \frac{10.34}{6.9} = 1.499 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$A_v = -g_m R_L' = -0.127 \times 1499 = -190.4$$

$$R_{in} = 10 \text{ k} \parallel 5 \text{ k} \parallel 787.4 = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{10} + \frac{1}{5} + \frac{1}{0.7874}} = \frac{1}{0.1 + 0.2 + 1.27} = \frac{1}{1.57} = 637 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$A_{vs} = -190.4 \times \frac{637}{600 + 637} = -190.4 \times 0.515 = -98.0$$

$$R_{out} = R_C = 2.2 \text{ k}\Omega \quad (\text{since } r_o \gg R_C)$$

**Step 4 — Low-Frequency Cutoffs.**

$$f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1 \mu F \times (600 + 637)} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-6} \times 1237} = 128.7 \text{ Hz}$$

$$f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1 \mu F \times (2200 + 4700)} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-6} \times 6900} = 23.07 \text{ Hz}$$

For $C_E$, $R_S' = 600 \parallel 10\text{k} \parallel 5\text{k} \approx 530 \text{ }\Omega$:

$$R_{e,in} \approx \frac{1}{0.127} + \frac{530}{100} = 7.87 + 5.3 = 13.17 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$R_{eq,E} = 1000 \parallel 13.17 = 12.99 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 100 \mu F \times 12.99} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1.299 \times 10^{-3}} = 122.5 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Overall Lower Cutoff (Sum).**

$$f_L \approx 128.7 + 23.07 + 122.5 = 274.3 \text{ Hz}$$

This is **too high for audio**! To make $f_L < 20$ Hz, we would need $C_E = 1000 \mu F$ or larger. This is a classic KTU design insight.

> [!NOTE]
> **The bypass capacitor is almost always the dominant pole** in a well-designed CE amplifier. If your final $f_L$ is too high in a KTU numerical, the *first place to look* is $C_E$.

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Design vs Analysis Summary

| Aspect | Design Mode (find $R_1, R_2, R_E, R_C, C_i, C_o, C_E$) | Analysis Mode (find $A_v, R_{in}, R_{out}, f_L$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Given** | $V_{CC}, I_C, V_{CE}, A_v, R_{in}, f_L, R_L, R_S$ | All component values, $\beta, V_A$ |
| **First step** | Find $I_C, V_{CE}$ from $V_{CC}$ | Find $V_B, I_C$ from DC analysis |
| **Second step** | Find $R_E = V_E/I_E$, $R_C = (V_{CC} - V_C)/I_C$ | Compute $g_m, r_\pi, r_o$ |
| **Third step** | Use $R_{in}$ requirement to find $R_1 \parallel R_2$ | Compute $R_L', A_v, R_{in}, R_{out}$ |
| **Fourth step** | Use $f_L$ spec to find $C_i, C_o, C_E$ | Compute $f_{L1}, f_{L2}, f_{L3}$, sum |
| **KTU marks** | Full 14-mark problem | 7–10 mark problem |

# MODULE 2 — SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Single-Stage RC Coupled CE Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sourceAC[AC Source vS with RS] --> capIn[Coupling Capacitor Ci]
    capIn --> biasNet[Bias Network R1 parallel R2]
    biasNet --> baseNode[Base Node B]
    baseNode --> bjt[BJT in CE Configuration]
    bjt --> collectorNode[Collector Node C]
    collectorNode --> rcRes[Collector Resistor RC]
    rcRes --> vcc[VCC Supply Rail]
    collectorNode --> capOut[Coupling Capacitor Co]
    capOut --> loadRes[Load Resistor RL]
    bjt --> emitterNode[Emitter Node E]
    emitterNode --> reRes[Emitter Resistor RE]
    reRes --> gnd[Ground GND]
    emitterNode --> bypassCap[Bypass Capacitor CE]
    bypassCap --> gnd
    baseNode --> rPi[Hybrid pi input rpi]
    rPi --> emitterNode
    bjt --> gmSrc[Controlled Current Source gm vpi]
    gmSrc --> collectorNode
    gmSrc --> emitterNode
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Design Workflow (How to Solve KTU Problems)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: Given Circuit Specification]) --> dcQ[Step 1: DC Analysis]
    dcQ --> findVBB[Calculate VBB and VE]
    findVBB --> findIC[Calculate IC and VCE]
    findIC --> checkQ{Q-point OK?}
    checkQ -->|No| redesign[Re-choose RC or RE]
    redesign --> findVBB
    checkQ -->|Yes| acG[Step 2: AC Small-Signal Parameters]
    acG --> calcm[Calculate gm]
    calcm --> calcrpi[Calculate rpi]
    calcrpi --> calcrO[Calculate ro]
    calcrO --> midB[Step 3: Mid-Band Analysis]
    midB --> rlprime[Compute RL prime]
    rlprime --> av[Compute Voltage Gain Av]
    av --> rin[Compute Input Resistance Rin]
    rin --> rout[Compute Output Resistance Rout]
    rout --> lowF[Step 4: Low-Frequency Analysis]
    lowF --> fl1[Compute fL1 from Ci]
    fl1 --> fl2[Compute fL2 from Co]
    fl2 --> fl3[Compute fL3 from CE]
    fl3 --> sumfL[Sum: fL approx fL1 plus fL2 plus fL3]
    sumfL --> final([End: Report Av, Rin, Rout, fL])
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flow — Hybrid-π Model Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    b1[B for Base] --> rpiNode[Resistance rpi]
    rpiNode --> e1[E for Emitter]
    b1 --> gmNode[Dependent Source gm vpi]
    gmNode --> c1[C for Collector]
    c1 --> roNode[Output Resistance ro]
    roNode --> e1
    c1 --> rcNode[RC to AC Ground]
    rcNode --> acgnd1[AC Ground]
    e1 --> reNode[RE parallel CE to AC Ground]
    reNode --> acgnd2[AC Ground]
    b1 --> r1Node[R1 to AC Ground]
    b1 --> r2Node[R2 to AC Ground]
    r1Node --> acgnd3[AC Ground]
    r2Node --> acgnd4[AC Ground]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Flow — Frequency Response Characteristics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    faxis[Frequency f in Hz] --> lowRegion[Low Frequency Region f less than fL]
    faxis --> midRegion[Mid Frequency Region fL less than f less than fH]
    faxis --> highRegion[High Frequency Region f greater than fH]
    lowRegion --> ciRes[Reactance of Ci, Co, CE is High]
    ciRes --> gainDrop[Gain drops at 20 dB per decade per capacitor]
    midRegion --> capsShort[All Coupling and Bypass Caps are Short]
    capsShort --> midGain[Constant Maximum Gain Av mid]
    highRegion --> capInt[Internal Cpi and Cmu are Effective]
    capInt --> highDrop[Gain drops at 40 dB per decade]
    gainDrop --> corner1[Corner at fL approx 274 Hz]
    midGain --> flatBand[Flat Bandwidth BW]
    highDrop --> corner2[Corner at fH]
```

## 4.5 Functional Block Description

| Block | Function | Key Element |
|---|---|---|
| **Source Stage** | Provides small-signal $v_s$ with internal $R_S$ | AC source with $R_S$ |
| **Input Coupling** | Blocks DC, passes AC | $C_i$ |
| **Bias Network** | Stabilises Q-point against $\beta$ and temperature variations | $R_1, R_2, R_E$ |
| **Transistor Core** | Amplifies via controlled current source $g_m v_\pi$ | BJT with hybrid-π model |
| **Output Coupling** | Blocks DC, passes AC to load | $C_o$ |
| **Bypass Path** | Prevents AC degeneration across $R_E$ | $C_E \parallel R_E$ |
| **Load** | Consumes amplified signal power | $R_L$ |

# MODULE 2 — SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Q: Draw the hybrid-π equivalent circuit of a BJT in CE configuration. List all parameters and state the physical meaning of $g_m$ and $r_\pi$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A BJT in CE configuration, when replaced by its small-signal hybrid-π model, consists of:

1. **$r_\pi$** between base (B) and emitter (E): represents the dynamic input resistance of the forward-biased base–emitter junction. **Value: $r_\pi = \beta / g_m$** (1 mark)
2. **$g_m v_\pi$** dependent current source between collector (C) and emitter (E): represents the amplifying action of the transistor. **Value: $g_m = I_C / V_T$** (1 mark)
3. **$r_o$** between collector and emitter: models the Early effect. $r_o = V_A / I_C$ (1 mark)
4. (Optional) $r_x$ in series with base for high frequencies; $C_\pi$ across $r_\pi$ and $C_\mu$ between B and C for high frequencies.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Dec 2024]`
**Q: Why is a coupling capacitor required between the amplifier stages in a multi-stage RC coupled amplifier? What will happen if it is removed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- A coupling capacitor $C_i$ (and $C_o$) is used to **block the DC bias** of one stage from disturbing the DC operating point of the next stage, while **allowing the AC signal to pass freely**. (1.5 marks)
- If the coupling capacitor is removed, the **DC collector voltage of the previous stage will be applied to the base of the next stage**, shifting the Q-point and driving the next transistor into **saturation or cut-off**, destroying amplification. (1 mark)
- In addition, $C_i$ and $C_o$ together with the source/load resistances form a **high-pass filter** that sets the **lower cutoff frequency** $f_L$. (0.5 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Many students write only "blocks DC" without explaining *why* blocking DC is necessary (Q-point disturbance). You will lose 1 mark. Always state the **consequence** of removing the capacitor.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question B — Choice A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q: Design a single-stage RC coupled CE amplifier using a BJT with $\beta = 100$, $V_{CC} = 15$ V, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V, $R_L = 4.7$ k$\Omega$, $R_S = 600 \Omega$ to meet the following specifications:**
- **(a)** Mid-band voltage gain $\vert A_{vs} \vert \geq 80$
- **(b)** Lower cutoff frequency $f_L \leq 50$ Hz
- **(c)** Input resistance $R_{in} \geq 1$ k$\Omega$

**Assume Early voltage $V_A = 100$ V, $V_T = 26$ mV. State all assumptions clearly.**

#### **Part (a) Solution — DC Design & Mid-Band Voltage Gain (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Choose Q-point.**
For maximum symmetrical swing, set $V_{CE} = V_{CC}/2 = 7.5$ V. Choose $I_C = 1$ mA (a reasonable low value for moderate gain and low noise). (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Compute $g_m$, $r_\pi$, $r_o$.**

$$g_m = \frac{1 \text{ mA}}{26 \text{ mV}} = 38.46 \text{ mA/V}$$

$$r_\pi = \frac{100}{0.03846} = 2600 \text{ }\Omega = 2.6 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$r_o = \frac{100 \text{ V}}{1 \text{ mA}} = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$$ (negligible compared to $R_C$, $R_L$) (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Compute $R_L'$ from gain requirement.**

$$\vert A_{vs} \vert = g_m R_L' \cdot \frac{R_{in}}{R_S + R_{in}}$$

Assuming $R_{in} = r_\pi = 2.6$ k$\Omega$ (ignoring bias resistors initially):

$$\frac{R_{in}}{R_S + R_{in}} = \frac{2600}{600 + 2600} = 0.8125$$

$$g_m R_L' \geq \frac{80}{0.8125} = 98.46$$

$$R_L' \geq \frac{98.46}{0.03846} = 2.56 \text{ k}\Omega$$ (1 mark)

**Step 4 — Find $R_C$ given $R_L = 4.7$ k$\Omega$.**

$$R_L' = \frac{R_C R_L}{R_C + R_L} \geq 2.56 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_C \geq \frac{R_L' R_L}{R_L - R_L'} = \frac{2.56 \times 4.7}{4.7 - 2.56} = \frac{12.03}{2.14} = 5.62 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Choose $R_C = 5.6$ k$\Omega$ (standard value). Then $R_L' = 2.56$ k$\Omega$. (1 mark)

**Step 5 — DC verification.**

$$V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E - V_E = V_{CC}/2$$

Choose $V_E = V_{CC}/4 = 3.75$ V (for good bias stability with $R_E$ un-bypassed partially, but we'll bypass fully).

$$R_E = \frac{V_E}{I_E} = \frac{3.75}{1} = 3.75 \text{ k}\Omega$$ (use 3.9 k$\Omega$ standard) (1 mark)

**Step 6 — Choose $R_1, R_2$ for $R_{in} \geq 1$ k$\Omega$.**

$$R_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi \geq 1 \text{ k}\Omega$$

If $R_1 \parallel R_2 \gg r_\pi$, then $R_{in} \approx r_\pi = 2.6$ k$\Omega$ which already satisfies. So choose $R_1 = 10$ k$\Omega$, $R_2 = 5$ k$\Omega$ (for $V_{BB} = 5$ V, $V_E \approx 4.3$ V, $I_E \approx 1.1$ mA — close to design). (1 mark)

**Verification of mid-band gain:**

$$R_{in} = 10k \parallel 5k \parallel 2.6k = 1.64 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$A_{vs} = -0.03846 \times 2560 \times \frac{1640}{600 + 1640} = -98.5 \times 0.732 = -72.1$$

Hmm — short of 80. So either increase $R_C$ to 6.8 k$\Omega$ or accept $I_C = 1.5$ mA. Let $I_C = 1.5$ mA:

$$g_m = 57.7 \text{ mA/V}, \quad r_\pi = 1.73 \text{ k}\Omega, \quad R_L' = 2.56 \text{ k}\Omega$$
$$A_v = -57.7 \times 2.56 = -147.7$$
$$R_{in} = 10k \parallel 5k \parallel 1.73k = 1.19 \text{ k}\Omega$$
$$A_{vs} = -147.7 \times \frac{1190}{600 + 1190} = -147.7 \times 0.665 = -98.2 \quad \checkmark$$ (1 mark)

#### **Part (b) Solution — Lower Cutoff Frequency & Coupling Capacitor Design (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Choose $C_i$.**

$$R_{eq,i} = R_S + R_{in} = 600 + 1190 = 1790 \text{ }\Omega$$

For $f_{L1} = 20$ Hz (one-tenth of the requirement, so that it does not dominate):

$$C_i = \frac{1}{2\pi f_{L1} R_{eq,i}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 20 \times 1790} = 4.44 \text{ }\mu F$$

Choose $C_i = 4.7 \mu$F (standard). (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Choose $C_o$.**

$$R_{eq,o} = R_C + R_L = 6800 + 4700 = 11500 \text{ }\Omega$$

For $f_{L2} = 20$ Hz:

$$C_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 20 \times 11500} = 0.692 \text{ }\mu F$$

Choose $C_o = 1 \mu$F (standard). (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Choose $C_E$ (dominant pole).**

$R_S' = R_S \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2 = 600 \parallel 10000 \parallel 5000 = 522 \text{ }\Omega$ (1 mark)

$$R_{e,in} \approx \frac{1}{g_m} + \frac{R_S'}{\beta} = \frac{1}{0.0577} + \frac{522}{100} = 17.33 + 5.22 = 22.55 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$R_{eq,E} = R_E \parallel R_{e,in} = \frac{R_E \times R_{e,in}}{R_E + R_{e,in}}$$

For $R_E = 3.9$ k$\Omega$:

$$R_{eq,E} = 3.9k \parallel 22.55 = 22.42 \text{ }\Omega$$

For $f_{L3} = 30$ Hz (dominant pole):

$$C_E = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 30 \times 22.42} = 236 \text{ }\mu F$$

Choose $C_E = 470 \mu$F (standard). (2 marks)

**Step 4 — Verify overall $f_L$.**

$$f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 4.7\mu \times 1790} = 18.9 \text{ Hz}$$
$$f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1\mu \times 11500} = 13.8 \text{ Hz}$$
$$f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 470\mu \times 22.42} = 15.1 \text{ Hz}$$

$$f_L \approx 18.9 + 13.8 + 15.1 = 47.8 \text{ Hz} \leq 50 \text{ Hz} \quad \checkmark$$ (2 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in KTU 14-mark Problems:**
> 1. Forgetting to **sum the three cutoff frequencies** — many students just take the maximum. **You will lose 3 marks** for this.
> 2. Computing $R_S'$ incorrectly (some students use $R_S$ alone). $R_S' = R_S \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2$.
> 3. Using $r_\pi$ as the only input resistance — forgetting $R_1 \parallel R_2$ in parallel.
> 4. For $C_E$, many students incorrectly compute $R_{eq,E}$ as just $R_E$ — losing 2 marks.

---

### **Question B — Choice B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]`

**Q: For the CE amplifier shown in the figure, the parameters are: $V_{CC} = 12$ V, $R_1 = 8.2$ k$\Omega$, $R_2 = 3.9$ k$\Omega$, $R_E = 1.2$ k$\Omega$, $R_C = 2.7$ k$\Omega$, $R_L = 4.7$ k$\Omega$, $R_S = 200 \Omega$, $\beta = 120$, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V, $V_A = 80$ V, $C_i = 10 \mu$F, $C_o = 10 \mu$F, $C_E = 470 \mu$F.**
- **(a)** Find the Q-point ($I_C, V_{CE}$). Calculate $g_m$, $r_\pi$, and $r_o$.
- **(b)** Find the mid-band voltage gain $A_{vs}$, input resistance $R_{in}$, output resistance $R_{out}$, and the overall lower cutoff frequency $f_L$.

#### **Part (a) Solution — DC & Hybrid-π Parameters (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — DC analysis using Thevenin equivalent of base bias.** (1 mark)

$$V_{BB} = 12 \cdot \frac{3.9}{8.2 + 3.9} = 12 \cdot 0.3223 = 3.868 \text{ V}$$

$$R_B = R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{8.2 \times 3.9}{8.2 + 3.9} = \frac{31.98}{12.1} = 2.643 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 2 — KVL around base–emitter loop.** (1 mark)

$$I_B = \frac{V_{BB} - V_{BE}}{R_B + (\beta + 1) R_E} = \frac{3.868 - 0.7}{2.643 + 121 \times 1.2} = \frac{3.168}{147.84} = 21.43 \text{ }\mu A$$

**Step 3 — Q-point values.** (1 mark)

$$I_C = \beta I_B = 120 \times 21.43 \text{ }\mu A = 2.572 \text{ mA}$$

$$I_E = (\beta + 1) I_B = 121 \times 21.43 \text{ }\mu A = 2.593 \text{ mA}$$

$$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E = 12 - 2.572 \times 2.7 - 2.593 \times 1.2$$
$$V_{CE} = 12 - 6.944 - 3.112 = 1.944 \text{ V}$$

Q-point: $(I_C, V_{CE}) = (2.57 \text{ mA}, 1.94 \text{ V})$.

**Step 4 — Small-signal parameters.** (1 mark)

$$g_m = \frac{2.572 \text{ mA}}{26 \text{ mV}} = 98.92 \text{ mA/V}$$

$$r_\pi = \frac{120}{0.09892} = 1213 \text{ }\Omega = 1.213 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$r_o = \frac{80 \text{ V}}{2.572 \text{ mA}} = 31.1 \text{ k}\Omega$$

#### **Part (b) Solution — Mid-Band Gain, Resistances & $f_L$ (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Mid-band effective load.** (0.5 mark)

$$R_L' = R_C \parallel R_L = 2.7 \text{k} \parallel 4.7 \text{k} = \frac{2.7 \times 4.7}{2.7 + 4.7} = \frac{12.69}{7.4} = 1.715 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Voltage gain from source.** (1.5 marks)

$$R_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi = 8.2 \text{k} \parallel 3.9 \text{k} \parallel 1.213 \text{k}$$

$$R_{in} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{8.2} + \frac{1}{3.9} + \frac{1}{1.213}} = \frac{1}{0.122 + 0.256 + 0.825} = \frac{1}{1.203} = 831 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$A_v = -g_m R_L' = -0.09892 \times 1715 = -169.6$$

$$A_{vs} = A_v \cdot \frac{R_{in}}{R_S + R_{in}} = -169.6 \times \frac{831}{200 + 831} = -169.6 \times 0.806 = -136.7$$

**Step 3 — Output resistance.** (0.5 mark)

$$R_{out} = R_C \parallel r_o = 2.7 \text{k} \parallel 31.1 \text{k} = 2.48 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Lower cutoff frequencies.** (3.5 marks)

**For $C_i = 10 \mu$F:**

$$R_{eq,i} = R_S + R_{in} = 200 + 831 = 1031 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10 \mu F \times 1031} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 0.01031} = 15.43 \text{ Hz}$$

**For $C_o = 10 \mu$F:**

$$R_{eq,o} = R_C + R_L = 2.7\text{k} + 4.7\text{k} = 7.4 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10 \mu F \times 7400} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 0.074} = 2.15 \text{ Hz}$$

**For $C_E = 470 \mu$F:**

$$R_S' = R_S \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2 = 200 \parallel 8200 \parallel 3900 = 188 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$R_{e,in} = \frac{1}{g_m} + \frac{R_S'}{\beta} = \frac{1}{0.09892} + \frac{188}{120} = 10.11 + 1.57 = 11.68 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$R_{eq,E} = R_E \parallel R_{e,in} = 1.2 \text{k} \parallel 11.68 = 11.57 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 470 \mu F \times 11.57} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 0.00544} = 29.26 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Overall lower cutoff.** (1 mark)

$$\boxed{f_L = f_{L1} + f_{L2} + f_{L3} = 15.43 + 2.15 + 29.26 = 46.84 \text{ Hz}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls in Numerical Problems (KTU):**
> 1. **Forgetting the base current** in DC analysis when $R_B$ is comparable to $\beta R_E$. Always use the exact KVL formula unless told to use the approximate voltage divider.
> 2. **Mixing units**: $I_C$ must be in **Amps** (not mA) when using $g_m = I_C / V_T$ with $V_T = 0.026$ V.
> 3. **Sign of $A_v$**: The minus sign is mandatory in $A_v = -g_m R_L'$. If you drop it, you lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Summing the cutoffs**: Use the formula $f_L \approx \sum f_{Lk}$ only when the cutoffs are well separated (within a factor of 4). For closer poles, use $1/f_L^2 = \sum 1/f_{Lk}^2$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] The **RC coupled CE amplifier** uses $C_i, C_o$ to isolate DC bias between stages, and $C_E$ to bypass $R_E$ for AC.
