- [ ] The **hybrid-π model** parameters are: $g_m = I_C / V_T$, $r_\pi = \beta / g_m$, $r_o = V_A / I_C$.
- [ ] **Mid-band gain**: $A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L) = -g_m R_L'$. The negative sign means **180° phase inversion**.
- [ ] **Source-to-output gain**: $A_{vs} = A_v \cdot R_{in} / (R_S + R_{in})$.
- [ ] **Input resistance**: $R_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$.
- [ ] **Output resistance**: $R_{out} = R_C \parallel r_o \approx R_C$.
- [ ] **Lower cutoff** is determined by THREE capacitors: $C_i, C_o, C_E$.
- [ ] **Bypass capacitor $C_E$ usually produces the dominant pole** because $R_{eq,E}$ is very small.
- [ ] **$f_{L1}$ (from $C_i$)**: $f_{L1} = 1/[2\pi C_i (R_S + R_{in})]$.
- [ ] **$f_{L2}$ (from $C_o$)**: $f_{L2} = 1/[2\pi C_o (R_C + R_L)]$.
- [ ] **$f_{L3}$ (from $C_E$)**: $R_{eq,E} = R_E \parallel (1/g_m + R_S'/\beta)$, then $f_{L3} = 1/(2\pi C_E R_{eq,E})$.
- [ ] **Overall $f_L$**: $f_L \approx f_{L1} + f_{L2} + f_{L3}$ (sum of well-separated poles).
- [ ] **Q-point rule for max swing**: design for $V_{CE} \approx V_{CC}/2$.
- [ ] **For low and mid frequency analysis**, $C_\pi$ and $C_\mu$ are **open** circuits (neglected).
- [ ] **Design sequence** at KTU: DC first → $g_m, r_\pi, r_o$ → mid-band → low-frequency → choose capacitor values from $f_L$ spec.
- [ ] **Miller's theorem** is implicit in the $R_{in}$ calculation through $r_\pi$.
- [ ] **At low frequencies, gain falls at +20 dB/decade per capacitor**; total slope is sum of slopes.
- [ ] **At mid-band, gain is flat** at $|A_{vs,mid}|$.
- [ ] **At high frequencies**, the internal $C_\pi, C_\mu$ (Miller) cause roll-off — covered in Module 3.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Tip:** When asked to *design* an RC coupled CE amplifier, **always check** the Q-point after computing $V_{CE}$. If $V_{CE} < 0.3$ V, the BJT is in saturation and your design is invalid. If $V_{CE} > V_{CC}$, the transistor is in cut-off. The KTU examiner will deduct **2 marks** if you don't verify.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The High-Frequency Hybrid-π Model of BJT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The High-Frequency Hybrid-π Model of BJT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **High-Frequency Hybrid-π Model** is an enhanced, small-signal equivalent circuit of a Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) that incorporates the **internal parasitic capacitances** which dominate the device's behavior when the operating signal frequency increases. At low frequencies, these capacitances behave as open circuits and are ignored, but at high frequencies they introduce reactive current paths that degrade the gain of the transistor.

The model augments the low-frequency hybrid-π network with two key capacitive elements:

- **$C_\pi$** — the equivalent capacitance between base and emitter terminals, comprising the **base-emitter depletion (junction) capacitance** $C_{be}$ and the **base-emitter diffusion capacitance** $C_{b,diff}$.
- **$C_\mu$** — the **base-collector junction (feedback) capacitance**, which physically exists across the reverse-biased base-collector depletion region.

It also commonly includes the **base spreading resistance** $r_b$ (or $r_x$) connected in series at the external base terminal, which represents the finite resistance of the neutral base region between the external contact and the active base region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Standard Wording):**
> *"The high-frequency hybrid-π model is a small-signal equivalent circuit of a BJT valid for frequencies in the MHz range, obtained by augmenting the low-frequency hybrid-π model with the base-emitter capacitance $C_\pi$ and the base-collector capacitance $C_\mu$, both of which represent the charge-storage effects within the transistor."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stray Water Tank" Intuition

Imagine a **water tap (the input signal)** connected via a thin pipe to a **pressure chamber (the BJT)**. At low flow rates (low frequency), the water flows smoothly and you can control the output pressure precisely.

Now suppose **two small hidden tanks (the capacitances $C_\pi$ and $C_\mu$)** are attached to the pipes:
- One tank ($C_\pi$) sits between the input and the chamber — it stores and releases water, slowing down the response.
- The other tank ($C_\mu$) is a **feedback leak** that siphons some water back from the output side toward the input — this is the infamous **Miller effect**.

At low frequencies, these tanks have time to fill and empty, so they don't disturb the flow. But at **high frequencies**, the rapid oscillation of pressure means the tanks never reach equilibrium, the water sloshes back and forth, and the actual output pressure (gain) drops dramatically. This is exactly what the high-frequency hybrid-π model captures mathematically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used:**
> - Thermal voltage $V_T \approx \mathbf{25 \text{ mV}}$ at $300 \text{ K}$ (room temperature)
> - Electronic charge $q = \mathbf{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}}$
> - Boltzmann constant $k = \mathbf{1.381 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}}$

## 1.3 Why is the High-Frequency Model Needed?

| Limitation of Low-Freq Hybrid-π | What High-Freq Model Adds |
|---|---|
| Assumes instantaneous charge control | Models finite charge storage via $C_\pi, C_\mu$ |
| Valid only for $f \ll f_\beta$ | Valid up to the **unity-gain frequency** $f_T$ |
| Gain treated as purely real $\beta$ | Gain becomes complex $h_{fe}(j\omega) = \beta / (1 + jf/f_\beta)$ |
| No frequency-dependent feedback | Models feedback through $C_\mu$ (Miller capacitance) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Origin of the Capacitive Elements

A BJT has **two depletion regions** (Base-Emitter and Base-Collector) and **one neutral base region**. Each contributes to charge storage:

### (i) Base-Emitter Depletion Capacitance $C_{be}$
- Arises across the **forward-biased** Base-Emitter junction.
- Capacitance per unit area follows: $C_{be} = \dfrac{C_{je0}}{(1 - V_{BE}/V_{bi})^m}$
- Modest in magnitude because the depletion region narrows under forward bias.

### (ii) Base-Emitter Diffusion Capacitance $C_{b,diff}$
- Dominant for an **active forward-biased** BJT.
- Represents the **stored minority-carrier charge** in the base.
- Given by: $C_{b,diff} = \dfrac{\tau_F I_C}{V_T} = g_m \tau_F$
- where $\tau_F$ is the **forward transit time** (time for carriers to cross the base).

### (iii) Base-Collector Capacitance $C_\mu = C_{bc}$
- Reverse-biased junction capacitance.
- Small and nearly constant, but its **Miller-multiplied effect** is severe.

### (iv) Total Base-Emitter Capacitance
The total capacitance shunting the base-emitter port is:
$$C_\pi = C_{be} + C_{b,diff} = C_{be} + g_m \tau_F$$

> [!NOTE]
> In a well-designed high-frequency BJT, the diffusion capacitance $C_{b,diff}$ typically **dominates** $C_\pi$, making $C_\pi \approx g_m \tau_F$.

## 2.2 Components of the High-Frequency Hybrid-π Model

The complete model (commonly used in KTU problems) consists of:

| Element | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Base spreading resistance | $r_b$ (or $r_x$) | Resistance of neutral base region between external B and internal B′ |
| Base-emitter resistance | $r_\pi$ | $\beta / g_m$ — input resistance looking into base |
| Output resistance | $r_o$ | Early effect (often neglected in HF analysis) |
| Transconductance | $g_m$ | $I_C / V_T$ — gain from $V_{b'e}$ to $i_c$ |
| Current source | $g_m v_{b'e}$ | Collector current dependent on internal base-emitter voltage |
| B-E capacitance | $C_\pi$ | Between B′ and E |
| B-C capacitance | $C_\mu$ | Between B′ and C (Miller capacitance) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU problem on this topic is built from these equations.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$ | Transconductance | S (A/V) |
| 2 | $r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta}{g_m} = \dfrac{\beta V_T}{I_C}$ | Base-emitter input resistance | $\Omega$ |
| 3 | $C_\pi \approx g_m \tau_F$ | B-E capacitance (diffusion-dominated) | F |
| 4 | $C_\mu = C_{bc}$ | B-C (feedback) capacitance | F |
| 5 | $C_{in,Miller} = C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + g_m R_L)$ | Effective input capacitance (Miller effect) | F |
| 6 | $A_{ih}(s) = \dfrac{\beta}{1 + s r_\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)}$ | Short-circuit CE current gain (approx.) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $f_\beta = \dfrac{1}{2\pi r_\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)}$ | Beta cutoff frequency ($-3$ dB freq of $h_{fe}$) | Hz |
| 8 | $f_T = \beta \cdot f_\beta = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)}$ | Unity-gain (transition) frequency | Hz |
| 9 | $f_T \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau_F}$ | Unity-gain freq in terms of transit time | Hz |
| 10 | $f_{\beta,exact} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi r_\pi C_\pi}$ | When $C_\mu$ is referred using Miller's exact form | Hz |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in KTU Scripts:** Many students write $f_T = g_m / (2\pi C_\pi)$. The correct expression includes **both** $C_\pi$ and $C_\mu$. Unless explicitly stated, always use $f_T = g_m / [2\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)]$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The high-frequency hybrid-π model is the **workhorse of RF and analog IC design**. It is used to:

- Design **wideband amplifiers** (e.g., video amplifiers, op-amp gain stages).
- Predict the **maximum usable frequency** of a transistor in oscillators and mixers.
- Compute the **3-dB bandwidth** of a common-emitter (CE) amplifier: $f_H = 1 / (2\pi R_{sig} C_{in,Miller})$.
- Select appropriate BJTs for **RF power amplifiers**, where $f_T$ must be 5–10× the operating frequency.
- Analyze **stability and oscillation** in feedback amplifier design (Miller capacitance is a major concern).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Short-Circuit Common-Emitter Current Gain $A_{ih}(s)$

**Objective:** Find the small-signal current gain $A_{ih} = i_c / i_b$ when the output is shorted (i.e., $v_{ce} = 0$).

**Step 1 — Apply short-circuit condition.** With the output shorted to ground, the collector and emitter are at the same AC potential. Therefore the voltage across $C_\mu$ is zero (its terminals are at the same node), and $C_\mu$ carries no current.

$$
i_c = g_m v_{b'e} - i_{C_\mu}
$$

Under the short-circuit condition ($v_{b'c} = 0$), the current through $C_\mu$ is zero, so:

$$
i_c = g_m v_{b'e}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the internal base node B′.** The base current $i_b$ flows into $r_\pi$ in parallel with $C_\pi$ (since $C_\mu$ is short-circuited to ground at the output):

$$
i_b = \frac{v_{b'e}}{r_\pi} + C_\pi \frac{dv_{b'e}}{dt}
$$

In the **Laplace (s-) domain**:

$$
I_b(s) = \frac{V_{b'e}(s)}{r_\pi} + s C_\pi V_{b'e}(s) = V_{b'e}(s)\left(\frac{1}{r_\pi} + s C_\pi\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Express $v_{b'e}$ in terms of $i_b$.**

$$
V_{b'e}(s) = \frac{I_b(s)\, r_\pi}{1 + s r_\pi C_\pi}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the collector current expression.**

$$
I_c(s) = g_m V_{b'e}(s) = \frac{g_m r_\pi I_b(s)}{1 + s r_\pi C_\pi}
$$

**Step 5 — Use the identity $g_m r_\pi = \beta$.**

$$
A_{ih}(s) = \frac{I_c(s)}{I_b(s)} = \frac{\beta}{1 + s r_\pi C_\pi}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,A_{ih}(s) = \frac{\beta}{1 + s r_\pi C_\pi}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The pole frequency is $f_\beta = \dfrac{1}{2\pi r_\pi C_\pi}$ — this is the $-3$ dB cutoff of the short-circuit current gain.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Exact Short-Circuit Current Gain Including $C_\mu$

In a more rigorous derivation, $C_\mu$ is **not** short-circuited to ground when the output is shorted between collector and emitter. The voltage across $C_\mu$ is now $V_{b'c} = V_{b'e} - V_{ce} = V_{b'e}$ (since $V_{ce} = 0$ for shorted output).

**Step 1 — KCL at B′ (including current into $C_\mu$).**

$$
I_b(s) = \frac{V_{b'e}(s)}{r_\pi} + s C_\pi V_{b'e}(s) + s C_\mu V_{b'e}(s)
$$

**Step 2 — Collector current now has $C_\mu$ current subtracted** (it flows from B′ to C, away from the controlled source).

$$
I_c(s) = g_m V_{b'e}(s) - s C_\mu V_{b'e}(s)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $V_{b'e}(s)$ from the input equation.**

$$
V_{b'e}(s) = \frac{I_b(s)\, r_\pi}{1 + s r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the collector equation.**

$$
I_c(s) = \left(g_m - s C_\mu\right) \cdot \frac{I_b(s)\, r_\pi}{1 + s r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)}
$$

**Step 5 — Use $g_m r_\pi = \beta$ and rearrange.**

$$
A_{ih}(s) = \frac{\beta - s r_\pi C_\mu}{1 + s r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)}
$$

At low frequencies ($s \to 0$): $A_{ih} \to \beta$. ✓
For $|s r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)| \gg 1$: the high-frequency asymptote is determined by $\beta / [s r_\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)]$.

**Step 6 — Find the magnitude at high frequencies.** For $f \gg f_\beta$:

$$
|A_{ih}(j\omega)| \approx \frac{\beta}{\omega r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)} = \frac{g_m}{\omega (C_\pi + C_\mu)}
$$

**Step 7 — The Unity-Gain Frequency $f_T$.** Setting $|A_{ih}| = 1$:

$$
1 = \frac{g_m}{2\pi f_T (C_\pi + C_\mu)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f_T = \frac{g_m}{2\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)} = \beta f_\beta\,}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Application of Miller's Theorem to the CE Stage

**Scenario:** Common-emitter amplifier with a load resistor $R_L$ at the collector. The Miller capacitance appears between B′ and the collector (across the gain stage).

**Miller's Theorem Statement:** If an impedance $Z$ is connected between input node (1) and output node (2), and the voltage gain is $A_v = V_2/V_1$, then:

$$
Z_1 = \frac{Z}{1 - A_v} \quad \text{(impedance at input node)}
$$

$$
Z_2 = \frac{Z}{1 - 1/A_v} \quad \text{(impedance at output node)}
$$

**Step 1 — Identify $Z$ and $A_v$ for our case.**

The feedback capacitor $C_\mu$ connects B′ to C. For a CE amplifier with midband gain $|A_v| = g_m R_L$ (in magnitude), and with $A_v$ being negative:

$$
A_v = -g_m R_L
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Miller capacitance referred to the input.**

For a capacitor $C_\mu$ in the feedback path:

$$
C_{M1} = C_\mu (1 - A_v) = C_\mu(1 + g_m R_L)
$$

$$
C_{M2} = C_\mu \left(1 - \frac{1}{A_v}\right) \approx C_\mu
$$

**Step 3 — Total input capacitance.**

$$
\boxed{\,C_{in} = C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + g_m R_L)\,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Why the Miller effect is so damaging:** The factor $(1 + g_m R_L)$ can be **100 or more** in a typical CE amplifier. Even a small $C_\mu$ of $2$ pF becomes an effective $200$ pF at the input, drastically reducing the upper cutoff frequency.

**Step 4 — Upper 3-dB Frequency of the CE Amplifier.**

The input node sees a Thevenin resistance $R_{th}$ (e.g., $R_{th} = R_B \parallel r_\pi$ in a basic configuration) driving $C_{in}$:

$$
\boxed{\,f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th}\, C_{in}}\,}
$$

This shows the **Gain-Bandwidth Tradeoff**: increasing midband gain $g_m R_L$ increases $C_{in}$ and therefore reduces $f_H$.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Quantity | Low-Frequency Hybrid-π | High-Frequency Hybrid-π |
|---|---|---|
| Number of elements | 4 ($r_\pi, g_m v_{b'e}, r_o$, source) | 6+ ($r_b, r_\pi, C_\pi, C_\mu, g_m v_{b'e}, r_o$) |
| Frequency range | $f \ll f_\beta$ | $f$ up to $f_T$ |
| $h_{fe}$ behavior | Real constant $\beta$ | Complex $h_{fe}(j\omega) = \beta / (1 + jf/f_\beta)$ |
| Phase shift | $0°$ | $0°$ at low $f$, approaches $-90°$ at high $f$ |
| Validity for gain calc | $A_v = -g_m R_L$ (constant) | $A_v(s) = \dfrac{A_{v,mid}}{1 + s/f_H}$ |
| Use case | DC bias, low-freq amplifiers | RF, video, wideband design |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: High-Frequency Hybrid-π Model (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Bext[Base External Terminal B]
    rb[Base Spreading Resistance rb]
    Bp[Internal Base Node B prime]
    rpi[r pi]
    Cpi[C pi]
    E[Emitter]
    Cext[Collector External Terminal C]
    ro[Output Resistance ro]
    gmV[g m times V b prime e]
    Cmu[C mu]
    RL[Load R L]

    Bext --> rb
    rb --> Bp
    Bp --- rpi
    rpi --- E
    Bp --- Cpi
    Cpi --- E
    Bp --- Cmu
    Cmu --- N1[Collector Internal Node C prime]
    N1 --- ro
    ro --- E
    N1 --- gmV
    gmV --- E
    N1 --- RL
    RL --- E
    N1 --- Cext

    style Bext fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style Bp fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style E fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style Cext fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style gmV fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The **external base B** connects through $r_b$ to the **internal node B′**.
- Between **B′ and E**: $r_\pi$ is in parallel with $C_\pi$.
- Between **B′ and C′**: the feedback capacitor $C_\mu$ is placed.
- The **dependent current source** $g_m v_{b'e}$ flows from C′ to E.
- $r_o$ (Early resistance) is in parallel with the output port (often omitted at HF for simplicity).

## 4.2 Functional Flow: Frequency Response Computation Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input: BJT Bias Point and Geometry] --> B[Compute DC Quantities: IC, VBE]
    B --> C[Compute Small-Signal Parameters: gm, r pi, ro]
    C --> D[Determine Capacitances: C pi, C mu]
    D --> E[Form Hybrid-pi Equivalent Circuit]
    E --> F{Analysis Type?}
    F -- Short Circuit Current Gain --> G[Compute A ih s using KCL at B prime]
    F -- CE Amplifier with Load --> H[Apply Miller Theorem: C in equals C pi plus C mu times 1 plus gm RL]
    G --> I[Extract f beta equals 1 over 2 pi r pi C pi]
    H --> J[Extract f H equals 1 over 2 pi R th C in]
    I --> K[Compute f T equals beta times f beta]
    J --> L[Compute Gain-Bandwidth Product: GBP equals A mid times f H]
    K --> M[Final Output: Frequency Response Plot]
    L --> M

    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style M fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style F fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style H fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Short-Circuit Current Gain Test Setup

```mermaid
graph LR
    Ib[Source ib] --> Rpi[r pi]
    Ib --> Cpi2[C pi]
    Rpi --> E2[Emitter grounded]
    Cpi2 --> E2
    Bp2[Node B prime] --> Rpi
    Bp2 --> Cpi2
    Bp2 --> Cmu2[C mu]
    Cmu2 --> Cc[Collector shorted to ground]
    Bp2 -. controls .-> Gm2[Current source g m v]
    Gm2 --> Cc
    Cc --> E2

    style Ib fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style E2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style Gm2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
```

This shows the **$C_\mu$ is effectively in parallel with $C_\pi$** when the output is shorted, which is why the cutoff frequency becomes $f_\beta = 1 / [2\pi r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)]$ in the more accurate model.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (2 marks each — short answer)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define transconductance $g_m$ of a BJT. Obtain its relationship with $\beta$ and $r_\pi$.**

**Model Answer:**

Transconductance $g_m$ is the small-signal parameter that quantifies the **change in collector current per unit change in base-emitter voltage**, with collector-emitter voltage held constant.

**Derivation of the relationship:**

Starting from the Ebers-Moll model, the collector current in the active region is:
$$I_C = I_S e^{V_{BE}/V_T}$$

Differentiating with respect to $V_{BE}$ at the operating point:
$$g_m = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial V_{BE}} = \frac{I_C}{V_T}$$

The base-emitter resistance $r_\pi$ is defined as:
$$r_\pi = \frac{\partial V_{BE}}{\partial I_B} = \frac{V_T}{I_B}$$

The current gain $\beta$ is:
$$\beta = \frac{I_C}{I_B}$$

Combining the three:
$$\beta = g_m \cdot r_\pi \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\,g_m = \frac{\beta}{r_\pi} = \frac{I_C}{V_T}\,}$$

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is Miller's theorem? How is it used in the analysis of the high-frequency hybrid-π model?**

**Model Answer:**

**Miller's Theorem** states that an impedance $Z$ connected between two nodes whose voltages are related by a gain factor $A_v$ can be replaced by two separate impedances to ground:

$$
Z_1 = \frac{Z}{1 - A_v} \quad \text{(referred to input node)}
$$

$$
Z_2 = \frac{Z}{1 - 1/A_v} \quad \text{(referred to output node)}
$$

**Application to the High-Frequency Hybrid-π Model:**

In a Common-Emitter amplifier, the feedback capacitor $C_\mu$ connects the input node (B′) to the output node (collector). With midband gain $A_v = -g_m R_L$, Miller's theorem replaces $C_\mu$ by:
- An input-referred capacitance: $C_{M1} = C_\mu(1 + g_m R_L)$
- An output-referred capacitance: $C_{M2} \approx C_\mu$

The **effective input capacitance** becomes:
$$C_{in} = C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + g_m R_L)$$

This is called the **Miller effect** and is the principal cause of bandwidth reduction in CE amplifiers. **(3 marks)**

---

## 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (14 marks — internal choice between Question A and Question B)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the expression for the short-circuit common-emitter current gain $A_{ih}(s)$ using the high-frequency hybrid-π model of a BJT. Hence obtain the $\beta$-cutoff frequency $f_\beta$ and the unity-gain frequency $f_T$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Draw the high-frequency hybrid-π model with output shorted.**

[Refer to the schematic in Section 4.3]

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at node B′ (including $C_\mu$ contribution since $V_{b'c} = V_{b'e} \neq 0$):**
$$I_b(s) = V_{b'e}(s)\left[\frac{1}{r_\pi} + s(C_\pi + C_\mu)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Express collector current:**
$$I_c(s) = g_m V_{b'e}(s) - s C_\mu V_{b'e}(s) = V_{b'e}(s)(g_m - sC_\mu)$$

**Step 4 — Eliminate $V_{b'e}(s)$ using the input equation:**
$$V_{b'e}(s) = \frac{I_b(s)\, r_\pi}{1 + s r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and use $g_m r_\pi = \beta$:**
$$A_{ih}(s) = \frac{I_c(s)}{I_b(s)} = \frac{\beta - s r_\pi C_\mu}{1 + s r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)}$$

**Step 6 — At low frequencies** ($s \to 0$): $A_{ih} \to \beta$. ✓

**Step 7 — At high frequencies**, the asymptotic magnitude is:
$$|A_{ih}(j\omega)| \approx \frac{\beta}{\omega r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)} = \frac{g_m}{\omega(C_\pi + C_\mu)}$$

**Step 8 — Define $f_\beta$** as the $-3$ dB frequency (the pole):
$$f_\beta = \frac{1}{2\pi r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)}$$

**Step 9 — Define $f_T$** as the frequency at which $|A_{ih}| = 1$:
$$\boxed{\,f_T = \frac{g_m}{2\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)} = \beta \cdot f_\beta\,}$$

**Step 10 — Relation to transit time.** Since $C_\pi \approx g_m \tau_F$ and $C_\pi \gg C_\mu$ in modern BJTs:
$$f_T \approx \frac{1}{2\pi \tau_F}$$

This is the **fundamental high-frequency limit** of the BJT — it is governed by how quickly carriers traverse the base region. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A BJT operating at $I_C = 1$ mA, $V_{CE} = 5$ V has $g_m = 40$ mA/V, $r_\pi = 2.5$ k$\Omega$, $C_\pi = 100$ pF, and $C_\mu = 2$ pF. The transistor is used in a CE amplifier with $R_L = 2$ k$\Omega$ and source resistance $R_s = 1$ k$\Omega$. Compute:**
- **(i) The $\beta$-cutoff frequency $f_\beta$.**
- **(ii) The unity-gain frequency $f_T$.**
- **(iii) The upper 3-dB frequency $f_H$ of the amplifier (using Miller's theorem).**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) $\beta$-cutoff frequency:**
$$f_\beta = \frac{1}{2\pi r_\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2500 \times (100 + 2)\times 10^{-12}}$$

$$
f_\beta = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2500 \times 102 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{1.602 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 624 \text{ kHz}
$$

**(ii) Unity-gain frequency:**

First compute $\beta = g_m r_\pi = 0.040 \times 2500 = 100$.

$$f_T = \beta \cdot f_\beta = 100 \times 624 \text{ kHz} = 62.4 \text{ MHz}$$

**Cross-check using $f_T = g_m/[2\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)]$:**
$$f_T = \frac{0.040}{2\pi \times 102 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{0.040}{6.408 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 62.4 \text{ MHz} \checkmark$$

**(iii) Upper 3-dB frequency using Miller's theorem:**

The effective input capacitance:
$$C_{in} = C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + g_m R_L)$$

$$g_m R_L = 0.040 \times 2000 = 80$$

$$C_{in} = 100 \text{ pF} + 2 \text{ pF} \times (1 + 80) = 100 + 162 = 262 \text{ pF}$$

Thevenin resistance at input (assuming $R_s = 1$ k$\Omega$ and ignoring $r_\pi$ for simplicity, or $R_{th} = R_s \parallel r_\pi$):
$$R_{th} = \frac{1000 \times 2500}{1000 + 2500} = 714.3 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_{in}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 714.3 \times 262 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$
f_H = \frac{1}{1.176 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 850 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Final Answer:** $f_\beta \approx 624$ kHz, $f_T \approx 62.4$ MHz, $f_H \approx 850$ kHz. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the physical origin of the capacitances $C_\pi$ and $C_\mu$ in a BJT. Discuss the role of Miller's theorem in determining the high-frequency response of a common-emitter amplifier.**

**Model Solution:**

**Origin of $C_\pi$:**

$C_\pi$ represents the **total capacitance between the base and emitter** terminals of the BJT. It has two components:

**(1) Base-Emitter Depletion Capacitance $C_{be}$:** This arises from the charge stored in the depletion region of the forward-biased base-emitter junction. It is described by:
$$C_{be} = \frac{C_{je0}}{(1 - V_{BE}/V_{bi})^m}$$
where $C_{je0}$ is the zero-bias value, $V_{bi}$ is the built-in potential, and $m$ is the grading coefficient (typically 0.5 for an abrupt junction).

**(2) Base-Emitter Diffusion Capacitance $C_{b,diff}$:** This is the **dominant** component in the active region. It represents the **minority-carrier charge stored in the quasi-neutral base region**. From the charge-control model:
$$Q_B = \tau_F I_C \quad \Longrightarrow \quad C_{b,diff} = \frac{dQ_B}{dV_{BE}} = \frac{\tau_F I_C}{V_T} = g_m \tau_F$$

Hence, $C_\pi = C_{be} + g_m \tau_F \approx g_m \tau_F$ (in active region).

**Origin of $C_\mu$:**

$C_\mu$ is the **capacitance of the reverse-biased base-collector junction depletion region**. Since this junction is reverse-biased, only depletion capacitance exists (no diffusion capacitance). It is given by:
$$C_\mu = \frac{C_{\mu 0}}{(1 - V_{BC}/V_{bi})^m}$$

Physically, $C_\mu$ is small (a few pF) because the reverse-biased depletion region is wide, but its **Miller effect** amplifies its impact on input.

**Role of Miller's Theorem:**

In a CE amplifier, $C_\mu$ bridges the high-gain input and output nodes. Using Miller's theorem with $A_v = -g_m R_L$:
$$C_{M,in} = C_\mu(1 + g_m R_L)$$

This greatly enlarges the effective input capacitance, creating a low-pass pole with the source resistance $R_s$:
$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi(R_s \parallel r_\pi)[C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + g_m R_L)]}$$

**Key Insight:** Although $C_\mu$ is small, the Miller multiplier $(1 + g_m R_L)$ can be 50–500, making $C_{M,in}$ the **dominant** term that limits the bandwidth. This is the fundamental reason cascode and emitter-follower topologies are preferred in high-frequency design. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**For a BJT with $f_T = 500$ MHz and $\beta = 125$ at $I_C = 5$ mA, calculate:**
- **(i) The $\beta$-cutoff frequency $f_\beta$.**
- **(ii) The forward transit time $\tau_F$.**
- **(iii) The diffusion capacitance $C_{b,diff}$ at the given bias.**
- **(iv) The approximate upper cutoff frequency $f_H$ if the BJT is used in a CE stage with $R_L = 1$ k$\Omega$, $R_s = 500$ $\Omega$, $C_\mu = 1$ pF, and $C_{be} = 5$ pF.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) $\beta$-cutoff frequency:**
$$f_\beta = \frac{f_T}{\beta} = \frac{500 \times 10^6}{125} = 4 \text{ MHz}$$

**(ii) Forward transit time:**
$$\tau_F = \frac{1}{2\pi f_T} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 500 \times 10^6} = \frac{1}{3.142 \times 10^9} \approx 318 \text{ ps}$$

**(iii) Diffusion capacitance:**

Using $V_T = 25$ mV:
$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{25 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.2 \text{ A/V}$$

$$C_{b,diff} = g_m \tau_F = 0.2 \times 318 \times 10^{-12} = 63.66 \text{ pF}$$

**(iv) Upper cutoff frequency of the CE stage:**

Total $C_\pi = C_{be} + C_{b,diff} = 5 + 63.66 = 68.66$ pF.

Thevenin resistance at input (assume $r_\pi = \beta V_T/I_C = 125 \times 25/5 = 625$ $\Omega$):
$$R_{th} = R_s \parallel r_\pi = \frac{500 \times 625}{500 + 625} = 278 \text{ }\Omega$$

Miller multiplier:
$$1 + g_m R_L = 1 + 0.2 \times 1000 = 201$$

Effective input capacitance:
$$C_{in} = 68.66 + 1 \times 201 = 269.66 \text{ pF}$$

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_{in}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 278 \times 269.66 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$
f_H \approx 2.12 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Observations:** Notice that $f_H \ll f_\beta$ due to the Miller effect. The original $f_T$ of the device is 500 MHz, but the usable CE bandwidth is only $\sim$2 MHz — a 250× reduction. This demonstrates the **Gain-Bandwidth Tradeoff** in CE amplifiers. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔥 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Points**
> 1. **Forgetting to add $C_\mu$ inside the cutoff frequency formula:** Many students write $f_\beta = 1/(2\pi r_\pi C_\pi)$ — this is **only valid** if $C_\mu$ is short-circuited. The exact expression uses $(C_\pi + C_\mu)$.
> 2. **Missing the $\tau_F$ concept:** Examiners often test $f_T = 1/(2\pi \tau_F)$ — make sure you can derive the transit-time relationship.
> 3. **Sign error in Miller's theorem:** $A_v$ is **negative** for CE, so $1 - A_v = 1 + |A_v|$. Writing $1 - g_m R_L$ instead of $1 + g_m R_L$ is a frequent mistake.
> 4. **Mixing up $f_\beta$ and $f_H$:** $f_\beta$ is a **device property**; $f_H$ depends on the **external circuit** (source resistance, load, Miller effect).
> 5. **Not converting units:** $pF = 10^{-12}$ F, $k\Omega = 10^3$ $\Omega$ — careful with powers of 10!
> 6. **Using $\beta$ where $g_m r_\pi$ should be:** $\beta = g_m r_\pi$ is a fundamental identity; if you forget this, the derivation collapses.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ The **High-Frequency Hybrid-π Model** extends the low-frequency model by adding $C_\pi$ (B-E) and $C_\mu$ (B-C) capacitances.
- ✅ $C_\pi$ is dominated by the **diffusion capacitance** $g_m \tau_F$ in the active region.
- ✅ $C_\mu$ is the **reverse-biased base-collector junction capacitance** — small in value but devastating due to Miller effect.
- ✅ The **transconductance** is $g_m = I_C/V_T$, and $r_\pi = \beta/g_m$.
- ✅ **Short-circuit current gain:** $A_{ih}(s) = \dfrac{\beta}{1 + s r_\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)}$.
- ✅ **$\beta$-cutoff frequency:** $f_\beta = \dfrac{1}{2\pi r_\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)}$ — the $-3$ dB frequency of $h_{fe}$.
- ✅ **Unity-gain frequency:** $f_T = \beta f_\beta = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau_F}$ — the **fundamental high-frequency limit** of the BJT.
- ✅ **Miller's Theorem** converts the feedback capacitor $C_\mu$ into an input capacitance $C_{M1} = C_\mu(1 + g_m R_L)$ — this is the **Miller effect**.
- ✅ **Upper 3-dB frequency** of CE amplifier: $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{th} [C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + g_m R_L)]}$.
- ✅ **Gain-Bandwidth Tradeoff:** Increasing midband gain $g_m R_L$ → increases Miller capacitance → reduces $f_H$.
- ✅ Practical remedies for Miller effect: **cascode amplifier**, **emitter follower**, **neutralization** techniques.
- ✅ Base spreading resistance $r_b$ further degrades HF response by adding series resistance; the actual cutoff becomes $f_H = 1/[2\pi(R_s + r_b)C_{in}]$ in more exact analyses.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Hack:** For any BJT-HF problem in KTU, follow this **3-step mental algorithm**:
> 1. Compute $g_m$ from $I_C$ and $V_T$.
> 2. Identify $C_\pi$ and $C_\mu$ (given or from $\tau_F$).
> 3. Plug into the relevant formula from the table in Section 2.3 — $f_\beta$, $f_T$, or $f_H$ as required.
> 
> This simple flow covers **90% of KTU ESE questions** on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Miller Effect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Miller Effect ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) In the context of analog electronics and small-signal analysis of transistor amplifiers, the **Miller Effect** (or **Miller's Theorem**) is a phenomenon where an impedance connected between the **input node** and the **output node** of an inverting amplifier appears as a multiplied (scaled) impedance when "looked into" from either the input or the output terminal. Mathematically, a floating impedance $Z$ between two nodes having a voltage gain $A_v = v_o/v_i$ across them is split into two grounded impedances at those nodes: a Miller input impedance $Z_1 = Z/(1 - A_v)$ and a Miller output impedance $Z_2 = Z \cdot A_v/(A_v - 1)$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCECT303 – Module 2):** > The Miller Effect is a foundational concept required to analyze the **high-frequency response** of BJT and FET small-signal amplifiers. It directly influences the **dominant pole**, the **upper 3-dB cutoff frequency ($f_H$)**, and the **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)** of common-emitter and common-source stages. > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition – Miller Capacitance:** > When the floating impedance $Z$ is purely capacitive ($Z = 1/sC = 1/j\omega C$), the input-side Miller capacitance becomes $C_{M,in} = C(1 - A_v)$ and the output-side Miller capacitance becomes $C_{M,out} = C(A_v - 1)/A_v$. Since $|A_v| \gg 1$ in a typical inverting amplifier, $C_{M,in}$ is **drastically enlarged**, shunting high-frequency signals to ground and limiting the bandwidth. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Elastic Bridge" Picture Imagine a **suspension bridge** with two towers (input and output) connected by a thick elastic cable (the floating capacitor $C$). - The input tower vibrates a little (small input signal), but the output tower vibrates **enormously** in the opposite direction (because the amplifier has high gain $A_v$). - Because the bridge amplifies the movement of the cable's right end, the left end (input) is forced to push against a **much larger mechanical resistance** for the same voltage. - To the input source, the cable *behaves as if* it were **many times stiffer** (i.e., the capacitance appears multiplied). In electronics, this "stiffness amplification" is exactly what the Miller Effect does: **a small physical capacitor between input and output appears as a huge capacitance at the input**, which dramatically reduces the high-frequency cutoff of the amplifier. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used in This Topic - **Open-circuit voltage gain** $|A_v| = \vert v_o / v_i \vert$ — dimensionless, typically **10 to 1000** in analog stages. - **Angular cutoff frequency** $\omega_H = 1/R_{th} C_{in,total}$ — measured in **rad/s**. - **3-dB bandwidth** $f_H = \omega_H / (2\pi)$ — measured in **Hz**. - **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)** $= A_{mid} \cdot f_H$ — a **figure of merit** in **Hz** or **MHz**. - **Miller Multiplication Factor** $(1 - A_v)$ — dimensionless, often called the **Miller Capacitance Multiplier** in KTU answer sheets. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic – Step-by-Step Breakdown of Miller's Theorem Miller's Theorem is built on a single core idea: **KCL must be satisfied before and after splitting the floating impedance.** The reasoning is as follows: 1. **Identify a floating impedance $Z$** between node 1 (input, voltage $v_1$) and node 2 (output, voltage $v_2$) of an amplifier. 2. **Calculate the voltage gain** $A_v = v_2 / v_1$ of the amplifier under the assumption that adding $Z$ does not appreciably change the operating point (loading effects are negligible, or we use the **unloaded** gain). 3. **The current through the floating impedance** is given by the fundamental relation: $$ i_Z = \frac{v_1 - v_2}{Z} = \frac{v_1 (1 - A_v)}{Z} $$ 4. **Equate this current to a current drawn from node 1 alone** by a shunt impedance $Z_1$ to ground: $$ \frac{v_1}{Z_1} \equiv \frac{v_1 (1 - A_v)}{Z} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; Z_1 = \frac{Z}{1 - A_v} $$ 5. **Repeat the analysis at node 2** to obtain the output-side Miller impedance: $$ Z_2 = \frac{Z \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1} $$ 6. **Verify the theorem** by ensuring the input power, output power, and total current flow are conserved (lossless equivalence) — this is the **"Why it works"** behind Miller's Theorem. ### 2.2 Why the Miller Effect Matters – The "How" Behind the Math - **High-Frequency Roll-off:** In a Common-Emitter (CE) BJT or Common-Source (CS) FET amplifier, the **base-collector (or gate-drain) parasitic capacitance** $C_\mu$ (or $C_{gd}$) sits between input and output. Because the CE/CS stage is **inverting** ($A_v < 0$), $C_\mu$ is multiplied by $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$, which is enormous. - **Dominant Pole Creation:** This multiplied capacitance, in parallel with the source resistance $R_S$ and the input resistance $R_{in}$, forms an **RC low-pass filter** that creates the **dominant pole** $f_H$ of the amplifier. - **Bandwidth Reduction:** Without accounting for the Miller Effect, the predicted bandwidth is **grossly overestimated** — a classic KTU board exam pitfall. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU Board Exams (PCECT303 – Module 2).** Note the use of `\mid` instead of `|` to avoid breaking the markdown table structure. | **#** | **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remarks** | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Miller Input Impedance | $Z_1$ | $Z / (1 - A_v)$ | $\Omega$ | Shunt at input node to ground | | 2 | Miller Output Impedance | $Z_2$ | $Z \cdot A_v / (A_v - 1)$ | $\Omega$ | Shunt at output node to ground | | 3 | Miller Input Capacitance | $C_{M1}$ | $C \cdot (1 - A_v)$ | F | For floating cap $C$, with $A_v$ complex gain | | 4 | Miller Output Capacitance | $C_{M2}$ | $C \cdot (A_v - 1) / A_v$ | F | Often small, usually neglected | | 5 | Mid-band Voltage Gain (CE) | $A_v$ | $-g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$ | dimensionless | Negative because CE is inverting | | 6 | Total Input Capacitance | $C_{in,total}$ | $C_\pi + C_{M1} = C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ | F | Drives high-frequency cutoff | | 7 | Upper Cutoff Frequency | $f_H$ | $1 / [2\pi R_{th} \cdot C_{in,total}]$ | Hz | Dominant pole approximation | | 8 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | $GBW$ | $\mid A_{mid} \mid \cdot f_H \approx g_m / (2\pi C_\mu)$ | Hz | Independent of $R_C$ in CE | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** The Miller Effect dictates the **maximum operating frequency** of integrated-circuit amplifiers (e.g., op-amp slew rate, op-amp gain-bandwidth). It is also the reason **cascode amplifiers** and **neutralized stages** are designed — they cancel or minimize Miller multiplication to extend bandwidth into the **GHz regime** in RFICs. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **RF and Microwave IC Design:** Engineers use **Miller neutralization** (adding a feed-forward capacitor) to cancel the input Miller capacitance in narrowband RF amplifiers. - **Op-Amp Data Sheets:** The **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)** listed in every op-amp datasheet (TL081, LM741, NE5532) is essentially the inverse signature of the Miller Effect. - **High-Speed ADC Buffers:** Choosing a current-feedback op-amp (CFA) over a voltage-feedback op-amp (VFA) is often necessary because CFAs **avoid the Miller multiplication** of $C_{gd}$ in the input stage, achieving slew rates > 1000 V/µs. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Miller's Theorem **Statement:** A floating impedance $Z$ connected between two nodes (with node voltages $v_1$ and $v_2$) can be replaced by two grounded impedances $Z_1$ (at node 1) and $Z_2$ (at node 2), where: $$ Z_1 = \frac{Z}{1 - A_v}, \qquad Z_2 = \frac{Z \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1} $$ provided $A_v = v_2 / v_1$ is the voltage gain between the two nodes. --- **Step 1 — Setup and Assumptions** Consider an inverting amplifier with an open-circuit voltage gain $A_v$. A floating impedance $Z$ is connected between the input node (voltage $v_1$) and the output node (voltage $v_2$), as shown in the block diagram in Section 4. Assumption: $Z$ is large enough that it does not significantly load the amplifier (i.e., it does not change the unloaded gain $A_v$). --- **Step 2 — Write the Current Through the Floating Impedance $Z$** Using Ohm's law, the current flowing from node 1 to node 2 through $Z$ is: $$ i_Z = \frac{v_1 - v_2}{Z} $$ **Logic:** The potential difference across $Z$ is the difference between the input node voltage and the output node voltage. The direction (node 1 → node 2) is conventional. --- **Step 3 — Express $v_2$ in Terms of $v_1$** By definition of the voltage gain: $$ v_2 = A_v \cdot v_1 $$ Substitute this into the current expression: $$ i_Z = \frac{v_1 - A_v v_1}{Z} = \frac{v_1 (1 - A_v)}{Z} $$ --- **Step 4 — Equate to a Shunt Impedance at Node 1** Now imagine replacing $Z$ with a shunt impedance $Z_1$ connected from node 1 to ground. The current through $Z_1$ would be: $$ i_{Z1} = \frac{v_1 - 0}{Z_1} = \frac{v_1}{Z_1} $$ For equivalence (same current drawn from node 1): $$ \frac{v_1}{Z_1} = \frac{v_1 (1 - A_v)}{Z} $$ Cancelling $v_1$ (non-zero signal): $$ \boxed{\,Z_1 = \frac{Z}{1 - A_v}\,} $$ **Logic:** This is the **Miller input impedance**. For an inverting amplifier with $A_v < 0$ and large magnitude, $(1 - A_v) = (1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ is large, so $Z_1$ is much **smaller** than $Z$. If $Z$ is a capacitor, a small $Z_1$ means a **large capacitance** at the input — the famous Miller capacitance multiplication. --- **Step 5 — Derive the Output-Side Miller Impedance $Z_2$** The same current $i_Z$ must also enter node 2. From node 2's perspective, the current flowing *out* of node 2 through $Z$ (i.e., entering node 1) is: $$ i_Z = \frac{v_2 - v_1}{Z} = \frac{A_v v_1 - v_1}{Z} = \frac{v_1 (A_v - 1)}{Z} $$ Expressing in terms of $v_2 = A_v v_1$, so $v_1 = v_2 / A_v$: $$ i_Z = \frac{v_2 (A_v - 1)}{A_v \cdot Z} $$ This current must be supplied by a shunt $Z_2$ at node 2: $$ i_{Z2} = \frac{v_2}{Z_2} $$ Equating: $$ \frac{v_2}{Z_2} = \frac{v_2 (A_v - 1)}{A_v \cdot Z} $$ Cancelling $v_2$: $$ \boxed{\,Z_2 = \frac{Z \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1}\,} $$ --- **Step 6 — Specialization to a Capacitive Impedance $Z = 1/(sC)$** For $Z$ being a pure capacitor $C$ connected between input and output, we have $Z = 1/(sC)$. Substituting into the Miller formulas: $$ Z_1 = \frac{1}{sC(1 - A_v)} = \frac{1}{s C(1 - A_v)} $$ This is equivalent to a capacitor of value: $$ C_{M1} = C(1 - A_v) $$ at node 1 to ground. Similarly: $$ Z_2 = \frac{A_v}{sC(A_v - 1)} = \frac{1}{sC(A_v - 1)/A_v} $$ This is equivalent to a capacitor of value: $$ C_{M2} = \frac{C(A_v - 1)}{A_v} $$ at node 2 to ground. --- **Step 7 — Special Case: Inverting Amplifier with $A_v = -A$ where $A > 0$** For a typical CE/CS amplifier, $A_v$ is negative. Let $A_v = -A$ where $A = \vert A_v \vert$ is the **magnitude** of the gain. Then: $$ C_{M1} = C(1 - (-A)) = C(1 + A) $$ $$ C_{M2} = \frac{C(-A - 1)}{-A} = \frac{C(A + 1)}{A} = C\left(1 + \frac{1}{A}\right) $$ For $A \gg 1$ (typical case): $$ C_{M1} \approx C \cdot A \;\;\text{(very large)} $$ $$ C_{M2} \approx C \;\;\text{(small, often negligible)} $$ This asymmetry is the entire reason the **input side is the bandwidth-limiting node** in a CE/CS amplifier. --- **Step 8 — Frequency Response and Gain-Bandwidth Product** The high-frequency cutoff of a CE amplifier is dominated by the input RC time constant: $$ \tau_H = (R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_\pi) \cdot (C_\pi + C_{M1}) = R_{th} \cdot (C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + A)) $$ The 3-dB upper cutoff frequency is: $$ f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \tau_H} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th}(C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + A))} $$ The mid-band gain of a CE stage with $R_C \parallel R_L = R'_L$ is: $$ A_{mid} = -g_m R'_L $$ The Gain-Bandwidth Product (a constant for a given transistor) is: $$ GBW = \vert A_{mid} \vert \cdot f_H = g_m R'_L \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th}(C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + A))} $$ Using $R'_L / R_{th} \approx 1$ (in a well-designed stage) and $C_\pi \ll C_\mu(1 + A)$ (Miller-dominated): $$ GBW \approx \frac{g_m}{2\pi C_\mu} $$ **This is the key result:** GBW is approximately constant for a given transistor, illustrating the classic **gain-bandwidth trade-off** enforced by the Miller Effect. --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Board Style) **Problem:** A common-emitter amplifier has a load resistance $R_C = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, a source resistance $R_S = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, transistor parameters $g_m = 50 \text{ mA/V}$, $r_\pi = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_\pi = 10 \text{ pF}$, and $C_\mu = 2 \text{ pF}$. Determine: - (i) The mid-band voltage gain. - (ii) The total input capacitance using the Miller Effect. - (iii) The upper 3-dB cutoff frequency $f_H$. **Solution:** **Part (i): Mid-band Gain** $$ A_{mid} = -g_m R_C = -(50 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (4.7 \times 10^3) = -235 \text{ V/V} $$ **Part (ii): Miller Input Capacitance** $$ C_{M1} = C_\mu (1 + \vert A_{mid} \vert) = 2 \text{ pF} \cdot (1 + 235) = 2 \cdot 236 = 472 \text{ pF} $$ $$ C_{in,total} = C_\pi + C_{M1} = 10 \text{ pF} + 472 \text{ pF} = 482 \text{ pF} $$ **Part (iii): Upper Cutoff Frequency** Thevenin resistance at the input: $$ R_{th} = R_S \parallel r_\pi = \frac{1 \text{ k}\Omega \cdot 1 \text{ k}\Omega}{1 \text{ k}\Omega + 1 \text{ k}\Omega} = 500 \, \Omega $$ Upper cutoff: $$ f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_{in,total}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 500 \cdot 482 \times 10^{-12}} $$ $$ f_H = \frac{1}{1.514 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 660.5 \text{ kHz} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sanity Check:** If we ignored the Miller Effect and used $C_{in} = C_\pi + C_\mu = 12 \text{ pF}$, the predicted $f_H$ would be $\approx 26.5 \text{ MHz}$ — a factor of **40×** too high! This is the most common KTU board-exam error: omitting the Miller multiplication. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Floating Impedance Replacement The diagram below illustrates how a floating impedance between two amplifier nodes is replaced by two grounded impedances (the essence of Miller's Theorem). ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeIn["INPUT NODE V1"] -->|Vin| nodeAmp["INVERTING AMPLIFIER
Gain Av = Vo / Vi"] nodeAmp -->|Vout| nodeOut["OUTPUT NODE V2"] nodeZ["FLOATING IMPEDANCE Z
Connected between V1 and V2"] -.-> nodeIn nodeZ -.-> nodeOut nodeIn --> nodeZ1["MILLER INPUT IMPEDANCE
Z1 = Z / 1 minus Av
Shunt to GND"] nodeZ1 --> nodeGnd1["GROUND"] nodeOut --> nodeZ2["MILLER OUTPUT IMPEDANCE
Z2 = Z * Av / Av minus 1
Shunt to GND"] nodeZ2 --> nodeGnd2["GROUND"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The dashed lines represent the **original floating configuration**, while the solid lines on the right represent the **equivalent Miller-split configuration**. The grounded impedances $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ are entirely equivalent to $Z$ for all external terminal currents and voltages. --- ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — CE Amplifier with Miller Capacitance ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeSrc["SIGNAL SOURCE Vs
with Source Resistance Rs"] --> nodeCpl["COUPLING CAPACITOR Cin
Short at Mid-band"] nodeCpl --> nodeBase["BJT BASE NODE
r_pi to GND
C_pi to GND"] nodeBase --> nodeBjt["BJT in Common Emitter
gm * Vbe Current Source"] nodeBjt --> nodeColl["BJT COLLECTOR NODE
Rc to VCC
C_mu Miller to Base"] nodeColl --> nodeCout["COUPLING CAPACITOR Cout
Short at Mid-band"] nodeCout --> nodeLoad["LOAD RL"] nodeBase --> nodeMiller["MILLER MULTIPLICATION
C_M1 = C_mu * 1 + Av"] nodeMiller --> nodeTotalCin["TOTAL INPUT CAPACITANCE
C_in = C_pi + C_M1"] nodeTotalCin --> nodePole["DOMINANT POLE
fH = 1 over 2pi Rs_eq * C_in"] nodePole --> nodeGbw["GAIN BANDWIDTH PRODUCT
GBW = g_m over 2pi C_mu"] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** Notice the **asymmetry** — the Miller capacitance is referenced from the **input side** because that's where the **dominant pole** forms. The output-side Miller capacitance is much smaller and is usually absorbed into the output node's stray capacitance for second-order analysis. --- ### 4.3 Concept-Mapping Diagram — From Physical Capacitor to Miller Capacitance ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["PHYSICAL C_mu
2 pF between Base and Collector"] --> nodeB["GAIN Av = NEGATIVE 235
CE Inverting Stage"] nodeB --> nodeC["MILLER FACTOR
1 plus Av magnitude = 236"] nodeC --> nodeD["MILLER CAPACITANCE
C_M1 = 2 pF times 236 = 472 pF"] nodeD --> nodeE["EFFECTIVE INPUT CAP
C_in total = 482 pF"] nodeE --> nodeF["CUTOFF FREQUENCY
fH drops to 660 kHz from 26 MHz"] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 1, 3 Marks]** *State and explain Miller's Theorem. Why is it particularly significant in the high-frequency analysis of common-emitter amplifiers?* **Model Answer:** **Statement (1 mark):** Miller's Theorem states that a floating impedance $Z$ connected between the input node (voltage $v_1$) and the output node (voltage $v_2$) of an amplifier with voltage gain $A_v = v_2/v_1$ can be replaced by two grounded impedances: - Input-side: $Z_1 = Z/(1 - A_v)$ - Output-side: $Z_2 = Z \cdot A_v/(A_v - 1)$ **Explanation (1 mark):** This splitting is valid because KCL is preserved at both nodes. The current through the floating $Z$ from node 1 to node 2 is the same as the current that would flow into the two grounded equivalents, given the voltage gain $A_v$. **Significance in CE Amplifiers (1 mark):** In a common-emitter stage, the base-collector parasitic capacitance $C_\mu$ is a floating capacitance. Because the CE stage is inverting with large gain magnitude $\vert A_v \vert$, the Miller input capacitance $C_{M1} = C_\mu(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ becomes very large (hundreds of pF even when $C_\mu$ is only a few pF). This drastically reduces the upper 3-dB cutoff frequency $f_H$ and dominates the high-frequency roll-off. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary, 3 Marks]** *What is Miller capacitance? Derive the expression for the input-side Miller capacitance of a common-emitter amplifier in terms of the base-collector capacitance $C_\mu$ and the mid-band gain $A_v$.* **Model Answer:** **Definition (1 mark):** Miller capacitance is the **equivalent input capacitance** that appears at the input node of an inverting amplifier due to the multiplication effect of a floating (base-collector) capacitance $C_\mu$ between input and output. **Derivation (2 marks):** The floating impedance between base and collector is: $$ Z = \frac{1}{s C_\mu} $$ Applying Miller's Theorem with $A_v$ being the (negative) gain from base to collector: $$ Z_1 = \frac{Z}{1 - A_v} = \frac{1}{s C_\mu (1 - A_v)} $$ Since $A_v < 0$, let $A_v = -A$ where $A = \vert A_v \vert$: $$ Z_1 = \frac{1}{s C_\mu (1 + A)} = \frac{1}{s C_{M1}} $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{\,C_{M1} = C_\mu (1 + \vert A_v \vert)\,} $$ This is the input-side Miller capacitance expression. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- **Q(A). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Exam, Module 2, 14 Marks]** **(a)** A common-emitter amplifier uses a BJT with $g_m = 40 \text{ mA/V}$, $r_\pi = 1.25 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_\pi = 5 \text{ pF}$, and $C_\mu = 1.5 \text{ pF}$. The collector resistance is $R_C = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$ and the source resistance is $R_S = 600 \text{ }\Omega$. The load is open-circuited (high-impedance measurement). - (i) Calculate the mid-band voltage gain $A_{mid}$. - (ii) Determine the Miller input capacitance $C_{M1}$. - (iii) Compute the upper 3-dB cutoff frequency $f_H$. - (iv) What is the Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With reference to Miller's Theorem, explain the concept of **Miller Capacitance Multiplication**. Show how this effect limits the high-frequency response of a single-stage CE amplifier. Discuss **two** techniques used in integrated-circuit design to mitigate the Miller Effect. **(7 Marks)** --- **Solution to Q(A):** **Part (a) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)** **(i) Mid-band Gain (2 marks):** $$ A_{mid} = -g_m R_C = -(40 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (5 \times 10^3) = -200 \text{ V/V} $$ **(ii) Miller Input Capacitance (2 marks):** $$ C_{M1} = C_\mu (1 + \vert A_{mid} \vert) = 1.5 \text{ pF} \cdot (1 + 200) = 1.5 \times 201 = 301.5 \text{ pF} $$ Total input capacitance: $$ C_{in,total} = C_\pi + C_{M1} = 5 \text{ pF} + 301.5 \text{ pF} = 306.5 \text{ pF} $$ **(iii) Upper Cutoff Frequency (2 marks):** Thevenin resistance at the input (assuming $R_B \to \infty$, so $R_{th} = R_S \parallel r_\pi$): $$ R_{th} = \frac{R_S \cdot r_\pi}{R_S + r_\pi} = \frac{600 \cdot 1250}{600 + 1250} = \frac{750{,}000}{1850} \approx 405.4 \, \Omega $$ Upper 3-dB cutoff: $$ f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_{in,total}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 405.4 \cdot 306.5 \times 10^{-12}} $$ $$ f_H = \frac{1}{7.81 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 1.28 \text{ MHz} $$ **(iv) Gain-Bandwidth Product (1 mark):** $$ GBW = \vert A_{mid} \vert \cdot f_H = 200 \cdot 1.28 \text{ MHz} = 256 \text{ MHz} $$ Or, using the approximate formula: $$ GBW \approx \frac{g_m}{2\pi C_\mu} = \frac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{2\pi \cdot 1.5 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 4.24 \text{ GHz} $$ (The discrepancy arises because the approximate formula assumes $C_{M1} \gg C_\pi$, which holds weakly here. The exact $GBW$ of 256 MHz is the correct answer for the given circuit.) --- **Part (b) — Conceptual Discussion (7 Marks)** **Definition of Miller Capacitance Multiplication (2 marks):** Miller Capacitance Multiplication refers to the phenomenon whereby a small physical capacitance $C_\mu$ connected between the input and output nodes of an inverting amplifier appears at the input node as a much larger equivalent capacitance $C_{M1} = C_\mu(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$. The factor $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ is the **Miller multiplication factor**. **How it limits high-frequency response (3 marks):** In a single-stage common-emitter (CE) amplifier, the base-collector capacitance $C_\mu$ is inherently present due to the reverse-biased B-C junction. The CE stage inverts the signal with a large gain magnitude $\vert A_v \vert = g_m R_C$. By Miller's Theorem, the effective input capacitance becomes $C_{in} = C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$, which can be **40 to 200 times** larger than the physical $C_\mu$. This enlarged capacitance, in parallel with the source resistance $R_S$ and the input resistance $r_\pi$, forms a low-pass RC filter at the input. The dominant pole is $f_H = 1/[2\pi R_{th} C_{in}]$, which can be in the **sub-MHz range** even for transistors with $f_T$ in the GHz range. The product $A_{mid} \cdot f_H$ is approximately constant for a given transistor, enforcing a fundamental gain-bandwidth trade-off. **Two IC Design Techniques to Mitigate the Miller Effect (2 marks):** 1. **Cascode Configuration:** A common-base (or common-gate) transistor is stacked on top of the CE (or CS) stage. The cascode isolates the input transistor's collector from the output node, reducing the voltage swing across $C_\mu$ of the input transistor to nearly zero. This **drastically reduces** the Miller multiplication, allowing the stage to operate near the transistor's $f_T$. 2. **Miller Neutralization:** A small **feed-forward capacitor** $C_N$ is added between the input and output, driven by an inverting transformer or cross-coupled pair, so that it injects a current equal and opposite to that through $C_\mu$ at the signal frequency. This **cancels** the Miller effect at a specific frequency and is widely used in narrowband RF amplifier ICs. (Other valid techniques: emitter/source degeneration reducing $\vert A_v \vert$; differential signaling; current-feedback op-amps; use of high-$f_T$ HBT or HEMT devices.) --- **Q(B). [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary, Module 2, 14 Marks — Alternative to Q(A)]** **(a)** Derive Miller's Theorem from first principles. Starting with a floating impedance $Z$ between two nodes with voltages $v_1$ and $v_2$, show rigorously that this impedance can be replaced by an input-side impedance $Z_1 = Z/(1 - A_v)$ and an output-side impedance $Z_2 = Z A_v/(A_v - 1)$, where $A_v = v_2/v_1$. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** For a common-source (CS) MOSFET amplifier with the following parameters: $g_m = 5 \text{ mA/V}$, $R_D = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_S = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_{gs} = 5 \text{ pF}$, $C_{gd} = 1 \text{ pF}$, determine: - (i) The mid-band voltage gain. - (ii) The Miller input capacitance. - (iii) The upper 3-dB cutoff frequency $f_H$. - (iv) Comment on how the Miller Effect would change if the same MOSFET is used in a **cascode** configuration. **(7 Marks)** --- **Solution to Q(B):** **Part (a) — Derivation of Miller's Theorem (7 Marks)** **Setup (1 mark):** Consider a linear amplifier with two accessible nodes: the input node (voltage $v_1$) and the output node (voltage $v_2$). A floating impedance $Z$ is connected between these two nodes. Let $A_v = v_2 / v_1$ be the open-circuit voltage gain of the amplifier (assumed independent of the loading by $Z$). **Current through the floating $Z$ (2 marks):** By Ohm's Law, the current flowing from node 1 to node 2 through $Z$ is: $$ i_Z = \frac{v_1 - v_2}{Z} = \frac{v_1 - A_v v_1}{Z} = \frac{v_1 (1 - A_v)}{Z} $$ **Input-side equivalent (2 marks):** To represent the effect of $Z$ at node 1 alone, we introduce a shunt impedance $Z_1$ from node 1 to ground that draws the same current $i_Z$ when $v_1$ is applied. Therefore: $$ \frac{v_1}{Z_1} = \frac{v_1 (1 - A_v)}{Z} $$ Cancelling $v_1$ (non-zero): $$ \boxed{Z_1 = \frac{Z}{1 - A_v}} $$ **Output-side equivalent (2 marks):** The same current must flow out of node 2 into $Z$, which from node 2's perspective is: $$ i_Z = \frac{v_2 - v_1}{Z} = \frac{A_v v_1 - v_1}{Z} = \frac{v_1 (A_v - 1)}{Z} $$ Expressing in terms of $v_2 = A_v v_1 \Rightarrow v_1 = v_2/A_v$: $$ i_Z = \frac{v_2 (A_v - 1)}{A_v Z} $$ This must equal the current $v_2/Z_2$ drawn by a shunt $Z_2$ at node 2: $$ \frac{v_2}{Z_2} = \frac{v_2 (A_v - 1)}{A_v Z} \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{Z_2 = \frac{Z \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1}} $$ This completes the derivation of Miller's Theorem. --- **Part (b) — MOSFET CS Amplifier Analysis (7 Marks)** **(i) Mid-band Gain (1 mark):** $$ A_{mid} = -g_m R_D = -(5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (10 \times 10^3) = -50 \text{ V/V} $$ **(ii) Miller Input Capacitance (2 marks):** For a MOSFET, the floating capacitance between input (gate) and output (drain) is $C_{gd}$. The Miller input capacitance is: $$ C_{M1} = C_{gd} (1 + \vert A_{mid} \vert) = 1 \text{ pF} \cdot (1 + 50) = 51 \text{ pF} $$ Total input capacitance: $$ C_{in,total} = C_{gs} + C_{M1} = 5 \text{ pF} + 51 \text{ pF} = 56 \text{ pF} $$ **(iii) Upper Cutoff Frequency (2 marks):** Thevenin resistance at the input (gate draws no DC current, so $R_{th} = R_S$): $$ R_{th} = R_S = 1 \text{ k}\Omega $$ Upper 3-dB cutoff: $$ f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_{in,total}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 1000 \cdot 56 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{3.52 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 2.84 \text{ MHz} $$ **(iv) Cascode Comparison (2 marks):** In a **cascode** (CS stacked with CG), the drain of the input MOSFET is held at a nearly constant AC voltage because the cascode transistor's source follows the gate bias. The voltage gain from the input MOSFET's gate to its drain is therefore $\approx -1$ (or even less), not $-50$. The Miller multiplication factor becomes $(1 + 1) = 2$ instead of $(1 + 50) = 51$. Hence the effective input capacitance is reduced from $56 \text{ pF}$ to $\approx C_{gs} + 2 C_{gd} = 5 + 2 = 7 \text{ pF}$, an **8× reduction**. This pushes the upper cutoff frequency up by approximately the same factor, into the tens of MHz range, making the cascode far more suitable for high-frequency and RF applications. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (PCECT303 Module 2):** > 1. **Forgetting the sign of $A_v$:** A CE/CS amplifier has $A_v < 0$. Students often plug in $\vert A_v \vert$ and forget the negative sign in $A_v$ itself, which leads to **wrong** signs in $Z_1$ and $Z_2$. Always write $A_v = -A$ explicitly. > 2. **Omitting Miller Effect on $C_\mu$:** Computing $f_H$ using $C_{in} = C_\pi + C_\mu$ (without multiplication) is the **#1 mark-deducting error** in KTU board sheets. Always compute $C_{M1} = C_\mu(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ first. > 3. **Confusing input and output Miller impedances:** $Z_1$ is the **input-side** (looking from source) and $Z_2$ is the **output-side** (looking from load). Reversing them is a 1-mark penalty. > 4. **Approximating $R_{th}$:** $R_{th}$ is $R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_\pi$ for BJT and $R_S \parallel R_G$ for MOSFET (where $R_G$ is the bias network). Skipping $R_B$ or $R_G$ is a frequent mistake. > 5. **Forgetting units in $C_\mu$:** Always convert pF to F before computing $f_H$ in Hz. A wrong unit prefix here causes order-of-magnitude errors. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep)** - ✅ **Miller's Theorem** splits a floating impedance $Z$ into two grounded impedances: $Z_1 = Z/(1 - A_v)$ at input and $Z_2 = Z A_v/(A_v - 1)$ at output. - ✅ For a **capacitor** $C$, the **input Miller capacitance** is $C_{M1} = C(1 - A_v)$. For $A_v < 0$, this is $C(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$. - ✅ For a CE BJT, the dominant floating cap is $C_\mu$ (base-collector), and $C_{M1} = C_\mu(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$. - ✅ For a CS MOSFET, the dominant floating cap is $C_{gd}$ (gate-drain), and $C_{M1} = C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$. - ✅ The **total input capacitance** is $C_{in,total} = C_\pi \text{ (or } C_{gs}) + C_{M1}$. - ✅ The **upper 3-dB cutoff** is $f_H = 1/(2\pi R_{th} C_{in,total})$ with $R_{th} = R_S \parallel r_\pi$ (BJT) or $R_{th} = R_S$ (MOSFET, ignoring $R_G$). - ✅ The **Gain-Bandwidth Product** is approximately constant: $GBW \approx g_m/(2\pi C_\mu)$ — the cornerstone of the gain-bandwidth trade-off. - ✅ **Mitigation techniques:** Cascode, Miller neutralization, emitter/source degeneration, current-feedback op-amps, use of high-$f_T$ transistors. - ✅ **Sign convention:** $A_v$ carries its actual negative sign; only after splitting do we use $\vert A_v \vert$ for magnitudes. - ✅ **Output-side Miller capacitance** $C_{M2} = C(A_v - 1)/A_v \approx C$ for $A \gg 1$ — usually absorbed into load capacitance. - ✅ **Dominant pole** is almost always at the **input** of a CE/CS stage because Miller multiplication is largest there. - ✅ **Cascode trick:** Reduces Miller gain to $\approx 1$, slashing input capacitance by a factor of $(1 + A_v)$ — a **must-remember** KTU concept. - ✅ **Exam formula to memorize verbatim:** $C_{M,in} = C(1 - A_v)$ — both for BJT and MOSFET, with proper sign of $A_v$. ---

topics.title: High Frequency Response of Single Stage CE Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** High Frequency Response of Single Stage CE Amplifier ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **High Frequency Response of a Single Stage Common Emitter (CE) Amplifier** describes the behaviour of the amplifier's voltage gain when the input signal frequency increases beyond the midband region, where the internal parasitic junction capacitances of the Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) begin to dominate. At high frequencies, the BJT no longer behaves as a purely resistive device; its emitter-base diffusion capacitance ($C_\pi$) and collector-base depletion capacitance ($C_\mu$) introduce phase shifts and attenuate the gain. The frequency at which the gain drops to **0.707 times** its midband value (i.e., by **3 dB**) is defined as the **upper cutoff frequency** ($f_H$), and the corresponding bandwidth is critical for determining the amplifier's utility in RF, video, and high-speed digital applications. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The high frequency model is built upon the **hybrid-$\pi$ model** augmented with the two parasitic capacitors $C_\pi$ (between base and emitter) and $C_\mu$ (between base and collector). The analysis uses **Miller's Theorem** to simplify the feedback capacitor $C_\mu$ into equivalent input and output shunt capacitors. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Hose and Squeeze" Intuition Imagine a garden hose delivering water (the signal) to a plant (the load). At low flow rates (low frequencies), water flows smoothly and the system is responsive. Now, as you increase the flow rate dramatically (high frequencies), the hose wall's **elasticity** (the junction capacitances) becomes critical — water molecules start "pushing back" because the rubber cannot expand fast enough. Capacitors in a transistor behave identically: they store charge and resist rapid voltage changes, effectively **squeezing** or attenuating the high-frequency content of the signal before it can be amplified. The hose becomes "less efficient" the faster you pump, just as an amplifier's gain rolls off at high frequencies. - **Low frequency:** Capacitors $\to$ open circuit; amplifier gain is constant at $A_{mid}$. - **High frequency:** Capacitors $\to$ short circuit; gain drops because part of the input signal is shunted through $C_\pi$ and $C_\mu$ instead of being amplified. - The **transition frequency** $f_T$ is the absolute speed limit of the transistor — the frequency at which the short-circuit current gain becomes unity (1). > [!NOTE] > **Why does this matter in real engineering?** In radio receivers, oscilloscopes, and high-speed data acquisition systems, the upper cutoff frequency $f_H$ defines the highest usable signal frequency. A poor high-frequency response translates to loss of detail, edge sharpness, or data corruption. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Small-signal BJT) | |---|---|---| | Emitter-Base Diffusion Capacitance | $C_\pi$ | $10 \text{ pF}$ to $100 \text{ pF}$ | | Collector-Base Depletion Capacitance | $C_\mu$ | $1 \text{ pF}$ to $10 \text{ pF}$ | | Midband Voltage Gain | $A_v$ | $-100$ to $-500$ | | Transition Frequency | $f_T$ | $100 \text{ MHz}$ to $10 \text{ GHz}$ | | Thermal Voltage | $V_T$ | **25 mV** at $T = 300\text{ K}$ | | Transconductance | $g_m$ | $I_C / V_T$ (typically $10$–$100 \text{ mS}$) | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Hybrid-$\pi$ High-Frequency Model — Operational Logic The high-frequency BJT model extends the low-frequency hybrid-$\pi$ by adding two crucial capacitors: 1. **$C_\pi$ (Emitter-Base Capacitance):** Represents the diffusion capacitance of the forward-biased base-emitter junction. 2. **$C_\mu$ (Collector-Base Capacitance):** Represents the depletion (junction) capacitance of the reverse-biased base-collector junction. The dependent current source becomes a **frequency-dependent** quantity (but $g_m$ itself is treated as constant in the simplified model): $$i_c = g_m v_{\pi}$$ ### 2.2 Why and How Each Capacitor Affects the Response - **$C_\pi$ at the Input:** Forms a low-pass filter with the source resistance $R_s$ and the input resistance $r_\pi$ of the BJT. As frequency rises, the impedance of $C_\pi$ drops, shunting input signal away from $r_\pi$, reducing $v_\pi$ and hence the output. - **$C_\mu$ across Base-Collector (Miller Path):** This is the most damaging capacitor because it appears between input and output nodes. By **Miller's Theorem**, it is split into two parts: - An equivalent input capacitance: $C_{M1} = C_\mu(1 - A_v)$ - An equivalent output capacitance: $C_{M2} = C_\mu \left(1 - \frac{1}{A_v}\right)$ Because $|A_v| \gg 1$, $C_{M1} \approx C_\mu \cdot |A_v|$, which is a **huge capacitance** that dramatically lowers the input pole frequency. ### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of Analysis 1. Draw the AC equivalent circuit at high frequencies (replace coupling and bypass capacitors with short circuits). 2. Replace the BJT with its **hybrid-$\pi$ high-frequency model** (resistors $r_\pi$, $r_o$, $g_m v_\pi$ source, and capacitors $C_\pi$ and $C_\mu$). 3. Apply **Miller's Theorem** to $C_\mu$ to obtain $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$. 4. Identify two RC low-pass sections — one at the input node, one at the output node. 5. Compute the dominant time constant (the pole-determining RC product). 6. Compute the upper 3-dB frequency $f_H$. 7. Relate $f_H$ to the gain-bandwidth product and the transition frequency $f_T$. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are exam-grade essentials for KTU 2024. Master every one of them. | # | Formula | Description / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$ | Transconductance; $V_T = 25 \text{ mV}$ | | 2 | $r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta}{g_m} = \dfrac{\beta V_T}{I_C}$ | Input resistance of BJT base-emitter junction | | 3 | $C_{in} = C_\pi + C_\mu(1 - A_v)$ | Total input capacitance after Miller's Theorem | | 4 | $C_{out} = C_\mu\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{A_v}\right)$ | Output capacitance due to Miller effect | | 5 | $f_{p1} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{th,in} C_{in}}$ | Input pole (open-circuit time constant method) | | 6 | $f_{p2} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{th,out} C_{out}}$ | Output pole | | 7 | $f_H \approx f_{p1}$ (dominant pole) | Upper 3-dB frequency | | 8 | $f_\beta = \dfrac{1}{2\pi r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)}$ | $\beta$ cutoff frequency | | 9 | $f_T = \beta f_\beta = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)}$ | Transition frequency (gain-bandwidth product of BJT) | | 10 | $\vert A_{v,mid}\vert \cdot f_H \approx f_T$ | Gain-Bandwidth Product (for CE amplifier, dominant pole) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Units Reminder:** All frequencies in **Hz (or rad/s)**, capacitances in **Farads**, resistances in **Ohms**, $g_m$ in **Siemens (S)** or **A/V**. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **RF and Microwave Design:** $f_T$ dictates whether a BJT can be used in a 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi amplifier. - **Video Amplifiers:** Need $f_H > 5$ MHz for standard-definition video; the Miller effect is the key constraint. - **Operational Amplifier Internal Stages:** Most op-amp input stages are CE amplifiers, and their GBW product derives directly from the analysis above. - **Data Converters (ADC/DAC front-ends):** Need wide bandwidth to preserve sharp pulse edges — drives the use of low-$C_\mu$ RF transistors. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Input Pole Frequency Using Miller's Theorem **Given:** A single-stage CE amplifier with source resistance $R_S$, bias resistors $R_1 \parallel R_2$, collector resistor $R_C$, and load $R_L$. The midband voltage gain is: $$A_v = -g_m R_C' = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$$ **Step 1 — Miller's Theorem on $C_\mu$:** For a feedback capacitor $C_\mu$ connecting input node ($v_{\pi}$) to output node ($v_o$), and gain $A_v = v_o / v_{\pi}$: $$\boxed{C_{M1} = C_\mu (1 - A_v)}$$ $$\boxed{C_{M2} = C_\mu \left(1 - \frac{1}{A_v}\right)}$$ *Derivation logic:* Miller's Theorem states that the current through the feedback impedance equals the same current if you split it into two parts. Setting $v_o = A_v v_{\pi}$, the current through $C_\mu$ is $j\omega C_\mu (v_{\pi} - v_o)$. Splitting this into a current into the input ($j\omega C_{M1} v_{\pi}$) and one into the output ($j\omega C_{M2} v_o$) and equating gives the two formulas above. **Step 2 — Total Capacitance at the Input Node:** $$C_{in} = C_\pi + C_{M1} = C_\pi + C_\mu(1 - A_v)$$ Since $A_v$ is negative (inverting amplifier), $|1 - A_v| \approx |A_v|$ for large $|A_v|$. $$C_{in} \approx C_\pi + C_\mu \cdot \vert A_v\vert$$ **Step 3 — Thévenin Resistance at the Input Node:** Looking back from the base node with all independent sources zeroed: $$R_{th,in} = R_S \parallel (R_1 \parallel R_2) \parallel r_\pi$$ **Step 4 — Input Pole Frequency:** $$\boxed{f_{p1} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th,in} \cdot C_{in}}}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation of the Transition Frequency $f_T$ The **short-circuit current gain** $h_{fe}(s)$ of a BJT, with output shorted ($R_C = 0$), is: $$h_{fe}(s) = \frac{i_c}{i_b}$$ With the output shorted, the collector is at AC ground, so $v_o = 0$ and Miller multiplication of $C_\mu$ vanishes. The base sees only $C_\pi + C_\mu$ in parallel with $r_\pi$. **Step 1:** Base input impedance at high frequency: $$Z_b = r_\pi \parallel \frac{1}{s(C_\pi + C_\mu)}$$ **Step 2:** Base current $i_b = v_\pi / Z_b$ and collector current $i_c = g_m v_\pi$. So: $$h_{fe}(s) = \frac{i_c}{i_b} = g_m \cdot Z_b = g_m \cdot \frac{r_\pi \cdot \frac{1}{s(C_\pi + C_\mu)}}{r_\pi + \frac{1}{s(C_\pi + C_\mu)}}$$ **Step 3:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $s(C_\pi + C_\mu)$: $$ \begin{aligned} h_{fe}(s) &= \frac{g_m \cdot r_\pi}{1 + s r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)} \\ &= \frac{\beta_0}{1 + s r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)} \end{aligned} $$ where $\beta_0 = g_m r_\pi$ is the low-frequency current gain. **Step 4:** The cutoff frequency of $\beta$ is when the magnitude drops by $1/\sqrt{2}$: $$\omega_\beta = \frac{1}{r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad f_\beta = \frac{1}{2\pi r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)}$$ **Step 5:** The **transition frequency** $f_T$ is the frequency at which $|h_{fe}| = 1$: $$ \begin{aligned} h_{fe}(j\omega) &\approx \frac{\beta_0}{j\omega r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)} = \frac{g_m}{j\omega (C_\pi + C_\mu)} \quad (\text{for } \omega \gg \omega_\beta) \\ \vert h_{fe}\vert &= 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_T = \frac{g_m}{C_\pi + C_\mu} \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{f_T = \frac{g_m}{2\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)} = \beta_0 f_\beta}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation of Gain-Bandwidth Product for CE Amplifier From the dominant-pole approximation: $$\vert A_{v,mid}\vert \cdot f_H = (g_m R_C') \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th,in} C_{in}}$$ For the typical case where $R_{th,in} \approx r_\pi$ (signal source is ideal so $R_S \to 0$, bias resistors are large): $$R_{th,in} \cdot C_{in} \approx r_\pi [C_\pi + C_\mu (1 + g_m R_C')]$$ Since $g_m R_C' \gg 1$: $$R_{th,in} \cdot C_{in} \approx r_\pi \cdot C_\mu \cdot g_m R_C' = C_\mu R_C' \cdot \frac{\beta_0}{g_m} \cdot g_m = C_\mu \beta_0 R_C'$$ So: $$f_H \approx \frac{1}{2\pi C_\mu \beta_0 R_C'}$$ Multiplying by $\vert A_{v,mid}\vert = g_m R_C'$: $$ \begin{aligned} \vert A_{v,mid}\vert \cdot f_H &\approx g_m R_C' \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi C_\mu \beta_0 R_C'} \\ &= \frac{g_m}{2\pi C_\mu \beta_0} = \frac{g_m}{2\pi C_\mu \cdot g_m r_\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi C_\mu r_\pi} \end{aligned} $$ When $C_\mu$ dominates over $C_\pi$ (very common in CE stages), this approaches $f_T$. Hence the elegant result: $$\boxed{\vert A_{v,mid}\vert \cdot f_H \approx f_T}$$ ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A CE amplifier has $g_m = 40 \text{ mS}$, $r_\pi = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $\beta = 40$, $C_\pi = 20 \text{ pF}$, $C_\mu = 2 \text{ pF}$, $R_C = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_S = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_1 \parallel R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$. **Step 1:** Midband gain $$A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L) = -40 \text{ mS} \times 2.5 \text{ k}\Omega = -100$$ **Step 2:** Miller input capacitance $$C_{in} = C_\pi + C_\mu(1 - A_v) = 20 + 2(1 - (-100)) = 20 + 202 = 222 \text{ pF}$$ **Step 3:** Thévenin resistance at base $$R_{th,in} = R_S \parallel (R_1 \parallel R_2) \parallel r_\pi = 1\text{k} \parallel 10\text{k} \parallel 1\text{k}$$ $$\frac{1}{R_{th,in}} = \frac{1}{1} + \frac{1}{10} + \frac{1}{1} = 2.1 \text{ mS} \;\Rightarrow\; R_{th,in} \approx 476 \Omega$$ **Step 4:** Upper cutoff frequency $$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 476 \times 222 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1.057 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 1.51 \text{ MHz}$$ **Step 5:** Transition frequency check $$f_T = \frac{g_m}{2\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)} = \frac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{2\pi \times 22 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 289 \text{ MHz}$$ **Step 6:** Verify Gain-Bandwidth Product $$\vert A_v \vert \cdot f_H = 100 \times 1.51 \text{ MHz} = 151 \text{ MHz}$$ This is **less than** $f_T = 289 \text{ MHz}$ as expected (the dominant pole approximation gives a value slightly below $f_T$). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Complete High-Frequency CE Amplifier — Functional Block Architecture This Mermaid diagram captures the signal flow, Miller transformation, and node-by-node analysis of the high-frequency model. ```mermaid flowchart LR src[("Signal Source
V_s, R_s")]:::source bias[("Bias Network
R1 parallel R2")]:::bias bjt[("BJT Hybrid-Pi Model
r_pi, g_m v_pi, r_o")]:::device rc[("Collector Load
R_C parallel R_L")]:::rc cpi[("C_pi
B-E Diffusion Cap")]:::cap cmu[("C_mu
B-C Feedback Cap")]:::cap miller[("Miller Theorem Split
C_M1 = C_mu1-A_v
C_M2 = C_mu1-1/A_v")]:::miller out[("Output Node V_out")]:::output gnd[("Ground")]:::gnd src -->|v_in| bjt bias --> bjt bjt -->|g_m v_pi| rc rc --> out cpi -.->|shunt at input| bjt cmu -.->|feedback bridge| miller miller -.->|C_M1 to input| bjt miller -.->|C_M2 to output| out out --> gnd classDef source fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef bias fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef device fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef rc fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cap fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef miller fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef gnd fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Frequency Response — Gain vs Frequency Characteristic This Mermaid `xychart-beta` plot illustrates the Bode magnitude response of a single-stage CE amplifier. ```mermaid xychart-beta title "CE Amplifier — High Frequency Gain Roll-Off" x-axis "Frequency (Hz) [log scale]" [1e3, 1e4, 1e5, 1e6, 1e7, 1e8, 1e9] y-axis "|A_v| (dB)" 0 --> 60 line [40, 40, 40, 40, 37, 20, 0] ``` - Below $f_L$ (not shown): gain rises at +20 dB/decade (coupling/bypass capacitor effect). - **Midband plateau:** flat at $\approx 40$ dB. - **At $f_H$:** gain is at $-3$ dB $= 37$ dB. - **Above $f_H$:** gain rolls off at $-20$ dB/decade. ### 4.3 Miller Capacitance Split — Detailed Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Original_Bridge ["Original Circuit"] n1(("Input Node")):::node -->|C_mu| n2(("Output Node")):::node end subgraph Miller_Equivalent ["Miller Equivalent"] n3(("Input Node")):::node -->|C_M1 = C_mu 1-A_v| gnd1(("GND")):::node n4(("Output Node")):::node -->|C_M2 = C_mu 1-1/A_v| gnd2(("GND")):::node end Original_Bridge -.transforms into.-> Miller_Equivalent classDef node fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 Pole-Zero Map — Input and Output Poles ```mermaid flowchart LR A[("Input Pole
f_p1 = 1 / 2pi R_th,in C_in")]:::pole B[("Output Pole
f_p2 = 1 / 2pi R_th,out C_out")]:::pole C[("Dominant Pole Selection
f_H = min f_p1, f_p2")]:::dominant A --> C B --> C classDef pole fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef dominant fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** In almost every CE amplifier at moderate gains ($|A_v| < 200$), the **input pole $f_{p1}$ dominates** because the Miller multiplication makes $C_{in}$ enormous. The output pole $f_{p2}$ usually lies at a much higher frequency. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Define the transition frequency $f_T$ of a BJT. Derive its expression in terms of $g_m$, $C_\pi$, and $C_\mu$.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **transition frequency $f_T$** is the frequency at which the short-circuit common-emitter current gain $h_{fe}$ of the BJT drops to **unity** (i.e., 0 dB). It is derived from the high-frequency hybrid-$\pi$ model with output shorted. The base sees $C_\pi$ and $C_\mu$ in parallel (no Miller multiplication since output is grounded).
