The current gain magnitude for $\omega \gg \omega_\beta$ is: $$\vert h_{fe}\vert = \frac{g_m}{\omega (C_\pi + C_\mu)}$$ Setting $\vert h_{fe}\vert = 1$ gives: $$\boxed{f_T = \frac{g_m}{2\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)}}$$ Equivalently, $f_T = \beta \cdot f_\beta$, where $f_\beta$ is the $\beta$-cutoff frequency. --- #### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **State Miller's theorem. Show how it is applied to find the input capacitance of a CE amplifier.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Miller's Theorem:** If an impedance $Z$ connects a node of voltage $V_1$ to another node of voltage $V_2 = K V_1$, then $Z$ can be replaced by: - An impedance $Z_1 = Z / (1 - K)$ from node 1 to ground. - An impedance $Z_2 = Z \cdot K / (K - 1)$ from node 2 to ground. For the CE amplifier, $C_\mu$ connects base (node 1, voltage $v_\pi$) to collector (node 2, voltage $A_v \cdot v_\pi$). So $K = A_v$ (negative). Replacing $C_\mu$ by $Z = 1/(sC_\mu)$: $$C_{M1} = C_\mu (1 - A_v) \approx C_\mu \cdot \vert A_v\vert \quad (\text{since } A_v \text{ is negative and } \vert A_v\vert \gg 1)$$ The total input capacitance at the base node is: $$\boxed{C_{in} = C_\pi + C_\mu(1 - A_v)}$$ This shows the **Miller multiplication effect**, which is the dominant cause of bandwidth limitation in CE amplifiers. --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]` **(a)** Draw the high-frequency **hybrid-$\pi$ equivalent circuit** of a single-stage CE amplifier. Mark clearly the components $r_\pi$, $g_m v_\pi$, $r_o$, $C_\pi$, and $C_\mu$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using **Miller's theorem**, derive the upper 3-dB frequency $f_H$ of the CE amplifier in terms of $g_m$, $r_\pi$, $C_\pi$, $C_\mu$, $R_S$, $R_C'$, and the bias resistors. Discuss why the input pole usually dominates. **(7 marks)** --- ##### Model Solution to Question A **Solution to (a) — High-Frequency Hybrid-$\pi$ Equivalent Circuit:** The complete high-frequency model has the following components (drawn conceptually): - **$r_\pi$** between base node $B'$ and emitter node $E'$ (representing the input resistance of the forward-biased base-emitter junction). - **Dependent current source $g_m v_\pi$** between collector node $C'$ and emitter node $E'$, with arrow pointing from $C'$ to $E'$ (sourcing current out of collector for $v_\pi > 0$). - **$r_o$** (output resistance) in parallel with the dependent source, between $C'$ and $E'$. - **$C_\pi$** in parallel with $r_\pi$ between $B'$ and $E'$ (diffusion capacitance). - **$C_\mu$** between $B'$ and $C'$ (collector-base depletion capacitance) — this is the **feedback capacitor** that causes the Miller effect. The **signal flow** is: $v_s \to R_S \to$ (parallel of bias resistors $R_1, R_2$) $\to B' \to r_\pi \parallel C_\pi \to E' \to$ ground. The collector node connects to $R_C \parallel R_L$ (modelled as $R_C'$) to give $v_{out} = -g_m v_\pi \cdot R_C'$. **Solution to (b) — Upper 3-dB Frequency Derivation:** **Step 1:** Midband voltage gain: $$A_v = -g_m R_C' \quad \text{where } R_C' = R_C \parallel R_L$$ **Step 2:** Apply Miller's theorem to $C_\mu$: $$C_{M1} = C_\mu(1 - A_v) \quad \text{at input}; \quad C_{M2} = C_\mu\left(1 - \frac{1}{A_v}\right) \quad \text{at output}$$ **Step 3:** Total input capacitance: $$C_{in} = C_\pi + C_\mu(1 - A_v) \approx C_\pi + C_\mu \cdot g_m R_C'$$ (using $A_v = -g_m R_C'$, so $1 - A_v = 1 + g_m R_C'$). **Step 4:** Thévenin equivalent resistance seen by $C_{in}$: $$R_{th,in} = R_S \parallel (R_1 \parallel R_2) \parallel r_\pi$$ **Step 5:** Input pole: $$f_{p1} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th,in} C_{in}}$$ **Step 6:** Output pole: $$f_{p2} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th,out} \cdot C_{out}}, \quad \text{where } R_{th,out} = R_C \parallel R_L \parallel r_o$$ and $C_{out} = C_{ce} + C_\mu \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{A_v}\right) \approx C_\mu$. **Step 7:** Since $C_{in} \gg C_{out}$ (because $C_{in}$ contains $C_\mu \cdot g_m R_C'$, a hugely multiplied value), the input pole has a much lower frequency: $$f_{p1} \ll f_{p2}$$ Therefore the **input pole dominates**, and the upper 3-dB frequency is: $$\boxed{f_H \approx f_{p1} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th,in} \left[C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + g_m R_C')\right]}}$$ **Discussion — Why Input Pole Dominates:** The Miller multiplication factor $(1 + g_m R_C')$ typically ranges from 50 to 500. So $C_{in} \approx C_\mu \cdot g_m R_C' \gg C_\mu$. The output capacitance $C_{out}$ remains approximately equal to $C_\mu$. Hence the input RC time constant is much larger, making $f_{p1}$ much lower than $f_{p2}$. The dominant-pole approximation $f_H \approx f_{p1}$ is widely used in CE amplifier design. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper]` **(a)** Define $f_\beta$ and $f_T$ for a BJT. Establish the relationship $f_T = \beta_0 f_\beta$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A CE amplifier uses a BJT with $g_m = 50$ mS, $C_\pi = 50$ pF, $C_\mu = 5$ pF, $R_C = 2$ k$\Omega$, $R_L = 2$ k$\Omega$, and source resistance $R_S = 200$ $\Omega$. The bias network resistance $R_B = R_1 \parallel R_2 = 10$ k$\Omega$. Calculate: - (i) Midband voltage gain $A_v$ - (ii) The Miller input capacitance $C_{in}$ - (iii) The upper cutoff frequency $f_H$ - (iv) The transition frequency $f_T$ and the gain-bandwidth product. **(7 marks)** --- ##### Model Solution to Question B **Solution to (a) — Definitions and Relationship:** The **$\beta$-cutoff frequency $f_\beta$** is the frequency at which the magnitude of the common-emitter short-circuit current gain $h_{fe}$ drops to $0.707 \cdot \beta_0$ (i.e., 3 dB below its low-frequency value). The **transition frequency $f_T$** is the frequency at which $\vert h_{fe}\vert$ drops to **unity** (1). From the high-frequency hybrid-$\pi$ analysis with output shorted: $$h_{fe}(s) = \frac{\beta_0}{1 + s r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)}$$ Setting $s = j\omega$, the cutoff is at $\omega_\beta r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu) = 1$: $$f_\beta = \frac{1}{2\pi r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)}$$ For $f \gg f_\beta$: $$\vert h_{fe}\vert \approx \frac{\beta_0}{\omega r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)} = \frac{g_m}{\omega (C_\pi + C_\mu)}$$ Setting $\vert h_{fe}\vert = 1$: $$f_T = \frac{g_m}{2\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)} = \frac{\beta_0}{2\pi r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)} = \beta_0 \cdot f_\beta$$ $$\boxed{f_T = \beta_0 \cdot f_\beta}$$ **Solution to (b) — Numerical Calculation:** **(i) Midband gain:** $$R_C' = R_C \parallel R_L = 2\text{k} \parallel 2\text{k} = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$$ $$A_v = -g_m R_C' = -50 \text{ mS} \times 1 \text{ k}\Omega = -50$$ **(ii) Miller input capacitance:** $$C_{in} = C_\pi + C_\mu (1 - A_v) = 50 + 5(1 - (-50)) = 50 + 255 = 305 \text{ pF}$$ **(iii) Upper cutoff frequency:** Thévenin resistance: $$R_{th,in} = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_\pi$$ Need $r_\pi = \beta_0 / g_m$. Since $\beta_0 = f_T \cdot 2\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)/g_m$, we'll compute it from $f_T$ later. Alternatively, take $r_\pi = 1$ k$\Omega$ (typical for $g_m = 50$ mS with $\beta_0 = 50$): $$R_{th,in} = 200 \parallel 10\text{k} \parallel 1\text{k} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{200} + \frac{1}{10000} + \frac{1}{1000}} \approx \frac{1}{0.0061} \approx 164 \Omega$$ $$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 164 \times 305 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5.00 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 3.18 \text{ MHz}$$ **(iv) Transition frequency:** $$f_T = \frac{g_m}{2\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{2\pi \times 55 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{3.456 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 144.7 \text{ MHz}$$ Gain-Bandwidth Product: $$\vert A_v \vert \cdot f_H = 50 \times 3.18 \text{ MHz} \approx 159 \text{ MHz}$$ This is slightly larger than $f_T \approx 144.7$ MHz, which is expected because the dominant-pole approximation neglects the contribution of $C_\pi$ to $C_{in}$ and other second-order effects. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign Error in Miller's Theorem:** Students frequently write $C_{M1} = C_\mu(1 + A_v)$ instead of $C_\mu(1 - A_v)$. Since $A_v$ is negative, both give a positive answer, but the **form** matters for examiner credit. Use the correct formula: $C_{M1} = C_\mu(1 - A_v)$. > 2. **Forgetting the bias network resistance:** Many students compute $R_{th,in}$ using only $R_S \parallel r_\pi$, ignoring the parallel combination of $R_1$ and $R_2$. This loses 1–2 marks. > 3. **Confusing $f_\beta$ with $f_T$:** $f_\beta$ is the $\beta$ cutoff; $f_T$ is the unity-gain frequency. The relationship is $f_T = \beta \cdot f_\beta$. > 4. **Units mix-up:** Capacitances in pF must be converted to Farads ($\times 10^{-12}$) before substitution, or the answer will be off by $10^{12}$. Always state units in the final answer. > 5. **Output pole ignored:** Examiners often award partial credit for noting that $f_{p2}$ is at higher frequency and that $f_H \approx f_{p1}$. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — High Frequency CE Amplifier** - **Hybrid-$\pi$ model** at high frequencies adds $C_\pi$ (B-E diffusion) and $C_\mu$ (B-C depletion) to the low-frequency model. - **Miller's Theorem** converts the feedback capacitor $C_\mu$ into two shunt capacitors: input side $C_{M1} = C_\mu(1 - A_v)$, output side $C_{M2} = C_\mu(1 - 1/A_v)$. - **Total input capacitance** $C_{in} = C_\pi + C_\mu(1 - A_v)$ — this is huge due to Miller multiplication. - **Upper cutoff frequency** $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{th,in} C_{in}}$ (input pole dominates). - **Transition frequency** $f_T = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)}$ — the intrinsic speed limit of the BJT. - **$\beta$ cutoff frequency** $f_\beta = \dfrac{1}{2\pi r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)}$. - **Relationship:** $f_T = \beta_0 f_\beta$. - **Gain-Bandwidth Product:** $\vert A_{v,mid}\vert \cdot f_H \approx f_T$ (dominant-pole CE amplifier). - **Transconductance:** $g_m = I_C / V_T$ where $V_T = 25$ mV at 300 K. - **Input resistance:** $r_\pi = \beta / g_m$. - The **Miller effect** is the single largest cause of bandwidth reduction in CE amplifiers. - To **extend bandwidth**: reduce $R_C'$, use a transistor with low $C_\mu$, use **cascode** configuration, or apply **neutralization** (feed-forward cancellation of $C_\mu$). - **Output pole** $f_{p2}$ is usually much higher and rarely dominates unless gain is very low. - Always convert all capacitances to **Farads** before computing frequencies. - The **3-dB bandwidth** is from $f_L$ to $f_H$; for a single-stage CE amplifier, $BW \approx f_H$ when $f_L \ll f_H$.

topics.title: Short Circuit Current Gain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Short Circuit Current Gain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Short Circuit Current Gain** is a fundamental **small-signal hybrid (h-) parameter** of the Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) that quantifies the forward current amplification capability of the device when the output port is **AC short-circuited** to the signal source.

In the **Common-Emitter (CE) configuration**, it is denoted as:

$$h_{fe} \equiv \left. \frac{i_2}{i_1} \right|_{v_2 = 0}$$

In the **Common-Base (CB) configuration**, it is denoted as:

$$h_{fb} \equiv \left. \frac{i_2}{i_1} \right|_{v_2 = 0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The parameter $h_{fe}$ is numerically equal to the **AC beta ($\beta_{ac}$)** of the BJT, and $h_{fb}$ is numerically equal to **$-\alpha$**, the negative of the **AC alpha ($\alpha_{ac}$)** of the BJT in CB configuration.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **water pipeline system** where:
- A small control valve at the input allows you to regulate a thin trickle of water (this is the **input current $i_1$ = base current $I_b$**).
- At the output, a large main pipe releases a much bigger stream of water (this is the **output current $i_2$ = collector current $I_c$**).
- A **short circuit** at the output is like attaching a wide, low-resistance hose to the output pipe so that water flows freely without any back-pressure buildup.

**How much bigger is the output stream compared to your input trickle?** That ratio is exactly the **Short Circuit Current Gain**. It tells you, *"For every 1 mA I push into the base, how many mA will the collector deliver into a shorted load?"* — and the answer is typically **$\beta$**, which can range from 50 to 300 in a typical small-signal BJT like the BC547.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** $h_{fe}$ is essentially the "efficiency of transistor action" — it measures how effectively a tiny base current controls a much larger collector current under small-signal, AC conditions with the output port shorted.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **$h_{fe}$** — CE short circuit forward current gain (dimensionless, typically 50–500)
- **$h_{fb}$** — CB short circuit forward current gain (dimensionless, typically $-0.95$ to $-0.999$)
- **$\beta_{ac}$** — Common-emitter AC current gain (equals $h_{fe}$)
- **$\alpha_{ac}$** — Common-base AC current gain (equals $-h_{fb}$)
- **$I_b$** — Base current (AC small-signal: $i_b$)
- **$I_c$** — Collector current (AC small-signal: $i_c$)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The h-Parameter Model Framework

The **hybrid-pi / h-parameter** model is the standard small-signal representation used by KTU for BJT analysis. It treats the BJT as a **two-port network** and characterizes it using four measurable parameters. The two-port equations are:

$$v_1 = h_i \cdot i_1 + h_r \cdot v_2$$

$$i_2 = h_f \cdot i_1 + h_o \cdot v_2$$

Where the subscripts (dropped here for brevity) are $i$ = input, $r$ = reverse, $f$ = forward, $o$ = output. The second equation directly contains our topic: **$h_f$ is the short circuit current gain** because it is measured with $v_2 = 0$.

### 2.2 The Four h-Parameters (Bulleted Logic)

1. **$h_i$ (Input Impedance)** — Ratio $v_1/i_1$ with output shorted ($v_2=0$). Units: **ohms ($\Omega$)**.
   - **Why?** It tells us how much the input "resists" the AC signal entering the base.
   
2. **$h_r$ (Reverse Voltage Ratio)** — Ratio $v_1/v_2$ with input open ($i_1=0$). Dimensionless.
   - **Why?** It represents the unwanted feedback from output back to input (typically very small, $\approx 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$).
   
3. **$h_f$ (Forward Short Circuit Current Gain)** — Ratio $i_2/i_1$ with output shorted ($v_2=0$). Dimensionless.
   - **Why?** This is the **transistor's main amplification function** — directly the topic of this module section.

4. **$h_o$ (Output Admittance)** — Ratio $i_2/v_2$ with input open ($i_1=0$). Units: **siemens (S) or mhos**.
   - **Why?** It models the finite output resistance $r_o$ of the transistor.

### 2.3 The Three BJT Configurations and Their Current Gains

Each BJT configuration (CE, CB, CC) has its own set of h-parameters. The current gains differ significantly:

- **CE (Common Emitter):** $h_{fe}$ is **large** (50 to 500), provides both voltage and current gain. Most commonly used.
- **CB (Common Base):** $h_{fb}$ is **slightly less than unity in magnitude** (e.g., $-0.98$), provides voltage gain but no current gain. Used in high-frequency applications.
- **CC (Common Collector / Emitter Follower):** $h_{fc}$ is **large** (similar to $h_{fe}$), provides current gain but no voltage gain. Used as a buffer.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| CE Short Circuit Current Gain (definition) | $h_{fe} = \left. \dfrac{i_c}{i_b} \right|_{v_{ce}=0}$ | Dimensionless, $\beta_{ac}$ |
| CB Short Circuit Current Gain (definition) | $h_{fb} = \left. \dfrac{i_c}{i_e} \right|_{v_{cb}=0}$ | Dimensionless, $\approx -\alpha$ |
| Fundamental relation | $h_{fe} = \dfrac{-h_{fb}}{1 + h_{fb}}$ | Links CE and CB gains |
| Equivalent relation | $\beta = \dfrac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}$ | $\beta = h_{fe}$, $\alpha = -h_{fb}$ |
| Inverse relation | $\alpha = \dfrac{\beta}{1 + \beta}$ | $h_{fb} = \dfrac{-h_{fe}}{1 + h_{fe}}$ |
| Identity | $I_e = I_b + I_c$ | KCL at the BJT |
| Current relationships | $I_c = \alpha I_e$, $I_c = \beta I_b$ | DC forms |
| h-parameter port equation (forward) | $i_2 = h_f \, i_1 + h_o \, v_2$ | Two-port hybrid model |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention (KTU Exam Favorite Trap):** The CB short circuit current gain $h_{fb}$ is **negative** because the conventional current flowing *out* of the collector in CB configuration is opposite in sign to the emitter current flowing *in*. The CE gain $h_{fe}$ is **positive**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The short circuit current gain is the **cornerstone of amplifier design**. It determines:
- **Voltage Gain of a CE amplifier:** $A_v \approx -h_{fe} \cdot (R_C / h_{ie})$
- **Current Gain:** $A_i \approx h_{fe}$
- **Power Gain:** $A_P = A_v \cdot A_i \approx h_{fe}^2 \cdot (R_C / h_{ie})$

It is used universally in designing **audio amplifiers, RF front-ends, operational transconductance amplifiers (OTAs), and switching circuits** in digital logic (saturation / cut-off analysis).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between $h_{fe}$ and $h_{fb}$

**Goal:** Prove that $h_{fe} = \dfrac{-h_{fb}}{1 + h_{fb}}$.

**Starting point — KCL at the BJT terminal (always true, both DC and AC):**

$$i_e = i_b + i_c$$

We now write this in the **AC small-signal** domain. The AC emitter current is the sum of AC base current and AC collector current:

$$i_e = i_b + i_c$$

**Step 1 — Express $i_c$ in terms of $i_b$ using $h_{fe}$:**

By definition, with output shorted, the CE short circuit current gain gives:

$$h_{fe} = \frac{i_c}{i_b} \quad \Rightarrow \quad i_c = h_{fe} \cdot i_b$$

**Step 2 — Express $i_c$ in terms of $i_e$ using $h_{fb}$:**

By definition, with output shorted, the CB short circuit current gain gives:

$$h_{fb} = \frac{i_c}{i_e} \quad \Rightarrow \quad i_c = h_{fb} \cdot i_e$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into KCL:**

Since $i_e = i_b + i_c$, we can write $i_b = i_e - i_c$. Substitute this into the CE definition:

$$h_{fe} = \frac{i_c}{i_e - i_c}$$

**Step 4 — Divide numerator and denominator by $i_e$:**

$$h_{fe} = \frac{\frac{i_c}{i_e}}{\frac{i_e - i_c}{i_e}} = \frac{\frac{i_c}{i_e}}{1 - \frac{i_c}{i_e}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $h_{fb} = i_c / i_e$:**

$$h_{fe} = \frac{h_{fb}}{1 - h_{fb}}$$

**Step 6 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $-1$ (KTU standard form):**

$$h_{fe} = \frac{h_{fb}}{1 - h_{fb}} \cdot \frac{-1}{-1} = \frac{-h_{fb}}{-(1 - h_{fb})} = \frac{-h_{fb}}{h_{fb} - 1}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $-1$ again gives the **canonical form**:

$$h_{fe} = \frac{-h_{fb}}{1 + h_{fb}}$$

**Result (boxed formula):**

$$\boxed{\,h_{fe} = \frac{-h_{fb}}{1 + h_{fb}}\,}$$

**Step 7 — Verification with typical values:** If $h_{fb} = -0.98$, then $h_{fe} = -(-0.98)/(1 + (-0.98)) = 0.98 / 0.02 = 49$. ✓ Matches the expected $\beta$ of a typical BJT.

### 3.2 Derivation: $I_c = \beta I_b$ from $I_c = \alpha I_e$

**Goal:** Show that $I_c = \beta I_b$ where $\beta = \alpha / (1 - \alpha)$.

**Start with KCL:**

$$I_e = I_b + I_c$$

**Step 1 — Solve for $I_b$:**

$$I_b = I_e - I_c$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $I_c = \alpha I_e$:**

$$I_b = I_e - \alpha I_e = (1 - \alpha) I_e$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $I_e$ in terms of $I_b$:**

$$I_e = \frac{I_b}{1 - \alpha}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into $I_c = \alpha I_e$:**

$$I_c = \alpha \cdot \frac{I_b}{1 - \alpha} = \left(\frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}\right) I_b$$

**Step 5 — Define $\beta$:**

$$\boxed{\,\beta = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha} \quad \text{and} \quad I_c = \beta I_b\,}$$

This is one of the most important derivations in BJT small-signal analysis. It tells us that the **CE current gain $\beta$ is always larger than the CB current gain $\alpha$**, because $1 - \alpha$ is a small positive number (since $\alpha \approx 1$).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Numerical Examples

| **Given** | **Find** | **Computation** | **Result** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $h_{fe} = 100$ | $h_{fb}$ | $h_{fb} = -h_{fe}/(1 + h_{fe}) = -100/101$ | $h_{fb} \approx -0.9901$ |
| $h_{fb} = -0.99$ | $h_{fe}$ | $h_{fe} = -h_{fb}/(1 + h_{fb}) = 0.99/0.01$ | $h_{fe} = 99$ |
| $\alpha = 0.98$ | $\beta$ | $\beta = 0.98 / 0.02$ | $\beta = 49$ |
| $\beta = 200$ | $\alpha$ | $\alpha = 200/201$ | $\alpha \approx 0.995$ |
| $I_b = 20 \mu A$, $\beta = 150$ | $I_c$ | $I_c = 150 \times 20 \mu A$ | $I_c = 3 \, mA$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The BJT as a Current-Controlled Current Source

The diagram below shows how the BJT behaves as a **current-controlled current source (CCCS)** in the h-parameter model, with the short circuit current gain $h_{fe}$ acting as the controlling proportionality constant.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Input Port Base Emitter] --> nodeB[hie Input Impedance]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Internal Control Node]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Current Controlled Current Source hfe times ib]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Output Port Collector Emitter]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Feedback Path hre times v2]
    nodeF --> nodeB
    nodeE --> nodeG[Output Admittance hoe]
    nodeG --> nodeE
    nodeH[Base Current ib Small Signal] --> nodeB
    nodeI[Collector Current ic equals hfe times ib] --> nodeE
```

### 4.2 Two-Port h-Parameter Model — Equation Mapping

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[Port 1 Input v1 i1] --> node2[Hybrid Input Block hie and hre]
    node2 --> node3[Equation 1 v1 equals hie i1 plus hre v2]
    node4[Port 2 Output v2 i2] --> node5[Hybrid Output Block hfe and hoe]
    node5 --> node6[Equation 2 i2 equals hfe i1 plus hoe v2]
    node7[Short Circuit Condition v2 equals 0] --> node8[Simplified hfe equals i2 divided by i1]
    node7 --> node9[Simplified hie equals v1 divided by i1]
    node6 --> node7
    node3 --> node7
```

### 4.3 Configuration Comparison Flow — CE vs CB vs CC

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[BJT Three Terminals B C E] --> nodeB[CE Common Emitter]
    nodeA --> nodeC[CB Common Base]
    nodeA --> nodeD[CC Common Collector]
    nodeB --> nodeE[hfe Beta 50 to 500 Large]
    nodeC --> nodeF[hfb minus Alpha 0.95 to 0.999 Magnitude near Unity]
    nodeD --> nodeG[hfc near Beta Slightly Greater than 1]
    nodeE --> nodeH[High Power Gain Voltage and Current Amplifier]
    nodeF --> nodeI[High Frequency Voltage Amplifier]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Buffer Impedance Matcher]
```

### 4.4 Relationship Map Between $h_{fe}$, $h_{fb}$, $\alpha$, and $\beta$

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[hfb CB Current Gain] --> nodeB[alpha equals minus hfb]
    nodeB --> nodeC[beta equals alpha divided by 1 minus alpha]
    nodeC --> nodeD[hfe equals beta]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Two Port Equation i2 equals hfe i1 plus hoe v2]
    nodeA --> nodeF[Canonical Formula hfe equals minus hfb divided by 1 plus hfb]
    nodeF --> nodeD
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the short circuit current gain of a BJT in CE configuration and write its h-parameter notation.**

**Model Answer:**
The short circuit current gain of a BJT in Common-Emitter (CE) configuration is defined as the **ratio of output AC current to input AC current** with the output port AC short-circuited. It is denoted by the h-parameter **$h_{fe}$**.

$$h_{fe} = \left. \frac{i_c}{i_b} \right|_{v_{ce} = 0}$$

It is a dimensionless quantity, also equal to the AC beta ($\beta_{ac}$) of the transistor, with typical values ranging from 50 to 500 for small-signal BJTs.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] State the relationship between $h_{fe}$ and $h_{fb}$. Why is $h_{fb}$ negative?**

**Model Answer:**
The relationship between the CE and CB short circuit current gains is:

$$h_{fe} = \frac{-h_{fb}}{1 + h_{fb}}$$

**Reason for negative $h_{fb}$:** In the CB configuration, the **input current is the emitter current $i_e$** (flowing *into* the emitter) and the **output current is the collector current $i_c$** (flowing *out* of the collector with respect to the base). By the standard h-parameter sign convention, the output current $i_2$ is defined as flowing *into* port 2, which is opposite to the conventional collector current direction. Therefore, $h_{fb}$ is assigned a **negative sign**, typically around $-0.95$ to $-0.99$.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign error trap:** Forgetting that $h_{fb}$ is **negative** leads to a wrong magnitude of $\beta$. Always substitute $h_{fb} \approx -0.98$, not $+0.98$.
> 2. **Confusing $h_{fe}$ with DC $\beta$:** $h_{fe}$ is the **AC / small-signal** gain. In CE output equation $i_c = h_{fe} \cdot i_b + h_{oe} \cdot v_{ce}$, you must NOT use DC $\beta_{DC} = I_C/I_B$ when computing AC quantities.
> 3. **Unit oversight:** $h_{fe}$ and $h_{fb}$ are **dimensionless** — never write ohms or siemens for them.
> 4. **Formula misapplication:** Using $\beta = (1-\alpha)/\alpha$ instead of $\beta = \alpha/(1-\alpha)$ is the most common error in derivations. **Memorize the correct form.**

---

**Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] — 14 Marks**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the four h-parameters of a BJT in CE configuration. Write the two-port equations and draw the equivalent circuit model.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a BJT, the common-base short circuit current gain is $h_{fb} = -0.985$. Calculate the common-emitter short circuit current gain $h_{fe}$. If the base current is $i_b = 25 \, \mu A$ (AC), find the short circuit collector current.

---

**Model Solution for Q3 (A):**

**(a)** The four h-parameters in CE configuration are:

1. **$h_{ie}$ (Input impedance with output shorted):**
$$h_{ie} = \left. \frac{v_{be}}{i_b} \right|_{v_{ce}=0} \quad \text{(units: ohms)}$$

2. **$h_{re}$ (Reverse voltage ratio with input open):**
$$h_{re} = \left. \frac{v_{be}}{v_{ce}} \right|_{i_b=0} \quad \text{(dimensionless, very small)}$$

3. **$h_{fe}$ (Forward short circuit current gain with output shorted):**
$$h_{fe} = \left. \frac{i_c}{i_b} \right|_{v_{ce}=0} \quad \text{(dimensionless, large)}$$

4. **$h_{oe}$ (Output admittance with input open):**
$$h_{oe} = \left. \frac{i_c}{v_{ce}} \right|_{i_b=0} \quad \text{(units: siemens)}$$

**Two-port equations:**

$$v_{be} = h_{ie} \cdot i_b + h_{re} \cdot v_{ce}$$

$$i_c = h_{fe} \cdot i_b + h_{oe} \cdot v_{ce}$$

**Equivalent circuit model:** Input side has $h_{ie}$ in series with a voltage source $h_{re} \cdot v_{ce}$ (feedback); output side has a CCCS of value $h_{fe} \cdot i_b$ in parallel with admittance $h_{oe}$.

---

**(b) Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1 — Use the canonical formula:**

$$h_{fe} = \frac{-h_{fb}}{1 + h_{fb}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $h_{fb} = -0.985$:**

$$h_{fe} = \frac{-(-0.985)}{1 + (-0.985)} = \frac{0.985}{0.015}$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$h_{fe} = 65.67$$

**Step 4 — Compute the short circuit collector current:**

Using the definition $h_{fe} = i_c / i_b$:

$$i_c = h_{fe} \cdot i_b = 65.67 \times 25 \times 10^{-6} \, A$$

$$i_c = 1641.7 \times 10^{-6} \, A \approx 1.64 \, mA$$

**Final Answer:** $h_{fe} \approx 65.67$, $i_c \approx 1.64 \, mA$.

---

**Q3 (B). [KTU University Exam – June 2023] — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the relationship $h_{fe} = \dfrac{-h_{fb}}{1 + h_{fb}}$ starting from the BJT's fundamental current equation $i_e = i_b + i_c$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a silicon BJT with DC emitter current $I_E = 5 \, mA$ and $\alpha = 0.985$, find (i) $\beta$, (ii) $I_C$, (iii) $I_B$, and (iv) the small-signal parameter $h_{fe}$ (assuming $h_{fe} \approx \beta$).

---

**Model Solution for Q3 (B):**

**(a)** *(See Section 3.1 derivation above for the complete step-by-step proof. Key steps: KCL $\rightarrow$ substitute definitions $\rightarrow$ divide by $i_e$ $\rightarrow$ rearrange $\rightarrow$ canonical form.)*

---

**(b) Step-by-step solution:**

**(i) Find $\beta$:**

$$\beta = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha} = \frac{0.985}{1 - 0.985} = \frac{0.985}{0.015}$$

$$\beta = 65.67$$

**(ii) Find $I_C$:**

$$I_C = \alpha \cdot I_E = 0.985 \times 5 \, mA = 4.925 \, mA$$

**(iii) Find $I_B$:**

Using KCL: $I_B = I_E - I_C = 5 \, mA - 4.925 \, mA = 0.075 \, mA = 75 \, \mu A$

**Verification using $\beta$:** $I_C = \beta \cdot I_B = 65.67 \times 75 \, \mu A = 4.925 \, mA$ ✓

**(iv) Find $h_{fe}$:**

$$h_{fe} \approx \beta = 65.67$$

**Final Answer:** $\beta = 65.67$, $I_C = 4.925 \, mA$, $I_B = 75 \, \mu A$, $h_{fe} = 65.67$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** $h_{fe}$ is the ratio $i_c / i_b$ with $v_{ce} = 0$ (CE configuration). $h_{fb}$ is the ratio $i_c / i_e$ with $v_{cb} = 0$ (CB configuration).
- **Master Formula:** $\boxed{h_{fe} = \dfrac{-h_{fb}}{1 + h_{fb}}}$ and $\boxed{\beta = \dfrac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}}$.
- **Sign of $h_{fb}$:** Always **negative** (typically $-0.95$ to $-0.999$); $h_{fe}$ is always **positive** (50 to 500).
- **AC vs DC:** $h_{fe}$ is the **AC small-signal** current gain; it equals AC $\beta$ but may differ from DC $\beta_{DC} = I_C/I_B$ at high frequencies or low currents.
- **Two-port equation:** $i_c = h_{fe} \cdot i_b + h_{oe} \cdot v_{ce}$ — the first term is due to current gain, the second due to output admittance (Early effect).
- **KCL identity:** $I_E = I_B + I_C$ is the foundation of all BJT current gain derivations.
- **Engineering use:** Determines voltage gain $A_v$, current gain $A_i$, and input/output impedances of every BJT amplifier stage.
- **Trap to avoid:** Do not confuse the **port current direction sign convention** of h-parameters with the physical current direction in the BJT.
- **Quick check formula:** If $h_{fb} = -0.99$, then $h_{fe} = 99$. If $h_{fb} = -0.98$, then $h_{fe} = 49$. If $h_{fb} = -0.995$, then $h_{fe} = 199$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cut-off Frequency fβ and Unity Gain Bandwidth fT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cut-off Frequency fβ and Unity Gain Bandwidth fT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the **hybrid-pi (or h-parameter) high-frequency model** of a Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) in Common Emitter (CE) configuration, two specific frequencies completely characterize the small-signal AC gain roll-off behavior:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cut-off Frequency (fβ):** It is the frequency at which the magnitude of the **short-circuit common-emitter current gain** $\vert h_{fe} \vert$ drops to **0.707 times (i.e., $1/\sqrt{2}$ times)** its low-frequency value $h_{fe}$. At this frequency, the **power gain also drops to half** (i.e., −3 dB point).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unity Gain Bandwidth (fT):** It is the frequency at which the magnitude of the short-circuit common-emitter current gain $\vert h_{fe} \vert$ becomes **unity (i.e., 1)**. It is also called the **Current-Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)** because the product of current gain and bandwidth is approximately constant in the roll-off region.

The relationship is mathematically encapsulated as:

$$f_T \approx h_{fe} \cdot f_{\beta}$$

where $h_{fe}$ is the **low-frequency CE short-circuit current gain**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Tap and Drain Analogy"

Imagine a **water tap (transistor amplifier)** delivering water (signal current) into a **draining sink (load)**:

- At **low frequencies**, the drain is wide and the tap delivers a **steady, full-pressure flow** — analogous to the flat gain region of $h_{fe}$.
- As you slowly **increase the flow rate (frequency)**, the drain begins to clog — analogous to the internal junction capacitances ($C_{\pi}$ and $C_{\mu}$) shunting high-frequency signal currents.
- **fβ** is the flow rate at which the delivered water current becomes **70.7 %** of the original steady flow — it is the **first sign of significant loss**.
- **fT** is the flow rate at which the tap delivers **only a trickle** — the gain is so reduced that the amplifier can no longer amplify (gain = 1). Yet the tap is *still working*, just unable to do useful amplification.

> [!NOTE]
> A transistor with $f_T = 300\ \text{MHz}$ is **incredibly fast** and is used in **RF amplifiers, video circuits, and high-speed digital switching**. A general-purpose audio transistor typically has $f_T$ in the range of **30 – 100 MHz**.

## 1.3 Physical Significance in Engineering

| Frequency Parameter | Physical Meaning | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| $f_{\beta}$ | 3-dB roll-off point of CE current gain | 10 kHz – few MHz |
| $f_T$ | Frequency where current gain = 1 | 50 MHz – 10 GHz |
| $h_{fe}$ | DC/low-frequency current gain | 50 – 500 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Frequency Dependence of CE Current Gain

When a BJT is modeled using the **hybrid-pi small-signal equivalent circuit** at high frequencies, the intrinsic emitter-base capacitance $C_{\pi}$ and the feedback collector-base capacitance $C_{\mu}$ introduce frequency-dependent poles.

The **short-circuit current gain** of the CE configuration is given by:

$$h_{fe}(f) = \frac{h_{fe}}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{f}{f_{\beta}}\right)^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This equation tells us that the gain is **constant (= $h_{fe}$)** at low frequencies and begins to **roll off at −20 dB/decade** beyond $f_{\beta}$.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Roll-off

1. **Internal capacitances** $C_{\pi}$ (diffusion + junction capacitance) and $C_{\mu}$ (Miller capacitance) act as **short circuits at very high frequencies**, shunting signal current away from the output.
2. The **input node of the hybrid-pi model** therefore has a frequency-dependent impedance.
3. The resulting transfer function has a **single dominant pole** (in the simplified analysis) at $f_{\beta}$.
4. Beyond $f_{\beta}$, the gain decreases monotonically at a rate of **−20 dB per decade**.
5. The product of the gain at any frequency $f$ (in the roll-off region) and the frequency $f$ itself is **constant** — this is the **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **high-yield** for both **University Exams (ESE)** and **Internal Assessments (ISA)** in KTU 2024 scheme.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h_{fe}(f) = \dfrac{h_{fe}}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_{\beta})^2}}$ | Frequency-dependent CE short-circuit current gain | dimensionless |
| 2 | $f_{\beta} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (r_{\pi} \, C_{\pi} + r_{\pi} \, C_{\mu})}$ | Cut-off frequency in terms of hybrid-pi components | Hz |
| 3 | $f_T = h_{fe} \cdot f_{\beta}$ | Unity-gain bandwidth (gain-bandwidth product) | Hz |
| 4 | $f_T = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi (C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$ | $f_T$ in terms of transconductance and capacitances | Hz |
| 5 | $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$ | Transconductance of BJT | S (Siemens) |
| 6 | $r_{\pi} = \dfrac{h_{fe}}{g_m} = \dfrac{V_T \, h_{fe}}{I_C}$ | Input resistance of hybrid-pi model | $\Omega$ |
| 7 | $\vert h_{fe}(f) \vert \cdot f = f_T$ | General GBW relation (valid in roll-off region) | Hz |
| 8 | $f_{\alpha} = h_{fb} \cdot f$ | Relation to common-base cut-off (advanced) | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize:** $f_T = h_{fe} \cdot f_{\beta}$ — this is the **single most-tested formula** in this module for KTU examinations.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **RF and Microwave Design:** Designers use $f_T$ to select BJTs for **oscillators, mixers, and tuned amplifiers** operating in the MHz–GHz range.
- **High-Speed Digital Switching:** In **ECL and Schottky TTL logic families**, transistors must switch in nanoseconds, requiring $f_T \geq 5$ GHz.
- **Video Amplifiers:** Wideband video amplifiers (e.g., in CRT/LCD drivers) require $f_{\beta} \geq 4$–6 MHz to pass the full video bandwidth.
- **Audio Preamplifiers:** Low $f_{\beta}$ is acceptable since audio ends at ~20 kHz.
- **Op-Amp Internal Stages:** The op-amp's input differential pair uses transistors with $f_T$ in the GHz range to achieve unity-gain bandwidth in the MHz range.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Frequency-Dependent Current Gain $h_{fe}(f)$

### Step 1: Start with the hybrid-pi small-signal model.

The BJT in CE configuration is replaced by its hybrid-pi equivalent, which contains:
- $r_{\pi}$ — base-emitter dynamic resistance
- $C_{\pi}$ — base-emitter diffusion + junction capacitance
- $C_{\mu}$ — collector-base (Miller) capacitance
- $g_m \, v_{be}$ — current source
- $r_o$ — output resistance (neglected for simplicity)

### Step 2: Define the short-circuit current gain.

With the output **shorted** ($v_{ce} = 0$), the input current $i_b$ flows into the parallel combination of $r_{\pi}$, $C_{\pi}$, and $C_{\mu}$ (since $C_{\mu}$ is now grounded at both ends due to the short).

The voltage developed across the input is:

$$v_{be} = i_b \cdot Z_{in}$$

where the input impedance is:

$$Z_{in} = r_{\pi} \parallel \dfrac{1}{j\omega (C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})} = \dfrac{r_{\pi}}{1 + j\omega r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$$

### Step 3: Compute the output short-circuit current.

The collector current source is $i_c = g_m \, v_{be}$. Substituting $v_{be}$:

$$i_c = g_m \cdot \dfrac{r_{\pi} \, i_b}{1 + j\omega r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$$

### Step 4: Form the current gain ratio.

$$h_{fe}(j\omega) = \dfrac{i_c}{i_b} = \dfrac{g_m \, r_{\pi}}{1 + j\omega r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$$

### Step 5: Identify $h_{fe}$ and $f_{\beta}$.

Recognizing that $g_m r_{\pi} = h_{fe}$ (a fundamental identity of the hybrid-pi model) and defining:

$$f_{\beta} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$$

we obtain:

$$h_{fe}(j\omega) = \dfrac{h_{fe}}{1 + j(f/f_{\beta})}$$

### Step 6: Take the magnitude.

$$ \vert h_{fe}(f) \vert = \dfrac{h_{fe}}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_{\beta})^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **standard textbook form** required in KTU derivations. Always show the magnitude step explicitly.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Unity Gain Bandwidth $f_T$

### Step 1: Apply the definition of $f_T$.

By definition, at $f = f_T$, the magnitude of current gain is **unity**:

$$ \vert h_{fe}(f_T) \vert = 1$$

### Step 2: Substitute into the gain equation.

$$1 = \dfrac{h_{fe}}{\sqrt{1 + (f_T/f_{\beta})^2}}$$

### Step 3: Cross-multiply and square both sides.

$$1 + \left(\dfrac{f_T}{f_{\beta}}\right)^2 = h_{fe}^2$$

### Step 4: Solve for $f_T$.

$$\left(\dfrac{f_T}{f_{\beta}}\right)^2 = h_{fe}^2 - 1$$

$$f_T = f_{\beta} \cdot \sqrt{h_{fe}^2 - 1}$$

### Step 5: Apply the engineering approximation.

Since in practice $h_{fe} \gg 1$ (typically 50 – 500), we have $h_{fe}^2 - 1 \approx h_{fe}^2$. Therefore:

$$\sqrt{h_{fe}^2 - 1} \approx h_{fe}$$

### Step 6: Final expression.

$$\boxed{f_T \approx h_{fe} \cdot f_{\beta}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The full expression $f_T = f_{\beta} \sqrt{h_{fe}^2 - 1}$ is **exact**. The approximation $f_T \approx h_{fe} f_{\beta}$ is valid only when $h_{fe} \gg 1$. In KTU valuation, **always write the exact form first**, then justify the approximation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: $f_T$ in Terms of $g_m$, $C_{\pi}$, $C_{\mu}$

### Step 1: Start from $f_T = h_{fe} \cdot f_{\beta}$.

$$f_T = h_{fe} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2\pi r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$$

### Step 2: Substitute $h_{fe} = g_m r_{\pi}$.

$$f_T = (g_m r_{\pi}) \cdot \dfrac{1}{2\pi r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$$

### Step 3: Cancel $r_{\pi}$.

$$f_T = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi (C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical insight:** $f_T$ is **independent of $h_{fe}$**! It depends only on the **transconductance $g_m$** (set by the DC bias current) and the **internal capacitances $C_{\pi}$ and $C_{\mu}$** (set by the physical BJT structure).

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A BJT has $h_{fe} = 120$ at low frequencies and $f_{\beta} = 2.5$ MHz. Calculate the unity-gain bandwidth $f_T$. Also, find the short-circuit current gain at $f = 50$ MHz.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute $f_T$:**

$$f_T = h_{fe} \cdot f_{\beta} = 120 \times 2.5 \times 10^6 = 300 \times 10^6\ \text{Hz} = 300\ \text{MHz}$$

**Step 2 — Use the GBW relation in the roll-off region:**

Since $f = 50$ MHz $> f_{\beta} = 2.5$ MHz, the transistor is in the roll-off region. Using $\vert h_{fe}(f) \vert \cdot f = f_T$:

$$\vert h_{fe}(50\ \text{MHz}) \vert = \dfrac{f_T}{f} = \dfrac{300\ \text{MHz}}{50\ \text{MHz}} = 6$$

**Step 3 — Verification using exact formula:**

$$\vert h_{fe}(f) \vert = \dfrac{120}{\sqrt{1 + (50/2.5)^2}} = \dfrac{120}{\sqrt{1 + 400}} = \dfrac{120}{\sqrt{401}} \approx \dfrac{120}{20.025} \approx 5.99 \approx 6$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **GBW approximation** $f_T \approx h_{fe}(f) \cdot f$ is highly accurate when $f \gg f_{\beta}$, which is the case in the roll-off region.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Frequency Response of CE Current Gain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: BJT in CE Configuration]) --> biasA[Apply DC Bias: VBE, VCE]
    biasA --> smallSig[Small-Signal AC Input: ib]
    smallSig --> hpiModel[Hybrid-Pi Model Activated]
    hpiModel --> rpiNode["Input Branch: rπ in parallel with Cπ"]
    rpiNode --> cmuNode["Miller Capacitance: Cμ between Base and Collector"]
    cmuNode --> gmStage["Dependent Source: gm × vbe"]
    gmStage --> shortOut["Output Short-Circuited for Gain Computation"]
    shortOut --> deriveGain["Derive: hfe(f) = hfe / sqrt(1 + f/fβ squared)"]
    deriveGain --> lowFreq["Region 1: f less than fβ → Gain ≈ hfe (Flat, Maximum)"]
    deriveGain --> cutoffFreq["Region 2: f = fβ → Gain = 0.707 × hfe (−3 dB point)"]
    deriveGain --> rolloff["Region 3: f greater than fβ → Gain falls at −20 dB/decade"]
    cutoffFreq --> fbetaDef["fβ is the Cut-off Frequency, 3-dB point of CE current gain"]
    rolloff --> ftDef["At f = fT, Gain becomes Unity = 1"]
    ftDef --> gbw["Gain-Bandwidth Product: fT ≈ hfe × fβ"]
    gbw --> endA([End: High-Frequency Characterization Complete])
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Mapping of $f_{\beta}$ vs $f_T$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph lowRegion["LOW-FREQUENCY REGION (f less than fβ)"]
        dirA["Gain = hfe (Constant)"]
    end
    subgraph midRegion["TRANSITION REGION (f near fβ)"]
        dirB["Gain = 0.707 × hfe at fβ"]
    end
    subgraph highRegion["HIGH-FREQUENCY ROLL-OFF REGION (f greater than fβ)"]
        dirC["Gain = fT divided by f"]
    end
    subgraph unityRegion["UNITY GAIN POINT"]
        dirD["Gain = 1 at fT"]
    end
    lowRegion --> midRegion
    midRegion --> highRegion
    highRegion --> unityRegion
    unityRegion --> formulaOut["Key Identity: fT = hfe × fβ"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Bode Plot (Asymptotic Magnitude Response)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pA["DC to fβ: Flat Gain of 20 log hfe dB"] --> pB["At f = fβ: Gain drops by 3 dB to 20 log hfe minus 3 dB"]
    pB --> pC["Beyond fβ: Slope becomes −20 dB per decade"]
    pC --> pD["At f = fT: Gain crosses 0 dB line (unity)"]
    pD --> pE["Beyond fT: Gain continues to fall as 0 dB minus 20 log f by fT dB"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the terms cut-off frequency $f_{\beta}$ and unity-gain bandwidth $f_T$ for a BJT in CE configuration.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Cut-off frequency $f_{\beta}$:** It is the frequency at which the short-circuit common-emitter current gain $\vert h_{fe} \vert$ of a BJT drops to **0.707 times** (i.e., $1/\sqrt{2}$ times) its low-frequency value $h_{fe}$. It corresponds to the **−3 dB point** of the CE current gain.
>
> **Unity-gain bandwidth $f_T$:** It is the frequency at which the magnitude of the short-circuit common-emitter current gain $\vert h_{fe} \vert$ becomes **unity (i.e., 1)**. Mathematically, $f_T = h_{fe} \cdot f_{\beta}$ (assuming $h_{fe} \gg 1$). It is also known as the **gain-bandwidth product**.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the relationship between $f_T$ and $f_{\beta}$? Why is $f_T$ considered a more useful parameter than $f_{\beta}$?**

**Model Answer:**

The relationship is:

$$f_T = h_{fe} \cdot f_{\beta} = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi (C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$$

**Why $f_T$ is more useful:**

1. $f_T$ is **independent of $h_{fe}$** (DC current gain), so it is a **true figure of merit** of the transistor's high-frequency capability.
2. $f_T$ directly specifies the **maximum useful frequency** of the device.
3. $f_T$ is a **constant** for a given BJT at a given bias, while $f_{\beta}$ depends on the value of $h_{fe}$ (which varies with bias and temperature).
4. The product $\vert h_{fe}(f) \vert \cdot f$ in the roll-off region is constant and equals $f_T$ — making design calculations straightforward.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice-Based]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the short-circuit current gain of a BJT in CE configuration as a function of frequency, clearly identifying the cut-off frequency $f_{\beta}$. (7 marks)**

**(b) A BJT has $h_{fe} = 150$ and $f_{\beta} = 4$ MHz. Determine: (i) the unity-gain bandwidth $f_T$, (ii) the current gain at $f = 75$ MHz, and (iii) the frequency at which the current gain drops to 20. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** Draw the hybrid-pi small-signal model of the BJT in CE configuration with $r_{\pi}$, $C_{\pi}$, $C_{\mu}$, and the dependent source $g_m v_{be}$.

**Step 2:** Short the output terminals to obtain the **short-circuit current gain**, i.e., $v_{ce} = 0$.

**Step 3:** With the output shorted, $C_{\mu}$ appears directly across the input (between base and emitter, since the collector is at AC ground).

**Step 4:** The total input capacitance is $C_{\pi} + C_{\mu}$, and the input impedance is:

$$Z_{in} = \dfrac{r_{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega(C_{\pi}+C_{\mu})}}{r_{\pi} + \dfrac{1}{j\omega(C_{\pi}+C_{\mu})}} = \dfrac{r_{\pi}}{1 + j\omega r_{\pi}(C_{\pi}+C_{\mu})}$$

**Step 5:** The voltage $v_{be} = i_b \cdot Z_{in}$, and the short-circuit output current is $i_c = g_m v_{be}$.

**Step 6:** Forming the ratio:

$$h_{fe}(j\omega) = \dfrac{i_c}{i_b} = \dfrac{g_m r_{\pi}}{1 + j\omega r_{\pi}(C_{\pi}+C_{\mu})}$$

**Step 7:** Using $g_m r_{\pi} = h_{fe}$ and defining $f_{\beta} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi r_{\pi}(C_{\pi}+C_{\mu})}$:

$$h_{fe}(f) = \dfrac{h_{fe}}{1 + j(f/f_{\beta})}$$

$$\boxed{\vert h_{fe}(f) \vert = \dfrac{h_{fe}}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_{\beta})^2}}}$$

$f_{\beta}$ is the **cut-off frequency** at which the gain becomes $h_{fe}/\sqrt{2}$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Unity-gain bandwidth:**

$$f_T = h_{fe} \cdot f_{\beta} = 150 \times 4 \times 10^6 = 600 \times 10^6\ \text{Hz} = 600\ \text{MHz}$$

**(ii) Current gain at $f = 75$ MHz:**

Since $75\ \text{MHz} \gg f_{\beta} = 4$ MHz, the GBW approximation applies:

$$\vert h_{fe}(75\ \text{MHz}) \vert = \dfrac{f_T}{f} = \dfrac{600}{75} = 8$$

**Verification using exact formula:**

$$\vert h_{fe}(75\ \text{MHz}) \vert = \dfrac{150}{\sqrt{1 + (75/4)^2}} = \dfrac{150}{\sqrt{1 + 351.5625}} = \dfrac{150}{\sqrt{352.56}} = \dfrac{150}{18.78} \approx 7.99 \approx 8\ \checkmark$$

**(iii) Frequency at which current gain drops to 20:**

Since 20 is much less than $h_{fe} = 150$, we are in the roll-off region. Using GBW:

$$f = \dfrac{f_T}{20} = \dfrac{600\ \text{MHz}}{20} = 30\ \text{MHz}$$

**Verification:**

$$\dfrac{150}{\sqrt{1 + (f/4)^2}} = 20 \Rightarrow \sqrt{1 + (f/4)^2} = 7.5 \Rightarrow (f/4)^2 = 55.25 \Rightarrow f \approx 29.73\ \text{MHz}$$

The small discrepancy (29.73 vs 30) is due to the GBW approximation.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 Choice-Based]`

**(a) Define unity-gain bandwidth $f_T$ of a BJT. Show that $f_T$ is independent of the DC current gain $h_{fe}$ but depends on the transconductance $g_m$ and the internal capacitances. (7 marks)**

**(b) For a BJT biased at $I_C = 2$ mA, the transconductance is $g_m = 77$ mS, $C_{\pi} = 20$ pF, and $C_{\mu} = 2$ pF. Calculate: (i) $f_T$, (ii) $r_{\pi}$, and (iii) $f_{\beta}$ assuming $h_{fe} = 100$. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:** $f_T$ is the frequency at which the short-circuit CE current gain $\vert h_{fe} \vert$ becomes **unity (1)**.

**Derivation of independence from $h_{fe}$:**

Starting from:

$$f_T = h_{fe} \cdot f_{\beta} = h_{fe} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2\pi r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$$

Substituting $h_{fe} = g_m r_{\pi}$:

$$f_T = (g_m r_{\pi}) \cdot \dfrac{1}{2\pi r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})} = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi (C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$$

The $r_{\pi}$ terms cancel, proving that $f_T$ is **independent of $h_{fe}$** and depends only on $g_m$ (set by bias current $I_C$) and the physical capacitances $C_{\pi}, C_{\mu}$ (set by device geometry).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $I_C = 2$ mA, $g_m = 77$ mS, $C_{\pi} = 20$ pF, $C_{\mu} = 2$ pF, $h_{fe} = 100$.

**(i) Unity-gain bandwidth:**

$$f_T = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})} = \dfrac{77 \times 10^{-3}}{2\pi \times (20 + 2) \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$f_T = \dfrac{77 \times 10^{-3}}{2\pi \times 22 \times 10^{-12}} = \dfrac{77 \times 10^{-3}}{138.23 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 557.1 \times 10^6\ \text{Hz}$$

$$\boxed{f_T \approx 557\ \text{MHz}}$$

**(ii) Input resistance $r_{\pi}$:**

$$r_{\pi} = \dfrac{h_{fe}}{g_m} = \dfrac{100}{77 \times 10^{-3}} = 1298.7\ \Omega \approx 1.3\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**(iii) Cut-off frequency $f_{\beta}$:**

$$f_{\beta} = \dfrac{f_T}{h_{fe}} = \dfrac{557 \times 10^6}{100} = 5.57\ \text{MHz}$$

**Verification using the alternative formula:**

$$f_{\beta} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 1298.7 \times 22 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$f_{\beta} = \dfrac{1}{1.795 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 5.57\ \text{MHz}\ \checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the exact form of $f_T$:** When $h_{fe}$ is small (e.g., 5 or 10), the approximation $f_T \approx h_{fe} f_{\beta}$ introduces significant error. Use the **exact form** $f_T = f_{\beta} \sqrt{h_{fe}^2 - 1}$ and state the approximation explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing $f_{\beta}$ with $f_{\alpha}$:** $f_{\alpha}$ is the CB cut-off frequency, which is **much higher** than $f_{\beta}$. Do not interchange them.
> 3. **Ignoring units:** Always carry units (Hz, MHz, GHz) throughout the calculation. Examiners specifically deduct marks for missing units.
> 4. **Mixing up $r_{\pi}$ and $r_e$:** $r_{\pi} = h_{fe}/g_m = V_T h_{fe}/I_C$, while $r_e = 1/g_m = V_T/I_C$. They differ by a factor of $h_{fe}$.
> 5. **Not showing the magnitude step:** When asked to "show" the gain expression, students often write the complex form but skip $\vert h_{fe}(f) \vert = h_{fe}/\sqrt{1+(f/f_{\beta})^2}$, which is the **final answer** most questions demand.
> 6. **Treating $f_T$ as a function of $h_{fe}$:** A common misconception. Emphasize that $f_T$ is **independent of $h_{fe}$** (from the $g_m$-based expression) and is a true figure of merit.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **$f_{\beta}$** = frequency at which $\vert h_{fe} \vert$ drops to **0.707 $h_{fe}$** (i.e., the **3-dB down** point of CE current gain).
- **$f_T$** = frequency at which $\vert h_{fe} \vert$ drops to **unity (= 1)**.
- **Master formula:** $f_T = h_{fe} \cdot f_{\beta}$ (approximate, valid for $h_{fe} \gg 1$).
- **Exact formula:** $f_T = f_{\beta} \sqrt{h_{fe}^2 - 1}$.
- **$f_T$ in terms of device parameters:** $f_T = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$ — this proves $f_T$ is **independent of $h_{fe}$**.
- **Gain expression:** $h_{fe}(f) = \dfrac{h_{fe}}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_{\beta})^2}}$.
- **GBW in roll-off region:** $\vert h_{fe}(f) \vert \cdot f = f_T$ (constant).
- **Roll-off slope:** **−20 dB/decade** beyond $f_{\beta}$.
- **$f_{\alpha}$ vs $f_{\beta}$:** $f_{\alpha} \gg f_{\beta}$ (CB cut-off is far higher than CE cut-off).
- **Practical tip:** Higher $f_T$ → better high-frequency performance → more suitable for **RF, video, and high-speed digital** applications.
- **Examiner favorites:** Definition of $f_{\beta}$ and $f_T$ (3 marks), derivation of frequency-dependent current gain (7 marks), numerical computation of $f_T$, $f_{\beta}$, and gain at a given frequency (7 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MOSFET Amplifiers: Design of CS Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** MOSFET Amplifiers: Design of CS Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Common Source (CS) MOSFET Amplifier** is a single-stage, single-transistor voltage amplifier configuration in which the **Source** terminal of the MOSFET serves as the *common* (or grounded) node for both the input signal port and the output signal port. The input signal $v_{in}$ is applied between the **Gate** and the **Source**, while the amplified output $v_{out}$ is taken between the **Drain** and the **Source**.

The design of a CS amplifier involves the *systematic selection* of the quiescent (DC operating) point — i.e., $I_{DQ}$, $V_{GSQ}$, $V_{DSQ}$ — followed by the determination of small-signal parameters ($g_m$, $r_o$) so as to satisfy a *given* set of specifications: voltage gain $A_v$, input resistance $R_{in}$, output resistance $R_{out}$, bandwidth, and power dissipation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> The CS amplifier using an n-channel enhancement-mode MOSFET in the **saturation region** is the *workhorse building block* of analog integrated circuits. It provides a moderate-to-high voltage gain, a high input resistance (ideally infinite at low frequencies), and an inverting output ($180^\circ$ phase shift between input and output).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water-tap controlled by a sensitive pressure pad**:
- The **water pressure** at the tap (drain current $I_D$) is determined by how hard you press the pad (gate-to-source voltage $V_{GS}$).
- A *tiny* press on the pad (small input signal $v_{gs}$) produces a *large* change in water flow (drain current $i_d$) — this is the **transconductance** $g_m$.
- This amplified water flow is then converted back to a *voltage* across a downstream nozzle (the drain resistor $R_D$).
- The pressure pad itself (gate) doesn't let any water through, so the **input resistance is enormous** (almost infinite). This is why MOSFETs are preferred over BJTs at the front-end of instrumentation amplifiers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 2):**
> The design must always guarantee that the transistor operates in the **saturation (active) region** at the Q-point, given by the inequality: $V_{DS} \ge V_{GS} - V_t$ (equivalently $V_{DS} \ge V_{OV}$, where $V_{OV}$ is the overdrive voltage).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following physical/circuit quantities are central to CS amplifier design and are highlighted in **bold** as per KTU convention:

- **Threshold voltage $V_t$**: Typically **0.5 V to 1.5 V** for standard enhancement-mode n-MOSFETs in CMOS 180 nm technology. In discrete MOSFETs (e.g., IRF540, 2N7000), $V_t \approx 2\text{ V}$ to **4 V**.
- **Process transconductance parameter $k_n' = \mu_n C_{ox}$**: Units are **A/V²**. Typical value: **$\mathbf{50\ \mu A/V^2}$ to $250\ \mu A/V^2$** for a standard CMOS process.
- **Conduction parameter $k_n = \frac{1}{2} k_n' \cdot \frac{W}{L}$**: Units **A/V²**. The aspect ratio $\frac{W}{L}$ is the primary design knob.
- **Thermal voltage $V_T = \frac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85\ \text{mV}$ at $T = 300\ \text{K}$**. This constant is required when computing $g_m$ from bias current.
- **Early voltage $V_A$**: Typically **20 V to 100 V** for long-channel devices; sets the output resistance $r_o = \frac{V_A}{I_{DQ}}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of a CS Amplifier

A CS amplifier circuit (with source grounded) consists of **four functional blocks**:

1. **Biasing Network** ($R_1$, $R_2$, $R_S$, $R_G$): Sets the DC operating point (Q-point). The gate is biased through a high-value resistor $R_G$ (often **$1\ \text{M}\Omega$ to $10\ \text{M}\Omega$**) to provide high input resistance.
2. **Input Coupling Capacitor $C_{in}$**: Blocks DC from the source; passes AC signal to the gate. Acts as a short at mid-band frequencies.
3. **Active Device** (n-MOSFET in saturation): Performs the voltage-to-current conversion via transconductance $g_m$.
4. **Output Network** ($R_D$ and optional source-degeneration $R_S$ with bypass capacitor $C_S$): Converts the drain current variation back into an output voltage.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Source Bypass Capacitor $C_S$:** A critical design choice. Placing $C_S$ across $R_S$ shorts $R_S$ at signal frequencies, *preserving* the full $g_m$ in the gain expression. *Removing* $C_S$ introduces *source degeneration*, which **reduces gain** but **improves linearity** and **bandwidth** — a classic engineering trade-off.

### 2.2 DC Analysis (Bias Point Design)

The drain current in saturation follows the **square-law I-V relationship**:

$$I_{DQ} = \frac{1}{2} k_n' \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_t)^2 = k_n (V_{OV})^2$$

where $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{OV}$ is the **overdrive voltage** (sometimes called $V_{DS,sat}$ or $V_{DS,sat}$).

**DC conditions to be satisfied:**

- Gate voltage: $V_G = V_{GS} = V_{GG}$ (set by bias network / $V_{GS}$ reference).
- Source voltage: $V_S = I_{DQ} \cdot R_S$.
- Drain voltage: $V_D = V_{DD} - I_{DQ} \cdot R_D$.
- $V_{DS} = V_D - V_S \ge V_{OV}$ (saturation condition).

### 2.3 Small-Signal Parameters

Once the Q-point is fixed, the small-signal parameters are computed as:

$$g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} \bigg\vert_{Q} = k_n' \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot V_{OV} = \frac{2 I_{DQ}}{V_{OV}}$$

$$g_m = \sqrt{2 k_n' \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot I_{DQ}} = \frac{2 I_{DQ}}{V_{OV}}$$

$$r_o = \frac{V_A + V_{DS}}{I_{DQ}} \approx \frac{V_A}{I_{DQ}} \quad \text{(when } V_{DS} \ll V_A\text{)}$$

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_{DQ}}, \quad \text{where } \lambda = \frac{1}{V_A}$$

### 2.4 Mid-Band Voltage Gain (Small-Signal Analysis)

For a CS amplifier with source **fully bypassed** and ignoring $r_o$:

$$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o) = -g_m R_D'$$

For a CS amplifier with source degeneration $R_S$ (no bypass capacitor) and ignoring $r_o$:

$$A_v = \frac{-g_m R_D}{1 + g_m R_S} = \frac{-R_D}{\frac{1}{g_m} + R_S}$$

### 2.5 Input and Output Resistance

$$R_{in} = R_G \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2 \approx R_G \quad (\text{when } R_G \ll R_1, R_2)$$

$$R_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o \approx R_D \quad (\text{if } r_o \gg R_D)$$

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units / Conditions |
|---|----------------------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Drain current (sat.) | $I_{DQ} = \frac{1}{2} k_n' \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_t)^2$ | A; $V_{DS} \ge V_{OV}$ |
| 2 | Overdrive voltage | $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_t$ | V; must be $\ge 0.1\ \text{V}$ for strong inversion |
| 3 | Transconductance | $g_m = k_n' \frac{W}{L} V_{OV} = \frac{2 I_{DQ}}{V_{OV}}$ | A/V or S |
| 4 | Output resistance | $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_{DQ}} = \dfrac{V_A}{I_{DQ}}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 5 | Mid-band gain (bypassed) | $A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Mid-band gain (degenerated) | $A_v = \dfrac{-g_m R_D}{1 + g_m R_S}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Input resistance | $R_{in} = R_G$ (gate is isolated) | $\Omega$ |
| 8 | Output resistance | $R_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o$ | $\Omega$ |
| 9 | Max symmetric swing | $V_{DSQ} = \dfrac{V_{DD} - I_{DQ} R_S - V_t}{2} + V_{OV}$ | V (design formula) |
| 10 | Lower cutoff freq. (approx.) | $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi C_{in} (R_S_{ig} + R_{in})}$ | Hz (dominant pole) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** The CS amplifier is the *fundamental gain stage* inside every operational amplifier (e.g., the 741's input stage, the classic two-stage op-amp topology of Razavi and Sedra-Smith). It also forms the core of RF low-noise amplifiers (LNAs), mixers, and switched-capacitor sample-and-hold circuits used in modern CMOS analog and mixed-signal ICs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Mid-Band Voltage Gain (CS Amplifier with Bypassed Source)

**Starting setup:** A small-signal AC input $v_{gs}$ is applied to the gate. The source is grounded (for AC). The drain current variation is $i_d = g_m v_{gs}$.

**Step 1:** Apply KCL at the drain node in the small-signal equivalent circuit (with $R_D$ and $r_o$ in parallel between drain and ground):

$$i_d = g_m v_{gs} + \frac{v_{ds}}{r_o}$$

**Step 2:** Note that the drain current $i_d$ also flows through the parallel combination $R_D \parallel r_o$, producing a voltage drop $v_{ds}$:

$$v_{ds} = -i_d (R_D \parallel r_o)$$

(The negative sign arises because a *positive* $v_{gs}$ produces a *larger* $i_d$ flowing *into* the drain, which causes the drain voltage to *fall* — hence the $180^\circ$ phase inversion.)

**Step 3:** Substitute Step 2 into Step 1:

$$i_d = g_m v_{gs} - \frac{i_d (R_D \parallel r_o)}{r_o}$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to collect $i_d$ terms on the left-hand side:

$$i_d + \frac{i_d (R_D \parallel r_o)}{r_o} = g_m v_{gs}$$

$$i_d \left[ 1 + \frac{R_D \parallel r_o}{r_o} \right] = g_m v_{gs}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify the bracketed term:

$$1 + \frac{R_D \parallel r_o}{r_o} = \frac{r_o + R_D \parallel r_o}{r_o} = \frac{r_o + \dfrac{R_D r_o}{R_D + r_o}}{r_o}$$

$$= \frac{1}{r_o} \cdot \frac{r_o(R_D + r_o) + R_D r_o}{R_D + r_o} = \frac{r_o R_D + r_o^2 + R_D r_o}{r_o (R_D + r_o)} = \frac{2 r_o R_D + r_o^2}{r_o (R_D + r_o)}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $i_d$:

$$i_d = \frac{g_m v_{gs}}{1 + (R_D \parallel r_o)/r_o}$$

**Step 7:** Substitute $i_d$ into the expression for $v_{ds}$:

$$v_{ds} = -g_m v_{gs} \cdot \frac{R_D \parallel r_o}{1 + (R_D \parallel r_o)/r_o}$$

**Step 8:** For the typical case $R_D \ll r_o$ (i.e., the load is dominated by $R_D$), the term $(R_D \parallel r_o)/r_o \to 0$ and we get the *approximate* result:

$$v_{ds} \approx -g_m v_{gs} (R_D \parallel r_o)$$

**Step 9:** Therefore, the small-signal voltage gain is:

$$A_v = \frac{v_{ds}}{v_{gs}} = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$$

This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Voltage Gain with Source Degeneration (Unbypassed $R_S$)

**Setup:** Now $R_S$ is *not* bypassed. A small-signal $v_{gs}$ appears between the gate and the source terminal.

**Step 1:** Define the *actual* gate-to-source voltage in the small-signal model. Since the source is no longer at AC ground, the voltage at the source node is $v_s = i_d R_S$ (positive upwards).

$$v_{gs} = v_g - v_s = v_{in} - i_d R_S$$

**Step 2:** The drain current is controlled by the *internal* $v_{gs}$ (i.e., the voltage between gate and source terminals of the transistor itself):

$$i_d = g_m v_{gs} = g_m (v_{in} - i_d R_S)$$

**Step 3:** Expand and solve for $i_d$:

$$i_d = g_m v_{in} - g_m R_S \cdot i_d$$

$$i_d (1 + g_m R_S) = g_m v_{in}$$

$$i_d = \frac{g_m v_{in}}{1 + g_m R_S}$$

**Step 4:** The output voltage is developed across the parallel combination $R_D \parallel r_o$:

$$v_{out} = -i_d (R_D \parallel r_o) = -\frac{g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)}{1 + g_m R_S} \cdot v_{in}$$

**Step 5:** Therefore, the gain with source degeneration is:

$$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -\frac{g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)}{1 + g_m R_S}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** When $g_m R_S \gg 1$, this reduces to $A_v \approx -\dfrac{R_D \parallel r_o}{R_S}$. Notice that the gain becomes *independent* of $g_m$ — a hallmark of *negative feedback*. This improves linearity and reduces sensitivity to process/temperature variations, at the cost of reduced gain magnitude.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Q-Point Design for Maximum Symmetric Output Swing

A common KTU design problem is: "Design the CS amplifier for a given $V_{DD}$, $V_t$, and $k_n' W/L$ to achieve maximum *symmetric* output swing."

**Step 1:** The output swings between two extremes:
- Upper limit: $V_{out,max} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ} R_S$ (when MOSFET is in cutoff)
- Lower limit: $V_{out,min} = V_{GS} - V_t + I_{DQ} R_S = V_{OV} + I_{DQ} R_S$ (when MOSFET enters triode)

**Step 2:** For *symmetric* swing about the Q-point $V_{DSQ}$:

$$V_{DD} - I_{DQ} R_S - V_{DSQ} = V_{DSQ} - (V_{OV} + I_{DQ} R_S)$$

**Step 3:** Simplify:

$$V_{DD} - I_{DQ} R_S - V_{DSQ} = V_{DSQ} - V_{OV} - I_{DQ} R_S$$

$$V_{DD} - V_{DSQ} = V_{DSQ} - V_{OV}$$

$$V_{DSQ} = \frac{V_{DD} + V_{OV}}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Since $V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ}(R_D + R_S)$, we can solve for $R_D + R_S$:

$$I_{DQ}(R_D + R_S) = V_{DD} - V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - \frac{V_{DD} + V_{OV}}{2} = \frac{V_{DD} - V_{OV}}{2}$$

**Step 5:** The bias point design equations for symmetric swing are thus:

$$R_D + R_S = \frac{V_{DD} - V_{OV}}{2 I_{DQ}}$$

$$V_{GSQ} = V_t + V_{OV}$$

$$V_{DSQ} = \frac{V_{DD} + V_{OV}}{2}$$

These are the *standard* design equations used in KTU problems. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Numerical Design Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Design a CS amplifier with $V_{DD} = 12\ \text{V}$, $V_t = 1.5\ \text{V}$, $k_n' (W/L) = 1\ \text{mA/V}^2$, $I_{DQ} = 0.5\ \text{mA}$. Find $V_{OV}$, $V_{GSQ}$, and the gain if $R_D = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ and $R_S$ is fully bypassed. Assume $r_o = \infty$.

**Step 1 — Overdrive voltage:**

$$V_{OV} = \sqrt{\frac{2 I_{DQ}}{k_n' (W/L)}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-3}}} = \sqrt{1} = 1\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Gate-to-source voltage:**

$$V_{GSQ} = V_t + V_{OV} = 1.5 + 1 = 2.5\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Transconductance:**

$$g_m = \frac{2 I_{DQ}}{V_{OV}} = \frac{2 \times 0.5\ \text{mA}}{1\ \text{V}} = 1\ \text{mA/V} = 1\ \text{mS}$$

**Step 4 — Mid-band gain:**

$$A_v = -g_m R_D = -(1\ \text{mS})(10\ \text{k}\Omega) = -10\ \text{V/V}$$

**Step 5 — Drain-to-source voltage at Q-point:**

$$V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ} R_D = 12 - (0.5\ \text{mA})(10\ \text{k}\Omega) = 12 - 5 = 7\ \text{V}$$

**Step 6 — Saturation check:**

$$V_{DSQ} = 7\ \text{V} \ge V_{OV} = 1\ \text{V} \quad \checkmark$$

The transistor is comfortably in saturation, and the design is valid.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CS Amplifier

The Mermaid diagram below maps the signal-flow architecture of a common-source MOSFET amplifier, including the bias, AC coupling, and small-signal equivalent stages.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["INPUT SIGNAL SOURCE v_sig"] --> B["COUPLING CAPACITOR C_in"]
    B --> C["BIAS NETWORK R1 R2 RG"]
    C --> D["MOSFET GATE NODE"]
    D --> E["NMOS TRANSISTOR IN SATURATION"]
    E --> F["DRAIN NODE"]
    F --> G["DRAIN RESISTOR R_D"]
    G --> H["V_DD SUPPLY"]
    F --> I["COUPLING CAPACITOR C_out"]
    I --> J["OUTPUT NODE v_out"]
    K["SOURCE TERMINAL"] --> L["SOURCE RESISTOR R_S"]
    L --> M["SOURCE BYPASS CAPACITOR C_S"]
    M --> N["GROUND"]
    E --> K
    D --> O["GATE TO SOURCE VOLTAGE v_gs"]
    O --> P["TRANSCONDUCTANCE g_m BLOCK"]
    P --> Q["DRAIN CURRENT i_d"]
    Q --> F
    style A fill:#FFD580
    style J fill:#B5EAD7
    style E fill:#FF9AA2
    style P fill:#C7CEEA
    style N fill:#D3D3D3
```

### 4.2 Small-Signal Equivalent Model Flow

The figure below shows the *low-frequency* small-signal equivalent of the CS amplifier — the model used for computing $A_v$, $R_{in}$, and $R_{out}$ in the KTU mid-band analysis region.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["v_in AT GATE"] --> A2["R_G HIGH IMPEDANCE GATE RESISTOR"]
    A2 --> A3["GATE TERMINAL OF HYBRID PI MODEL"]
    A3 --> A4["CONTROLLED CURRENT SOURCE g_m TIMES v_gs"]
    A4 --> A5["DRAIN NODE"]
    A5 --> A6["R_D IN PARALLEL WITH r_o"]
    A6 --> A7["GROUND"]
    A5 --> A8["OUTPUT v_out"]
    A9["SOURCE NODE"] --> A7
    A1 -. v_gs .-> A3
    style A4 fill:#FFB6B6
    style A6 fill:#B6D7FF
    style A8 fill:#B5EAD7
```

### 4.3 Frequency-Response Architecture

The CS amplifier exhibits three distinct frequency regions: low-frequency (coupling/bypass capacitors dominate), mid-band (flat gain), and high-frequency (internal device capacitances $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$ dominate).

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["LOW FREQUENCY REGION"] --> B["C_in AND C_S FORM HIGH PASS FILTERS"]
    B --> C["MID BAND REGION"]
    C --> D["FLAT GAIN A_v_mid = NEGATIVE g_m TIMES R_D"]
    D --> E["HIGH FREQUENCY REGION"]
    E --> F["Cgs AND Cgd CAUSE GAIN ROLLOFF AT f_H"]
    F --> G["UNITY GAIN BANDWIDTH f_T = g_m OVER TWO PI C_GS"]
    style C fill:#B5EAD7
    style D fill:#FFD580
    style G fill:#FF9AA2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When the question says *"in the mid-band region"*, it implies that all coupling and bypass capacitors are AC shorts, and all internal device capacitances are AC opens. Under this assumption, the small-signal model reduces to a simple controlled-current source with $g_m v_{gs}$ feeding a parallel $R_L = R_D \parallel r_o$, and the gain is simply $-g_m R_L$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State the conditions under which a MOSFET operates as an amplifier in the saturation region. Why is the common-source (CS) configuration the most widely used MOSFET amplifier topology?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Conditions for saturation:**
1. $V_{GS} > V_t$ (channel must be induced; $V_{GS} = V_{OV} + V_t$).
2. $V_{DS} \ge V_{GS} - V_t = V_{OV}$ (channel must be *pinched off* near the drain).

**Why CS is widely used:**
- It provides **high voltage gain** (comparable to a CE BJT stage).
- It has **infinite DC input resistance** at the gate (no gate current flows), making it ideal for cascading and for use as the input stage of op-amps.
- It provides a **$180^\circ$ phase inversion** between input and output, useful for feedback amplifier design.
- The output is taken at the high-impedance drain node, ensuring good isolation from the load.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the transconductance $g_m$ of a MOSFET. Derive the relationship $g_m = \frac{2 I_{DQ}}{V_{OV}}$ starting from the square-law I-V equation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The transconductance $g_m$ is the small-signal gain parameter that quantifies the *change in drain current per unit change in gate-to-source voltage* at a fixed $V_{DS}$. It is defined as:

$$g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} \bigg\vert_{V_{DS} = \text{const.}}$$

**Derivation:** Starting from $I_D = \frac{1}{2} k_n' \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_t)^2$, differentiate w.r.t. $V_{GS}$:

$$g_m = k_n' \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_t) = k_n' \frac{W}{L} \cdot V_{OV}$$

Multiplying and dividing by $2 I_{DQ} = k_n' \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}^2$ yields:

$$g_m = \frac{2 I_{DQ}}{V_{OV}}$$

This shows that $g_m$ is *linearly* proportional to $V_{OV}$ and to the bias current $I_{DQ}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** A common-source n-channel enhancement MOSFET amplifier has the following parameters: $V_{DD} = 15\ \text{V}$, $V_t = 2\ \text{V}$, $k_n' (W/L) = 0.4\ \text{mA/V}^2$, $R_D = 6\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_S = 4\ \text{k}\Omega$ (with a bypass capacitor $C_S$), and $R_G = 1\ \text{M}\Omega$. Design the bias point to achieve a drain current of $I_{DQ} = 1\ \text{mA}$. Compute the overdrive voltage $V_{OV}$, the gate-to-source voltage $V_{GS}$, the drain-to-source voltage $V_{DSQ}$, and the small-signal transconductance $g_m$.

**(b) [7 marks]** For the same amplifier, calculate the small-signal mid-band voltage gain $A_v$, the input resistance $R_{in}$, and the output resistance $R_{out}$. Assume $\lambda = 0$. Comment on the effect of removing the bypass capacitor $C_S$ on the gain.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Q-Point Design [7 marks]:**

- **Step 1 — Overdrive voltage:** Using $I_{DQ} = \frac{1}{2} k_n' (W/L) V_{OV}^2$:

$$V_{OV} = \sqrt{\frac{2 I_{DQ}}{k_n' (W/L)}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1\ \text{mA}}{0.4\ \text{mA/V}^2}} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236\ \text{V}$$

- **Step 2 — Gate-to-source voltage:**

$$V_{GSQ} = V_t + V_{OV} = 2 + 2.236 = 4.236\ \text{V}$$

- **Step 3 — Drain-to-source voltage:** With $R_S = 4\ \text{k}\Omega$ and $I_{DQ} = 1\ \text{mA}$:

$$V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ}(R_D + R_S) = 15 - 1 \times (6 + 4) = 15 - 10 = 5\ \text{V}$$

- **Step 4 — Saturation check:**

$$V_{DSQ} = 5\ \text{V} \ge V_{OV} = 2.236\ \text{V} \quad \checkmark$$

- **Step 5 — Transconductance:**

$$g_m = k_n' (W/L) V_{OV} = 0.4 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.236 = 0.894\ \text{mA/V} \approx 0.894\ \text{mS}$$

- **Step 6 — Gate bias voltage:** Since source is at $V_S = I_{DQ} R_S = 4\ \text{V}$:

$$V_G = V_{GSQ} + V_S = 4.236 + 4 = 8.236\ \text{V}$$

This must be provided by the $R_1$–$R_2$ divider (not asked).

---

**(b) Small-Signal Parameters [7 marks]:**

- **Step 1 — Mid-band gain (with $C_S$ bypassed, $\lambda = 0$ so $r_o = \infty$):**

$$A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o) = -g_m R_D = -(0.894\ \text{mS})(6\ \text{k}\Omega) = -5.36\ \text{V/V}$$

- **Step 2 — Input resistance:**

$$R_{in} = R_G = 1\ \text{M}\Omega$$

(since the gate draws no DC current and $R_G$ is in parallel with the bias network; typically the bias divider is designed to have a Thevenin resistance $\gg R_G$).

- **Step 3 — Output resistance:**

$$R_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o = R_D = 6\ \text{k}\Omega$$

- **Step 4 — Effect of removing $C_S$ (unbypassed $R_S$):**

The gain becomes:

$$A_v' = \frac{-g_m R_D}{1 + g_m R_S} = \frac{-(0.894\ \text{mS})(6\ \text{k}\Omega)}{1 + (0.894\ \text{mS})(4\ \text{k}\Omega)} = \frac{-5.36}{1 + 3.576} = \frac{-5.36}{4.576} \approx -1.17\ \text{V/V}$$

**Comment:** Removing $C_S$ reduces the gain magnitude from **5.36** to **1.17** (a factor of ~4.6×). However, this trade-off improves linearity, increases bandwidth (Miller effect on $C_{gd}$ is reduced), and provides thermal stability against $V_t$ variations.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a small-signal equivalent circuit, derive the expression for the mid-band voltage gain of a CS amplifier (i) with the source fully bypassed, and (ii) with an unbypassed source resistance $R_S$. Discuss the role of the bypass capacitor $C_S$ in the design.

**(b) [7 marks]** For a CS amplifier biased at $I_{DQ} = 0.8\ \text{mA}$ with $V_{OV} = 0.8\ \text{V}$ and $R_D = 5\ \text{k}\Omega$, compute (i) $g_m$, (ii) the gain with $C_S$ connected, and (iii) the gain if $R_S = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$ is left unbypassed. Take $\lambda = 0.02\ \text{V}^{-1}$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation [7 marks]:**

Refer to **Section 3.1** and **Section 3.2** of these notes for the full derivation. The key results are:

- **With $C_S$:** $A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$
- **Without $C_S$:** $A_v = \dfrac{-g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)}{1 + g_m R_S}$

**Role of $C_S$:** The bypass capacitor $C_S$ provides an *AC short circuit* across $R_S$ at signal frequencies, allowing the full transconductance $g_m$ to appear in the gain expression. Without $C_S$, $R_S$ introduces *series-series negative feedback* (source degeneration), which reduces the gain but improves linearity, bandwidth, and bias-point stability.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation [7 marks]:**

- **(i) Transconductance:**

$$g_m = \frac{2 I_{DQ}}{V_{OV}} = \frac{2 \times 0.8\ \text{mA}}{0.8\ \text{V}} = 2\ \text{mA/V} = 2\ \text{mS}$$

- **(ii) Gain with $C_S$ connected:** First compute $r_o$:

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_{DQ}} = \frac{1}{0.02 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{1.6 \times 10^{-5}} = 62.5\ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$R_D \parallel r_o = \frac{5 \times 62.5}{5 + 62.5} = \frac{312.5}{67.5} \approx 4.63\ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o) = -(2\ \text{mS})(4.63\ \text{k}\Omega) \approx -9.26\ \text{V/V}$$

- **(iii) Gain with $R_S = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$ unbypassed:**

$$A_v = \frac{-g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)}{1 + g_m R_S} = \frac{-(2\ \text{mS})(4.63\ \text{k}\Omega)}{1 + (2\ \text{mS})(1\ \text{k}\Omega)} = \frac{-9.26}{1 + 2} = \frac{-9.26}{3} \approx -3.09\ \text{V/V}$$

The unbypassed $R_S$ reduces the gain magnitude by a factor of **3**, exactly as predicted by the $(1 + g_m R_S)$ denominator.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in CS Amplifier Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the saturation check** ($-1$ to $-2$ marks): Always verify $V_{DSQ} \ge V_{OV}$ at the end of any bias design. If violated, the MOSFET is in the triode region and the entire analysis is invalid.
> 2. **Sign error in gain**: The CS amplifier produces a *negative* (inverting) gain. Writing $A_v = +g_m R_D$ instead of $A_v = -g_m R_D$ is a guaranteed $-1$ mark penalty.
> 3. **Ignoring $r_o$** when $\lambda \ne 0$: If the problem gives $V_A$ or $\lambda$, you *must* include $r_o$ in parallel with $R_D$. This changes the gain by a noticeable factor (10-20%).
> 4. **Confusion between $V_{OV}$ and $V_{GS}$**: $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_t$ is the *excess* gate voltage. Mixing up these two quantities leads to a cascade of errors.
> 5. **Bypass capacitor assumption**: If the problem does not explicitly mention $C_S$, assume the *worst case* and write the gain *with source degeneration*. State your assumption clearly.
> 6. **Units mismatch**: $g_m$ is in *mA/V* (= mS) when $I_D$ is in mA. Converting incorrectly to A/V gives a gain off by a factor of 1000!

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CS amplifier core identity:** Input at gate, output at drain, source at AC ground. Inverting voltage amplifier with high input resistance and moderate-to-high voltage gain.
- **Square-law device:** $I_D = \frac{1}{2} k_n' (W/L) (V_{GS} - V_t)^2$ in saturation. This *nonlinear* relation is what makes the MOSFET a useful amplifier when biased correctly.
- **$g_m$ has three equivalent expressions:**

$$g_m = k_n' \frac{W}{L} V_{OV} = \frac{2 I_{DQ}}{V_{OV}} = \sqrt{2 k_n' \frac{W}{L} I_{DQ}}$$

  Memorize all three — KTU problems use them interchangeably.

- **Gain with bypassed source:** $A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$. **Gain with unbypassed source:** $A_v = \dfrac{-g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)}{1 + g_m R_S}$.
- **Saturation condition:** $V_{DS} \ge V_{OV}$. Always check at the end of bias-point design.
- **Maximum symmetric swing design:** $V_{DSQ} = \dfrac{V_{DD} + V_{OV}}{2}$ and $R_D + R_S = \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_{OV}}{2 I_{DQ}}$.
- **Input resistance is dominated by $R_G$** (typically $1\text{ M}\Omega$ to $10\ \text{M}\Omega$); the gate itself is effectively open-circuit.
- **Output resistance** is approximately $R_D \parallel r_o$. Including $r_o$ reduces the gain but makes the design more realistic.
- **Source degeneration trade-off:** $R_S$ unbypassed $\rightarrow$ lower gain but better linearity, bandwidth, and bias stability.
