- **Cascading rule of thumb:** To cascade two CS stages, use a *level shifter* or a *buffer* (source follower) between them, since the high output DC voltage of stage 1 would saturate stage 2.
- **High-frequency behaviour:** The Miller effect multiplies $C_{gd}$ by $(1 - A_v) \approx (1 + g_m R_D)$, which is the dominant limitation on the upper cutoff frequency $f_H$.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line KTU Mantra:** *"CS amp = square-law device in saturation, mid-band gain = $-g_m R_D'$ (or degenerated form), and always verify $V_{DSQ} \ge V_{OV}$ before writing the final answer."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Small Signal Analysis Using Hybrid-π Model (Mid Frequency Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Small Signal Analysis Using Hybrid-π Model (Mid Frequency Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Small Signal Analysis** is a linearized AC analysis technique applied to transistor amplifier circuits in which the input signal amplitude is sufficiently small (typically $\leq 10\,\text{mV}$ peak) so that the transistor's nonlinear $i$–$v$ characteristics can be approximated by a linear equivalent circuit about a fixed **DC Quiescent Operating Point (Q-point)**.

**The Hybrid-π Model** is the most widely used **two-port small-signal equivalent circuit of an intrinsic BJT**, valid in the **forward-active region** and accurate over a wide frequency range. It represents the transistor as a network of three terminal pairs (Base, Collector, Emitter) containing three fundamental parameters:

| Parameter | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|--------|------------------|
| Base–Emitter diffusion resistance | $r_{\pi}$ | Input resistance looking into the base |
| Transconductance | $g_m$ | Voltage-controlled current gain element |
| Output (Early) resistance | $r_o$ | Output resistance looking into the collector |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mid-Frequency Definition:** In the mid-frequency band, all **coupling and bypass capacitors act as short circuits** (since their reactances are negligible), while all **internal transistor capacitances** (junction capacitances $C_\pi$, $C_\mu$) act as **open circuits** (since their reactances are extremely large). Thus the hybrid-π model contains **only resistive elements** in this band, making pure algebraic (real-valued) analysis possible.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hydraulic Valve"

Imagine a **transistor** as a *precision hydraulic valve*:

- The **base–emitter junction** is the *control nozzle*. The amount you push on it (small input voltage $v_{be}$) controls how much water (current $i_c$) flows from the collector pipe to the emitter pipe.
- The **valve's mechanical stiffness** is captured by $r_\pi$ — a stiffer valve requires more force (voltage) per unit flow (current).
- The **valve's sensitivity** (flow rate per unit push) is captured by $g_m$ — a more sensitive valve produces a large output flow for a tiny input push.
- The **leaky seal** that lets a small amount of water escape backward is captured by $r_o$ — a finite, large output resistance representing the Early effect.

At **mid-frequency**, the system behaves like an **ideal linear valve** — no sluggish water-hammer effects from inertias (capacitors), no leakage transients. It is in its "comfort zone" of operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Hybrid-π"?** The name comes from the *π-shaped (pi-shaped)* resistive network ($r_\pi$ between base and emitter, $r_o$ between collector and emitter) with a *hybrid* (mixed) current-controlled source $g_m v_{be}$ injected at the collector node. The hybrid-π model was developed by L.J. Giacoletto and supersedes the older **T-model** because it is far more convenient for high-frequency analysis.

---

## 1.3 The Standard Hybrid-π Equivalent Circuit

The complete low-to-mid frequency small-signal model of an intrinsic BJT is:

**Nodes:** $b$ (Base), $c$ (Collector), $e$ (Emitter)
**Reference:** Common Emitter (emitter is the grounded terminal)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Base–Emitter branch:} \quad & r_\pi \text{ between } b \text{ and } e \\
\text{Collector–Emitter branch:} \quad & r_o \text{ between } c \text{ and } e \\
\text{Dependent source:} \quad & g_m \, v_{be} \text{ flowing from } c \text{ to } e \\
& \text{where } v_{be} \text{ is the voltage across } r_\pi
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition (Mid-Band):** Coupling capacitors $C_{in}, C_{out}$ and emitter-bypass capacitor $C_E$ are replaced by **short circuits**. Internal capacitances $C_\pi, C_\mu$ are **open circuits**. All impedances are purely **real (resistive)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Derivation of the Hybrid-π Parameters from Device Physics

The three fundamental small-signal parameters are **not independent** — they are derived from the **DC bias currents** of the Q-point and the physical constants of the transistor.

### 2.1.1 Transconductance $g_m$

By definition, $g_m$ is the rate of change of collector current with respect to base–emitter voltage, evaluated at the Q-point:

$$
g_m = \left.\frac{\partial i_C}{\partial v_{BE}}\right|_{Q\text{-point}}
$$

Using the Ebers–Moll equation in the forward-active region, $i_C = I_S \, e^{v_{BE}/V_T}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_m &= \frac{I_S \, e^{v_{BE}/V_T}}{V_T} = \frac{I_{CQ}}{V_T}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ is the **thermal voltage**, approximately $\mathbf{26\,\text{mV}}$ at room temperature ($\mathbf{300\,K}$).

### 2.1.2 Base–Emitter Diffusion Resistance $r_\pi$

Applying Ohm's law to the base–emitter junction at the Q-point:

$$
r_\pi = \frac{V_T}{I_{BQ}} = \frac{\beta \, V_T}{I_{CQ}}
$$

Using the identity $I_{CQ} = \beta \, I_{BQ}$.

### 2.1.3 Output Resistance $r_o$ (Early Effect)

Due to base-width modulation (Early effect), the collector current has a finite slope with respect to $v_{CE}$. The output resistance is:

$$
r_o = \frac{V_A + V_{CEQ}}{I_{CQ}} \approx \frac{V_A}{I_{CQ}}
$$

where $V_A$ is the **Early voltage** (typically $50$–$200\,\text{V}$).

### 2.1.4 Critical Relationship

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single most important KTU-exam relation connecting all three parameters:
> $$g_m \, r_\pi = \beta$$
> This means: $r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta}{g_m}$ and $g_m = \dfrac{\beta}{r_\pi}$.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Mid-Frequency, Common-Emitter)

Below is the **complete formula bank** required to solve any mid-band small-signal problem using the Hybrid-π model.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Thermal Voltage | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 26\,\text{mV}$ at $300\,\text{K}$ | V |
| 2 | Transconductance | $g_m = \dfrac{I_{CQ}}{V_T}$ | S (Siemens) |
| 3 | Base Diffusion Resistance | $r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta}{g_m} = \dfrac{V_T}{I_{BQ}}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 4 | Output Resistance | $r_o = \dfrac{V_A}{I_{CQ}}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 5 | CE Voltage Gain (no $R_E$, with $R_L$) | $A_v = -g_m \bigl(R_C \,\parallel\, R_L \,\parallel\, r_o\bigr)$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | CE Input Resistance (looking into base) | $R_{in,b} = r_\pi$ | $\Omega$ |
| 7 | CE Overall Input Resistance | $R_{in} = R_B \,\parallel\, r_\pi$ | $\Omega$ |
| 8 | CE Output Resistance | $R_{out} = R_C \,\parallel\, r_o$ | $\Omega$ |
| 9 | CE Short-Circuit Current Gain | $A_{is} = \beta$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | CE Current Gain (with load) | $A_i = -\beta \,\dfrac{R_C}{R_C + R_L}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | CE Voltage Gain with Unbypassed $R_E$ | $A_v = \dfrac{-g_m (R_C \,\parallel\, R_L \,\parallel\, r_o)}{1 + g_m R_E}$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Common-Base Voltage Gain | $A_v \approx g_m (R_C \,\parallel\, R_L)$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | Common-Base Input Resistance | $R_{in} \approx \dfrac{r_\pi}{\beta+1} \approx \dfrac{1}{g_m}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 14 | Common-Collector (Emitter Follower) Voltage Gain | $A_v \approx \dfrac{g_m R_E}{1 + g_m R_E} \approx 1$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas above are valid **only in the mid-frequency band** where all capacitors are either short-circuited (external coupling/bypass) or open-circuited (internal device capacitances).

---

## 2.3 Physical Intuition Behind the Negative Sign in CE Gain

The negative sign in $A_v = -g_m R_C$ for a CE amplifier arises because of a **phase reversal** between input and output:

- A **positive** $v_{in}$ increases $v_{BE}$, which **increases** $i_C$.
- An increased $i_C$ produces a **larger voltage drop** across $R_C$.
- Since $v_{CE} = V_{CC} - i_C R_C$, the **collector voltage drops**.
- Therefore $v_{out}$ becomes **negative** while $v_{in}$ is positive — a **180° phase shift**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Significance:** This inverting property makes the CE stage the workhorse of analog integrated circuits, enabling cascaded amplifier stages, op-amp internal design, and feedback networks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Common-Emitter (CE) Amplifier with Bypassed $R_E$

### 3.1.1 Given Circuit

A typical CE amplifier has:
- Coupling capacitors $C_{in}, C_{out}$ at input and output.
- Bias resistor $R_B$ from $V_{CC}$ to base.
- Collector resistor $R_C$ from $V_{CC}$ to collector.
- Emitter resistor $R_E$ fully bypassed by $C_E$ at the frequency of interest.

### 3.1.2 AC Equivalent (Mid-Band) Steps

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — DC Sources → Ground:** Set $V_{CC} = 0$ (short to ground) since it is a DC source.
>
> **Step 2 — Capacitors → Short:** $C_{in}, C_{out}, C_E$ all become short circuits.
>
> **Step 3 — Replace BJT with Hybrid-π Model:** Substitute the small-signal equivalent between base, collector, and emitter nodes.
>
> **Step 4 — Solve the resulting linear resistive network** for $A_v, R_{in}, R_{out}, A_i$.

### 3.1.3 Detailed Derivation of Voltage Gain $A_v$

Let $v_{be}$ be the voltage across $r_\pi$ (i.e., from base to emitter). The dependent current source delivers a current of $g_m v_{be}$ flowing from the collector node into the emitter node.

Apply **KCL at the collector node** (taking currents leaving the node as positive):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{v_c}{R_C} + \frac{v_c}{r_o} + \frac{v_c}{R_L} + g_m v_{be} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Factor out $v_c$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_c \left(\frac{1}{R_C} + \frac{1}{r_o} + \frac{1}{R_L}\right) &= -g_m v_{be} \\[4pt]
v_c &= \dfrac{-g_m v_{be}}{\dfrac{1}{R_C} + \dfrac{1}{r_o} + \dfrac{1}{R_L}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Recognize that $\dfrac{1}{R_C} + \dfrac{1}{r_o} + \dfrac{1}{R_L} = \dfrac{1}{R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L}$, hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_c &= -g_m \, v_{be} \, (R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L)
\end{aligned}
$$

The output voltage $v_{out} = v_c$ (since the output coupling capacitor is a short), and the input voltage $v_{in} = v_{be}$ (since the emitter is grounded through the bypass capacitor):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_v \;=\; \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} \;=\; \frac{v_c}{v_{be}} \;=\; -g_m \,(R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Neglect Approximation:** If $r_o \geq 10 R_C$ (a very common situation), the textbook practice is to neglect $r_o$, giving the simplified result $A_v \approx -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$.

### 3.1.4 Detailed Derivation of Input Resistance $R_{in}$

Apply **KCL at the base node**. The current entering from $v_{in}$ splits into two branches: one through $R_B$ to ground, and one through $r_\pi$ to the emitter (which is grounded).

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{in} &= \frac{v_{in}}{R_B} + \frac{v_{in}}{r_\pi} \\[4pt]
\frac{v_{in}}{i_{in}} &= \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{R_B} + \dfrac{1}{r_\pi}} \;=\; R_B \parallel r_\pi
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{R_{in} = R_B \parallel r_\pi}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The current through $r_\pi$ is $i_b = \dfrac{v_{in}}{r_\pi}$, and this is **strictly the base current** flowing into the transistor. The dependent source draws additional current $g_m v_{be} = \beta \, i_b$ from the collector but **does not affect the input node**.

### 3.1.5 Detailed Derivation of Output Resistance $R_{out}$

To find $R_{out}$, set $v_{in} = 0$ (turn off the independent source), remove $R_L$, and apply a test voltage $v_t$ at the output node.

Since $v_{in} = 0 \Rightarrow v_{be} = 0 \Rightarrow g_m v_{be} = 0$, the dependent current source becomes an **open circuit**.

The resistance seen from the output node is then $R_C$ in parallel with $r_o$:

$$
\boxed{R_{out} = R_C \parallel r_o}
$$

### 3.1.6 Detailed Derivation of Current Gain $A_i$

Define $A_i = \dfrac{i_{out}}{i_{in}}$ where $i_{out}$ is the current through the load $R_L$ and $i_{in}$ is the current drawn from the source.

Current through $R_L$ (with the negative sign indicating direction):

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{out} &= -\frac{v_{out}}{R_L} \;=\; -\frac{A_v \, v_{in}}{R_L} \;=\; \frac{g_m \, v_{in} \, (R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L)}{R_L}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L = \dfrac{R_C \, r_o \, R_L}{R_C \, r_o + R_C \, R_L + r_o \, R_L}$, the current through $R_L$ simplifies to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{out} &= g_m \, v_{in} \, \dfrac{R_C \, r_o}{R_C \, r_o + R_C \, R_L + r_o \, R_L}
\end{aligned}
$$

The input current $i_{in} = \dfrac{v_{in}}{R_B \parallel r_\pi}$. Recognizing $g_m \, r_\pi = \beta$ and simplifying further, the overall current gain for the matched case $R_C \parallel r_o \gg R_L$ (very common assumption) reduces to:

$$
\boxed{A_i = \dfrac{-\beta \, R_C}{R_C + R_L}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: CE Amplifier with Unbypassed Emitter Resistor $R_E$

When the bypass capacitor $C_E$ is **removed or ineffective** (at low frequencies or by design), $R_E$ remains in the AC equivalent circuit. The derivation changes significantly.

### 3.2.1 Modified AC Equivalent

The emitter is no longer grounded. Current $i_e$ flowing out of the emitter produces a voltage drop $v_e = i_e R_E$.

Apply **KVL around the base–emitter loop:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{in} &= i_b \, r_\pi + i_e \, R_E
\end{aligned}
$$

Using $i_e = (\beta + 1) \, i_b$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{in} &= i_b \, r_\pi + (\beta + 1) \, i_b \, R_E \;=\; i_b \bigl[r_\pi + (\beta + 1) R_E\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Resistance Looking into the Emitter

The resistance looking into the emitter terminal (with base AC-grounded) is:

$$
\boxed{R_{in,e} = \frac{r_\pi + R_B'}{\beta + 1} \approx \frac{1}{g_m} + \frac{R_B'}{\beta + 1}}
$$

where $R_B' = R_B \parallel R_S$ (source resistance). For typical values, this is approximately $\dfrac{1}{g_m}$.

### 3.2.3 Voltage Gain with Unbypassed $R_E$

Since $v_{be} = i_b \, r_\pi$ and $v_{out} = -g_m v_{be} (R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L) = -\beta \, i_b (R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_v &= \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} \;=\; \frac{-\beta \, i_b \, (R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L)}{i_b \bigl[r_\pi + (\beta + 1) R_E\bigr]} \\[6pt]
A_v &= \frac{-g_m (R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L)}{1 + g_m R_E}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Unbypassed $R_E$ reduces $|A_v|$ by a factor of $(1 + g_m R_E)$ but **stabilizes the gain** against variations in $\beta$ and temperature — this is called **emitter degeneration** and is the foundation of negative-feedback amplifiers.

---

## 3.3 Comparison of the Three BJT Amplifier Configurations

| Parameter | Common-Emitter (CE) | Common-Base (CB) | Common-Collector (CC) |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|------------------------|
| Voltage Gain $A_v$ | **Large, Inverting** ($\approx -g_m R_C$) | Large, Non-Inverting ($\approx g_m R_C$) | $\approx 1$, Non-Inverting |
| Current Gain $A_i$ | $\beta$ | $\approx 1$ | $\approx \beta + 1$ |
| Power Gain | **Highest** | Moderate | Moderate |
| Input Resistance | Moderate ($\sim r_\pi$) | **Very Low** ($\sim 1/g_m$) | **Very High** ($\sim \beta R_E$) |
| Output Resistance | Moderate ($\sim R_C$) | Very High ($\sim R_C$) | **Very Low** ($\sim 1/g_m$) |
| Phase Reversal | Yes ($180°$) | No ($0°$) | No ($0°$) |
| Typical Use | General-purpose voltage amplifier | High-frequency, low-impedance driver | Buffer, impedance matcher |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete CE Amplifier with Hybrid-π Small-Signal Model

The diagram below is the **canonical mid-frequency AC equivalent** of a Common-Emitter BJT amplifier using the Hybrid-π model. This is the exact circuit students must draw for KTU derivations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_SIDE["INPUT SIDE"]
        Vsrc["v_in Source"]
        Rsrc["R_S Source Resistance"]
        Cin["C_in Short Circuit"]
        Vsrc --- Rsrc
        Rsrc --- Cin
    end

    subgraph BASE_REGION["BASE NODE b"]
        Cin --> bNode(("Base Node b"))
        RbRes{{"R_B"}}
        bNode --- RbRes
        RbRes --- gndA[("GND AC")]
        rpiRes{{"r_pi"}}
        bNode --- rpiRes
        rpiRes --- emitNode(("Emitter Node e"))
        emitNode --- gndB[("GND AC")]
    end

    subgraph COLLECTOR_REGION["COLLECTOR NODE c"]
        gmSrc{{"g_m times v_be Dependent Current Source"}}
        bNode -.Controls.-> gmSrc
        gmSrc --- collNode(("Collector Node c"))
        RcRes{{"R_C"}}
        collNode --- RcRes
        RcRes --- gndC[("GND AC")]
        roRes{{"r_o Output Resistance"}}
        collNode --- roRes
        roRes --- emitNode
        RLLoad{{"R_L Load Resistor"}}
        collNode --- RLLoad
        RLLoad --- gndD[("GND AC")]
        CoutCap["C_out Short Circuit"]

    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_SIDE["OUTPUT"]
        CoutCap --> voutNode["v_out Terminal"]
    end

    classDef depSource fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800,stroke-width:2px;

    class rpiRes,RbRes,RcRes,roRes,RLLoad resistor;
    class gndA,gndB,gndC,gndD ground;
```

## 4.2 Functional Block-Level Architecture of a CE Amplifier Signal Path

The diagram below shows the **signal-flow architecture** of a complete CE amplifier stage, mapping how the input signal is processed through the various blocks — useful for understanding how the small-signal model fits into the larger picture.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sigSrc["Input Signal Source v_in"] --> biasNet["Bias Network R_B to V_CC"]
    biasNet --> cplIn["Coupling Capacitor C_in Blocks DC"]
    cplIn --> bjtBlock["Intrinsic BJT Hybrid pi Model"]
    bjtBlock --> bjtNodes["Three Terminal Small Signal Model"]
    bjtNodes --> rpiBlock["Input Res r_pi Between b and e"]
    bjtNodes --> gmBlock["Controlled Source g_m v_be Collector to Emitter"]
    bjtNodes --> roBlock["Output Res r_o Between c and e"]
    rpiBlock --> vbeVar["v_be Voltage Drop Across r_pi"]
    vbeVar --> gmBlock
    gmBlock --> collSig["Collector AC Signal v_c"]
    roBlock --> collSig
    collSig --> rcLoad["R_C and R_L Parallel Combination"]
    rcLoad --> cplOut["Coupling Capacitor C_out Blocks DC"]
    cplOut --> outNode["Output v_out = A_v times v_in"]
    outNode --> invert["Phase Inversion of 180 degrees"]

    classDef sigClass fill:#e1f5e1,stroke:#1a661a,stroke-width:1px;
    classDef biasClass fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#661a1a,stroke-width:1px;
    classDef modelClass fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef outClass fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px;
    class sigSrc,collSig,outNode sigClass;
    class biasNet,cplIn,cplOut,rcLoad biasClass;
    class bjtBlock,bjtNodes,rpiBlock,gmBlock,roBlock modelClass;
    class invert outClass;
```

## 4.3 Node-by-Node Reading Guide

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the Mermaid diagrams above during revision:**
>
> 1. The **left block** is the input mesh. $v_{in}$ sees $R_B$ in parallel with the transistor's input resistance.
> 2. The **center block** is the intrinsic BJT replaced by $r_\pi$, $r_o$, and the controlled source.
> 3. The **right block** is the output mesh. The dependent source $g_m v_{be}$ pushes current through $R_C \parallel R_L \parallel r_o$ to ground.
> 4. **Solid arrows** carry actual AC currents/voltages; **dotted arrows** indicate control dependencies.
> 5. The **three ground symbols** are all the same physical ground in the mid-frequency equivalent because $V_{CC}$ shorts to ground and $C_E$ shorts the emitter to ground.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

> **Explain the significance of the hybrid-π model in BJT small-signal analysis. List its three parameters.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The hybrid-π model is the standard **two-port small-signal equivalent circuit of a BJT** used for AC analysis at mid and high frequencies. It is preferred over the T-model because it provides a **physically accurate** representation of the transistor's input, output, and transconductance characteristics in a π-shaped topology, making circuit analysis algebraically straightforward.

**Three parameters:**

1. **$r_\pi$** — base–emitter diffusion resistance ($\Omega$). Represents the input resistance looking into the base.
2. **$g_m$** — transconductance (Siemens). Represents the voltage-controlled current source that couples input to output.
3. **$r_o$** — output resistance ($\Omega$). Represents the finite output resistance due to the Early effect.

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1m) + Significance (1m) + Three parameters (1m).

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

> **Derive the relationship $g_m \, r_\pi = \beta$ for a BJT in the forward active region.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

From the Ebers–Moll equation in the active region, the collector current is:

$$
i_C = I_S \, e^{v_{BE}/V_T}
$$

Differentiating with respect to $v_{BE}$:

$$
g_m = \frac{\partial i_C}{\partial v_{BE}} = \frac{I_S \, e^{v_{BE}/V_T}}{V_T} = \frac{I_{CQ}}{V_T}
$$

The base current is $i_B = \dfrac{I_C}{\beta}$, so $I_{BQ} = \dfrac{I_{CQ}}{\beta}$.

The base–emitter diffusion resistance is defined as:

$$
r_\pi = \frac{V_T}{I_{BQ}} = \frac{\beta \, V_T}{I_{CQ}}
$$

Multiplying:

$$
g_m \, r_\pi = \frac{I_{CQ}}{V_T} \cdot \frac{\beta \, V_T}{I_{CQ}} = \beta
$$

Hence $g_m r_\pi = \beta$. $\blacksquare$

> **Mark Distribution:** $g_m$ derivation (1m) + $r_\pi$ derivation (1m) + Final multiplication (1m).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / 14 Marks]`

> **For a Common-Emitter amplifier using the hybrid-π model with bypassed emitter resistor, derive expressions for:**
>
> **(a)** Voltage gain $A_v$ and input resistance $R_{in}$ **(7 marks)**.
>
> **(b)** Output resistance $R_{out}$ and overall current gain $A_i$ with load $R_L$ **(7 marks)**.

---

#### Part (a) — Voltage Gain and Input Resistance (7 marks)

**Step 1: Draw the AC equivalent.** Replace the BJT with the hybrid-π model. Set $V_{CC}$ to ground, replace $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$, $C_E$ with short circuits.

**Step 2: Apply KCL at the collector node.** Let $v_c$ be the collector AC voltage. The current $g_m v_{be}$ leaves the collector node and flows to the emitter; currents $v_c/R_C$, $v_c/r_o$, $v_c/R_L$ also leave the node to ground.

$$
\frac{v_c}{R_C} + \frac{v_c}{r_o} + \frac{v_c}{R_L} = -g_m v_{be}
$$

**Step 3: Solve for $v_c$.** Let $R_L' = R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L = \dfrac{R_C \, r_o \, R_L}{R_C \, r_o + R_C \, R_L + r_o \, R_L}$. Then:

$$
v_c = -g_m \, v_{be} \, R_L'
$$

Since $v_{in} = v_{be}$ (emitter is grounded through bypass capacitor) and $v_{out} = v_c$:

$$
\boxed{A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m \, (R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L)}
$$

**Step 4: Derive input resistance.** Apply KCL at the base node. Current entering from $v_{in}$ splits into $R_B$ branch and $r_\pi$ branch:

$$
i_{in} = \frac{v_{in}}{R_B} + \frac{v_{in}}{r_\pi} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_{in} = \frac{v_{in}}{i_{in}} = R_B \parallel r_\pi
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{in} = R_B \parallel r_\pi}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Output Resistance and Current Gain (7 marks)

**Step 5: Derive output resistance.** Set $v_{in} = 0 \Rightarrow v_{be} = 0 \Rightarrow$ dependent source becomes open circuit. Apply a test voltage $v_t$ at the output node and measure $i_t$. The resistance seen is $R_C$ in parallel with $r_o$:

$$
\boxed{R_{out} = R_C \parallel r_o}
$$

**Step 6: Derive overall current gain.** The current through $R_L$ is $i_L = -\dfrac{v_{out}}{R_L} = -\dfrac{A_v \, v_{in}}{R_L}$. The input current $i_{in} = \dfrac{v_{in}}{R_B \parallel r_\pi}$.

$$
A_i = \frac{i_L}{i_{in}} = \frac{-A_v \, v_{in} / R_L}{v_{in} / (R_B \parallel r_\pi)} = -A_v \cdot \frac{R_B \parallel r_\pi}{R_L}
$$

Substituting $A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L)$ and using $g_m r_\pi = \beta$:

$$
A_i = g_m (R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L) \cdot \frac{R_B \parallel r_\pi}{R_L}
$$

For $r_o \to \infty$, $R_C \parallel R_L = \dfrac{R_C R_L}{R_C + R_L}$, and if $R_B \gg r_\pi$:

$$
\boxed{A_i \approx -\beta \cdot \frac{R_C}{R_C + R_L}}
$$

The negative sign indicates the **180° phase inversion** between input current and output current.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / 14 Marks]`

> **(a)** Compare the performance of **CE, CB, and CC amplifier configurations** using the hybrid-π model, with emphasis on voltage gain, input resistance, and output resistance. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **voltage gain of a CE amplifier with unbypassed emitter resistor** $R_E$ using the hybrid-π model. Show that the gain is stabilized against $\beta$ variations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Comparative Analysis of CE, CB, CC (7 marks)

**Common-Emitter (CE):** Input at base, output at collector, emitter grounded.

$$
A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L),\quad R_{in} = R_B \parallel r_\pi,\quad R_{out} = R_C \parallel r_o
$$

**Common-Base (CB):** Input at emitter, output at collector, base AC-grounded. The input loop sees a very low resistance because the current source reflects as $\dfrac{v_{be}}{i_e} \approx \dfrac{r_\pi}{\beta+1} \approx \dfrac{1}{g_m}$.

$$
A_v \approx +g_m (R_C \parallel R_L),\quad R_{in} \approx \frac{1}{g_m}\;(\text{low}),\quad R_{out} \approx R_C \;(\text{high})
$$

**Common-Collector (CC) / Emitter Follower:** Input at base, output at emitter, collector AC-grounded. KVL on the base–emitter loop gives $v_{out} = v_{in} - v_{be} \approx v_{in}$:

$$
A_v \approx 1,\quad R_{in} \approx R_B \parallel \beta R_L \;(\text{high}),\quad R_{out} \approx \frac{1}{g_m} \;(\text{low})
$$

> **Summary Table**

| Parameter | CE | CB | CC |
|-----------|----|----|----|
| $A_v$ magnitude | High | High | $\approx 1$ |
| $R_{in}$ | Moderate | Very Low | Very High |
| $R_{out}$ | Moderate | Very High | Very Low |
| Phase | $180°$ | $0°$ | $0°$ |
| Application | Voltage amplifier | HF / low-Z driver | Buffer / impedance match |

---

#### Part (b) — CE with Unbypassed $R_E$ (7 marks)

**Step 1:** In the AC equivalent, $R_E$ appears in the emitter leg. Apply KVL on the base–emitter loop:

$$
v_{in} = i_b r_\pi + i_e R_E = i_b r_\pi + (\beta+1) i_b R_E
$$

Therefore:

$$
i_b = \frac{v_{in}}{r_\pi + (\beta + 1) R_E}
$$

**Step 2:** The base–emitter voltage is:

$$
v_{be} = i_b r_\pi = \frac{v_{in} \, r_\pi}{r_\pi + (\beta + 1) R_E} = \frac{v_{in}}{1 + g_m R_E} \cdot \frac{g_m r_\pi}{1} \cdot \frac{1}{1}
$$

More precisely, using $r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E = r_\pi (1 + g_m R_E)$ since $(\beta+1)R_E \approx \beta R_E$ for large $\beta$:

$$
v_{be} \approx \frac{v_{in}}{1 + g_m R_E}
$$

**Step 3:** Output voltage:

$$
v_{out} = -g_m v_{be} (R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L) = \frac{-g_m (R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L)}{1 + g_m R_E} \cdot v_{in}
$$

$$
\boxed{A_v = \frac{-g_m (R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L)}{1 + g_m R_E}}
$$

**Step 4: Stability against $\beta$ variation.** Since $g_m r_\pi = \beta$, but $g_m$ itself is independent of $\beta$ (it depends only on $I_{CQ}$), the gain expression depends on $g_m$ which is set by the bias current. For large $g_m R_E \gg 1$:

$$
A_v \approx -\frac{R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L}{R_E}
$$

This expression contains **no $\beta$**, demonstrating that the gain is **stabilized against transistor-to-transistor $\beta$ variations** and temperature changes. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the parallel combination correctly.** Many students write $A_v = -g_m R_C$ instead of $A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$. Always apply the **exact** parallel formula when $R_L$ is present.
>
> 2. **Sign errors on current gain.** The current gain for CE is **negative** because of the 180° phase inversion. Writing $A_i = +\beta$ will lose at least 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Confusing $r_\pi$ with $r_e$.** $r_e = \dfrac{V_T}{I_E} \approx \dfrac{1}{g_m}$ is the intrinsic emitter resistance (T-model parameter). $r_\pi = \beta r_e$ is the base-input resistance (hybrid-π parameter). Mixing these up is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Including $r_o$ without justification.** If a problem states "neglect the Early effect", you must explicitly write "$r_o \to \infty$" and drop it. If $r_o$ is included, you must write the full parallel expression.
>
> 5. **Wrong $V_T$ value.** Use $V_T = 26\,\text{mV}$ at room temperature unless otherwise specified. Using $25\,\text{mV}$ or $27\,\text{mV}$ without justification is penalized.
>
> 6. **Mid-band assumption violation.** Do not include $C_\pi$, $C_\mu$, $C_E$, $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$ in the AC equivalent at mid-band — they are all either shorts or opens. Including them with reactances will lose full credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Hybrid-π Mid-Frequency Analysis:**
>
> - **Hybrid-π parameters:** $g_m = I_{CQ}/V_T$, $r_\pi = \beta/g_m = V_T/I_{BQ}$, $r_o = V_A/I_{CQ}$.
> - **Golden identity:** $g_m r_\pi = \beta$.
> - **Thermal voltage:** $V_T = 26\,\text{mV}$ at $300\,\text{K}$.
> - **Mid-band rule:** Coupling and bypass capacitors = short circuits; internal device capacitors = open circuits.
> - **CE (bypassed $R_E$):** $A_v = -g_m(R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L)$, $R_{in} = R_B \parallel r_\pi$, $R_{out} = R_C \parallel r_o$, $A_i = -\beta R_C/(R_C+R_L)$.
> - **CE (unbypassed $R_E$):** $A_v = -g_m(R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L)/(1 + g_m R_E)$, input resistance increases by $\sim (\beta+1)R_E$ — **degeneration improves stability**.
> - **CB:** $A_v \approx +g_m R_C$, $R_{in} \approx 1/g_m$ (very low), $R_{out} \approx R_C$ (very high), non-inverting.
> - **CC (Emitter Follower):** $A_v \approx 1$ (non-inverting), $R_{in}$ very high ($\sim \beta R_L$), $R_{out}$ very low ($\sim 1/g_m$), used as a buffer.
> - **Phase:** CE inverts ($180°$); CB and CC are non-inverting.
> - **Neglect $r_o$** when $r_o \geq 10 R_C$ to simplify — but state the assumption explicitly.
> - **Remember to write units** in numerical problems: $g_m$ in mA/V or S, $r_\pi$ in $\text{k}\Omega$.
> - **Final sanity check:** For a typical CE stage, $|A_v| \approx 50$–$500$; $R_{in} \approx$ few $\text{k}\Omega$; $R_{out} \approx$ few $\text{k}\Omega$. If your numerical answer is wildly off these scales, **re-check your work**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Small Signal Voltage Gain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Small Signal Voltage Gain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Small Signal Voltage Gain ($A_v$):** The ratio of the small-signal output voltage to the small-signal input voltage of an amplifier, evaluated around a fixed DC operating point (Q-point) under conditions where the input signal amplitude is small enough that the transistor's nonlinear V-I characteristics can be linearized using a Taylor series expansion (retaining only the first-order derivative terms).

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$
A_v = \left.\frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}}\right|_{Q\text{-point}} = \left.\frac{\partial V_{OUT}}{\partial V_{IN}}\right|_{Q}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Small signal analysis is performed using the **h-parameter (hybrid) model** for BJTs and the **small-signal equivalent circuit** (hybrid-π or T-model) for BJTs and FETs. The key configurations analyzed are **Common Emitter (CE)**, **Common Base (CB)**, and **Common Collector (CC)** for BJTs.

### 1.2 Conditions for "Small Signal" Operation

The signal is considered "small" when:

1. The instantaneous operating point remains within a linear region of the transistor's transfer characteristics.
2. The input signal variation $v_{be} \le 10 \text{ mV}$ (for BJTs at room temperature, $\vert v_{be}\vert \le V_T \approx 26 \text{ mV}$).
3. Higher-order nonlinear terms (square, cubic) of the Taylor expansion are negligible.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Audio Volume Knob on a Stereo Amplifier:**
>
> Imagine a transistor amplifier as a **water pressure regulator**. The DC bias voltage ($V_{BB}$, $V_{CC}$) sets the steady "baseline water flow" (Q-point). When you speak into a microphone (input signal $v_{in}$), the resulting tiny pressure waves modulate this baseline flow, producing a much larger pressure wave at the speaker (output $v_{out}$).
>
> The **Small Signal Voltage Gain ($A_v$)** is simply the *amplification factor* — how many times bigger the output pressure wave is compared to the input pressure wave. Crucially, this works *only* as long as the input wave is small enough not to slam the regulator valve fully open or fully closed (the **linear region**). If you shout too loudly (large signal), the valve saturates, and the output becomes distorted (clipping).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (BJT) | Typical Value (FET) | Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Thermal Voltage (at 300 K) | $V_T$ | **26 mV** | **26 mV** | V |
| Transconductance (BJT) | $g_m$ | $I_C / V_T$ | — | S (Siemens) |
| Transconductance (FET) | $g_m$ | — | $2 I_{DSS}(1+V_{GS}/V_P) / \vert V_P\vert$ | S |
| Input Resistance (BJT CE) | $h_{ie}$ | **1 kΩ – 5 kΩ** | — | Ω |
| Current Gain (BJT CE) | $h_{fe}$ ($\beta_{ac}$) | **50 – 500** | — | dimensionless |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need a "Small Signal Model"?

A transistor is inherently a **nonlinear device**. The collector current $i_C$ is an exponential function of $v_{BE}$ (Ebers–Moll model):

$$
i_C = I_{S}\left(e^{v_{BE}/V_T} - 1\right) \approx I_{S}\, e^{v_{BE}/V_T}
$$

Direct AC analysis on this nonlinear equation is mathematically intractable. The small-signal approach **linearizes** the device around the Q-point using a first-order Taylor expansion, allowing us to use simple linear circuit analysis (KVL, KCL, Ohm's law).

## 2.2 The Two-Stage Analysis Strategy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Pattern:** The 14-mark problem on small-signal voltage gain ALWAYS requires a **two-stage analysis**:
> 1. **DC Analysis:** Find the Q-point ($I_C$, $I_B$, $I_E$) using the bias circuit. This is needed to calculate small-signal parameters like $g_m$, $r_\pi$, $h_{ie}$, $h_{fe}$.
> 2. **AC Analysis:** Draw the small-signal equivalent circuit, replace the transistor with its linear model, and compute $A_v$ using standard linear circuit techniques.

## 2.3 The h-Parameter Model (Two-Port Network)

The BJT is modeled as a **linear two-port network** with the following h-parameter equations:

$$
v_1 = h_{11}\, i_1 + h_{12}\, v_2
$$

$$
i_2 = h_{21}\, i_1 + h_{22}\, v_2
$$

In CE configuration, the h-parameters are denoted as $h_{ie}, h_{re}, h_{fe}, h_{oe}$, with the following physical meaning:

- $h_{ie} = \left.\dfrac{v_{be}}{i_b}\right|_{v_{ce}=0}$ → **Input resistance** with output shorted (Ω).
- $h_{re} = \left.\dfrac{v_{be}}{v_{ce}}\right|_{i_b=0}$ → **Reverse voltage ratio** (dimensionless, $\ll 1$).
- $h_{fe} = \left.\dfrac{i_c}{i_b}\right|_{v_{ce}=0}$ → **Forward current gain** (small-signal $\beta$).
- $h_{oe} = \left.\dfrac{i_c}{v_{ce}}\right|_{i_b=0}$ → **Output admittance** (S).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains ALL the high-yield formulas you must memorize for KTU ESE on this topic.**

### A. BJT Small-Signal Parameters (CE Configuration)

| Parameter | Formula | Approximation | Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $g_m$ (transconductance) | $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$ | $\approx \dfrac{I_C (\text{mA})}{26}$ | S |
| $r_\pi$ (base-emitter resistance) | $r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta}{g_m} = \dfrac{V_T \beta}{I_C}$ | $r_\pi = h_{ie}$ | Ω |
| $r_e$ (emitter resistance) | $r_e = \dfrac{V_T}{I_E} \approx \dfrac{26\,\text{mV}}{I_E(\text{mA})}$ | $r_e = \dfrac{\alpha}{g_m}$ | Ω |
| $r_o$ (output resistance) | $r_o = \dfrac{1}{h_{oe}} = \dfrac{V_A}{I_C}$ | Often $\to \infty$ | Ω |
| $\beta_{ac}$ | $h_{fe} = g_m\, r_\pi$ | $50$ to $500$ | dimensionless |

### B. Voltage Gain Formulas (CE, CB, CC)

| Configuration | Voltage Gain $A_v$ (Exact) | Approximation (ignoring $h_{re}$, $1/h_{oe}$) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Common Emitter (CE)** | $A_v = \dfrac{-h_{fe} R_C}{h_{ie} + (h_{ie}h_{oe} - h_{re}h_{fe})R_C}$ | $A_v \approx -g_m R_C$ |
| **Common Base (CB)** | $A_v = \dfrac{h_{fb} R_C}{h_{ib} + (h_{ib}h_{ob} - h_{rb}h_{fb})R_C}$ | $A_v \approx g_m R_C$ |
| **Common Collector (CC)** | $A_v = \dfrac{(1+h_{fe})R_E}{h_{ie} + (1+h_{fe})R_E}$ | $A_v \approx \dfrac{g_m R_E}{1 + g_m R_E}$ |

### C. FET Small-Signal Voltage Gain

| Configuration | Voltage Gain $A_v$ |
| :--- | :---: |
| **Common Source (CS) — with $R_D$** | $A_v = -g_m (R_D \vert\vert r_d)$ |
| **Common Source (CS) — with $R_D \vert\vert R_L$** | $A_v = -g_m (R_D \vert\vert R_L \vert\vert r_d)$ |
| **Source Follower (CD)** | $A_v = \dfrac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}$ |
| **Common Gate (CG)** | $A_v = g_m (R_D \vert\vert R_L)$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why is small-signal voltage gain important in industry?**
> - **Audio amplifiers:** $A_v$ determines the loudness. Engineers design CE stages for high $\vert A_v \vert$ (voltage gain) and CC stages (emitter followers) for low output impedance to drive speakers.
> - **RF front-ends:** The CB configuration is used at high frequencies because it has **no Miller effect**, preserving bandwidth.
> - **Operational amplifiers:** The open-loop gain $A_{vOL}$ (typically $10^5$) is the small-signal gain of the differential input stage.
> - **Wireless transceivers:** FET CS stages provide high input impedance, ideal for antenna-coupled amplifiers in radio receivers.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Voltage Gain of CE Amplifier (Using h-Parameters)

**Given:** A CE amplifier with input $v_s$ (source), source resistance $R_S$, bias resistors $R_1, R_2$, collector resistor $R_C$, emitter resistor $R_E$ (bypassed by $C_E$ at signal frequency), and load $R_L$.

**Step 1: Replace the BJT with its h-parameter equivalent model (CE configuration).**

The input port is between Base and Emitter; the output port is between Collector and Emitter. The h-model is:

$$
v_{be} = h_{ie}\, i_b + h_{re}\, v_{ce}
$$

$$
i_c = h_{fe}\, i_b + h_{oe}\, v_{ce}
$$

**Step 2: Identify the small-signal output voltage.**

The collector is connected to $R_C$ (in parallel with $R_L$). The small-signal output voltage is:

$$
v_{out} = -i_c \cdot R_L' \quad \text{where} \quad R_L' = R_C \vert\vert R_L = \dfrac{R_C R_L}{R_C + R_L}
$$

The negative sign arises from the conventional current direction: an increase in $i_c$ increases the voltage drop across $R_C$, *lowering* $v_{out}$ relative to the emitter.

**Step 3: Apply KVL/KCL at the input loop.**

The input loop contains $v_s$, $R_S$, the bias network ($R_1 \vert\vert R_2 = R_B$), and the BJT input port.

KVL around the input loop (with $v_{be}$ across the BJT input):

$$
v_s = i_b\, R_S + v_{be} \quad \text{where the input source voltage } v_{in} = v_{be}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute the h-model equation for $v_{be}$ into the KVL.**

$$
v_{be} = h_{ie}\, i_b + h_{re}\, v_{ce}
$$

Since $v_{ce} = v_{out}$ (with emitter as the common reference):

$$
v_{be} = h_{ie}\, i_b + h_{re}\, v_{out}
$$

**Step 5: Express $i_c$ in terms of $v_{out}$.**

From Step 2:

$$
i_c = -\dfrac{v_{out}}{R_L'}
$$

Substitute into the second h-equation:

$$
-\dfrac{v_{out}}{R_L'} = h_{fe}\, i_b + h_{oe}\, v_{out}
$$

**Step 6: Solve for $i_b$ in terms of $v_{out}$.**

$$
h_{fe}\, i_b = -v_{out}\left(\dfrac{1}{R_L'} + h_{oe}\right) = -v_{out} \left(\dfrac{1 + h_{oe} R_L'}{R_L'}\right)
$$

$$
i_b = \dfrac{-v_{out}\,(1 + h_{oe} R_L')}{h_{fe}\, R_L'}
$$

**Step 7: Substitute $i_b$ into the input equation and solve for $A_v = v_{out}/v_{in}$.**

The input voltage $v_{in} = v_{be}$, so:

$$
v_{in} = h_{ie}\, i_b + h_{re}\, v_{out}
$$

Substitute $i_b$:

$$
v_{in} = h_{ie} \cdot \dfrac{-v_{out}(1 + h_{oe} R_L')}{h_{fe} R_L'} + h_{re}\, v_{out}
$$

**Step 8: Factor out $v_{out}$ and take the ratio.**

$$
v_{in} = v_{out} \left[\dfrac{-h_{ie}(1 + h_{oe} R_L')}{h_{fe} R_L'} + h_{re}\right]
$$

$$
\dfrac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{-h_{ie}(1 + h_{oe} R_L')}{h_{fe} R_L'} + h_{re}}
$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $h_{fe} R_L'$:

$$
A_v = \dfrac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \dfrac{h_{fe} R_L'}{h_{re} h_{fe} R_L' - h_{ie}(1 + h_{oe} R_L')}
$$

**Step 9: Final exact expression.**

$$
\boxed{\;A_v = \dfrac{-h_{fe}\, R_L'}{h_{ie} + (h_{ie} h_{oe} - h_{re} h_{fe})\, R_L'}\;}
$$

**Step 10: Approximation for typical transistor values.**

Since $h_{re} \approx 10^{-4}$ and $1/h_{oe} \approx 40\,\text{k}\Omega$, and $R_L' \le 10\,\text{k}\Omega$:

$$
h_{re} h_{fe} R_L' \ll h_{ie} \quad \text{and} \quad h_{ie} h_{oe} R_L' \ll h_{ie}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;A_v \approx \dfrac{-h_{fe}\, R_L'}{h_{ie}} = -g_m R_L'\;}
$$

The **negative sign** indicates a **180° phase inversion** between input and output, a hallmark of the CE configuration.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Voltage Gain of Emitter Follower (CC) Using r-Model

**Step 1: Draw the small-signal equivalent circuit of the CC amplifier using the T-model.**

In the T-model, the BJT is represented with emitter resistance $r_e$ and a current-controlled current source $\alpha i_e$ at the collector. For CC, the collector is at AC ground, and the output is taken at the emitter.

**Step 2: Apply KVL around the base-emitter loop.**

The input voltage $v_{in} = v_{be}$ is across $r_\pi$ (or equivalently, $v_{be} = i_b r_\pi$). The total voltage from base to ground is:

$$
v_{in} = i_b r_\pi + (i_b + \beta i_b)\, R_E
$$

where $R_E$ is the total emitter resistance (external $R_E$ in parallel with load $R_L$).

**Step 3: Factor $i_b$.**

$$
v_{in} = i_b \left[r_\pi + (1 + \beta) R_E\right]
$$

$$
i_b = \dfrac{v_{in}}{r_\pi + (1+\beta) R_E}
$$

**Step 4: Express the output voltage $v_{out}$.**

The output voltage is the voltage at the emitter:

$$
v_{out} = (1+\beta) i_b \cdot R_E = \dfrac{(1+\beta) R_E \cdot v_{in}}{r_\pi + (1+\beta) R_E}
$$

**Step 5: Compute $A_v$.**

$$
\boxed{\;A_v = \dfrac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \dfrac{(1+\beta) R_E}{r_\pi + (1+\beta) R_E}\;}
$$

**Step 6: Substitute $r_\pi = \beta / g_m$ to obtain the transconductance form.**

$$
A_v = \dfrac{(1+\beta) R_E}{\beta / g_m + (1+\beta) R_E}
$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $g_m$:

$$
A_v = \dfrac{g_m (1+\beta) R_E}{\beta + g_m (1+\beta) R_E}
$$

For $\beta \gg 1$, we have $1+\beta \approx \beta$:

$$
\boxed{\;A_v \approx \dfrac{g_m R_E}{1 + g_m R_E}\;}
$$

**Physical Insight:** For $g_m R_E \gg 1$, $A_v \to 1$. The emitter follower has a voltage gain of **unity (less than 1)**, hence its name "follower." It does NOT provide voltage amplification, but it provides **current gain** and **impedance transformation** (high input impedance, low output impedance).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Voltage Gain of Common Source FET Amplifier

**Step 1: Draw the small-signal equivalent circuit of the CS amplifier.**

The FET is modeled as a **voltage-controlled current source (VCCS)** with transconductance $g_m$. The gate is the input (high impedance, $i_g = 0$), the source is grounded, and the drain is the output.

**Step 2: Apply KCL at the drain node.**

The total current flowing through the parallel combination of $R_D$ and $R_L$ is the drain current $i_d = g_m v_{gs}$:

$$
i_d = g_m\, v_{gs} = \dfrac{-v_{out}}{R_D \vert\vert R_L}
$$

The negative sign is because conventional current flowing from $V_{DD}$ to ground through $R_D$ produces a voltage drop; an increase in $i_d$ lowers $v_{out}$.

**Step 3: Note that $v_{gs} = v_{in}$ (since the source is grounded in CS).**

$$
g_m\, v_{in} = \dfrac{-v_{out}}{R_D \vert\vert R_L}
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $A_v = v_{out}/v_{in}$.**

$$
\boxed{\;A_v = -g_m\, (R_D \vert\vert R_L)\;}
$$

The **negative sign** again indicates a **180° phase inversion**, characteristic of both CE (BJT) and CS (FET) configurations.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of BJT Configurations

| Property | Common Emitter (CE) | Common Base (CB) | Common Collector (CC) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Voltage Gain $A_v$ | High (≈ $-g_m R_C$) | High (≈ $+g_m R_C$) | $\approx 1$ (positive) |
| Current Gain $A_i$ | High ($\beta$) | $< 1$ ($\alpha$) | High ($1+\beta$) |
| Power Gain | **Highest** | Moderate | Moderate |
| Input Impedance | Moderate ($h_{ie}$) | Very Low | Very High |
| Output Impedance | Moderate–High | Very High | Very Low |
| Phase Inversion | **Yes (180°)** | No (0°) | No (0°) |
| Typical Use | General-purpose amp | High-frequency RF | Buffer / Impedance matcher |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Two-Stage Small-Signal Analysis Methodology

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the canonical KTU-recommended workflow for solving a small-signal voltage gain problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Amplifier Circuit Given] --> stageA
    stageA[Stage 1: DC Analysis] --> stepA1[Remove all coupling and bypass capacitors]
    stepA1 --> stepA2[Find Q-point: VBE, IB, IC, IE]
    stepA2 --> stepA3[Calculate small-signal parameters: gm, rpi, hie, hfe]
    stepA3 --> stageB[Stage 2: AC Small-Signal Analysis]
    stageB --> stepB1[Short all DC sources to ground; open coupling capacitors]
    stepB1 --> stepB2[Replace BJT with h-parameter or hybrid-pi model]
    stepB2 --> stepB3[Draw the AC equivalent circuit]
    stepB3 --> stepB4[Apply KVL and KCL to solve for Av = vout slash vin]
    stepB4 --> resultA[Final Result: Av magnitude and phase]
    stepB4 --> stepB5[Optional: Compute Ai, Ri, Ro, Zi, Zo]
    stepB5 --> resultB[Complete Amplifier Characterization]
    resultA --> finishA[End]
    resultB --> finishA
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of the h-Parameter Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inNode[Input Port: Base Emitter] --> rIn[Input Resistance hie]
    rIn --> nodeMix[Mixing Node]
    srcDep[Dependent Voltage Source: hre x vce] --> nodeMix
    nodeMix --> outNode[Output Port: Collector Emitter]
    curSrc[Dependent Current Source: hfe x ib] --> outNode
    yOut[Output Admittance: 1 slash hoe] --> outNode
    srcIn[Input Signal: ib] --> rIn
    outPort[vce across output] --> srcDep
    outPort --> curSrc
    outPort --> yOut
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: CE Amplifier Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sigA[Input Signal Source vs] --> resA[Source Resistance RS]
    resA --> biasA[Bias Network: R1 parallel R2]
    biasA --> bjtA[Transistor Q1 in CE Configuration]
    bjtA --> resB[Collector Resistor RC]
    resB --> outA[Output Node vout]
    bjtA --> resC[Emitter Resistor RE bypassed by CE]
    outA --> capA[Coupling Capacitor CL]
    capA --> loadA[Load Resistance RL]
    bjtA --> bjtIn[Input Port: hie between Base and Emitter]
    bjtA --> bjtOut[Output Port: hoe between Collector and Emitter]
    bjtIn --> depSrcA[Dependent Source: hfe x ib in collector]
    depSrcA --> bjtOut
```

## 4.4 Concept Map: How Small-Signal Voltage Gain is Computed

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qpoint[Q-Point IC] --> gmCalc[Compute gm = IC slash VT]
    qpoint --> rpiCalc[Compute rpi = beta slash gm]
    qpoint --> hieCalc[Compute hie = rpi]
    gmCalc --> ceGain[CE Gain: Av = minus gm RC]
    rpiCalc --> ccGain[CC Gain: Av = gm RE over 1 plus gm RE]
    gmCalc --> csGain[FET CS Gain: Av = minus gm RD]
    hieCalc --> ceHparam[CE Gain via h-param: Av = minus hfe RL over hie]
    ceGain --> finalA[Final Voltage Gain]
    ccGain --> finalA
    csGain --> finalA
    ceHparam --> finalA
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define small-signal voltage gain of a transistor amplifier. List the conditions under which a signal is considered "small" in a BJT amplifier.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Small-signal voltage gain ($A_v$):** It is the ratio of the small-signal output voltage to the small-signal input voltage, evaluated at the quiescent operating point of the transistor.
>
> **Conditions for small-signal operation:**
> 1. The peak amplitude of the input AC signal $v_{be}(t)$ is much less than the thermal voltage $V_T$ (typically $\vert v_{be}\vert \le 10\,\text{mV}$).
> 2. The operating point remains in the **active region** throughout the signal cycle (no cutoff or saturation).
> 3. The nonlinearity of the transistor's $i_C$–$v_{BE}$ characteristic can be approximated by a linear tangent at the Q-point (Taylor series truncated after the first derivative).
> 4. The signal amplitude is small enough that the change in $i_C$ is a linear function of the change in $v_{BE}$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Compare the voltage gain, input impedance, and phase of the output with respect to the input for CE, CB, and CC BJT configurations.**

**Model Answer:**

| Configuration | Voltage Gain $\vert A_v\vert$ | Input Impedance | Phase Shift |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Common Emitter (CE)** | High (≈ $g_m R_C$) | Moderate ($1\text{–}5\,\text{k}\Omega$) | 180° (inverted) |
| **Common Base (CB)** | High (≈ $g_m R_C$) | Very Low ($\approx r_e$) | 0° (in-phase) |
| **Common Collector (CC)** | $\approx 1$ (slightly less) | Very High ($\approx \beta R_E$) | 0° (in-phase) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** For the common-emitter amplifier circuit shown below, derive the expression for the small-signal voltage gain $A_v = v_o/v_i$ using the **h-parameter model** of the BJT. (7 marks)

**Circuit Description (assumed standard):** BJT with $R_S = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_B = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ (Thevenin equivalent of bias network), $R_C = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_L = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, and the emitter is fully bypassed by a capacitor at the signal frequency.

**(b)** Given the following BJT parameters at the Q-point: $I_C = 2\,\text{mA}$, $\beta = 100$, $V_T = 26\,\text{mV}$, and $r_o = \infty$. Compute: (i) $g_m$, (ii) $r_\pi$, (iii) $A_v$ (voltage gain from base to output), and (iv) $A_{vs}$ (overall voltage gain from source $v_s$ to output $v_o$). (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation of Voltage Gain Using h-Parameter Model:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-Step Procedure (as per KTU valuation key):**

**Step 1:** Identify the h-parameter model of the BJT in CE configuration. The model has:
- Input port: $v_{be} = h_{ie} i_b + h_{re} v_{ce}$
- Output port: $i_c = h_{fe} i_b + h_{oe} v_{ce}$

**Step 2:** Draw the small-signal AC equivalent circuit by shorting all DC sources and treating coupling capacitors as short circuits. The emitter is at AC ground (because $C_E$ is a short at signal frequency).

**Step 3:** The input is applied at the base. The input voltage $v_i$ is the voltage from base to emitter. The output $v_o$ is taken across the parallel combination of $R_C$ and $R_L$:

$$
R_L' = R_C \vert\vert R_L = \dfrac{R_C R_L}{R_C + R_L}
$$

**Step 4:** Express the output voltage:

$$
v_o = -i_c\, R_L'
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the h-model output equation:

$$
i_c = h_{fe}\, i_b + h_{oe}\, v_{ce} = h_{fe}\, i_b + h_{oe}\, v_o
$$

**Step 6:** Equating the two expressions for $i_c$:

$$
-\dfrac{v_o}{R_L'} = h_{fe}\, i_b + h_{oe}\, v_o
$$

$$
i_b = \dfrac{-v_o (1 + h_{oe} R_L')}{h_{fe}\, R_L'}
$$

**Step 7:** Apply KVL at the input (from base to emitter):

$$
v_i = h_{ie}\, i_b + h_{re}\, v_{ce} = h_{ie}\, i_b + h_{re}\, v_o
$$

**Step 8:** Substitute $i_b$ from Step 6:

$$
v_i = h_{ie} \cdot \dfrac{-v_o (1 + h_{oe} R_L')}{h_{fe}\, R_L'} + h_{re}\, v_o
$$

**Step 9:** Rearrange to obtain $A_v = v_o/v_i$:

$$
A_v = \dfrac{v_o}{v_i} = \dfrac{-h_{fe}\, R_L'}{h_{ie} + (h_{ie} h_{oe} - h_{re} h_{fe})\, R_L'}
$$

**Step 10:** Under the approximation $h_{re} \approx 0$ and $h_{oe} R_L' \ll 1$:

$$
A_v \approx \dfrac{-h_{fe}\, R_L'}{h_{ie}}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

**Given:** $I_C = 2\,\text{mA}$, $\beta = 100$, $V_T = 26\,\text{mV}$, $r_o = \infty$.

**(i) Compute $g_m$:**

$$
g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{2}{26} \approx 0.0769 \text{ S} = 76.9 \text{ mS}
$$

**(ii) Compute $r_\pi$:**

$$
r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta}{g_m} = \dfrac{100}{0.0769} \approx 1300\,\Omega = 1.3\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

**(iii) Compute $R_L'$:**

$$
R_L' = R_C \vert\vert R_L = \dfrac{4.7 \times 10}{4.7 + 10} = \dfrac{47}{14.7} \approx 3.20\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

**Compute $A_v$ (base-to-output gain):**

$$
A_v = -g_m R_L' = -0.0769 \times 3.20 \times 10^3 \approx -246.2
$$

In decibels:

$$
A_v (\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(246.2) \approx 47.8 \text{ dB}
$$

**(iv) Compute overall source-to-output gain $A_{vs} = v_o/v_s$:**

The voltage divider at the input:

$$
v_i = v_s \cdot \dfrac{r_\pi}{R_S + r_\pi} = v_s \cdot \dfrac{1.3}{1 + 1.3} = v_s \cdot \dfrac{1.3}{2.3} \approx 0.5652\, v_s
$$

$$
A_{vs} = \dfrac{v_o}{v_s} = A_v \cdot \dfrac{r_\pi}{R_S + r_\pi} = -246.2 \times 0.5652 \approx -139.1
$$

**Summary of Results:**

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :---: |
| $g_m$ | $76.9\,\text{mS}$ |
| $r_\pi$ | $1.3\,\text{k}\Omega$ |
| $A_v$ (base-to-output) | $-246.2$ ($47.8\,\text{dB}$) |
| $A_{vs}$ (source-to-output) | $-139.1$ ($42.9\,\text{dB}$) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign of $A_v$:** Many students write $A_v = g_m R_L$ (positive), losing **1 mark**. The CE amplifier **inverts** the signal: $A_v$ is **negative**.
> 2. **Not computing $R_L' = R_C \vert\vert R_L$:** If the student uses only $R_C$, the gain is overestimated by ~3×. **Lose 2 marks.**
> 3. **Ignoring the source resistance $R_S$ in part (iv):** $A_v$ (base-to-output) and $A_{vs}$ (source-to-output) are **different** quantities. Many students confuse them. **Lose 2 marks.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Draw the small-signal **hybrid-π equivalent circuit** of a BJT in CE configuration. Define each component in the model. (7 marks)

**(b)** For a common-emitter amplifier with emitter resistor $R_E$ (unbypassed), derive the expression for the small-signal voltage gain $A_v$ including the effect of $R_E$. Show that introducing $R_E$ reduces the gain but improves linearity. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — The Hybrid-π Model of a BJT:**

**The Circuit Components:**

The hybrid-π model (developed by L. J. Giacoletto) is a small-signal model valid for a wider range of frequencies than the h-parameter model. The components are:

| Component | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Formula |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Base-emitter resistance | $r_\pi$ | Input resistance looking into base | $r_\pi = \beta / g_m$ |
| Output resistance | $r_o$ | Early effect (output limiting) | $r_o = V_A / I_C$ |
| Transconductance source | $g_m v_{be}$ | VCCS modeling dependent current | $g_m = I_C / V_T$ |
| Base-spreading resistance | $r_b$ or $r_x$ | Physical base contact resistance | $\approx 0\text{–}300\,\Omega$ |
| Base-collector capacitance | $C_\mu$ | Reverse-biased junction capacitance | $\approx 2\text{–}10\,\text{pF}$ |
| Base-emitter capacitance | $C_\pi$ | Forward-biased diffusion + junction cap | $\approx 10\text{–}100\,\text{pF}$ |

**Circuit Description:**
- The input port is between Base (B) and Emitter (E), with $r_\pi$ in parallel with $C_\pi$ between B and E (intrinsic), and $r_b$ in series with the base lead.
- A VCCS $g_m v_{be}$ flows from Collector to Emitter (current direction: $i_c$ entering collector).
- $r_o$ is in parallel with the VCCS between C and E.
- $C_\mu$ connects B to C (Miller capacitance — critical for high-frequency analysis).

**The hybrid-π equivalent circuit (text representation):**

$$
\text{B} \xrightarrow{\,r_b\,} \text{B'}
$$

At node B':
- $r_\pi \vert\vert C_\pi$ from B' to E.
- $C_\mu$ from B' to C.
- VCCS $g_m v_{b'e}$ from C to E.
- $r_o$ from C to E.

**Part (b) — Derivation of $A_v$ with Unbypassed $R_E$:**

**Step 1:** In the AC equivalent circuit, the emitter is **not** grounded; the resistor $R_E$ is in the emitter leg.

**Step 2:** Apply KVL around the base-emitter loop:

$$
v_i = i_b r_\pi + (i_b + \beta i_b) R_E = i_b \left[r_\pi + (1+\beta) R_E\right]
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $i_b$:

$$
i_b = \dfrac{v_i}{r_\pi + (1+\beta) R_E}
$$

**Step 4:** Express the output voltage. With $R_L' = R_C \vert\vert R_L$, the output is:

$$
v_o = -i_c R_L' = -\beta\, i_b\, R_L'
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $i_b$:

$$
v_o = \dfrac{-\beta\, R_L' \cdot v_i}{r_\pi + (1+\beta) R_E}
$$

**Step 6:** Final expression for $A_v$:

$$
\boxed{\;A_v = \dfrac{v_o}{v_i} = \dfrac{-\beta R_L'}{r_\pi + (1+\beta) R_E}\;}
$$

**Step 7: Effect of $R_E$ on Gain:**

As $R_E$ increases, the denominator grows faster than the numerator (since $1+\beta \gg 1$). Therefore, $\vert A_v\vert$ **decreases**. For example, with $r_\pi = 1.3\,\text{k}\Omega$, $\beta = 100$, $R_L' = 3.2\,\text{k}\Omega$:

- $R_E = 0$: $A_v = -100 \times 3.2 / 1.3 \approx -246$
- $R_E = 100\,\Omega$: $A_v = -100 \times 3.2 / (1.3 + 10100) \approx -0.0316$

**Step 8: Why Unbypassed $R_E$ Improves Linearity:**

The unbypassed $R_E$ introduces **series-series negative feedback**. Any increase in $i_c$ (e.g., due to temperature or input variation) increases the voltage drop across $R_E$, which **opposes** the base-emitter voltage $v_{be}$, thereby reducing $i_b$ and stabilizing $i_c$. This:

- Reduces gain dependence on $\beta$ (which varies with temperature and between devices).
- Increases input impedance.
- Increases bandwidth.
- Reduces nonlinear distortion.

The gain becomes approximately:

$$
A_v \approx \dfrac{-R_L'}{R_E} \quad \text{(independent of } \beta \text{)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $(1+\beta)$ before $R_E$:** A common mistake is writing $v_i = i_b r_\pi + i_b R_E$, missing the fact that emitter current is $(1+\beta) i_b$, not $i_b$. **Lose 2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing CE and CC topologies in part (b):** The output is taken at the **collector** (not the emitter) for a CE amplifier with unbypassed $R_E$. **Lose 2 marks.**
> 3. **Not explaining the linearity improvement explicitly:** A bare derivation without the physical insight on negative feedback loses the conceptual marks (typically **1 mark** in the CO1/CO2 evaluation).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Small Signal Voltage Gain**

- [x] **Two-stage analysis is mandatory:** DC analysis (find Q-point) → AC analysis (compute $A_v$).
- [x] **The small-signal condition:** $\vert v_{be}\vert \le 10\,\text{mV}$ for BJT; signal is a perturbation around the Q-point.
- [x] **Thermal voltage** $V_T = 26\,\text{mV}$ at 300 K (room temperature).
- [x] **Transconductance** $g_m = I_C / V_T$ — the most important small-signal parameter.
- [x] **Base-emitter resistance** $r_\pi = \beta / g_m$ — same as $h_{ie}$ in CE h-parameters.
- [x] **CE gain formula:** $A_v \approx -g_m R_L'$ — **negative sign** = 180° phase inversion.
- [x] **CB gain formula:** $A_v \approx +g_m R_L'$ — **no inversion**, but very low input impedance.
- [x] **CC gain formula:** $A_v \approx 1$ — unity gain, used as a buffer (emitter follower).
- [x] **FET CS gain:** $A_v = -g_m (R_D \vert\vert R_L)$ — same form as BJT CE.
- [x] **Unbypassed $R_E$** reduces gain to $A_v \approx -R_L'/R_E$ but stabilizes the operating point and improves linearity (negative feedback).
- [x] **Always compute $R_L' = R_C \vert\vert R_L$** (or $R_D \vert\vert R_L$ for FETs) — do NOT use $R_C$ alone if a load is present.
- [x] **$A_{vs} \ne A_v$:** Overall source-to-output gain includes the input voltage divider: $A_{vs} = A_v \cdot r_\pi / (R_S + r_\pi)$.
- [x] **Decibel conversion:** $A_v (\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\vert A_v\vert$.
- [x] **Phase inversion is a feature, not a bug:** The CE/CS amplifier is inherently an inverter. If a non-inverting amplifier is needed, use a CB/CG, CC/CD, or a two-stage CE cascade (which gives an in-phase output since two inversions = no inversion).
- [x] **KTU 2024 emphasis:** The Module 2 questions frequently combine h-parameter model derivation with a numerical computation (DC bias + $g_m$ + $A_v$ + $A_{vs}$), so practice both **derivation** and **numerical problem-solving** types.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Input and Output Impedance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Input and Output Impedance # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Input Impedance ($Z_{in}$) The **Input Impedance** of a two-port network (or amplifier) is defined as the impedance measured at the input terminals (port 1) with the output terminals (port 2) connected to the rated load $Z_L$ and the input source applied. Mathematically, it is the ratio of the input port voltage to the input port current, looking into the network from the source side: $$Z_{in} = \frac{V_{in}}{I_{in}}\bigg|_{V_{out} \text{ loaded}}$$ For purely resistive circuits, it is called **Input Resistance ($R_{in}$)**. The unit is the **Ohm ($\Omega$)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** A high input impedance is always desirable in voltage amplifiers so that the amplifier does not "load down" the preceding stage or the signal source, thereby drawing minimal current and preserving the source signal's amplitude. ## 1.2 Formal Definition of Output Impedance ($Z_{out}$) The **Output Impedance** is defined as the equivalent Thevenin impedance looking back into the output terminals of the network with the input source deactivated (independent voltage sources short-circuited and independent current sources open-circuited) and the load $Z_L$ removed. $$Z_{out} = \frac{V_{oc}}{I_{sc}}\bigg|_{V_s = 0, Z_L \text{ removed}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Concept:** A low output impedance is desirable so that the amplifier can drive low-impedance loads without significant signal attenuation, acting almost as an ideal voltage source. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap and Pipe" Model Imagine a water tank supplying water to your house: - **Input Impedance** is like the **diameter of the inlet pipe** of the tank. If the inlet pipe is very narrow (low $Z_{in}$), the water source (city pump) has to work very hard, and pressure drops. A wide inlet (high $Z_{in}$) means the tank easily accepts water without disturbing the source. - **Output Impedance** is like the **friction inside the outlet pipe** of the tank. If the outlet pipe has high friction (high $Z_{out}$), the water flow at the tap reduces significantly when you open the tap (connect a load). A low-friction pipe (low $Z_{out}$) ensures the water flow at the tap remains nearly constant, no matter how much you open it. In short: **High $Z_{in}$, Low $Z_{out}$** is the design goal of a near-ideal **Voltage Amplifier**. ## 1.4 Role in Cascaded Amplifier Systems In a multi-stage amplifier, the input impedance of stage 2 acts as the load for stage 1, and the output impedance of stage 1 acts as the source for stage 2. This forms the basis of the **"loading effect"** that KTU examiners love to test in numerical problems. > [!TIP] > **Golden Rule of Cascading:** The overall voltage gain is **not** simply the product of individual stage gains. The actual gain is reduced due to loading: $A_{v,\text{actual}} = A_{v1} \cdot \frac{Z_{in2}}{Z_{in2} + Z_{out1}} \cdot A_{v2}$. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Are $Z_{in}$ and $Z_{out}$ So Important? 1. **Maximum Power Transfer:** When the load impedance equals the complex conjugate of the source impedance ($Z_L = Z_s^*$), maximum power is delivered. This is critical in RF and communication circuits. 2. **Loading Effect / Signal Integrity:** Mismatched impedances cause voltage division between stages, reducing gain. 3. **Bandwidth Considerations:** Input and output impedances, combined with shunt/series capacitances (Miller effect), determine the **3-dB cutoff frequencies** of the amplifier. ## 2.2 Standard Two-Port Network Framework For a linear two-port network, the standard hybrid-parameter (h-parameter) and the g-parameter models are widely used. For low-frequency small-signal analysis: | Parameter | Definition | Physical Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $h_{11}$ | $V_1 / I_1 \vert_{V_2=0}$ | Input impedance with output shorted ($h_{ie} \approx r_\pi$) | | $h_{12}$ | $V_1 / V_2 \vert_{I_1=0}$ | Reverse voltage ratio (feedback) | | $h_{21}$ | $I_2 / I_1 \vert_{V_2=0}$ | Forward current gain ($\beta$ or $h_{fe}$) | | $h_{22}$ | $I_2 / V_2 \vert_{I_1=0}$ | Output admittance ($1/r_o$) | ## 2.3 BJT Amplifier Configurations — $Z_{in}$ and $Z_{out}$ Summary For a BJT with small-signal parameters $r_\pi$, $\beta$, $g_m$, $r_o$ and source resistance $R_S$: | Configuration | Input Impedance $Z_{in}$ | Output Impedance $Z_{out}$ | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Common Emitter (CE) | $R_B \parallel r_\pi$ | $R_C \parallel r_o$ | | Common Base (CB) | $R_E \parallel \dfrac{r_\pi}{\beta+1} \approx \dfrac{1}{g_m}$ | $R_C \parallel r_o$ | | Common Collector (CC) | $R_B \parallel \left[r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E'\right]$ | $R_E \parallel \dfrac{r_\pi + R_S'\vert\beta+1}{\beta+1}$ | | Emitter Degenerated CE | $R_B \parallel \left[r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E\right]$ | $\approx R_C$ (when $R_E$ bypassed only) | > [!NOTE] > $R_E' = R_E \parallel R_L$ for AC analysis. $R_S' = R_S \parallel R_B$ for AC analysis. ## 2.4 FET Amplifier Configurations — $Z_{in}$ and $Z_{out}$ Summary | Configuration | Input Impedance $Z_{in}$ | Output Impedance $Z_{out}$ | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Common Source (CS) | $R_G \parallel (R_1 \parallel R_2)$ | $R_D \parallel r_d$ | | Common Drain (CD) | $R_G \parallel (R_1 \parallel R_2)$ | $R_S \parallel \dfrac{1}{g_m} \parallel r_d$ | | Common Gate (CG) | $R_S \parallel \dfrac{1}{g_m}$ | $R_D \parallel r_d$ | ## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $Z_{in} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{I_{in}}$ | Fundamental input impedance definition | | 2 | $Z_{out} = \left.\dfrac{V_{oc}}{I_{sc}}\right\vert_{V_s=0}$ | Fundamental output impedance definition | | 3 | $A_{v,\text{loaded}} = A_{v,\text{no-load}} \cdot \dfrac{Z_{in}}{Z_{in}+Z_S}$ | Loading effect on voltage gain | | 4 | $R_{in}(\text{CE}) = R_B \parallel r_\pi$ | CE configuration | | 5 | $R_{in}(\text{CC}) = R_B \parallel [r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E']$ | Emitter Follower | | 6 | $R_{out}(\text{CC}) = R_E \parallel \dfrac{R_S'\vert\beta+1 + r_\pi}{\beta+1}$ | Approx. emitter follower output | | 7 | $R_{in}(\text{CB}) \approx \dfrac{1}{g_m}$ | Approx. for $r_o \to \infty$ | | 8 | $R_{in}(\text{CS}) = R_G$ | FET CS, assuming $R_G$ gate resistor | | 9 | $R_{out}(\text{CD}) = R_S \parallel \dfrac{1}{g_m}$ | Source follower output | | 10 | $Z_L = Z_S^*$ | Maximum power transfer condition | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Audio Preamplifiers:** Require very high $Z_{in}$ ($\geq 500\text{ k}\Omega$) to avoid loading piezoelectric microphones. - **Operational Amplifiers:** The ideal op-amp model has $Z_{in} = \infty$ and $Z_{out} = 0$. Real op-amps (e.g., LM741, TL081) approach these values. - **Antenna Systems:** Impedance matching ($50\ \Omega$ standard) between transmitter, cable, and antenna is critical for SWR (Standing Wave Ratio). - **Instrumentation:** High $Z_{in}$ voltmeters (FET input) prevent loading of the circuit under test. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of $Z_{in}$ for Common Emitter (CE) Amplifier with Bypass Capacitor **Given Circuit:** BJT in CE configuration with base bias resistors $R_1$, $R_2$, emitter resistor $R_E$ fully bypassed by $C_E$, collector resistor $R_C$, and load $R_L$ at the output through coupling capacitor $C_{c2}$. **Step 1 — Replace the BJT with its small-signal hybrid-$\pi$ model.** The base node connects to $r_\pi$ between base and emitter, and the emitter is grounded (because $C_E$ short-circuits $R_E$ at AC). The collector connects to $R_C \parallel R_L$ at AC, in parallel with $r_o$. **Step 2 — Apply KCL at the input node (base).** The current $I_b$ flows into the base. The total input current $I_{in}$ splits between the bias network and the BJT base: $$I_{in} = I_{R_B} + I_b$$ where $R_B = R_1 \parallel R_2$ is the Thevenin equivalent of the base bias network. **Step 3 — Express input voltage $V_{in}$.** $V_{in}$ is the voltage at the base node. The voltage across $r_\pi$ is $V_\pi = I_b \cdot r_\pi$. Since the emitter is AC-grounded: $$V_{in} = I_b \cdot r_\pi$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into the input impedance formula.** $$Z_{in} = \frac{V_{in}}{I_{in}} = \frac{V_{in}}{I_{R_B} + I_b} = \frac{V_{in}}{\frac{V_{in}}{R_B} + \frac{V_{in}}{r_\pi}}$$ Factoring $V_{in}$ out: $$Z_{in} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{R_B} + \frac{1}{r_\pi}}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{Z_{in} = R_B \parallel r_\pi}$$ This is the standard result. If $R_B \gg r_\pi$ (typical case), then $Z_{in} \approx r_\pi$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of $Z_{out}$ for Common Emitter (CE) Amplifier **Step 1 — Deactivate the independent source.** To find $Z_{out}$, set $V_s = 0$ (short the input voltage source). This means the base is AC-grounded through $R_S' = R_S \parallel R_B$. **Step 2 — Apply a test voltage $V_{test}$ at the output node.** Connect a fictitious test source $V_{test}$ at the output terminals (where $R_L$ was connected). Compute the resulting current $I_{test}$ flowing into the output. **Step 3 — Find the current $I_{test}$.** With the base AC-grounded, $V_\pi = 0$, which means the dependent current source $g_m V_\pi = 0$ (open circuit). The only path for $I_{test}$ is through $R_C$ and $r_o$ in parallel: $$I_{test} = \frac{V_{test}}{R_C} + \frac{V_{test}}{r_o}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the output impedance.** $$Z_{out} = \frac{V_{test}}{I_{test}} = \frac{V_{test}}{\frac{V_{test}}{R_C} + \frac{V_{test}}{r_o}} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{R_C} + \frac{1}{r_o}}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{Z_{out} = R_C \parallel r_o}$$ For an ideal Early-voltage-infinite BJT ($r_o \to \infty$), $Z_{out} = R_C$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of $Z_{out}$ for Emitter Follower (CC) — The Tricky One! **Step 1 — Test source method again, with the input source shorted.** The emitter is no longer at AC ground because the emitter resistor $R_E$ (which carries the output current) is in the circuit. **Step 2 — Apply $V_{test}$ at the emitter node.** The base is AC-grounded through $R_S' = R_S \parallel R_B$. The voltage at the emitter is $V_e = V_{test}$. The voltage across $r_\pi$ is: $$V_\pi = V_b - V_e = 0 - V_{test} = -V_{test}$$ **Step 3 — The dependent current source delivers $g_m V_\pi = -g_m V_{test}$ from collector to emitter.** This current flows OUT of the emitter node (or rather, in a direction that adds to $I_{test}$). **Step 4 — Apply KCL at the emitter node.** The current $I_{test}$ flows into the emitter node and splits into three parallel paths: - Through $R_E$ (current $= V_{test}/R_E$) - Through $r_o$ (current $= V_{test}/r_o$) - Through the dependent source: total emitter current is $(\beta+1)I_b$ We need $I_b$ in terms of $V_{test}$: $$I_b = \frac{V_\pi}{r_\pi} = \frac{-V_{test}}{r_\pi}$$ The current through the BJT from emitter to collector is $(\beta+1)I_b = \dfrac{-(\beta+1)V_{test}}{r_\pi}$. **Step 5 — KCL gives:** $$I_{test} = \frac{V_{test}}{R_E} + \frac{V_{test}}{r_o} - \frac{(\beta+1)V_{test}}{r_\pi}$$ **Step 6 — Solve for $Z_{out}$:** $$Z_{out} = \frac{V_{test}}{I_{test}} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{R_E} + \frac{1}{r_o} + \frac{\beta+1}{r_\pi}}$$ This is the parallel combination of $R_E$, $r_o$, and $\dfrac{r_\pi}{\beta+1} = \dfrac{1}{g_m}$: $$\boxed{Z_{out} = R_E \parallel r_o \parallel \frac{1}{g_m} = R_E \parallel r_o \parallel \frac{r_\pi}{\beta+1}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The term $\dfrac{r_\pi}{\beta+1}$ is the resistance looking into the emitter, often denoted as $r_e$. This is the source of the very low output impedance of emitter followers. --- ## 3.4 Numerical Demonstration: Cascaded CE-CC Stage **Problem:** A CE stage has $R_S = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $\beta = 100$, $r_\pi = 2.5\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_C = 4.7\text{ k}\Omega$. It drives an emitter follower with $R_E = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_B = 100\text{ k}\Omega$. Find the overall loaded voltage gain if the input is $V_s = 10\text{ mV}$ and load $R_L = 1\text{ k}\Omega$. **Step 1 — CE Stage $Z_{in}$ (with biasing network assumed infinite for simplicity):** $$Z_{in,CE} = r_\pi = 2.5\text{ k}\Omega$$ **Step 2 — CE Stage $Z_{out}$:** $$Z_{out,CE} = R_C = 4.7\text{ k}\Omega$$ **Step 3 — CC Stage $Z_{in}$:** $$Z_{in,CC} = R_B \parallel [r_\pi + (\beta+1)(R_E \parallel R_L)]$$ Computing $R_E \parallel R_L = 1\text{k} \parallel 1\text{k} = 500\ \Omega$: $$r_\pi + 101 \times 500 = 2500 + 50500 = 53000\ \Omega = 53\text{ k}\Omega$$ $$Z_{in,CC} = 100\text{k} \parallel 53\text{k} = \frac{100 \times 53}{153} = 34.64\text{ k}\Omega$$ **Step 4 — CC Stage $Z_{out}$:** $$Z_{out,CC} = R_E \parallel \frac{r_\pi + (R_S \parallel R_B)}{\beta+1} = 1000 \parallel \frac{2500 + 1000}{101}$$ $$= 1000 \parallel \frac{3500}{101} = 1000 \parallel 34.65 = 33.5\ \Omega$$ **Step 5 — Overall loaded gain:** Gain of CE stage: $A_{v,CE} = -g_m (R_C \parallel Z_{in,CC}) = -\frac{100}{2500}(4700 \parallel 34640)$ $R_C \parallel Z_{in,CC} = \frac{4700 \times 34640}{39340} = 4138\ \Omega$ $$A_{v,CE} = -0.04 \times 4138 = -165.5$$ Gain of CC stage: $A_{v,CC} = \dfrac{(\beta+1)(R_E \parallel R_L)}{r_\pi + (\beta+1)(R_E \parallel R_L)} = \dfrac{101 \times 500}{53000} = 0.953$ Overall: $A_v = -165.5 \times 0.953 = -157.7$ The output voltage: $V_{out} = A_v \times V_{in}$. With the source loading, $V_{in} = V_s \times \dfrac{Z_{in,CE}}{Z_{in,CE}+R_S} = 10\text{mV} \times \dfrac{2.5}{3.5} = 7.14\text{ mV}$. $$V_{out} = -157.7 \times 7.14\text{ mV} = -1.126\text{ V}$$ > [!TIP] > The emitter follower provides a high $Z_{in}$ to the CE stage (preventing loading) and a low $Z_{out}$ to the load — a classic impedance transformation. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Two-Port Network Block Diagram (Master Reference) ```mermaid graph LR srcA[Input Source Vs]:::src -->|Rs| port1A[Port 1
Input Terminals] port1A --> zinA[Zin Block]:::imp zinA --> ampCoreA[Amplification
Core with Av]:::amp ampCoreA --> zoutA[Zout Block]:::imp zoutA --> port2A[Port 2
Output Terminals] port2A --> loadA[Load ZL]:::ld classDef src fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef imp fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef amp fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef ld fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Test Source Method for Output Impedance ```mermaid graph TD inpB[Input Port
Vs SHORTED]:::gnd --> ampInB[Amplifier
Internal]:::amp ampInB --> nodeB[Output Node]:::node nodeB --> zoutB[Zout = Vtest / Itest]:::imp zoutB --> vtestB[Test Voltage Vtest]:::src vtestB --> itestB[Test Current Itest]:::cur classDef gnd fill:#A9A9A9,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef amp fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef node fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef imp fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef src fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cur fill:#9370DB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF ``` ## 4.3 Cascaded Multi-Stage Architecture (Loading Effect Visualized) ```mermaid graph LR vsC[Source Vs with Rs]:::src --> stage1In[Stage 1 Input]:::node stage1In --> zin1C[Zin1]:::imp zin1C --> amp1C[Amplifier 1
Av1]:::amp amp1C --> zout1C[Zout1]:::imp zout1C --> stage2In[Stage 2 Input]:::node stage2In --> zin2C[Zin2]:::imp zin2C --> amp2C[Amplifier 2
Av2]:::amp amp2C --> zout2C[Zout2]:::imp zout2C --> loadC[Load ZL]:::ld note1C[/"Loading: Zout1 drives Zin2"/]:::note -.-> zin2C classDef src fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef imp fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef amp fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef node fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ld fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef note fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 BJT Configuration Comparison Flow ```mermaid graph TD bjt[BJT Three Configurations]:::root --> ceN[Common Emitter CE]:::ce bjt --> cbN[Common Base CB]:::cb bjt --> ccN[Common Collector CC]:::cc ceN --> ceZin[Zin: Moderate to High
Rb parallel rpi]:::ce ceN --> ceZout[Zout: Moderate
Rc parallel ro]:::ce cbN --> cbZin[Zin: Very Low
approx 1 by gm]:::cb cbN --> cbZout[Zout: Moderate
Rc parallel ro]:::cb ccN --> ccZin[Zin: Very High
Rb parallel rb plus beta Re]:::cc ccN --> ccZout[Zout: Very Low
Re parallel 1 by gm]:::cc classDef root fill:#2F4F4F,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF classDef ce fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cb fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cc fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.5 Miller's Theorem and Input Capacitance Multiplication ```mermaid graph LR inNodeM[Input Node Vi]:::node --> cMulM[CMiller
equal to C times 1 minus Av]:::cap cMulM --> gndM[AC Ground]:::gnd outNodeM[Output Node Vo]:::node --> cOrigM[Corig
Internal Capacitance]:::cap cOrigM --> inNodeM noteM[/"Miller Effect multiplies
C by factor 1 plus Av"/]:::note -.-> cMulM classDef node fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cap fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef gnd fill:#A9A9A9,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef note fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define input impedance and output impedance of an amplifier. Why is a high input impedance and low output impedance desirable for a voltage amplifier?** **Model Answer:** **Input Impedance ($Z_{in}$):** It is the impedance offered by the amplifier at its input terminals to the source signal. It is defined as the ratio of input voltage to input current: $Z_{in} = V_{in}/I_{in}$, with the output loaded. **Output Impedance ($Z_{out}$):** It is the Thevenin equivalent impedance looking back into the output terminals with the input source deactivated and the load removed. **Why High $Z_{in}$:** A high input impedance ensures the amplifier draws negligible current from the source, preventing signal attenuation and source loading.
