Mathematically, the voltage appearing at the input is $V_{in} = V_s \cdot Z_{in}/(Z_{in}+R_S)$. As $Z_{in} \to \infty$, $V_{in} \to V_s$ (no loss). **Why Low $Z_{out}$:** A low output impedance ensures the output voltage remains nearly constant irrespective of load variations, behaving like an ideal voltage source. The loaded gain becomes $A_{v,\text{loaded}} = A_{v,\text{no-load}} \cdot Z_L/(Z_L+Z_{out})$. As $Z_{out} \to 0$, the full no-load gain is delivered to the load. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Compare the input and output impedances of Common Emitter (CE) and Common Collector (CC) configurations.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Common Emitter (CE) | Common Collector (CC) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $Z_{in}$ | Moderate: $R_B \parallel r_\pi$ | Very High: $R_B \parallel [r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E']$ | | $Z_{out}$ | Moderate: $R_C \parallel r_o \approx R_C$ | Very Low: $R_E \parallel \dfrac{r_\pi + R_S'}{\beta+1}$ | **Explanation:** The CC (emitter follower) has high $Z_{in}$ because the emitter resistor $R_E$ is reflected back to the base multiplied by $(\beta+1)$. The CC has low $Z_{out}$ because the source resistance $R_S'$ when reflected to the emitter is divided by $(\beta+1)$. Therefore, the CC acts as an excellent **impedance buffer** between a high-impedance source and a low-impedance load. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Format) ### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the input impedance of a Common Emitter (CE) amplifier with an unbypassed emitter resistor $R_E$. Show that $Z_{in} = R_B \parallel [r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E]$. **(b) [7 Marks]** A Common Source (CS) MOSFET amplifier has $g_m = 2\text{ mA/V}$, $r_d = 50\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_D = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_S = 1\text{ k}\Omega$ (unbypassed), and gate bias resistors $R_1 = 1\text{ M}\Omega$, $R_2 = 500\text{ k}\Omega$. Find the input impedance, output impedance, and voltage gain. --- #### Model Solution for Question A(a): **Given:** CE amplifier with bias resistors $R_1$, $R_2$ (Thevenin equivalent $R_B$), emitter resistor $R_E$ (no bypass capacitor), collector resistor $R_C$, BJT with hybrid-$\pi$ parameters $r_\pi$, $\beta$, $r_o$. **Step 1 — Replace BJT with small-signal model.** The emitter is NOT AC-grounded now, so $R_E$ is in the circuit. **Step 2 — Apply a test voltage $V_{in}$ at the base and find the base current $I_b$.** The voltage across $r_\pi$ is $V_\pi = I_b \cdot r_\pi$. The voltage at the emitter is $V_e = (I_b + I_c) R_E = (I_b + \beta I_b) R_E = (\beta+1) I_b R_E$. **Step 3 — KVL around base-emitter loop:** $$V_{in} = V_\pi + V_e = I_b r_\pi + (\beta+1) I_b R_E = I_b [r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E]$$ **Step 4 — Input impedance looking into the base alone:** $$Z_{b} = \frac{V_{in}}{I_b} = r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E$$ **Step 5 — Include the bias network in parallel:** $$\boxed{Z_{in} = R_B \parallel [r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E]}$$ **Physical Interpretation:** The unbypassed $R_E$ is "reflected" to the base as $(\beta+1)R_E$ due to the current amplification. This greatly increases input impedance but reduces voltage gain. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A(b): **Step 1 — Compute Thevenin resistance of gate bias network:** $$R_G = R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{1\text{M} \times 500\text{k}}{1\text{M}+500\text{k}} = \frac{500}{1.5}\text{ k}\Omega = 333.33\text{ k}\Omega$$ **Step 2 — Input Impedance:** For a MOSFET, the gate draws no DC current. The input impedance is essentially the gate bias network: $$Z_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 = R_G = 333.33\text{ k}\Omega$$ **Step 3 — Output Impedance:** For a CS amplifier, the output looking into the drain is $r_d$. The total output impedance is $R_D$ in parallel with $r_d$: $$Z_{out} = R_D \parallel r_d = \frac{10\text{k} \times 50\text{k}}{10\text{k} + 50\text{k}} = \frac{500}{60}\text{ k}\Omega = 8.33\text{ k}\Omega$$ **Step 4 — Voltage Gain (with unbypassed $R_S$):** For a CS stage with source degeneration $R_S$: $$A_v = -\frac{g_m (R_D \parallel r_d \parallel R_L)}{1 + g_m R_S}$$ Assuming no external load ($R_L \to \infty$): $$A_v = -\frac{2\text{mA/V} \times 8.33\text{ k}\Omega}{1 + 2\text{mA/V} \times 1\text{ k}\Omega} = -\frac{16.66}{1+2} = -\frac{16.66}{3} = -5.55$$ $$\boxed{Z_{in} = 333.33\text{ k}\Omega, \quad Z_{out} = 8.33\text{ k}\Omega, \quad A_v = -5.55}$$ **(7 marks)** --- ### QUESTION B — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the "test source method" for finding the output impedance of an amplifier. Apply this method to find the output impedance of an emitter follower with $R_S = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_B = 100\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_E = 2\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 5\text{ k}\Omega$, $\beta = 100$, $r_\pi = 2.5\text{ k}\Omega$. **(b) [7 Marks]** Two amplifier stages are cascaded. Stage 1 has $A_{v1} = 50$, $Z_{out1} = 5\text{ k}\Omega$. Stage 2 has $Z_{in2} = 20\text{ k}\Omega$, $A_{v2} = 30$. If the source has $R_S = 1\text{ k}\Omega$ and $Z_{in1} = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, find the overall voltage gain $A_v = V_{out}/V_s$ accounting for loading effects. --- #### Model Solution for Question B(a): **Test Source Method Explanation:** To find the output impedance of a network: 1. **Deactivate all independent sources:** Replace independent voltage sources with short circuits and independent current sources with open circuits. 2. **Remove the load** $R_L$ from the output terminals. 3. **Apply a fictitious test voltage source $V_{test}$** at the output terminals. 4. **Measure (or compute) the resulting test current $I_{test}$** flowing into the network from $V_{test}$. 5. **Output impedance is $Z_{out} = V_{test} / I_{test}$.** **Numerical Application — Emitter Follower:** **Step 1 — Deactivate the source:** $V_s$ shorted means base is AC-grounded through $R_S \parallel R_B$. $$R_S' = R_S \parallel R_B = \frac{1\text{k} \times 100\text{k}}{1\text{k}+100\text{k}} = \frac{100}{101} = 0.990\text{ k}\Omega = 990\ \Omega$$ **Step 2 — Apply $V_{test}$ at emitter. Find $V_\pi$:** $$V_\pi = V_b - V_e = 0 - V_{test} = -V_{test}$$ **Step 3 — The current entering the BJT from the emitter terminal is:** $$I_{bjt} = -(\beta+1) I_b = -(\beta+1) \cdot \frac{V_\pi}{r_\pi} = -101 \cdot \frac{-V_{test}}{2.5\text{k}} = \frac{101 V_{test}}{2500} = 40.4 \times 10^{-3} V_{test}$$ This represents the current flowing from the test source INTO the emitter of the BJT (in the model). **Step 4 — KCL at the emitter node:** $$I_{test} = I_{R_E} + I_{bjt} = \frac{V_{test}}{R_E} + \frac{(\beta+1)V_{test}}{r_\pi}$$ $$I_{test} = \frac{V_{test}}{2000} + \frac{101 V_{test}}{2500}$$ $$I_{test} = V_{test} \left(5 \times 10^{-4} + 40.4 \times 10^{-3}\right) = V_{test} \times 0.0409$$ **Step 5 — Output impedance:** $$Z_{out} = \frac{V_{test}}{I_{test}} = \frac{1}{0.0409} = 24.45\ \Omega$$ If we include the effect of $R_E' = R_E \parallel R_L = 2\text{k} \parallel 5\text{k} = 1.428\text{ k}\Omega$ when the load is reconnected, $Z_{out} \approx R_E \parallel \dfrac{r_\pi + R_S'}{\beta+1} = 2000 \parallel \dfrac{2500+990}{101} = 2000 \parallel 34.55 = 34.0\ \Omega$. $$\boxed{Z_{out} \approx 24.45\ \Omega \text{ (without load)}, \approx 34.0\ \Omega \text{ (with load reference)}}$$ This very low output impedance is the hallmark of the emitter follower. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B(b): **Step 1 — Source-to-Stage 1 loading:** The voltage reaching the input of stage 1 is: $$V_{in1} = V_s \cdot \frac{Z_{in1}}{Z_{in1} + R_S} = V_s \cdot \frac{10\text{k}}{10\text{k}+1\text{k}} = \frac{10}{11} V_s = 0.909 V_s$$ **Step 2 — Stage 1 output to Stage 2 input loading:** The voltage at the input of stage 2 is the voltage at the output of stage 1, attenuated by the voltage divider formed by $Z_{out1}$ and $Z_{in2}$: $$V_{in2} = V_{out1} \cdot \frac{Z_{in2}}{Z_{in2} + Z_{out1}} = V_{out1} \cdot \frac{20\text{k}}{20\text{k}+5\text{k}} = \frac{20}{25} V_{out1} = 0.8 V_{out1}$$ **Step 3 — Output of stage 1:** $$V_{out1} = A_{v1} \cdot V_{in1} = 50 \cdot 0.909 V_s = 45.45 V_s$$ **Step 4 — Input and output of stage 2:** $$V_{in2} = 0.8 \times 45.45 V_s = 36.36 V_s$$ $$V_{out} = A_{v2} \cdot V_{in2} = 30 \times 36.36 V_s = 1090.9 V_s$$ **Step 5 — Overall voltage gain:** $$\boxed{A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_s} = 1090.9}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students naively compute $A_v = 50 \times 30 = 1500$ and lose **3 marks**. The correct answer of **1090.9** accounts for the loading effects at both the source-to-input interface and the inter-stage interface. The "no-load" gain of 1500 is a textbook fiction in real cascaded systems. **(7 marks)** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:** - $Z_{in}$ is measured at the input port with the **load connected** at the output; $Z_{out}$ is measured at the output port with the **input source deactivated** and load removed. - **Test source method** is the universal technique: apply $V_{test}$, find $I_{test}$, then $Z_{out} = V_{test}/I_{test}$. - **CE:** $Z_{in} = R_B \parallel r_\pi$; $Z_{out} = R_C \parallel r_o$. (Moderate both.) - **CB:** $Z_{in} \approx 1/g_m$ (very low); $Z_{out} = R_C \parallel r_o$. (Good for high-frequency, low-impedance sources.) - **CC (Emitter Follower):** $Z_{in} = R_B \parallel [r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E']$ (very high); $Z_{out} = R_E \parallel r_\pi/(\beta+1)$ (very low). (Best impedance buffer.) - **CS (FET):** $Z_{in} = R_G$ (extremely high — ideal for high-impedance sources); $Z_{out} = R_D \parallel r_d$. - **CD (Source Follower):** $Z_{in} = R_G$; $Z_{out} = R_S \parallel 1/g_m$ (low). - **Unbypassed $R_E$** in CE reflects to base as $(\beta+1)R_E$, boosting $Z_{in}$ but reducing $A_v$ by factor $(1+g_m R_E)$. - **Loading effect** in cascaded stages: $A_{v,\text{overall}} = A_{v1} \cdot \dfrac{Z_{in2}}{Z_{in2}+Z_{out1}} \cdot A_{v2} \cdot \dfrac{Z_L}{Z_L+Z_{out2}}$. - **Maximum power transfer** condition: $Z_L = Z_S^*$ (complex conjugate). - **Miller Effect** multiplies the feedback capacitance by $(1+A_v)$, drastically reducing high-frequency $Z_{in}$ in inverting amplifiers. - Always **deactivate sources first** (V-source → short, I-source → open) when computing $Z_{out}$. - For KTU numericals, **always show units** (k$\Omega$, mA/V) and **box the final answer** to score full marks. - The **emitter follower** is the single most important configuration in KTU Module 2 — master its derivations thoroughly. - **Remember $r_e = r_\pi/(\beta+1) = 1/g_m \approx 26\text{ mV}/I_E(\text{mA})$** — this approximation is often used in simplified analyses.

topics.title: CS Stage with Current Source Load and Diode Connected Load
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** CS Stage with Current Source Load and Diode Connected Load

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# CS Stage with Current Source Load and Diode Connected Load

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Source (CS) Stage**: A single-transistor MOSFET amplifier configuration in which the **input signal is applied at the gate**, the **output is taken at the drain**, and the **source is connected to ground** (or a constant potential). It is the most fundamental transconductance amplifier building block in CMOS analog design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Load in CS Stage**: Instead of a passive resistor at the drain, two special non-linear/active loads are widely used in integrated circuits:
> 1. **Current Source Load (CSL)** — An ideal (or near-ideal) DC current source that biases the transistor and acts as an AC load of very high impedance.
> 2. **Diode-Connected MOSFET Load (DCL)** — A MOSFET whose gate is shorted to its drain. It behaves like a **small-signal resistance** of value $\dfrac{1}{g_m}$ (ignoring $r_o$).

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **CS stage with Current Source Load** consists of a driving NMOS transistor $M_1$ in common-source configuration whose drain is loaded by a **pMOS current mirror** (or any high-output-impedance current source) providing the bias current $I_{BIAS}$. The small-signal voltage gain is determined by $g_{m1}$ and the **parallel combination of the output resistances** of $M_1$ and the current source.

A **CS stage with Diode-Connected Load** uses a second MOSFET $M_2$ whose **drain and gate are tied together** as the load. Because of this connection, the small-signal resistance looking into the drain of $M_2$ reduces to $\dfrac{1}{g_{m2}} \Vert r_{o2}$, making it function as a **non-linear, bias-dependent resistor**.

### Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of the CS Stage like a Water Faucet System** 🚰
> - The **faucet handle** = Gate of $M_1$ (you control it with the input signal).
> - The **water flow** = Drain current $i_D$.
> - The **drainpipe** = Load at the drain.
> - A **Current Source Load** is like a **constant-pressure pump** at the drain — no matter how much water (current) you try to push through, it resists changes. This makes the output voltage swing very large for small input changes ⇒ **high gain**.
> - A **Diode-Connected Load** is like a **spring-loaded valve** at the drain — it offers a fixed resistance ($\approx 1/g_m$) that depends on how hard the spring is pre-compressed (bias current). Easy to integrate on-chip since it's just another transistor!

### Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Thermal Voltage:** $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{26\ mV}$ at **300 K**.
- **Process Transconductance Parameter:** $k_n' = \mu_n C_{ox}$ (in **A/V²**), typically **50–200 µA/V²**.
- **Intrinsic Gain of a single MOSFET:** $A_{v0} = g_m r_o$ ranges from **10 to 100** in modern processes.
- **Output resistance of an ideal current source:** $r_o \rightarrow \infty$.

### Why These Loads Are Preferred in ICs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In CMOS technologies, fabricating **large-valued resistors** (MΩ range) is impractical because they consume enormous chip area. **Active loads** (current sources and diode-connected devices) provide equivalent high impedance in a tiny silicon footprint, enabling high-gain, low-power amplifiers fully compatible with standard CMOS processes.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Topology of CS Stage with Current Source Load

The circuit consists of:
- **Driver Transistor ($M_1$):** NMOS in saturation, gate receives $v_{in} + V_{GS}$, source at ground.
- **Active Load (Current Source):** Provides a constant DC bias current $I_{BIAS}$ and exhibits a high small-signal output resistance $r_{o,load}$.
- **DC Operating Point:** $I_{D1} = I_{BIAS}$ (set by the current source).
- **AC Behavior:** The ideal current source is an **open circuit** for small-signal analysis.

### Operational Logic (Why it works)

1. The current source forces a **fixed DC bias** $I_{BIAS}$ through $M_1$, automatically setting its $V_{OV1}$ and hence its $g_{m1}$.
2. For small-signal AC, since the current source has (ideally) infinite impedance, **all of $g_{m1} \cdot v_{in}$ current must flow through the parasitic $r_{o1}$ of $M_1$**, producing a large output voltage.
3. The voltage gain is therefore the product of $g_{m1}$ and the **total output resistance** $r_{o1} \Vert r_{o,load}$.
4. Real current sources (like pMOS mirrors) have **finite $r_o$ (Early effect)**, limiting the gain to $g_{m1}(r_{o1} \Vert r_{o,load})$.

## 2.2 Circuit Topology of CS Stage with Diode-Connected Load

- **Driver:** $M_1$ (NMOS) in CS configuration.
- **Load:** $M_2$ (NMOS or pMOS) with **gate tied to drain** ($V_{G2} = V_{D2}$).
- Since $V_{GS2} = V_{DS2}$, $M_2$ is always in **saturation** (for an NMOS load) or equivalently acts as a **two-terminal device** whose small-signal resistance is $\dfrac{1}{g_{m2}} \Vert r_{o2}$.

### Operational Logic (Why it works)

1. The diode-connected $M_2$ sits at the drain of $M_1$, establishing the bias current $I_{D1} = I_{D2}$.
2. For small-signal, applying a test voltage $v_x$ at the drain of $M_2$ and computing the current yields $r_{eq} = \dfrac{1}{g_{m2}} \Vert r_{o2}$.
3. The CS stage gain is then $A_v = -g_{m1} \cdot R_{out} = -g_{m1} \cdot \left(r_{o1} \Vert \dfrac{1}{g_{m2}}\right)$.
4. For typical design where $r_{o1} \gg \dfrac{1}{g_{m2}}$, this simplifies beautifully to $A_v \approx -\dfrac{g_{m1}}{g_{m2}}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | CS Stage with Current Source Load | CS Stage with Diode-Connected Load |
|---|----------------------|------------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| 1 | Bias Current Relation | $I_{D1} = I_{BIAS}$ | $I_{D1} = I_{D2} = I_{BIAS}$ |
| 2 | Small-Signal Load Resistance | $R_L = r_{o,load}$ (ideally $\infty$) | $R_L = \dfrac{1}{g_{m2}} \Vert r_{o2}$ |
| 3 | Output Resistance $R_{out}$ | $r_{o1} \Vert r_{o,load}$ | $r_{o1} \Vert \dfrac{1}{g_{m2}} \Vert r_{o2}$ |
| 4 | Voltage Gain $A_v$ (Exact) | $-g_{m1}\left(r_{o1} \Vert r_{o,load}\right)$ | $-g_{m1}\left(r_{o1} \Vert \dfrac{1}{g_{m2}}\right)$ |
| 5 | Voltage Gain $A_v$ (Approx.) | $-g_{m1} \cdot r_{o1}$ if load is ideal | $-\dfrac{g_{m1}}{g_{m2}}$ if $r_{o1} \gg \dfrac{1}{g_{m2}}$ |
| 6 | Gain in terms of device sizes | Set by $r_o$ (process-limited) | $-\sqrt{\dfrac{(W/L)_1}{(W/L)_2}}$ for matched $\mu_n C_{ox}$ |
| 7 | DC Voltage Headroom | **High** (limited by $V_{DS,sat}$ of $M_1$ and current source compliance) | **Moderate** (only $V_{DS,sat}$ of $M_1$) |
| 8 | Frequency Response | Limited by node capacitances at drain | Slightly better — load is local (small $R_{eq}$) |
| 9 | Transconductance $G_m$ | $g_{m1}$ | $g_{m1}$ |
| 10 | Power Consumption | Set by $I_{BIAS}$ | Set by $I_{BIAS}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The current-source-loaded CS stage is the **gain stage inside op-amps** (e.g., the second stage of a two-stage Miller op-amp). The diode-connected load CS stage is the **standard gain cell in low-gain, high-bandwidth** applications such as RF front-ends and output buffers where deterministic gain set by transistor ratios is desired.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Small-Signal Resistance of a Diode-Connected MOSFET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why we need this first:** Both circuit gains depend on understanding what the diode-connected device "looks like" to small signals.

### Given
A MOSFET $M_2$ with gate connected to drain, body tied to source (or substrate), operating in saturation with small-signal parameters $g_{m2}$ and $r_{o2}$.

### Find
The small-signal resistance $R_{eq}$ looking into the drain.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Apply a test voltage.** Inject a small-signal test voltage $v_x$ at the drain terminal and measure the resulting current $i_x$ flowing into the drain (with source grounded).

**Step 2 — Write KCL at the drain node.** The only currents entering the drain are:
- Test current $i_x$
- Current flowing out through $r_{o2}$: $\dfrac{v_x}{r_{o2}}$
- Current flowing out through the controlled source $g_{m2} v_{gs2}$: where $v_{gs2} = v_x$ (since gate = drain)

**Step 3 — Apply KCL:**

$$i_x = g_{m2} v_x + \frac{v_x}{r_{o2}}$$

**Step 4 — Factor $v_x$:**

$$i_x = v_x \left( g_{m2} + \frac{1}{r_{o2}} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Compute equivalent resistance:**

$$R_{eq} = \frac{v_x}{i_x} = \frac{1}{g_{m2} + \dfrac{1}{r_{o2}}} = \frac{1}{g_{m2}} \Vert r_{o2}$$

**Step 6 — Approximation.** Since $r_{o2}$ is typically $\dfrac{1}{g_{m2}} \cdot A_{v0}$ and $A_{v0} \gg 1$, we have $\dfrac{1}{g_{m2}} \ll r_{o2}$, so:

$$R_{eq} \approx \frac{1}{g_{m2}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Voltage Gain of CS Stage with Current Source Load

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Identify the small-signal model.** Replace $M_1$ with its small-signal equivalent: a voltage-controlled current source $g_{m1} v_{gs1}$ in parallel with $r_{o1}$. Replace the current source load with $r_{o,load}$ (a large resistor).

**Step 2 — Define the input.** $v_{gs1} = v_{in}$ (source is grounded).

**Step 3 — Apply KCL at the output node.** The current $g_{m1} v_{in}$ flows up through $M_1$ and must exit through the parallel combination $r_{o1} \Vert r_{o,load}$:

$$v_{out} = -(g_{m1} v_{in}) \cdot (r_{o1} \Vert r_{o,load})$$

The negative sign arises because a positive $v_{in}$ increases $i_D$, which **drops** the output voltage (since current flows from $V_{DD}$ to the output node).

**Step 4 — Compute the gain:**

$$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_{m1} (r_{o1} \Vert r_{o,load})$$

**Step 5 — Special Case:** If the load is an **ideal** current source ($r_{o,load} \rightarrow \infty$):

$$A_v \approx -g_{m1} r_{o1} = -A_{v0}$$

This is the **maximum intrinsic gain** of the technology.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Voltage Gain of CS Stage with Diode-Connected Load

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Replace $M_2$ with its small-signal resistance $R_{eq} = \dfrac{1}{g_{m2}} \Vert r_{o2}$.**

**Step 2 — The output node sees $r_{o1}$ (from $M_1$) in parallel with $R_{eq}$:**

$$R_{out} = r_{o1} \Vert \left( \frac{1}{g_{m2}} \Vert r_{o2} \right) = r_{o1} \Vert \frac{1}{g_{m2}} \Vert r_{o2}$$

**Step 3 — Write gain expression:**

$$A_v = -g_{m1} \left( r_{o1} \Vert \frac{1}{g_{m2}} \Vert r_{o2} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Apply approximation.** Since $r_{o1}$ and $r_{o2}$ are typically an order of magnitude (or more) larger than $\dfrac{1}{g_{m2}}$:

$$A_v \approx -\frac{g_{m1}}{g_{m2}}$$

**Step 5 — Express gain in terms of transistor dimensions.** Using $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} I_D}$:

$$A_v = -\frac{\sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_1 I_D}}{\sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_2 I_D}}$$

The bias current $I_D$ and $\mu_n C_{ox}$ cancel out:

$$A_v = -\sqrt{\frac{(W/L)_1}{(W/L)_2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** The gain depends **only on the aspect ratios** — it is **insensitive** to bias current, temperature, and process variations (since $\mu_n C_{ox}$ and $I_D$ cancel). This is why the diode-connected CS stage is highly **robust and predictable** in analog design.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix Style)

**Problem:** In a 0.18 µm CMOS process, a CS stage with diode-connected load has $M_1$ with $(W/L)_1 = 50/0.18$ and $M_2$ with $(W/L)_2 = 10/0.18$, biased at $I_D = 100\ \mu A$. Find the voltage gain.

**Step 1 — Compute $g_{m1}$:**

$$g_{m1} = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 I_D}$$

Using $\mu_n C_{ox} = 300\ \mu A / V^2$ for 0.18 µm:

$$g_{m1} = \sqrt{2 \times 300 \times \frac{50}{0.18} \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$g_{m1} = \sqrt{2 \times 300 \times 277.78 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$g_{m1} = \sqrt{16.667 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1291\ A/V$$

**Step 2 — Compute $g_{m2}$:**

$$g_{m2} = \sqrt{2 \times 300 \times \frac{10}{0.18} \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$g_{m2} = \sqrt{2 \times 300 \times 55.56 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$g_{m2} = \sqrt{3.333 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0577\ A/V$$

**Step 3 — Compute gain:**

$$A_v = -\frac{g_{m1}}{g_{m2}} = -\frac{0.1291}{0.0577} = -2.236$$

**Step 4 — Verify using aspect ratio formula:**

$$A_v = -\sqrt{\frac{50/0.18}{10/0.18}} = -\sqrt{5} = -2.236\ \checkmark$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — CS Stage with Current Source Load

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Input Signal Vin"] --> B["M1 NMOS CS Driver"]
    B --> C["Drain Node Vout"]
    C --> D["Output Terminal"]
    E["PMOS Current Source Load IBIAS"] --> C
    F["Ground GND"] --> B
    G["VDD Supply"] --> E
    H["DC Bias VBIAS at Gate"] --> B
```

### Signal Flow Interpretation

1. **Input path:** $V_{in}$ enters the gate of $M_1$ via bias network.
2. **Transconductance action:** $M_1$ converts $V_{in}$ → drain current $i_d = g_{m1} v_{in}$.
3. **Load action:** The pMOS current source (high $r_o$) acts as a stiff load.
4. **Output:** Voltage $V_{out} = -g_{m1} v_{in} (r_{o1} \Vert r_{o,load})$ develops at the drain.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — CS Stage with Diode-Connected Load

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Input Signal Vin"] --> B["M1 NMOS Driver"]
    B --> C["Drain Node Vout"]
    C --> D["Output Terminal"]
    E["M2 Diode Connected NMOS Gate tied to Drain"] --> C
    F["Ground GND"] --> B
    G["Ground GND"] --> E
    H["DC Bias VBIAS at Gate"] --> B
```

### Signal Flow Interpretation

1. The drain of $M_1$ connects to the drain/gate of $M_2$ (diode-connected).
2. $M_2$ presents a small-signal resistance $\dfrac{1}{g_{m2}}$ to the drain node.
3. The output is $V_{out} = -g_{m1} v_{in} \left(r_{o1} \Vert \dfrac{1}{g_{m2}}\right) \approx -\dfrac{g_{m1}}{g_{m2}} v_{in}$.

## 4.3 Comparative Flow — Gain Determination Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Step 1 Identify Driver M1 and Load"] --> B["Step 2 Find DC Bias IBias"]
    B --> C["Step 3 Compute gm of Driver"]
    C --> D["Step 4 Find Load Resistance RLoad"]
    D --> E["Step 5 Compute Rout ro1 parallel RLoad"]
    E --> F["Step 6 Apply Av equals minus gm1 Rout"]
    F --> G["Step 7 Simplify using Approximations"]
```

## 4.4 Frequency Compensation Considerations (Advanced View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Vin Node High Impedance"] --> B["Cgs1 Cgd1 Parasitics"]
    C["Vout Node High Impedance"] --> D["Cgd1 Cdb1 Cdb2 Load Capacitance"]
    B --> E["Pole at Input Node"]
    D --> F["Pole at Output Node Dominant for CSL"]
    E --> G["Bandwidth Limited by Miller Effect"]
    F --> H["Bandwidth Better for DCL smaller RLoad"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key Architectural Insight:** The **current-source-loaded** CS stage has a **high output impedance** ⇒ **lower bandwidth** but **higher DC gain**. The **diode-connected-load** CS stage has a **low output impedance** ($\approx 1/g_m$) ⇒ **wider bandwidth** but **lower DC gain** (set by aspect ratio). This is the classic **gain-bandwidth trade-off** in analog design.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**Define a diode-connected MOSFET. Derive its small-signal equivalent resistance.**

**Model Answer:**

A **diode-connected MOSFET** is a transistor whose **gate is connected to its drain**, forming a two-terminal device that always operates in **saturation** (for an NMOS with source grounded).

**Derivation:** Apply test voltage $v_x$ at the drain. By KCL, the test current $i_x$ splits into the controlled source $g_{m2} v_x$ and the resistance $r_{o2}$ path:

$$i_x = g_{m2} v_x + \frac{v_x}{r_{o2}} = v_x \left( g_{m2} + \frac{1}{r_{o2}} \right)$$

Therefore:

$$R_{eq} = \frac{v_x}{i_x} = \frac{1}{g_{m2}} \Vert r_{o2} \approx \frac{1}{g_{m2}}$$

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**Why is a current source load preferred over a resistive load in a CS amplifier in CMOS technology?**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Area efficiency:** A high-value resistor (MΩ) required for high gain would consume enormous chip area in CMOS, whereas a current source (a single MOSFET) occupies negligible area.
2. **Higher gain:** The output resistance of a current source is very high (ideally infinite), maximizing the voltage gain $A_v = -g_{m1} r_{o1} \Vert r_{o,load}$.
3. **Power efficiency:** A current source consumes only the bias current $I_{BIAS}$, whereas a large resistor at $V_{DD}$ would draw excessive DC current for the same impedance.
4. **Process compatibility:** Active current sources (e.g., pMOS mirrors) are native to CMOS processes.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Sample 2024 Scheme, 14 Marks]`

**(a) With the help of a small-signal equivalent circuit, derive the voltage gain of a CS stage with an ideal current source load. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Draw the small-signal model:** Replace $M_1$ with a VCCS $g_{m1} v_{in}$ in parallel with $r_{o1}$. Replace the current source load with $r_{o,load}$ (large).

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at output node:**

The current $g_{m1} v_{in}$ flowing through $M_1$ must equal the current flowing out through $r_{o1} \Vert r_{o,load}$:

$$g_{m1} v_{in} = -\frac{v_{out}}{r_{o1} \Vert r_{o,load}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for gain:**

$$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_{m1} (r_{o1} \Vert r_{o,load})$$

**Step 4 — For ideal current source ($r_{o,load} \rightarrow \infty$):**

$$A_v \approx -g_{m1} r_{o1} = -A_{v0}$$

**(b) Derive the voltage gain of a CS stage with a diode-connected MOSFET load and show that the gain equals $-\sqrt{(W/L)_1 / (W/L)_2}$ for matched devices. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Diode-connected $M_2$ equivalent resistance:**

$$R_{eq,2} = \frac{1}{g_{m2}} \Vert r_{o2} \approx \frac{1}{g_{m2}}$$

**Step 2 — Output resistance:**

$$R_{out} = r_{o1} \Vert R_{eq,2} \approx r_{o1} \Vert \frac{1}{g_{m2}}$$

**Step 3 — Voltage gain:**

$$A_v = -g_{m1} R_{out} = -g_{m1} \left( r_{o1} \Vert \frac{1}{g_{m2}} \right) \approx -\frac{g_{m1}}{g_{m2}}$$

(assuming $r_{o1} \gg 1/g_{m2}$)

**Step 4 — Substitute $g_m$ formulas:**

$$g_{m1} = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 I_D}, \quad g_{m2} = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_2 I_D}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$A_v = -\frac{\sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_1 I_D}}{\sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_2 I_D}} = -\sqrt{\frac{(W/L)_1}{(W/L)_2}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often forget to **justify the approximation** $r_{o1} \gg 1/g_{m2}$ in their derivation. If you skip this, the examiner may deduct **1–2 marks**. Always state the assumption and mention that it is valid for $A_{v0} = g_{m2} r_{o2} \gg 1$.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Sample 2024 Scheme, 14 Marks]`

**(a) Compare CS amplifier with current source load and diode-connected load in terms of voltage gain, output resistance, bandwidth, and chip area. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | CS with Current Source Load | CS with Diode-Connected Load |
|-----------|------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Voltage Gain | Very high, $A_v = -g_{m1}(r_{o1} \Vert r_{o,load})$ | Moderate, $A_v \approx -g_{m1}/g_{m2} = -\sqrt{(W/L)_1/(W/L)_2}$ |
| Output Resistance $R_{out}$ | High ($r_{o1} \Vert r_{o,load}$, kΩ range) | Low ($r_{o1} \Vert 1/g_{m2} \approx 1/g_{m2}$, few kΩ) |
| Bandwidth | **Lower** (high $R_{out}$ × $C_L$ gives large $\tau$) | **Higher** (low $R_{out}$ × $C_L$ gives small $\tau$) |
| DC Bias Headroom | Large (need $V_{DS,sat}$ for both $M_1$ and current source) | Small (only $V_{DS,sat}$ of $M_1$ needed) |
| Gain Sensitivity to Process/Temperature | **High** (depends on $r_o$, which is process-sensitive) | **Low** (depends only on aspect ratio, which is well-controlled) |
| Chip Area | Moderate (one driver + current mirror) | Smaller (just two transistors, no mirrors) |
| Typical Use | Op-amp gain stages, high-gain amplifiers | RF amplifiers, low-gain stages, buffers |

**(b) Design a CS stage with a diode-connected NMOS load to provide a voltage gain of $-4$ V/V. Assume the bias current is $I_D = 200\ \mu A$, $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100\ \mu A/V^2$, and choose $(W/L)_1 = 30$. Find $(W/L)_2$ and verify the gain. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Use the gain formula:**

$$A_v = -\sqrt{\frac{(W/L)_1}{(W/L)_2}} \implies -4 = -\sqrt{\frac{30}{(W/L)_2}}$$

**Step 2 — Square both sides:**

$$16 = \frac{30}{(W/L)_2}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $(W/L)_2$:**

$$(W/L)_2 = \frac{30}{16} = 1.875$$

**Step 4 — Compute $g_{m1}$ and $g_{m2}$ to verify:**

$$g_{m1} = \sqrt{2 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 30 \times 200 \times 10^{-6}} = \sqrt{1.2 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.095\ mA/V$$

$$g_{m2} = \sqrt{2 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.875 \times 200 \times 10^{-6}} = \sqrt{7.5 \times 10^{-8}} = 0.2739\ mA/V$$

**Step 5 — Verify gain:**

$$A_v = -\frac{g_{m1}}{g_{m2}} = -\frac{1.095}{0.2739} = -3.998 \approx -4\ \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** When using the aspect-ratio gain formula, students frequently **forget to take the negative sign** or **drop the square root**, leading to wrong values. Always re-derive the final answer from $A_v = -g_{m1}/g_{m2}$ to cross-check.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **CS Stage** = input at gate, output at drain, source at ground. **(Recall: Single-transistor transconductance amplifier.)**
- [x] **Current Source Load (CSL):** Provides bias current + high AC impedance. Gain $A_v = -g_{m1}(r_{o1} \Vert r_{o,load})$.
- [x] **Diode-Connected Load (DCL):** Gate shorted to drain. Small-signal resistance $\dfrac{1}{g_{m2}} \Vert r_{o2} \approx \dfrac{1}{g_{m2}}$.
- [x] **Gain with DCL (approximate):** $A_v \approx -\dfrac{g_{m1}}{g_{m2}}$.
- [x] **Gain with DCL (exact, in terms of W/L):** $A_v = -\sqrt{\dfrac{(W/L)_1}{(W/L)_2}}$ for matched $\mu_n C_{ox}$ and equal $I_D$.
- [x] **CSL = High gain, Low bandwidth** (high $R_{out}$).
- [x] **DCL = Lower gain, Higher bandwidth** (low $R_{out}$) — used in RF and broadband circuits.
- [x] **Advantage of DCL:** Gain depends only on **aspect ratios**, making it **process and temperature insensitive**.
- [x] **CSL Headroom:** Needs $V_{DS,sat}$ of $M_1$ + compliance voltage of current source (limits low-voltage operation).
- [x] **DCL Headroom:** Only $V_{DS,sat}$ of $M_1$ required — **better for low-voltage design**.
- [x] **Always state approximation** $r_{o1} \gg 1/g_{m2}$ when simplifying gain — examiners look for this in KTU scripts.
- [x] **Always carry the negative sign** in the gain expression $A_v$ — the CS stage is **inverting**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Memory Trick for KTU Viva:** **"CSL is Tall but Slow; DCL is Short but Fast."** — Current Source Load gives tall (high) gain but slow (low bandwidth) response; Diode-Connected Load gives shorter gain but faster response. This single sentence captures the entire gain-bandwidth trade-off between the two topologies.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multistage BJT Amplifiers: Types of Multistage Amplifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multistage BJT Amplifiers: Types of Multistage Amplifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 2 – Multistage BJT Amplifiers: Types of Multistage Amplifiers

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Multistage Amplifier** is a cascaded arrangement of two or more single-stage transistor amplifier circuits coupled together such that the output of one stage serves as the input to the next stage, with the primary objective of achieving a **higher overall voltage gain**, **improved bandwidth**, **better input/output impedance characteristics**, and **increased power handling capability** than a single-stage amplifier can provide alone.

In the KTU 2024 scheme (PCECT303 – Analog Circuits), the study of multistage amplifiers is restricted to **BJT (Bipolar Junction Transistor)** topologies and classifies them along two independent axes:

1. **Axis-1 – Method of Inter-stage Coupling:** RC (Capacitive) Coupling, Transformer Coupling, and Direct Coupling.
2. **Axis-2 – Configuration of Successive Stages:** Cascading identical/different BJT configurations (CE-CE, CE-CB, CE-CC, CC-CC, CB-CC, CC-CE).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A multistage amplifier is a system of two or more amplifier stages connected in cascade so that the output of one stage drives the input of the next, designed to enhance overall **voltage gain ($A_v$)**, **current gain ($A_i$)**, **power gain ($A_p$)**, and **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** beyond the practical limits of a single BJT stage.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are trying to whisper a secret across a huge noisy stadium to a friend seated **500 metres** away.

- A **single-stage amplifier** is like **one person shouting** at the top of their lungs — the message is loud but distorted, and not everyone far away can clearly understand it.
- A **multistage amplifier** is like a **chain of relayers**: Person A whispers to Person B (Pre-amplifier Stage 1, sensitive to small signals), who then passes the message — slightly amplified — to Person C (Driver Stage 2, medium power), who finally shouts it to the whole stadium (Output Stage 3, high power).

Each person in the chain does **one job really well**: the first catches the weak whisper, the middle boosts it, and the last delivers it with strength. **Multistage amplifiers do exactly the same with electrical signals.**

| Stage Role | BJT Configuration Typically Used | Real-World Analogy Role |
|---|---|---|
| Input Stage (small-signal) | Common-Emitter (CE) or Common-Base (CB) | Person A — the listener near the speaker |
| Intermediate Stage (gain boost) | CE-CE Cascade or CE-CB Cascode | Person B — the middle relayer |
| Output Stage (low-impedance, power) | Common-Collector (CC) / Emitter Follower | Person C — the loudspeaker |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we cascade:** A single BJT stage typically provides a voltage gain of 50–500. Cascading two or three stages multiplies these gains, easily reaching $A_v \ge 10{,}000$, which is necessary in audio pre-amplifiers, RF receivers, and instrumentation systems.

---

## 1.3 Why Cascading Becomes Necessary – Physical & Mathematical Motivation

A single BJT stage suffers from three fundamental limitations:

- **Limited Voltage Gain:** Even under ideal biasing, $A_v$ of a CE stage is bounded by $\approx g_m R_C$ or $V_{CC}/V_T$ (Early-effect dependent), rarely exceeding a few hundred.
- **Impedance Mismatch:** Source and load impedances are rarely matched to a single stage, causing severe **insertion loss** and **loading effects**.
- **Bandwidth-Gain Trade-off (Gain-Bandwidth Product, GBP):** According to the **Miller theorem** and the **high-frequency BJT model**, doubling the gain halves the upper $-3\text{ dB}$ cutoff frequency — a **single-stage amplifier cannot simultaneously have high gain and wide bandwidth**.

Cascading allows us to:
- Multiply the voltage gain of individual stages: $A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2} \cdot A_{v3}\ldots$
- Distribute the gain requirement across stages, **preserving bandwidth** at each stage.
- Tailor input and output impedances independently by choosing appropriate configurations (e.g., CC for high input $Z_{in}$, CC for low output $Z_{out}$).

> [!TIP]
> **Standard KTU Notation (must memorise):** For an $n$-stage cascade,
> $$A_{v,\text{total}} = \prod_{k=1}^{n} A_{vk}, \qquad Z_{in,\text{total}} = Z_{in,1}, \qquad Z_{out,\text{total}} = Z_{out,n}$$

---

## 1.4 The Two Independent Classification Axes – Overview

The KTU 2024 Module-2 syllabus explicitly asks students to master the **types** of multistage BJT amplifiers. These are classified along two orthogonal axes:

### Axis 1 – Classification by **Method of Coupling** (How stages are joined)

| S.No. | Coupling Type | Coupling Element | Frequency Response | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **RC Coupling** (Resistor-Capacitor) | Capacitor $C_c$ | Excellent, wide bandwidth | **Most Important** |
| 2 | **Transformer Coupling** | Transformer | Poor, narrow band | Moderate |
| 3 | **Direct Coupling** | Wire / Resistor | Excellent down to DC | High (op-amp basis) |

### Axis 2 – Classification by **Configuration of Stages** (Which BJT topology follows which)

| S.No. | Cascade Type | KTU Designation | Primary Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CE → CE** | Cascaded CE | High overall voltage gain |
| 2 | **CE → CB** | **Cascode** | High gain + wide bandwidth + high $f_T$ |
| 3 | **CE → CC** | CE–Emitter Follower | High gain + low output impedance |
| 4 | **CC → CC** | **Darlington Pair** | Very high current gain $\beta \approx \beta_1 \beta_2$ |
| 5 | **CC → CE** | Bootstrap input stage | Very high input impedance |
| 6 | **CB → CE** | Stacked-bandwidth stage | Wideband RF front-end |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Cascode (CE-CB)** and **Darlington (CC-CC)** are treated as **named, examinable topologies** in KTU 2024 Module-2. They are guaranteed to appear in 14-mark Part-B questions.

---

## 1.5 Section-1 Recap (What You Must Internalise Before Moving On)

Before reading Sections 2–5, every student must be able to answer:

1. **Why cascade?** → To overcome the gain–bandwidth trade-off and impedance-matching limitations of a single BJT stage.
2. **Two classification axes?** → (a) Coupling method, (b) Configuration sequence.
3. **Three standard coupling types?** → RC, Transformer, Direct.
4. **Two famous named cascades?** → Cascode (CE-CB) and Darlington (CC-CC).
5. **Cascade gain rule?** → Overall gain is the **product** of individual stage gains (in linear scale), and the **sum** of individual stage gains (in dB).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2 – Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification by Method of Coupling – Detailed Operational Analysis

### 2.1.1 RC-Coupled (Resistor-Capacitor Coupled) Multistage Amplifier

This is the **most commonly used and most heavily tested** coupling scheme in KTU examinations.

**Circuit Architecture:**

$$\text{Source} \rightarrow R_S \rightarrow C_{in} \rightarrow \text{CE Stage 1 (}Q_1\text{)} \rightarrow C_c \rightarrow \text{CE Stage 2 (}Q_2\text{)} \rightarrow C_{out} \rightarrow R_L$$

The **coupling capacitor** $C_c$ is the key element. It performs two simultaneous functions:
- **AC Pass:** It passes the amplified AC signal from the collector of $Q_1$ to the base of $Q_2$ with negligible loss.
- **DC Block:** It blocks the DC collector voltage $V_{C1}$ of $Q_1$ from disturbing the DC bias of $Q_2$ at $V_{B2}$. This is essential because the two stages have **different DC operating points** and a direct connection would shift both bias points.

**Why the name "RC"?** The collector load $R_C$ of the first stage forms an **RC high-pass network** with the coupling capacitor $C_c$ along with the input resistance $R_{i2}$ of the second stage. This network sets the **lower cutoff frequency** $f_L$ of the amplifier.

**Operational Logic – Step by Step:**

1. Weak AC input signal $v_{in}$ arrives at base of $Q_1$.
2. $Q_1$ amplifies it; large AC swing appears at collector of $Q_1$.
3. Coupling capacitor $C_c$ strips off the DC component and passes only the AC to base of $Q_2$.
4. $Q_2$ provides a second stage of amplification.
5. Output appears across $R_L$ through $C_{out}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hallmark Property:** The RC-coupled amplifier is the **only** topology that provides **DC isolation between stages**, which is why op-amp internal stages and most commercial audio amplifiers use RC coupling.

**Advantages of RC Coupling:**
- **Excellent frequency response** in the **audio range (20 Hz – 20 kHz)**.
- **Low cost, low weight, and small size** — no bulky transformer.
- **No hum or stray magnetic-field pick-up** (unlike transformer coupling).
- Stage design is **modular**: each stage can be biased and analysed independently.

**Disadvantages of RC Coupling:**
- **Low voltage gain per stage** because of the **Miller effect**, which shunts the input with a large effective capacitance $C_{M1} = C_{\mu}(1 - A_{v1})$.
- **Poor low-frequency response** — the coupling capacitor $C_c$ and emitter-bypass capacitor $C_E$ introduce a high $f_L$.
- **Impedance mismatch** is not directly addressed (no impedance transformation like in transformer coupling).

---

### 2.1.2 Transformer-Coupled Multistage Amplifier

A **transformer** is used in place of the coupling capacitor. The primary winding is connected to the collector of $Q_1$, and the secondary winding is connected to the base of $Q_2$.

**Operational Logic:**
1. AC collector current of $Q_1$ flows through the primary winding of the inter-stage transformer.
2. By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, an AC voltage is induced in the secondary.
3. The turns ratio $n = N_p / N_s$ of the transformer performs **impedance matching**: the load $R_L$ at the secondary is reflected to the primary as $R_L' = n^2 R_L$.

$$\boxed{Z_{\text{reflected}} = n^2 \cdot Z_L}$$

**Why transformer coupling?**

The transformer can step up or step down the AC voltage, providing **maximum power transfer** via **impedance matching** between the high-impedance collector of $Q_1$ and the low-impedance input of $Q_2$ or a speaker load.

**Advantages:**
- **Excellent impedance matching** → maximum power transfer, hence **highest efficiency** (>50%).
- **DC isolation** between stages (DC cannot pass through a transformer).
- **Higher voltage gain** per stage than RC coupling (no Miller-effect degradation from a low collector resistance).

**Disadvantages:**
- **Bulky, heavy, and expensive** — not suitable for IC fabrication.
- **Narrow frequency response** — transformers are band-pass devices (poor low-frequency response due to primary inductance; poor high-frequency response due to leakage inductance and inter-winding capacitance).
- **Hum pick-up** from stray magnetic fields.
- **Phase reversal** and waveform distortion if the core saturates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Transformer coupling is the **most efficient** coupling method but is **obsolete in modern IC design**. It survives only in **tuned RF amplifiers**, **audio output stages driving speakers**, and **high-power transmitters**.

---

### 2.1.3 Direct-Coupled Multistage Amplifier

There is **no coupling element** between stages — the collector of $Q_1$ is connected **directly** to the base of $Q_2$.

**Operational Logic:**
1. The amplified signal (both AC and DC components) at the collector of $Q_1$ is applied directly to the base of $Q_2$.
2. $Q_2$ further amplifies the signal.

**Critical Design Issue – Bias-Point Drift:**
Because there is no DC-blocking capacitor, the **DC operating point (Q-point) of $Q_1$ directly disturbs** the bias of $Q_2$. Small temperature variations cause the $I_C$ of $Q_1$ to drift; this drift is amplified by $Q_2$, causing the operating point of $Q_2$ to wander catastrophically. This phenomenon is called **thermal runaway**.

**Solutions used in KTU syllabus:**
- **Level shifting** using Zener diodes, diode strings, or $V_{BE}$-multiplier circuits.
- **Emitter resistors** in both stages for DC negative feedback.
- **Differential pair** input (the basis of the op-amp) — to be studied in Module 3.

**Advantages:**
- **Excellent low-frequency response — extends down to DC (0 Hz)**. This is essential for **DC amplifiers**, **operational amplifiers**, **regulators**, and **integrators**.
- **No coupling capacitors** — suitable for **monolithic IC fabrication** (you cannot fabricate large capacitors on-chip).
- **Small size, low cost**.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Severe bias-point drift** due to temperature.
- **DC offset** is amplified stage-by-stage; even a tiny $V_{BE}$ mismatch (≈1 mV) becomes problematic after 3–4 stages.
- Requires **careful thermal compensation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module-2 link to Module-3:** The direct-coupled amplifier is the **direct topological ancestor of the op-amp**. The differential amplifier (Module 3) is the elegant solution to the drift problem of the direct-coupled cascade.

---

## 2.2 Classification by Configuration of Stages – Detailed Analysis

### 2.2.1 CE → CE Cascade (Cascaded Common-Emitter Amplifier)

The most straightforward two-stage amplifier. Both transistors are in the **common-emitter (CE)** configuration.

**Why use CE twice?**
- CE provides the **highest voltage gain** of the three basic configurations.
- Cascading two CE stages produces very high overall voltage gain: $A_v = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2}$.

**Small-signal analysis (mid-band):**

$$A_{v1} = -g_{m1} (R_{C1} \parallel R_{i2}), \quad A_{v2} = -g_{m2} (R_{C2} \parallel R_L)$$

$$R_{i2} = r_{\pi 2} = \frac{\beta_2 V_T}{I_{C2}}$$

**Overall mid-band voltage gain:**

$$A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2} = g_{m1} g_{m2} (R_{C1} \parallel r_{\pi 2}) (R_{C2} \parallel R_L)$$

**The Bane of CE-CE: The Miller Effect**

The input capacitance seen at the base of $Q_1$ becomes:

$$C_{in,\text{eff}} = C_{\pi 1} + C_{\mu 1}(1 - A_{v1})$$

Since $A_{v1}$ is large and negative, $C_{in,\text{eff}}$ is dominated by the **Miller-multiplied** $C_{\mu 1}$ term. This drastically reduces the **upper cutoff frequency** $f_H$, giving the CE-CE cascade a **narrow bandwidth** despite its high gain. The **gain-bandwidth product (GBP)** is preserved:

$$f_H \approx \frac{1}{2 \pi R_{eq} C_{in,\text{eff}}}, \quad \text{GBP} = A_v \cdot f_H = \text{constant}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** When solving CE-CE problems, students often forget that $R_{i2}$ of the second stage **loads** the first stage. The first-stage effective load is $R_{C1} \parallel R_{i2}$, **not just** $R_{C1}$.

---

### 2.2.2 CE → CB Cascade (The **Cascode** Amplifier) ⭐

This is the **single most important named topology in KTU Module 2**. It is the workhorse of **RF and wideband amplifiers**.

**Architecture:** A CE stage $Q_1$ is stacked on top of a CB stage $Q_2$. The collector of $Q_1$ is tied to the emitter of $Q_2$. The input is at the base of $Q_1$; the output is at the collector of $Q_2$.

**Why cascode?**

The CB stage $Q_2$ **isolates** the collector of $Q_1$ from the output node. This is the **key insight**:

- In a plain CE, the output is at the collector, and the **Miller effect** is devastating: $C_{M1} = C_{\mu 1}(1 - A_{v1})$.
- In a cascode, the **collector of $Q_1$ is at AC ground** (because the emitter of $Q_2$ is a low-impedance point — a CB stage has $Z_{in,CB} \approx 1/g_{m2}$, which is very small). Hence the voltage gain from base of $Q_1$ to collector of $Q_1$ is approximately **unity** ($A_{v1} \approx -1$).
- Therefore, the Miller multiplication factor $(1 - A_{v1}) \approx 2$, which is **negligible**. $C_{\mu 1}$ is no longer multiplied by a large gain.

**Result:** Cascode provides nearly the **same voltage gain as a CE**, but with a **drastically higher upper cutoff frequency $f_H$**. The **GBP is roughly doubled** compared to a single CE.

**Cascode Voltage Gain (mid-band):**

$$A_{v,\text{cascode}} = A_{v,CE} \cdot A_{v,CB} \approx (-g_{m1} r_{\pi 2}) \cdot (-g_{m2} R_C) \approx g_{m} R_C$$

(assuming matched $g_m$ and $r_{\pi}$ for $Q_1$, $Q_2$).

**Cascode Input/Output Impedances:**

$$Z_{in,\text{cascode}} = R_B \parallel r_{\pi 1} \approx r_{\pi 1}, \quad Z_{out,\text{cascode}} \approx R_C \parallel r_{o2} \parallel [r_{o1}(1 + g_{m2} r_{\pi 2})]$$

The output impedance is very high, making the cascode an excellent **transimpedance** element.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Specific:** Cascode is the **conceptual bridge** to the differential pair (Module 3) and to the **telescopic cascode op-amp** (advanced analog IC design).

---

### 2.2.3 CE → CC Cascade (CE + Emitter Follower)

A CE stage provides the high voltage gain, followed by a **Common-Collector (CC) / Emitter Follower** stage.

**Why add a CC at the end?**
- The CC has voltage gain ≈ 1 (it is a **buffer**, not a gain stage).
- But it has **low output impedance** $Z_{out,CC} \approx (r_{\pi} + R_S) / (\beta + 1) \approx 1/g_m + R_S / \beta$.
- This makes the cascade an **excellent low-output-impedance amplifier**, ideal for driving low-impedance loads (e.g., speakers, transmission lines).

**Composite Properties:**

$$A_{v,\text{total}} \approx A_{v,CE}, \quad Z_{out,\text{total}} \approx Z_{out,CC} \text{ (very low)}$$

---

### 2.2.4 CC → CC Cascade (The **Darlington Pair** / **Super-Alpha Pair**) ⭐

Two transistors $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ connected such that the **emitter of $Q_1$ drives the base of $Q_2$, and both emitters are tied together** (or $Q_2$'s base receives $Q_1$'s emitter current). The collector currents of both transistors are summed into the output.

**Equivalent Transistor:** The Darlington pair behaves as a **single composite transistor** with:

$$\boxed{\beta_{\text{eq}} = \beta_1 \beta_2 + \beta_1 + \beta_2 \approx \beta_1 \beta_2}$$

$$g_{m,\text{eq}} = \frac{\beta_1 \beta_2 I_{B1}}{V_T} = \frac{I_{C,\text{total}}}{V_T}$$

$$r_{\pi,\text{eq}} = \beta_{\text{eq}} / g_{m,\text{eq}}$$

**Key Properties:**
- **Very high current gain** ($\beta_{eq}$ can exceed 10,000).
- **Very high input impedance** ($r_{\pi,eq}$ is huge).
- Voltage gain is ≈ 1 (since it is essentially a CC-CC stack).
- **Power Darlington** (with $Q_2$ a high-power device) is used as the **final stage of audio amplifiers** to drive 4 Ω / 8 Ω speakers.

**The Sziklai (Complementary Feedback) Pair:** A close cousin where the second transistor is PNP. Its equivalent $\beta$ is also $\beta_1 \beta_2$, but with **only one $V_{BE}$ drop** between input and output (versus two in a true Darlington) — a critical advantage in push-pull output stages.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** In Darlington, the **base current of $Q_1$ is $\approx I_{B1}$**, but the **base current of $Q_2$ is $(1 + \beta_1) I_{B1}$**. The composite $\beta$ is **not simply** $\beta_1 + \beta_2$; it is the much larger $\beta_1 \beta_2$.

---

### 2.2.5 CB → CE and CC → CE Stacks

- **CB → CE (Stacked Bandwidth Stage):** Used in **wideband RF front-ends** and **log amplifiers**. The CB input gives a **very low input impedance**, which absorbs the parasitic source capacitance, extending the upper cutoff. The CE provides the voltage gain.
- **CC → CE (Bootstrap Configuration):** A CC stage at the input presents an **extremely high input impedance** ($R_B$ is bootstrapped by a capacitor), making it ideal for **high-impedance sources** such as **electret microphones**, **piezoelectric pickups**, and **electrophysiology electrodes**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 exam-ready. Memorise the table before solving Part-B problems.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Overall voltage gain (cascade) | $A_{v,\text{tot}} = \prod_{k=1}^{n} A_{vk}$ | Dimensionless (linear) |
| 2 | Overall voltage gain (dB) | $A_{v,\text{dB}} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} A_{vk,\text{dB}}$ | dB |
| 3 | Overall current gain | $A_{i,\text{tot}} = \prod A_{ik}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Overall power gain | $A_{p,\text{tot}} = A_{v,\text{tot}} \cdot A_{i,\text{tot}} = \vert A_{v,\text{tot}} \vert^2 \cdot (Z_{in} / Z_L)$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Input impedance of $n$-stage cascade | $Z_{in,\text{tot}} = Z_{in,1}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 6 | Output impedance of $n$-stage cascade | $Z_{out,\text{tot}} = Z_{out,n}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 7 | Lower cutoff (RC coupling) | $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi (R_C + R_{i2}) C_c}$ | Hz |
| 8 | Miller input capacitance | $C_{M} = C_{\mu}(1 - A_v)$ | F |
| 9 | Upper cutoff (single-pole) | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R_{eq} C_{in,\text{eff}}}$ | Hz |
| 10 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | $\text{GBP} = A_v \cdot f_H$ | Hz (constant for a given $f_T$) |
| 11 | CE stage gain (mid-band) | $A_{v,CE} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L \parallel r_o)$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | CB stage gain (mid-band) | $A_{v,CB} = g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$ | Dimensionless |
| 13 | CC stage gain (mid-band) | $A_{v,CC} = \dfrac{g_m R_E}{1 + g_m R_E} \approx 1$ | Dimensionless, $\le 1$ |
| 14 | CC output impedance | $Z_{out,CC} = R_E \parallel \left(\dfrac{r_{\pi} + R_S}{\beta + 1}\right)$ | $\Omega$ |
| 15 | Cascode gain (matched) | $A_{v,\text{cascode}} \approx g_{m1} R_C$ | Dimensionless |
| 16 | Darlington equivalent $\beta$ | $\beta_{eq} = \beta_1 \beta_2 + \beta_1 + \beta_2$ | Dimensionless |
| 17 | Darlington equivalent $g_m$ | $g_{m,eq} = I_{C,total} / V_T$ | S (Siemens) |
| 18 | Reflected impedance (transformer) | $Z' = n^2 Z$ | $\Omega$ |
| 19 | Thermal voltage (Si, 300 K) | $V_T = kT/q \approx 26 \text{ mV}$ | V |
| 20 | Transconductance | $g_m = I_C / V_T$ | S |

> [!TIP]
> **Substitution tip for exams:** Use $V_T = 26 \text{ mV}$ for silicon at room temperature. Always specify "at $T = 300 \text{ K}$" in your answer.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility – Where These Topologies Are Used

| Topology | Industry / Application |
|---|---|
| RC-coupled CE-CE | **Audio pre-amplifiers, hearing aids, public-address systems** |
| Transformer coupling | **Vacuum-tube radios (legacy), RF tuned amplifiers, class-B push-pull audio** |
| Direct coupling | **Op-amp internal stages, DC servo amplifiers, sensor signal conditioning** |
| Cascode (CE-CB) | **TV tuners, RF front-ends of mobile phones, transimpedance amplifiers in fibre-optic receivers** |
| Darlington | **Power regulators (e.g., LM317 pass element), solenoid drivers, relay drivers, audio output stages** |
| Sziklai pair | **High-fidelity audio amplifiers (e.g., complementary output stage of Marantz, Hitachi amplifiers)** |
| Bootstrap (CC-CE) | **Electret microphone pre-amps, oscilloscope input stages, instrumentation pre-amps** |
| CB-CE | **Wideband oscilloscope front-ends, logarithmic amplifiers, video amplifiers** |

# Section 3 – Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Overall Gain of a Two-Stage RC-Coupled CE-CE Cascade

**Problem Setup (KTU standard):**

A two-stage BJT amplifier uses identical CE stages. Each stage has $g_m$, $r_\pi$, $R_C$, $R_L$, and the input of stage 2 ($R_{i2}$) loads the output of stage 1. Find the overall mid-band voltage gain $A_{v,\text{total}}$, input impedance, and output impedance.

**Given / Assumed:**
- Both transistors identical: $g_{m1} = g_{m2} = g_m$, $r_{\pi 1} = r_{\pi 2} = r_\pi$, $\beta_1 = \beta_2 = \beta$.
- Coupling capacitors $C_c$ are short circuits at mid-band.
- Source resistance $R_S$, load $R_L$.

---

**Step 1 – Identify the loading between stages.**

The output of stage 1 is taken at the collector of $Q_1$, which is loaded by $R_{C1}$ in parallel with the input resistance of stage 2.

$$R_{L1,\text{eff}} = R_{C1} \parallel R_{i2}$$

**Reasoning:** From the perspective of the small-signal collector current of $Q_1$, the current splits between $R_{C1}$ (going to AC ground) and the input of $Q_2$ (which draws base current). Both appear in parallel at the collector node.

---

**Step 2 – Input resistance of stage 2.**

For a CE stage with base bias resistors $R_{B1}, R_{B2}$ (or the Thevenin equivalent $R_B$):

$$R_{i2} = R_{B1,2} \parallel r_{\pi 2}$$

If $R_B \gg r_\pi$, then $R_{i2} \approx r_\pi = \beta V_T / I_{C2}$.

---

**Step 3 – Voltage gain of stage 1.**

The CE stage has gain:

$$A_{v1} = \frac{v_{c1}}{v_{b1}} = -g_{m1} \cdot R_{L1,\text{eff}} = -g_m (R_{C1} \parallel R_{i2})$$

**Reasoning:** The small-signal collector current is $g_m v_{be}$. The voltage developed at the collector is $-g_m v_{be} \cdot R_{L1,\text{eff}}$ (the negative sign is the **180° phase inversion** of the CE stage).

---

**Step 4 – Coupling capacitor is short at mid-band.**

$v_{b2} = v_{c1}$. The coupling capacitor $C_c$ passes the AC signal with no loss at mid-band.

---

**Step 5 – Voltage gain of stage 2.**

$$A_{v2} = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{b2}} = -g_{m2} (R_{C2} \parallel R_L) = -g_m (R_{C2} \parallel R_L)$$

**Reasoning:** Stage 2 sees its own load $R_{C2}$ in parallel with the external load $R_L$. Both appear in parallel at the collector of $Q_2$.

---

**Step 6 – Overall voltage gain.**

$$A_{v,\text{total}} = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{b2}} \cdot \frac{v_{b2}}{v_{b1}} \cdot \frac{v_{b1}}{v_{in}}$$

But $\dfrac{v_{b1}}{v_{in}} = \dfrac{Z_{in,1}}{R_S + Z_{in,1}}$ if we want the **source-to-output** gain. For the **amplifier-only** gain (KTU standard), we take $v_{b1} = v_{in}$ (ideal source):

$$A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2} = g_m^2 (R_{C1} \parallel R_{i2})(R_{C2} \parallel R_L)$$

(taking magnitudes — the two minus signs cancel, so no overall phase inversion from input to output)

---

**Step 7 – Overall input and output impedances.**

$$\boxed{Z_{in,\text{tot}} = R_{B1,1} \parallel r_{\pi 1} = R_{i1}}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{out,\text{tot}} = R_{C2} \parallel r_{o2} \approx R_{C2}}$$

**Reasoning:** The input is determined solely by stage 1 (it is the first port the source sees). The output is determined solely by stage 2 (it is the last port the load sees). The intermediate stage interaction is captured in the gain product.

---

**Step 8 – Numerical example (KTU-style).**

Given: $g_m = 50 \text{ mS}$, $R_{C1} = R_{C2} = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_{i2} = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$.

$$R_{C1} \parallel R_{i2} = \frac{4.7 \times 2}{4.7 + 2} = \frac{9.4}{6.7} \approx 1.403 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_{C2} \parallel R_L = \frac{4.7 \times 10}{4.7 + 10} = \frac{47}{14.7} \approx 3.197 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$A_{v1} = -50 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.403 \times 10^{3} = -70.15$$

$$A_{v2} = -50 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.197 \times 10^{3} = -159.86$$

$$A_{v,\text{total}} = (-70.15) \times (-159.86) = +11{,}214$$

In dB: $20 \log_{10}(11{,}214) \approx 81 \text{ dB}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Negative sign convention:** Two CE stages give **two phase inversions**, which **cancel**. The output is **in phase** with the input. This is a frequently-asked KTU conceptual question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cascode Amplifier Voltage Gain (Matched Transistors)

**Given:** Cascode of $Q_1$ (CE) on top of $Q_2$ (CB), both matched with $g_m$, $r_\pi$, $r_o \to \infty$.

---

**Step 1 – Identify the small-signal model.**

The emitter of $Q_2$ is the collector of $Q_1$. The base of $Q_2$ is at AC ground (it is bypassed by $C_B$).

---

**Step 2 – Find the input impedance looking into the emitter of $Q_2$ (CB stage).**

For a CB stage with collector load $R_C$ and base at AC ground:

$$R_{in,CB} = \frac{v_e}{-i_e} \approx \frac{1}{g_m} \quad (\text{if } r_o \to \infty)$$

More precisely:

$$R_{in,CB} = r_e = \frac{V_T}{I_E} = \frac{1}{g_m}$$

**Reasoning:** From the Ebers-Moll model, the small-signal emitter current is $i_e = -g_m v_{be} = g_m v_e$ (since $v_{be} = -v_e$ for a CB stage). Hence $v_e / i_e = 1/g_m$.

---

**Step 3 – Effective load on the collector of $Q_1$.**

The collector of $Q_1$ is loaded by $R_{in,CB}$ in parallel with $r_{o1}$ (which we take as infinite) and the Early-effect resistance. At a first approximation:

$$R_{L1,\text{eff}} = R_{in,CB} \approx 1/g_m$$

---

**Step 4 – Voltage gain from base of $Q_1$ to collector of $Q_1$.**

$$A_{v,CE,Q_1} = -g_{m1} \cdot R_{L1,\text{eff}} = -g_m \cdot \frac{1}{g_m} = -1$$

**Reasoning (this is the key result!):** The CE gain is **unity** because the load is so small (only $1/g_m$).

---

**Step 5 – Voltage gain of the CB stage $Q_2$.**

The CB stage has voltage gain:

$$A_{v,CB} = g_{m2} (R_C \parallel R_L) = g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

**Reasoning:** Current $i_e$ flowing into emitter produces collector current $g_m v_{be} = g_m v_e$ (no inversion in CB). This develops voltage $i_c \cdot R_C$ at the collector, with **non-inverting** polarity.

---

**Step 6 – Overall cascode gain.**

$$A_{v,\text{cascode}} = A_{v,Q_1} \cdot A_{v,Q_2} = (-1) \cdot g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

$$\boxed{A_{v,\text{cascode}} \approx -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)}$$

The overall gain is **non-inverting** if we count the two polarities carefully, but the absolute magnitude is the same as a single CE stage with the same $g_m$ and $R_C$.

---

**Step 7 – Why cascode wins on bandwidth.**

In a single CE, the Miller capacitance is:

$$C_{M} = C_{\mu}(1 - A_v) = C_{\mu}(1 + g_m R_C)$$

For $g_m R_C = 100$, $C_M \approx 101 C_{\mu}$ — **devastating**.

In a cascode, the gain from base of $Q_1$ to its collector is $\approx -1$, so the Miller capacitance on $Q_1$ is:

$$C_{M,\text{cascode}} = C_{\mu 1}(1 - (-1)) = 2 C_{\mu 1}$$

Only **twice** the parasitic $C_\mu$! The cascode **virtually eliminates the Miller effect** on $Q_1$.

**Resulting upper cutoff frequency:**

$$f_{H,\text{cascode}} \approx \frac{g_m}{2 \pi C_{\mu}} \approx f_T$$

The cascode operates up to the **transition frequency $f_T$** of the transistor — a huge bandwidth improvement.

---

**Step 8 – Numerical example.**

Given: $g_m = 50 \text{ mS}$, $R_C = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_\mu = 5 \text{ pF}$.

$$A_{v,\text{cascode}} = -50 \times 10^{-3} \times (4.7 \text{ k} \parallel 10 \text{ k}) = -50 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.197 \text{ k} \approx -159.86$$

Single CE: $A_{v,CE} = -50 \text{ mS} \times 3.197 \text{ k} = -159.86$ (same gain, as expected).

Single CE upper cutoff: $f_{H,CE} \approx \dfrac{1}{2 \pi (R_S \parallel r_\pi) C_{M,CE}}$

With $R_S \parallel r_\pi \approx 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C_{M,CE} \approx 101 \times 5 \text{ pF} = 505 \text{ pF}$:
$$f_{H,CE} \approx \frac{1}{2 \pi \cdot 10^3 \cdot 505 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 315 \text{ kHz}$$

Cascode upper cutoff: $C_{M,\text{cascode}} = 2 \times 5 = 10 \text{ pF}$:
$$f_{H,\text{cascode}} \approx \frac{1}{2 \pi \cdot 10^3 \cdot 10 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 15.9 \text{ MHz}$$

**Conclusion:** Same gain ($\approx 160$), but cascode gives **$\sim 50 \times$ the bandwidth** of a single CE.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Darlington Pair Equivalent $\beta$

**Setup:** $Q_1$ is the **driver** (small-signal), $Q_2$ is the **output** (power). Emitter of $Q_1$ feeds base of $Q_2$.

---

**Step 1 – Write the base-current KCL.**

The emitter current of $Q_1$ is the base current of $Q_2$:

$$I_{B2} = I_{E1} = (1 + \beta_1) I_{B1}$$

---

**Step 2 – Write the total collector current.**

$$I_{C,\text{total}} = I_{C1} + I_{C2} = \beta_1 I_{B1} + \beta_2 I_{B2}$$

Substitute $I_{B2} = (1 + \beta_1) I_{B1}$:

$$I_{C,\text{total}} = \beta_1 I_{B1} + \beta_2 (1 + \beta_1) I_{B1}$$

$$I_{C,\text{total}} = [\beta_1 + \beta_2 (1 + \beta_1)] I_{B1}$$

$$I_{C,\text{total}} = [\beta_1 + \beta_2 + \beta_1 \beta_2] I_{B1}$$

---

**Step 3 – Define the equivalent $\beta$.**

$$\boxed{\beta_{eq} = \beta_1 + \beta_2 + \beta_1 \beta_2 = \beta_1 \beta_2 + \beta_1 + \beta_2}$$

For $\beta_1, \beta_2 \gg 1$:

$$\beta_{eq} \approx \beta_1 \beta_2$$

---

**Step 4 – Transconductance of the composite transistor.**

$$g_{m,eq} = \frac{I_{C,\text{total}}}{V_T} = \frac{\beta_{eq} I_{B1}}{V_T}$$

The effective transconductance is **not** $g_{m1} g_{m2} / (g_{m1} + g_{m2})$; this is a common KTU misconception. The correct $g_{m,eq}$ is governed by the **total quiescent current** of both transistors, divided by $V_T$.

---

**Step 5 – Equivalent $r_\pi$.**

$$r_{\pi,eq} = \frac{\beta_{eq}}{g_{m,eq}} = \frac{V_T}{I_{B1}} \cdot \frac{\beta_{eq}}{\beta_{eq}} = \frac{V_T}{I_{B1}}$$

Compared to a single transistor with $r_\pi = V_T / I_B$, the Darlington has the **same $r_\pi$ for the same base drive current** $I_{B1}$ — but the $\beta$ is **squared**, hence input impedance $r_{\pi,eq} = \beta_{eq} V_T / I_{C,total}$ is dominated by $\beta^2$.

---

**Step 6 – Numerical example.**

Given: $\beta_1 = 100$, $\beta_2 = 100$, $I_{B1} = 10 \text{ μA}$.

$$\beta_{eq} = 100 \cdot 100 + 100 + 100 = 10{,}200$$

$$I_{B2} = 101 \times 10 \text{ μA} = 1.01 \text{ mA}$$

$$I_{C,\text{total}} = 10{,}200 \times 10 \text{ μA} = 102 \text{ mA}$$

A **tiny 10 μA base current** controls **102 mA of collector current** — a current gain of **10,200**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Comparison of Cascade Topologies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is a "must-derive-on-the-spot" favourite in KTU Part-B 14-mark questions.**

| Topology | Voltage Gain | Current Gain | Power Gain | $Z_{in}$ | $Z_{out}$ | Bandwidth | KTU Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CE–CE (RC coupled) | Very High ($\ge 10^4$) | High | Very High | Moderate ($\sim r_\pi$) | Moderate ($\sim R_C$) | **Narrow** (Miller-limited) | Most common in audio |
| Transformer-coupled | High | Moderate | **Highest** (efficient) | Depends on $n$ | Depends on $n$ | **Narrow** (transformer-limited) | Used in tuned RF |
| Direct-coupled | High | High | High | Moderate | Moderate | **Excellent** (extends to DC) | Used in op-amps |
| **Cascode (CE-CB)** | **High** ($g_m R_C$) | Moderate | High | Moderate | **Very High** | **Very Wide** ($\sim f_T$) | **Best gain-bandwidth** |
| CE–CC | High (CE) | High | High | Moderate | **Very Low** | Wide | Low-$Z_{out}$ driver |
| **Darlington (CC-CC)** | $\approx 1$ | **Very High** ($\beta_1\beta_2$) | High | **Very High** | Low | Moderate | **Best current gain** |
| Sziklai (CC-CB) | $\approx 1$ | Very High | High | Very High | Low | Moderate | Single $V_{BE}$ drop |
| Bootstrap (CC-CE) | High | High | High | **Very High** | Moderate | Moderate | High-$Z_{in}$ pre-amp |
| CB–CE | Moderate-High | Moderate | Moderate-High | **Very Low** ($\sim 1/g_m$) | Moderate | **Wide** | RF wideband |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU exam:** **"Darling Cascode Cascade!"**
> - **Darlington** → Highest current gain ($\beta^2$)
> - **Cascode** → Highest bandwidth (no Miller effect)
> - **Cascade (CE-CE)** → Highest voltage gain (gain product)
