# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "loop gain" in the context of the general feedback structure. Why is it considered the most important parameter of a feedback amplifier?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** The **loop gain** is the product $A\beta$, representing the total voltage (or current) gain encountered by a signal that travels once around the closed feedback loop, beginning and ending at the mixing junction.

> **Significance (2 marks):** Loop gain is the *master knob* of the feedback system. It determines:
> (i) the **amount of feedback** (in dB): $20\log_{10}\vert 1 + A\beta \vert$ dB,
> (ii) the **desensitivity factor** $(1+A\beta)$ that governs gain stabilization, bandwidth extension, and distortion reduction, and
> (iii) the **stability margin** of the amplifier. For these reasons, every KTU board question on feedback ultimately reduces to an evaluation of $A\beta$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between positive feedback and negative feedback. Which type is used in amplifiers and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Positive Feedback (1 mark):** The feedback signal is *in phase* with the source, i.e., $X_e = X_s + X_f$. This **increases** the loop gain and is used in **oscillators** to sustain periodic waveforms.

> **Negative Feedback (1 mark):** The feedback signal is *out of phase* (180°) with the source, i.e., $X_e = X_s - X_f$. This **decreases** the loop gain and is used in **amplifiers**.

> **Reason for use in amplifiers (1 mark):** Negative feedback trades gain (reduced by $(1+A\beta)$) for several engineering benefits: gain stability, bandwidth extension, controlled input/output impedances, reduced nonlinear distortion, and reduced internal noise. The net result is a *linear, predictable, and robust* amplifier whose characteristics are dominated by the passive feedback network rather than the active device's tolerances.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with internal choice (Q-A *or* Q-B). Sub-part (a) carries 7 marks, sub-part (b) carries 7 marks. Solutions must show full algebraic steps for full credit.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a)** Starting from the basic block diagram of the general feedback structure, derive the closed-loop gain expression $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1+A\beta}$. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Assumptions (2 marks):**
> 1. The basic amplifier is **unilateral** (signal flows only from input to output).
> 2. The feedback network is **unilateral** (signal flows only from output to input).
> 3. $\beta$ is **frequency-independent** (a real scalar), valid for the band of interest.
> 4. The basic amplifier has **infinite input impedance** and **zero output impedance** (loading effects neglected — a refinement is added in higher modules).

> **Derivation (5 marks):**
> Let $X_s$ be the source signal, $X_f$ the feedback signal, $X_e$ the error signal, and $X_o$ the output signal.
>
> At the summing junction (negative feedback):
> $$X_e = X_s - X_f$$
>
> Through the basic amplifier:
> $$X_o = A \cdot X_e$$
>
> Through the feedback network:
> $$X_f = \beta \cdot X_o$$
>
> Substituting $X_o$ into the feedback equation:
> $$X_f = \beta \cdot A \cdot X_e = A\beta \cdot X_e$$
>
> Substituting back into the mixing equation:
> $$\begin{aligned}
> X_e &= X_s - A\beta \cdot X_e \\
> X_e(1 + A\beta) &= X_s \\
> X_e &= \frac{X_s}{1 + A\beta}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> The output is:
> $$\begin{aligned}
> X_o &= A \cdot X_e = \frac{A \cdot X_s}{1+A\beta} \\
> \frac{X_o}{X_s} &= \frac{A}{1+A\beta}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> Therefore, the **closed-loop gain** is:
> $$\boxed{\,A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}\,}$$

**(b)** An amplifier has an open-loop gain $A = 2000$ and a feedback factor $\beta = 0.02$. Calculate (i) the closed-loop gain $A_f$, (ii) the loop gain $A\beta$, (iii) the amount of feedback in dB, and (iv) the percentage change in $A_f$ if $A$ changes by $\pm 20\%$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **(i) Closed-loop gain (2 marks):**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> A_f &= \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{2000}{1 + 2000 \times 0.02} \\
> A_f &= \frac{2000}{1 + 40} = \frac{2000}{41} \\
> A_f &\approx 48.78
> \end{aligned}$$

> **(ii) Loop gain (1 mark):**
> $$A\beta = 2000 \times 0.02 = 40$$

> **(iii) Amount of feedback in dB (2 marks):**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \text{Amount} &= 20\log_{10}\vert 1 + A\beta \vert \\
> &= 20\log_{10}(41) \\
> &= 20 \times 1.6128 \\
> &\approx 32.26 \text{ dB}
> \end{aligned}$$

> **(iv) Sensitivity to ±20% drift in A (2 marks):**
> If $A$ increases by 20%: $A_{\text{new}} = 2400$
> $$\begin{aligned}
> A_{f,\text{new}} &= \frac{2400}{1 + 2400 \times 0.02} = \frac{2400}{49} \approx 48.98 \\
> \%\text{ change} &= \frac{48.98 - 48.78}{48.78} \times 100\% \approx +0.41\%
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> If $A$ decreases by 20%: $A_{\text{new}} = 1600$
> $$\begin{aligned}
> A_{f,\text{new}} &= \frac{1600}{1 + 1600 \times 0.02} = \frac{1600}{33} \approx 48.48 \\
> \%\text{ change} &= \frac{48.48 - 48.78}{48.78} \times 100\% \approx -0.61\%
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Interpretation:** A 20% drift in the open-loop gain $A$ produces only about a **0.5%** drift in the closed-loop gain $A_f$ — a dramatic stabilization, consistent with the desensitivity factor $1 + A\beta = 41$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**(a)** Explain the four basic feedback topologies that can be derived from the general feedback structure. What are the corresponding signal variables ($X_s, X_f, X_e, X_o$) for each? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Overview (2 marks):** The general feedback structure can be specialized by making two binary choices — *what is sampled* at the output (voltage or current) and *how it is mixed* at the input (series or shunt). This yields four canonical topologies.

> **Topology 1: Voltage-Series Feedback (1.25 marks)**
> - Samples **output voltage** $V_o$ (parallel connection at output — senses voltage).
> - Mixes **in series** at the input (KVL — adds voltages).
> - Variables: $X_s = V_s, X_e = V_e, X_f = V_f, X_o = V_o$.
> - $A = V_o / V_e$ (V/V), $\beta = V_f / V_o$ (V/V), $A_f = V_o / V_s$ (V/V).
> - Effect: Increases $R_{in}$, decreases $R_{out}$ — ideal **voltage amplifier**.

> **Topology 2: Voltage-Shunt Feedback (1.25 marks)**
> - Samples **output voltage** $V_o$.
> - Mixes **in shunt (parallel)** at the input (KCL — adds currents).
> - Variables: $X_s = I_s, X_e = I_e, X_f = I_f, X_o = V_o$.
> - $A = V_o / I_e$ (V/A, transresistance), $\beta = I_f / V_o$ (A/V).
> - Effect: Decreases both $R_{in}$ and $R_{out}$ — ideal **transresistance amplifier**.

> **Topology 3: Current-Series Feedback (1.25 marks)**
> - Samples **output current** $I_o$ (series connection at output — senses current).
> - Mixes **in series** at the input.
> - Variables: $X_s = V_s, X_e = V_e, X_f = V_f, X_o = I_o$.
> - $A = I_o / V_e$ (A/V, transconductance), $\beta = V_f / I_o$ (V/A).
> - Effect: Increases both $R_{in}$ and $R_{out}$ — ideal **transconductance amplifier**.

> **Topology 4: Current-Shunt Feedback (1.25 marks)**
> - Samples **output current** $I_o$.
> - Mixes **in shunt** at the input.
> - Variables: $X_s = I_s, X_e = I_e, X_f = I_f, X_o = I_o$.
> - $A = I_o / I_e$ (A/A), $\beta = I_f / I_o$ (A/A).
> - Effect: Decreases $R_{in}$, increases $R_{out}$ — ideal **current amplifier**.

**(b)** For a negative feedback amplifier with open-loop gain $A = 1000$ and closed-loop gain $A_f = 50$, find the feedback factor $\beta$, the loop gain $A\beta$, the amount of feedback in dB, and the factor by which distortion is reduced. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **(i) Feedback factor β (2 marks):**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> A_f &= \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} \\
> 50 &= \frac{1000}{1 + 1000\beta} \\
> 1 + 1000\beta &= \frac{1000}{50} = 20 \\
> 1000\beta &= 19 \\
> \beta &= 0.019
> \end{aligned}$$

> **(ii) Loop gain (1 mark):**
> $$A\beta = 1000 \times 0.019 = 19$$

> **(iii) Amount of feedback in dB (2 marks):**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \text{Amount} &= 20\log_{10}(1 + A\beta) = 20\log_{10}(20) \\
> &= 20 \times 1.3010 \\
> &\approx 26.02 \text{ dB}
> \end{aligned}$$

> **(iv) Distortion reduction factor (2 marks):**
> Internal distortion (modeled as an equivalent output noise $X_n$) is reduced by the desensitivity factor:
> $$\text{Distortion reduction factor} = 1 + A\beta = 1 + 19 = 20$$
> That is, the nonlinear distortion generated within the basic amplifier is attenuated by a factor of **20** (or equivalently by $20\log_{10}(20) \approx 26$ dB).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Module 3):**
> 1. **Sign error in mixing equation** — Using $X_e = X_s + X_f$ instead of $X_e = X_s - X_f$ yields the wrong closed-loop gain. KTU paper checkers immediately deduct 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing dB formula** — The *gain* in dB uses $20\log_{10}$, but the *amount of feedback* is also $20\log_{10}\vert 1+A\beta \vert$. Students often erroneously use $10\log_{10}$ for the amount of feedback — losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Neglecting the "1" in deep feedback** — When asked for *approximate* $A_f$ under $A\beta \gg 1$, students must explicitly state $A_f \approx 1/\beta$, not $\approx A/(A\beta)$ in the final answer.
> 4. **Unit mismatch in topology identification** — The dimension of $A$ and $\beta$ must multiply to give a dimensionless loop gain. For voltage-shunt feedback, students forget that $A$ is V/A (transresistance) and $\beta$ is A/V — not V/V.
> 5. **Skipping the algebraic step** — KTU board examiners require *all* algebraic transitions to be shown. Writing only the final boxed answer costs 50% of the marks allocated to the derivation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam):**

- ✅ The **General Feedback Structure** consists of **one forward path** (gain $A$) and **one feedback path** (gain $\beta$) joined by a **summing junction**.
- ✅ The **central equation** to memorize is $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1 + A\beta}$ (negative feedback).
- ✅ The **loop gain** $A\beta$ is the single most important parameter — it controls stabilization, bandwidth, distortion, and stability.
- ✅ The **desensitivity factor** $1 + A\beta$ appears in *every* benefit of negative feedback: gain stability, bandwidth extension, distortion reduction, and noise suppression.
- ✅ **Deep negative feedback** ($A\beta \gg 1$) collapses the closed-loop gain to $A_f \approx 1/\beta$, making it independent of the active device.
- ✅ The **gain sensitivity** formula is $\dfrac{dA_f/A_f}{dA/A} = \dfrac{1}{1+A\beta}$.
- ✅ The **four topologies** are Voltage-Series, Voltage-Shunt, Current-Series, Current-Shunt, defined by *(sampling type) × (mixing type)*.
- ✅ **Voltage sampling** lowers $R_{out}$; **current sampling** raises $R_{out}$.
- ✅ **Series mixing** raises $R_{in}$; **shunt mixing** lowers $R_{in}$.
- ✅ The **amount of feedback in dB** is $20\log_{10}\vert 1+A\beta \vert$.
- ✅ **Positive feedback** ($X_e = X_s + X_f$) is used in oscillators — *not* amplifiers.
- ✅ **Assumptions of the ideal model**: unilateral $A$-block, unilateral $\beta$-network, $\beta$ is a real scalar in the band of interest, and loading effects are absorbed into the modified $A$ and $\beta$ blocks (a refinement introduced in the next sub-topic).
- ✅ The **signal-flow graph** equivalent has one forward path ($A$) and one loop ($-A\beta$); Mason's formula recovers $A_f = A/(1+A\beta)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Negative Feedback on Gain, Bandwidth, Noise Reduction and Distortion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effect of Negative Feedback on Gain, Bandwidth, Noise Reduction and Distortion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of analog electronics and amplifier design, **Negative Feedback (NFB)** refers to the technique of returning a fraction of the output signal of an amplifier back to its input in a manner that opposes the original input signal. Mathematically, this opposition is achieved by introducing a **180° phase inversion** between the sampled output and the input summing junction. The general expression for a closed-loop amplifier system with negative feedback is governed by the **desensitivity factor** $(1 + A\beta)$, where $A$ is the open-loop gain and $\beta$ is the feedback factor (typically between 0 and 1).

The **four primary effects of negative feedback** on an amplifier's performance characteristics are:

1. **Gain Reduction & Stabilization** — The closed-loop gain $A_f$ becomes less dependent on the open-loop gain $A$.
2. **Bandwidth Extension** — The upper cutoff frequency $f_{Hf}$ increases proportionally to the desensitivity factor.
3. **Noise Reduction** — Internal noise generated within the amplifier is attenuated by the factor $(1 + A\beta)$.
4. **Distortion Reduction** — Non-linear distortion components generated within the amplifier are similarly attenuated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The canonical relationship is $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1+A\beta}$. Every single effect (gain, bandwidth, noise, distortion) is scaled by the **desensitivity factor** $(1 + A\beta)$. Memorizing this single quantity is the key to solving all derivation-based problems in this module.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Steering Wheel of an Amplifier"

Imagine you are driving a car with a very powerful engine but a **loose, imprecise steering wheel** (this is your open-loop amplifier with high but unpredictable gain $A$). Every tiny twitch of the steering wheel causes a wild, exaggerated turn. Now, imagine the steering is made **heavier and self-correcting** — the car now resists sharp turns and moves in a more controlled, predictable direction. The car is less responsive to your hand movements, but it goes exactly where you want it to go.

- The **steering heaviness** = your negative feedback (controlled by $\beta$).
- The **reduced responsiveness** = **reduced gain** $A_f < A$.
- The **predictable, smooth motion** = **stabilized performance** (less noise, less distortion).
- The **wider range of speeds at which the car remains stable** = **increased bandwidth**.

The "cost" of using the steering (feedback) is that you lose raw power (gain), but you gain **precision, predictability, and robustness** — a trade-off every analog circuit designer willingly accepts.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | KTU Importance |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Open-loop gain | $A$ | Dimensionless (dB) | Fundamental input to all formulas |
| Feedback factor | $\beta$ | Dimensionless | Sampling network parameter |
| Desensitivity factor | $(1 + A\beta)$ | Dimensionless | Appears in EVERY NFB equation |
| Loop gain | $A\beta$ | Dimensionless | Determines feedback "strength" |
| Bandwidth | $BW$ | Hertz (Hz) | Increases with NFB |

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Trade-off Rule:** In negative feedback, you can never get something for nothing. The **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)** remains essentially constant — gain is exchanged for bandwidth, and the noise/distortion reduction comes at the cost of overall signal amplification.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generalized Feedback Equation (Foundation)

The starting point for ALL four effects is the fundamental closed-loop gain equation:

$$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

Where:
- $A$ = Open-loop gain of the amplifier (without feedback).
- $\beta$ = Reverse transmission factor / feedback fraction (set by the external feedback network).
- $A\beta$ = **Loop gain** — the total gain as the signal travels once around the feedback loop.
- $(1 + A\beta)$ = **Desensitivity factor** or **Return Difference** — the master scaling term.

The basic feedback block diagram consists of:
- A **summing junction** (often marked $\Sigma$) where the input signal is combined with the feedback signal.
- A forward-path amplifier of gain $A$.
- A feedback network of gain $\beta$ that samples the output and returns a portion to the input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **NEGATIVE** feedback, the feedback signal is **subtracted** from the input. This is the defining characteristic that makes the system self-correcting and stable.

## 2.2 Effect on Gain — The Stabilization Phenomenon

### Why does gain decrease?
Because we are dividing the open-loop gain $A$ by a number greater than 1, namely $(1 + A\beta)$, the magnitude of $A_f$ is always smaller than $A$.

### Why does gain become more stable?
Consider a fractional change in open-loop gain, $dA$. The corresponding fractional change in closed-loop gain is:

$$\frac{dA_f}{A_f} = \frac{1}{1 + A\beta} \cdot \frac{dA}{A}$$

This means that if the open-loop gain $A$ drifts by (say) 50% due to temperature, component aging, or supply voltage variation, the **closed-loop gain $A_f$ drifts by only 50% / $(1 + A\beta)$** — a massive improvement in predictability.

**Key Insight:** The closed-loop gain $A_f$ depends almost entirely on $\beta$ (which is set by stable, passive components like resistors) rather than on $A$ (which depends on active, temperature-sensitive transistors).

## 2.3 Effect on Bandwidth — The Extension Phenomenon

In a typical single-pole amplifier, the open-loop gain rolls off at a rate of **−20 dB/decade** above the dominant pole frequency $f_H$. The closed-loop gain rolls off at the same rate but starts from a **lower DC value**, which means it takes a higher frequency before the gain drops to unity.

The upper cutoff frequency with feedback is:

$$f_{Hf} = f_H (1 + A\beta)$$

The lower cutoff frequency with feedback is:

$$f_{Lf} = \frac{f_L}{(1 + A\beta)}$$

For mid-band analysis, the bandwidth becomes:

$$BW_f = f_{Hf} - f_{Lf} \approx f_{Hf} = f_H (1 + A\beta)$$

The **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)** remains constant:

$$A \cdot f_H \approx A_f \cdot f_{Hf} = \text{constant}$$

This is a **fundamental invariant** of single-pole feedback systems.

## 2.4 Effect on Noise Reduction

Any internal noise voltage $V_n$ generated inside the amplifier (thermal noise, shot noise, flicker noise, power supply ripple) appears at the output as $A \cdot V_n$ in the open-loop case. With negative feedback, the same noise source produces:

$$V_{n,\text{out (with feedback)}} = \frac{A \cdot V_n}{1 + A\beta}$$

So the **noise at the output is reduced by the factor $(1 + A\beta)$**, while the **desired signal is also reduced by $(1 + A\beta)$**. This means the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) is NOT improved by negative feedback** if the input signal is held constant.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often incorrectly state that "negative feedback improves SNR." This is FALSE for a simple feedback loop around a single amplifier. SNR improvement requires a **pre-amplifier stage** (low-noise front end) followed by the feedback amplifier, so that the signal is boosted BEFORE entering the noisy region.

## 2.5 Effect on Distortion Reduction

Non-linear distortion generates harmonics of the input signal. The harmonic amplitude $D$ (open-loop distortion) appears at the output multiplied by $A$. With negative feedback, the same distortion component becomes:

$$D_f = \frac{D}{1 + A\beta}$$

This is a **direct, real reduction in the distortion voltage** at the output, **without** affecting the input signal level. This is a genuine, free improvement in linearity — a major reason why negative feedback is universally used in high-fidelity audio amplifiers.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Quantity | Open-Loop | With Negative Feedback | Scaling Factor | Units |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Gain | $A$ | $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1+A\beta}$ | $\div (1+A\beta)$ | dimensionless |
| Gain Sensitivity | $\dfrac{dA_f}{A_f}$ | $\dfrac{1}{1+A\beta} \cdot \dfrac{dA}{A}$ | $\div (1+A\beta)$ | dimensionless |
| Upper Cutoff Frequency | $f_H$ | $f_{Hf} = f_H(1+A\beta)$ | $\times (1+A\beta)$ | Hz |
| Lower Cutoff Frequency | $f_L$ | $f_{Lf} = \dfrac{f_L}{1+A\beta}$ | $\div (1+A\beta)$ | Hz |
| Bandwidth | $BW$ | $BW_f = BW(1+A\beta)$ | $\times (1+A\beta)$ | Hz |
| Gain-Bandwidth Product | $A \cdot f_H$ | $A_f \cdot f_{Hf} = \text{const}$ | invariant | Hz |
| Output Noise Voltage | $A \cdot V_n$ | $\dfrac{A \cdot V_n}{1+A\beta}$ | $\div (1+A\beta)$ | V |
| Distortion | $D$ | $D_f = \dfrac{D}{1+A\beta}$ | $\div (1+A\beta)$ | % or dB |
| Input Resistance (Series) | $R_i$ | $R_{if} = R_i(1+A\beta)$ | $\times (1+A\beta)$ | $\Omega$ |
| Input Resistance (Shunt) | $R_i$ | $R_{if} = \dfrac{R_i}{1+A\beta}$ | $\div (1+A\beta)$ | $\Omega$ |
| Output Resistance (Voltage) | $R_o$ | $R_{of} = \dfrac{R_o}{1+A\beta}$ | $\div (1+A\beta)$ | $\Omega$ |
| Output Resistance (Current) | $R_o$ | $R_{of} = R_o(1+A\beta)$ | $\times (1+A\beta)$ | $\Omega$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The four effects (gain stabilization, bandwidth extension, noise reduction, distortion reduction) are the **reason negative feedback was discovered**. Harold Black invented it in 1927 at Bell Labs specifically to stabilize the gain of telephone repeater amplifiers. Today, every operational amplifier, audio amplifier, RF amplifier, and control system uses negative feedback for these very reasons.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Loop Gain Expression

**Starting Assumption:** We have a basic feedback system with a forward amplifier of gain $A$ and a feedback network of gain $\beta$. The input signal is $V_s$, the feedback signal is $V_f$, and the error signal (input to the amplifier) is $V_e$.

**Step 1:** The error signal is the difference between the input and the feedback signal:

$$V_e = V_s - V_f$$

**Step 2:** The output of the amplifier is the error signal multiplied by the open-loop gain $A$:

$$V_o = A \cdot V_e = A(V_s - V_f)$$

**Step 3:** The feedback network samples the output and produces the feedback signal:

$$V_f = \beta \cdot V_o$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the expression for $V_f$ from Step 3 into the equation in Step 2:

$$V_o = A(V_s - \beta V_o)$$

**Step 5:** Expand the right-hand side:

$$V_o = A \cdot V_s - A \beta V_o$$

**Step 6:** Collect all $V_o$ terms on the left-hand side:

$$V_o + A\beta V_o = A \cdot V_s$$

**Step 7:** Factor $V_o$ on the left:

$$V_o(1 + A\beta) = A \cdot V_s$$

**Step 8:** Divide both sides by $(1 + A\beta)$:

$$V_o = \frac{A}{1+A\beta} \cdot V_s$$

**Step 9:** Recognize that $V_o / V_s$ is the closed-loop gain $A_f$:

$$\boxed{A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}}$$

This is the **master equation** of negative feedback. Note: For this to be NEGATIVE feedback, the sign of $\beta$ must be such that the subtraction in Step 1 actually opposes the input.

## 3.2 Derivation: Gain Sensitivity (Desensitization)

**Starting Point:** We have $A_f = A / (1 + A\beta)$. We want to find how a small change $dA$ in $A$ affects $A_f$.

**Step 1:** Take the differential of $A_f$ with respect to $A$, treating $\beta$ as constant:

$$\frac{dA_f}{dA} = \frac{d}{dA}\left[\frac{A}{1+A\beta}\right]$$

**Step 2:** Apply the quotient rule $\left(\frac{u}{v}\right)' = \frac{u'v - uv'}{v^2}$, where $u = A$ and $v = 1 + A\beta$:

$$\frac{dA_f}{dA} = \frac{(1)(1+A\beta) - (A)(\beta)}{(1+A\beta)^2}$$

**Step 3:** Expand the numerator:

$$\frac{dA_f}{dA} = \frac{1 + A\beta - A\beta}{(1+A\beta)^2} = \frac{1}{(1+A\beta)^2}$$

**Step 4:** Divide both sides by $A_f$ to get fractional change:

$$\frac{dA_f}{A_f} = \frac{dA}{(1+A\beta)^2} \cdot \frac{1}{A_f}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $A_f = A/(1+A\beta)$, which means $1/A_f = (1+A\beta)/A$:

$$\frac{dA_f}{A_f} = \frac{dA}{(1+A\beta)^2} \cdot \frac{1+A\beta}{A}$$

**Step 6:** Simplify by canceling one factor of $(1+A\beta)$:

$$\boxed{\frac{dA_f}{A_f} = \frac{1}{1+A\beta} \cdot \frac{dA}{A}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The fractional change in closed-loop gain is $(1+A\beta)$ times **smaller** than the fractional change in open-loop gain. For example, with $A\beta = 99$ (so $1 + A\beta = 100$), a 20% change in $A$ produces only a 0.2% change in $A_f$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Bandwidth Extension

**Starting Assumption:** The open-loop amplifier has a single dominant pole at $f_H$, so its gain is:

$$A(f) = \frac{A_0}{1 + j(f/f_H)}$$

where $A_0$ is the DC open-loop gain.

**Step 1:** The closed-loop gain (assuming $\beta$ is frequency-independent, i.e., a resistive divider) is:

$$A_f(f) = \frac{A(f)}{1 + A(f)\beta}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the expression for $A(f)$:

$$A_f(f) = \frac{\dfrac{A_0}{1+j(f/f_H)}}{1 + \dfrac{A_0\beta}{1+j(f/f_H)}}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 + j(f/f_H))$:

$$A_f(f) = \frac{A_0}{1 + j(f/f_H) + A_0\beta}$$

**Step 4:** Group the constant terms in the denominator (since $A_0\beta$ is real and $1 + A_0\beta$ is a number):

$$A_f(f) = \frac{A_0}{1 + A_0\beta + j(f/f_H)}$$

**Step 5:** Factor out $(1 + A_0\beta)$ from the denominator:

$$A_f(f) = \frac{A_0/(1+A_0\beta)}{1 + j\dfrac{f}{f_H(1+A_0\beta)}}$$

**Step 6:** Identify the new DC closed-loop gain and the new upper cutoff frequency:

$$A_{f0} = \frac{A_0}{1+A_0\beta}, \qquad f_{Hf} = f_H(1+A_0\beta)$$

**Step 7:** Therefore:

$$\boxed{f_{Hf} = f_H(1+A\beta)}$$

The bandwidth has been extended by exactly the desensitivity factor. The **Gain-Bandwidth Product** is:

$$A_{f0} \cdot f_{Hf} = \frac{A_0}{1+A_0\beta} \cdot f_H(1+A_0\beta) = A_0 f_H = \text{constant}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Noise Reduction

**Setup:** Consider an internal noise source $V_n$ injected at the output of the amplifier's gain stage (a common model for transistor collector noise). Without feedback, the output noise is simply:

$$V_{n,\text{out, OL}} = A \cdot V_n$$

**Step 1:** With feedback, the total output is the sum of the amplified signal and the amplified noise, but the feedback loop also acts on the noise.

**Step 2:** The output with feedback, including the noise source, is:

$$V_o = \frac{A(V_s - \beta V_o) + A \cdot V_n}{1}$$

Wait — let us set this up more carefully. The noise $V_n$ is internal to the amplifier, so it gets amplified by $A$ along with the error signal. The fundamental relationship is:

$$V_o = A \cdot V_e + A \cdot V_n$$

where $V_e = V_s - \beta V_o$.

**Step 3:** Substitute $V_e$:

$$V_o = A(V_s - \beta V_o) + A \cdot V_n$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange:

$$V_o + A\beta V_o = A \cdot V_s + A \cdot V_n$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $V_o$:

$$V_o(1+A\beta) = A \cdot V_s + A \cdot V_n$$

$$V_o = \frac{A \cdot V_s}{1+A\beta} + \frac{A \cdot V_n}{1+A\beta}$$

**Step 6:** Identify the noise term at the output:

$$\boxed{V_{n,\text{out, feedback}} = \frac{A \cdot V_n}{1+A\beta} = \frac{V_{n,\text{out, OL}}}{1+A\beta}}$$

The output noise is reduced by the factor $(1+A\beta)$.

## 3.5 Derivation: Distortion Reduction

**Setup:** Assume the open-loop amplifier produces a distortion component $D$ (in volts) at the output that is proportional to the open-loop gain. So $D_{OL} = A \cdot d$, where $d$ is the per-unit distortion generated inside the amplifier.

**Step 1:** Following the same procedure as the noise derivation, the distortion component at the output with feedback is:

$$D_{\text{feedback}} = \frac{A \cdot d}{1+A\beta}$$

**Step 2:** If we define the open-loop distortion as $D_{OL} = A \cdot d$, then:

$$\boxed{D_{\text{feedback}} = \frac{D_{OL}}{1+A\beta}}$$

The distortion is reduced by the desensitivity factor, exactly like the noise.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Style)

**Problem:** An amplifier has an open-loop gain $A = 1000$, feedback factor $\beta = 0.01$, open-loop bandwidth $BW = 10 \text{ kHz}$, open-loop output noise $V_n = 5 \text{ mV}$, and open-loop distortion $D = 2\%$. Find the closed-loop gain, bandwidth, output noise, and output distortion.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the desensitivity factor:

$$1 + A\beta = 1 + (1000)(0.01) = 1 + 10 = 11$$

**Step 2:** Closed-loop gain:

$$A_f = \frac{A}{1+A\beta} = \frac{1000}{11} \approx 90.91$$

**Step 3:** Closed-loop bandwidth:

$$BW_f = BW \cdot (1+A\beta) = 10 \text{ kHz} \times 11 = 110 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 4:** Closed-loop output noise:

$$V_{n,\text{feedback}} = \frac{5 \text{ mV}}{11} \approx 0.4545 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 5:** Closed-loop distortion:

$$D_f = \frac{2\%}{11} \approx 0.1818\%$$

**Verification of GBW Invariance:**
$$A \cdot BW = 1000 \times 10 \text{ kHz} = 10^7 \text{ Hz}$$
$$A_f \cdot BW_f = 90.91 \times 110 \text{ kHz} = 10^7 \text{ Hz} \checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generalized Negative Feedback Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **fundamental negative feedback topology**, showing how the four sampling/mixing configurations all share the same forward path $A$ and feedback network $\beta$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["INPUT SIGNAL Vs"] --> node2["SUMMING JUNCTION Sigma"]
    node2 -->|"Error Signal Ve = Vs - Vf"| node3["FORWARD AMPLIFIER Gain A"]
    node3 -->|"Amplified Signal A times Ve"| node4["OUTPUT NODE Vo"]
    node4 -->|"Sampled Output"| node5["FEEDBACK NETWORK Gain Beta"]
    node5 -->|"Feedback Signal Vf = Beta times Vo"| node2
    node4 --> node6["OUTPUT TO LOAD"]

    style node1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style node2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style node3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style node4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style node5 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style node6 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Effect of Negative Feedback — Architectural Flow Chart

This diagram summarizes **how the single desensitivity factor $(1 + A\beta)$ simultaneously modifies four amplifier parameters**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["DESENSITIVITY FACTOR one plus A beta"] --> nodeB["GAIN REDUCTION"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["BANDWIDTH EXTENSION"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["NOISE ATTENUATION"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["DISTORTION ATTENUATION"]

    nodeB --> nodeB1["Af = A divided by one plus A beta"]
    nodeC --> nodeC1["fHf = fH times one plus A beta"]
    nodeD --> nodeD1["Vn feedback = Vn open loop divided by one plus A beta"]
    nodeE --> nodeE1["D feedback = D open loop divided by one plus A beta"]

    nodeB1 --> nodeX["OUTPUT: STABLE PRECISE LOW-DISTORTION AMPLIFIER"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeX
    nodeD1 --> nodeX
    nodeE1 --> nodeX

    style nodeA fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    style nodeB fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeC fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeD fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeE fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeX fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

## 4.3 Frequency Response Comparison — Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop

This diagram visualizes the **trade-off** between gain and bandwidth. The open-loop response (high gain, narrow bandwidth) is "pushed down and stretched" by the feedback action, producing the closed-loop response (lower gain, wider bandwidth), with the **Gain-Bandwidth Product** preserved.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph openloop["OPEN LOOP RESPONSE"]
        nodeOL1["DC Gain = A0"] --> nodeOL2["Rolls off at -20 dB per decade"]
        nodeOL2 --> nodeOL3["Upper Cutoff at fH"]
    end

    subgraph closedloop["CLOSED LOOP RESPONSE"]
        nodeCL1["DC Gain = Af = A0 divided by one plus A beta"] --> nodeCL2["Same slope: -20 dB per decade"]
        nodeCL2 --> nodeCL3["Upper Cutoff at fHf = fH times one plus A beta"]
    end

    nodeOL3 --- nodeCL3

    nodeOL1 -. "GAIN REDUCED by factor" .-> nodeCL1
    nodeOL3 -. "BANDWIDTH EXTENDED by factor" .-> nodeCL3

    style openloop fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style closedloop fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeOL1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style nodeOL2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style nodeOL3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style nodeCL1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style nodeCL2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style nodeCL3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Note how the closed-loop curve is a "shifted-down, shifted-right" version of the open-loop curve. The horizontal shift to the right represents **bandwidth extension**; the vertical shift downward represents **gain reduction**. The product of gain and bandwidth at the new −3 dB point is the same as at the original point — this is the **Gain-Bandwidth Product invariance**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"State and explain the effect of negative feedback on the gain of an amplifier."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Negative feedback reduces the gain of an amplifier. The closed-loop gain $A_f$ is related to the open-loop gain $A$ by the equation:

$$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

where $\beta$ is the feedback factor. Since the desensitivity factor $(1 + A\beta) > 1$, the magnitude of $A_f$ is always less than $A$. The key benefit is **gain stabilization**: a fractional change $dA/A$ in the open-loop gain results in a much smaller fractional change $dA_f/A_f = \dfrac{1}{1+A\beta} \cdot \dfrac{dA}{A}$ in the closed-loop gain. The closed-loop gain therefore depends primarily on the stable passive feedback network rather than on the temperature-sensitive active devices. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`
**"How does negative feedback affect the bandwidth of an amplifier? Justify your answer with a suitable expression."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Negative feedback **increases (extends) the bandwidth** of an amplifier. The upper cutoff frequency with feedback is given by:

$$f_{Hf} = f_H(1 + A\beta)$$

where $f_H$ is the open-loop upper cutoff frequency. The bandwidth is extended by the desensitivity factor $(1 + A\beta)$. The underlying reason is the **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW) invariance**: since $A \cdot f_H = A_f \cdot f_{Hf} = \text{constant}$, reducing the gain by $(1+A\beta)$ automatically increases the bandwidth by the same factor. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**(a)** Derive the expression for the closed-loop gain of an amplifier with negative feedback. Explain how negative feedback stabilizes the gain against variations in the open-loop gain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An amplifier has an open-loop gain of $A = 2000$ and a feedback factor of $\beta = 0.02$. The open-loop bandwidth is $20 \text{ kHz}$ and the open-loop output noise voltage is $V_n = 10 \text{ mV}$. Calculate: (i) the closed-loop gain, (ii) the closed-loop bandwidth, (iii) the closed-loop output noise voltage, and (iv) the percentage reduction in gain. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Let the input signal be $V_s$, the error signal at the input of the amplifier be $V_e$, the output be $V_o$, and the feedback signal be $V_f$.

**Step 1:** The error signal is the input minus the feedback signal:

$$V_e = V_s - V_f$$

**Step 2:** The output is the error signal multiplied by the open-loop gain:

$$V_o = A \cdot V_e = A(V_s - V_f)$$

**Step 3:** The feedback signal is the output multiplied by $\beta$:

$$V_f = \beta \cdot V_o$$

**Step 4:** Substituting Step 3 into Step 2:

$$V_o = A(V_s - \beta V_o) = A V_s - A\beta V_o$$

**Step 5:** Collecting $V_o$ terms:

$$V_o(1 + A\beta) = A V_s$$

**Step 6:** Solving for closed-loop gain:

$$A_f = \frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} \quad \textbf{(4 marks for derivation)}$$

**Gain Stabilization Explanation (3 marks):**

Differentiating $A_f$ with respect to $A$:

$$\frac{dA_f}{dA} = \frac{1}{(1+A\beta)^2}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{dA_f}{A_f} = \frac{1}{1+A\beta} \cdot \frac{dA}{A}$$

This shows that the **fractional change in closed-loop gain is $(1+A\beta)$ times smaller** than the fractional change in open-loop gain. The closed-loop gain becomes almost entirely dependent on the **stable passive components** of the feedback network (typically precision resistors) rather than on the temperature-sensitive transistor parameters that determine $A$. For large $A\beta$, the term $A\beta$ dominates, and $A_f \approx 1/\beta$, which is set by the external feedback network.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given: $A = 2000$, $\beta = 0.02$, $BW = 20 \text{ kHz}$, $V_n = 10 \text{ mV}$.

**Step 1:** Calculate the desensitivity factor:

$$1 + A\beta = 1 + (2000)(0.02) = 1 + 40 = 41$$

**Step 2:** **(i) Closed-loop gain:**

$$A_f = \frac{A}{1+A\beta} = \frac{2000}{41} \approx 48.78 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3:** **(ii) Closed-loop bandwidth:**

$$BW_f = BW \cdot (1+A\beta) = 20 \text{ kHz} \times 41 = 820 \text{ kHz} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4:** **(iii) Closed-loop output noise voltage:**

$$V_{n,\text{feedback}} = \frac{V_n}{1+A\beta} = \frac{10 \text{ mV}}{41} \approx 0.244 \text{ mV} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 5:** **(iv) Percentage reduction in gain:**

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{A - A_f}{A} \times 100\% = \frac{2000 - 48.78}{2000} \times 100\% = 97.56\% \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: `[KTU University Exam - June 2022]`
**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the general concept of negative feedback. Discuss in detail the effect of negative feedback on (i) gain, (ii) bandwidth, and (iii) distortion of an amplifier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An amplifier without feedback gives a harmonic distortion of $5\%$ when the output is $10 \text{ V}$. If the amplifier has an open-loop gain of $A = 100$ and a feedback factor of $\beta = 0.1$, find: (i) the percentage harmonic distortion with feedback, (ii) the output voltage with feedback, and (iii) the value of $\beta$ required to reduce the distortion to $0.5\%$ while keeping the output voltage the same. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation (7 marks):**

A negative feedback system consists of:
- A forward amplifier of gain $A$.
- A feedback network of gain $\beta$ that samples the output.
- A summing junction that **subtracts** the feedback signal from the input, producing the error signal $V_e = V_s - V_f$.

The closed-loop gain is $A_f = A/(1+A\beta)$. **(1 mark for block diagram description)**

**Effect on Gain (2 marks):**
- Gain is **reduced** to $A_f = A/(1+A\beta)$.
- Gain is **stabilized**: $\dfrac{dA_f}{A_f} = \dfrac{1}{1+A\beta} \cdot \dfrac{dA}{A}$.
- Closed-loop gain depends primarily on the stable $\beta$ network.

**Effect on Bandwidth (2 marks):**
- Upper cutoff frequency is **extended**: $f_{Hf} = f_H(1+A\beta)$.
- Lower cutoff frequency is **reduced**: $f_{Lf} = f_L/(1+A\beta)$.
- **Gain-Bandwidth Product** is invariant: $A \cdot f_H = A_f \cdot f_{Hf}$.

**Effect on Distortion (2 marks):**
- Harmonic distortion is **reduced** by the desensitivity factor: $D_f = D/(1+A\beta)$.
- Linearity of the amplifier is **improved**, which is critical in audio and RF applications.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given: $D = 5\%$, $V_o = 10 \text{ V}$ (open-loop), $A = 100$, $\beta = 0.1$.

**Step 1:** Calculate the desensitivity factor:

$$1 + A\beta = 1 + (100)(0.1) = 1 + 10 = 11$$

**Step 2:** **(i) Percentage harmonic distortion with feedback:**

$$D_f = \frac{D}{1+A\beta} = \frac{5\%}{11} \approx 0.4545\% \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3:** **(ii) Output voltage with feedback:**

The output with feedback is reduced by the desensitivity factor:

$$V_{of} = \frac{V_o}{1+A\beta} = \frac{10 \text{ V}}{11} \approx 0.909 \text{ V} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4:** **(iii) New $\beta$ for $D_f = 0.5\%$ with $V_{of} = 10 \text{ V}$:**

To keep the output at 10 V, we need an **input pre-amplifier** with gain of $(1 + A\beta)$ — but for this problem, we interpret the requirement as: find the new feedback factor such that the closed-loop output equals the original output of 10 V, which means the new closed-loop gain must equal the open-loop gain... 

Let me reinterpret: To keep $V_{of} = 10 \text{ V}$ while reducing distortion, we need to keep $A_f = A = 100$ (no gain reduction). This requires $1 + A\beta' = 1$, i.e., $\beta' = 0$, which contradicts the goal.

A more realistic interpretation: We want $D_f = 0.5\%$ while $V_{of}$ matches the original $V_o = 10 \text{ V}$. This is only possible by **increasing the input signal** to compensate for the gain loss. The required compensation factor is $(1 + A\beta)$. With $A = 100$:

$$\frac{D}{1 + A\beta} = 0.5\% \implies 1 + A\beta = \frac{D}{0.5\%} = \frac{5}{0.5} = 10$$

$$100 \beta = 9 \implies \beta = 0.09 \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

The new output with feedback: $V_{of}' = \dfrac{10}{10} = 1 \text{ V}$, but to get 10 V at the output, we must drive the input 10 times harder — so the input signal $V_s$ is increased by a factor of 10. The output noise (from the previous context) and distortion are both reduced by a factor of 10.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing SNR and noise reduction:** Negative feedback reduces the output noise voltage by $(1+A\beta)$, but it also reduces the signal by the same factor. The **SNR does NOT improve** with a single-loop negative feedback amplifier. Many students write "NFB improves SNR" and lose 2-3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Sign convention for $\beta$:** Negative feedback requires the feedback signal to be **subtracted** from the input at the summing junction. If you write $V_e = V_s + V_f$, you have **positive feedback**, which leads to instability and oscillation. Always verify the sign in your block diagram.
> 3. **Forgetting the desensitivity factor:** Every effect (gain, bandwidth, noise, distortion) must be expressed in terms of $(1+A\beta)$. Students who write the formulas without explicitly identifying this factor lose marks for "lack of conceptual clarity."
> 4. **GBW invariance check:** In numerical problems, always verify $A \cdot f_H = A_f \cdot f_{Hf}$. Examiners often give partial credit for this verification, and it catches arithmetic errors.
> 5. **Units:** Bandwidth is in Hz (or kHz, MHz), gain is dimensionless, distortion is in % or dB. Mixing units is a common error.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- ✅ **Master Equation:** $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1 + A\beta}$ — the closed-loop gain in terms of open-loop gain $A$ and feedback factor $\beta$.
- ✅ **Desensitivity Factor:** $(1 + A\beta)$ — appears in EVERY formula related to NFB. Memorize it as a single quantity.
- ✅ **Gain Reduction:** $A_f = A/(1+A\beta)$ — gain always decreases with NFB.
- ✅ **Gain Stabilization:** $\dfrac{dA_f}{A_f} = \dfrac{1}{1+A\beta} \cdot \dfrac{dA}{A}$ — fractional change is reduced by desensitivity factor.
- ✅ **Bandwidth Extension:** $f_{Hf} = f_H(1+A\beta)$ — upper cutoff is multiplied by desensitivity factor.
- ✅ **Lower Cutoff Reduction:** $f_{Lf} = f_L/(1+A\beta)$ — lower cutoff is divided by desensitivity factor.
- ✅ **GBW Invariance:** $A \cdot f_H = A_f \cdot f_{Hf} = \text{constant}$ — gain is traded for bandwidth, one-for-one.
- ✅ **Noise Reduction:** $V_{n,\text{feedback}} = V_{n,\text{OL}}/(1+A\beta)$ — output noise is reduced.
- ✅ **Distortion Reduction:** $D_f = D_{\text{OL}}/(1+A\beta)$ — harmonic distortion is reduced.
- ✅ **SNR is NOT improved** by a single-loop NFB amplifier — both signal and noise are reduced equally.
- ✅ **For SNR improvement**, use a **low-noise pre-amplifier** before the feedback stage.
- ✅ **The cost of NFB:** Loss of raw gain, but gain of stability, bandwidth, linearity, and noise immunity.
- ✅ **KTU Frequently Asked:** Derivation of $A_f$ (4-5 marks), effect on each of the four parameters (2-3 marks each), numerical problems combining all four effects (7 marks).
- ✅ **Real-world example:** Operational amplifiers (μA741, LM358) rely entirely on internal negative feedback to achieve their high open-loop gain (~10⁵) and define a stable, predictable closed-loop gain.
- ✅ **Sign rule:** NFB requires $V_e = V_s - V_f$ at the summing junction; reversing the sign gives positive feedback (oscillator).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Four Basic Feedback Topologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Four Basic Feedback Topologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Feedback in Analog Electronics?

**Feedback** is the process of taking a portion of the output signal of an amplifier and routing it back to the input, where it is combined (mixed) with the original input signal. The combined signal is then applied to the basic amplifier.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Feedback* in an amplifier refers to a deliberate connection from output back to the input such that a fraction of the output signal is superimposed on the input signal, modifying the effective input driving the active device.

In **negative feedback**, the returned signal **opposes** the original input, reducing the net input magnitude. In **positive feedback**, it **aids** the input. KTU Analog Circuits Module 3 deals almost exclusively with **negative feedback** because of its powerful linearization benefits.

## 1.2 Why Do We Need Feedback? (The Engineering Motivation)

Open-loop amplifiers suffer from:
- Gain that drifts with temperature, supply voltage, and device parameters.
- Nonlinear distortion.
- Limited bandwidth.
- Uncontrolled input/output impedances.

Feedback is a *system-level technique* that trades raw gain for vastly superior performance in all these areas. It is the single most important concept in analog design.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Cruise Control of a Car

Imagine driving a car at a target speed of 60 km/h (set-point). You are the "amplifier" whose output is the car speed.

- **Open-Loop (No Feedback):** You press the gas pedal to a fixed position. A hill comes, the car slows — you don't compensate. Output is wrong.
- **Negative Feedback:** You *measure* the speed (output), *compare* it with the desired 60 km/h, and *adjust* the gas pedal. The "difference" is the **error signal** that drives the system.

Key parallels:
| Car Analogy | Feedback Amplifier |
|---|---|
| Speedometer (senses output) | Sampling network |
| Difference between target and actual | Error signal $\,x_i\,$ |
| Gas pedal adjustment | Basic amplifier action |
| Driver's neural loop | Feedback network $\beta$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **error signal** is always smaller in a negative feedback system. This is *not* a flaw — the system constantly *corrects* the error, making the output follow the input faithfully, regardless of disturbances.

## 1.4 Classification of Feedback Topologies

A feedback connection is characterised by two independent decisions:
1. **How is the output sampled?** Either **voltage** (in parallel with load) or **current** (in series with load).
2. **How is the feedback combined at the input?** Either in **series** (KVL subtraction) or in **shunt** (KCL subtraction).

This gives a 2 × 2 matrix yielding exactly **four basic topologies**:

| # | Topology Name (Sedra-Smith) | Equivalent Name | Ideal Amplifier Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Voltage–Series** | Series–Shunt | **Voltage Amplifier** |
| 2 | **Current–Series** | Series–Series | **Transconductance Amplifier** |
| 3 | **Current–Shunt** | Shunt–Series | **Current Amplifier** |
| 4 | **Voltage–Shunt** | Shunt–Shunt | **Transresistance Amplifier** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The first term describes the *sampling* mechanism; the second term describes the *mixing* mechanism. Mastering this convention is **mandatory** for KTU Module 3 problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Two-Port Feedback Model

Every one of the four topologies can be redrawn as a **two-port network** with a basic amplifier $A$ and a feedback network $\beta$, interconnected through ideal mixing/sampling interfaces.

$$
x_s \;\xrightarrow{\;\Sigma\;}\; x_i \;\xrightarrow{\;A\;}\; x_o \;\xrightarrow{\;\;\text{sample}\;\;}\; x_f = \beta\,x_o
$$

The summing junction enforces the **negative** sign:
$$
x_i = x_s - x_f = x_s - \beta\,x_o
$$

The **open-loop gain** $A$ is defined as $A = x_o / x_i$ and the **feedback factor** $\beta = x_f / x_o$. Solving for $x_o / x_s$ yields the canonical closed-loop result:

$$
\boxed{\,A_f \;=\; \dfrac{x_o}{x_s} \;=\; \dfrac{A}{1 + A\beta}\,}
$$

The dimensionless product $A\beta$ is the **loop gain**, and $(1 + A\beta)$ is the **desensitivity factor** or **return difference**. The KTU 2024 question bank regularly tests the recognition that $A_f \to 1/\beta$ as $A\beta \to \infty$, meaning the closed-loop transfer is set entirely by the (passive) feedback network.

## 2.2 The Four Topologies: A Complete Analytical Matrix

The variables $x_s, x_i, x_o, x_f$ change physical meaning depending on topology. The table below is your **master cheat sheet** — bookmark it.

> [!NOTE]
> In the table below, $R_i$ is the input resistance of the basic amplifier $A$ block, $R_o$ is its output resistance, and $R_{if}$, $R_{of}$ denote the corresponding values *with* feedback loop closed.

| Topology | Input Signal $x_s$ | Error Signal $x_i$ | Output Signal $x_o$ | Feedback Signal $x_f$ | Gain $A$ | $\beta$ Units | $R_{if}$ | $R_{of}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Voltage–Series** | $V_s$ | $V_i$ | $V_o$ | $V_f$ | $A_v = V_o/V_i$ | V/V | $R_i(1+A\beta)$ | $R_o/(1+A\beta)$ |
| **Current–Series** | $V_s$ | $V_i$ | $I_o$ | $V_f$ | $G_M = I_o/V_i$ (S) | V/A | $R_i(1+A\beta)$ | $R_o(1+A\beta)$ |
| **Current–Shunt** | $I_s$ | $I_i$ | $I_o$ | $I_f$ | $A_i = I_o/I_i$ | A/A | $R_i/(1+A\beta)$ | $R_o(1+A\beta)$ |
| **Voltage–Shunt** | $I_s$ | $I_i$ | $V_o$ | $I_f$ | $R_M = V_o/I_i$ (Ω) | A/V | $R_i/(1+A\beta)$ | $R_o/(1+A\beta)$ |

**Universal Consequences of Negative Feedback (independent of topology):**

- **Gain desensitization:** $\dfrac{dA_f/A_f}{dA/A} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1+A\beta}$
- **Bandwidth extension:** $(BW)_f \;=\; (BW)(1+A\beta)$
- **Nonlinear distortion reduction:** $D_f \;=\; D/(1+A\beta)$
- **Noise reduction (in-loop sources only):** $N_f \;=\; N/(1+A\beta)$
- **Input/Output impedance change** as per the table above.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Topology | Typical Real-World Application |
|---|---|
| **Voltage–Series** | Non-inverting op-amp, audio pre-amplifier, precision voltage buffer |
| **Current–Series** | Cascode stage, transconductance cell in OTAs, active load in op-amps |
| **Current–Shunt** | Current-mode driver, photodiode transimpedance first stage, current conveyor |
| **Voltage–Shunt** | Shunt-feedback op-amp (inverting configuration), TIA, ADC input buffer |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Print & Carry)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise these equations *symbolically*. KTU valuation expects variable names that match these exactly.

$$
A_f = \frac{A}{1+A\beta}
$$

$$
R_{if}^{(\text{series})} = R_i(1+A\beta) \qquad R_{if}^{(\text{shunt})} = \frac{R_i}{1+A\beta}
$$

$$
R_{of}^{(\text{voltage-sampling})} = \frac{R_o}{1+A\beta} \qquad R_{of}^{(\text{current-sampling})} = R_o(1+A\beta)
$$

$$
\text{Gain desensitivity} = \frac{1}{1+A\beta}
$$

$$
\text{Bandwidth with feedback} = (1+A\beta)\times(\text{Open-loop BW})
$$

$$
\text{Gain–Bandwidth product (GBP)} = \text{constant (Bode's theorem)}
$$

$$
\text{Loop gain} = A\beta \quad\quad \text{Return difference} = 1+A\beta
$$

> [!TIP]
> Two **"voltage"** keywords $\Rightarrow$ **shunt** at that port.
> Two **"current"** keywords $\Rightarrow$ **series** at that port.
> **Series mixing** at input $\Rightarrow$ feedback signal is a **voltage**.
> **Shunt mixing** at input $\Rightarrow$ feedback signal is a **current**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Loop Gain $A_f$

We begin with the **general feedback block diagram** (KCL/KVL abstracted into generic signal variables $x$).

**Step 1 — Define the open-loop quantities.**
The basic amplifier (without feedback) is described by:
$$
A \;\equiv\; \frac{x_o}{x_i}
$$
The feedback network senses the output and returns a fraction $\beta$ of it:
$$
\beta \;\equiv\; \frac{x_f}{x_o}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the summing junction constraint.**
For negative feedback, the feedback signal is subtracted from the source:
$$
x_i \;=\; x_s - x_f
$$

**Step 3 — Express $x_f$ in terms of $x_i$.**
Substitute the definition of $\beta$:
$$
x_f \;=\; \beta\,x_o \;=\; \beta\,A\,x_i
$$

**Step 4 — Eliminate $x_i$ from the summing junction equation.**
$$
x_i \;=\; x_s - \beta A x_i \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; x_i(1+\beta A) \;=\; x_s
$$
$$
\Longrightarrow\;\; x_i \;=\; \frac{x_s}{1+A\beta}
$$

**Step 5 — Recover $x_o$ and form the closed-loop ratio.**
Since $x_o = A\,x_i$:
$$
x_o \;=\; A\cdot\frac{x_s}{1+A\beta} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{x_o}{x_s} \;=\; \frac{A}{1+A\beta}
$$

**Final Result:**
$$
\boxed{\,A_f \;=\; \frac{x_o}{x_s} \;=\; \frac{A}{1+A\beta}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The quantity $(1+A\beta)$ is the **desensitivity factor**. If $A\beta \gg 1$, then $A_f \to 1/\beta$, a value that depends only on the (highly stable) passive feedback network.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Input Resistance with Feedback

The derivation is the same in *form* for all four topologies; only the input mixing (series vs shunt) changes the impedance expression.

**Step 1 — Apply a test voltage $V_t$ at the source-side port and measure the resulting current $I_t$.**

For **series mixing** (input is a series loop, KVL):
$$
I_t \;=\; \frac{x_i}{R_i} \qquad \text{(since the basic amplifier draws $x_i/R_i$)}
$$

For **shunt mixing** (input is a parallel node, KCL):
$$
I_t \;=\; \frac{V_t}{R_i} \;=\; \frac{x_i + x_f}{R_i} \quad \text{where the total source current splits.}
$$

**Step 2 — Relate $x_i$ to the test signal using the closed-loop result.**

For the **series-mixing case** the test source sees $x_s$ as $V_t$ and the error $x_i = V_t/(1+A\beta)$:
$$
R_{if}^{(\text{series})} \;=\; \frac{V_t}{I_t} \;=\; \frac{V_t}{V_t/R_i(1+A\beta)} \;=\; R_i(1+A\beta)
$$

For the **shunt-mixing case** the source current $I_s$ produces $x_i = I_s R_i / (1+A\beta)$ (via the KCL counterpart of Step 4 in §3.1):
$$
R_{if}^{(\text{shunt})} \;=\; \frac{V_t}{I_t} \;=\; \frac{R_i}{1+A\beta}
$$

**Final Results:**

$$
\boxed{\,R_{if}^{(\text{series})} \;=\; R_i(1+A\beta)\,} \qquad
\boxed{\,R_{if}^{(\text{shunt})} \;=\; \dfrac{R_i}{1+A\beta}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Series mixing → input impedance is BOOSTED.** This is why the non-inverting op-amp has an input resistance of giga-ohms.
> **Shunt mixing → input impedance is REDUCED.** This is exploited to widen bandwidth by trading off input resistance.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Output Resistance with Feedback

We deactivate the independent source ($x_s = 0$) and apply a test source $V_t$ (or $I_t$) at the output port.

**Step 1 — Voltage-sampling (shunt at output).**
Without feedback, applying $V_t$ causes current $I_t = V_t/R_o$. With the loop closed, the feedback network senses $V_t$, generates $x_f = \beta V_t$, and produces a **counter-EMF** that reduces the net excitation across $R_o$ by a factor $(1+A\beta)$. Hence:
$$
R_{of}^{(\text{voltage-sampling})} \;=\; \frac{R_o}{1+A\beta}
$$

**Step 2 — Current-sampling (series at output).**
Without feedback, the load current divides between $R_o$ and the load. With the loop closed, the feedback creates an extra drive that *cancels* a fraction of the test current through $R_o$, effectively multiplying the resistance seen:
$$
R_{of}^{(\text{current-sampling})} \;=\; R_o(1+A\beta)
$$

**Final Results:**

$$
\boxed{\,R_{of}^{(\text{voltage})} \;=\; \dfrac{R_o}{1+A\beta}\,} \qquad
\boxed{\,R_{of}^{(\text{current})} \;=\; R_o(1+A\beta)\,}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard)

> **Problem.** A voltage-series feedback amplifier has open-loop gain $A = 1000$, $R_i = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_o = 100\,\Omega$, and $\beta = 0.01$. Calculate $A_f$, $R_{if}$, $R_{of}$, and the percentage change in gain if $A$ drops by 10%.

**Step 1 — Desensitivity factor.**
$$
1+A\beta \;=\; 1 + 1000 \times 0.01 \;=\; 11
$$

**Step 2 — Closed-loop gain.**
$$
A_f \;=\; \frac{A}{1+A\beta} \;=\; \frac{1000}{11} \;\approx\; 90.91\;\text{V/V}
$$

**Step 3 — Input resistance with feedback.**
$$
R_{if} \;=\; R_i(1+A\beta) \;=\; 10{,}000 \times 11 \;=\; 110\;\text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 4 — Output resistance with feedback.**
$$
R_{of} \;=\; \frac{R_o}{1+A\beta} \;=\; \frac{100}{11} \;\approx\; 9.09\;\Omega
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage change in $A_f$ for 10% drop in $A$.**
New $A' = 900$.
$$
A_f' \;=\; \frac{900}{1 + 900 \times 0.01} \;=\; \frac{900}{10} \;=\; 90\;\text{V/V}
$$
$$
\%\Delta A_f \;=\; \frac{90.91 - 90.00}{90.91} \times 100\% \;\approx\; 1.0\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Open-loop would have changed by 10%; the closed-loop version changed by only ~1%. **The feedback has desensitised the gain by a factor of 10**, exactly equal to the desensitivity factor $1+A\beta = 11$. The 1% residual is the *price* paid for using a finite (not infinite) open-loop gain.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 General Two-Port Feedback Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef src fill:#FFF7E6,stroke:#D49A2A,color:#000
    classDef amp fill:#E6F0FF,stroke:#1F4E8C,color:#000
    classDef fb fill:#EAF7EA,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#333,color:#000

    Vs["Source xs"]:::src --> SUM(("SUM"))
    FB["Feedback xf"]:::fb --> SUM
    SUM -->|xi| A["Basic Amplifier A"]:::amp
    A -->|xo| OUT["Output Port xo"]
    OUT --> LOAD["Load RL"]
    OUT -.->|sample| FNET["Feedback Network beta"]:::fb
    FNET --> FB
```

## 4.2 Topology 1 — Voltage–Series Feedback (Voltage Amplifier)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef src fill:#FFF7E6,stroke:#D49A2A
    classDef amp fill:#E6F0FF,stroke:#1F4E8C
    classDef fb fill:#EAF7EA,stroke:#2E7D32

    VS["Source Vs"]:::src -->|Rs| NODE_A(("Series Mix"))
    VF["Feedback Vf"]:::fb --> NODE_A
    NODE_A -->|Vi| AMP["Amplifier A_v = Vo/Vi"]:::amp
    AMP -->|Vo| NODE_B(("Voltage Sample"))
    NODE_B --> LOAD["Load RL"]
    NODE_B -.->|parallel| FNET["Feedback Network beta = Vf/Vo"]:::fb
    FNET --> VF
```

> **Interpretation:** Output is sensed in **parallel** (voltage sampling) and feedback $V_f$ subtracts in **series** at the input. Result: high $R_{if}$, low $R_{of}$.

## 4.3 Topology 2 — Current–Series Feedback (Transconductance Amplifier)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef src fill:#FFF7E6,stroke:#D49A2A
    classDef amp fill:#E6F0FF,stroke:#1F4E8C
    classDef fb fill:#EAF7EA,stroke:#2E7D32

    VS["Source Vs"]:::src --> NODE_A(("Series Mix"))
    VF["Feedback Vf"]:::fb --> NODE_A
    NODE_A -->|Vi| AMP["Amplifier G_M = Io/Vi"]:::amp
    AMP -->|Io| NODE_B(("Current Sample"))
    NODE_B --> LOAD["Load RL"]
    NODE_B -.->|series insert| FNET["Feedback Network beta = Vf/Io"]:::fb
    FNET --> VF
```

> **Interpretation:** Output current $I_o$ is sensed in **series** with the load. The feedback network converts this current into a voltage $V_f$ that is subtracted in series at the input. Result: high $R_{if}$, high $R_{of}$.

## 4.4 Topology 3 — Current–Shunt Feedback (Current Amplifier)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef src fill:#FFF7E6,stroke:#D49A2A
    classDef amp fill:#E6F0FF,stroke:#1F4E8C
    classDef fb fill:#EAF7EA,stroke:#2E7D32

    IS["Source Is"]:::src --> NODE_A(("Shunt Mix"))
    IF["Feedback If"]:::fb --> NODE_A
    NODE_A -->|Ii| AMP["Amplifier A_i = Io/Ii"]:::amp
    AMP -->|Io| NODE_B(("Current Sample"))
    NODE_B --> LOAD["Load RL"]
    NODE_B -.->|series insert| FNET["Feedback Network beta = If/Io"]:::fb
    FNET --> IF
```

> **Interpretation:** Output is current-sensed; feedback current $I_f$ is subtracted at the input **node** (KCL). Result: low $R_{if}$, high $R_{of}$.

## 4.5 Topology 4 — Voltage–Shunt Feedback (Transresistance Amplifier)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef src fill:#FFF7E6,stroke:#D49A2A
    classDef amp fill:#E6F0FF,stroke:#1F4E8C
    classDef fb fill:#EAF7EA,stroke:#2E7D32

    IS["Source Is"]:::src --> NODE_A(("Shunt Mix"))
    IF["Feedback If"]:::fb --> NODE_A
    NODE_A -->|Ii| AMP["Amplifier R_M = Vo/Ii"]:::amp
    AMP -->|Vo| NODE_B(("Voltage Sample"))
    NODE_B --> LOAD["Load RL"]
    NODE_B -.->|parallel| FNET["Feedback Network beta = If/Vo"]:::fb
    FNET --> IF
```

> **Interpretation:** Output voltage is sampled in parallel; the feedback network converts $V_o$ into a current $I_f$ that is subtracted via KCL at the input. Result: low $R_{if}$, low $R_{of}$. This is the topology of the classic **inverting op-amp** configuration.

## 4.6 Functional Architecture: How the Four Topologies Interact

```mermaid
    ROOT --> G2["Sampling = Current"]:::group

    G1 --> L1A["Series Mix at Input"]:::leaf
    G1 --> L1B["Shunt Mix at Input"]:::leaf
    G2 --> L2A["Series Mix at Input"]:::leaf
    G2 --> L2B["Shunt Mix at Input"]:::leaf

    L1A -->|produces| OUT1["Voltage-Series: Voltage Amplifier"]
    L1B -->|produces| OUT2["Voltage-Shunt: Transresistance Amplifier"]

    L2B -->|produces| OUT4["Current-Shunt: Current Amplifier"]

    OUT1 --> C1["Rif high, Rof low"]
    OUT2 --> C2["Rif low, Rof low"]
    OUT3 --> C3["Rif high, Rof high"]
    OUT4 --> C4["Rif low, Rof high"]
```
