# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`  
> **List and name the four basic feedback topologies in amplifiers. For each, state the type of amplifier it ideally realises.**

**Model Answer:**

The four basic feedback topologies, classified by the *sampling* method (output) and *mixing* method (input), are:

| # | Topology | Ideal Amplifier Type |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Voltage–Series (Series–Shunt) | Voltage amplifier (V/V) |
| 2 | Current–Series (Series–Series) | Transconductance amplifier (A/V) |
| 3 | Current–Shunt (Shunt–Series) | Current amplifier (A/A) |
| 4 | Voltage–Shunt (Shunt–Shunt) | Transresistance amplifier (V/A) |

> **Key Idea:** The first term refers to the output (sampled quantity) and the second to the input (mixed quantity). **(3 marks)**

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`  
> **What is the "desensitivity factor" in a feedback amplifier? Write the expression for closed-loop gain and explain the significance of $A\beta \gg 1$.**

**Model Answer:**

The **desensitivity factor** is the dimensionless quantity $(1 + A\beta)$ that quantifies the reduction in sensitivity of the closed-loop gain to variations in the open-loop gain $A$.

The closed-loop gain is given by:
$$
A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}
$$

If $A\beta \gg 1$, then $(1+A\beta) \approx A\beta$, and the expression reduces to:
$$
A_f \;\approx\; \frac{1}{\beta}
$$

**Significance:** The closed-loop gain becomes essentially independent of $A$, depending only on the (stable, passive) feedback network $\beta$. The amplifier is therefore robust against temperature drift, device ageing, and supply variations. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A (Choice 1)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`**  
> **(a)** Derive the general expression for the closed-loop gain $A_f$ of a negative feedback amplifier in terms of the open-loop gain $A$ and feedback factor $\beta$. Hence, obtain the relation between $A_f$ and the loop gain $A\beta$. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **voltage-series feedback amplifier**. Derive the expressions for the input resistance with feedback ($R_{if}$) and the output resistance with feedback ($R_{of}$). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

Define:
- Open-loop gain: $A = x_o / x_i$
- Feedback factor: $\beta = x_f / x_o$

Summing junction (negative sign):
$$
x_i = x_s - x_f = x_s - \beta x_o
$$

Also $x_o = A x_i$, hence $x_i = x_o/A$. Substituting:
$$
\frac{x_o}{A} = x_s - \beta x_o \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; x_o\!\left(\frac{1}{A}+\beta\right) = x_s
$$

$$
\boxed{\,A_f = \frac{x_o}{x_s} = \frac{A}{1+A\beta}\,}
$$

**Relation to loop gain:** Loop gain $L = A\beta$. Hence:
$$
A_f = \frac{A}{1+L}
$$
When $L \gg 1$: $A_f \to 1/\beta$, and $\dfrac{dA_f}{A_f} = \dfrac{1}{1+L}\dfrac{dA}{A}$. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b):**

In voltage-series feedback, the output voltage is sampled (parallel/shunt) and the feedback voltage $V_f$ is mixed in series at the input.

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Sampling | Voltage (parallel) |
| Mixing | Series |
| Basic gain $A$ | $A_v = V_o/V_i$ |
| Feedback factor $\beta$ | $V_f/V_o$ |
| Ideal closed-loop transfer | $A_{vf} \to 1/\beta$ |

**Input Resistance with Feedback (Series Mixing):**
Apply test voltage $V_t$ at the source side. Current $I_t = V_i/R_i$. But $V_i = V_t/(1+A\beta)$ (from the closed-loop relation).
$$
R_{if} = \frac{V_t}{I_t} = \frac{V_t}{V_t/[R_i(1+A\beta)]} = R_i(1+A\beta)
$$

**Output Resistance with Feedback (Voltage Sampling):**
Set $V_s = 0$ and apply test voltage $V_t$ at output. Without feedback, $I_t = V_t/R_o$. With feedback, the loop opposes the test signal by factor $(1+A\beta)$:
$$
R_{of} = \frac{V_t}{I_t} = \frac{R_o}{1+A\beta}
$$

**Final Expressions:**
$$
\boxed{\,R_{if} = R_i(1+A\beta)\,} \qquad \boxed{\,R_{of} = \dfrac{R_o}{1+A\beta}\,}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Voltage-series feedback **increases input resistance** and **decreases output resistance** — the ideal behaviour of a voltage amplifier. **(7 marks)**

---

### 🔹 Question B (Choice 2)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`**  
> **(a)** Compare the four basic feedback topologies with respect to: (i) sampling quantity, (ii) mixing quantity, (iii) ideal amplifier type, (iv) input resistance with feedback, and (v) output resistance with feedback. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** For a **voltage-shunt feedback amplifier**, the open-loop parameters are $A = 5\times 10^3$, $R_i = 5\;\text{k}\Omega$, $R_o = 2\;\text{k}\Omega$, and the feedback factor $\beta = 2\times 10^{-3}$ (A/V). Calculate: (i) the desensitivity factor, (ii) the closed-loop transresistance $R_{Mf}$, (iii) $R_{if}$, and (iv) $R_{of}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):** Comparison Table

| # | Topology | Sampling | Mixing | Ideal Type | $R_{if}$ | $R_{of}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Voltage–Series | Voltage | Series | Voltage amp. | $R_i(1+A\beta)$ | $R_o/(1+A\beta)$ |
| 2 | Current–Series | Current | Series | Transconductance | $R_i(1+A\beta)$ | $R_o(1+A\beta)$ |
| 3 | Current–Shunt | Current | Shunt | Current amp. | $R_i/(1+A\beta)$ | $R_o(1+A\beta)$ |
| 4 | Voltage–Shunt | Voltage | Shunt | Transresistance | $R_i/(1+A\beta)$ | $R_o/(1+A\beta)$ |

> **Mnemonic:** "**V**oltage + **V**oltage ⇒ S**h**unt at that port" (for output and input respectively when both are voltage-current combinations that share a port). **(7 marks)**

**Part (b):** Voltage-Shunt Feedback Calculations

Given: $A = R_M = 5\times 10^3\;\Omega$, $R_i = 5\;\text{k}\Omega$, $R_o = 2\;\text{k}\Omega$, $\beta = 2\times 10^{-3}\;\text{A/V}$.

**(i) Desensitivity factor:**
$$
1 + A\beta = 1 + (5\times 10^3)(2\times 10^{-3}) = 1 + 10 = 11
$$

**(ii) Closed-loop transresistance:**
$$
R_{Mf} = \frac{A}{1+A\beta} = \frac{5\times 10^3}{11} \approx 454.5\;\Omega
$$

**(iii) Input resistance with feedback (shunt mixing):**
$$
R_{if} = \frac{R_i}{1+A\beta} = \frac{5{,}000}{11} \approx 454.5\;\Omega
$$

**(iv) Output resistance with feedback (voltage sampling):**
$$
R_{of} = \frac{R_o}{1+A\beta} = \frac{2{,}000}{11} \approx 181.8\;\Omega
$$

> **Observations:** Both $R_{if}$ and $R_{of}$ drop by a factor of 11 — the hallmark of the voltage-shunt topology. The transresistance drops from $5\,\text{k}\Omega$ to $454.5\,\Omega$, a price paid for desensitisation. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU Module 3 evaluation:**
> 1. **Mixing up the two naming conventions.** Some textbooks use "Series–Shunt" (mixing–sampling), others use "Voltage–Series" (sampling–mixing). Always **declare the convention you are using** at the start of your answer.
> 2. **Using wrong $R_{if}$ / $R_{of}$ formulas.** Remember: *series-mixing* gives $R_i(1+A\beta)$ but *current-sampling* gives $R_o(1+A\beta)$. Get both directions right.
> 3. **Forgetting that $A$ has different physical units for each topology.** $A$ is V/V, A/V, A/A, or V/A. Carrying a numerical value across topologies without changing units is a frequent error.
> 4. **Skipping the sign convention in the summing junction.** A common 1-mark loss when students write $x_i = x_s + x_f$ instead of $x_i = x_s - x_f$ for negative feedback.
> 5. **Confusing $A\beta$ (loop gain) with $A_f$ (closed-loop gain).** They are different quantities; $A\beta$ is the *loop*; $A_f$ is the *system* transfer.
> 6. **Failing to convert $\beta$ to the correct physical units.** KTU often gives $\beta$ in mixed units (A/V or V/A). Convert before multiplying by $A$.
> 7. **Drawing block diagrams without labelling all four signal variables ($x_s, x_i, x_o, x_f$).** Lose 1 mark immediately.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the Four Basic Feedback Topologies**

- ✅ **Definition of Feedback:** Returning a portion of the output to the input for comparison.
- ✅ **Negative vs Positive:** Negative reduces error; positive increases it (used in oscillators only).
- ✅ **Sampling Rules:** Voltage-sampling = parallel/shunt at output; Current-sampling = series at output.
- ✅ **Mixing Rules:** Series-mixing = KVL subtraction of voltage; Shunt-mixing = KCL subtraction of current.
- ✅ **Universal Closed-Loop Gain:** $A_f = A/(1 + A\beta)$.
- ✅ **Loop Gain = $A\beta$**, Return Difference = $1 + A\beta$.
- ✅ **Desensitisation:** Closed-loop gain depends on $\beta$, not $A$, when $A\beta \gg 1$.
- ✅ **Bandwidth Extension:** $(BW)_f = (BW)(1+A\beta)$; **GBP remains constant** (Bode's theorem).
- ✅ **Distortion & Noise (in-loop):** Both reduced by factor $(1+A\beta)$.
- ✅ **$R_{if}$:** Series-mixing → multiplied by $(1+A\beta)$; Shunt-mixing → divided by $(1+A\beta)$.
- ✅ **$R_{of}$:** Voltage-sampling → divided by $(1+A\beta)$; Current-sampling → multiplied by $(1+A\beta)$.
- ✅ **Ideal Amplifier Realisations:**  
   - Voltage–Series → Voltage amplifier (high $Z_{in}$, low $Z_{out}$)  
   - Current–Series → Transconductance amplifier (high $Z_{in}$, high $Z_{out}$)  
   - Current–Shunt → Current amplifier (low $Z_{in}$, high $Z_{out}$)  
   - Voltage–Shunt → Transresistance amplifier (low $Z_{in}$, low $Z_{out}$)
- ✅ **Real-world associations:**  
   - Non-inverting op-amp = Voltage–Series  
   - Inverting op-amp / TIA = Voltage–Shunt  
   - Cascode / OTA = Current–Series  
   - Current conveyor input = Current–Shunt
- ✅ **Two-port analysis principle:** Open the loop at any point, terminate both ports with the **opposite** ideal source (voltage source → short, current source → open), then compute $A$ and $\beta$ including loading effects.

> **One-line memory aid for KTU viva:** *"Voltage–Voltage = Shunt–Shunt. Current–Current = Series–Series. Mixed = Mixed."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Discrete BJT Circuits in Voltage-Series and Voltage-Shunt Feedback Topologies - Voltage Gain, Input and Output Impedance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Discrete BJT Circuits in Voltage-Series and Voltage-Shunt Feedback Topologies - Voltage Gain, Input and Output Impedance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Feedback Topology?

In analog electronics, a **feedback amplifier** is an amplifier circuit that routes a portion of its output back to its input through a feedback network. The classification of feedback topologies is based on two fundamental criteria:

1. **How the output signal is sampled** (Voltage sampling vs Current sampling)
2. **How the feedback signal is mixed with the input** (Series mixing vs Shunt mixing)

The **four basic feedback topologies** are:

| Output Sampling \ Input Mixing | Series Mixing | Shunt Mixing |
|---|---|---|
| **Voltage Sampling** | Voltage-Series Feedback | Voltage-Shunt Feedback |
| **Current Sampling** | Current-Series Feedback | Current-Shunt Feedback |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This module specifically focuses on **Voltage-Series** and **Voltage-Shunt** feedback topologies applied to **discrete BJT circuits** (using BJTs as the active amplifying device rather than op-amps).

### 1.2 Voltage-Series Feedback (Series-Shunt Feedback)

**Formal Definition:** A feedback topology in which the output voltage is sampled (shunt connection at output) and the feedback voltage is mixed in series with the input voltage source. The feedback signal is a **voltage** proportional to the output voltage.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Cruise Control of an Elevator:**
Imagine you are driving an elevator. You press a button (input voltage $V_s$) to set a desired floor. A sensor measures the elevator's actual position (output voltage $V_o$) and sends back a signal. The error detector compares your desired floor with the actual floor and adjusts the motor. 
- The **elevator position sensor** samples the **output** (voltage sampling at the load).
- The **comparison** is done in **series** with your input command (series mixing).
- Result: Higher accuracy, higher input impedance (the source sees a larger impedance), and lower output impedance (better voltage regulation).

**Key Properties:**
- **Stabilizes the voltage gain** $A_{vf}$
- **Increases input impedance** $R_{if} = R_i (1 + A\beta)$
- **Decreases output impedance** $R_{of} = \dfrac{R_o}{1 + A\beta}$
- Closed-loop gain approximates $A_{vf} \approx \dfrac{1}{\beta}$ for large loop gain $A\beta$

### 1.3 Voltage-Shunt Feedback (Shunt-Shunt Feedback)

**Formal Definition:** A feedback topology in which the output voltage is sampled (shunt at output) and the feedback current is mixed in shunt (parallel) with the input current source. The feedback signal is a **current** proportional to the output voltage.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Bathroom Shower Mixer:**
A shower has hot and cold water inputs. The output water temperature (analogous to output voltage) is monitored by your skin. Your hand adjusts a knob (input) to mix water appropriately. 
- The **temperature** is sensed at the output (voltage sampling).
- The **mixing** is done by joining water streams together at a node (shunt/parallel mixing).
- Result: Lower input impedance (current flows into the node), lower output impedance, and a controlled, stable output.

**Key Properties:**
- **Stabilizes the transresistance gain** $A_{vf}$ (or voltage gain with proper loading)
- **Decreases input impedance** $R_{if} = \dfrac{R_i}{1 + A\beta}$
- **Decreases output impedance** $R_{of} = \dfrac{R_o}{1 + A\beta}$
- Closed-loop gain approximates $A_{vf} \approx \dfrac{1}{\beta}$ for large loop gain

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Feedback Identity:** The **amount of feedback** is governed by the **loop gain** $A\beta$ (also called the **return difference** $T = A\beta$). The **desensitivity factor** $(1 + A\beta)$ is the central parameter in all four feedback topologies.

### 1.4 Identifying the Topology in a Discrete BJT Circuit

| Circuit Configuration | Topology | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| **CE amplifier with unbypassed emitter resistor $R_E$** | Voltage-Series | $R_E$ samples $I_E \approx I_C$ proportional to $V_o$, creates feedback voltage $V_f = I_E R_E$ in series with $V_{BE}$ |
| **CE amplifier with feedback resistor $R_f$ from collector to base** | Voltage-Shunt | $R_f$ samples $V_C \approx V_o$ and injects feedback current into the base node (shunt mixing) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Block Diagram Model of Feedback

Every feedback amplifier can be represented by a **two-port network** containing:
- A basic amplifier (A-block) with open-loop gain $A$
- A feedback network (β-block) with transfer function $\beta$
- A summing junction (subtractor) for input mixing
- A sampling node for output extraction

The closed-loop transfer function (universal feedback equation):

$$A_f = \dfrac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

where:
- $A$ = open-loop gain (gain without feedback)
- $\beta$ = reverse transmission of feedback network
- $A\beta$ = **loop gain** (return ratio)
- $1 + A\beta$ = **desensitivity factor** or **return difference**

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Principle

**For Voltage-Series Feedback (Series-Shunt):**

1. **Sampling Stage:** The feedback network is connected in **shunt** (parallel) across the output port — it senses the output voltage $V_o$.
2. **Mixing Stage:** The feedback voltage $V_f = \beta V_o$ is injected in **series** with the source $V_s$, producing an error voltage $V_i = V_s - V_f$.
3. **Amplification:** The basic amplifier processes the error voltage: $V_o = A V_i$.
4. **Loop Closure:** Substituting, we get $V_o = A(V_s - \beta V_o)$, yielding $V_o/V_s = A/(1+A\beta)$.
5. **Why Series mixing increases input impedance:** The series connection adds the feedback voltage in opposition, reducing the net current drawn from the source for a given input voltage — effectively increasing the input resistance by factor $(1+A\beta)$.

**For Voltage-Shunt Feedback (Shunt-Shunt):**

1. **Sampling Stage:** The feedback network is connected in **shunt** across the output — senses $V_o$.
2. **Mixing Stage:** The feedback current $I_f = \beta V_o$ is injected in **parallel** (KCL at the input node) with the source current.
3. **Amplification:** The basic amplifier processes the input current: $I_o = A I_i$ (transconductance) or $V_o = A_{rs} I_i$ (transresistance form).
4. **Loop Closure:** Yields $A_{rsf} = A_{rs}/(1 + A_{rs}\beta)$ in the transresistance domain.
5. **Why Shunt mixing decreases input impedance:** The parallel current injection increases the net current drawn from the source for a given input voltage — effectively decreasing the input resistance by factor $(1+A\beta)$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Formula Compilation Table** — Master these equations for any feedback analysis problem.

| # | Parameter | Voltage-Series Feedback | Voltage-Shunt Feedback |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Input Mixing Type | Series | Shunt (Parallel) |
| 2 | Output Sampling Type | Shunt (Voltage) | Shunt (Voltage) |
| 3 | Open-Loop Gain $A$ | Voltage Gain $A_v = V_o/V_i$ | Transresistance $A_{rs} = V_o/I_i$ (units: V/A = Ω) |
| 4 | Feedback Factor $\beta$ | Voltage Ratio $V_f/V_o$ (dimensionless) | Transconductance $I_f/V_o$ (units: A/V = S) |
| 5 | Closed-Loop Gain $A_f$ | $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1+A\beta}$ (dimensionless) | $A_{rsf} = \dfrac{A_{rs}}{1+A_{rs}\beta}$ (Ω) |
| 6 | Ideal Closed-Loop Gain (for $A\beta \gg 1$) | $A_{vf} \approx \dfrac{1}{\beta}$ | $A_{rsf} \approx \dfrac{1}{\beta}$ |
| 7 | Input Resistance with Feedback $R_{if}$ | $R_{if} = R_i (1+A\beta)$ | $R_{if} = \dfrac{R_i}{1+A\beta}$ |
| 8 | Output Resistance with Feedback $R_{of}$ | $R_{of} = \dfrac{R_o}{1+A\beta}$ | $R_{of} = \dfrac{R_o}{1+A\beta}$ |
| 9 | Source Loading Effect | $V_s$ drives $R_{if}$ directly | $I_s$ splits between $R_{if}$ and $R_s$ |
| 10 | Voltage Gain with $R_s$ and $R_L$ | $A_{vsf} = A_{vf} \cdot \dfrac{R_i}{R_i + R_s} \cdot \dfrac{R_L}{R_L + R_{of}}$ | $A_{vsf} = -A_{rsf} \cdot \dfrac{R_s \vert R_{if}}{R_s \vert R_{if}} \cdot \dfrac{R_L}{R_L + R_{of}}$ |

### 2.4 Practical Utility in Engineering

- **Voltage-Series Feedback** is used in **non-inverting op-amp configurations**, **CE amplifiers with emitter degeneration**, and **audio pre-amplifiers** where high input impedance and stable voltage gain are critical.
- **Voltage-Shunt Feedback** is used in **inverting op-amp configurations**, **transimpedance amplifiers** (TIA) for photodiode readout, and **CE amplifiers with collector-to-base feedback** where low input impedance and current summing are required.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern:** In problems involving discrete BJTs, you must:
> 1. Draw the **small-signal h-parameter equivalent circuit**.
> 2. **Identify** the topology by inspecting the feedback element location.
> 3. Apply the **two-port analysis method** (open the feedback loop, find $A$ and $\beta$, then close the loop).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: CE Amplifier with Unbypassed $R_E$ (Voltage-Series Feedback)

**Circuit Description:** A common-emitter amplifier with emitter resistor $R_E$ that is **not** bypassed by a capacitor. The emitter resistor acts as the feedback element.

**Step 1: Small-Signal Equivalent Circuit**

Using the approximate hybrid-π model with $r_\pi$, $g_m$, and $r_o \to \infty$ (or finite if specified):

The output voltage at the collector is $V_o = -g_m V_\pi R_C$.
The emitter voltage is $V_E = (1 + g_m r_\pi) V_\pi \cdot \dfrac{R_E}{r_\pi + (1 + g_m r_\pi) R_E} \approx g_m V_\pi R_E$ (for $g_m r_\pi R_E \gg r_\pi$).

**Step 2: Open-Loop Analysis (Conceptual)**

To find $A$ and $\beta$ separately, we open the feedback loop. A common technique is to **set $R_E = 0$** for the basic amplifier $A$-path, then identify $\beta$ from the feedback network alone.

**Step 3: Identifying $\beta$ for Voltage-Series Feedback**

The feedback network consists of $R_E$ (and any voltage divider if present). The feedback voltage is the voltage across $R_E$:

$$V_f = I_E R_E \approx I_C R_E = -\dfrac{V_o}{R_C} \cdot R_E$$

Therefore, the feedback factor is:

$$\beta = \dfrac{V_f}{V_o} = -\dfrac{R_E}{R_C}$$

The negative sign indicates $180°$ phase shift in the feedback (sampling the inverted output).

**Step 4: Identifying the Open-Loop Gain $A$**

With $R_E$ acting as a series element at the emitter (considering $R_E$ as part of the amplifier for the A-block calculation, but bypassing the feedback sampling effect):

$$A = \dfrac{V_o}{V_i} = \dfrac{-g_m R_C}{1 + g_m R_E} \approx -\dfrac{R_C}{R_E} \quad \text{(for } g_m R_E \gg 1\text{)}$$

**Step 5: Computing Loop Gain $A\beta$**

$$A\beta = \left(-\dfrac{R_C}{R_E}\right) \cdot \left(-\dfrac{R_E}{R_C}\right) = 1$$

Wait — this gives a special case. The standard derivation actually requires careful analysis. Let's redo it using the **exact small-signal model**.

**Step 5 (Corrected): Exact Loop Gain Derivation**

The small-signal KVL/KCL at the emitter node:

$$V_i = V_\pi + (g_m V_\pi + V_\pi/r_\pi) R_E = V_\pi [1 + (g_m + 1/r_\pi) R_E]$$

$$V_o = -g_m V_\pi R_C$$

Therefore, the open-loop voltage gain (with feedback present, but the basic amplifier definition) is:

$$A_v = \dfrac{V_o}{V_i} = \dfrac{-g_m R_C}{1 + (g_m + 1/r_\pi) R_E}$$

For $g_m R_E \gg 1$ and $1/r_\pi \ll g_m$:

$$A_v \approx -\dfrac{g_m R_C}{g_m R_E} = -\dfrac{R_C}{R_E}$$

**Step 6: Closed-Loop Voltage Gain $A_{vf}$**

Using the feedback formula:

$$A_{vf} = \dfrac{A_v}{1 + A_v \beta}$$

With $A_v \approx -R_C/R_E$ and $\beta = -R_E/R_C$:

$$A_{vf} = \dfrac{-R_C/R_E}{1 + (-R_C/R_E)(-R_E/R_C)} = \dfrac{-R_C/R_E}{1 + 1} = -\dfrac{R_C}{2 R_E}$$

**Step 7: Input Resistance Calculation**

The input resistance looking into the base (without $R_E$ effect on input) is $R_{i(base)} = r_\pi + (1 + \beta_{dc}) R_E \approx r_\pi(1 + g_m R_E)$.

With the base-bias resistors $R_1 \vert R_2$ in parallel:

$$R_i = R_1 \vert R_2 \vert [r_\pi + (1 + \beta_{dc}) R_E]$$

Using the feedback formula with the desensitivity factor:

$$R_{if} = R_i (1 + A\beta)$$

where $A$ is the **gain with load considered** and $\beta$ is from the feedback network. In this case, $A_v \beta \approx 1$, so $(1 + A_v\beta) = 2$:

$$R_{if} = R_i \times 2 = 2 [R_1 \vert R_2 \vert (r_\pi + (1 + \beta_{dc}) R_E)]$$

**Step 8: Output Resistance Calculation**

The open-loop output resistance (looking into the collector with $R_E$ bypassed effectively) is $R_o = R_C \vert r_o \approx R_C$.

The feedback factor at the output for voltage sampling is $\beta = -R_E/R_C$, but the **proper derivation** for $R_{of}$ uses:

$$R_{of} = \dfrac{R_o}{1 + A_v \beta} = \dfrac{R_C}{2}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: CE Amplifier with $R_f$ from Collector to Base (Voltage-Shunt Feedback)

**Circuit Description:** A common-emitter amplifier where a resistor $R_f$ connects the collector (output) to the base (input). This is the classic **voltage-shunt feedback** topology.

**Step 1: Small-Signal Equivalent Circuit**

Replace the BJT with its hybrid-π model. Apply a test source $V_s$ with source resistance $R_s$ at the base. The output is taken at the collector across $R_C \vert R_L$.

**Step 2: Open-Loop Analysis — Disconnect the Feedback**

To find the open-loop transresistance $A_{rs}$, we **break the loop** by removing $R_f$ and grounding the base side of the now-open $R_f$. Apply an input test current $I_i$ at the base.

With $R_f$ removed, the input current $I_i$ flows entirely into the base:

$$V_\pi = I_i \cdot r_\pi$$

$$V_o = -g_m V_\pi R_C = -g_m r_\pi I_i R_C = -\beta_{ac} I_i R_C$$

Therefore, the open-loop transresistance:

$$A_{rs} = \dfrac{V_o}{I_i} = -\beta_{ac} R_C \quad \text{(units: ohms)}$$

**Step 3: Open-Loop Input Resistance**

With $R_f$ disconnected, the input resistance looking into the base is simply $R_i = r_\pi$ (ignoring bias resistors initially; we'll add them later).

**Step 4: Open-Loop Output Resistance**

With $R_f$ disconnected and the input port open-circuited (since input is current-driven for this topology), the output resistance is $R_o = R_C \vert r_o \approx R_C$.

**Step 5: Feedback Factor $\beta$**

The feedback network is just $R_f$ (a single resistor). The output voltage $V_o$ at the collector drives current through $R_f$ into the base node:

$$I_f = \dfrac{V_o - V_i}{R_f} \approx \dfrac{V_o}{R_f} \quad \text{(since } V_i \ll V_o \text{ typically)}$$

Therefore, the feedback transconductance:

$$\beta = \dfrac{I_f}{V_o} = \dfrac{1}{R_f} \quad \text{(units: siemens, A/V)}$$

**Step 6: Loop Gain $A_{rs}\beta$**

$$A_{rs} \beta = (-\beta_{ac} R_C) \cdot \dfrac{1}{R_f} = -\dfrac{\beta_{ac} R_C}{R_f}$$

The **desensitivity factor**:

$$1 + A_{rs}\beta = 1 + \dfrac{\beta_{ac} R_C}{R_f}$$

**Step 7: Closed-Loop Transresistance**

$$A_{rsf} = \dfrac{A_{rs}}{1 + A_{rs}\beta} = \dfrac{-\beta_{ac} R_C}{1 + \beta_{ac} R_C / R_f}$$

Simplifying by multiplying numerator and denominator by $R_f$:

$$A_{rsf} = \dfrac{-\beta_{ac} R_C R_f}{R_f + \beta_{ac} R_C}$$

**Step 8: Closed-Loop Input Resistance**

$$R_{if} = \dfrac{R_i}{1 + A_{rs}\beta} = \dfrac{r_\pi}{1 + \beta_{ac} R_C / R_f}$$

Including bias resistors $R_B = R_1 \vert R_2$:

$$R_{if,total} = R_B \vert R_{if(base)} = R_B \vert \left[\dfrac{r_\pi}{1 + \beta_{ac} R_C / R_f}\right]$$

**Step 9: Closed-Loop Output Resistance**

$$R_{of} = \dfrac{R_o}{1 + A_{rs}\beta} = \dfrac{R_C}{1 + \beta_{ac} R_C / R_f}$$

**Step 10: Overall Voltage Gain (with Source Resistance $R_s$)**

The overall voltage gain from source $V_s$ to output $V_o$ is:

$$A_{vsf} = \dfrac{V_o}{V_s} = \dfrac{A_{rsf}}{R_s + R_{if}}$$

Or equivalently, since $V_s$ drives $R_s$ in series with $R_{if}$:

$$V_i = V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_{if}}{R_s + R_{if}}$$

$$V_o = A_{vsf} \cdot V_s$$

where:

$$A_{vsf} = \dfrac{A_{rsf}}{R_s + R_{if}} = \dfrac{-\beta_{ac} R_C R_f / (R_f + \beta_{ac} R_C)}{R_s + r_\pi R_f / (R_f + \beta_{ac} R_C)}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

**Problem:** For the CE amplifier shown below, find the voltage gain with and without feedback, input resistance, and output resistance.

Given: $R_C = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_E = 470 \text{ }\Omega$, $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 2.2 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_s = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $\beta_{dc} = \beta_{ac} = 100$, $V_{CC} = 12 \text{ V}$, $V_{BE} = 0.7 \text{ V}$, $r_o = \infty$.

**Step 1: DC Bias Analysis**

The base voltage from the voltage divider:

$$V_B = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 12 \cdot \dfrac{2.2}{12.2} = 2.164 \text{ V}$$

The emitter voltage:

$$V_E = V_B - V_{BE} = 2.164 - 0.7 = 1.464 \text{ V}$$

The quiescent emitter current:

$$I_E = \dfrac{V_E}{R_E} = \dfrac{1.464}{470} = 3.115 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 2: Small-Signal Parameters**

$$g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T} \approx \dfrac{I_E}{25 \text{ mV}} = \dfrac{3.115}{25} = 0.1246 \text{ S}$$

$$r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta_{ac}}{g_m} = \dfrac{100}{0.1246} = 802.6 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 3: Without Feedback (Bypass $R_E$ with capacitor)**

$$A_{v,wo} = -g_m (R_C \vert R_L) = -0.1246 \cdot (4.7\text{k} \vert 5\text{k}) = -0.1246 \cdot 2421.6 = -301.8$$

Input resistance:

$$R_{i,wo} = R_1 \vert R_2 \vert r_\pi = 10\text{k} \vert 2.2\text{k} \vert 802.6 = 1.8\text{k} \vert 802.6 = 554.6 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 4: With Feedback (Unbypassed $R_E$)**

$$A_{v,w} = -\dfrac{g_m (R_C \vert R_L)}{1 + g_m R_E} = -\dfrac{301.8}{1 + 0.1246 \cdot 470} = -\dfrac{301.8}{1 + 58.56} = -\dfrac{301.8}{59.56} = -5.068$$

Input resistance:

$$R_{i,w} = R_1 \vert R_2 \vert [r_\pi + (1 + \beta_{dc}) R_E] = 1.8\text{k} \vert [802.6 + 101 \cdot 470] = 1.8\text{k} \vert 47.27\text{k} = 1.735 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5: Verification Using Feedback Formula**

$$\beta = -\dfrac{R_E}{R_C \vert R_L} = -\dfrac{470}{2421.6} = -0.1941$$

$$A\beta = (-301.8)(-0.1941) = 58.59$$

$$A_{vf} = \dfrac{-301.8}{1 + 58.59} = \dfrac{-301.8}{59.59} = -5.065 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Note:** The two methods (direct analysis and feedback formula) give identical results, confirming the validity of the feedback analysis approach.


### 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow — Feedback Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Identify Topology] --> id1{Output sampling type?}
    id1 -->|Shunt across output| id2{Input mixing type?}
    id1 -->|Series in output| currFB[Current-Series or Current-Shunt]
    id2 -->|Series with source| vsfb[Voltage-Series Feedback]
    id2 -->|Shunt at input node| vshfb[Voltage-Shunt Feedback]
    vsfb --> step1[Step 1: Set RE to zero to find A]
    vshfb --> step1b[Step 1: Disconnect Rf to find A]
    step1 --> step2[Step 2: Compute beta from feedback network]
    step1b --> step2b[Step 2: Compute beta = 1 over Rf]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Calculate A times beta loop gain]
    step2b --> step3b[Step 3: Calculate A times beta loop gain]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Apply Rif = Ri times 1 plus A beta]
    step3b --> step4b[Step 4: Apply Rif = Ri over 1 plus A beta]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: Apply Rof = Ro over 1 plus A beta]
    step4b --> step5b[Step 5: Apply Rof = Ro over 1 plus A beta]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6: Compute Avf = A over 1 plus A beta]
    step5b --> step6b[Step 6: Compute Arsf and convert to Avsf]
    classDef decision fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#F9AB00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#188038,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class id1,id2 decision
    class step1,step2,step3,step1b,step2b,step3b process
    class step4,step5,step6,step4b,step5b,step6b result
```

### 4.5 Two-Port Network Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    port1L[Port 1 Input] --> port1R[Two-Port Network]
    port1R --> port2L[Port 2 Output]
    port1L --- n1a[Node 1a]
    n1a --- n1b[Node 1b]
    n1b --- n2a[Node 2a]
    n2a --- n2b[Node 2b]
    port2L --- n2b
    classDef twoport fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#9334E6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class port1L,port1R,port2L,n1a,n1b,n2a,n2b twoport
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The block diagrams above illustrate the signal flow direction (left to right) and the feedback path (right back to left). The summing junction subtracts $V_f$ from $V_s$ to produce the error signal $V_i$ which drives the basic amplifier. For Voltage-Series feedback, this subtraction happens via **series connection** (voltages add in a loop); for Voltage-Shunt feedback, it happens via **KCL at a node** (currents add at a junction).
