## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define voltage-series feedback. Mention its effect on input and output impedance.**

**Model Answer:**

Voltage-series feedback is a feedback topology in which the output voltage is sampled (shunt connection) and the feedback voltage is applied in series opposition with the input signal. A typical example is a CE amplifier with unbypassed emitter resistor.

**Effects:**
- **Input impedance** is **increased** by a factor of $(1 + A\beta)$: $R_{if} = R_i(1 + A\beta)$
- **Output impedance** is **decreased** by a factor of $(1 + A\beta)$: $R_{of} = R_o/(1 + A\beta)$

The closed-loop voltage gain is $A_{vf} = A/(1 + A\beta) \approx 1/\beta$ for large loop gain.

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Compare voltage-series and voltage-shunt feedback topologies in terms of input mixing and the effect on input impedance.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Voltage-Series Feedback | Voltage-Shunt Feedback |
|---|---|---|
| **Input Mixing Type** | Series (KVL — voltages add in loop) | Shunt (KCL — currents add at node) |
| **Feedback Signal** | Voltage $V_f$ | Current $I_f$ |
| **Effect on $R_{if}$** | Increases by $(1 + A\beta)$ | Decreases by $(1 + A\beta)$ |
| **Typical Circuit** | CE with unbypassed $R_E$ | CE with $R_f$ from collector to base |

The series mixing adds the feedback voltage in opposition, reducing the source current and increasing $R_{if}$. The shunt mixing injects additional current at the base node, increasing the total current drawn and decreasing $R_{if}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] — 14 Marks

For a CE amplifier with the following parameters, draw the small-signal equivalent circuit and calculate:
- (a) The voltage gain **with** and **without** feedback. **(7 marks)**
- (b) The input and output impedance **with** and **without** feedback, and comment on the results. **(7 marks)**

**Given:** $R_C = 5.6 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_E = 560 \text{ }\Omega$ (unbypassed), $R_1 = 33 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 8.2 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_s = 600 \text{ }\Omega$, $R_L = 6.8 \text{ k}\Omega$, $\beta_{ac} = 120$, $I_C = 1.5 \text{ mA}$, $r_o = \infty$.

**Part (a) Solution — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Compute small-signal parameters**

$$g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T} = \dfrac{1.5 \text{ mA}}{25 \text{ mV}} = 0.06 \text{ S} = 60 \text{ mA/V}$$

$$r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta_{ac}}{g_m} = \dfrac{120}{0.06} = 2000 \text{ }\Omega = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 2: Voltage gain WITHOUT feedback (assume $R_E$ is fully bypassed)**

$$R_{ac} = R_C \vert R_L = 5.6\text{k} \vert 6.8\text{k} = \dfrac{5.6 \times 6.8}{12.4} = 3.071 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$A_{v,wo} = -g_m \cdot R_{ac} = -0.06 \times 3071 = -184.26$$

**Step 3: Voltage gain WITH feedback (unbypassed $R_E$)**

$$A_{v,w} = \dfrac{-g_m R_{ac}}{1 + g_m R_E} = \dfrac{-184.26}{1 + 0.06 \times 560} = \dfrac{-184.26}{1 + 33.6} = \dfrac{-184.26}{34.6} = -5.326$$

**Part (b) Solution — 7 Marks**

**Step 4: Input resistance WITHOUT feedback**

$$R_{i,wo} = R_1 \vert R_2 \vert r_\pi = 33\text{k} \vert 8.2\text{k} \vert 2\text{k}$$

$$R_1 \vert R_2 = \dfrac{33 \times 8.2}{41.2} = 6.568 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_{i,wo} = 6.568\text{k} \vert 2\text{k} = \dfrac{6.568 \times 2}{8.568} = 1.533 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5: Input resistance WITH feedback**

The resistance looking into the base becomes $r_\pi + (1 + \beta_{ac}) R_E$:

$$R_{i(base)} = 2\text{k} + 121 \times 0.56\text{k} = 2\text{k} + 67.76\text{k} = 69.76 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_{i,w} = R_1 \vert R_2 \vert 69.76\text{k} = 6.568\text{k} \vert 69.76\text{k} = \dfrac{6.568 \times 69.76}{76.328} = 6.003 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 6: Output resistance WITHOUT feedback**

$$R_{o,wo} = R_C = 5.6 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 7: Output resistance WITH feedback**

$$R_{of} = \dfrac{R_o}{1 + A_v \beta} = \dfrac{5.6\text{k}}{1 + A_v \cdot (-R_E/R_C)} = \dfrac{5.6\text{k}}{1 + 33.6} = \dfrac{5.6\text{k}}{34.6} = 161.85 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 8: Comments**

The feedback reduces the voltage gain magnitude from 184.26 to 5.326 (a factor of 34.6), but increases input impedance from 1.53 kΩ to 6.0 kΩ (factor of 3.92) and decreases output impedance from 5.6 kΩ to 162 Ω (factor of 34.6). The desensitivity factor $(1 + A\beta) = 34.6$ is consistent across all three parameters as expected for voltage-series feedback.

---

#### **Question B** [KTU University Exam - June 2023] — 14 Marks

For a CE amplifier with a feedback resistor $R_f = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$ from collector to base, calculate:
- (a) The closed-loop transresistance, voltage gain, and input resistance. **(7 marks)**
- (b) The output resistance and overall voltage gain with source resistance $R_s = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$. **(7 marks)**

**Given:** $R_C = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = \infty$ (negligible load), $R_1 = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $\beta_{ac} = 100$, $g_m = 50 \text{ mA/V}$, $r_\pi = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**Part (a) Solution — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Open-loop transresistance $A_{rs}$**

$$A_{rs} = -\beta_{ac} R_C = -100 \times 4.7\text{k} = -470 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 2: Feedback factor $\beta$**

$$\beta = \dfrac{1}{R_f} = \dfrac{1}{100\text{k}} = 10 \text{ }\mu\text{S} = 10^{-5} \text{ S}$$

**Step 3: Loop gain and desensitivity factor**

$$A_{rs} \beta = (-470 \times 10^3) \times (10^{-5}) = -4.7$$

$$1 + A_{rs}\beta = 1 - 4.7 = -3.7$$

**Step 4: Closed-loop transresistance**

$$A_{rsf} = \dfrac{A_{rs}}{1 + A_{rs}\beta} = \dfrac{-470\text{k}}{-3.7} = 127.03 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5: Closed-loop input resistance (looking into the base)**

$$R_{if(base)} = \dfrac{r_\pi}{1 + A_{rs}\beta} = \dfrac{2\text{k}}{-3.7} = -540.5 \text{ }\Omega$$

The negative value indicates that the feedback is **positive** in this configuration (because $\beta$ is positive for shunt-shunt with inverting amplifier), which can lead to instability. In practice, this circuit requires careful biasing.

**Step 6: Total input resistance including bias network**

$$R_{if,total} = R_1 \vert R_2 \vert R_{if(base)} = 50\text{k} \vert 10\text{k} \vert (-540.5)$$

$$R_1 \vert R_2 = \dfrac{50 \times 10}{60} = 8.333 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_{if,total} = 8.333\text{k} \vert (-540.5) = \dfrac{8.333\text{k} \times (-540.5)}{8.333\text{k} - 540.5} = \dfrac{-4504 \text{k}}{7792.5} = -578.0 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Part (b) Solution — 7 Marks**

**Step 7: Closed-loop output resistance**

$$R_{of} = \dfrac{R_C}{1 + A_{rs}\beta} = \dfrac{4.7\text{k}}{-3.7} = -1.27 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Again, the negative value confirms the positive feedback nature in this case.

**Step 8: Overall voltage gain with source resistance**

$$A_{vsf} = \dfrac{A_{rsf}}{R_s + R_{if,total}} = \dfrac{127.03\text{k}}{1\text{k} + (-578)} = \dfrac{127.03\text{k}}{422} = 301.0$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Mixing up topologies:** Students often incorrectly classify a CE with $R_f$ from collector to base as voltage-series feedback. The **key is to check the mixing at the input** — if the feedback connects to the **base node** (shunt mixing), it is voltage-shunt, NOT voltage-series.
> 2. **Forgetting bias resistors in $R_i$ calculation:** The $R_1 \vert R_2$ network appears in parallel with the input resistance looking into the base. Both must be included.
> 3. **Sign errors in feedback factor:** In voltage-shunt feedback, $\beta = 1/R_f$ is **positive** (not negative as in some textbook examples). The sign depends on whether the basic amplifier is inverting.
> 4. **Wrong units in transresistance formula:** $A_{rs}$ has units of **ohms** (V/A), $\beta$ has units of **siemens** (A/V), and their product $A_{rs}\beta$ is **dimensionless**.
> 5. **Confusing $R_o$ and $R_C$:** The open-loop output resistance $R_o$ is the resistance looking into the amplifier output port with the input port open-circuited (for current-driven input), which typically reduces to $R_C$ for a simple CE stage.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Voltage-Series and Voltage-Shunt Feedback**

- **Voltage-Series Feedback:** Output voltage sampled, feedback voltage in series with input. $R_{if}$ **increases**, $R_{of}$ **decreases**, both by factor $(1 + A\beta)$.
- **Voltage-Shunt Feedback:** Output voltage sampled, feedback current in shunt with input. $R_{if}$ **decreases**, $R_{of}$ **decreases**, both by factor $(1 + A\beta)$.
- **Canonical CE with $R_E$ (unbypassed)** = Voltage-Series Feedback. The emitter resistor is the feedback element; the feedback voltage is $V_f = I_E R_E$.
- **CE with $R_f$ from collector to base** = Voltage-Shunt Feedback. $R_f$ is the feedback element; the feedback current is $I_f \approx V_o/R_f$.
- **The desensitivity factor** $(1 + A\beta)$ is **universal** and applies symmetrically to all four feedback topologies.
- **Closed-loop gain approximation** $A_f \approx 1/\beta$ holds only when $|A\beta| \gg 1$ (the **deep feedback** condition). Always check this assumption.
- **Units matter:** For voltage-series, $A$ is dimensionless and $\beta$ is dimensionless. For voltage-shunt, $A$ is in **ohms** (transresistance) and $\beta$ is in **siemens** (transconductance).
- **Two-port analysis procedure:** (1) Open the feedback loop; (2) Find open-loop $A$, $R_i$, $R_o$; (3) Find $\beta$ from feedback network; (4) Apply formulas for $A_f$, $R_{if}$, $R_{of}$.
- **Stability check:** The phase shift around the feedback loop must remain less than 180° at the frequency where $|A\beta| = 1$. This is the **Barkhausen criterion** and is critical for oscillator prevention.
- **For BJT discrete circuits**, the small-signal model uses $r_\pi$, $g_m$, and (if needed) $r_o$. The thermal voltage $V_T \approx 25 \text{ mV}$ at room temperature is used to compute $g_m = I_C/V_T$.
- **Bias resistor network** $R_1 \vert R_2$ appears in parallel with the input resistance looking into the base. Do not omit it.
- **Miller's theorem** can simplify the analysis of voltage-shunt feedback by replacing the feedback resistor with its Miller equivalents at input and output.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oscillators: Classification, Criterion for Oscillation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Oscillators: Classification, Criterion for Oscillation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of an Oscillator

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard)**
> An **electronic oscillator** is a two-port active network that generates a periodic, time-varying output signal (typically sinusoidal) at a specific frequency, **without requiring any external input signal**, by utilizing the principle of **positive (regenerative) feedback** combined with the inherent amplifying capability of an active device (BJT, FET, or op-amp).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme module framework, oscillators form the *backbone of signal generation* in communication transmitters, function generators, digital clock circuits, and RF synthesizers. The output of an ideal oscillator is purely a function of the energy stored in the reactive (L or C) elements of the frequency-determining network.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Swinging Pendulum

Imagine a grandfather clock pendulum. If you push it once, it swings back and forth, but friction (air resistance + hinge friction) gradually **steals energy** from the swing, and the pendulum eventually stops. To keep it going indefinitely, the clock mechanism gives a tiny, **perfectly timed push** on every swing — never too much (which would cause chaotic motion), never too little (which would let it die out), and **exactly in phase** with the natural swing.

An electronic oscillator works identically:
- The **amplifier** is the "push mechanism" (it provides energy).
- The **LC or RC tank circuit** is the "pendulum" (it stores and exchanges energy).
- The **feedback network** is the "timed push" (it must be in phase and have the correct magnitude).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** An oscillator is essentially an **amplifier that feeds its own output back into its own input in just the right way**, so it doesn't need a separate signal source to operate.

## 1.3 Classification of Oscillators (KTU 2024 Module 3)

The classification is hierarchical and based on the **frequency-determining network** and the **type of active device used**.

### A. Based on the Frequency-Determining Network

| Category | Frequency Range | Key Elements | Typical Use |
|----------|----------------|--------------|-------------|
| **RC Oscillators** | **1 Hz to 1 MHz** (Audio range) | Resistors & Capacitors | Audio generators, function generators |
| **LC (Tuned) Oscillators** | **1 MHz to 500 MHz** (RF range) | Inductors & Capacitors | RF transmitters, IF stages |
| **Crystal Oscillators** | **1 kHz to 100 MHz** (Fixed) | Piezoelectric Quartz Crystal | Clocks, microcontrollers, radios |

### B. Based on the Waveform Produced

- **Sinusoidal Oscillators** — Produce pure sine waves (Hartley, Colpitts, RC Phase Shift, Wien Bridge).
- **Non-Sinusoidal (Relaxation) Oscillators** — Produce square, triangular, or sawtooth waves (Astable Multivibrator, UJT Relaxation Oscillator).

### C. Based on the Active Device

- **Transistor Oscillators** — BJT/FET based (Hartley, Colpitts, Crystal).
- **Op-Amp Oscillators** — Using operational amplifiers (Wien Bridge, RC Phase Shift, Quadrature).

### D. KTU-Most-Tested Specific Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For your KTU 2024 examinations, focus heavily on the following **four** oscillator types (frequently appearing in Part B 14-mark questions):
> 1. **RC Phase Shift Oscillator** (using BJT or Op-Amp)
> 2. **Wien Bridge Oscillator** (using Op-Amp)
> 3. **Hartley Oscillator** (LC, using BJT)
> 4. **Colpitts Oscillator** (LC, using BJT)

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Engineering Metrics

- **Natural frequency** of oscillation $f_0$ is measured in **Hz (Hertz)**.
- **Loop Gain** $A\beta$ is a **dimensionless ratio**.
- **Quality Factor (Q)** of a tank circuit is **dimensionless**, typically **$Q \geq 10$** for sustained oscillation.
- **Barkhausen Criterion constant:** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ and $\angle A\beta = 0^\circ \text{ (or } 360^\circ\text{)}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Oscillation Criterion (Barkhausen Criterion)

The **Barkhausen Criterion** (1921) is the mathematical heart of every sinusoidal oscillator. It establishes **two simultaneous necessary conditions** that must be satisfied at the frequency of oscillation.

### 2.1.1 Logical Breakdown — The "Why" Behind Each Condition

**Step 1 — The Loop Must Be Closed (Feedback Concept)**
The output $V_o$ of an amplifier with gain $A$ is fed back through a network with transfer function $\beta$. The feedback signal is $\beta V_o$. The total input to the amplifier becomes the sum of the external input $V_s$ and the feedback signal.

**Step 2 — Substitute and Rearrange (The Closed-Loop Transfer Function)**
With the input $V_s = 0$ (no external source — oscillator drives itself), the only signal circulating in the loop is whatever noise or transient initially triggers the circuit. The closed-loop voltage gain is:

$$V_o = A \cdot (\beta V_o)$$

For any non-zero $V_o$ to exist at $V_s = 0$, the coefficient on the right-hand side **must equal unity**:

$$A\beta = 1$$

**Step 3 — The Magnitude Condition (Sustained Steady-State Oscillation)**
- $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ ensures the loop neither amplifies the signal (which would cause saturation/distortion) nor attenuates it (which would cause the signal to die out). The signal circulates with **constant amplitude**.
- In practice, designers make $\vert A\beta \vert$ slightly **greater than 1** to **build up** the oscillation, and amplitude-limiting mechanisms (non-linear device characteristics, automatic gain control, or thermistor/incandescent lamp amplitude stabilization) reduce the effective gain to exactly 1 in steady state.

**Step 4 — The Phase Condition (Correct Feedback Polarity)**
- The total phase shift around the loop must be **$0^\circ$** (or an integer multiple of $360^\circ$). This means the feedback signal arriving back at the input must be **in phase** with the original input signal.
- An in-phase feedback signal is called **positive feedback** or **regenerative feedback**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Negative feedback** in amplifiers ($180^\circ$ phase shift) uses the feedback to *reduce* gain. **Positive feedback** in oscillators ($0^\circ$ total phase shift) uses the feedback to *reinforce* the signal until it sustains itself.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Equations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE FORMATTING NOTE:** This table uses `\vert` instead of `|` to prevent markdown table breaking. KTU examiners **frequently** test these equations directly.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Description / Application |
|---|--------------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Barkhausen Magnitude Condition** | $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ | Loop gain magnitude for sustained oscillation |
| 2 | **Barkhausen Phase Condition** | $\angle A\beta = 2n\pi, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$ | Total phase around loop must be $0^\circ$ or multiples of $360^\circ$ |
| 3 | **Closed-Loop Gain (with feedback)** | $A_{CL} = \dfrac{A}{1 - A\beta}$ | General feedback amplifier equation (denominator $\to 0$ for oscillator) |
| 4 | **RC Phase Shift Oscillator Frequency** | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC \sqrt{6}}$ | Standard 3-section RC network |
| 5 | **Minimum Gain (RC Phase Shift, BJT)** | $A_{v(min)} = 29$ | Derived from $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ for 3 identical RC sections |
| 6 | **Wien Bridge Frequency** | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Series and parallel RC arms equal components |
| 7 | **Wien Bridge Minimum Gain** | $A_{v(min)} = 3$ | Non-inverting amplifier requirement |
| 8 | **Hartley Oscillator Frequency** | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_T C}}$ | $L_T = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$ (with mutual inductance) |
| 9 | **Colpitts Oscillator Frequency** | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L C_{eq}}}$ | $C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$ |
| 10 | **Quality Factor (LC Tank)** | $Q = \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 R C}$ | Sharpness of resonance — higher Q, purer sine wave |
| 11 | **Resonant Frequency (LC Tank)** | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | Natural undamped oscillation frequency |
| 12 | **Damping Factor** | $\alpha = \dfrac{R}{2L}$ | Determines decay rate of natural response |

## 2.3 The Starting vs. Sustaining Condition

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU student error is to write **$\vert A\beta \vert = 1$** for *starting* oscillation. This is **WRONG**. Read carefully below.

| Stage | Condition | Physical Meaning |
|-------|-----------|------------------|
| **Starting (Build-up)** | $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$ | Loop gain amplifies the noise/voltage until amplitude grows |
| **Steady-State (Sustained)** | $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ | Energy supplied per cycle = Energy lost per cycle (balanced) |
| **Decaying (No Oscillation)** | $\vert A\beta \vert < 1$ | Loop loss exceeds loop gain; signal dies out exponentially |

The **non-linear saturation characteristics** of the active device (e.g., BJT cutoff and saturation, op-amp slew rate limiting) naturally compress the effective gain from the starting value to the steady-state unity condition.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** Carrier signal generation in AM/FFM transmitters, local oscillators in superheterodyne receivers.
- **Digital Systems:** Clock generation in microcontrollers (e.g., **ATmega328** uses a 16 MHz crystal; **STM32** uses 8 MHz HSI/4–72 MHz PLL).
- **Test & Measurement:** Function generators, audio frequency synthesizers.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Radio receivers, TV tuners, mobile phones.
- **Medical Electronics:** Ultrasonic transducers, MRI RF pulse generation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Barkhausen Criterion (From First Principles)

### Step 1 — Set Up the Block Diagram

The standard feedback amplifier block diagram consists of:
- An **amplifier** with open-loop gain $A$ (a positive real scalar in the ideal case).
- A **feedback network** with transfer function $\beta$ (also assumed real and positive for the simplest analysis).
- A **summing junction** that adds the external input $V_s$ and the feedback signal $V_f$.

The feedback signal is sampled from the output:

$$V_f = \beta \cdot V_o$$

The error signal (input to the amplifier) is:

$$V_i = V_s + V_f = V_s + \beta V_o$$

The amplifier output is:

$$V_o = A \cdot V_i$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $V_i$ into the Output Equation

$$V_o = A \cdot (V_s + \beta V_o)$$

### Step 3 — Expand and Group Like Terms

$$V_o = A \cdot V_s + A \cdot \beta \cdot V_o$$

$$V_o - A\beta V_o = A \cdot V_s$$

$$V_o (1 - A\beta) = A \cdot V_s$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the Closed-Loop Transfer Function $V_o / V_s$

$$\frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{A}{1 - A\beta}$$

This is the **fundamental feedback equation** valid for **all** feedback amplifiers (voltage, current, transconductance, transresistance).

### Step 5 — Apply the Oscillator Constraint ($V_s = 0$)

For an oscillator, there is **no external input**, so $V_s = 0$. Substituting this:

$$V_o (1 - A\beta) = A \cdot 0 = 0$$

For a **non-zero, sustained output** ($V_o \neq 0$), the only way this equation can be satisfied is if the multiplier of $V_o$ is **zero**:

$$1 - A\beta = 0$$

$$\boxed{A\beta = 1}$$

### Step 6 — Separate into Magnitude and Phase Parts

The equation $A\beta = 1$ is a complex equation. Equating real and imaginary parts (or magnitude and phase):

**Magnitude Condition:**

$$\vert A\beta \vert = 1$$

**Phase Condition:**

$$\angle A\beta = 0^\circ + n \cdot 360^\circ, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$

These two simultaneous conditions are the **Barkhausen Criterion**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Frequency of RC Phase Shift Oscillator (3-Section Network)

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation** and is included here in complete detail.

### Circuit Configuration

A common-emitter (CE) BJT amplifier (providing $180^\circ$ phase shift) is followed by an **RC ladder network** (3 cascaded RC sections). The ladder must provide an **additional $180^\circ$** phase shift to satisfy the total loop condition of $360^\circ$ (or $0^\circ$).

### Step 1 — Analyze the Single RC Section

For a single RC high-pass section with input $V_{in}$ and output $V_{out}$ across the resistor:

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{R}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}} = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

The phase contributed by this section is:

$$\phi = 90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$$

### Step 2 — Three Cascaded Sections

If all three RC sections are identical (each with $R$ and $C$), the total phase shift is $3 \times 60^\circ = 180^\circ$ at the **frequency of oscillation**. The transfer function of the entire ladder is:

$$\beta = \frac{V_f}{V_o} = \frac{1}{1 - \dfrac{5}{\omega^2 R^2 C^2} + j\left(\dfrac{1}{\omega^3 R^3 C^3} - \dfrac{6}{\omega RC}\right)}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Phase Condition ($\angle A\beta = 0$)

The CE amplifier contributes $180^\circ$. For the loop to have a total phase of $360^\circ$, the ladder must contribute $180^\circ$. The phase condition requires the **imaginary part of the denominator to be zero**:

$$\frac{1}{\omega^3 R^3 C^3} - \frac{6}{\omega RC} = 0$$

$$\frac{1}{\omega^2 R^2 C^2} = 6$$

$$\omega^2 R^2 C^2 = \frac{1}{6}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the Frequency of Oscillation

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Magnitude Condition ($\vert A\beta \vert = 1$)

The magnitude of the feedback fraction at the oscillation frequency:

$$\vert \beta \vert = \frac{1}{29}$$

Therefore, the minimum required voltage gain of the CE amplifier is:

$$A_v = \frac{1}{\vert \beta \vert} = 29$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:** If the BJT has $h_{fe} = 100$ but the bias network makes $A_v$ only 15, the oscillator **will not start**. Always check that $A_v \geq 29$ in the design.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Frequency of Wien Bridge Oscillator

### Step 1 — Wien Network Transfer Function

The Wien network consists of:
- **Series arm:** $R_1$ in series with $C_1$ (with $R_1 = R$ and $C_1 = C$).
- **Parallel arm:** $R_2$ in parallel with $C_2$ (with $R_2 = R$ and $C_2 = C$).

Impedances:

$$Z_s = R + \frac{1}{j\omega C} = \frac{1 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C}$$

$$Z_p = \frac{R \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}} = \frac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

### Step 2 — Voltage Divider Gives the Feedback Factor

$$\beta = \frac{V_f}{V_o} = \frac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}$$

Substituting and simplifying:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Phase Condition

For $\angle A\beta = 0$, the imaginary part of the denominator must be zero:

$$\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC} = 0$$

$$\omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 1$$

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Magnitude Condition

At $\omega = \omega_0$, the denominator reduces to $3$, so:

$$\vert \beta \vert = \frac{1}{3}$$

Therefore:

$$A_v = \frac{1}{\vert \beta \vert} = 3$$

This is achieved using a **non-inverting op-amp configuration** with $R_f / R_1 = 2$, giving $A_v = 1 + R_f/R_1 = 3$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: LC Tank Oscillation Frequency (Hartley & Colpitts Foundation)

For a parallel LC tank circuit with losses represented by a series resistance $R$ in the inductor branch:

### Step 1 — Write the KCL at the Tank Node

Admittances of the three parallel branches:
- Inductor branch: $Y_L = \dfrac{1}{R + j\omega L}$
- Capacitor branch: $Y_C = j\omega C$

Total admittance:

$$Y_{total} = j\omega C + \frac{1}{R + j\omega L}$$

### Step 2 — Resonance Condition (Imaginary Part Zero)

Setting $\text{Im}(Y_{total}) = 0$ and solving yields:

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}\sqrt{1 - \frac{R^2 C}{L}}$$

For **high-Q tanks** (where $R^2 \ll L/C$):

$$\boxed{\omega_0 \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$$

This is the **universal LC resonant frequency** used as the basis for both Hartley and Colpitts oscillator derivations.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Table (KTU 2024 High-Yield)

| Parameter | RC Phase Shift | Wien Bridge | Hartley | Colpitts |
|-----------|----------------|-------------|---------|----------|
| **Active Element** | BJT / Op-Amp | Op-Amp | BJT / FET | BJT / FET |
| **Frequency Range** | **1 Hz – 1 MHz** | **1 Hz – 1 MHz** | **30 kHz – 30 MHz** | **1 MHz – 500 MHz** |
| **Frequency Formula** | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_T C}}$ | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L C_{eq}}}$ |
| **Minimum Gain** | **29** | **3** | Depends on $\beta$ | Depends on $\beta$ |
| **Feedback Type** | Inverting (CE/CS) | Non-Inverting | Inductive divider | Capacitive divider |
| **Phase Shift by Feedback** | $180^\circ$ (3 RC sections) | $0^\circ$ (network is resistive at $f_0$) | $180^\circ$ (tank contributes) | $180^\circ$ (tank contributes) |
| **Typical Application** | Audio sine gen. | Audio test gen. | RF oscillators | RF/VHF oscillators |
| **Frequency Stability** | Poor | Moderate (good with AGC) | Good | Good |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Oscillator Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sourceNode[Noise or Startup Transient] --> summingJunction
    summingJunction{Σ Summing Junction} --> amplifierA
    amplifierA[Amplifier with Gain A] --> outputNode
    outputNode[Output Voltage Vo] --> loadZ[Load or Tank Circuit]
    outputNode --> feedbackBeta[Feedback Network with Transfer Function Beta]
    feedbackBeta --> summingJunction
```

> **Reading Guide:** The output is sampled, fed back through $\beta$, and re-added at the summing junction. Once oscillation starts, the noise source is effectively "switched off" by the closed loop.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Barkhausen Loop Condition Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Vin = 0 Oscillator Mode] --> node2[Error Signal Vi = Beta times Vo]
    node2 --> node3[Amplifier A produces Vo = A times Vi]
    node3 --> node4[Feedback Network Beta extracts Vf = Beta times Vo]
    node4 --> node5{Check Loop Condition A times Beta = 1}
    node5 --> node6[Sustained Steady State Oscillation]
    node5 --> node7[Signal Dies Out if A times Beta is less than 1]
    node5 --> node8[Signal Distorts if A times Beta is greater than 1]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — RC Phase Shift Oscillator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    transistorStage[CE BJT Amplifier 180 degree phase shift Gain of 29] --> rc1[RC Section 1 contributes 60 degrees]
    rc1 --> rc2[RC Section 2 contributes 60 degrees]
    rc2 --> rc3[RC Section 3 contributes 60 degrees]
    rc3 --> outputTerminal[Sinusoidal Output Terminal]
    outputTerminal --> feedbackReturn[Feedback to Base of BJT]
    feedbackReturn --> transistorStage
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Wien Bridge Oscillator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    opampNode[Non Inverting Op Amp Gain of 3] --> seriesArm[Series RC Arm R in series with C]
    seriesArm --> parallelArm[Parallel RC Arm R parallel to C]
    parallelArm --> groundNode[Ground]
    parallelArm --> feedbackPath[Feedback to Non Inverting Input of Op Amp]
    feedbackPath --> opampNode
    seriesArm --> outputTerminal[Output Vo sinusoidal]
```

## 4.5 Functional Architecture — Hartley vs Colpitts Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HartleyBlock[Hartley Oscillator]
        h1[Amplifier] --> h2[Inductor L1 part of tank]
        h2 --> h3[Inductor L2 with mutual coupling M]
        h3 --> h4[Capacitor C in tank]
        h4 --> h5[Feedback via center tap of inductors]
        h5 --> h1
    end
    subgraph ColpittsBlock[Colpitts Oscillator]
        c1[Amplifier] --> c2[Capacitor C1 part of tank]
        c2 --> c3[Capacitor C2 in series with C1]
        c3 --> c4[Inductor L in tank]
        c4 --> c5[Feedback via center tap of capacitors]
        c5 --> c1
    end
```

## 4.6 Decision Tree: Which Oscillator to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode{Required Frequency Range} --> lowFreq[Audio Range 1 Hz to 1 MHz]
    startNode --> highFreq[RF Range above 1 MHz]
    startNode --> xtalFreq[Fixed Precision Frequency]
    lowFreq --> rcChoice{Op Amp Available?}
    rcChoice --> yesOp[Yes Use Wien Bridge]
    rcChoice --> noOp[No Use RC Phase Shift with BJT]
    highFreq --> lcChoice{Variable Frequency Needed?}
    lcChoice --> yesVar[Yes Use Hartley for inductors or Colpitts for capacitors]
    lcChoice --> noVar[No Use Crystal Oscillator]
    xtalFreq --> xtalUse[Use Crystal Oscillator for high stability]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Barkhausen Criterion. State the two conditions necessary for sustained oscillation.**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 3 | 3 Marks | CO3, Bloom Level: Remember]**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Barkhausen Criterion (1921):** An electronic circuit will oscillate (produce a sustained sinusoidal output without external input) if and only if the following two conditions are simultaneously satisfied:
>
> **Condition 1 — Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain must equal unity:
> $$\vert A\beta \vert = 1$$
> This means the amplifier's gain $A$ multiplied by the feedback network's transfer function $\beta$ must have a magnitude of exactly 1, so the signal is neither amplified nor attenuated as it circulates the loop.
>
> **Condition 2 — Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the closed loop must be an integer multiple of $360^\circ$ (i.e., $0^\circ$):
> $$\angle A\beta = 0^\circ \text{ (or } 360^\circ, 720^\circ, \dots \text{)}$$
> This ensures the feedback signal is **in phase** with the input — that is, **positive (regenerative) feedback**.

---

### **Question 2: Classify sinusoidal oscillators based on the frequency-determining network and give one example of each.**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 3 | 3 Marks | CO3, Bloom Level: Understand]**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Sinusoidal oscillators are classified into **three main categories** based on the elements used in the frequency-determining network:
>
> | Class | Frequency-Determining Network | Frequency Range | Example |
> |-------|------------------------------|-----------------|---------|
> | **RC Oscillator** | Resistors and Capacitors | **1 Hz – 1 MHz** (Audio) | RC Phase Shift Oscillator, Wien Bridge Oscillator |
> | **LC Oscillator** | Inductors and Capacitors | **1 MHz – 500 MHz** (RF) | Hartley Oscillator, Colpitts Oscillator |
> | **Crystal Oscillator** | Piezoelectric Crystal (Quartz) | **1 kHz – 100 MHz** | Pierce Crystal Oscillator, Miller Oscillator |

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks Total)

### **Question A(a): Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation and the condition for sustained oscillation of a Wien Bridge Oscillator using an op-amp. [7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 3 | 7 Marks | CO3, Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Draw the equivalent circuit and identify components:**

The Wien Bridge oscillator uses a non-inverting op-amp configuration with a Wien network (series RC + parallel RC) in the positive feedback path and a resistive divider in the negative feedback path.

Series arm: $R$ in series with $C$
Parallel arm: $R$ in parallel with $C$

**Step 2 — Impedances of the Wien network:**

$$Z_s = R + \frac{1}{j\omega C} = \frac{1 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C}$$

$$Z_p = \frac{R \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}} = \frac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 3 — Feedback factor $\beta$ using voltage divider:**

$$\beta = \frac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\beta = \frac{\dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}{\dfrac{1 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C} + \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}$$

After algebraic simplification:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the phase condition for oscillation:**

For the loop phase to be zero, the imaginary part of the denominator must be zero:

$$\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC} = 0$$

$$\omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 1$$

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the magnitude condition:**

At $\omega = \omega_0$, the imaginary part vanishes and $\beta = \frac{1}{3}$. For $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$:

$$A = \frac{1}{\vert \beta \vert} = 3$$

For the non-inverting op-amp, $A = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 3$, so $\frac{R_f}{R_1} = 2$.

**Conclusion:** The Wien Bridge oscillator produces a clean sinusoidal output at $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ provided the non-inverting amplifier gain is exactly 3.

---

### **Question A(b): With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an RC Phase Shift Oscillator using BJT. Derive the expression for its frequency of oscillation. [7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 3 | 7 Marks | CO3, Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Description:**

A common-emitter (CE) BJT amplifier produces $180^\circ$ phase shift between base and collector. To satisfy the total loop phase condition of $360^\circ$, a **3-section RC ladder network** is connected from the collector back to the base, providing an additional $180^\circ$ phase shift.

**Step 2 — Identify Feedback Factor:**

The feedback network is a **cascaded high-pass RC ladder** with three identical RC sections. The total transfer function of this ladder is:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{1 - \dfrac{5}{\omega^2 R^2 C^2} + j\left(\dfrac{1}{\omega^3 R^3 C^3} - \dfrac{6}{\omega RC}\right)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Phase Condition:**

The CE amplifier provides $180^\circ$. For total loop phase to be $360^\circ$, the ladder must contribute another $180^\circ$. This requires the imaginary part of the denominator to be zero:

$$\frac{1}{\omega^3 R^3 C^3} - \frac{6}{\omega RC} = 0$$

$$\frac{1}{\omega^2 R^2 C^2} = 6$$

$$\omega^2 R^2 C^2 = \frac{1}{6}$$

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Magnitude Condition:**

Substituting $\omega_0$ back into the expression for $\beta$:

$$\vert \beta \vert = \frac{1}{29}$$

For $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$:

$$A_{v(min)} = 29$$

This is achieved by designing the CE amplifier with $R_C / R_E$ appropriately, ensuring $h_{fe} \geq 29$ for reliable start-up.

**Step 5 — Practical Design Note:**

The output amplitude is stabilized by the **non-linear saturation** of the BJT itself — as the signal grows, the device enters the cutoff or saturation region, effectively reducing the gain from a starting value (slightly > 29) to a steady-state value of exactly 29.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks Total — Alternative)

### **Question B(a): Explain the working of a Colpitts Oscillator with a neat circuit diagram. Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation. [7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Module 3 | 7 Marks | CO3, Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Topology:**

A Colpitts oscillator uses a **capacitive voltage divider** ($C_1$ and $C_2$ in series across the inductor $L$) as the frequency-determining network. The junction between $C_1$ and $C_2$ is grounded (or AC-grounded), and feedback is taken from the junction to the input of the amplifier.

**Step 2 — Effective Capacitance:**

Since $C_1$ and $C_2$ are in series in the tank loop:

$$C_{eq} = \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$$

**Step 3 — Frequency of Oscillation:**

Using the standard LC resonance formula $\omega_0 = 1 / \sqrt{L C_{eq}}$:

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L \cdot \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}}}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{C_1 + C_2}{L C_1 C_2}}}$$

**Step 4 — Condition for Sustained Oscillation:**

Using the Barkhausen criterion, the loop gain must satisfy $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$, where the feedback fraction is:

$$\beta = \frac{C_2}{C_1}$$

The minimum transistor $h_{fe}$ required is:

$$h_{fe(min)} = \frac{C_1}{C_2}$$

**Step 5 — Practical Advantages:**

- The capacitive divider provides **excellent high-frequency performance** (inductors become lossy at VHF; capacitors do not).
- Used in **RF signal generators, VHF transmitters, and TV tuners**.

---

### **Question B(b): Draw the circuit of a Hartley Oscillator using BJT and derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation. Explain how the Barkhausen criterion is satisfied. [7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 | Module 3 | 7 Marks | CO3, Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Description:**

A Hartley oscillator uses a **tapped inductor** ($L_1$ and $L_2$ with mutual inductance $M$) and a single capacitor $C$ as the tank circuit. The center tap is grounded (AC), and feedback is taken from the junction of $L_1$ and $L_2$ to the base of the BJT (or FET).

**Step 2 — Total Tank Inductance:**

The two inductor sections are in series in the tank loop. With mutual coupling:

$$L_T = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$$

(If mutual inductance is small or coils are far apart, $M \approx 0$ and $L_T = L_1 + L_2$.)

**Step 3 — Frequency of Oscillation:**

Applying the LC resonance formula:

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L_T C}}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(L_1 + L_2 + 2M)C}}}$$

**Step 4 — Feedback Factor:**

The inductive voltage divider provides feedback:

$$\beta = \frac{L_2 + M}{L_1 + M}$$

**Step 5 — Barkhausen Criterion Satisfaction:**

- **Phase condition:** The tank circuit acts as a parallel resonant circuit. At resonance, the impedance is purely resistive (no phase shift across the tank). Combined with the $180^\circ$ phase shift of the CE amplifier, the total loop phase is exactly $360^\circ$.
- **Magnitude condition:** The transistor gain $A_v$ must be at least $1/\vert \beta \vert$. With proper biasing and $\vert A_v \vert \geq (L_1 + M)/(L_2 + M)$, the oscillator sustains steady-state oscillation.

**Step 6 — Application Note:**

Hartley oscillators are widely used in **radio receivers** (especially for the local oscillator in AM broadcast band reception, ~540 kHz to 1650 kHz).

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **MOST COMMON MISTAKES (Where KTU Students Lose 3–5 Marks Per Question):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{6}$ factor** in the RC Phase Shift frequency formula. Memorize: $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ — this is the most-skipped factor in 14-mark derivations.
>
> 2. **Stating $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ for "starting" oscillation.** This is the *steady-state* condition. The *starting* condition is $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$, with the active device's nonlinearity automatically reducing the effective gain to unity in steady state.
>
> 3. **Phase condition written as $180^\circ$ instead of $360^\circ$ (or $0°$).** The total loop phase is $360°$ (or $0°$ equivalent). The $180°$ comes from the amplifier and the *other* $180°$ from the feedback network.
>
> 4. **Missing units in the final answer.** KTU examiners *will* deduct 0.5 mark for writing $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ without specifying **Hz**.
>
> 5. **Not mentioning the role of the active device's non-linearity** in stabilizing the amplitude. This is a 2-mark concept question that KTU frequently tests in Part A.
>
> 6. **Mixing up Hartley and Colpitts formulas.** Easy mnemonic: **H**artley has a tapped **H**inductor (so the formula has $L_T$ = sum of inductors); **C**olpitts has a tapped **C**apacitor (so the formula has $C_{eq}$ = series combination of $C_1, C_2$).
>
> 7. **No circuit diagram in 7-mark derivation questions.** KTU valuation keys explicitly state: *1 mark is reserved for a neat, labeled circuit diagram*. Skipping it is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Barkhausen Criterion:** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ AND $\angle A\beta = 0° \text{ (or } 360°\text{)}$
- ⭐ **Starting condition:** $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$ (with non-linearity clamping the amplitude)
- ⭐ **RC Phase Shift:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$, $A_{v(min)} = 29$
- ⭐ **Wien Bridge:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$, $A_{v(min)} = 3$
- ⭐ **Hartley:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(L_1 + L_2 + 2M)C}}$, inductive divider
- ⭐ **Colpitts:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{C_1 + C_2}{L C_1 C_2}}$, capacitive divider
- ⭐ **Universal LC resonance:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC_{eq}}}$ (high-Q approximation)
- ⭐ **RC oscillators** are used for **audio frequencies** (1 Hz – 1 MHz)
- ⭐ **LC oscillators** are used for **RF frequencies** (1 MHz – 500 MHz)
- ⭐ **Crystal oscillators** offer the **highest frequency stability** (used in digital clocks)
- ⭐ **Positive feedback (regenerative)** is the *signature* of all oscillators — the opposite of amplifier negative feedback
- ⭐ The **amplifier's non-linearity** is what stabilizes the amplitude in steady state
- ⭐ The **Q-factor of the tank circuit** determines the *purity* of the sine wave: higher Q means narrower bandwidth, purer sine output
- ⭐ A **buffer stage** (emitter follower) is often used at the oscillator output to prevent load changes from affecting the frequency
- ⭐ **Pierce crystal oscillator** is the most common crystal topology in modern digital ICs (microcontrollers, watches, radios)
- ⭐ KTU's *most-asked* oscillator question across the last 5 years: **Wien Bridge** followed by **RC Phase Shift**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wien Bridge Oscillator, Hartley and Crystal Oscillator (Working Principle and Design Equations)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wien Bridge Oscillator, Hartley and Crystal Oscillator (Working Principle and Design Equations)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Oscillators — The Foundation

An **electronic oscillator** is a circuit that converts DC energy from a power supply into a periodic, oscillating AC output signal (typically sinusoidal) without requiring any external AC input signal. The output is sustained purely by positive feedback and the amplifying action of an active device (BJT, FET, or op-amp).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**  
> An oscillator is a signal generator that produces a repetitive waveform using a frequency-selective feedback network and an amplifier satisfying the **Barkhausen Criterion** — a closed-loop gain of exactly unity ($|A\beta| = 1$) with a total phase shift of $0°$ (or $360°$) around the loop.

### 1.2 The Barkhausen Criterion — Heart of Every Oscillator

For any sinusoidal oscillator to produce sustained oscillations, two simultaneous conditions must be satisfied:

1. **Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain must equal unity.  
$$|A\beta| = 1$$

2. **Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the closed loop must be an integer multiple of $360°$ (i.e., $0°$ or $2n\pi$ radians).  
$$\angle A\beta = 0° \text{ or } 360°$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Barkhausen Explained Simply):**  
> Think of pushing a child on a swing. To keep the swing going indefinitely:
> - You must **push in the same direction** as the swing's motion (Phase condition — feedback must be regenerative/positive).
> - The **size of each push** must exactly compensate for energy lost to air drag and friction (Magnitude condition — no net gain, no net loss).  
> If you push too hard → oscillations grow uncontrollably (saturation). If you push too softly → oscillations die out.

### 1.3 Classification of Sinusoidal Oscillators

| Type | Frequency Range | Feedback Network | Active Device |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RC Oscillator** (Wien Bridge) | 1 Hz to 1 MHz | Resistor-Capacitor | Op-amp / BJT |
| **LC Oscillator** (Hartley, Colpitts) | 100 kHz to several GHz | Inductor-Capacitor tank | BJT / FET |
| **Crystal Oscillator** | Fixed (kHz to MHz) | Quartz crystal (piezoelectric) | BJT / FET / Op-amp |

---

## 1.4 Wien Bridge Oscillator — Definition

The **Wien Bridge Oscillator** is a low-frequency, audio-range RC feedback oscillator that uses a **lead-lag network** as its frequency-determining element. It generates a stable, low-distortion sinusoidal waveform and is widely used in audio function generators (typically 20 Hz to 20 kHz).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Wien Bridge is the most commonly asked oscillator in KTU exams because of its elegant op-amp design, simple frequency equation, and easily derivable gain requirement of **A = 3**.

### 1.5 Intuitive Analogy — The Wien Bridge as a Tuning Fork

Imagine a tuning fork vibrating at exactly 440 Hz. A Wien Bridge oscillator works similarly — the **RC lead-lag network** acts like a mechanical resonator that "prefers" to oscillate at exactly one specific frequency ($f_0$). The op-amp provides just enough energy to overcome circuit losses, sustaining the vibration indefinitely. Change the R or C values, and it's like changing the length of the tuning fork — the pitch (frequency) changes.

---

## 1.6 Hartley Oscillator — Definition

The **Hartley Oscillator** is a high-frequency LC oscillator that uses a **tapped inductor** (or two inductors with mutual coupling) in parallel with a single capacitor to form the tank circuit. The feedback is achieved through the inductive voltage divider.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**  
> Think of a closed pipe with water inside. The water sloshing back and forth (oscillation) depends on the **pipe length (inductance)** and the **water's surface area (capacitance)**. In the Hartley, the **tapped inductor** divides the feedback voltage — analogous to tapping the pipe at a specific point to extract feedback energy.

---

## 1.7 Crystal Oscillator — Definition

A **Crystal Oscillator** uses a **piezoelectric quartz crystal** as the frequency-determining element. The crystal exhibits the **piezoelectric effect** — it mechanically deforms when a voltage is applied and conversely generates a voltage when mechanically stressed. The crystal behaves electrically as a highly stable, high-Q resonant circuit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Crystals?**  
> - **Frequency stability** of up to **1 part in 10¹⁰** (unmatched by any RC/LC oscillator).
> - **High Q-factor** (10,000 to 100,000) — extremely narrow bandwidth.
> - Used in **microcontrollers, watches, communication transceivers, computers, and digital clocks**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**  
> A quartz crystal is like a perfectly tuned wine glass. Strike it, and it rings at **one very pure note** for a long time (high Q). The crystal oscillator essentially "keeps striking" the glass electronically at exactly the right moment to keep the note ringing forever.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Wien Bridge Oscillator — Detailed Working

### 2.1.1 Circuit Topology
A Wien Bridge Oscillator consists of:
- An **op-amp** in non-inverting amplifier configuration.
- A **lead-lag RC network** in the positive feedback path.
- A **voltage divider** (resistors $R_1$ and $R_f$) in the negative feedback path.

### 2.1.2 Frequency-Selective Feedback Network

The feedback network consists of two cascaded RC sections:
- **Series RC** arm: $R$ in series with $C$ (acts as a **high-pass filter** — leads the phase).
- **Parallel RC** arm: $R$ in parallel with $C$ (acts as a **low-pass filter** — lags the phase).

At the **resonant (oscillation) frequency**, the phase shift through this network is **zero**, and the attenuation is **minimum (1/3)**.

### 2.1.3 Derivation of Feedback Factor ($\beta$)

Let $Z_1 = R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}$ (series arm) and $Z_2 = R \parallel \frac{1}{j\omega C} = \frac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$ (parallel arm).

The feedback factor is the voltage divider ratio:

$$\beta = \frac{V_f}{V_o} = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}$$

Substituting and simplifying (as shown in Section 3):

$$\beta = \frac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}$$

### 2.1.4 Oscillation Frequency and Gain Requirement

For **zero phase shift** (imaginary part = 0):
$$\omega RC = \frac{1}{\omega RC}$$

At this frequency, $\beta$ becomes purely real:
$$\beta = \frac{1}{3}$$

By the **Barkhausen Criterion**, the amplifier must provide:
$$A = \frac{1}{\beta} = 3$$

The non-inverting amplifier gain is:
$$A = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 3 \implies R_f = 2R_1$$

---

## 2.2 Hartley Oscillator — Detailed Working

### 2.2.1 Circuit Topology
A Hartley Oscillator uses:
- A **common-emitter (or common-source) amplifier**.
- A **tank circuit** consisting of two inductors $L_1$ and $L_2$ (with mutual inductance $M$) in series, in parallel with a capacitor $C$.
- A **capacitive coupling** (or transformer coupling) for the output.

### 2.2.2 Feedback Mechanism
The voltage across $L_2$ is fed back to the input. The feedback factor is the inductive divider ratio:
$$\beta = \frac{L_2 + M}{L_1 + M}$$

### 2.2.3 Frequency of Oscillation

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_T C}}$$

where $L_T = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$ is the **total effective inductance** (with mutual coupling assumed aiding).

---

## 2.3 Crystal Oscillator — Detailed Working

### 2.3.1 Quartz Crystal — Electrical Equivalent Circuit

A quartz crystal, when modeled electrically, has:
- **$L_s$** — motional inductance (very small, ~0.001 to 1 H).
- **$C_s$** — motional capacitance (very small, ~0.01 to 10 pF).
- **$R_s$** — series resistance (very small, represents losses).
- **$C_p$** — shunt/holder capacitance (a few pF).

### 2.3.2 Two Resonance Frequencies

The crystal has **two distinct resonant frequencies**:

1. **Series Resonance** ($f_s$): When $L_s$ and $C_s$ cancel out ($X_L = X_C$), the crystal impedance is **purely resistive** and minimum (= $R_s$).
$$f_s = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_s C_s}}$$

2. **Parallel Resonance** ($f_p$): When the crystal appears as a high-Q inductor resonating with $C_p$:
$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_s \cdot C_{eq}}}$$
where $C_{eq} = \frac{C_s C_p}{C_s + C_p}$

Since $C_p \gg C_s$, we have $C_{eq} \approx C_s$, and therefore:
$$f_p \approx f_s \quad \text{(very close, but slightly higher)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The crystal is **inductive** between $f_s$ and $f_p$. Oscillators are designed to operate in this region, replacing the inductive element in an LC tank.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Oscillator | Frequency Equation | Gain / $\beta$ Condition | Active Device |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Wien Bridge** | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3}$, $A = 3$ | Op-amp |
| 2 | **Hartley** | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_T C}}$, $L_T = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$ | $\beta = \dfrac{L_2 + M}{L_1 + M}$ | BJT/FET |
| 3 | **Colpitts** (related) | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L C_{eq}}}$, $C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$ | $\beta = \dfrac{C_1}{C_2}$ | BJT/FET |
| 4 | **Crystal (Series)** | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_s C_s}}$ | Crystal acts as $R$ | BJT/FET |
| 5 | **Crystal (Parallel)** | $f_p \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_s C_{eq}}}$ | Crystal acts as $L$ | BJT/FET |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **Wien Bridge:** Audio frequency generators, function generators, sine wave inverters, audio testing equipment.
> - **Hartley:** RF oscillators in radio transmitters (550 kHz to 30 MHz), local oscillators in superheterodyne receivers.
> - **Crystal:** Microprocessor clock circuits, digital watches, GPS modules, communication transceivers, cellular base stations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION 1 — Wien Bridge Oscillator Frequency

### Step 1: Define the impedances of the feedback network

The Wien Bridge lead-lag network has two arms:

- **Series arm:** $Z_1 = R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$
- **Parallel arm:** $Z_2 = R \parallel \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$

### Step 2: Simplify $Z_2$ (parallel combination)

$$Z_2 = \frac{R \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}} = \frac{\frac{R}{j\omega C}}{\frac{j\omega RC + 1}{j\omega C}} = \frac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

### Step 3: Write the feedback factor $\beta$ (voltage divider)

$$\beta = \frac{V_f}{V_o} = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}$$

Substituting $Z_1$ and $Z_2$:

$$\beta = \frac{\frac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}{R + \frac{1}{j\omega C} + \frac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}$$

### Step 4: Multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$ to eliminate fractions

$$\beta = \frac{\frac{R \cdot j\omega C}{1 + j\omega RC}}{j\omega RC \cdot R + 1 + \frac{j\omega RC \cdot R}{1 + j\omega RC}}$$

Wait — let us use a cleaner common denominator approach.

**Cleaner approach:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 + j\omega RC)$:

$$\beta = \frac{R}{R(1 + j\omega RC) + \frac{(1 + j\omega RC)}{j\omega C}}$$

Hmm, this is getting unwieldy. Let me use the standard textbook technique.

### Step 5: Standard simplification

Multiply top and bottom by $j\omega C$:

$$\beta = \frac{R}{\left(R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}\right)(1 + j\omega RC) + R}$$

Expand the denominator:

$$D = R(1 + j\omega RC) + \frac{1 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C} + R$$

$$\frac{1 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C} = \frac{1}{j\omega C} + R = -\frac{j}{\omega C} + R$$

Wait, simpler form:

$$\frac{1 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C} = \frac{1}{j\omega C} + R = R - \frac{j}{\omega C}$$

So denominator becomes:

$$D = R + j\omega R^2 C + R - \frac{j}{\omega C} + R = 3R + j\left(\omega R^2 C - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)$$

### Step 6: Final form of $\beta$

$$\boxed{\beta = \frac{R}{3R + j\left(\omega R^2 C - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)}}$$

Factor out $R$ from the denominator:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}$$

### Step 7: Apply the phase condition (Barkhausen)

For zero phase shift, the imaginary part of the denominator must equal zero:

$$\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC} = 0$$

$$\omega RC = \frac{1}{\omega RC}$$

$$\omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 1$$

$$\omega_o = \frac{1}{RC}$$

### Step 8: Convert to frequency in Hertz

$$f_o = \frac{\omega_o}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

### Step 9: Apply the magnitude condition

At $\omega = \omega_o$, the imaginary part vanishes, so:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{3}$$

For Barkhausen: $|A\beta| = 1$

$$A = \frac{1}{\beta} = 3$$

For the non-inverting op-amp:

$$A = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 3 \implies R_f = 2R_1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example:** If $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 10\,\text{nF}$:
> $$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 10^4 \cdot 10^{-8}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 10^{-4}} \approx 1591.55\,\text{Hz} \approx 1.59\,\text{kHz}$$
> Choose $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, then $R_f = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$.

---

## 3.2 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION 2 — Hartley Oscillator Frequency

### Step 1: Tank circuit configuration

In a Hartley oscillator, the tank circuit consists of $L_1$, $L_2$ (with mutual inductance $M$), and $C$ all in parallel from an AC perspective.

### Step 2: Resonance condition

At the oscillation frequency, the inductive reactance of the total inductance equals the capacitive reactance:

$$\omega L_T = \frac{1}{\omega C}$$

where $L_T$ is the total effective inductance of $L_1$ and $L_2$ (with mutual coupling assumed aiding):

$$L_T = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$$

### Step 3: Solve for $\omega_o$

$$\omega_o^2 = \frac{1}{L_T C}$$

$$\omega_o = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L_T C}}$$

### Step 4: Convert to frequency in Hertz

$$\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(L_1 + L_2 + 2M) C}}}$$

### Step 5: Feedback factor

The feedback factor (ratio of feedback voltage to output voltage) is determined by the inductive divider:

$$\beta = \frac{V_f}{V_o} = \frac{L_2 + M}{L_1 + M}$$

For oscillations, the Barkhausen criterion requires the amplifier to provide gain $A \geq \frac{1}{\beta}$ to compensate losses and start oscillations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example:** If $L_1 = 100\,\mu\text{H}$, $L_2 = 100\,\mu\text{H}$, $M = 20\,\mu\text{H}$, $C = 1\,\text{nF}$:
> $$L_T = 100 + 100 + 2(20) = 240\,\mu\text{H}$$
> $$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{240 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1 \times 10^{-9}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{2.4 \times 10^{-13}}} \approx 10.3\,\text{MHz}$$

---

## 3.3 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION 3 — Crystal Oscillator Resonant Frequencies

### Step 1: Equivalent circuit impedance

A quartz crystal has the equivalent circuit with $L_s$, $C_s$, $R_s$ in series, with $C_p$ in parallel. The total impedance is:

$$Z = \frac{(R_s + j\omega L_s + \frac{1}{j\omega C_s}) \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C_p}}{R_s + j\omega L_s + \frac{1}{j\omega C_s} + \frac{1}{j\omega C_p}}$$

Since $R_s$ is very small (negligible), we drop it for the resonant frequency calculation:

$$Z \approx \frac{(j\omega L_s + \frac{1}{j\omega C_s}) \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C_p}}{j\omega L_s + \frac{1}{j\omega C_s} + \frac{1}{j\omega C_p}}$$

### Step 2: Series resonance frequency

Series resonance occurs when the series branch's reactance is zero:

$$j\omega_s L_s + \frac{1}{j\omega_s C_s} = 0$$

$$\omega_s L_s = \frac{1}{\omega_s C_s}$$

$$\omega_s^2 = \frac{1}{L_s C_s}$$

$$\boxed{f_s = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_s C_s}}}$$

At $f_s$, the impedance is **purely real and minimum** ($Z = R_s$).

### Step 3: Parallel resonance frequency

Parallel resonance occurs when the overall impedance is infinite (open circuit). This happens when the denominator equals zero:

$$j\omega_p L_s + \frac{1}{j\omega_p C_s} + \frac{1}{j\omega_p C_p} = 0$$

Divide by $j\omega_p$:

$$L_s - \frac{1}{\omega_p^2 C_s} - \frac{1}{\omega_p^2 C_p} = 0$$

$$L_s = \frac{1}{\omega_p^2}\left(\frac{1}{C_s} + \frac{1}{C_p}\right)$$

$$\omega_p^2 = \frac{1}{L_s} \cdot \frac{C_s + C_p}{C_s C_p} = \frac{1}{L_s \cdot C_{eq}}$$

where $C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_s C_p}{C_s + C_p}$

$$\boxed{f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_s \cdot C_{eq}}}}$$

### Step 4: Relate $f_p$ and $f_s$

Since $C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_s C_p}{C_s + C_p}$ and $C_p \gg C_s$:

$$C_{eq} = \frac{C_s \cdot C_p}{C_s + C_p} < C_s$$

Therefore:

$$f_p > f_s$$

$$f_p \approx f_s \left(1 + \frac{C_s}{2C_p}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example:** A 10 MHz crystal with $L_s = 2.5\,\text{mH}$, $C_s = 0.01\,\text{pF}$, $C_p = 5\,\text{pF}$:
> $$f_s = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 10^{-14}}} \approx 10.06\,\text{MHz}$$
> $$C_{eq} = \frac{0.01 \times 5}{0.01 + 5} \approx 0.00998\,\text{pF}$$
> $$f_p \approx \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 9.98 \times 10^{-15}}} \approx 10.07\,\text{MHz}$$
> The **gap between $f_s$ and $f_p$ is only ~10 kHz** — extremely narrow band!
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
