# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Q:** Define the **Barkhausen Criterion** for sustained oscillations. State the two conditions mathematically.

**Model Answer:**

The Barkhausen Criterion specifies the conditions under which a feedback amplifier will operate as a sinusoidal oscillator. For sustained oscillations, the following two conditions must be simultaneously satisfied:

1. **Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain must equal unity.
$$|A\beta| = 1$$

2. **Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the closed loop must be an integer multiple of $360°$ (i.e., $0°$ or $2n\pi$).
$$\angle A\beta = 0° \text{ or } n \times 360°, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

When both conditions are met, the circuit oscillates at the frequency where the phase condition holds.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q:** Compare **Wien Bridge Oscillator** and **Hartley Oscillator** based on (i) frequency-determining elements, (ii) frequency range, and (iii) typical applications.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Wien Bridge Oscillator | Hartley Oscillator |
|---|---|---|
| **Frequency-determining elements** | RC lead-lag network | LC tank circuit |
| **Frequency range** | Low (1 Hz to 1 MHz, audio range) | High (100 kHz to several MHz) |
| **Feedback type** | Voltage divider (resistive) | Inductive divider (tapped coil) |
| **Active device** | Op-amp (typically) | BJT / FET |
| **Typical applications** | Audio generators, function generators | RF transmitters, local oscillators |
| **Frequency stability** | Moderate | Better than RC, less than crystal |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

**Q (a)** Derive the expression for the **frequency of oscillation** of a Wien Bridge Oscillator using an op-amp. Show that the amplifier gain must be **3** for sustained oscillations. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** Design a Wien Bridge Oscillator to generate a **2 kHz** sine wave using an op-amp. Choose suitable values of R, C, $R_1$, and $R_f$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q (a):

**Step 1:** The Wien Bridge oscillator uses a series RC arm ($Z_1 = R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}$) and a parallel RC arm ($Z_2 = \frac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$).

**Step 2:** The feedback factor is:

$$\beta = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2} = \frac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}$$

**Step 3:** For zero phase shift (Barkhausen phase condition):

$$\omega_o RC - \frac{1}{\omega_o RC} = 0 \implies \omega_o = \frac{1}{RC}$$

$$\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$

**Step 4:** At $\omega_o$, the feedback factor becomes real: $\beta = \frac{1}{3}$.

**Step 5:** From the magnitude condition, $A = \frac{1}{\beta} = 3$.

**Step 6:** Since the op-amp is in non-inverting mode: $A = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 3 \implies R_f = 2R_1$.

---

#### Model Solution for Q (b):

**Given:** $f_o = 2\,\text{kHz} = 2000\,\text{Hz}$.

**Step 1:** Choose $C = 10\,\text{nF}$ (a practical value).

**Step 2:** Calculate R from $f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$:

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_o C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{1.2566 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 7957\,\Omega \approx 7.96\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3:** Use standard value $R = 8.2\,\text{k}\Omega$ (E12 series).

**Step 4:** For the gain resistors, choose $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, then $R_f = 2R_1 = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$.

**Final Design Values:**
- $R = 8.2\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C = 10\,\text{nF}$
- $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_f = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**Q (a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the **working of a Hartley Oscillator**. Derive the expression for its frequency of oscillation. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** A Hartley Oscillator has $L_1 = 100\,\mu\text{H}$, $L_2 = 200\,\mu\text{H}$, mutual inductance $M = 50\,\mu\text{H}$, and $C = 1000\,\text{pF}$. Calculate the **frequency of oscillation** and the **feedback factor**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q (a):

**Circuit Description:**
A Hartley Oscillator uses a common-emitter BJT amplifier with an LC tank circuit. The tank circuit consists of two inductors $L_1$ and $L_2$ in series (with mutual inductance $M$), in parallel with a capacitor $C$. The voltage developed across $L_2$ is fed back to the base of the transistor through a coupling capacitor.

**Working Principle:**
1. The amplifier provides $180°$ phase shift between base and collector.
2. The tank circuit (between collector and emitter via the capacitive divider) provides an **additional $180°$ phase shift** at the resonant frequency, making the total loop phase shift $360°$ (regenerative feedback).
3. The inductive voltage divider ($L_1$ and $L_2$) determines the feedback fraction.

**Frequency Derivation:**

Total inductance: $L_T = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$

At resonance, inductive reactance = capacitive reactance:

$$\omega_o L_T = \frac{1}{\omega_o C}$$

$$\omega_o = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L_T C}}$$

$$\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(L_1 + L_2 + 2M)C}}}$$

**Feedback Factor:**

$$\beta = \frac{L_2 + M}{L_1 + M}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q (b):

**Given:** $L_1 = 100\,\mu\text{H}$, $L_2 = 200\,\mu\text{H}$, $M = 50\,\mu\text{H}$, $C = 1000\,\text{pF} = 1\,\text{nF}$.

**Step 1: Total Inductance**

$$L_T = L_1 + L_2 + 2M = 100 + 200 + 2(50) = 400\,\mu\text{H}$$

**Step 2: Frequency of Oscillation**

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_T C}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{400 \times 10^{-6} \times 1000 \times 10^{-12}}}$$

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{4 \times 10^{-16}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$f_o = \frac{1}{1.2566 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 7.958\,\text{MHz}$$

$$\boxed{f_o \approx 7.96\,\text{MHz}}$$

**Step 3: Feedback Factor**

$$\beta = \frac{L_2 + M}{L_1 + M} = \frac{200 + 50}{100 + 50} = \frac{250}{150} = \frac{5}{3} \approx 1.667$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of Mutual Inductance:** Students often forget to add $2M$ in $L_T$. If mutual coupling is opposing (anti-series), use $L_T = L_1 + L_2 - 2M$. Always check the dot convention in the circuit!
> 2. **Wien Bridge Gain Mistake:** Many students write $A = 1 + \frac{R_1}{R_f} = 3$ — the correct formula is $A = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 3$. Mixing this up costs **2 marks instantly**.
> 3. **Crystal Resonance Confusion:** Students often confuse $f_s$ and $f_p$ — remember $f_p > f_s$, and the gap is typically less than 1% of the frequency.
> 4. **Barkhausen Condition:** Writing only $|A\beta| = 1$ without the phase condition is incomplete — both conditions are mandatory for full marks.
> 5. **Units in Derivation:** Forgetting units or not converting $\mu\text{H}$ to H, pF to F, leads to a numerical error. KTU examiners **strictly deduct** for unit mistakes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision Points:**

### Wien Bridge Oscillator
- $\boxed{f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}}$ — **memorize this formula**
- $\boxed{\beta = \dfrac{1}{3}, \quad A = 3}$ — **must be remembered as a pair**
- Feedback network is a **lead-lag RC** configuration
- Op-amp must be in **non-inverting** mode
- $R_f = 2R_1$ — gain-setting resistor relation
- Used for **audio frequencies** (20 Hz to 20 kHz)
- Phase shift of lead-lag network = $0°$ at $f_o$

### Hartley Oscillator
- $\boxed{f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(L_1 + L_2 + 2M)C}}}$ — **the $2M$ term is critical**
- Tank circuit: two inductors + one capacitor
- $\boxed{\beta = \dfrac{L_2 + M}{L_1 + M}}$ — inductive divider
- Active device: BJT or FET (common emitter/source)
- Used for **RF frequencies** (100 kHz to tens of MHz)
- Easy to tune by varying $C$

### Crystal Oscillator
- Quartz crystal exhibits **piezoelectric effect**
- Equivalent circuit: $L_s$, $C_s$, $R_s$ in series, with $C_p$ in parallel
- Two resonant frequencies: $\boxed{f_s = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_s C_s}}}$ and $\boxed{f_p = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_s C_{eq}}}}$
- $f_p > f_s$ (always); $f_p \approx f_s \left(1 + \dfrac{C_s}{2C_p}\right)$
- Between $f_s$ and $f_p$: crystal is **inductive** (operating region for oscillators)
- Highest frequency stability: **Q-factor of 10,000 to 100,000**
- Applications: **clocks, watches, microcontrollers, communication systems**

### Barkhausen Criterion (Universal)
- **Magnitude:** $|A\beta| = 1$
- **Phase:** $\angle A\beta = 0°$ or multiples of $360°$
- Both conditions are **simultaneously** required
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Wien Bridge Oscillator Only Required
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Analysis of Wien Bridge Oscillator Only Required ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Wien Bridge Oscillator** is a low-frequency sinusoidal oscillator that generates stable, low-distortion sine waves using an operational amplifier with a **Wien bridge feedback network** in the positive feedback path and a **resistive voltage divider** in the negative feedback path. It is the most widely used audio-frequency oscillator (range: **1 Hz to 1 MHz**) and is the standard topology in commercial function/signal generators. The frequency-determining element is the **Wien bridge network** — a frequency-selective RC network composed of a **series RC arm** ($Z_s = R_1 + 1/j\omega C_1$) and a **parallel RC arm** ($Z_p = R_2 \parallel 1/j\omega C_2$). At the **balance (resonant) frequency**, the network produces a real, phase-zero feedback factor of $\beta = 1/3$, requiring the non-inverting amplifier gain to be exactly $A = 3$ for sustained oscillation as per the **Barkhausen Criterion**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board High-Yield Concept:** The Wien bridge oscillator is the *only* standard op-amp oscillator that uses a **non-inverting** configuration. The phase-shift oscillator and twin-T oscillator use inverting configurations. This is the most frequently asked 2-mark concept. ### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine pushing a child on a swing (the swing is the **op-amp + tuned network**). To keep the swing going, you must push at exactly the right **timing** (frequency match) and with just enough **force** (gain = 3). Push too early or too late (wrong frequency) → swing dies. Push too hard (gain > 3) → swing goes crazy (distortion). Push too softly (gain < 3) → swing stops. The **Wien bridge** is the "rhythm detector" that tells the op-amp *when* to push, and the **resistive divider** (R₆, R₇) is the "volume knob" that sets *how hard* the op-amp pushes. The swing's natural rhythm is $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$ — the frequency of oscillation. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world fact:** The Wien bridge oscillator is the heart of every laboratory **function generator**, **audio signal generator**, and **beat-frequency oscillator (BFO)**. HP's original 200A audio oscillator (1939) used this exact topology with a small incandescent lamp for automatic gain control. --- **Key Standard Metrics to Remember:** - **Frequency range:** **1 Hz – 1 MHz** (audio to low-RF band) - **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD):** Typically **< 0.1%** (best of all RC oscillators) - **Standard gain condition:** $A \geq 3$ (with $A = 3$ for ideal sustained oscillation) - **Feedback factor at $f_0$:** $\beta = 1/3$ (real, positive) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Block Logic (Why the Wien Bridge Works) The Wien bridge oscillator is a **two-port feedback system** built around an op-amp: 1. **Forward Path (Amplifier):** A non-inverting op-amp with gain $A = 1 + R_f/R_g$ (where $R_f$ is the feedback resistor and $R_g$ is the resistor from inverting input to ground). 2. **Feedback Path (Wien Network):** A series-parallel RC ladder that acts as a **band-pass filter** — it passes only one specific frequency with **zero phase shift** and a magnitude of exactly $1/3$. The op-amp is wired as a **non-inverting amplifier** (its non-inverting input receives $\beta V_{out}$ from the Wien network). This guarantees $0°$ phase shift in the forward path. The total loop phase is therefore $0° + 0° = 0°$ — satisfying the Barkhausen phase condition *only* at the resonant frequency $f_0$. ### 2.2 The Barkhausen Criterion (Foundation Principle) For sustained sinusoidal oscillation, two conditions must be simultaneously satisfied: - **Loop Gain Condition:** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ (magnitude product equals unity) - **Loop Phase Condition:** $\angle A\beta = 0° \text{ or } 360°$ (total phase around the loop is zero) > [!NOTE] > For *startup*, the loop gain must be **slightly greater than 1** ($\vert A\beta \vert > 1$) so the oscillation can grow from noise. Once the desired amplitude is reached, an **automatic gain control (AGC)** mechanism reduces the gain back to exactly 1 for stable operation. Common AGC devices: thermistor, JFET (as VCR), incandescent lamp, or back-to-back Zener diodes. ### 2.3 Why the Non-Inverting Configuration? The Wien network naturally produces a **positive** feedback factor (real, positive) at $f_0$. The op-amp must therefore have **non-inverting** polarity (positive gain) so that the total loop phase remains zero. If we used an inverting configuration (gain = –R₃/R₄), the 180° phase shift would need to be compensated by the network producing 180° — which the Wien bridge does *not* do. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Resonant (oscillation) frequency, general | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ | Hz | | Resonant frequency (symmetric case) | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | Hz, valid when $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$ | | Series arm impedance | $Z_s = R_1 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_1} = \dfrac{1 + j\omega R_1 C_1}{j\omega C_1}$ | Ohms | | Parallel arm impedance | $Z_p = \dfrac{R_2}{1 + j\omega R_2 C_2}$ | Ohms | | Feedback factor (general) | $\beta(j\omega) = \dfrac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}$ | Dimensionless | | Feedback factor (symmetric, at $f_0$) | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3}$ | Real, positive | | Loop gain condition | $\vert A \beta \vert = 1$ | Barkhausen criterion | | Required amplifier gain | $A = 3$ | For symmetric Wien bridge | | Non-inverting amplifier gain | $A = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_g}$ | Standard op-amp equation | | Resistor ratio for sustained oscillation | $\dfrac{R_f}{R_g} = 2$ | i.e., $R_f = 2 R_g$ | | Quality factor (symmetric network) | $Q = \dfrac{1}{3}$ | Lower Q = wider bandwidth, lower distortion | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Function generators & lab signal sources:** The HP 33120A, Tektronix AFG3000 series, and Rigol DG1000 series all use Wien-bridge-derived topologies. - **Audio testing & measurement:** Standard audio test-tone source (1 kHz @ 0 dBm). - **Musical electronics:** Old-style electric organ tone generators. - **PLL references:** Wien-derived VCOs serve as low-noise frequency references. - **Bio-medical signal simulation:** ECG/EEG calibrator tones. - **Automatic test equipment (ATE):** Multi-frequency stimulus for testing audio amplifiers and filters. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Transfer Function $\beta(j\omega)$ of the Wien Bridge Network The Wien bridge consists of a **series arm** ($R_1$ in series with $C_1$) and a **parallel arm** ($R_2$ in parallel with $C_2$). The output of the network is taken across the parallel arm. **Step 1 — Write the impedances in standard complex form:** The series arm: $$Z_s = R_1 + \frac{1}{j\omega C_1} = \frac{j\omega R_1 C_1 + 1}{j\omega C_1}$$ The parallel arm (a resistor in parallel with a capacitor): $$Z_p = R_2 \parallel \frac{1}{j\omega C_2} = \frac{R_2 \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C_2}}{R_2 + \frac{1}{j\omega C_2}} = \frac{R_2}{1 + j\omega R_2 C_2}$$ **Step 2 — Form the feedback factor $\beta$:** Using the voltage divider rule across the Wien network: $$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{V_f}{V_{out}} = \frac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute $Z_s$ and $Z_p$:** $$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{\dfrac{R_2}{1 + j\omega R_2 C_2}}{\dfrac{1 + j\omega R_1 C_1}{j\omega C_1} + \dfrac{R_2}{1 + j\omega R_2 C_2}}$$ **Step 4 — Clear the inner denominator by multiplying numerator and denominator by $j\omega C_1(1 + j\omega R_2 C_2)$:** Numerator becomes: $$N = R_2 \cdot j\omega C_1$$ Denominator becomes: $$D = (1 + j\omega R_1 C_1)(1 + j\omega R_2 C_2) + j\omega R_2 C_1$$ **Step 5 — Expand the product $(1 + j\omega R_1 C_1)(1 + j\omega R_2 C_2)$:** $$(1 + j\omega R_1 C_1)(1 + j\omega R_2 C_2) = 1 + j\omega R_2 C_2 + j\omega R_1 C_1 + (j\omega)^2 R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2$$ Since $(j\omega)^2 = -\omega^2$: $$= 1 + j\omega(R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2) - \omega^2 R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2$$ **Step 6 — Add the $j\omega R_2 C_1$ term from the numerator clearing:** $$D = 1 - \omega^2 R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2 + j\omega(R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2 + R_2 C_1)$$ **Step 7 — Write the complete transfer function:** $$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega R_2 C_1}{\left(1 - \omega^2 R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2\right) + j\omega(R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2 + R_2 C_1)}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation — Frequency of Oscillation $f_0$ **Step 8 — Apply the zero-phase condition (Barkhausen phase rule):** For the loop phase to be zero, $\beta$ must be **purely real** (no imaginary component). Looking at the transfer function, the numerator $j\omega R_2 C_1$ is purely imaginary (phase $= +90°$). Therefore, the denominator must also be purely imaginary (have the same phase), which means its **real part must equal zero**: $$1 - \omega^2 R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2 = 0$$ **Step 9 — Solve for $\omega_0$:** $$\omega_0^2 = \frac{1}{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$$ **Step 10 — Convert angular frequency to Hz:** $$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$$ **Step 11 — Simplify for the symmetric case $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$:** $$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R \cdot R \cdot C \cdot C}} = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** The examiner expects you to derive the **general expression first** and *then* substitute the symmetric case as a special case. This shows conceptual understanding and fetches full marks. --- ### 3.3 Derivation — Required Amplifier Gain $A = 3$ **Step 12 — Evaluate $\beta$ at $\omega = \omega_0$:** When the real part of the denominator vanishes, the denominator reduces to: $$D\big|_{\omega_0} = j\omega_0(R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2 + R_2 C_1)$$ Therefore: $$\beta\big|_{\omega_0} = \frac{j\omega_0 R_2 C_1}{j\omega_0(R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2 + R_2 C_1)} = \frac{R_2 C_1}{R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2 + R_2 C_1}$$ **Step 13 — Apply the symmetric case $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$:** $$\beta = \frac{RC}{RC + RC + RC} = \frac{RC}{3RC} = \frac{1}{3}$$ **Step 14 — Apply the magnitude condition of the Barkhausen criterion:** $$\vert A \beta \vert = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad A \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad A = 3$$ **Step 15 — Translate to op-amp resistor values:** The non-inverting amplifier gain is: $$A = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_g} = 3 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{R_f}{R_g} = 2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{R_f = 2R_g}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Practical note:** In a real circuit, $R_f$ is set *slightly greater* than $2R_g$ (e.g., $R_f \approx 2.1 R_g$) so that the loop gain $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$ at startup, allowing oscillation to grow. An amplitude-limiting element (thermistor, JFET, or lamp) in the feedback path dynamically reduces the effective gain to exactly 3 once the desired amplitude is reached. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Condition vs Behaviour | Condition | Loop Gain $\vert A\beta \vert$ | Oscillator Behaviour | Practical Action | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $R_f < 2R_g$ (i.e., $A < 3$) | $< 1$ | Oscillation **damps out** — no output | Increase $R_f$ | | $R_f = 2R_g$ (i.e., $A = 3$) | $= 1$ | Sustained oscillation (ideal, mathematically) | Pure sine wave (theoretical) | | $R_f > 2R_g$ (i.e., $A > 3$) | $> 1$ | Oscillation **grows** → clipping → distorted square-like wave | Add AGC (thermistor, JFET, lamp) | | Frequency $\omega \neq \omega_0$ | $\vert \beta \vert < 1/3$ and $\beta$ has phase | Oscillation decays (filter rejects off-frequency components) | None — automatic by network | | At $\omega = \omega_0$ exactly | $\beta = 1/3$ (real, max) | Full amplitude sustained oscillation | Desired operating point | ### 3.5 Gain & Phase Bode-Shape Interpretation - The Wien network's **magnitude response** peaks at $f_0$ with a maximum value of $\vert \beta \vert = 1/3$. - The **phase response** crosses through $0°$ at $f_0$ (going from $+90°$ at low frequencies to $-90°$ at high frequencies). - This single-frequency selectivity is what makes the Wien bridge an excellent sinusoidal oscillator. - Compared to the **RC phase-shift oscillator** (which produces a maximum of $180°$ phase shift across three sections but with $\beta = 1/29$), the Wien bridge has a much **higher feedback factor** ($1/3$ vs $1/29$), so it requires a smaller amplifier gain and produces **less distortion**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Wien Bridge Oscillator System ```mermaid graph LR nodeA[DC Supply
+Vcc and -Vee] --> nodeO[Op-Amp 741
Non-Inverting] nodeO -->|Vout Sine Wave| nodeB[Output Node] nodeB --> nodeC[Wien Network
Series RC + Parallel RC] nodeC -->|Beta times Vout
At f0 Beta equals 1 by 3| nodeD[Non-Inverting Input Pin 3] nodeB --> nodeE[Resistive Divider
Rf and Rg] nodeE -->|Negative Feedback| nodeF[Inverting Input Pin 2] nodeD --> nodeO nodeF --> nodeO nodeB --> nodeG[Load Speaker or Scope] style nodeA fill:#fff4e1 style nodeO fill:#e1f5ff style nodeB fill:#e8f5e9 style nodeC fill:#fce4ec style nodeD fill:#f3e5f5 style nodeE fill:#fff9c4 style nodeF fill:#f3e5f5 style nodeG fill:#ffebee ``` ### 4.2 Internal Architecture of the Wien Network (Positive Feedback Block) ```mermaid graph TB nodeIn[Vout from Op-Amp] --> nodeS1[Series Resistor R1] nodeS1 --> nodeS2[Series Capacitor C1] nodeS2 --> nodeGnd1[Ground Reference] nodeIn --> nodeP1[Parallel Resistor R2] nodeIn --> nodeP2[Parallel Capacitor C2] nodeP1 --> nodeMid[Junction Node A] nodeP2 --> nodeMid nodeMid --> nodeOut[Vf to Non-Inverting Input] nodeMid --> nodeGnd2[Ground Reference] style nodeIn fill:#e1f5ff style nodeS1 fill:#fff4e1 style nodeS2 fill:#fff4e1 style nodeP1 fill:#e8f5e9 style nodeP2 fill:#e8f5e9 style nodeMid fill:#fce4ec style nodeOut fill:#f3e5f5 style nodeGnd1 fill:#eeeeee style nodeGnd2 fill:#eeeeee ``` ### 4.3 Closed-Loop Feedback Signal-Flow Diagram ```mermaid graph LR nodeA[Input Noise
Thermal Noise at f0] --> nodeSum[Summing Junction] nodeSum --> nodeAmp[Op-Amp
Gain A equals 3] nodeAmp --> nodeOut[Vout
Sine Wave at f0] nodeOut --> nodeBeta[Wien Network
Beta equals 1 by 3] nodeBeta --> nodeFb[Feedback Voltage Vf] nodeFb --> nodeSum style nodeA fill:#fff9c4 style nodeSum fill:#ffccbc style nodeAmp fill:#e1f5ff style nodeOut fill:#c8e6c9 style nodeBeta fill:#f8bbd0 style nodeFb fill:#d1c4e9 ``` ### 4.4 Signal-Waveform Progression Through the Loop ```mermaid graph LR nodeN[Initial Noise
Broadband] --> nodeAmp[Amplifier A equals 3
Increases Amplitude] nodeAmp --> nodeF[Frequency Selection
Wien Network] nodeF --> nodeS[At f0 Beta equals 1 by 3
Phase Zero] nodeS --> nodeG[Growth Stage
Loop Gain greater than 1] nodeG --> nodeAGC[AGC Activates
Thermistor or JFET] nodeAGC --> nodeSS[Steady State
Loop Gain equals 1] nodeSS --> nodeOut[Pure Sine Wave Output
Frequency f0 equals 1 by 2piRC] style nodeN fill:#eeeeee style nodeAmp fill:#e1f5ff style nodeF fill:#fff4e1 style nodeS fill:#fce4ec style nodeG fill:#fff9c4 style nodeAGC fill:#ffccbc style nodeSS fill:#c8e6c9 style nodeOut fill:#a5d6a7 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** **State the Barkhausen criterion for sustained oscillations. Why is it necessary that the loop gain be slightly greater than unity at the start?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Barkhausen criterion states that for sustained sinusoidal oscillations, two conditions must be simultaneously satisfied: 1. The magnitude of the loop gain must equal unity: $\vert A \beta \vert = 1$. 2. The total phase shift around the loop must be zero (or an integer multiple of 360°): $\angle A \beta = 0° \text{ or } 360°$. The loop gain must be *slightly greater than unity* at startup so that the oscillation amplitude can build up from the inevitable thermal noise present at the op-amp input. If $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ from the very beginning, the noise would neither grow nor decay and a clean sine wave would never emerge. Once the desired output amplitude is reached, an automatic gain control (AGC) circuit reduces the effective gain back to exactly 1 for stable, undistorted operation. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **List the two arms of the Wien bridge network and write the expression for the feedback factor $\beta$ at the frequency of oscillation.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Wien bridge network consists of: 1. **Series arm:** $Z_s = R_1 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_1}$ (a resistor $R_1$ in series with a capacitor $C_1$). 2. **Parallel arm:** $Z_p = R_2 \parallel \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_2}$ (a resistor $R_2$ in parallel with a capacitor $C_2$). The feedback factor is given by the voltage-divider relation: $$\beta = \frac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}$$ At the frequency of oscillation $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$ (for symmetric components), this simplifies to the well-known result $\beta = 1/3$. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B carries 14 marks per question with sub-parts (a) of 7 marks and (b) of 7 marks. Students must attempt **one full question** out of the choice provided. --- #### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]* **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation of an op-amp based Wien bridge oscillator. Show that the gain of the amplifier must be 3. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Draw the circuit** *(1 mark)*: The op-amp is in non-inverting configuration. The Wien network (series arm $R_1$–$C_1$, parallel arm $R_2$–$C_2$) is connected between the output and the non-inverting input. The feedback resistors $R_f$ and $R_g$ are connected between output and inverting input, and from inverting input to ground respectively. **Step 2 — Write the impedances** *(1 mark)*: $$Z_s = R_1 + \frac{1}{j\omega C_1}, \qquad Z_p = \frac{R_2}{1 + j\omega R_2 C_2}$$ **Step 3 — Form $\beta$ and simplify** *(2 marks)*: After rationalising the voltage divider and multiplying numerator and denominator by $j\omega C_1(1 + j\omega R_2 C_2)$, the transfer function becomes: $$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega R_2 C_1}{(1 - \omega^2 R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2) + j\omega(R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2 + R_2 C_1)}$$ **Step 4 — Apply zero-phase condition** *(1 mark)*: Set real part of denominator to zero: $$1 - \omega_0^2 R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2 = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$$ For $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$: $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$. **Step 5 — Evaluate $\beta$ at $f_0$ and apply Barkhausen** *(2 marks)*: $\beta = 1/3$ at $f_0$. From $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ we get $A = 3$. For non-inverting op-amp: $1 + R_f/R_g = 3 \Rightarrow R_f = 2R_g$. **Q.E.D.** --- **(b)** Design a Wien bridge oscillator using an op-amp to generate a **1 kHz** sine wave. Choose appropriate component values and justify your selection. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Choose the frequency formula** *(1 mark)*: Use the symmetric case $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$. **Step 2 — Pick a convenient capacitor value** *(1 mark)*: Let $C = C_1 = C_2 = 10 \text{ nF}$ (standard value, good for audio range). **Step 3 — Solve for R** *(2 marks)*: $$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_0 C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1}{6.283 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 15.92 \text{ k}\Omega$$ Choose standard value $R_1 = R_2 = 16 \text{ k}\Omega$ (E12 series). This gives an actual frequency of $f_0 = 1/(2\pi \times 16\text{k} \times 10\text{n}) \approx 995 \text{ Hz}$, within 0.5% of target. **Step 4 — Choose gain-setting resistors** *(1 mark)*: For $A = 3$, choose $R_g = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$ (standard E12). To ensure startup, use $R_f = 22 \text{ k}\Omega$ (slightly more than 20 kΩ) and add a **thermistor** (NTC, 10 kΩ at 25°C) in parallel with $R_f$ for AGC. **Step 5 — Choose the op-amp** *(1 mark)*: Use **IC 741** or **LF351** (JFET input for high input impedance, so it does not load the Wien network). **Step 6 — Power supply and stability** *(1 mark)*: Use $\pm 12$ V dual supply; add a $0.1 \mu F$ decoupling capacitor close to the op-amp supply pins. Output amplitude limited to about $\pm 10$ V peak-to-peak. **Final Design Summary:** | Component | Value | Purpose | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $R_1, R_2$ | $16 \text{ k}\Omega$ | Wien network resistors (set $f_0$) | | $C_1, C_2$ | $10 \text{ nF}$ | Wien network capacitors (set $f_0$) | | $R_f$ | $20 \text{ k}\Omega$ + 10 kΩ NTC | Sets gain = 3, NTC provides AGC | | $R_g$ | $10 \text{ k}\Omega$ | Ground reference for inverting input | | Op-amp | 741 or LF351 | Non-inverting amplifier | | Supply | $\pm 12 \text{ V}$ | Dual supply for op-amp | --- #### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]* **(a)** Derive the transfer function of the Wien bridge network in its general form (unsymmetrical case). Show that the network behaves as a band-pass filter with peak gain 1/3 at $f_0 = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2})$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Define impedances** *(1 mark)*: $Z_s = R_1 + 1/(j\omega C_1)$ and $Z_p = R_2/(1 + j\omega R_2 C_2)$. **Step 2 — Form $\beta(j\omega)$ and rationalise** *(3 marks)*: Following the same procedure as in Question A(a), we obtain: $$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega R_2 C_1}{(1 - \omega^2 R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2) + j\omega(R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2 + R_2 C_1)}$$ **Step 3 — Compute magnitude squared** *(2 marks)*: $$\vert \beta(j\omega) \vert^2 = \frac{\omega^2 R_2^2 C_1^2}{(1 - \omega^2 R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2)^2 + \omega^2(R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2 + R_2 C_1)^2}$$ **Step 4 — Maximise** *(1 mark)*: Differentiate the denominator w.r.t. $\omega^2$ and set to zero. The minimum of the denominator occurs when $1 - \omega^2 R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2 = 0$, giving the peak frequency $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}$. **Step 5 — Show peak value** *(1 mark)*: At $\omega_0$, the second term of denominator is $\omega_0^2 (R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2 + R_2 C_1)^2$ and the first term vanishes. Substituting and simplifying gives $\vert \beta \vert_{max} = R_2 C_1 / (R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2 + R_2 C_1)$, which equals $1/3$ in the symmetric case. **Q.E.D.** --- **(b)** Compare the Wien bridge oscillator with the RC phase-shift oscillator under the following heads: (i) Frequency-determining network, (ii) Feedback factor, (iii) Gain requirement, (iv) Output waveform purity, (v) Frequency range, (vi) Op-amp configuration. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | Comparison Head | Wien Bridge Oscillator | RC Phase-Shift Oscillator | Marks | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | (i) Frequency network | Series-parallel RC (Wien network) | Three cascaded RC sections (each giving 60°) | 1.5 | | (ii) Feedback factor $\beta$ | $1/3$ (high — easier to drive) | $1/29$ (very low) | 1.5 | | (iii) Required gain $A$ | $3$ (low — easy to realise) | $29$ (high — needs precise high-gain op-amp) | 1 | | (iv) Waveform purity | Excellent (low distortion, < 0.1%) | Moderate (higher THD ~ 1–2%) | 1 | | (v) Frequency range | $1 \text{ Hz} – 1 \text{ MHz}$ (audio-focused) | $1 \text{ Hz} – 100 \text{ kHz}$ (audio-focused) | 1 | | (vi) Op-amp configuration | **Non-inverting** (positive gain) | **Inverting** (negative gain — phase inverted three times) | 1 | **Concluding statement:** The Wien bridge oscillator is preferred for **commercial audio applications** due to its simpler gain requirement ($A = 3$ vs $A = 29$) and lower distortion. The RC phase-shift oscillator is used when a single inverting op-amp stage is desired, but it requires careful gain trimming. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students lose marks on Wien Bridge Oscillator questions:** > 1. **Confusing series and parallel arms** — Some students write the parallel arm as $R_2 + 1/(j\omega C_2)$ instead of $R_2 \parallel 1/(j\omega C_2)$. This single error makes the entire derivation wrong. **Remember:** "Parallel" = product-over-sum. > 2. **Forgetting to apply both Barkhausen conditions** — A common mistake is to find $f_0$ but forget to derive the gain condition $A = 3$. KTU examiners deduct up to 3 marks for this omission. > 3. **Mixing up non-inverting vs inverting configuration** — The Wien bridge *must* be non-inverting because $\beta$ is positive real at $f_0$. Writing $A = -R_f/R_g$ (inverting) will lose full marks on the gain derivation. > 4. **Not simplifying the symmetric case** — Even if the general case is derived, the examiner expects the special case $R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$ leading to $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$ and $\beta = 1/3$. Skipping this loses 2 marks. > 5. **Algebraic slip in expanding the product** — $(1 + j\omega R_1 C_1)(1 + j\omega R_2 C_2)$ expansion must yield $1 + j\omega(R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2) - \omega^2 R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2$. Watch the sign of $j^2 = -1$. > 6. **No mention of AGC** — If the question asks "why is $A$ slightly greater than 3 in practice?", failure to mention amplitude stabilisation (thermistor/JFET/lamp) costs 1–2 marks. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Print this section and keep it for last-minute revision before the exam.** - **Wien Bridge Oscillator = Non-inverting op-amp + Wien (series-parallel RC) network in positive feedback + resistive divider in negative feedback.** - **Two frequency-selective components:** $R_1$–$C_1$ (series) and $R_2$–$C_2$ (parallel). - **General oscillation frequency:** $f_0 = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2})$. - **Symmetric case ($R_1=R_2=R$, $C_1=C_2=C$):** $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$ — the form to remember. - **Feedback factor at $f_0$:** $\beta = 1/3$ (real, positive, max value). - **Required gain:** $A = 3$ from $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$. - **Resistor ratio for gain = 3:** $R_f / R_g = 2$, i.e., $R_f = 2 R_g$ in the negative feedback path. - **For startup:** $R_f$ must be *slightly* greater than $2 R_g$ (loop gain $> 1$). - **Barkhausen criterion:** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ AND $\angle A\beta = 0°$ (or multiples of 360°). - **Op-amp type:** 741, LF351, or any general-purpose op-amp with sufficient slew rate for the chosen frequency. - **Amplitude stabilisation:** Thermistor (NTC), JFET (VCR), incandescent lamp, or Zener diode pair. - **Quality factor of symmetric Wien network:** $Q = 1/3$ (low — broad bandwidth, low distortion). - **Frequency range:** Audio band $1 \text{ Hz}$ to $1 \text{ MHz}$ — *the* standard audio oscillator. - **Phase-shift oscillator vs Wien bridge:** Wien bridge has $\beta = 1/3$ (vs $1/29$), $A = 3$ (vs $29$), and lower distortion. Wien bridge uses non-inverting configuration; phase-shift uses inverting. - **Typical applications:** Function generators, audio test equipment, beat-frequency oscillators, organ tone generators, ECG/EEG calibrators. - **Historical note (for context only):** The Wien bridge topology was commercialised by William R. Hewlett in the 1930s and became the basis of the famous HP 200A oscillator. - **KTU two-line answer:** "A Wien bridge oscillator uses a non-inverting op-amp with a Wien bridge (series-parallel RC) network in the positive feedback path. It oscillates at $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$ with a feedback factor $\beta = 1/3$, requiring an amplifier gain $A = 3$."

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🎯 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Analog Circuits (Module 4 – Power Amplifiers), **Classification** refers to the systematic categorization of **Power Amplifiers** based on the **conduction angle (θc)** of the output current waveform — i.e., the portion of the input AC cycle during which the active device (BJT/MOSFET) remains in conduction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *“Classification of power amplifiers based on the position of the Q-point and the length of the conduction angle: Class A, Class B, Class AB, and Class C.”*

The conduction angle `θc` and the quiescent (Q-point) bias current `ICQ` together determine:
- Efficiency (η)
- Linearity / Distortion
- Power dissipation in the transistor

---

## 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model

Imagine four different water taps delivering power to a bucket:

| Amplifier Class | Tap Analogy | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Class A** | Tap is **fully open** at idle; we just twist the knob a little up/down | Always flowing, smooth, but wastes water (power) even when no signal |
| **Class B** | Tap is **closed** at idle; one tap opens on the push, another on the pull | Two taps work — one for +ve half, one for –ve half. Efficient but a tiny gap at zero-crossing |
| **Class AB** | Tap is **slightly open** at idle — small trickle, but two taps still work together | Compromise: eliminates cross-over gap, good efficiency |
| **Class C** | Tap is **firmly closed** at idle; only opens for a brief burst | Used with a flywheel (resonant tank) to reconstruct the full sine wave |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "fully on" tap (Class A) wastes power even when idle** — that's why its efficiency is so poor. The push-pull (Class B) arrangement mimics the human heart: left ventricle pumps for half cycle, right ventricle for the other half.

---

## 📌 Standard Metrics & Constants (Bolded for Recall)

- **Conduction angle (θc) = 360°** → Class A
- **Conduction angle (θc) = 180°** → Class B
- **Conduction angle (180° < θc < 360°)** → Class AB
- **Conduction angle (θc < 180°)** → Class C
- **Maximum theoretical efficiency of Class A (transformer-coupled)** = **50%**
- **Maximum theoretical efficiency of Class A (series-fed)** = **25%**
- **Maximum theoretical efficiency of Class B (push-pull)** = **π/4 ≈ 78.6%**
- **Maximum theoretical efficiency of Class C (ideal)** = **100%**

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## ⚙️ Classification Criteria — The Two Master Variables

Every power amplifier class is defined by **two simultaneous conditions**:

1. **Position of the Q-point on the DC load line**
2. **Length of the conduction angle (θc)** — the fraction of the input cycle during which `iC > 0`

---

## 📐 Class-by-Class Breakdown

### 🔹 Class A Power Amplifier
- **Q-point:** At the **center** of the DC load line (`ICQ = IC(sat)/2`, `VCEQ = VCC/2`)
- **Conduction Angle:** `θc = 360°` (full cycle)
- **Bias Condition:** Transistor is **always ON** during the entire signal cycle
- **Configurations:** Series-fed, Transformer-coupled
- **Distortion:** **Lowest** (most linear)
- **Efficiency:** **Poor** (25% series-fed, 50% transformer-coupled) — wastes most power as heat
- **Application:** High-fidelity audio pre-driver stages, low-power audio output

### 🔹 Class B Power Amplifier
- **Q-point:** Exactly at **cutoff** (`VCEQ = VCC`, `ICQ = 0`)
- **Conduction Angle:** `θc = 180°` (each transistor conducts half cycle)
- **Bias Condition:** Each transistor is ON for **exactly half** the input cycle
- **Configuration:** **Push-Pull** (two complementary transistors)
- **Distortion:** Suffers from **Crossover Distortion** near zero-crossing
- **Efficiency:** **High** — up to **π/4 = 78.6%**
- **Application:** High-power audio amplifiers, radio transmitters

### 🔹 Class AB Power Amplifier
- **Q-point:** **Slightly above cutoff** (small bias current `ICQ > 0`)
- **Conduction Angle:** `180° < θc < 360°` (each transistor conducts *more than* half cycle, *less than* full)
- **Bias Condition:** Both transistors are slightly forward-biased to eliminate crossover distortion
- **Efficiency:** **Between Class A and Class B** (~50% to ~70%)
- **Application:** **Most widely used** — commercial audio amplifiers, op-amp output stages

### 🔹 Class C Power Amplifier
- **Q-point:** **Below cutoff** (`VBE < VBE(on)`)
- **Conduction Angle:** `θc < 180°` (typically 60°–90°)
- **Bias Condition:** Transistor conducts for only a **fraction** of the cycle
- **Configuration:** **Always with a tuned (LC) load** — resonant tank reconstructs the full sine wave
- **Efficiency:** **Highest** (theoretically 100%)
- **Distortion:** **Highest** — needs resonant load to filter
- **Application:** RF transmitters, IF amplifiers, oscillators, frequency multipliers

---

## 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — it covers **>90% of KTU numerical questions** on Classification.

| Parameter | Class A (Series-Fed) | Class A (Transformer) | Class B (Push-Pull) | Class C (Tuned) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Q-point | `VCEQ = VCC/2` | `VCEQ = VCC` | At cutoff (`ICQ = 0`) | Below cutoff |
| Conduction Angle `θc` | **360°** | **360°** | **180°** | **< 180°** |
| Max. AC Power `Pac` | `VCC² / (8RC)` | `VCC² / (2RL)` | `VCC · Im / 2` | `(Im · Vm) / 2` |
| DC Power `PDC` | `VCC² / (2RC)` | `2VCC · ICQ` | `2VCC · Im / π` | `VCC · IC(avg)` |
| Max. Efficiency `η` | **25%** | **50%** | **π/4 ≈ 78.6%** | → 100% (ideal) |
| Distortion | Lowest | Lowest | Crossover | Highest |
| Load Type | Resistive | Transformer | Resistive/Transformer | **Tuned LC** |

### Key Auxiliary Formulas

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{ac}}{P_{DC}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
P_{DC} = V_{CC} \times I_{C(\text{avg})}
$$

$$
P_{ac} = \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{2} = \frac{V_m^2}{2R_L} = \frac{I_m^2 R_L}{2}
$$

$$
\text{Conduction Angle} \;\; \theta_c = \frac{I_{C(\text{avg})}}{I_m} \times 360^\circ
$$

$$
I_{C(\text{avg})} = \frac{I_m}{\pi} \quad \text{(for pure Class B, full sine half-wave)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Class A** is preferred in hi-fi audio (low noise, high linearity).
> - **Class B / AB** dominate **commercial audio ICs** (LM386, TDA2030, etc.).
> - **Class C** is the **workhorse of RF transmitters** — every AM/FAM broadcast, radar, and wireless base-station PA is essentially a Class C amplifier with a tuned tank.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🔬 Derivation 1: Maximum Efficiency of Class A Series-Fed Amplifier

**Given:** Resistive load `RC`, supply `VCC`, Q-point biased at the midpoint of the DC load line.

### Step 1 — Establish the Q-point

Because the Q-point is at the **center** of the DC load line:

$$
V_{CEQ} = \frac{V_{CC}}{2}
$$

$$
I_{CQ} = \frac{V_{CC}}{2 R_C}
$$

### Step 2 — Express the DC Power Supplied

The DC supply delivers current `ICQ` continuously at voltage `VCC`:

$$
P_{DC} = V_{CC} \times I_{CQ} = V_{CC} \times \frac{V_{CC}}{2 R_C} = \frac{V_{CC}^{2}}{2 R_C}
$$

### Step 3 — Express the Maximum AC Power Delivered to the Load

For **maximum** (undistorted) sinusoidal swing, the peak values equal the Q-point values:
- `Vm = VCEQ = VCC / 2`
- `Im = ICQ = VCC / (2RC)`

Average AC power delivered to load:

$$
P_{ac} = \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{2} = \frac{\left(\dfrac{V_{CC}}{2}\right)\left(\dfrac{V_{CC}}{2 R_C}\right)}{2}
$$

Simplifying step-by-step:

$$
P_{ac} = \frac{V_{CC}^{2}}{4 \times 2 \times 2 R_C} = \frac{V_{CC}^{2}}{8 R_C}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Maximum Efficiency

$$
\eta_{\max} = \frac{P_{ac}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{\dfrac{V_{CC}^{2}}{8 R_C}}{\dfrac{V_{CC}^{2}}{2 R_C}} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\max}^{\text{Class A (series)}} = 25\%}
$$

### Step 5 — Power Dissipated in the Transistor

$$
P_{D(\text{transistor})} = P_{DC} - P_{ac} = \frac{V_{CC}^{2}}{2 R_C} - \frac{V_{CC}^{2}}{8 R_C} = \frac{3 V_{CC}^{2}}{8 R_C}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** At maximum signal, the transistor still dissipates **3× the AC power** delivered to the load! This is why Class A is thermally demanding.

---

## 🔬 Derivation 2: Maximum Efficiency of Class B Push-Pull Amplifier

**Given:** Two complementary BJTs (NPN + PNP) in push-pull, each biased at cutoff, supply `±VCC`.

### Step 1 — Current Waveform in Each Transistor

Each transistor conducts for **180°** (half cycle). The current waveform is a **half-sinusoid** with peak `Im`.

The average (DC) current drawn by **each** transistor from its supply:

$$
I_{DC(\text{each})} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} I_m \sin(\theta) \, d\theta = \frac{I_m}{\pi}
$$

### Step 2 — Total DC Power from Both Supplies

Two supplies (`+VCC` and `–VCC`), each delivering `Im/π`:

$$
P_{DC} = 2 \times V_{CC} \times \frac{I_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi}
$$

### Step 3 — Maximum AC Power Delivered to Load

For maximum swing, `Vm = VCC` (full rail-to-rail swing):

$$
P_{ac} = \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{2} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_m}{2}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Maximum Efficiency

$$
\eta_{\max} = \frac{P_{ac}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{\dfrac{V_{CC} I_m}{2}}{\dfrac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi}}
$$

Cancel `VCC · Im`:

$$
\eta_{\max} = \frac{1/2}{2/\pi} = \frac{\pi}{4}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\max}^{\text{Class B}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.7857 = 78.57\%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "π/4 magic"** is a famous KTU exam question. Always derive it step-by-step — partial credit is awarded for each step.

---

## 🔬 Derivation 3: Conduction Angle from Average Current

**Given:** Sinusoidal current with peak `Im` and average `IC(avg)`.

The conduction angle `θc` is related to average current by:

$$
\theta_c = \frac{I_{C(\text{avg})}}{I_m} \times 360^\circ
$$

### Verification for Class B

For a half-sine waveform: `IC(avg) = Im / π`

$$
\theta_c = \frac{I_m / \pi}{I_m} \times 360^\circ = \frac{360^\circ}{\pi} = 180^\circ \;\; ✅
$$

### Verification for Class A

For a **full** sine waveform: `IC(avg) = 0` (average of pure sine is zero, but DC bias adds the offset).

When biased with `ICQ` at center:
- `ic(t) = ICQ + Im sin(ωt)`
- Average value = `ICQ` (the bias current itself)
- The conduction angle over which the device conducts = `360°` since `ic(t) > 0` for all `t` (as long as `ICQ > Im`).

---

## 📋 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Sub-Question Ready)

| Feature | Class A | Class B | Class AB | Class C |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Q-point location | Center of load line | At cutoff | Slightly above cutoff | Below cutoff |
| Conduction angle | 360° | 180° | 180°–360° | < 180° |
| Efficiency range | 25–50% | up to 78.6% | 50–70% | up to 100% |
| Linearity | Excellent | Poor (crossover) | Good | Very poor |
| Need for heat sink | Largest | Moderate | Moderate | Smallest |
| Output stage of choice? | Hi-fi pre-drivers | RF/Hi-power audio | **Audio ICs (most common)** | RF transmitters |

    MID --- CUTO[Class B: Q at cutoff, VCEQ equals VCC, ICQ equals 0]
    CUTO --- ABOVE[Class AB: Q slightly above cutoff line]

    subgraph WAVE[Output Current Waveform Comparison]
    direction TB
    W1[Input Sine Wave Reference] --> W2[Class A: Full 360 degrees conduction, always positive current]
    W1 --> W3[Class B: 180 degrees conduction, half sine pulses]
    W1 --> W4[Class AB: 180 to 360 degrees conduction, small overlap region]

## 🧭 Figure 4: Block-Level Architecture of a Class AB Audio Power Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Audio Signal Vi] --> B[Voltage Amplifier Stage]
    B --> C[VAS: Vas Driver]
    C --> D[Class AB Push Pull Output]
    D --> E[Speaker Load RL]
    D --> F[Bias Network: Vbe Multiplier]
    F --> C
    
    G[DC Supply Plus VCC] --> D
    H[DC Supply Minus VEE] --> D
    
    D --> I[Heat Sink]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Block Diagram:**
> - The **Vbe multiplier** (bias network) sets a small quiescent current to eliminate crossover distortion — this is what makes it "Class AB" instead of pure Class B.
> - **Push-pull output** = NPN transistor sources current (positive half) + PNP transistor sinks current (negative half).
> - The **heat sink** is mandatory because even at 70% efficiency, 30% of the supply power is dissipated as heat.

---

## 🧭 Figure 5: Efficiency vs. Conduction Angle — The KTU Trade-off Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRADEOFF[Efficiency versus Conduction Angle]
    A[Class C, theta less than 180, eta approaches 100 percent, high distortion] --> B[Class B, theta equals 180, eta equals 78.6 percent]
    B --> C[Class AB, theta 180 to 360, eta 50 to 70 percent]
    C --> D[Class A, theta equals 360, eta 25 to 50 percent, lowest distortion]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fundamental Trade-off:** As conduction angle **decreases** (A → B → C), efficiency **increases** but distortion also **increases**. Class AB is the **engineering sweet spot** for most audio applications.
