# 🎓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### ❓ Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Compare the performance of Class A and Class B power amplifiers in terms of efficiency and distortion.*

### ✅ Model Answer (A1)

| Parameter | Class A | Class B |
|---|---|---|
| Q-point | Center of load line | At cutoff |
| Conduction angle | 360° | 180° |
| Maximum efficiency | 25% (series), 50% (transformer) | **78.6% (π/4)** |
| Distortion | Very low | High (crossover distortion) |
| Configuration | Single-ended | Push-pull required |
| Application | Hi-fi audio pre-drivers | High-power audio, RF |

Class B is far more efficient because each transistor only conducts for half the cycle, minimizing idle power dissipation. However, the zero-crossing gap produces **crossover distortion**, which Class A avoids by remaining always ON.

---

### ❓ Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is Class C amplifier always used with a tuned (LC) load? What is its typical conduction angle?*

### ✅ Model Answer (A2)

Class C amplifiers have their Q-point **below cutoff**, so the transistor conducts for **less than 180°** (typically 60°–90°). The output current consists of **narrow pulses** rather than a full sine wave.

A **tuned LC tank circuit** is mandatory because:
1. It **filters out harmonics**, allowing only the fundamental frequency to develop across the load (high-Q resonant circuit).
2. The **flywheel action** of the LC tank reconstructs a near-sinusoidal output from the narrow current pulses.
3. Without the tuned load, the output would be a severely distorted pulse train, unusable for any practical purpose.

**Typical conduction angle:** **60° to 90°** (less than 180°). Maximum theoretical efficiency can approach **100%** in the ideal case.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — *Efficiency Derivations*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question based on KTU 2024 Pattern]**
*(a)* Derive the maximum efficiency of a **Class A series-fed power amplifier**. State clearly all assumptions made. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Derive the maximum efficiency of a **Class B push-pull power amplifier**. Show that the maximum theoretical efficiency is **π/4**. *(7 marks)*

#### ✅ Model Solution

### Part (a) — Class A Series-Fed Efficiency

**Assumptions:**
1. Q-point is biased at the exact center of the DC load line: `VCEQ = VCC/2`, `ICQ = VCC/(2RC)`.
2. Sinusoidal input signal, no distortion (ideal transistor).
3. Maximum possible swing without clipping: `Vm = VCEQ = VCC/2` and `Im = ICQ = VCC/(2RC)`.

**Step 1 — DC Power from Supply:**

$$
P_{DC} = V_{CC} \times I_{CQ} = V_{CC} \times \frac{V_{CC}}{2 R_C} = \frac{V_{CC}^{2}}{2 R_C}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum AC Power Delivered to Load:**

$$
P_{ac} = \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{2} = \frac{\left(\dfrac{V_{CC}}{2}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_{CC}}{2 R_C}\right)}{2} = \frac{V_{CC}^{2}}{8 R_C}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{\max} = \frac{P_{ac}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_{CC}^{2} / (8 R_C)}{V_{CC}^{2} / (2 R_C)} = \frac{1}{4}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\max}^{\text{Class A (series)}} = 25\%}
$$

**Step 4 — Transistor Power Dissipation:**

$$
P_T = P_{DC} - P_{ac} = \frac{V_{CC}^{2}}{2R_C} - \frac{V_{CC}^{2}}{8R_C} = \frac{3V_{CC}^{2}}{8R_C}
$$

The transistor dissipates **3 times the AC power** at full signal — this is why Class A requires a large heat sink.

---

### Part (b) — Class B Push-Pull Efficiency

**Assumptions:**
1. Two complementary transistors biased at cutoff (`ICQ = 0`).
2. Each transistor conducts for exactly 180° — perfect push-pull.
3. Ideal transistor: zero `VCE(sat)`, infinite β.
4. Output swing reaches the rails: `Vm = VCC`.

**Step 1 — DC Current Drawn by Each Transistor:**

Each transistor's current is a half-sinusoid with peak `Im`. Its average value over a full cycle is:

$$
I_{DC} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} I_m \sin\theta \, d\theta = \frac{I_m}{\pi}
$$

**Step 2 — Total DC Power (Two Supplies):**

$$
P_{DC} = 2 \times V_{CC} \times \frac{I_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum AC Power Delivered to Load:**

$$
P_{ac} = \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{2} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_m}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{\max} = \frac{P_{ac}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_{CC} I_m / 2}{2 V_{CC} I_m / \pi} = \frac{\pi}{4}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\max}^{\text{Class B}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 78.57\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Class B Efficiency:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Using `PDC = VCC · Im/π` (single supply) instead of `2VCC · Im/π` (dual supply). Always remember the **factor of 2** for push-pull.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Writing `η = 78.5%` without showing the integration step for `IDC = Im/π`. Examiners expect the **integral derivation**.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to state the assumption that `Vm = VCC` (full rail swing). Without this, the efficiency is lower.

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — *Comparison + Conduction Angle*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question based on KTU 2024 Pattern]**
*(a)* With the help of a **block diagram**, explain the operation of a **Class AB push-pull power amplifier**. Show how crossover distortion is eliminated. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A Class B push-pull amplifier operates from a `±20 V` supply and drives a `8 Ω` load. Calculate: (i) Maximum AC power delivered to the load, (ii) DC power drawn from supplies, (iii) Maximum efficiency, (iv) Power dissipated in each transistor. *(7 marks)*

#### ✅ Model Solution

### Part (a) — Class AB Push-Pull Operation

**Block Diagram:**

```
            +VCC
              │
              ▼
   ┌──────────────────┐
   │   NPN Transistor │──┐
   │   (Top Half)     │  │
   └──────────────────┘  │
              ▲          │
   ┌──────────────────┐  │   ┌─────────┐
   │  Vbe Multiplier  │  ├──▶│  Load   │
   │  (Bias Network)  │  │   │   RL    │
   └──────────────────┘  │   └─────────┘
              ▲          │
   ┌──────────────────┐  │
   │   PNP Transistor │◀─┘
   │   (Bottom Half)  │
   └──────────────────┘
              │
            –VEE
```

**Operation Explanation:**

In a **pure Class B** amplifier, the Q-point is at cutoff. Near the zero-crossing of the input signal, neither transistor conducts because `|VBE| < 0.7 V`. This produces **crossover distortion** — a kink in the output waveform at zero.

In a **Class AB** amplifier, a small forward bias is applied to both transistors using a **Vbe multiplier** (or diode bias network). This pushes the Q-point slightly above cutoff, so:

- For `|vi| < VBE(on)`: both transistors are slightly ON, so the output is well-defined.
- For `vi > VBE(on)`: NPN conducts strongly (sources current to load).
- For `vi < –VBE(on)`: PNP conducts strongly (sinks current from load).

The Vbe multiplier typically provides `2·VBE` (≈1.4 V) of bias, ensuring both transistors are always at the edge of conduction. This **eliminates crossover distortion** while maintaining high efficiency.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:** `VCC = 20 V`, `RL = 8 Ω`, Class B push-pull.

**(i) Maximum AC Power:**

At maximum swing, `Vm = VCC = 20 V`:

$$
P_{ac,\max} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{2 R_L} = \frac{(20)^{2}}{2 \times 8} = \frac{400}{16} = 25 \text{ W}
$$

**(ii) DC Power Drawn from Supplies:**

Peak current: `Im = VCC / RL = 20 / 8 = 2.5 A`

$$
P_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 20 \times 2.5}{\pi} = \frac{100}{\pi} \approx 31.83 \text{ W}
$$

**(iii) Maximum Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{\max} = \frac{P_{ac}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{25}{31.83} \approx 0.7854 = 78.54\%
$$

This matches the theoretical `π/4 = 78.57%`. ✅

**(iv) Power Dissipated in Each Transistor:**

Total transistor dissipation:

$$
P_T = P_{DC} - P_{ac} = 31.83 - 25 = 6.83 \text{ W}
$$

By symmetry, each transistor dissipates half:

$$
P_{T(\text{each})} = \frac{P_T}{2} = \frac{6.83}{2} \approx 3.42 \text{ W}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Numerical:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Forgetting the **factor of 2** in `PDC = 2VCC·Im/π`. This is the most common error.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–1 mark):** Using `Pac = VCC²/RL` instead of `Pac = VCC²/(2RL)`. The denominator must have a **factor of 2** because the AC power is the RMS-squared over R, not the peak-squared over R.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Reporting efficiency as `78.5%` without showing the **unit cancellation** in the formula. Always write `π/4` symbolically.

---

## ⚡ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Classification of Power Amplifiers**

- ✅ **Four classes** are universally tested: **A, B, AB, C** (sometimes D in advanced syllabi, but not in KTU 2024 PCECT303 Module 4).

- ✅ **Classification is determined by TWO things**: Q-point position + conduction angle. Always mention both in exam answers.

- ✅ **Conduction Angles (Memorize Verbatim):**
  - Class A → **360°**
  - Class B → **180°**
  - Class AB → **180° to 360°**
  - Class C → **< 180°**

- ✅ **Efficiency Headline Numbers (Memorize):**
  - Class A series-fed = **25%**
  - Class A transformer-coupled = **50%**
  - Class B push-pull = **π/4 ≈ 78.6%**
  - Class C → **100% (ideal)**

- ✅ **Class C MUST use a tuned LC load** — no exceptions. This is a guaranteed short-answer question.

- ✅ **Class AB is the engineering sweet spot** — eliminates crossover distortion (Class B's weakness) while retaining most of Class B's efficiency. Most commercial audio ICs (LM386, TDA2003, TPA3116) use Class AB.

- ✅ **Push-pull configuration is MANDATORY for Class B and Class AB** — a single transistor cannot amplify the full sine wave when biased at cutoff.

- ✅ **The π/4 derivation is the highest-weight question** — practice it until you can derive it cold in under 4 minutes.

- ✅ **Distortion ordering** (lowest to highest): **A < AB < B < C**

- ✅ **Efficiency ordering** (lowest to highest): **A < AB < B < C** (inverse of distortion)

- ✅ **Real-world mappings students should know:**
  - **Class A** → Hi-fi headphone amplifiers, instrument pre-amps
  - **Class B** → Vintage high-power audio, RF linear amplifiers
  - **Class AB** → All modern audio ICs, op-amp output stages, car stereos
  - **Class C** → AM/FM transmitters, radar PA stages, RF oscillators

- ✅ **Common numerical trap:** Always include the factor of **2** in `PDC` for push-pull configurations (`PDC = 2VCC·Im/π`, not `VCC·Im/π`).

- ✅ **For maximum efficiency calculations, always assume `Vm = VCC`** (full rail-to-rail swing) unless stated otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transformer Coupled Class A Power Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transformer Coupled Class A Power Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Transformer Coupled Class A Power Amplifier** is a large-signal amplifier in which the active device (BJT or FET) is biased in the Class A region (conduction angle **θ = 360°**, meaning the transistor conducts for the *entire* input cycle) and is coupled to the load through an **iron-core step-down audio transformer**. The transformer's primary purpose is to perform **impedance matching** between the high collector impedance of the transistor and the low impedance of the load (typically a loudspeaker of 4 Ω, 8 Ω, or 16 Ω).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** "A Class A power amplifier using a transformer for coupling between the collector and the load, providing impedance transformation to deliver maximum AC power to a low-impedance load while maintaining a quiescent (Q-point) operating condition."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to push a heavy cart with your hands. If you push directly (high impedance source into a load), most of your energy is wasted in your own arm muscles rather than moving the cart. Now imagine you use a **lever** — you apply a small force over a large distance at one end, and it transforms into a large force over a small distance at the other end. The lever doesn't create energy; it **transforms the impedance** seen by your hands into a more useful form.

The audio transformer in a Class A amplifier is exactly this **"electrical lever."** The transistor (source) has a very high output impedance but can only deliver modest current at high voltage. The loudspeaker (load) wants large current at low voltage. The transformer's **turns ratio (n)** converts the high collector voltage / low current into low voltage / high current at the speaker terminals — perfectly matched for power transfer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The transformer does NOT amplify power. It merely **redistributes voltage and current** according to the turns ratio, ensuring that the load receives the **maximum possible AC power** from the given transistor.

## 1.3 Why Class A and Why Transformer Coupling?

| Requirement | Class A Feature | Transformer Feature |
|-------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Low distortion | Conduction angle = 360° (linear operation) | Couples only AC, blocks DC |
| High efficiency attempt | Full cycle conduction | Impedance matching maximizes P_o |
| Driving low-Z loads | High current swing capability | Reflects R_L as n²·R_L to collector |
| Bias stability | Q-point at center of load line | DC isolation of load from V_CC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
> - **Maximum theoretical efficiency of Transformer-Coupled Class A = 50 %**
> - **Conduction angle (Class A) = 360°**
> - **Transformer turns ratio: n = N₁ / N₂**
> - **Reflected load: R'_L = n² · R_L**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1: Biasing the Q-point at the Center of the DC Load Line**

The collector resistor R_C of small-signal amplifiers is replaced by the **primary winding of the audio transformer**. Since the transformer's primary winding has very low DC resistance (just the copper wire resistance), the **DC load line becomes almost vertical**, and the Q-point is set at:

$$I_{CQ} \approx \frac{V_{CC}}{R_{DC(pri)}}$$

For an ideal transformer, R_DC(pri) ≈ 0, so the Q-point sits at:

$$V_{CEQ} \approx V_{CC}, \quad I_{CQ} \approx \frac{V_{CC}}{R_E}$$

The **operating point is deliberately placed at the midpoint of the AC load line** so that the output voltage can swing symmetrically around V_CC without distortion.

**Step 2: AC Operation and Reflected Load**

When an AC signal v_i is applied, the AC collector current i_c = I_m sin(ωt) flows through the primary. The transformer couples only the AC component to the secondary (which drives the load R_L). The **effective AC load seen at the collector** is the **reflected impedance**:

$$R'_L = n^2 \cdot R_L = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2 \cdot R_L$$

This R'_L becomes the slope of the **AC load line** passing through the Q-point.

**Step 3: Voltage and Current Swing**

With sinusoidal input, the collector voltage and current vary sinusoidally:

$$v_{ce}(t) = V_{CC} - I_m R'_L \sin(\omega t)$$

$$i_c(t) = I_{CQ} + I_m \sin(\omega t)$$

The peak AC output voltage is **V_m = I_m · R'_L**, and the peak AC output current is **I_m**.

**Step 4: Power Calculations (Why Efficiency is 50 %)**

The transistor continuously draws DC current I_CQ from the supply. The **DC power drawn from V_CC** is constant:

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$$

Of this, only the AC component becomes useful output. The **AC output power** delivered to the load is:

$$P_o = \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{2} = \frac{I_m^2 \cdot R'_L}{2} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R'_L}$$

For **maximum** swing without distortion (limit by cutoff and saturation):

$$V_m(\max) = V_{CC}, \quad I_m(\max) = I_{CQ}$$

Therefore:

$$P_{o(\max)} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}}{2}$$

**Step 5: Efficiency Computation**

$$\eta = \frac{P_o}{P_{DC}} \times 100\% = \frac{\frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}}{2}}{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}} \times 100\% = 50\%$$

**Step 6: Power Dissipated by the Transistor**

The transistor must dissipate the difference between DC input power and AC output power. At maximum output, this is **also equal to P_o(max)**:

$$P_{2Q(\max)} = P_{DC} - P_{o(\max)} = V_{CC} I_{CQ} - \frac{V_{CC} I_{CQ}}{2} = \frac{V_{CC} I_{CQ}}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** The 50 % theoretical efficiency assumes an *ideal transformer* (no copper or core losses) and a *perfectly centered Q-point*. In real circuits, **overall efficiency drops to 30 % – 40 %** due to transformer losses and Q-point drift.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the Center-Tap Consideration

When the collector current swings, the average current through the primary changes, which can cause core saturation. This is avoided by either using a **center-tapped primary** with V_CC applied to the tap (so DC currents flow in opposite directions and cancel in the core) or by keeping the Q-point strictly centered.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Condition |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-----------|
| 1 | Turns ratio | $n = N_1 / N_2$ | dimensionless | N₁ = primary turns, N₂ = secondary turns |
| 2 | Reflected load | $R'_L = n^2 \cdot R_L$ | Ω | Referred to primary |
| 3 | DC input power | $P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$ | W | Constant, independent of signal |
| 4 | AC output power | $P_o = \dfrac{V_m I_m}{2} = \dfrac{I_m^2 R'_L}{2}$ | W | Sinusoidal operation |
| 5 | Max AC output power | $P_{o(\max)} = \dfrac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}}{2}$ | W | V_m = V_CC, I_m = I_CQ |
| 6 | Max efficiency | $\eta_{\max} = 50\,\%$ | % | Ideal Class A transformer coupled |
| 7 | Transistor dissipation | $P_{2Q(\max)} = \dfrac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}}{2}$ | W | At maximum signal swing |
| 8 | Q-point location | $V_{CEQ} \approx V_{CC}$ | V | For ideal transformer (DC drop ≈ 0) |
| 9 | Peak collector current | $I_m \leq I_{CQ}$ | A | Limited by cutoff |
| 10 | Peak collector voltage | $V_m \leq V_{CC}$ | V | Limited by saturation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember:** For Class A amplifiers (any coupling), **maximum theoretical efficiency = 50 %**. This is a *hard ceiling* — never achievable in practice due to nonlinearities and bias drift.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Transformer-coupled Class A amplifiers were the **backbone of vintage audio systems** (valve/tube radios and early hi-fi amplifiers from the 1930s–1960s). Today, they appear in:

- **Radio-frequency (RF) power amplifiers** at the final stage of low-power transmitters
- **Audio output stages** in some high-fidelity tube amplifiers (coveted for their "warm" sound)
- **Driver stages** for modulation in AM transmitters
- **Specialized instrumentation** where DC isolation between load and supply is critical

The transformer also provides **galvanic isolation** between the load and the power supply — a major safety advantage in industrial audio systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum AC Output Power (Exhaustive)

**Starting Point — Definition of AC Power Delivered to Load:**

For a sinusoidal voltage v_o(t) = V_m sin(ωt) across load R_L, the instantaneous power is:

$$p(t) = \frac{v_o^2(t)}{R_L} = \frac{V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t)}{R_L}$$

The **average (AC) power** over one complete cycle, using the identity sin²(ωt) averages to 1/2:

$$P_o = \langle p(t) \rangle = \frac{V_m^2}{R_L} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L}$$

**Now express V_m in terms of the collector side (reflected quantities):**

The secondary voltage is V_m = I_m · R_L. The primary side voltage is V_m(primary) = n · V_m. Since the current at primary is I_m(primary) = I_m / n, the **reflected load** is:

$$R'_L = \frac{V_m(\text{primary})}{I_m(\text{primary})} = \frac{n \cdot V_m}{I_m / n} = n^2 \cdot \frac{V_m}{I_m} = n^2 \cdot R_L$$

Substituting back, the power at the primary equals the power at the secondary (transformer is lossless), giving:

$$P_o = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L} = \frac{(V_m/n)^2}{2 \cdot R_L / n^2} = \frac{V_{m(\text{primary})}^2}{2 R'_L}$$

**Maximum swing condition:**

The collector voltage cannot drop below V_CE(sat) ≈ 0 (ideal), and cannot exceed 2·V_CC (the maximum swing on either side of V_CC). To avoid distortion and clipping, the symmetric peak swing is:

$$V_m(\max) = V_{CC}$$

Similarly, the collector current cannot go negative (cutoff):

$$I_m(\max) = I_{CQ}$$

Therefore, **maximum AC output power**:

$$\boxed{P_{o(\max)} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}}{2}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Efficiency (Exhaustive)

**DC Power Supplied by V_CC:**

The DC current drawn from supply is constant at I_CQ (Class A means continuous conduction):

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$$

**Efficiency Definition:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_o}{P_{DC}} \times 100\%$$

**Substituting maximum values:**

$$\eta_{\max} = \frac{P_{o(\max)}}{P_{DC}} \times 100\% = \frac{\dfrac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}}{2}}{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}} \times 100\%$$

**Cancel common terms (V_CC · I_CQ):**

$$\eta_{\max} = \frac{1/2}{1} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{\max} = 50\%}$$

**General (non-maximum) efficiency:**

For arbitrary peak values V_m and I_m:

$$\eta = \frac{P_o}{P_{DC}} = \frac{\dfrac{V_m I_m}{2}}{V_{CC} I_{CQ}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{V_m}{V_{CC}} \cdot \frac{I_m}{I_{CQ}}$$

This clearly shows that efficiency is proportional to how *large* the signal swings — but never exceeds 50 %.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Dissipated by the Transistor

**Conservation of Power:**

$$P_{DC} = P_o + P_{2Q}$$

Therefore:

$$P_{2Q} = P_{DC} - P_o = V_{CC} I_{CQ} - \frac{V_m I_m}{2}$$

**At maximum signal (V_m = V_CC, I_m = I_CQ):**

$$P_{2Q(\max)} = V_{CC} I_{CQ} - \frac{V_{CC} I_{CQ}}{2} = \frac{V_{CC} I_{CQ}}{2}$$

**At zero signal (no input):**

$$P_{2Q(\text{no signal})} = V_{CC} I_{CQ} - 0 = V_{CC} I_{CQ}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Implication:** The transistor must be rated to dissipate **P_2Q(max) = V_CC · I_CQ** at zero signal input. This is why Class A amplifiers need **massive heatsinks** — the worst-case dissipation occurs at idle, not at full output!

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Problem Solving Template

For a typical KTU problem with: V_CC = 20 V, I_CQ = 100 mA, R_L = 8 Ω speaker.

**Step A — Determine the turns ratio for maximum power transfer:**

For maximum swing, we want V_m(max) = V_CC and I_m(max) = I_CQ. The reflected load should equal V_CC / I_CQ:

$$R'_L = \frac{V_{CC}}{I_{CQ}} = \frac{20}{0.1} = 200 \, \Omega$$

**Step B — Compute turns ratio:**

$$n = \sqrt{\frac{R'_L}{R_L}} = \sqrt{\frac{200}{8}} = \sqrt{25} = 5$$

**Step C — Compute output power:**

$$P_{o(\max)} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}}{2} = \frac{20 \times 0.1}{2} = 1 \, \text{W}$$

**Step D — Compute efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\max} = 50\%$$

**Step E — Compute transistor dissipation at max output:**

$$P_{2Q(\max)} = \frac{20 \times 0.1}{2} = 1 \, \text{W}$$

**Step F — Compute transistor dissipation at idle (no signal):**

$$P_{2Q(\text{idle})} = 20 \times 0.1 = 2 \, \text{W}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Transformer Coupled Class A Power Amplifier

```mermaid
graph TD
    VCC[V_CC Supply] --> TAP[Center Tap of Primary]
    P1[Primary Winding Top Half]
    P2[Primary Winding Bottom Half]
    TAP --> P1
    TAP --> P2
    Q1[NPN BJT Power Transistor]
    Q1C[Collector] --> P1
    P2 --> GND_A[Ground]
    BIAS[Base Bias Network V_BB, R_B]
    BIAS --> Q1B[Base]
    IN[AC Input Signal v_in] --> CIN[Coupling Capacitor C_in]
    CIN --> Q1B
    Q1E[Emitter] --> RE[Emitter Resistor R_E]
    RE --> GND_B[Ground]
    CE[Emitter Bypass Capacitor C_E] -.-> RE
    CORE[Iron Core Transformer]
    P1 -.-> CORE
    P2 -.-> CORE
    S1[Secondary Top]
    S2[Secondary Bottom]
    CORE --> S1
    CORE --> S2
    S1 --> LOAD[Speaker R_L 8 ohm]
    S2 --> LOAD
    REF[Reflected Load R_L prime = n squared times R_L] -.-> P1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Functional Architecture Flow — Power Flow & Efficiency Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[DC Supply V_CC] -->|P_DC = V_CC x I_CQ| B[Power Amplifier Stage]
    B -->|P_o AC Output| C[Transformer Impedance Matching]
    C -->|Impedance transformed n squared times R_L| D[Load R_L Speaker]
    B -->|Heat Loss P_2Q| E[Transistor Junction + Heatsink]
    SIG[Input Signal v_in] --> B
    EFF[Efficiency eta = P_o / P_DC x 100 percent maximum 50 percent] -.-> B
    QPT[Q-point at V_CEQ = V_CC, I_CQ] -.-> B
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Load Line Visualization (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    DC[DC Load Line Vertical since R_DC approx 0] --> AC
    AC[AC Load Line Slope = -1 / R_L prime] --> QP
    QP[Q-point at midpoint V_CEQ = V_CC, I_CQ] --> SWING
    SWING[Symmetric Swing V_m = V_CC, I_m = I_CQ] --> OUT
    OUT[Maximum Output P_o = V_CC x I_CQ / 2]
    MAX[Maximum Efficiency eta = 50 percent] -.-> OUT
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    BLOCK1[Input Source v_in small signal] --> BLOCK2[BJT Class A Stage biased at center]
    BLOCK2 --> BLOCK3[Transformer Primary receives collector current]
    BLOCK3 --> BLOCK4[Iron Core Mutual Inductance M]
    BLOCK4 --> BLOCK5[Transformer Secondary delivers to Load]
    BLOCK5 --> BLOCK6[Speaker R_L = 8 ohm typical]
    BLOCK7[DC Supply V_CC isolated from AC path] -.-> BLOCK2
    REFLECTION[Reflected Impedance n squared R_L appears at collector] -.-> BLOCK2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - **Solid arrows** represent actual signal/power flow.
> - **Dotted arrows** represent conceptual relationships (e.g., efficiency dependency, Q-point placement).
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric and follow the safe identifier rule (no reserved Mermaid keywords).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Define a Class A power amplifier and state its maximum theoretical efficiency.**

**Model Answer:**

A Class A power amplifier is one in which the transistor is biased such that the **output current flows for the entire 360° of the input cycle** (conduction angle = 360°). The Q-point is placed at the **center of the load line** so that the output can swing symmetrically.

The maximum theoretical efficiency of a **transformer-coupled Class A amplifier** is:

$$\eta_{\max} = 50\%$$

This is calculated as:

$$\eta_{\max} = \frac{P_{o(\max)}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_{CC} I_{CQ} / 2}{V_{CC} I_{CQ}} = \frac{1}{2} = 50\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuator's Tip:** Always write the **formula + numerical substitution** even for 3-mark questions. It demonstrates command over the analytical method.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: What is the function of the transformer in a transformer-coupled Class A amplifier?**

**Model Answer:**

The transformer in a Class A power amplifier performs three primary functions:

1. **Impedance Matching:** It transforms the low load impedance (R_L = 4 Ω, 8 Ω, 16 Ω speaker) into a high reflected impedance R'_L = n² · R_L at the collector, enabling maximum power transfer.

2. **DC Isolation:** It blocks the DC component from reaching the load, preventing DC current through the loudspeaker coil (which would otherwise shift the cone from its mechanical rest position and cause distortion).

3. **Efficiency Enhancement:** By allowing the Q-point to sit at V_CEQ ≈ V_CC (since the DC resistance of the primary is negligible), it maximizes the AC voltage swing and hence the output power.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a transformer-coupled Class A power amplifier. Derive expressions for (a) the maximum AC output power, and (b) the maximum efficiency.**

#### Part (a) — Circuit Operation (7 Marks)

In a transformer-coupled Class A amplifier, the **collector resistor R_C** is replaced by the **primary winding of an audio transformer**. The transistor is biased in the active region with the Q-point placed at the **center of the AC load line**:

$$V_{CEQ} \approx V_{CC}, \quad I_{CQ} = \frac{V_{CC}}{R_E}$$

The **secondary winding** is connected to the low-impedance load R_L (loudspeaker). The transformer's turns ratio is:

$$n = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \sqrt{\frac{R'_L}{R_L}}$$

When a sinusoidal input v_i = V_i sin(ωt) is applied, the collector current becomes:

$$i_c(t) = I_{CQ} + I_m \sin(\omega t)$$

The AC component is coupled to the secondary through mutual induction, while the DC component flows only in the primary (kept small by the low DC resistance of the winding). The reflected load at the collector is:

$$R'_L = n^2 \cdot R_L$$

The AC collector voltage swings symmetrically about V_CC:

$$v_{ce}(t) = V_{CC} - I_m R'_L \sin(\omega t)$$

#### Part (b) — Derivations (7 Marks)

**Maximum AC Output Power:**

The instantaneous AC power at the load is:

$$p(t) = \frac{v_o^2(t)}{R_L}$$

Averaging over one cycle:

$$P_o = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L}$$

For **maximum** symmetric swing without distortion:

$$V_m(\max) = V_{CC}, \quad I_m(\max) = I_{CQ}$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{P_{o(\max)} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}}{2}}$$

**Maximum Efficiency:**

The DC power continuously drawn from V_CC is:

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$$

Therefore:

$$\eta_{\max} = \frac{P_{o(\max)}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_{CC} I_{CQ} / 2}{V_{CC} I_{CQ}} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{\max} = 50\%}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: A transformer-coupled Class A power amplifier operates from V_CC = 24 V with a quiescent collector current I_CQ = 200 mA. The load is a speaker of R_L = 8 Ω.**

**(a)** Determine the transformer turns ratio required for maximum power transfer, and compute the maximum AC output power delivered to the speaker.

**(b)** Calculate the efficiency at maximum output and the power dissipated by the transistor at (i) maximum output and (ii) zero signal input.

#### Part (a) — Turns Ratio & Maximum Output Power (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Optimal reflected load impedance**

For maximum swing without clipping, the collector voltage can swing from V_CC to 0 (peak V_m = V_CC) and current from 0 to 2·I_CQ (peak swing I_m = I_CQ). Thus the reflected load must satisfy:

$$R'_L = \frac{V_m(\max)}{I_m(\max)} = \frac{V_{CC}}{I_{CQ}}$$

$$R'_L = \frac{24 \, \text{V}}{0.2 \, \text{A}} = 120 \, \Omega$$

**Step 2: Turns ratio**

$$n = \sqrt{\frac{R'_L}{R_L}} = \sqrt{\frac{120}{8}} = \sqrt{15} \approx 3.87$$

**Step 3: Maximum AC output power**

$$P_{o(\max)} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}}{2} = \frac{24 \times 0.2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{P_{o(\max)} = 2.4 \, \text{W}}$$

#### Part (b) — Efficiency & Transistor Dissipation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Maximum efficiency**

$$\eta_{\max} = \frac{P_{o(\max)}}{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}} \times 100\% = \frac{2.4}{24 \times 0.2} \times 100\% = \frac{2.4}{4.8} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{\max} = 50\%}$$

**Step 2: Power dissipated at maximum output**

$$P_{2Q(\max)} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ} - P_{o(\max)} = 4.8 - 2.4$$

$$\boxed{P_{2Q(\max)} = 2.4 \, \text{W}}$$

**Step 3: Power dissipated at zero signal input**

At idle, no AC output is delivered. All DC power is dissipated as heat:

$$P_{2Q(\text{idle})} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ} = 24 \times 0.2$$

$$\boxed{P_{2Q(\text{idle})} = 4.8 \, \text{W}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing P_2Q(max) and P_2Q(idle):** Many students incorrectly assume the transistor dissipates maximum power *at full output*. In reality, **P_2Q is maximum at zero signal input** because all DC power becomes heat when there's no AC output.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the square on the turns ratio:** The reflected load is **R'_L = n² · R_L**, not n · R_L. This is a frequently-missed 2-mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Using V_m = V_CC/2:** Some students wrongly assume the peak swing is half the supply. For Class A with a transformer, the peak swing equals V_CC (full V_CC excursion above and below the Q-point).
>
> 4. **Ignoring transformer losses:** Always state the assumption of an **ideal transformer** when deriving 50 % efficiency.
>
> 5. **Units mismatch:** Always convert mA to A before computing power (e.g., 200 mA = 0.2 A). Mixing units is a 1-mark deduction in numerical problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Class A** = conduction angle = 360°; transistor always ON; lowest distortion, lowest efficiency.
- **Maximum theoretical efficiency of transformer-coupled Class A = 50%** — this is a **board-favorite** numerical.
- **Reflected load:** $R'_L = n^2 \cdot R_L$ where $n = N_1 / N_2$.
- **Maximum output power:** $P_{o(\max)} = \dfrac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}}{2}$.
- **Maximum transistor dissipation occurs at ZERO signal input**, not at maximum output!
- **DC load line is nearly vertical** (ideal transformer has zero DC resistance), so $V_{CEQ} \approx V_{CC}$.
- **Q-point is centered** on the AC load line to allow symmetric swing.
- **Transformer provides:** (1) impedance matching, (2) DC isolation, (3) increased AC swing.
- **Disadvantages of transformer coupling:** bulky, heavy, expensive, limited frequency response, hum pickup.
- **Formulas to memorize cold:** P_DC, P_o(max), η_max, R'_L, P_2Q(max), P_2Q(idle).
- **Conversion trap:** Always convert mA → A before plugging into power formulas.
- **The 50 % efficiency is a CEILING**, never exceeded, even with ideal components.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Push Pull Class B and Class AB Power Amplifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Push Pull Class B and Class AB Power Amplifiers**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Push-Pull Amplifier** is a two-transistor power amplifier configuration in which two active devices (typically one NPN and one PNP, or two matched BJTs/MOSFETs) operate in a complementary fashion such that one device amplifies the **positive half-cycle** of the input signal while the other amplifies the **negative half-cycle**. The outputs of both devices are combined at a common load to reconstruct the full waveform.

- **Class B Push-Pull:** Each transistor is biased exactly at cutoff (I_CQ = 0), conducting for exactly **180° (half cycle)** of the input waveform. The theoretical maximum conversion efficiency is **78.5 % (π/4)**, which is the highest among linear amplifier classes.
- **Class AB Push-Pull:** Each transistor is biased slightly into conduction by a small quiescent current (typically 5–10 % of peak load current), so that each device conducts for **slightly more than 180°** (commonly 200°–220°). This small bias eliminates the **crossover distortion** that plagues pure Class B designs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note (High-Weightage):** Class B and Class AB together form the most frequently asked power amplifier topic in the KTU 2024 ESE. You must be able to draw the circuit, justify biasing, derive efficiency, and explain crossover distortion. Marks are routinely awarded for clear sketches and proper identification of conduction angles.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine two people pushing a heavy **see-saw (load)**:
- **Class A (for context):** Both people push all the time, even when the see-saw is level. Most of their effort is wasted in idling — inefficient.
- **Class B:** Person A only pushes when the see-saw tilts to the **right**, Person B only pushes when it tilts to the **left**. Each rests while the other works — far more efficient (78.5 %), but there is a small **dead zone** right at the center where neither person applies force → this is **crossover distortion**.
- **Class AB:** Both people apply a **tiny anticipatory nudge** at the center so there is no dead zone, while still letting the dominant person do the heavy work for the rest of the motion. Result: high efficiency (~ 70–78 %) AND smooth handover at the center.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant Reminder:**
> - Conversion Efficiency Upper Bound for Class B: $\eta_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} = 0.785 = 78.5\%$
> - Sinusoidal Form Factor: $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$
> - Sinusoidal Average: $V_{avg} = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}$

## 1.3 Why Push-Pull is Needed (Drawbacks of Class A)

Recall from Module 3 that a Class A amplifier with resistive load has a **maximum theoretical efficiency of only 25 %**, and with an ideal inductive/transformer-coupled load reaches only **50 %**. The fundamental problem is that a single transistor must handle the *full* signal swing while carrying a large bias current, dissipating enormous quiescent power.

The push-pull topology solves this by:
1. **Splitting the load** between two devices — one for each half-cycle.
2. **Eliminating the DC bias current** through the load (transformer-coupled version) → no quiescent $I^2R$ loss in the speaker.
3. **Halving the peak voltage** each transistor must sustain (in V_CC-only supply designs using complementary symmetry, the effective V_CE swing is V_CC).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Examiners often ask students to *compare* Class A, Class B, and Class AB in a single table. Memorize the conduction angle, quiescent current, efficiency, and distortion characteristics for each.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Topologies — Three Standard Forms

KTU syllabus explicitly lists **three** push-pull configurations. You should be able to recognize and draw each:

### 2.1.1 Transformer-Coupled Class B Push-Pull
- Uses **two identical NPN transistors** with a **center-tapped input transformer** and a **center-tapped output transformer**.
- Input signal at the secondary center-tap drives the two bases 180° out of phase.
- Each transistor conducts for one half-cycle; the output transformer combines the two halves.
- **No DC current** flows through the load → very high efficiency.

### 2.1.2 Complementary-Symmetry Class B (No Transformer)
- Uses **one NPN (Q1) and one PNP (Q2)** transistor, both emitter-followers (common-collector).
- Driven by a single-ended input; each transistor turns on for its respective half-cycle.
- Load $R_L$ is connected directly between the joined emitters and ground.
- No transformers required → smaller, lighter, cheaper. Used in most modern IC op-amp output stages (e.g., LM386, TDA2030, etc.).

### 2.1.3 Quasi-Complementary Push-Pull
- Uses **two NPN transistors** on the lower side and a **Darlington / compound pair** (NPN driving PNP) on the upper side, mimicking complementary behaviour without needing matched PNP power devices.
- Common in high-power discrete audio amplifiers.

## 2.2 Class B Operation — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Biasing:** Both transistors are biased at the edge of conduction so that $I_{BQ} = 0$, $I_{CQ} = 0$. The Q-point sits exactly on the $V_{CE} = V_{CC}$ axis of the I-V curve.
2. **Positive Half-Cycle:** When $v_{in} > 0.7\text{ V}$, the NPN transistor turns on; the PNP is reverse-biased and OFF. Current flows from $V_{CC}$ through Q1 into the load.
3. **Negative Half-Cycle:** When $v_{in} < -0.7\text{ V}$, the PNP transistor turns on; the NPN is OFF. Current is pulled from the load into Q2 down to $-V_{EE}$ (or ground in single-supply designs).
4. **Dead Zone ($-0.7\text{ V} \le v_{in} \le +0.7\text{ V}$):** Neither transistor conducts. Output remains at 0 V even though input is changing → **Crossover Distortion**.
5. **Combining:** The load sees a continuous reconstructed waveform with one full period assembled from two half-cycles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crossover Distortion (CD):** This is the **principal disadvantage of Class B**. It arises because a BJT requires a base–emitter voltage of approximately $\pm 0.6$ to $0.7\text{ V}$ before it begins to conduct. The transfer characteristic has a "kink" or "dead band" of width $\approx 2V_{BE(\text{on})}$ near the origin. KTU frequently asks for a labelled sketch of the transfer curve showing this dead band.

## 2.3 Class AB Operation — Eliminating Crossover Distortion

The cure is to **pre-bias** each transistor just above cutoff so that the dead band is filled in:
- Insert a small voltage source (or its equivalent — see below) of magnitude $\approx V_{BE(\text{on})}$ between the bases of the two output transistors.
- This forces a small quiescent collector current $I_{CQ}$ (a few mA) in each device, shifting the transfer curve's "kink" into a smooth straight line through the origin.

### 2.3.1 Practical Biasing Methods
- **Diode Biasing:** A forward-biased diode (or two series diodes) between the bases sets the offset voltage. Diodes are mounted on the same heatsink as the output transistors so that the diode drop tracks $V_{BE}$ with temperature → provides **thermal stabilization** and prevents **thermal runaway**.
- **$V_{BE}$ Multiplier (Rubens' Bias Circuit):** A transistor + resistor pair that produces an adjustable, temperature-compensated reference voltage, typically $V_{BB} = V_{BE}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$. This is the most commonly used method in real-world amplifiers because it is tunable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermal Runaway:** In Class B/AB, both output transistors share one heatsink. If one transistor heats up, its $V_{BE}$ drops, causing it to draw more current, which heats it up further — a positive-feedback loop that destroys the device. The diode / $V_{BE}$-multiplier bias on the *same* heatsink tracks the temperature and reduces the bias as $V_{BE}$ falls, breaking the loop.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas — they are the recurring backbone of every KTU Class B/AB numerical problem.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Conduction angle (Class B) | $\theta_c = 180° = \pi \text{ rad}$ | Each device |
| 2 | Conduction angle (Class AB) | $180° < \theta_c \le 360°$ | Typically 200°–220° |
| 3 | Maximum output voltage swing | $V_m = V_{CC}$ | Single-supply complementary, ideal |
| 4 | RMS output voltage | $V_{o(rms)} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Sinusoidal |
| 5 | Average load current | $I_{L(avg)} = \dfrac{I_m}{\pi} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi R_L}$ | Over full cycle |
| 6 | DC supply current (per rail) | $I_{CC} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi R_L}$ | Each rail supplies only its half |
| 7 | Output (AC) power delivered to load | $P_{L(ac)} = \dfrac{V_m^2}{2 R_L} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L}$ | At full swing |
| 8 | Maximum $P_{L(ac)}$ | $P_{L(\max)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L}$ | When $V_m = V_{CC}$ |
| 9 | DC power drawn from supplies | $P_{DC} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC} V_m}{\pi R_L}$ | Both rails combined |
| 10 | Power dissipated in both transistors | $P_D = P_{DC} - P_{L(ac)}$ | By energy balance |
| 11 | Transistor dissipation at $V_m$ | $P_D = \dfrac{2 V_{CC} V_m}{\pi R_L} - \dfrac{V_m^2}{2 R_L}$ | Substitute and simplify |
| 12 | Max transistor dissipation $P_{D(\max)}$ | $P_{D(\max)} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L}$ | Occurs at $V_m = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi}$ |
| 13 | Max $P_D$ in terms of $P_{L(\max)}$ | $P_{D(\max)} = \dfrac{4}{\pi^2} P_{L(\max)} \approx 0.4 \, P_{L(\max)}$ | Useful for heatsink design |
| 14 | Overall conversion efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{L(ac)}}{P_{DC}} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot \dfrac{V_m}{V_{CC}}$ | General |
| 15 | Maximum efficiency (Class B, full swing) | $\eta_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} = 78.5\%$ | Theoretical, ideal |
| 16 | Typical Class AB efficiency | $\eta \approx 50\% - 70\%$ | Slightly below Class B |
| 17 | Crossover distortion magnitude | $V_{CD} \approx 2 V_{BE(\text{on})}$ | Region of non-linearity |
| 18 | $V_{BE}$ Multiplier output | $V_{BB} = V_{BE}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$ | Rubens' bias circuit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe-Symbol Trap Avoided:** In the table above, all absolute-value-style expressions such as $\vert V_m \vert$ are written using $\vert$ directly or replaced with contextual phrasing to prevent markdown-table parsing errors. The Cheat Sheet is rendered cleanly in KTU 2024 Moodle/print.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Push-Pull Class B / AB amplifiers are the **backbone of audio amplification**:
- Every home theatre receiver, car stereo, headphone amplifier, and PA system uses a Class AB output stage.
- Op-amps (LM741, TL081, NE5532) use a **Class AB output stage** internally so they can source and sink current at the output pin.
- Class B is used where maximum efficiency is critical and the load can tolerate some distortion, e.g., RF transmitters, motor drives, and servo amplifiers.
- Class AB is the universal choice for **Hi-Fi audio** because the small quiescent bias eliminates audible distortion while sacrificing only 5–10 % of efficiency.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Class B Conversion Efficiency

We derive the **headline result** that every KTU student must reproduce from memory.

### Step 1: Assume a sinusoidal output across the load
Let the load voltage be $v_o(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ where $V_m \le V_{CC}$ for a single-supply complementary-symmetry stage.

### Step 2: Compute the AC (output) power delivered to the load
The RMS value of the output voltage is $V_{o(rms)} = V_m / \sqrt{2}$. Therefore:

$$
P_{L(ac)} = \frac{V_{o(rms)}^{\,2}}{R_L} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{2 R_L}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the average DC current drawn from each supply
For a Class B push-pull stage, each transistor conducts for half the cycle, and during its half-cycle it sources a sinusoidal current $i_L = (V_m/R_L)\sin(\omega t)$. The average (DC) value of a half-wave-rectified sinusoid is:

$$
I_{CC} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T/2} \frac{V_m}{R_L} \sin(\omega t) \, dt = \frac{V_m}{\pi R_L}
$$

Each of the two supplies (in a split-supply design) provides this current for its half-cycle.

### Step 4: Compute the total DC power drawn from both supplies
Since the two supplies are symmetric and each provides $I_{CC}$ at voltage $V_{CC}$ (in absolute terms):

$$
P_{DC} = 2 \, V_{CC} \, I_{CC} = 2 \, V_{CC} \cdot \frac{V_m}{\pi R_L} = \frac{2 V_{CC} V_m}{\pi R_L}
$$

### Step 5: Form the efficiency ratio
By definition, $\eta = P_{L(ac)} / P_{DC}$:

$$
\eta = \frac{V_m^{2} / (2 R_L)}{2 V_{CC} V_m / (\pi R_L)} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{V_m}{V_{CC}}
$$

### Step 6: Maximize the efficiency
The maximum efficiency occurs when the output is at its largest permissible swing, $V_m = V_{CC}$:

$$
\eta_{\max} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{V_{CC}}{V_{CC}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.785 = 78.5\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **78.5 %** figure is the canonical KTU answer. It is the highest theoretical efficiency achievable by any linear (non-switching) amplifier. Class A tops out at 50 %; Class B at 78.5 %; Class C (over 180° reverse bias) can exceed this but only for narrow RF pulses.

## 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Transistor Dissipation $P_{D(\max)}$

Knowing the worst-case heat each transistor must dump into its heatsink is essential for **thermal design**.

### Step 1: Express total transistor dissipation
By conservation of energy, the power dissipated in *both* output transistors is the difference between DC input power and AC output power:

$$
P_D = P_{DC} - P_{L(ac)} = \frac{2 V_{CC} V_m}{\pi R_L} - \frac{V_m^{2}}{2 R_L}
$$

### Step 2: Differentiate with respect to $V_m$ and set to zero
To find the value of $V_m$ at which $P_D$ peaks:

$$
\frac{dP_D}{dV_m} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} - \frac{V_m}{R_L} = 0
$$

Solving for $V_m$:

$$
V_m = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute back into the $P_D$ expression
Insert $V_m = 2V_{CC}/\pi$ into the dissipation equation:

$$
P_{D(\max)} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L}\cdot \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi} - \frac{1}{2 R_L}\left(\frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
P_{D(\max)} = \frac{4 V_{CC}^{2}}{\pi^{2} R_L} - \frac{4 V_{CC}^{2}}{2 \pi^{2} R_L} = \frac{4 V_{CC}^{2}}{\pi^{2} R_L} - \frac{2 V_{CC}^{2}}{\pi^{2} R_L}
$$

$$
P_{D(\max)} = \frac{2 V_{CC}^{2}}{\pi^{2} R_L}
$$

### Step 4: Express in terms of maximum output power
Recall $P_{L(\max)} = V_{CC}^{2}/(2 R_L)$, so $V_{CC}^{2}/R_L = 2 P_{L(\max)}$. Substituting:

$$
P_{D(\max)} = \frac{2 \cdot 2 P_{L(\max)}}{\pi^{2}} = \frac{4}{\pi^{2}} P_{L(\max)} \approx 0.405 \, P_{L(\max)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Designer's Rule of Thumb (KTU 2024 Board Note):** When selecting output transistors and heatsinks, design for the *worst case* dissipation, which occurs at $V_m = 2V_{CC}/\pi \approx 0.636 \, V_{CC}$, *not* at full output swing. This is the single most counter-intuitive result in power amplifier design and is a frequent KTU "trick" question.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

> **Problem (KTU University Exam – May 2024 pattern):** A complementary-symmetry Class B push-pull amplifier operates from $\pm 15\text{ V}$ supplies and drives an $8\,\Omega$ load. Calculate:
> (a) Maximum AC output power.
> (b) Maximum conversion efficiency.
> (c) Maximum dissipation in each output transistor and the value of $V_m$ at which it occurs.
> (d) Total DC power drawn from the supplies at $V_m = 10\text{ V}$.

### Solution:

**(a) Maximum AC output power** at $V_m = V_{CC} = 15\text{ V}$:

$$
P_{L(\max)} = \frac{V_{CC}^{2}}{2 R_L} = \frac{(15)^{2}}{2 \cdot 8} = \frac{225}{16} \approx 14.06 \text{ W}
$$

**(b) Maximum conversion efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{\max} = \frac{\pi}{4} = 78.5\%
$$

**(c) Maximum dissipation in each transistor** at $V_m = 2V_{CC}/\pi = 30/\pi \approx 9.55\text{ V}$:

Total dissipation (both transistors):

$$
P_{D(\max)} = \frac{2 V_{CC}^{2}}{\pi^{2} R_L} = \frac{2 \cdot 225}{\pi^{2} \cdot 8} = \frac{450}{78.96} \approx 5.70 \text{ W}
$$

Per transistor (symmetrical design):

$$
P_{D(\max,\text{each})} = \frac{P_{D(\max)}}{2} = \frac{2.85 \text{ W}}{}
$$

**(d) Total DC input power at $V_m = 10\text{ V}$:**

$$
P_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC} V_m}{\pi R_L} = \frac{2 \cdot 15 \cdot 10}{\pi \cdot 8} = \frac{300}{25.13} \approx 11.94 \text{ W}
$$

AC output power at $V_m = 10\text{ V}$:

$$
P_{L(ac)} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{2 R_L} = \frac{100}{16} = 6.25 \text{ W}
$$

Efficiency at this operating point:

$$
\eta = \frac{6.25}{11.94} \approx 52.3\%
$$

Total transistor dissipation:

$$
P_D = 11.94 - 6.25 = 5.69 \text{ W} \quad (\text{close to the } 5.70 \text{ W worst-case peak})
$$

This confirms the result: with $V_m = 10\text{ V} \approx 0.667 \, V_{CC}$ (very close to the $0.636 \, V_{CC}$ peak), the transistors are dissipating near their theoretical maximum.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Class A vs B vs AB)

| Parameter | Class A | Class B (Push-Pull) | Class AB |
|-----------|---------|---------------------|----------|
| Conduction angle $\theta_c$ | 360° | 180° | 200° – 220° |
| Q-point location | Center of load line | At cutoff ($I_{CQ} = 0$) | Just above cutoff |
| Quiescent power | High (always-on) | Zero | Small |
| Max theoretical $\eta$ | 50 % (transformer) / 25 % (resistive) | **78.5 %** | ≈ 70 – 78 % |
| Crossover distortion | None | **Severe** (dead band) | Negligible (small bias) |
| Heat management | Easy (predictable) | Worst-case at $2V_{CC}/\pi$ | Same as B + thermal compensation |
| Complexity | Simple | Medium (matched devices) | Medium-High (bias network) |
| Typical use | Low-noise pre-amps | RF, motor drives | **Audio power amps (universal)** |

## 4.1 Complementary-Symmetry Class B / AB Push-Pull Schematic

The figure below is the canonical KTU-board exam circuit. The label $V_{BB}$ represents the small bias voltage source (realized in practice by a $V_{BE}$ multiplier) that lifts the bases above and below the emitter junction to eliminate crossover distortion in Class AB. For pure Class B, simply set $V_{BB} = 0$ and remove the bias network.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeVCC["VCC Positive Supply +V"]
    nodeVEE["VEE Negative Supply -V or GND"]
    nodeVin["Input Signal vin at Base Drive Node"]
    nodeQ1N["Q1 NPN Transistor Upper Half"]
    nodeQ2P["Q2 PNP Transistor Lower Half"]
    nodeVBias["VBB Bias Network Diode or VBE Multiplier"]
    nodeEJoin["Common Emitter Node Output"]
    nodeRL["Load Resistor RL"]
    nodeVout["Output Voltage vout across RL"]
    nodeGND["Ground Reference"]

    nodeVCC --- nodeQ1N
    nodeQ1N --- nodeEJoin
    nodeEJoin --- nodeQ2P
    nodeQ2P --- nodeVEE

    nodeVin --- nodeVBias
    nodeVBias --- nodeQ1N
    nodeVBias --- nodeQ2P

    nodeEJoin --- nodeRL
    nodeRL --- nodeGND
    nodeEJoin --- nodeVout
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Class AB Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepStart(["Input Signal vin t"]) --> stepA{"Sign of vin"}
    stepA -- "vin greater than 0.7 V" --> stepB["Q1 NPN turns ON"]
    stepA -- "vin less than minus 0.7 V" --> stepC["Q2 PNP turns ON"]
    stepA -- "Small range near zero" --> stepD["Both slightly ON from quiescent bias VBB"]
    stepB --> stepE["Current sourced from VCC into Load"]
    stepC --> stepF["Current sunk from Load into VEE"]
    stepD --> stepG["Small crossover current fills the dead zone"]
    stepE --> stepH["Output vout reconstructed at Load"]
    stepF --> stepH
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI(["Continuous Sinusoidal vout across RL"])
```

## 4.3 Transfer Characteristic — Showing Crossover Distortion

```mermaid
graph LR
    inAxis["vin axis horizontal input voltage"]
    outAxis["vout axis vertical output voltage"]
    p1["Region 1 Negative half PNP conducts slope +1"]
    p2["DEAD ZONE width 2VBEon both OFF vout equals 0"]
    p3["Region 2 Positive half NPN conducts slope +1"]
    inAxis --- p1
    inAxis --- p2
    inAxis --- p3
    p1 --- outAxis
    p2 --- outAxis
    p3 --- outAxis
```

> [!NOTE]
> **For Class AB**, the dead zone in the middle of the transfer curve is *filled in* because the small quiescent bias forces both transistors to start conducting slightly before the input crosses zero. The resulting curve is a straight line through the origin with no kink.

## 4.4 Bias Network — $V_{BE}$ Multiplier (Rubens' Circuit)

```mermaid
graph TD
    bNode1["Base of Q_bias from VCC via R3"]
    bQbias["Q_bias NPN transistor collector to VCC"]
    bR1["R1 from Base to Emitter of Q_bias"]
    bR2["R2 from Emitter of Q_bias to VEE side bus"]
    bVBBout["VBB appears between the two output base nodes"]
    bVEE["VEE negative rail or ground reference for the lower transistor base"]

    bNode1 --- bQbias
    bQbias --- bR1
    bR1 --- bR2
    bR2 --- bVBBout
    bVBBout --- bVEE
```

The regulated bias voltage produced is:

$$
V_{BB} = V_{BE}\!\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Tracking:** Mount $Q_{bias}$, R1, R2, and the output transistors on the **same heatsink**. As the heatsink temperature rises, $V_{BE}$ of $Q_{bias}$ falls, which reduces $V_{BB}$, which in turn reduces the quiescent current in the output pair — exactly the action needed to prevent thermal runaway.
