# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define crossover distortion in a Class B push-pull amplifier. How is it eliminated in a Class AB amplifier?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Crossover Distortion (CD):** In a Class B push-pull amplifier, neither transistor conducts when the input signal lies in the range $-V_{BE(\text{on})} \le v_{in} \le +V_{BE(\text{on})}$ (typically $\pm 0.6$ to $0.7\text{ V}$). During this "dead band" the output remains at 0 V even as the input changes, producing a kink in the transfer characteristic and a notch in the output waveform at every zero crossing.
- (1 mark) **Elimination in Class AB:** The bases of the two output transistors are pre-biased by a small voltage $V_{BB} \approx 2 V_{BE(\text{on})}$ (set by diodes or a $V_{BE}$ multiplier). This produces a small quiescent collector current $I_{CQ}$ in each device, ensuring both transistors begin conducting exactly at $v_{in} = 0$ and the dead band is filled in.
- (1 mark) The resulting transfer curve is linear through the origin, and the output is a clean sinusoid.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Why is the maximum theoretical efficiency of a Class B amplifier 78.5 % and not 100 %? What limits it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) The DC supply current in a Class B stage is **proportional to the signal amplitude** ($I_{CC} = V_m / \pi R_L$). Even at full output swing, the supply is *not* continuously delivering current — it delivers a half-wave-rectified sinusoid whose average is $V_m/\pi R_L$, not $V_m/R_L$.
- (1 mark) The output power scales as $V_m^{2}$, but the input DC power scales only as $V_m$. Their ratio $\eta = (\pi/4) (V_m/V_{CC})$ therefore saturates at $\pi/4$.
- (1 mark) **Limitations:** (i) Finite $V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ and on-resistance of the transistors prevent the full $V_{CC}$ from appearing across the load. (ii) At small signal levels, the supply current becomes a larger fraction of the signal current, and efficiency drops. (iii) Class B cannot reach 100 % because some DC power is *always* required to establish the bias point and replenish charge carriers; only switching amplifiers (Class D) approach 100 %.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a complementary-symmetry Class B push-pull power amplifier. Derive expressions for (i) conversion efficiency and (ii) maximum power dissipation in the output transistors.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A complementary-symmetry Class B amplifier operates from $\pm 20\text{ V}$ supplies and drives a $16\,\Omega$ load. Compute (i) the maximum AC output power, (ii) the maximum conversion efficiency, (iii) the maximum dissipation per transistor and the corresponding $V_m$, and (iv) the overall efficiency when $V_m = 12\text{ V}$.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

1. **Circuit Diagram (2 marks):** Draw the complementary-symmetry stage (NPN Q1, PNP Q2, joined emitters to $R_L$, bases driven by $v_{in}$, collectors to $\pm V_{CC}$). For Class B, $V_{BB} = 0$.

2. **Operation (1 mark):** Describe the half-cycle conduction: Q1 (NPN) conducts during $v_{in} > 0$; Q2 (PNP) conducts during $v_{in} < 0$; both OFF in the small dead zone $\rightarrow$ crossover distortion.

3. **Efficiency Derivation (2 marks):**

$$
P_{L(ac)} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{2 R_L}, \qquad I_{CC} = \frac{V_m}{\pi R_L}, \qquad P_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC} V_m}{\pi R_L}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{L(ac)}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{\pi}{4}\cdot\frac{V_m}{V_{CC}}
$$

At $V_m = V_{CC}$, $\eta_{\max} = \pi/4 = 78.5\%$.

4. **Max Dissipation (2 marks):**

$$
P_D = \frac{2 V_{CC} V_m}{\pi R_L} - \frac{V_m^{2}}{2 R_L}, \qquad \frac{dP_D}{dV_m} = 0 \Rightarrow V_m = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi}
$$

$$
P_{D(\max)} = \frac{2 V_{CC}^{2}}{\pi^{2} R_L} \quad \text{(both transistors)}
$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Given: $V_{CC} = 20\text{ V}$, $R_L = 16\,\Omega$.

(i) Maximum AC output power:

$$
P_{L(\max)} = \frac{V_{CC}^{2}}{2 R_L} = \frac{400}{32} = 12.5 \text{ W} \quad \text{(1.75 marks)}
$$

(ii) Maximum conversion efficiency:

$$
\eta_{\max} = 78.5\% \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

(iii) Maximum dissipation per transistor at $V_m = 2 V_{CC}/\pi = 40/\pi \approx 12.73\text{ V}$:

Total dissipation:

$$
P_{D(\max)} = \frac{2 \cdot (20)^{2}}{\pi^{2} \cdot 16} = \frac{800}{157.91} \approx 5.07 \text{ W}
$$

Per transistor:

$$
P_{D(\max,\text{each})} = \frac{5.07}{2} \approx 2.53 \text{ W} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

(iv) Efficiency at $V_m = 12\text{ V}$:

$$
P_{L(ac)} = \frac{(12)^{2}}{32} = 4.5 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{DC} = \frac{2 \cdot 20 \cdot 12}{\pi \cdot 16} = \frac{480}{50.27} \approx 9.55 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{4.5}{9.55} \approx 47.1\% \quad \text{(2.25 marks)}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with diagrams three different biasing arrangements used to eliminate crossover distortion in a push-pull amplifier. Discuss the role of thermal runaway and how it is prevented.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare Class A, Class B, and Class AB power amplifiers on the basis of (i) conduction angle, (ii) Q-point location, (iii) maximum efficiency, (iv) distortion, and (v) typical applications. State two advantages and one disadvantage of Class AB over Class B.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

1. **Why bias is needed (1 mark):** At $v_{in} = 0$ in pure Class B, both transistors are OFF; the input must exceed $\pm 0.7\text{ V}$ before either conducts → crossover distortion.

2. **Diode biasing (1.5 marks):** Two forward-biased diodes (or one diode and a resistor) between the bases of Q1 and Q2 set $V_{BB} \approx 2 V_{BE}$. When the diodes are mounted on the same heatsink as the output transistors, their forward drop tracks $V_{BE}$ of the outputs and provides automatic thermal compensation.

3. **$V_{BE}$ Multiplier (1.5 marks):** A single transistor $Q_{bias}$ with a voltage-divider $R_1$–$R_2$ in its emitter produces a tunable, temperature-stable offset:

$$
V_{BB} = V_{BE}\!\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)
$$

This is the most common professional bias method because the offset can be precisely adjusted.

4. **Resistive biasing (0.5 mark):** A simple resistor between bases gives a less stable $V_{BB}$; rarely used in production.

5. **Thermal Runaway (1.5 marks):** A rise in junction temperature reduces $V_{BE}$ (≈ $-2\text{ mV/°C}$), which increases $I_C$, which increases dissipation, which raises the temperature further — a positive-feedback loop. Mounting the bias network on the same heatsink makes the bias voltage decrease as temperature rises, automatically reducing the quiescent current and stabilizing the operating point.

6. **Summary (1 mark):** Mention at least one disadvantage of diode biasing (e.g., cannot fine-tune $V_{BB}$ since each diode step is ~ 0.7 V).

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

1. **Comparison Table (4 marks):** Use the Analytical Matrix from Section 3.4 (reproduced in brief):

| Parameter | Class A | Class B | Class AB |
|-----------|---------|---------|----------|
| Conduction angle | 360° | 180° | 200°–220° |
| Q-point | Centre of load line | At cutoff | Just above cutoff |
| Max efficiency | 25–50 % | 78.5 % | ~ 70–78 % |
| Distortion | None | Severe CD | Negligible |
| Applications | Pre-amps, Hi-Fi small-signal | RF, motor drives | Audio PA, op-amp outputs |

2. **Two advantages of Class AB over Class B (2 marks):**
   - **No crossover distortion** — smooth handover between the two transistors.
   - **Better linearity** — suitable for Hi-Fi audio.
   - (Bonus) Reduced odd-order harmonic content in the output.

3. **One disadvantage of Class AB (1 mark):**
   - Slightly lower efficiency (5–10 % less than Class B) due to the quiescent current overhead, and the requirement of a stable, temperature-compensated bias network adds complexity and cost.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 1. **Confusing total dissipation with per-transistor dissipation.** When asked for "power dissipated in each transistor," students often write the total $P_{D(\max)} = 2V_{CC}^{2}/(\pi^{2}R_L)$ without halving it. KTU key explicitly demands $P_{D(\text{each})} = V_{CC}^{2}/(\pi^{2}R_L)$. **Always halve it for "per transistor" wording.**
> 2. **Forgetting to state the value of $V_m$ at maximum dissipation.** Many students compute $P_{D(\max)}$ correctly but forget to mention that it occurs at $V_m = 2V_{CC}/\pi \approx 0.636\,V_{CC}$, not at full output. Examiners specifically look for this — losing 1 mark if omitted.
> 3. **Drawing the dead zone in the transfer curve without labelling its width.** The "kink" diagram must show $\pm V_{BE}$ on the input axis; a sketch without labels is treated as incomplete and loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing up the supplies in the $P_{DC}$ formula.** For split-supply $\pm V_{CC}$ the *total* DC power is $P_{DC} = 2V_{CC} V_m / (\pi R_L)$, **not** $V_{CC} V_m / (\pi R_L)$.
> 5. **Stating $\eta_{\max} = 78.5\,\%$ without justifying it.** Always show the substitution $V_m = V_{CC}$ in $\eta = (\pi/4)(V_m/V_{CC})$ to earn full marks.
> 6. **Ignoring thermal runaway.** In any Class AB biasing question, the examiner expects a comment on thermal compensation. Omitting it is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before Walking Into the Exam**

- ✅ **Class B Conduction Angle:** Exactly **180°** per device. Q-point on $I_C = 0$ axis.
- ✅ **Class AB Conduction Angle:** **200°–220°** per device. Small $I_{CQ}$ (few mA) to eliminate CD.
- ✅ **Three Topologies:** Transformer-coupled, Complementary-symmetry, Quasi-complementary.
- ✅ **Crossover Distortion Cause:** $V_{BE}$ threshold $\approx 0.7\text{ V}$ creates a dead band of width $\approx 2V_{BE}$.
- ✅ **Crossover Distortion Cure:** Diode biasing OR $V_{BE}$ multiplier with thermal tracking on the same heatsink.
- ✅ **Headline Efficiency:** $\eta_{\max} = \pi/4 = 78.5\,\%$ (Class B / AB at full output swing).
- ✅ **Efficiency Formula:** $\eta = (\pi/4)\cdot(V_m/V_{CC})$ — remember to substitute $V_m$ for partial-swing problems.
- ✅ **Max Output Power:** $P_{L(\max)} = V_{CC}^{2}/(2R_L)$ (Class B / AB at $V_m = V_{CC}$).
- ✅ **Max Transistor Dissipation:** $P_{D(\max)} = 2V_{CC}^{2}/(\pi^{2}R_L)$ **total** or $V_{CC}^{2}/(\pi^{2}R_L)$ **per transistor** at $V_m = 2V_{CC}/\pi$.
- ✅ **Max Dissipation Rule of Thumb:** $P_{D(\max)} \approx 0.4 \, P_{L(\max)}$ — useful for heatsink sizing.
- ✅ **DC Power:** $P_{DC} = 2 V_{CC} V_m / (\pi R_L)$ for split supply; $V_{CC} V_m / (\pi R_L)$ for single supply.
- ✅ **Thermal Runaway:** $V_{BE}$ falls with temperature → $I_C$ rises → more heat → destroy. Diode / $V_{BE}$ multiplier on the *same* heatsink breaks the loop.
- ✅ **$V_{BE}$ Multiplier Formula:** $V_{BB} = V_{BE}(1 + R_2/R_1)$ — adjustable, thermally stable.
- ✅ **Class AB vs Class B Trade-off:** ~ 5–10 % efficiency penalty for vastly reduced distortion.
- ✅ **Universal Application:** Class AB is the industry standard for audio power amplifiers and op-amp output stages.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Complementary-Symmetry Class B and Class AB Power Amplifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Complementary-Symmetry Class B and Class AB Power Amplifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Complementary-Symmetry Power Amplifier** is a two-transistor push-pull output stage that uses a pair of *complementary* BJTs (an NPN and a PNP) or complementary MOSFETs (NMOS and PMOS) connected in a totem-pole-like configuration. Each transistor conducts for **exactly one half (180°)** of the input signal cycle, making the configuration inherently a **Class B** amplifier when no DC bias is applied across the base-emitter junctions.

**Class B Operation (KTU Terminology):** A power amplifier biased such that the quiescent (DC) collector current $I_{CQ} \approx 0$. The conduction angle of each transistor is exactly **180°**. One transistor (NPN) amplifies the positive half-cycle, while the complementary transistor (PNP) amplifies the negative half-cycle.

**Class AB Operation (KTU Terminology):** A small forward bias ($V_{BE} \approx 0.5$ to $0.7$ V per transistor) is applied across the base-emitter junctions so that both transistors conduct for a small portion of the cycle *in addition* to their respective half-cycles. The conduction angle lies between **180° and 360°**, eliminating **crossover distortion** while retaining most of the high efficiency of Class B.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 4):** The course explicitly focuses on *Complementary-Symmetry* topology. This is a *direct-coupled* (transformerless) output stage. The student must understand the *push-pull action*, the origin of **crossover distortion**, and the biasing techniques (diode bias / $V_{BE}$ multiplier) that convert a Class B into a Class AB stage.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Swing-Pushers" Analogy:**
> Imagine a heavy pendulum (the speaker cone / load). A **Class A** stage is like a single exhausted person trying to push the swing in *both* directions with constant force — inefficient because they waste energy even when the swing is at rest.
>
> A **Class B complementary stage** is like having *two* people standing on opposite sides of the swing. **Person A (NPN)** pushes *only* when the swing is going right (positive half), and **Person B (PNP)** pushes *only* when the swing is going left (negative half). They each rest during the other's turn — very efficient! However, near the *center* (zero crossing), neither person is pushing because they wait until the swing moves a noticeable distance. This causes a tiny "dead zone" at the center, called **crossover distortion**.
>
> A **Class AB stage** is the same two people, but they have been given a *small starting push* so that both are *slightly* engaged even at the center. The dead zone disappears, the swing moves smoothly through zero, and yet the energy wasted at idle remains small. **This is precisely why Class AB is the industry standard for audio amplifiers.**

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Symbol | Typical Value / Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Conduction Angle | $\theta_c$ | 180° (Class B), 180°–360° (Class AB) |
| Quiescent Collector Current | $I_{CQ}$ | **0 mA** (Class B), small (Class AB) |
| Peak Output Voltage | $V_{m}$ | Limited by $V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}$ |
| Crossover Distortion | — | Caused by the $V_{BE}$ threshold $\approx 0.7$ V |
| Maximum Theoretical Efficiency | $\eta_{max}$ | **78.5% (Class B & AB)** |
| Power Supply Rails | $V_{CC}, -V_{EE}$ | Symmetrical dual supply typically used |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Circuit Topology — The Complementary Pair

The complementary-symmetry emitter follower uses:
- **Q₁ (NPN)** — handles the **positive half-cycle** (source current to the load)
- **Q₂ (PNP)** — handles the **negative half-cycle** (sink current from the load)
- **Load $R_L$** — connected from the joined emitters to ground

Both bases are tied together (driven by the same input $v_i$), and both emitters are tied together (forming the output node $v_o$). The transistors act as **emitter followers** with a voltage gain of approximately **unity** ($A_v \approx 1$).

### 2.2 Why Pure Class B Suffers from Crossover Distortion

In a pure Class B stage:
- For $|v_i| < V_{BE(on)} \approx 0.7$ V, **both** transistors are in cutoff.
- Output $v_o$ remains at **0 V** for an input window of approximately $\pm 0.7$ V.
- The output waveform therefore has a "notch" near the zero crossings.

This notch is called **crossover distortion**. Its frequency is twice the input signal frequency (because it occurs at *both* zero crossings per cycle), and it produces unwanted harmonics — unacceptable in hi-fi audio.

### 2.3 The Biasing Fix: From Class B to Class AB

To eliminate crossover distortion, we introduce a small forward bias $V_{BB}$ between the two bases such that:

$$V_{BB} = V_{BE1} + V_{BE2} = 2 V_{BE} \approx 1.3 \text{ to } 1.4 \text{ V}$$

This ensures both Q₁ and Q₂ are on the *verge of conduction* (Class AB) at $v_i = 0$. Common biasing networks:

**(a) Diode Bias:** Two forward-biased diodes in series between the bases. Simple, but the bias voltage tracks temperature only as well as the $V_{BE}$ of the output transistors (1:1 tracking).

**(b) $V_{BE}$ Multiplier (Preferred):** A transistor + resistor network that produces a stable, adjustable, well-temperature-tracked bias voltage. It also provides excellent $V_{BE}$ compensation (typically a 2:1 or 3:1 ratio to the output transistors' $V_{BE}$).

> [!NOTE]
> The $V_{BE}$ multiplier is the **industry-standard** biasing method. It also has the added benefit of *negative temperature coefficient* — as temperature rises, $V_{BE}$ drops, and the bias voltage drops, preventing thermal runaway. KTU examiners frequently ask students to derive the output voltage of a $V_{BE}$ multiplier.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions of the KTU ESE.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Output Voltage (sinusoidal) | $v_o(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ | $V_m \le V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}$ |
| 2 | Average Load Power | $P_L = \dfrac{V_m^2}{2 R_L}$ | Watts (W) |
| 3 | Maximum Load Power | $P_{L(max)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L}$ | When $V_m = V_{CC}$ |
| 4 | DC Supply Current (avg) | $I_{DC} = \dfrac{I_m}{\pi} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi R_L}$ | One supply at a time |
| 5 | Total DC Power from Both Supplies | $P_{DC} = 2 V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC} V_m}{\pi R_L}$ | Dual supply |
| 6 | Maximum DC Power | $P_{DC(max)} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi R_L}$ | At full output |
| 7 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_L}{P_{DC}} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot \dfrac{V_m}{V_{CC}}$ | Ratio, 0 to 1 |
| 8 | Maximum Efficiency | $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} = 78.54\%$ | At $V_m = V_{CC}$ |
| 9 | Power Dissipated by Transistors | $P_D = P_{DC} - P_L$ | Difference |
| 10 | Max Transistor Dissipation | $P_{D(max)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L} \approx \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{9.87 R_L}$ | At $V_m = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi}$ |
| 11 | $V_{BE}$ Multiplier Output | $V_{BB} = V_{BE} \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ | Adjustable |
| 12 | Conduction Angle Class B | $\theta_c = 180°$ | Each device |
| 13 | Conduction Angle Class AB | $180° < \theta_c < 360°$ | Slight overlap |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pipe-Symbol Note:** The notation used above uses `V_m` instead of `|V_m|` to avoid breaking the markdown table. The `V_m` symbol represents the **peak amplitude** of the output sinusoid (always positive by convention).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The complementary-symmetry Class AB stage is the **workhorse** of all audio power amplifier ICs — the LM386, TDA2030, LM3886, TPA3116 (Class D derivative), and the output stages of op-amps like the LM741. It is also used in:
- Motor driver H-bridges (complementary FET pairs)
- Line drivers in communication systems
- Headphone amplifiers in portable devices
- Power op-amps and audio amplifiers in KTU lab kits (e.g., the μA741 trainer)

The transition from Class A to Class B to Class AB represents a clear engineering trade-off: efficiency vs. linearity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Average Power Delivered to the Load ($P_L$)

**Given:** Output voltage $v_o(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ across a load $R_L$.

**Step 1 — Instantaneous power delivered to the load:**

$$p_L(t) = \frac{v_o^2(t)}{R_L} = \frac{V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t)}{R_L}$$

**Step 2 — Average over one full period using the identity $\sin^2(\theta) = \dfrac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:**

$$P_L = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T p_L(t) \, dt = \frac{V_m^2}{R_L} \cdot \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T \sin^2(\omega t) \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral (the cosine term averages to zero over a full period):**

$$P_L = \frac{V_m^2}{R_L} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L}$$

This is the **average AC power** delivered to the speaker or load. The factor of 2 in the denominator appears because the average of $\sin^2$ is $\frac{1}{2}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Total DC Power Drawn from Both Supplies ($P_{DC}$)

**Given:** Symmetrical dual supply $\pm V_{CC}$. Only one supply delivers current at a time (the transistor sourcing from $+V_{CC}$ delivers current during the positive half-cycle, and the transistor sinking to $-V_{EE}$ draws current during the negative half-cycle).

**Step 1 — Output current through the load:**

$$i_o(t) = \frac{v_o(t)}{R_L} = \frac{V_m \sin(\omega t)}{R_L}$$

**Step 2 — Current from the positive supply $+V_{CC}$:**

$$i_{CC}(t) = i_o(t) \quad \text{when} \quad v_o > 0 \quad \text{i.e., for the half-cycle}$$

**Step 3 — Average current drawn from one supply (taking the average of $\vert \sin(\omega t) \vert$ over a full period):**

$$I_{DC} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi} \frac{V_m \sin\theta}{R_L} \, d\theta \text{ over a half-cycle}$$

$$I_{DC} = \frac{V_m}{\pi R_L}$$

**Step 4 — Power from one supply:**

$$P_{+V_{CC}} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC} = \frac{V_{CC} V_m}{\pi R_L}$$

**Step 5 — By symmetry, the negative supply delivers the same average power:**

$$P_{-V_{EE}} = \frac{V_{CC} V_m}{\pi R_L}$$

**Step 6 — Total DC power from both supplies:**

$$P_{DC} = P_{+V_{CC}} + P_{-V_{EE}} = \frac{2 V_{CC} V_m}{\pi R_L}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Conversion Efficiency ($\eta$)

**Step 1 — Definition of efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{\text{AC Power Delivered to Load}}{\text{DC Power Drawn from Supplies}} = \frac{P_L}{P_{DC}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the derived expressions:**

$$\eta = \frac{\dfrac{V_m^2}{2 R_L}}{\dfrac{2 V_{CC} V_m}{\pi R_L}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify (the $R_L$ cancels, one factor of $V_m$ cancels):**

$$\eta = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L} \cdot \frac{\pi R_L}{2 V_{CC} V_m} = \frac{\pi V_m}{4 V_{CC}}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum occurs when $V_m = V_{CC}$ (ideal limit, ignoring $V_{CE(sat)}$):**

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi}{4} = 0.7854 = 78.54\%$$

This is the **famous Class B/AB efficiency limit**, and KTU examiners love to test it.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Maximum Transistor Power Dissipation ($P_{D(max)}$)

This is a *classic KTU 14-mark derivation*. The power dissipated by the two transistors equals the difference between DC input power and AC output power:

$$P_D = P_{DC} - P_L = \frac{2 V_{CC} V_m}{\pi R_L} - \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L}$$

**Step 1 — Find the value of $V_m$ that maximizes $P_D$ by differentiation:**

$$\frac{d P_D}{d V_m} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} - \frac{2 V_m}{2 R_L} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} - \frac{V_m}{R_L} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $V_m$:**

$$\frac{V_m}{R_L} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} \implies V_m = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $V_m = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi}$ back into the $P_D$ expression:**

$$P_{D(max)} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} \cdot \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi} - \frac{1}{2 R_L} \left( \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi} \right)^2$$

**Step 4 — Expand each term:**

$$P_{D(max)} = \frac{4 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L} - \frac{4 V_{CC}^2}{2 \pi^2 R_L} = \frac{4 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L} - \frac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L}$$

**Step 5 — Final result:**

$$P_{D(max)} = \frac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L} \approx \frac{V_{CC}^2}{4.93 R_L}$$

**Important engineering observation:** The maximum transistor dissipation does **not** occur at maximum output. It occurs at $V_m = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi} \approx 0.636 V_{CC}$ — which is *less* than full output. The KTU examiner often tests whether the student understands this non-intuitive fact.

---

### 3.5 Comparison: Class A vs Class B vs Class AB

| Parameter | Class A | Class B (Comp. Sym.) | Class AB |
|---|---|---|---|
| Conduction Angle | 360° | 180° | 180°–360° |
| Quiescent Current | $I_{CQ} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_L}$ | $\approx 0$ | Small (few mA) |
| Max Efficiency | **25%** | **78.5%** | **~70–78%** |
| Crossover Distortion | None | **Severe** | Eliminated |
| Transformer Required | Yes (typical) | No (transformerless) | No (transformerless) |
| Heat Sink Required | Large | Small | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complementary-Symmetry Output Stage

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef inputStyle fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef npnStyle fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef pnpStyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef loadStyle fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000
    classDef suppStyle fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000

    Vi["Input Signal v_i"]:::inputStyle
    Bias["Bias Network V_BB approx 1.3 V"]:::inputStyle

    Q1N["Q1 NPN Transistor\nUpper Half\nSource Current to Load"]:::npnStyle
    Q2P["Q2 PNP Transistor\nLower Half\nSink Current from Load"]:::pnpStyle

    Vp["+V_CC Supply Rail"]:::suppStyle
    Vn["-V_EE Supply Rail"]:::suppStyle

    Vo["Output Node v_o\nEmitter Follower"]:::loadStyle
    RL["Load R_L\nSpeaker or Motor"]:::loadStyle
    GND["Ground Reference"]:::suppStyle

    Vi --> Q1N
    Bias --> Q1N
    Bias --> Q2P
    Vi --> Q2P

    Vp --> Q1N
    Q1N --> Vo
    Q2P --> Vo
    Q2P --> Vn

    Vo --> RL
    RL --> GND
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Operating Cycle of a Class B Stage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef posStyle fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef negStyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef zeroStyle fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000
    classDef outStyle fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000

    A["Input Cycle Starts\nv_i approx 0 V"]:::zeroStyle
    B["Positive Half Cycle\n0 to 180 degrees\nv_i greater than 0.7 V"]:::posStyle
    C["Q1 NPN Conducts\nQ2 PNP Cut Off\nCurrent flows from +V_CC to Load"]:::posStyle
    D["Negative Half Cycle\n180 to 360 degrees\nv_i less than minus 0.7 V"]:::negStyle
    E["Q2 PNP Conducts\nQ1 NPN Cut Off\nCurrent flows from Load to -V_EE"]:::negStyle
    F["Zero Crossing Zone\nv_i between plus 0.7 V and minus 0.7 V"]:::zeroStyle
    G["Class B: Both Cut Off\nOutput is 0 V\nCrossover Distortion Appears"]:::zeroStyle
    H["Class AB: Both Slightly On\nSmooth Transition\nNo Crossover Distortion"]:::outStyle

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    F --> H
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — $V_{BE}$ Multiplier Bias Network

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef biasStyle fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef resStyle fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000
    classDef trStyle fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef outStyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000

    Vcc1["+V_CC"]:::biasStyle
    Vcc2["-V_EE"]:::biasStyle

    R1["Resistor R1\nTop Resistor"]:::resStyle
    R2["Resistor R2\nBottom Resistor"]:::resStyle
    Q3["Bias Transistor Q_B\nNPN Small Signal"]:::trStyle

    BaseA["Node A\nConnected to Q1 Base"]:::outStyle
    BaseB["Node B\nConnected to Q2 Base"]:::outStyle

    Vcc1 --> R1
    R1 --> BaseA
    BaseA --> Q3
    Q3 --> Vcc2
    BaseA --> R2
    R2 --> BaseB

    Vbb["V_BB = V_BE times 1 plus R1 over R2\nTypical 1.2 V to 1.5 V"]:::biasStyle
    Vbb -.-> BaseA
    Vbb -.-> BaseB
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Sequence — Comparison of Three Classes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef aStyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef bStyle fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef abStyle fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000

    A["Class A Amplifier\nConduction 360 degrees\nQ point at center of load line\nEfficiency max 25 percent\nNo crossover distortion"]:::aStyle
    B["Class B Amplifier\nConduction 180 degrees\nQ point at cutoff\nEfficiency max 78.5 percent\nHas crossover distortion"]:::bStyle
    AB["Class AB Amplifier\nConduction 180 to 360 degrees\nQ point slightly above cutoff\nEfficiency 50 to 78 percent\nMinimal crossover distortion"]:::abStyle

    A --> AB
    B --> AB
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Question 1: Define Crossover Distortion** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Crossover distortion** is the unwanted discontinuity that appears in the output waveform of a Class B (or improperly biased) push-pull amplifier near the zero-crossing points of the input signal.

**Cause:** It arises because the base-emitter junction of each transistor requires approximately **0.6 to 0.7 V** of forward bias before it begins to conduct. Therefore, when the input signal lies in the range $-0.7\,\text{V} \le v_i \le +0.7\,\text{V}$, both transistors are in cutoff, and the output remains stuck at **0 V** instead of following the input linearly.

**Effect:** The output waveform has a "notch" at every zero crossing, producing **even-order harmonics** (especially second harmonic) of the input signal — highly undesirable in audio applications.

**Remedy:** Apply a small forward bias (Class AB operation) using a diode-bias network or a **$V_{BE}$ multiplier** between the bases of the two transistors so that both are on the *verge of conduction* at $v_i = 0$.

---

#### **Question 2: What is the Maximum Theoretical Efficiency of a Class B Complementary-Symmetry Amplifier?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The maximum theoretical conversion efficiency of an ideal Class B complementary-symmetry power amplifier is:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi}{4} = 0.7854 = \mathbf{78.54\%}$$

**Conditions for achieving this:**
- Output voltage swing reaches the full supply rail, i.e., $V_m = V_{CC}$ (ignoring $V_{CE(sat)}$).
- Both transistors are perfectly matched.
- No losses in the biasing network or driver stage.

**Comparison:** This is more than **three times** the maximum efficiency of a Class A stage (25%), which is why Class B/AB is preferred for power applications.

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The student must answer EITHER the full Question A OR the full Question B (internal choice is at the *question* level, not sub-part level).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **complementary-symmetry Class B push-pull power amplifier**. Derive the expression for its **conversion efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A complementary-symmetry Class B amplifier operates from $\pm 15$ V supplies and drives a load $R_L = 8\,\Omega$. Calculate: **(7 marks)**
- (i) Maximum AC power delivered to the load.
- (ii) DC power drawn from the supplies at maximum output.
- (iii) Maximum conversion efficiency.
- (iv) Power dissipated by the two output transistors at maximum output.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Circuit Operation & Efficiency Derivation:**

The complementary-symmetry Class B amplifier uses an **NPN transistor Q₁** and a **PNP transistor Q₂** connected in an emitter-follower configuration. The collectors are tied to $+V_{CC}$ and $-V_{EE}$ respectively, and the emitters are joined to drive the load $R_L$.

During the **positive half-cycle** of the input $v_i$, Q₁ (NPN) is forward-biased and conducts, while Q₂ (PNP) is reverse-biased and is in cutoff. Current flows from $+V_{CC}$ through Q₁ into $R_L$, producing a positive output voltage.

During the **negative half-cycle**, Q₂ (PNP) conducts and Q₁ is in cutoff. Current is pulled from $R_L$ into Q₂ and into the $-V_{EE}$ rail, producing a negative output voltage.

Each transistor conducts for exactly **180°** of the cycle. There is a small "dead zone" of approximately $\pm 0.7$ V near the zero crossings where neither transistor conducts — this is **crossover distortion**.

**Efficiency Derivation:**

Let the output be $v_o(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. Then:

- **AC power delivered to load:**

$$P_L = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L}$$

- **DC current drawn from each supply (average):**

$$I_{DC} = \frac{V_m}{\pi R_L}$$

- **Total DC power from both supplies:**

$$P_{DC} = 2 V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC} V_m}{\pi R_L}$$

- **Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_L}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_m^2 / (2 R_L)}{2 V_{CC} V_m / (\pi R_L)} = \frac{\pi V_m}{4 V_{CC}}$$

- **Maximum efficiency** (when $V_m \to V_{CC}$):

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi}{4} = 78.54\%$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculations:**

**Given:** $V_{CC} = 15$ V, $R_L = 8\,\Omega$, $V_m = V_{CC} = 15$ V (ideal).

**(i) Maximum AC power delivered to load:**

$$P_{L(max)} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L} = \frac{(15)^2}{2 \times 8} = \frac{225}{16} = 14.06 \text{ W}$$

**(ii) DC power drawn from supplies at maximum output:**

$$P_{DC(max)} = \frac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi R_L} = \frac{2 \times (15)^2}{\pi \times 8} = \frac{450}{25.133} = 17.90 \text{ W}$$

**(iii) Maximum efficiency:**

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{P_{L(max)}}{P_{DC(max)}} = \frac{14.06}{17.90} = 0.7854 = 78.54\%$$

(Or directly: $\pi / 4 = 78.54\%$.)

**(iv) Power dissipated by the two transistors at maximum output:**

$$P_D = P_{DC(max)} - P_{L(max)} = 17.90 - 14.06 = 3.84 \text{ W}$$

(Equivalently: $P_D = P_{L(max)} \left( \dfrac{4}{\pi} - 1 \right) = 14.06 \times 0.2732 = 3.84$ W. The factor $\dfrac{4}{\pi} - 1$ appears often in KTU papers — memorize it!)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **$V_{BE}$ multiplier biasing** circuit used in Class AB power amplifiers. Derive the expression for the bias voltage produced. Why is this network preferred over simple diode biasing? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Class AB complementary-symmetry amplifier uses a $V_{BE}$ multiplier with $R_1 = 1.2$ k$\Omega$ and $R_2 = 2.7$ k$\Omega$. The output transistor pair has $V_{BE} = 0.65$ V at the operating temperature. Calculate: **(7 marks)**
- (i) The bias voltage $V_{BB}$ produced by the network.
- (ii) The quiescent (no-signal) current through the output transistors if the emitter resistors are $R_E = 0.5\,\Omega$ each.
- (iii) Comment on whether the transistors are operating in Class AB or have slipped into Class B.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — $V_{BE}$ Multiplier Analysis:**

The $V_{BE}$ multiplier (also called the "rubber diode") is a transistor-based network that produces a stable, well-defined bias voltage $V_{BB}$ between the bases of the complementary output pair. It consists of a small-signal NPN transistor Q₃ with two resistors $R_1$ (from collector to base) and $R_2$ (from base to emitter).

**Derivation of $V_{BB}$:**

Let $I_C$ be the collector current of Q₃. Assuming $I_C \approx I_E$ (neglecting base current), the current through $R_1$ is essentially the same as the current through $R_2$ (since the base draws negligible current).

The voltage across $R_2$ is equal to $V_{BE}$ of Q₃ (because Q₃'s base-emitter junction is in parallel with $R_2$):

$$V_{R_2} = V_{BE}$$

The total voltage $V_{BB}$ across the series combination of $R_1$ and $R_2$ is split in proportion to the resistances:

$$V_{BB} = V_{R_1} + V_{R_2} = I \cdot R_1 + V_{BE}$$

Since $I = V_{BE} / R_2$ (from $R_2$'s branch):

$$V_{BB} = \frac{V_{BE}}{R_2} \cdot R_1 + V_{BE} = V_{BE} \left( \frac{R_1}{R_2} + 1 \right)$$

$$\boxed{V_{BB} = V_{BE} \left( 1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2} \right)}$$

**Why preferred over diode bias:**

1. **Adjustability** — By varying $R_1 / R_2$, the bias can be precisely set, not just locked to $2 V_{BE}$.
2. **Temperature tracking** — The $V_{BE}$ of Q₃ can be selected to be a fraction of the output transistors' $V_{BE}$ (by using area-ratioed transistors), giving excellent thermal compensation and preventing thermal runaway.
3. **Stable bias** — The current through the divider is stabilized by the transistor's negative feedback action.
4. **Lower impedance** — Lower output impedance than a simple diode pair.

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculations:**

**Given:** $R_1 = 1.2$ k$\Omega$, $R_2 = 2.7$ k$\Omega$, $V_{BE} = 0.65$ V, $R_E = 0.5\,\Omega$.

**(i) Bias voltage $V_{BB}$:**

$$V_{BB} = V_{BE} \left( 1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2} \right) = 0.65 \times \left( 1 + \frac{1.2}{2.7} \right)$$

$$V_{BB} = 0.65 \times (1 + 0.4444) = 0.65 \times 1.4444 = 0.9389 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Quiescent current through output transistors:**

The DC voltage across each emitter resistor is half the bias voltage (since the $V_{BE}$ drops of the two output transistors absorb the other half — actually, the bias voltage $V_{BB}$ must equal $V_{BE,Q1} + V_{BE,Q2} + 2 I_Q R_E$):

$$V_{BB} = 2 V_{BE} + 2 I_Q R_E$$

$$0.9389 = 2(0.65) + 2 I_Q (0.5)$$

$$0.9389 = 1.30 + I_Q$$

$$I_Q = 0.9389 - 1.30 = -0.361 \text{ A}$$

A *negative* value indicates that $V_{BB}$ is **insufficient** to forward-bias both transistors — the stage has effectively slipped back into **Class B** (or worse, the output is dead). The student would *lose* the bias margin. To fix this, increase $R_1$ or decrease $R_2$ so that $V_{BB} > 1.30$ V (i.e., greater than $2 V_{BE}$).

**(iii) Comment on operating class:**

Since $V_{BB} \approx 0.94$ V $< 2 V_{BE} = 1.30$ V, the network is **under-biasing** the output stage. The transistors will not have a meaningful quiescent current. The amplifier is operating as a **Class B** stage (or a poorly biased Class AB) and will exhibit **crossover distortion**. The proper design target is $V_{BB}$ slightly greater than $2 V_{BE}$ (e.g., 1.4 V) to ensure a small standing current of 5–10 mA.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in $P_{DC}$: Students frequently write $P_{DC} = V_{CC} V_m / (\pi R_L)$ instead of the *correct* $2 V_{CC} V_m / (\pi R_L)$ — losing 2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Mixing up the conduction angles:** A Class B stage has $\theta_c = 180°$ per device, NOT 360°. Confusing this with Class A is a frequent error.
> 3. **Maximum power dissipation point:** Students incorrectly assume the transistors dissipate the most power at *maximum* output. In fact, maximum dissipation occurs at $V_m = 2 V_{CC}/\pi \approx 0.636 V_{CC}$ — a *non-intuitive* result worth 7 marks in a derivation.
> 4. **Forgetting the dual supply:** $P_{DC}$ in a complementary stage must account for *both* the $+V_{CC}$ and $-V_{EE}$ supplies. Drawing power from only one supply is wrong.
> 5. **$V_{BE}$ multiplier formula sign:** Writing $V_{BB} = V_{BE} (1 - R_1/R_2)$ instead of $+$ — a classic sign error.
> 6. **Crossover distortion cause:** Students often say it is due to "mismatch" between transistors. The *correct* reason is the non-zero $V_{BE}$ threshold of the base-emitter junction. Examiners deduct 1 mark for imprecise wording.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Class B Conduction Angle:** Each device conducts for exactly **180°** of the input cycle; quiescent current is **zero**.
- **Class AB Conduction Angle:** Each device conducts for **>180°** (typically 200°–210°); a small quiescent current flows.
- **Complementary Pair:** One **NPN** + one **PNP** in emitter-follower configuration; bases tied, emitters tied.
- **Crossover Distortion:** Caused by the **0.7 V $V_{BE}$** threshold. Fix: **bias the bases** (Class AB) using diodes or a **$V_{BE}$ multiplier**.
- **Maximum Efficiency:** **$\eta_{max} = \pi / 4 = 78.54\%$** (Class B and Class AB).
- **Maximum AC Power:** $P_{L(max)} = V_{CC}^2 / (2 R_L)$ (at full output swing).
- **Maximum DC Power:** $P_{DC(max)} = 2 V_{CC}^2 / (\pi R_L)$ — note the factor of **2** for dual supply.
- **Power Dissipated:** $P_D = P_{DC} - P_L$. The **maximum** $P_D$ occurs at $V_m = 2 V_{CC}/\pi$, NOT at maximum output.
- **Max Transistor Dissipation:** $P_{D(max)} = 2 V_{CC}^2 / (\pi^2 R_L) \approx V_{CC}^2 / (4.93 R_L)$.
- **$V_{BE}$ Multiplier Output:** $V_{BB} = V_{BE}(1 + R_1/R_2)$ — *preferred* over diode bias for adjustability and thermal tracking.
- **Heatsink Rule of Thumb:** The output transistors must be rated for at least $P_{D(max)} = V_{CC}^2 / (4.93 R_L)$ continuous dissipation; otherwise, thermal failure occurs.
- **Class A vs B vs AB:** A → $\eta_{max}$ 25%, no distortion. B → 78.5%, severe distortion. **AB → ~70–78%, distortion eliminated** (industry standard).
- **Transformerless advantage:** The complementary-symmetry topology is *direct-coupled* — no bulky, expensive, frequency-limiting output transformer is needed. This is why it dominates modern IC amplifier design.
- **Area-ratioed $V_{BE}$ multiplier:** Using a Q₃ with emitter area $= N \times$ Q₁'s area makes $V_{BE,Q3} = V_{BE,Q1} / N$, providing *better* thermal tracking — a frequent 7-mark derivation in KTU papers.
- **Even-order harmonics:** Crossover distortion is rich in second-harmonic content. In audio, this is perceived as a "buzzy" or "rough" tone.
- **Cross-over region width:** Approximately $\pm 0.7$ V in pure Class B; in Class AB it is essentially zero.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Class C and D Power Amplifier - Efficiency and Distortion (No Analysis Required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Class C and D Power Amplifier - Efficiency and Distortion (No Analysis Required)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Class C Power Amplifier — Formal Definition

A **Class C Power Amplifier** is a biased amplifier configuration in which the transistor's quiescent (Q) point is positioned **below the cutoff point** on the DC load line. As a result, the active device conducts for **less than half (180°) of the input cycle**. The conduction angle is typically in the range of **80° to 120°**, and a resonant (tank) LC circuit is mandatory at the output to reconstruct a complete sinusoidal waveform from the narrow current pulses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Terminology:**
> - **Conduction Angle (2θ_c):** The angular duration (in degrees/radians) for which the transistor conducts during one full cycle of the input signal.
> - **Operating Point:** Q-point lies **below the cutoff line (I_C = 0 axis)**, meaning V_BE(off) is greater than the quiescent base bias voltage.
> - **Class Boundary:** 2θ_c < 180° (i.e., θ_c < 90°)

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Water Gun" Principle

Imagine a garden hose with a manual trigger valve. The valve is normally **closed (OFF)**. Only when the user **squeezes the trigger hard enough** (signal exceeds cutoff bias) does a powerful jet of water shoot out for a very brief moment. After the burst, the valve snaps shut again.

The **Class C amplifier behaves identically**: the transistor is essentially OFF, and only delivers a sharp, high-amplitude **current pulse** when the input signal swings positively past a defined threshold. Because the device is OFF for most of the cycle, very little DC power is wasted — making it **the most efficient linear amplifier class** in continuous operation.

> [!NOTE]
> The output is **NOT** a replica of the input. A **parallel resonant tank circuit (LC)** at the load "rings" during the off-period, reconstructing a clean sine wave from the periodic pulses. This is why Class C is **only used in narrow-band, fixed-frequency RF applications** (e.g., RF transmitters, IF stages, oscillators).

---

## 1.2 Class D Power Amplifier — Formal Definition

A **Class D Power Amplifier** is a switching-mode amplifier in which the active device (BJT/MOSFET) is operated as a **binary switch** — either in **deep saturation (ON)** or **deep cutoff (OFF)**. It is never in the linear (active) region. Two complementary switches in a **push-pull arrangement** are driven by a **Pulse Width Modulated (PWM)** or square-wave signal, and a **low-pass LC filter** at the output reconstructs the original analog waveform.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Terminology:**
> - **Switching Operation:** Transistor is either fully ON (V_CE ≈ V_CE(sat) ≈ 0) or fully OFF (I_C ≈ 0).
> - **Ideal Power Dissipation:** P_diss = V_CE × I_C ≈ 0 in BOTH states (since one of the two is always zero).
> - **Theoretical Maximum Efficiency:** **η = 100%**
> - **Practical Efficiency:** 90% – 95% (limited by switching losses, R_DS(on), dead-time, etc.)

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Light Switch" Principle

A standard household light switch does not "dim" the bulb — it is either **completely ON (full brightness)** or **completely OFF (no light, no power consumed)**. The brightness of the room is not changed by varying the switch position; instead, the switch is **toggled very rapidly** so that the average light energy delivered to the bulb represents the desired dimming level.

A **Class D amplifier functions identically**. The audio signal is first converted into a **PWM waveform** (variable duty-cycle square wave at a high switching frequency, typically 250 kHz – 1 MHz). The transistors switch this high-frequency square wave into the load, and the **LC low-pass filter** averages it back into the original audio waveform. Because transistors never operate in their linear "lossy" region, almost all DC power is delivered to the speaker.

> [!NOTE]
> Class D is the **dominant amplifier topology** in modern portable audio (smartphones, laptops, Bluetooth speakers, subwoofers, car audio) precisely because of its near-100% efficiency, leading to longer battery life, smaller heat sinks, and lighter designs.

---

## 1.3 The Big Picture — Where do Class C and D Fit?

| Class | Conduction Angle | Q-Point Position | Theoretical Max Efficiency | Typical Application |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **A** | 360° | Center of load line | 50% (with coupled) / 25% (series) | Hi-Fi audio pre-amps |
| **B** | 180° | Exactly on cutoff axis | **78.5%** (π/4) | Audio push-pull |
| **AB** | 180° – 200° | Slightly above cutoff | 50% – 78.5% | General audio |
| **C** | < 180° (80°–120°) | **Below cutoff** | **≈ 100% (theoretical)** | RF transmitters, IF stages, oscillators |
| **D** | Switching (0°/360° equivalent) | Switch-mode | **100% (theoretical)** | Modern switching audio, SMPS |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Class C — Deep Operational Analysis

### 2.1.1 Biasing and Q-Point Placement

In Class C, the base-emitter junction is **reverse-biased** under quiescent (no-signal) conditions. Mathematically:

$$V_{BEQ} < V_{BE(\text{cut-in})} \approx 0.7\,\text{V} \quad \text{(for silicon BJT)}$$

Because of this, the collector current I_CQ is **zero** until the input signal's positive half-cycle exceeds the combined reverse-bias plus the cut-in voltage. Only a sharp tip of the input sinusoid triggers conduction.

### 2.1.2 Current Waveform and Conduction Geometry

Assume the input drive is large enough that the collector current pulse approximates a **truncated sinusoid** with peak value **I_p** and half-conduction angle **θ_c**. The collector current waveform is:

$$
i_C(t) = 
\begin{cases}
I_p \cdot \sin(\omega t) - I_p \cdot \cos(\theta_c) & \text{for } -\theta_c \leq \omega t \leq \theta_c \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

This is a **cosine-shifted sinusoid** that is zero at the boundaries ±θ_c.

### 2.1.3 Fourier Decomposition of the Current Pulse

The Fourier series of a Class C current pulse yields two key components:

**DC Component (drawn from V_CC supply):**

$$
I_{DC} = \frac{I_p}{\pi}\left[\sin(\theta_c) - \theta_c \cos(\theta_c)\right]
$$

**Fundamental (1st Harmonic) Component — the useful output current:**

$$
I_1 = \frac{I_p}{\pi}\left[\theta_c - \sin(\theta_c)\cos(\theta_c)\right]
$$

### 2.1.4 Output Power and DC Power

Assuming the tank circuit is **resonant at the fundamental** and presents a purely resistive load R_L at the operating frequency:

**AC Output Power (delivered to load):**

$$
P_{out} = \frac{1}{2}\,I_1^2 \cdot R_L
$$

**DC Input Power (drawn from V_CC supply):**

$$
P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC}
$$

### 2.1.5 Efficiency Equation — The Heart of Class C

The collector (conversion) efficiency is:

$$
\eta_C = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{\frac{1}{2} I_1^2 R_L}{V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC}}
$$

When V_CC = I_p · R_L / 2 (the condition for maximum undistorted swing with the tank), this simplifies to the **canonical Class C efficiency formula**:

$$
\boxed{\;\eta_C = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\theta_c - \sin(\theta_c)\cos(\theta_c)}{\sin(\theta_c) - \theta_c \cos(\theta_c)}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Limiting Behavior:**
> - As **θ_c → 90°** (Class B limit), η_C → π/4 = **78.5%** ✓
> - As **θ_c → 0°** (extreme Class C), η_C → **100%** (theoretical upper limit)
> - As **θ_c increases** beyond 90°, the formula is no longer valid (this is the Class A/B regime)

### 2.1.6 Distortion in Class C

Class C **cannot** be used for wideband audio because:

1. **Pulse-Density Distortion:** Output is a series of narrow pulses, not a continuous sinusoid. The LC tank only "fills in" the gaps at the **resonant frequency** — at any other frequency, the output is severely distorted.
2. **Harmonic Content:** The current pulse contains strong 2nd, 3rd, 4th ... harmonics. The tank suppresses them only at a **single tuned frequency**.
3. **Cross-Over Distortion:** Not present (since the device is OFF, not switching between two devices).

---

## 2.2 Class D — Deep Operational Analysis

### 2.2.1 Switching Topology

A typical Class D amplifier uses **two MOSFETs** in a half-bridge (or full-bridge) push-pull configuration. The PWM input drives:

- **M1 (high-side)** ON when PWM = HIGH
- **M2 (low-side)** ON when PWM = LOW

The output node toggles between **+V_DD** and **GND**, creating a high-frequency square wave.

### 2.2.2 Power Dissipation in the Ideal Switch

For an ideal switch, the instantaneous power dissipation is:

$$
P_{diss}(t) = v_{DS}(t) \cdot i_D(t)
$$

| State | v_DS | i_D | P_diss |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **ON** (saturation) | 0 V | I_load | **0 W** |
| **OFF** (cutoff) | V_DD | 0 A | **0 W** |
| **Switching transition** | V_DD/2 | I_load/2 | Brief loss |

Since the switch is never in the **linear region** (where both V and I are simultaneously large), the average power dissipated in the transistor is theoretically **zero**, giving **100% efficiency**.

### 2.2.3 PWM-Based Audio Reconstruction

The input audio signal v_in(t) is compared with a high-frequency triangular carrier v_tri(t) to generate PWM:

$$
\text{PWM}(t) = \begin{cases} \text{HIGH} & \text{if } v_{in}(t) > v_{tri}(t) \\ \text{LOW} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

The **duty cycle D(t)** is proportional to v_in(t). After the LC low-pass filter, the average voltage recovered at the load is:

$$
v_{out}(t) = D(t) \cdot V_{DD} = \frac{v_{in}(t)}{V_{tri,\text{peak}}} \cdot V_{DD}
$$

This is a **scaled, linear replica** of the input audio.

### 2.2.4 Output Power and DC Power

For a sinusoidal output of amplitude V_o at the load R_L:

**AC Output Power:**

$$
P_{out} = \frac{V_o^2}{2 R_L}
$$

**DC Input Power (from V_DD supply):**

$$
P_{DC} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{DC}
$$

For a complementary square wave switching between ±V_DD, the DC current drawn from each supply is the average of the current pulses. For a sinusoidal output, the **average current** drawn is:

$$
I_{DC} = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{V_o}{R_L} = \frac{2 V_o}{\pi R_L}
$$

Therefore:

$$
P_{DC} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{2 V_o}{\pi R_L}
$$

### 2.2.5 Efficiency of Class D

$$
\boxed{\;\eta_D = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{\frac{V_o^2}{2 R_L}}{V_{DD} \cdot \frac{2 V_o}{\pi R_L}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{V_o}{V_{DD}}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** Unlike Class A/B, the Class D efficiency **increases linearly with output amplitude** V_o:
> - At maximum output (V_o = V_DD): η_D = π/4 = **78.5%** (same as Class B, BUT only theoretically for the *signal-dependent* part)
> - **However**, with PWM and proper filtering, the **average efficiency** over a typical audio signal (which spends most time at low levels) is **90–95%** — vastly superior to Class A/B.

### 2.2.6 Distortion in Class D

Sources of non-linearity:
1. **PWM non-linearity:** Modulation index M = V_in/V_tri must be < 1 to avoid clipping.
2. **Switch dead-time:** Brief interval where both MOSFETs are OFF to prevent shoot-through. This introduces **notches** and **THD (typically 0.1% – 1%)**.
3. **LC filter non-linearity:** Component tolerances, saturation of inductors.
4. **Power supply ripple:** Couples into output.

Modern Class D amplifiers (with feedback) achieve **THD < 0.01%** — rivaling the best linear amplifiers.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Class C Application | Class D Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **RF Power Amplifiers** in AM/F transmitters | **Smartphone speakers** and audio amplifiers |
| **IF Amplifiers** in superheterodyne receivers | **Laptop / tablet** audio subsystems |
| **Oscillator stages** (Colpitts, Clapp) | **Subwoofers and PA systems** |
| **Plasma generation** drivers | **Electric vehicle audio** |
| **Induction heating** circuits | **Hearing aids** (micro-power Class D) |
| **CW radar** transmitters | **Class-D audio in home theaters / soundbars** |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **all critical formulas** expected in the KTU 2024 ESE for this topic. Memorize the boxed equations and the limiting cases.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Class C: DC component of i_C** | $I_{DC} = \dfrac{I_p}{\pi}\left[\sin\theta_c - \theta_c \cos\theta_c\right]$ | Amperes |
| 2 | **Class C: Fundamental component of i_C** | $I_1 = \dfrac{I_p}{\pi}\left[\theta_c - \sin\theta_c \cos\theta_c\right]$ | Amperes |
| 3 | **Class C: Output Power** | $P_{out} = \dfrac{1}{2} I_1^2 R_L$ | Watts |
| 4 | **Class C: DC Input Power** | $P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC}$ | Watts |
| 5 | **Class C: Efficiency (canonical)** | $\eta_C = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\theta_c - \sin\theta_c \cos\theta_c}{\sin\theta_c - \theta_c \cos\theta_c}$ | Dimensionless (×100 for %) |
| 6 | **Class C: Limiting case θ_c → 90°** | $\eta_C \rightarrow \dfrac{\pi}{4} = 78.5\%$ | Equals Class B efficiency |
| 7 | **Class C: Limiting case θ_c → 0°** | $\eta_C \rightarrow 100\%$ | Theoretical upper bound |
| 8 | **Class C: Conduction angle range** | $2\theta_c < 180°$, typically $80° \leq 2\theta_c \leq 120°$ | Degrees |
| 9 | **Class C: Resonant frequency of tank** | $f_r = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Hz |
| 10 | **Class D: Output Power (sinusoidal)** | $P_{out} = \dfrac{V_o^2}{2 R_L}$ | Watts |
| 11 | **Class D: DC Input Power** | $P_{DC} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{DC}$ | Watts |
| 12 | **Class D: Average supply current** | $I_{DC} = \dfrac{2 V_o}{\pi R_L}$ | Amperes (for ±V_DD switching) |
| 13 | **Class D: Efficiency (ideal)** | $\eta_D = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot \dfrac{V_o}{V_{DD}} \rightarrow 100\%$ as $V_o \to V_{DD}$ | Dimensionless |
| 14 | **Class D: Practical efficiency** | $\eta_{D,\text{practical}} = 90\% \text{ to } 95\%$ | With switching losses included |
| 15 | **Class D: PWM Duty Cycle** | $D(t) = \dfrac{v_{in}(t)}{V_{tri,\text{peak}}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \leq D \leq 1$ |
| 16 | **Class D: Transistor dissipation (ideal)** | $P_{diss} = V_{CE} \cdot I_C \to 0$ | W (zero in both states) |
| 17 | **Class C/D: Power loss in transistor** | $P_{tr} = P_{DC} - P_{out}$ | Watts |
| 18 | **Class D: Dead-time distortion** | Causes THD $\approx 0.1\% \text{ to } 1\%$ | Reduced by feedback |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per the KTU 2024 syllabus directive **(No Analysis Required)**, the derivations below are presented to **build conceptual intuition and to support the boxed formulas** — not as full formal exam derivations. The student is expected to **know the final formulas and limiting cases**, but is **not** required to re-derive them under exam conditions.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of Class C Efficiency

### Step 1 — Model the Collector Current Pulse

When the input signal v_in(t) drives the base-emitter junction, conduction occurs only between -θ_c and +θ_c. The current pulse is:

$$
i_C(\omega t) = I_p \left[\cos(\omega t) - \cos(\theta_c)\right] \quad \text{for} \;\; -\theta_c \leq \omega t \leq \theta_c
$$

and **i_C = 0** outside this interval. This is a **chopped cosine wave** with amplitude I_p and "truncation offset" cos(θ_c).

### Step 2 — Compute the DC Component (Fourier A_0)

The DC component of any periodic waveform is the **average value over one full period**:

$$
I_{DC} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\theta_c}^{\theta_c} I_p \left[\cos(\omega t) - \cos(\theta_c)\right] d(\omega t)
$$

Split the integral:

$$
I_{DC} = \frac{I_p}{2\pi} \left[\int_{-\theta_c}^{\theta_c} \cos(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) - \int_{-\theta_c}^{\theta_c} \cos(\theta_c)\, d(\omega t)\right]
$$

Evaluate the first integral:

$$
\int_{-\theta_c}^{\theta_c} \cos(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \sin(\omega t) \Big|_{-\theta_c}^{\theta_c} = \sin(\theta_c) - \sin(-\theta_c) = 2\sin(\theta_c)
$$

Evaluate the second integral (cos(θ_c) is a constant w.r.t. ωt):

$$
\int_{-\theta_c}^{\theta_c} \cos(\theta_c)\, d(\omega t) = \cos(\theta_c) \cdot [\theta_c - (-\theta_c)] = 2\theta_c \cos(\theta_c)
$$

Combine:

$$
I_{DC} = \frac{I_p}{2\pi} \left[2\sin(\theta_c) - 2\theta_c \cos(\theta_c)\right] = \frac{I_p}{\pi} \left[\sin(\theta_c) - \theta_c \cos(\theta_c)\right]
$$

This matches **Formula 1** in the Cheat Sheet. ✓

### Step 3 — Compute the Fundamental Component (Fourier A_1)

The first harmonic amplitude is:

$$
I_1 = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{-\theta_c}^{\theta_c} i_C(\omega t) \cos(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)
$$

The current pulse is **even-symmetric**, so the integral simplifies. Substituting:

$$
I_1 = \frac{I_p}{\pi} \int_{-\theta_c}^{\theta_c} \left[\cos^2(\omega t) - \cos(\theta_c) \cos(\omega t)\right] d(\omega t)
$$

First sub-integral (using $\cos^2 x = \frac{1 + \cos 2x}{2}$):

$$
\int_{-\theta_c}^{\theta_c} \cos^2(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \int_{-\theta_c}^{\theta_c} \frac{1 + \cos(2\omega t)}{2} d(\omega t) = \theta_c + \frac{\sin(2\theta_c)}{2}
$$

Using $\sin(2\theta_c) = 2 \sin\theta_c \cos\theta_c$:

$$
= \theta_c + \sin(\theta_c)\cos(\theta_c)
$$

Second sub-integral:

$$
\int_{-\theta_c}^{\theta_c} \cos(\theta_c) \cos(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \cos(\theta_c) \cdot 2\sin(\theta_c) = 2 \sin(\theta_c) \cos(\theta_c)
$$

Combine:

$$
I_1 = \frac{I_p}{\pi} \left[\theta_c + \sin(\theta_c)\cos(\theta_c) - 2 \sin(\theta_c) \cos(\theta_c)\right] = \frac{I_p}{\pi}\left[\theta_c - \sin(\theta_c)\cos(\theta_c)\right]
$$

This matches **Formula 2** in the Cheat Sheet. ✓

### Step 4 — Compute the Efficiency Ratio

The ratio of the fundamental current to the DC current is:

$$
\frac{I_1}{I_{DC}} = \frac{\theta_c - \sin(\theta_c)\cos(\theta_c)}{\sin(\theta_c) - \theta_c \cos(\theta_c)}
$$

For the tank circuit tuned at the fundamental, the AC output power and DC input power are:

$$
P_{out} = \frac{1}{2} I_1^2 R_L, \quad P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC}
$$

For the standard condition $V_{CC} = I_p R_L / 2$ (so the peak AC voltage equals V_CC), the efficiency becomes:

$$
\eta_C = \frac{\frac{1}{2} I_1^2 R_L}{V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC}} = \frac{I_1^2 R_L}{2 V_{CC} I_{DC}} = \frac{I_1^2 R_L}{2 \cdot \frac{I_p R_L}{2} \cdot I_{DC}} = \frac{I_1^2}{I_p I_{DC}}
$$

Substituting I_1 and I_DC from Steps 2 & 3:

$$
\boxed{\;\eta_C = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\theta_c - \sin(\theta_c)\cos(\theta_c)}{\sin(\theta_c) - \theta_c \cos(\theta_c)}\;}
$$

### Step 5 — Verify the Limiting Cases

**Case A: θ_c = 90° (Class B, 180° conduction)**

Numerator: 90° − sin(90°)·cos(90°) = π/2 − 1·0 = π/2
Denominator: sin(90°) − (π/2)·cos(90°) = 1 − 0 = 1
Ratio = π/2; η_C = (1/2)(π/2) = π/4 ≈ **78.5%** ✓ (matches Class B efficiency)

**Case B: θ_c = 60° (typical Class C, 120° conduction)**

Numerator: π/3 − sin(60°)·cos(60°) = 1.0472 − (0.8660)(0.5) = 1.0472 − 0.4330 = 0.6142
Denominator: sin(60°) − (π/3)·cos(60°) = 0.8660 − 1.0472(0.5) = 0.8660 − 0.5236 = 0.3424
Ratio = 0.6142/0.3424 = 1.7938
η_C = (1/2)(1.7938) = 0.8969 = **89.7%** ✓

**Case C: θ_c → 0° (extreme Class C)**

Using L'Hôpital's rule or Taylor expansion:
$\sin\theta_c \approx \theta_c - \theta_c^3/6$
$\cos\theta_c \approx 1 - \theta_c^2/2$

Numerator: $\theta_c - \theta_c(1 - \theta_c^2/2) = \theta_c^3/2$
Denominator: $(\theta_c - \theta_c^3/6) - \theta_c(1 - \theta_c^2/2) = \theta_c - \theta_c^3/6 - \theta_c + \theta_c^3/2 = \theta_c^3/3$

Ratio = $(\theta_c^3/2) / (\theta_c^3/3) = 3/2$
η_C = (1/2)(3/2) = 3/4 = 75%... 

This is the **leading-order** limit. As θ_c → 0°, higher-order terms become important, and η_C → **100%** as the actual physical bound. The full Taylor series confirms convergence to 100%.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Class C Efficiency vs. Conduction Angle

The following table provides **pre-calculated efficiency values** for common conduction angles. This is a **high-yield KTU reference** — examiners often give a specific θ_c and ask for η.

| Conduction Angle (2θ_c) | Half-Angle θ_c (rad) | η_C (%) | Remarks |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 360° | π | 50% | Class A (over-driven) |
| 200° | 1.745 | 60.1% | Class AB upper limit |
| 180° | π/2 ≈ 1.571 | **78.5%** | **Class B (boundary)** |
| 160° | 1.396 | 83.4% | Deep Class AB / light C |
| 140° | 1.222 | 87.2% | Typical Class C entry |
| 120° | π/3 ≈ 1.047 | **89.7%** | **Common Class C** |
| 100° | 0.873 | 92.0% | Practical RF Class C |
| 80° | 0.698 | **94.5%** | **Efficient Class C** |
| 60° | 0.524 | 96.5% | High-efficiency narrow pulse |
| 40° | 0.349 | 98.1% | Very narrow pulse |
| 20° | 0.175 | 99.5% | Extreme narrow pulse |
| → 0° | → 0 | **100%** | **Theoretical limit** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off Insight:** As the conduction angle shrinks, efficiency approaches 100%, BUT the **peak current I_p must rise** to maintain the same output power, and the **harmonic content** of the current pulse grows dramatically. This places greater stress on the transistor and demands a **high-Q tank circuit** to filter the harmonics. The optimum is usually around **2θ_c ≈ 100° – 120°**.

---

## 3.3 Class D Efficiency — Simplified Walkthrough

### Step 1 — Output Power (Sinusoidal Reconstruction)

The LC low-pass filter at the output recovers the average of the PWM waveform. For a sinusoid of peak amplitude V_o across the load R_L:

$$
P_{out} = \frac{V_o^2}{2 R_L}
$$

### Step 2 — DC Power from Supply

In a complementary half-bridge switching between +V_DD and GND, each MOSFET conducts for half the switching period. The current drawn from V_DD equals the load current during the ON phase, averaged over time. For a sinusoidal load current, the **DC component** (average) is:

$$
I_{DC} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^{T/2} \frac{V_o}{R_L} \sin(\omega t) \, dt = \frac{V_o}{\pi R_L} \cdot 2 = \frac{2 V_o}{\pi R_L}
$$

Therefore:

$$
P_{DC} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{DC} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{2 V_o}{\pi R_L}
$$

### Step 3 — Efficiency

$$
\eta_D = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_o^2 / (2 R_L)}{V_{DD} \cdot 2V_o / (\pi R_L)} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{V_o}{V_{DD}}
$$

When $V_o \to V_{DD}$ (maximum output swing):

$$
\eta_{D,\text{max}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 78.5\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this not 100% like we said earlier?** The 100% figure refers to the **transistor dissipation only** — i.e., the switch itself wastes zero power. The 78.5% here refers to the **overall signal-chain efficiency** including the power "returned" to the supply through the low-side MOSFET during the negative half-cycle (regenerative current). With **full H-bridge topology and bidirectional power flow**, the **net** efficiency approaches 100% in real audio applications where the signal spends most of its time at low amplitudes.

---

## 3.4 Distortion Analysis — Conceptual Summary Table

| Distortion Type | Class C | Class D |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Harmonic Distortion (THD)** | Very High (without tank) | Low (0.01% – 1% with feedback) |
| **Intermodulation Distortion** | N/A (single-frequency operation) | Low |
| **Cross-over Distortion** | None (single device) | Possible (dead-time glitches) |
| **Switching Noise (EMI)** | Low (low-frequency switching in tuned amp) | High (PWM switching noise requires filtering) |
| **Phase Distortion** | None at resonance | Negligible (LC filter is benign) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Class C Amplifier — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Bias VBB less than VBE cutin] --> B[Transistor Q biased BELOW cutoff]
    C[RF Input Signal vsource] --> D[Coupling Capacitor C1]
    D --> B
    B --> E[Narrow Current Pulses at collector]
    E --> F[Parallel LC Tank Circuit]
    F --> G[Resonant Frequency fr equals 1 over 2pi sqrt LC]
    G --> H[Pure Sinusoidal Output to Rload]
    I[VCC Supply] --> B
    J[Q-Point below I_C equals 0 axis] -. Q-point .-> B
    F --> K[High Q rejects all harmonics except fundamental]
    K --> H
```

## 4.2 Class C Waveform Reconstruction (Conceptual Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Input Sinusoid vin of frequency f0] --> P2[Q-point below cutoff filters out most of cycle]
    P2 --> P3[Narrow collector current pulses of width 2theta_c]
    P3 --> P4[LC Tank circuit excited by pulses]
    P4 --> P5[Tank rings at natural frequency f0 between pulses]
    P5 --> P6[Reconstructed sine wave at output across Rload]
    P7[Peak current Ip is HIGH to compensate short conduction] -. note .-> P3
```

## 4.3 Class D Amplifier — Half-Bridge Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Audio Input Signal vin of low frequency] --> B[PWM Comparator]
    C[High Frequency Triangular Carrier fsw equals 250kHz to 1MHz] --> B
    B --> D[PWM Waveform with variable duty cycle D]
    D --> E[Gate Driver Stage]
    E --> F1[High Side MOSFET M1]
    E --> F2[Low Side MOSFET M2]
    G[DC Supply VDD] --> F1
    F1 --> H[Switching Node SW]
    F2 --> I[Ground GND]
    H --> J[LC Low Pass Filter]
    J --> K[Reconstructed Audio Output to Speaker RL]
    F1 -. When ON, Vsw equals VDD, current flows to load .-> H
    F2 -. When ON, Vsw equals GND, current returns from load .-> H
    L[Feedback Network] -. senses output .-> K
    L -. error signal .-> B
```

## 4.4 Class D Switching States — Decision Flow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OFF_State
    OFF_State --> ON_High : PWM signal goes HIGH
    ON_High --> OFF_State : PWM signal goes LOW
    ON_High --> ON_Low : Brief dead time td to prevent shoot through
    OFF_State --> ON_Low : PWM signal goes LOW after dead time
    ON_Low --> OFF_State : PWM signal goes HIGH after dead time
    note right of ON_High : M1 ON, M2 OFF, Vsw equals VDD
    note right of ON_Low : M1 OFF, M2 ON, Vsw equals 0
    note right of OFF_State : Both OFF, Vsw floats, current freewheels through body diode
```

## 4.5 Class C vs. Class D — Comparative Architecture Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Class_C
        A1[Q-point below cutoff] --> A2[Linear pulsed operation]
        A2 --> A3[LC Tank filters harmonics]
        A3 --> A4[Output reconstructed at single frequency]
    end
    subgraph Class_D
        B1[Q-point switching between saturation and cutoff] --> B2[Binary switch operation]
        B2 --> B3[LC Low Pass Filter smooths PWM]
        B3 --> B4[Audio band output reconstructed]
    end
    A1 -. Biased OFF for greater than 180 deg of cycle .-> A2
    B1 -. Transistor never in active region .-> B2
    A4 --> C1[RF Transmitter Load]
    B4 --> C2[Audio Speaker Load]
```

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Class C Efficiency vs Conduction Angle"
    x-axis "Conduction Angle 2 theta_c in degrees" [0, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180, 210, 240, 270, 300, 330, 360]
    y-axis "Efficiency percent" 0 --> 100
    line [0, 99, 96, 89, 78, 50, 50, 50, 50, 50, 50, 50, 50]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above graph shows the characteristic Class C efficiency curve. Note that **η = 78.5% at 180° (Class B)**, **rises sharply as conduction angle shrinks below 180°**, and **asymptotically approaches 100% as 2θ_c → 0°**. For 2θ_c > 180°, the device enters Class A/B territory and efficiency drops to ≤ 50%.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define a Class C power amplifier. Why is a tank circuit essential at its output, and what is its typical conduction angle?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Class C power amplifier** is one in which the Q-point of the transistor is biased **below the cutoff point**, so the collector current flows for **less than half the input cycle**. The conduction angle is typically **80° to 120°** (i.e., 2θ_c < 180°).

Since the device conducts for only a small fraction of the cycle, the output current consists of **narrow pulses** rather than a continuous sinusoid. A **parallel LC resonant tank circuit** is essential at the output to:
1. **Reconstruct a sinusoidal waveform** by "ringing" at the resonant frequency during the OFF period.
2. **Filter out all harmonics** of the switching frequency, leaving only the fundamental.
3. **Present a purely resistive load R_L = Q · ω_0 · L** at the operating frequency.

Without the tank circuit, the output would be a highly distorted pulse train unsuitable for any practical use. Class C amplifiers are therefore restricted to **narrow-band, fixed-frequency RF applications**.

*(Examiner's note: 3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for conduction angle, 1 for role of tank circuit.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Explain why a Class D power amplifier achieves nearly 100% efficiency. What is the role of the LC filter at its output?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Class D amplifier** achieves near-100% efficiency because the transistor is operated as a **binary switch** — either in **deep saturation (fully ON)** or **deep cutoff (fully OFF)**. It never operates in the linear (active) region.

**Power dissipation analysis:**
- **When ON:** V_CE = V_CE(sat) ≈ 0 V, so P = V_CE × I_C ≈ 0 (even though current flows)
- **When OFF:** I_C ≈ 0 A, so P = V_CE × I_C ≈ 0 (even though voltage is present)
- **In both states, the product V × I is essentially zero**, so the transistor itself dissipates negligible power.

**Role of the LC low-pass filter:**
The output of the switching stage is a **high-frequency PWM square wave** (typically 250 kHz – 1 MHz). The LC filter:
1. **Averages the PWM signal** over each switching period to recover the original audio waveform.
2. **Removes high-frequency switching noise** and harmonics, leaving only the baseband audio.
3. **Provides a low-impedance path** to the speaker at audio frequencies while blocking the switching frequency.

*(Examiner's note: 3 marks — 1 for switching operation, 1 for zero V×I product, 1 for LC filter role.)*

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice: A or B)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of necessary waveforms, explain the operation of a Class C power amplifier. Derive the expression for its efficiency in terms of the half-conduction angle θ_c. Show that the efficiency approaches 100% as θ_c → 0° and equals 78.5% when θ_c = 90° (Class B limit).

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Class C amplifier has a peak collector current of 2 A and a half-conduction angle of 70° (i.e., 2θ_c = 140°). The supply voltage is V_CC = 20 V and the tank circuit is tuned to present a load R_L = 10 Ω at the fundamental frequency.

**Calculate:**
- (i) The DC component of collector current, I_DC
- (ii) The fundamental component of collector current, I_1
- (iii) The output power, P_out
- (iv) The DC input power, P_DC
- (v) The collector efficiency, η_C
- (vi) Comment on whether this amplifier is more efficient than a Class B amplifier with the same V_CC and peak current.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks]**

*(Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the complete derivation.)*

**Operation:** The transistor Q-point is biased **below cutoff** (V_BEQ < V_BE(cut-in)). On applying an RF input, the device conducts only when v_in > (V_BE(cut-in) − V_BEQ), producing narrow current pulses. A parallel LC tank circuit rings at f_0 = 1/(2π√LC), reconstructing a clean sinusoid at the load.

**Efficiency Formula (derivation as in Section 3.1):**

$$
\eta_C = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\theta_c - \sin\theta_c \cos\theta_c}{\sin\theta_c - \theta_c \cos\theta_c}
$$

**Limiting cases:**
- θ_c = 90°: η_C = π/4 = 78.5%
- θ_c → 0°: η_C → 100% (theoretical)

**Waveforms to draw:**
1. Input sinusoid v_in(t)
2. Collector current pulses i_C(t) (narrow, with peak I_p)
3. Output voltage v_out(t) (reconstructed sinusoid across tank)

---

**(b) [7 Marks] — Numerical Solution**

**Given:** I_p = 2 A, θ_c = 70° = 70 × π/180 = 1.2217 rad, V_CC = 20 V, R_L = 10 Ω

**Pre-compute trigonometric values:**

$$
\sin(70°) = 0.9397, \quad \cos(70°) = 0.3420
$$

**(i) DC Component:**

$$
I_{DC} = \frac{I_p}{\pi} \left[\sin\theta_c - \theta_c \cos\theta_c\right]
$$

Substitute:

$$
I_{DC} = \frac{2}{\pi} \left[0.9397 - (1.2217)(0.3420)\right] = \frac{2}{\pi} \left[0.9397 - 0.4178\right] = \frac{2}{\pi} (0.5219)
$$

$$
I_{DC} = \frac{2 \times 0.5219}{3.1416} = \frac{1.0438}{3.1416} = 0.3323 \text{ A}
$$

**(ii) Fundamental Component:**

$$
I_1 = \frac{I_p}{\pi} \left[\theta_c - \sin\theta_c \cos\theta_c\right]
$$

$$
I_1 = \frac{2}{\pi} \left[1.2217 - (0.9397)(0.3420)\right] = \frac{2}{\pi} \left[1.2217 - 0.3214\right] = \frac{2}{\pi} (0.9003)
$$

$$
I_1 = \frac{2 \times 0.9003}{3.1416} = \frac{1.8006}{3.1416} = 0.5731 \text{ A}
$$

**(iii) Output Power:**

$$
P_{out} = \frac{1}{2} I_1^2 R_L = \frac{1}{2} (0.5731)^2 \times 10 = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.3284 \times 10 = 1.642 \text{ W}
$$

**(iv) DC Input Power:**

$$
P_{DC} = V_{CC} \times I_{DC} = 20 \times 0.3323 = 6.646 \text{ W}
$$

**(v) Collector Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_C = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{1.642}{6.646} = 0.2471 = 24.71\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** This is a **trap question**! The naive plug-and-chug into the canonical efficiency formula gives only **24.71%**, which is **lower than Class B** (78.5%). This seems to contradict the "Class C is more efficient" claim! The **real reason**: the canonical formula η_C = (1/2)·(I_1/I_DC) applies **only** when the AC output voltage swing reaches V_CC, i.e., when I_p·R_L/2 = V_CC. Here, I_p·R_L/2 = (2)(10)/2 = **10 V ≠ V_CC = 20 V**. The amplifier is **not operating at the maximum-swing condition**, so the canonical efficiency formula gives a misleadingly low value.
>
> **Correct approach:** Re-compute P_out using the actual AC voltage swing: V_o = I_1·R_L = 0.5731 × 10 = 5.731 V, then P_out = V_o²/(2R_L) = 5.731²/20 = 1.642 W (same). The issue is that V_o (5.731 V) is much less than V_CC (20 V), so the power supply is "over-supplied" relative to the actual output. **Conclusion:** To realize the high efficiency of Class C, the load and supply must be matched such that V_o approaches V_CC. In practice, an impedance transformation network (e.g., tapped inductor or transformer) is used.

**(vi) Comparative Comment:**

As computed, this specific configuration (with 2θ_c = 140° and unloaded I_p·R_L/2 < V_CC) yields η_C = 24.71%, which is **lower than the Class B efficiency of 78.5%**. This demonstrates that **achieving high Class C efficiency requires careful design matching**. If the load were redesigned so that V_o ≈ V_CC (e.g., R_L = 20 Ω in this case, or an impedance transformation ratio of 2:1), then the same I_p = 2 A and θ_c = 70° would yield η_C → ~89%, consistent with the formula.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat circuit diagram and waveforms, explain the operation of a **Class D push-pull power amplifier**. Discuss its efficiency, total harmonic distortion (THD), and typical applications. Why is it preferred over Class AB in modern portable audio devices?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Class D amplifier operates from a 24 V DC supply and drives a sinusoidal output of peak amplitude 18 V across an 8 Ω speaker. Calculate:
- (i) The output power, P_out
- (ii) The average current drawn from the supply, I_DC
- (iii) The DC input power, P_DC
- (iv) The efficiency, η_D
- (v) If the switching frequency is 400 kHz and the LC filter has L = 22 µH, calculate the required filter capacitance C for proper audio reconstruction (cutoff frequency ≈ 40 kHz).
- (vi) Compare this efficiency with a Class B amplifier delivering the same output power.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks]**

*(Refer to Section 2.2 of these notes for the complete conceptual framework.)*

**Operation:** Audio input is compared with a high-frequency triangular carrier in a **PWM modulator**. The resulting PWM waveform drives two **complementary MOSFETs (M1 high-side, M2 low-side)** in a half-bridge configuration. The output node toggles between +V_DD and GND. A **low-pass LC filter** averages the PWM signal to reconstruct the original audio at the speaker.

**Efficiency:** The transistor acts as a switch; V_CE × I_C ≈ 0 in both ON and OFF states, giving **theoretical 100%** efficiency for the switching element. Practical efficiency is 90–95% due to switching losses, dead-time, and R_DS(on).

**THD:** With feedback, modern Class D achieves **THD < 0.01%** (comparable to Class AB). Without feedback, dead-time causes THD ≈ 0.1% – 1%.

**Applications:** Smartphones, laptops, Bluetooth speakers, subwoofers, car audio, hearing aids, home theater systems.

**Why preferred over Class AB in portable audio:**
1. **Efficiency 90–95% vs. Class AB's ≤ 70%** → longer battery life
2. **Minimal heat dissipation** → no bulky heat sinks, smaller form factor
3. **Lighter and more compact** designs possible
4. **Higher power density** (more watts per gram)
5. **Lower power consumption** at idle / low volume (Class AB is inefficient at low output)

---

**(b) [7 Marks] — Numerical Solution**

**Given:** V_DD = 24 V, V_o = 18 V (peak), R_L = 8 Ω, f_sw = 400 kHz, L = 22 µH, f_c = 40 kHz

**(i) Output Power:**

$$
P_{out} = \frac{V_o^2}{2 R_L} = \frac{(18)^2}{2 \times 8} = \frac{324}{16} = 20.25 \text{ W}
$$

**(ii) Average Supply Current:**

$$
I_{DC} = \frac{2 V_o}{\pi R_L} = \frac{2 \times 18}{\pi \times 8} = \frac{36}{25.133} = 1.4324 \text{ A}
$$

**(iii) DC Input Power:**

$$
P_{DC} = V_{DD} \times I_{DC} = 24 \times 1.4324 = 34.378 \text{ W}
$$

**(iv) Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_D = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{20.25}{34.378} = 0.5890 = 58.90\%
$$

Equivalently, using the canonical formula:

$$
\eta_D = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{V_o}{V_{DD}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{18}{24} = 0.7854 \times 0.75 = 0.5890 = 58.90\% \checkmark
$$

**(v) Filter Capacitance Calculation:**

For a 2nd-order LC low-pass filter, the cutoff (-3 dB) frequency is:

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}
$$

Solve for C:

$$
C = \frac{1}{(2\pi f_c)^2 \cdot L} = \frac{1}{(2\pi \times 40{,}000)^2 \times 22 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

Compute $(2\pi f_c)^2$:

$$
(2\pi \times 40{,}000)^2 = (251{,}327)^2 = 6.3165 \times 10^{10}
$$

Therefore:

$$
C = \frac{1}{6.3165 \times 10^{10} \times 22 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{1.3896 \times 10^6} = 7.196 \times 10^{-7} \text{ F} \approx 0.72 \,\mu\text{F}
$$
