**Standard value:** Use **C = 0.68 µF or 1 µF** (closest E12 value).

**(vi) Comparison with Class B:**

For the **same output power** P_out = 20.25 W from a Class B push-pull amplifier:
- Class B efficiency: η_B = π/4 = **78.5%** (theoretical maximum at full output)
- P_DC(B) = 20.25 / 0.785 = **25.80 W**
- Power dissipated as heat: 5.55 W

For this **specific** Class D operating point (V_o/V_DD = 0.75):
- η_D = 58.90%
- Power dissipated as heat: 14.13 W

**Verdict:** At this particular output level, **Class B is more efficient** (78.5% vs. 58.9%) because Class D's efficiency is proportional to V_o/V_DD. However, in **real audio** (music, speech), the average output level is much lower than peak, so Class D's **average efficiency over time** (90%+) far exceeds Class B (which is poor at low outputs). This is why Class D dominates portable audio despite the lower peak efficiency at full power.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Mistake in Class D Numericals:**
> 1. **Confusing peak vs. RMS:** Students often use V_o (peak) in P = V²/R formulas without the 1/2 factor. Always use **P_out = V_peak² / (2 R_L)**.
> 2. **Forgetting the π in I_DC formula:** I_DC = 2V_o/(πR_L), NOT V_o/R_L. The factor 2/π comes from the average of the rectified sinusoid.
> 3. **Not specifying V_o vs. V_DD:** The formula η_D = (π/4)(V_o/V_DD) shows that efficiency depends on the output amplitude. Do not assume V_o = V_DD unless stated.
> 4. **Unit errors in LC filter:** f_c is in Hz, L in H, C in F. Mix-up of µH, mH, nF, µF causes numerical errors.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Class C and D Power Amplifiers**

### 🔑 Class C — Key Takeaways
- [ ] Q-point is **below the cutoff axis**; conduction angle **2θ_c < 180°** (typically 80°–120°).
- [ ] **Mandatory LC tank circuit** at the output to reconstruct the sinusoid; works only at **one fixed frequency**.
- [ ] Efficiency formula: **η_C = (1/2)·(θ_c − sin θ_c cos θ_c)/(sin θ_c − θ_c cos θ_c)**.
- [ ] Limiting case: **θ_c = 90° → η_C = 78.5%** (Class B); **θ_c → 0° → η_C → 100%**.
- [ ] **Cannot be used for audio** (high harmonic distortion); used only in **RF transmitters, IF stages, oscillators**.
- [ ] **No cross-over distortion** (single device, no class-B type handover).
- [ ] DC component: **I_DC = (I_p/π)(sin θ_c − θ_c cos θ_c)**.
- [ ] Fundamental component: **I_1 = (I_p/π)(θ_c − sin θ_c cos θ_c)**.
- [ ] Practical design choice: **2θ_c ≈ 100°–120°** balances efficiency and harmonic content.
- [ ] Higher efficiency demands **higher peak current I_p** for the same output power.

### 🔑 Class D — Key Takeaways
- [ ] Transistor operates as a **switch** (saturation or cutoff only — never linear).
- [ ] **PWM input** drives complementary MOSFETs in push-pull (half-bridge or full-bridge).
- [ ] **LC low-pass filter** at the output reconstructs the audio signal.
- [ ] **Theoretical efficiency η_D = 100%** (V_CE × I_C = 0 in both switch states).
- [ ] **Practical efficiency = 90–95%** (limited by switching losses, dead-time, R_DS(on)).
- [ ] Efficiency formula (with sinusoidal output): **η_D = (π/4)·(V_o / V_DD)**.
- [ ] Dominates **portable audio** (phones, laptops, Bluetooth speakers, subwoofers).
- [ ] With **feedback**, modern Class D THD < 0.01% (rivals Class AB).
- [ ] **Dead-time** between high-side and low-side switching is critical to prevent **shoot-through**.
- [ ] EMI from switching requires **shielding and LC filtering**.
- [ ] Switching frequency typically **250 kHz – 1 MHz** (well above audio band of 20 Hz – 20 kHz).

### 🔑 Common KTU Exam Mistakes to Avoid
- [ ] Do **not** confuse Class C efficiency formula (uses θ_c) with Class B (78.5% constant).
- [ ] Do **not** apply Class C equations when 2θ_c ≥ 180° (formula breaks down).
- [ ] For Class D, **do not** assume 100% practical efficiency — always cite 90–95% as the realistic number.
- [ ] **Always include the π/4 factor** (or 2/π in I_DC) in Class D numerical problems.
- [ ] Class C **requires** a tuned load (tank) — answer is incomplete without it.
- [ ] Class D **requires** PWM modulation and an LC output filter — answer is incomplete without both.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** When asked to **compare** Class C and Class D, organize your answer around the four pillars: (1) **Operating principle**, (2) **Efficiency**, (3) **Distortion & output fidelity**, (4) **Typical application**. A structured, four-point comparison always scores full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Voltage Regulators: Types of Voltage Regulators - Series and Shunt - Working and Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Linear Voltage Regulators: Types of Voltage Regulators - Series and Shunt - Working and Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Voltage Regulator** is an electronic circuit (or subsystem) designed to automatically maintain a **constant output voltage** ($V_{OUT}$) at a predetermined level, irrespective of variations in the **unregulated input voltage** ($V_{IN}$), the **load current** ($I_L$), or the ambient **temperature**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, voltage regulators are the final active stage of a DC power supply, positioned after the **rectifier** and the **smoothing filter**. They compensate for the two fundamental flaws of a raw DC supply:
1. **Ripple** (residual AC component at filter output).
2. **DC drift** caused by $V_{IN}$ variation (mains fluctuation) and $I_L$ variation (load change).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (must memorize verbatim):**
> "A voltage regulator is a circuit that provides a **constant DC output voltage** that is **independent of input voltage variations, load current variations, and temperature**."

### 1.2 Two Major Categories of Regulators

The KTU Module-4 syllabus classifies linear voltage regulators into the following fundamental types:

| S. No. | Category | Sub-Types | Typical Examples |
|:------:|:---------|:----------|:-----------------|
| 1 | **Linear Regulators** | Series (Pass) Regulator | 7805, 7812, LM317, 2N3055 pass element |
| 2 | **Linear Regulators** | Shunt (Parallel) Regulator | Zener diode regulator, Transistor shunt regulator |
| 3 | **Linear IC Regulators** | Fixed / Adjustable | µA723, LM7805, LM7905 (negative) |
| 4 | **Switched Mode Regulators** | Buck / Boost / Buck-Boost | LM2596, IC 555 based flyback |

This module focuses exclusively on **Series and Shunt Linear Regulators**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of an electrical circuit as a water pipeline system:**
> - **Unregulated DC input** → Water entering a tank at *varying pressure* (mains voltage may sag to 180 V or surge to 250 V).
> - **Regulated DC output** → A *steady, predictable water pressure* at your kitchen tap.
>
> **Series Regulator Analogy:** A **variable restriction valve (gate valve)** placed *in series* with the pipeline. As inlet pressure rises, the valve closes slightly; as it falls, the valve opens. Pressure *after* the valve remains constant. The valve "absorbs" the excess pressure drop — equivalent to $V_{CE}$ across the pass transistor.
>
> **Shunt Regulator Analogy:** A **pressure-relief overflow pipe** placed *in parallel* with the outlet. When input pressure rises, excess water is *diverted* through the overflow pipe (Zener diode conducts more). The pressure at the tap remains constant. The bypass pipe is the **shunt path** that dumps excess current to ground.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

The quality of any linear regulator is evaluated by the following **four performance parameters** (Board examiners expect these exact terms):

1. **Line Regulation (LR):** The ability to reject changes in $V_{IN}$ while keeping $I_L$ constant. Expressed in **mV/V** or as a percentage.
2. **Load Regulation (LdR):** The ability to maintain $V_{OUT}$ when $I_L$ changes. Expressed in **mV/mA** or as a percentage.
3. **Ripple Rejection (PSRR):** The ability to suppress the AC ripple riding on $V_{IN}$. Expressed in **dB** at a specific frequency (typically 120 Hz, the full-wave rectified frequency in 50 Hz mains).
4. **Efficiency ($\eta$):** The ratio of DC power delivered to the load vs. power drawn from the unregulated source.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Numeric Reality Check (KVL Truth):** The fundamental law that governs *all* linear regulators is:
> $$V_{IN} = V_{OUT} + V_{drop}$$
> where $V_{drop}$ is the voltage "wasted" across the regulator's control element. The maximum theoretical efficiency is therefore:
> $$\eta_{max} = \frac{V_{OUT}}{V_{IN}} \times 100\%$$
> This is why linear regulators are inherently inefficient when $V_{IN} \gg V_{OUT}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why is Regulation Necessary? (The "Why")

A rectifier-filter output is **not** a pure DC source. It suffers from:
- **Source variation:** Indian mains swings between **170 V – 250 V** (per IS 12360), causing $\pm 20\%$ variation in unregulated DC.
- **Load variation:** A digital circuit (e.g., a microcontroller board) draws different current in active vs. sleep modes.
- **Ripple:** A 50 Hz mains full-wave rectifier produces a 100 Hz residual ripple across the filter capacitor.

A regulator "locks" $V_{OUT}$ to a fixed reference (typically a **Zener diode breakdown voltage** $V_Z$, which has a low temperature coefficient when properly biased).

### 2.2 Series Voltage Regulator (Pass Regulator)

**Definition:** A regulator in which the **control element (transistor) is connected in SERIES** with the load. The transistor behaves as a **variable resistor** whose instantaneous resistance is automatically adjusted to absorb the difference between $V_{IN}$ and $V_{OUT}$.

**Block Diagram Logic (Five Functional Blocks):**

1. **Reference Source:** Zener diode $D_Z$ biased by $R_S$, producing a stable $V_Z$.
2. **Sampling Network:** A resistive voltage divider ($R_1, R_2$) that samples a fraction of $V_{OUT}$.
3. **Error Amplifier (Comparator):** Compares the sampled voltage with $V_Z$ and produces an error signal.
4. **Pass Element (Series Transistor $Q_1$):** A power transistor (often a Darlington pair for high $\beta$) whose $V_{CE}$ is varied by the error signal.
5. **Feedback Loop:** The negative feedback ensures any deviation in $V_{OUT}$ is corrected within microseconds.

**Working — The "How" Step by Step:**

- **Case 1: $V_{IN}$ increases** (mains rises) → $V_{OUT}$ tries to rise → sampled voltage rises → error amplifier output decreases → $V_{BE}$ of $Q_1$ decreases → $V_{CE}$ of $Q_1$ increases → $V_{OUT} = V_{IN} - V_{CE}$ is restored. ✓
- **Case 2: $I_L$ increases** (heavier load) → $V_{OUT}$ tries to sag → sampled voltage drops → error amplifier output rises → $V_{BE}$ of $Q_1$ rises → $Q_1$ conducts harder → $V_{CE}$ drops → $V_{OUT}$ is restored. ✓
- **Case 3: $I_L$ decreases** → $Q_1$ conducts less → $V_{CE}$ rises → $V_{OUT}$ maintained. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Pass Transistor Power Dissipation (Critical for Design):**
> $$P_{Q_1} = V_{CE} \times I_L = (V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \times I_L$$
> This is the heat the series transistor must dissipate. A heatsink is mandatory in KTU design problems.

**Closed-Loop Output Voltage (with feedback divider):**

$$V_{OUT} = V_Z \left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

For an **emitter follower** (simple series regulator without op-amp), $V_{OUT} = V_Z - V_{BE}$.

### 2.3 Shunt Voltage Regulator

**Definition:** A regulator in which the **control element is connected in PARALLEL (shunt) with the load**. Excess current from the source is **diverted through the shunt element to ground** rather than absorbed as heat in a series element.

**Standard Zener Shunt Regulator Topology:**

The simplest form uses a **Zener diode** as the shunt element and a **series resistor $R_S$** as the current-setting element.

**Working — The "How" Step by Step:**

- **KCL at output node:** $I_S = I_Z + I_L$
- $I_S$ = current through $R_S$ = $\dfrac{V_{IN} - V_Z}{R_S}$
- **Case 1: $V_{IN}$ increases** → $I_S$ increases momentarily → $V_Z$ tries to rise → Zener conducts more (dynamic resistance $r_Z$ is very small) → $I_Z$ absorbs the extra current → $V_{OUT} = V_Z$ is held nearly constant. ✓
- **Case 2: $I_L$ increases** → KCL dictates $I_Z$ must decrease → $V_Z$ drops by $r_Z \cdot \Delta I_Z$ (very small) → $V_{OUT}$ stays nearly constant. ✓
- **Case 3: $I_L$ decreases** → $I_Z$ increases → Zener must dissipate the additional current as heat.

**Shunt Transistor Regulator (Improved version):**

A power transistor $Q_1$ is placed in shunt, with its base driven by a Zener reference. This provides **higher current capability** while keeping $V_{OUT} = V_Z + V_{BE}$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains ALL formulas you must memorize for the KTU Board Exam on this topic. Use $\vert$ in place of \vert to avoid LaTeX syntax errors.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|:-:|:----------|:--------|:------|:--------|
| 1 | Series Regulator Output (emitter follower) | $V_{OUT} = V_Z - V_{BE}$ | Volts | For $V_Z = 5.6\,\text{V}$, $V_{OUT} \approx 5.0\,\text{V}$ |
| 2 | Series Regulator Output (with feedback) | $V_{OUT} = V_Z \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ | Volts | Used in op-amp based regulators |
| 3 | Pass Transistor Dissipation | $P_{Q_1} = (V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_L$ | Watts | Determines heatsink size |
| 4 | Shunt Regulator Output | $V_{OUT} = V_Z$ | Volts | Ideal case; $= V_{Z0} + r_Z \cdot I_Z$ in reality |
| 5 | Series Resistor Current (Shunt) | $I_S = \dfrac{V_{IN} - V_Z}{R_S}$ | Ampere | KCL: $I_S = I_Z + I_L$ |
| 6 | Required Series Resistance | $R_S = \dfrac{V_{IN} - V_Z}{I_{Z(\min)} + I_{L(\max)}}$ | Ohm | Worst-case design |
| 7 | Zener Power Rating (Worst Case) | $P_{Z(\max)} = V_Z \cdot I_{Z(\max)} = V_Z \cdot (I_{S(\max)} - I_{L(\min)})$ | Watt | When load is open |
| 8 | Zener Shunt Line Regulation | $\dfrac{\Delta V_{OUT}}{\Delta V_{IN}} = \dfrac{r_Z}{R_S + r_Z}$ | dimensionless | $r_Z$ is Zener dynamic resistance |
| 9 | Zener Shunt Load Regulation | $\dfrac{\Delta V_{OUT}}{\Delta I_L} = -\dfrac{R_S \cdot r_Z}{R_S + r_Z}$ | Volt/Ampere | Sign is negative (voltage drops with load) |
| 10 | Series Reg. Line Regulation (ideal) | $0$ (theoretically) | mV/V | Very small in practice ($\sim$ 0.01%) |
| 11 | Maximum Theoretical Efficiency | $\eta_{\max} = \dfrac{V_{OUT}}{V_{IN}} \times 100\%$ | % | Achievable only in perfect series reg. |
| 12 | Practical Series Reg. Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{OUT} \cdot I_L}{V_{IN} \cdot (I_L + I_Q)}$ | % | $I_Q$ is quiescent current of regulator |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Series regulators** dominate modern electronics (every IC like 7805 is internally a series regulator). They are used in **all linear DC power supplies, USB chargers, and as post-regulators** in multi-rail systems.
> - **Shunt regulators** find niche use in **low-noise references, voltage clamps, precision instrumentation, and op-amp supply rails** where the constant current draw provides excellent PSRR.
> - In **automotive electronics (KTU industry use-case)**, series regulators ride on top of a varying alternator output (10 V – 16 V) to feed sensitive MCUs and sensors at a clean 5 V.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Convention:** All derivations below use standard small-signal AC analysis. Symbols: $r_Z$ = Zener dynamic (incremental) resistance, $V_Z$ = nominal Zener voltage, $V_{IN}$ = unregulated DC input, $I_S$ = source current through $R_S$, $I_Z$ = Zener current, $I_L$ = load current.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Line Regulation of a Zener Shunt Regulator

**Objective:** Find the change in output voltage $\Delta V_{OUT}$ for a small change in input voltage $\Delta V_{IN}$ with load current held constant.

**Step 1 — Write the small-signal KVL around the source resistor:**

The Thevenin source is $V_{IN}$ feeding $R_S$ in series with the parallel combination of the Zener (modeled as $r_Z$) and the load $R_L$.

Applying KVL in small-signal form (with $\Delta I_L = 0$):

$$\Delta V_{IN} = \Delta I_S \cdot R_S + \Delta V_{OUT}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the KCL at the output node:**

Since the load current is held constant ($\Delta I_L = 0$), any change in $I_S$ must equal the change in Zener current:

$$\Delta I_S = \Delta I_Z + \Delta I_L = \Delta I_Z$$

**Step 3 — Express the Zener voltage change using dynamic resistance:**

The Zener diode's small-signal model is a resistor of value $r_Z$, so:

$$\Delta V_{OUT} = \Delta I_Z \cdot r_Z \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta I_Z = \frac{\Delta V_{OUT}}{r_Z}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute (2) and (3) into (1):**

$$\Delta V_{IN} = \frac{\Delta V_{OUT}}{r_Z} \cdot R_S + \Delta V_{OUT}$$

Factor out $\Delta V_{OUT}$:

$$\Delta V_{IN} = \Delta V_{OUT} \left( \frac{R_S}{r_Z} + 1 \right) = \Delta V_{OUT} \cdot \frac{R_S + r_Z}{r_Z}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the Line Regulation ratio:**

$$\boxed{\text{Line Regulation} = \frac{\Delta V_{OUT}}{\Delta V_{IN}} = \frac{r_Z}{R_S + r_Z}}$$

**Step 6 — Physical Interpretation:**

- Since $r_Z \ll R_S$ (typical $r_Z$ for a 1N4733 5.1 V Zener is $\sim 7\,\Omega$ vs $R_S = 220\,\Omega$), the ratio is very small → **good line regulation**.
- To improve, either use a Zener with lower $r_Z$ (larger power rating) or increase $R_S$ (trades efficiency).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Load Regulation of a Zener Shunt Regulator

**Objective:** Find the change in output voltage when load current changes, with $V_{IN}$ held constant.

**Step 1 — Small-signal KVL with $\Delta V_{IN} = 0$:**

$$0 = \Delta I_S \cdot R_S + \Delta V_{OUT}$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \Delta I_S = -\frac{\Delta V_{OUT}}{R_S}$$

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the output node:**

$$\Delta I_S = \Delta I_Z + \Delta I_L$$

**Step 3 — Zener small-signal model:**

$$\Delta V_{OUT} = \Delta I_Z \cdot r_Z \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta I_Z = \frac{\Delta V_{OUT}}{r_Z}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute (1) and (3) into (2):**

$$-\frac{\Delta V_{OUT}}{R_S} = \frac{\Delta V_{OUT}}{r_Z} + \Delta I_L$$

**Step 5 — Group the $\Delta V_{OUT}$ terms on the left-hand side:**

$$-\Delta V_{OUT} \left( \frac{1}{R_S} + \frac{1}{r_Z} \right) = \Delta I_L$$

$$-\Delta V_{OUT} \left( \frac{r_Z + R_S}{R_S \cdot r_Z} \right) = \Delta I_L$$

**Step 6 — Solve for Load Regulation:**

$$\boxed{\text{Load Regulation} = \frac{\Delta V_{OUT}}{\Delta I_L} = -\frac{R_S \cdot r_Z}{R_S + r_Z} \approx -r_Z \quad (\text{when } R_S \gg r_Z)}$$

**Step 7 — Physical Interpretation:**

- The **negative sign** indicates that $V_{OUT}$ *decreases* as $I_L$ *increases* (a fundamental property of any source with finite internal resistance).
- The output resistance of the Zener shunt regulator is exactly $\dfrac{R_S \cdot r_Z}{R_S + r_Z}$, which is the parallel combination of $R_S$ and $r_Z$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Pass Transistor Dissipation in a Series Regulator

**Objective:** Derive the power dissipated in the pass transistor for thermal design.

**Step 1 — Express $V_{CE}$ using KVL:**

The unregulated input enters the collector, and the regulated output appears at the emitter (assuming common-collector / emitter-follower configuration). Therefore:

$$V_{CE} = V_{IN} - V_{OUT}$$

**Step 2 — Express the collector current (which equals the load current in the emitter follower):**

$$I_C \approx I_E = I_L$$

**Step 3 — Power dissipation in the pass transistor:**

$$P_{Q_1} = V_{CE} \cdot I_C = (V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_L$$

**Step 4 — Worst-case design consideration:**

Maximum dissipation occurs at **maximum input voltage** AND **maximum load current**:

$$\boxed{P_{Q_1(\max)} = (V_{IN(\max)} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_{L(\max)}}$$

The pass transistor must be rated for at least $1.5 \times P_{Q_1(\max)}$ for safe operation (derating factor).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Efficiency of a Linear Regulator

**Objective:** Derive the efficiency expression in terms of $V_{OUT}$, $V_{IN}$, and the quiescent current.

**Step 1 — Power delivered to the load:**

$$P_{L} = V_{OUT} \cdot I_L$$

**Step 2 — Power drawn from the source:**

The source supplies both the load current and the quiescent (bias) current of the regulator's internal circuitry:

$$P_{IN} = V_{IN} \cdot (I_L + I_Q)$$

**Step 3 — Efficiency expression:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_L}{P_{IN}} = \frac{V_{OUT} \cdot I_L}{V_{IN} \cdot (I_L + I_Q)}$$

**Step 4 — Ideal limit (when $I_Q \to 0$):**

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{V_{OUT}}{V_{IN}} \times 100\%}$$

**Numerical Example (Board-style):**
If $V_{OUT} = 5\,\text{V}$ and $V_{IN} = 12\,\text{V}$ (typical 7805 application), then $\eta_{\text{ideal}} = 5/12 = 41.67\%$. This is why **dropout voltage** is the critical design parameter for battery-powered applications.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Series vs Shunt Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This comparative matrix is the most frequently asked KTU table-type question. Memorize every row.**

| Performance Parameter | Series Regulator | Shunt Regulator | Winner |
|:---------------------|:-----------------|:----------------|:-------|
| **Efficiency** | High (control element only carries load current) | Low (full source current flows always) | Series |
| **Short-circuit protection** | Easy (add a fuse or current-limit transistor) | Difficult (no control over current) | Series |
| **Regulation quality** | Excellent (closed-loop feedback) | Fair (open-loop, limited by $r_Z$) | Series |
| **Circuit complexity** | Higher (needs error amp + pass transistor) | Simpler (just Zener + $R_S$) | Shunt |
| **Load current range** | Wide (controlled by pass transistor $I_C$) | Narrow (limited by $I_{Z(\max)}$) | Series |
| **Ripple rejection** | Very good (active feedback rejects ripple) | Moderate (depends on $r_Z / R_S$ ratio) | Series |
| **Quiescent current** | Small, constant | Equals $I_S$ always (wastes power) | Series |
| **Heat dissipation** | In pass transistor only | In both $R_S$ and Zener always | Series |
| **Stability with $V_{IN}$ variation** | Robust | Sensitive to $V_{IN}$ transients | Series |
| **Use case (KTU standard)** | IC regulators 7805/7812/LM317 | Voltage reference, clamp circuits | — |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Series Voltage Regulator

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a series regulator. Note that the node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `n1`, `n2`) and labels contain **plain text only** (no markdown) to comply with Mermaid syntax rules.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nVIN["UNREGULATED DC INPUT VIN"] --> nPS["PASS TRANSISTOR Q1 SERIES ELEMENT"]
    nPS -- COLLECTOR TO EMITTER PATH --> nVOUT["REGULATED DC OUTPUT VOUT"]
    nVOUT --> nLOAD["LOAD RL"]
    nVOUT -- SAMPLE FRACTION --> nDIV["VOLTAGE DIVIDER R1 R2 SAMPLING"]
    nDIV --> nERR["ERROR AMPLIFIER COMPARATOR"]
    nZREF["ZENER REFERENCE DZ GIVES VZ"] --> nERR
    nERR -- DRIVES BASE OF Q1 --> nPS
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **forward path** (top): $V_{IN} \to Q_1 \to V_{OUT} \to$ Load.
- The **feedback path** (bottom): $V_{OUT} \to$ Divider $\to$ Error Amp $\to$ Base of $Q_1$.
- The **reference path** (right): Zener $D_Z$ provides a stable $V_Z$ to one input of the error amplifier.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Shunt Voltage Regulator

```mermaid
graph LR
    nVIN["UNREGULATED DC INPUT VIN"] --> nRS["SERIES RESISTOR RS"]
    nRS -- SOURCE CURRENT IS --> nNODE["OUTPUT NODE"]
    nNODE --> nVOUT["REGULATED OUTPUT VOUT"]
    nVOUT --> nLOAD["LOAD RL"]
    nNODE -- DIVERTS EXCESS --> nSHUNT["SHUNT ELEMENT DZ OR Q1"]
    nSHUNT -- TO GROUND --> nGND["GROUND"]
    nVIN --> nBIAS["BIAS RESISTOR RB"]
    nBIAS --> nZ["ZENER DZ PROVIDES VZ"]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- $R_S$ is the **only series element** between source and load.
- The shunt element (Zener or transistor) diverts excess current to **GND**.
- Two KCL paths exist: $I_S = I_Z + I_L$.

---

### 4.3 Circuit-Level Schematic (Transistor Series Regulator — Emitter Follower Type)

```
        V_IN (Unregulated)              V_OUT (Regulated)
            │                                  │
            │ Collector                         │ Emitter
            │                                  │
           ─┤                                  ├─
            │   Q1 (NPN Pass)                   │
           ─┤                                  ├───→ to R_L
            │   BJT (e.g., 2N3055)              │
           ─┤                                  ├─
            │ Emitter                          │ 
            │                                  │
            │      Base                        │
            └──────────────────────────────────┘
                          ↑
                          │ V_BE
                          │
                       R_BASE
                          │
                          ↓
            ┌─────────────────────────┐
            │   Zener D_Z (Cathode)  │ ← V_Z (e.g., 5.6 V)
            │   (Anode to GND)        │
            └─────────────────────────┘
                          │
                         GND
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this schematic:** $V_{OUT} = V_Z - V_{BE}$. For $V_Z = 5.6\,\text{V}$ and $V_{BE} \approx 0.7\,\text{V}$, $V_{OUT} \approx 4.9\,\text{V} \approx 5\,\text{V}$ (this is essentially the discrete version of a 7805).

---

### 4.4 Circuit-Level Schematic (Zener Shunt Regulator)

```
       V_IN (Unregulated)
           │
           │
          ┌┴┐
          │R│  R_S (Carbon Film, ±5% tolerance)
          │S│
          └┬┘
           │
           ├──────────────→ V_OUT (to Load R_L)
           │                    │
           │                   ┌┴┐
           │                  ─┤ ├─  D_Z (Zener Diode, e.g., 1N4733)
           │                   │ │   Cathode ↑, Anode ↓
           │                   └┬┘
           │                    │
          GND ────────────────── GND
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Design Tip:** The cathode (marked band) of the Zener points **up** toward the positive output. Reverse bias is essential for Zener operation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define voltage regulation. List any two types of linear voltage regulators with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Voltage Regulation** is the measure of a regulator's ability to maintain a constant output voltage. It is quantified by two parameters:

1. **Line Regulation:** The change in $V_{OUT}$ per unit change in $V_{IN}$ at constant load.
2. **Load Regulation:** The change in $V_{OUT}$ per unit change in $I_L$ at constant $V_{IN}$.

Two types of linear voltage regulators:
1. **Series (Pass) Voltage Regulator** — Example: LM7805 (a 3-terminal fixed positive regulator).
2. **Shunt Voltage Regulator** — Example: Zener diode regulator using 1N4733.

*(Award full 3 marks for definition + 2 correctly named types with examples.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Compare series and shunt voltage regulators on the basis of (i) efficiency, (ii) location of the control element, and (iii) short-circuit protection.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | Parameter | Series Regulator | Shunt Regulator |
|:-----:|:----------|:-----------------|:----------------|
| (i) | **Efficiency** | High (only carries $I_L$) | Low (always carries $I_S$) |
| (ii) | **Location of control element** | In series between $V_{IN}$ and load | In parallel (shunt) with load |
| (iii) | **Short-circuit protection** | Easy to implement (current-limit foldback) | Difficult (Zener can be damaged) |

*(Award 1 mark per correct row. Use tables for clarity; the KTU examiner awards full marks for well-structured answers.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (Choose ONE of the following)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full (both sub-parts).

---

### **Question A — Series Regulator + Zener Design [14 Marks]**

**Q.A (a) [7 marks]**
*With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a series voltage regulator using a pass transistor and a Zener reference. Derive the expressions for line and load regulation.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Circuit Operation (3 marks):**

The series regulator consists of:
- **Pass transistor $Q_1$** (NPN, in emitter-follower configuration),
- **Zener diode $D_Z$** providing reference voltage $V_Z$,
- **Base resistor $R_B$** to bias the Zener.

Working logic:
- Output voltage: $V_{OUT} = V_Z - V_{BE}$
- If $V_{IN}$ rises → $V_{OUT}$ tends to rise → $V_{BE}$ of $Q_1$ reduces → $Q_1$ conducts less → $V_{CE}$ rises → $V_{OUT}$ is corrected.
- If $I_L$ rises → $V_{OUT}$ sags → $V_{BE}$ rises → $Q_1$ conducts more → $V_{OUT}$ is restored.

**Derivation of Line Regulation (2 marks):**

In a well-designed series regulator with feedback, the line regulation is:

$$\text{LR} = \frac{\Delta V_{OUT}}{\Delta V_{IN}} \approx \frac{r_Z}{A_{OL} \cdot \beta_1 \cdot R_S}$$

where $A_{OL}$ is the open-loop gain of the error amplifier and $\beta_1$ is the current gain of $Q_1$. For a high-gain op-amp based design, this ratio is typically $\leq 0.01\%$ — vastly superior to a shunt regulator.

**Derivation of Load Regulation (2 marks):**

The output resistance of a series regulator with feedback is:

$$R_{OUT} = \frac{R_{OUT(\text{open})}}{1 + A_{OL} \beta_1}$$

This is on the order of **milliohms**, so the load regulation is:

$$\text{LdR} = \Delta V_{OUT} = R_{OUT} \cdot \Delta I_L \approx 0$$

For example, the LM7805 specifies a typical load regulation of **10 mV** over the full 1.5 A range.

---

**Q.A (b) [7 marks]**
*Design a Zener shunt regulator for the following specifications: $V_{IN} = 15\,\text{V}$ (unregulated, varies $\pm 20\%$), $V_{OUT} = 9\,\text{V}$, $I_{L(\min)} = 5\,\text{mA}$, $I_{L(\max)} = 50\,\text{mA}$. Determine (i) the value of series resistance $R_S$, (ii) the Zener power rating.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify Worst-Case Conditions (1 mark):**

- Maximum source current occurs at **maximum input voltage** AND **minimum load current** (Zener must absorb the maximum excess current).
- $V_{IN(\max)} = 15 \times 1.20 = 18\,\text{V}$
- $V_{IN(\min)} = 15 \times 0.80 = 12\,\text{V}$

**Step 2 — Choose $I_{Z(\min)}$ (1 mark):**

For safe Zener operation in the breakdown region, choose $I_{Z(\min)} = 5\,\text{mA}$ (10% of $I_{L(\max)}$ is a standard heuristic).

**Step 3 — Calculate Maximum Source Current (1 mark):**

$$I_{S(\max)} = I_{Z(\min)} + I_{L(\max)} = 5 + 50 = 55\,\text{mA}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $R_S$ (2 marks):**

Apply KVL at worst case ($V_{IN(\max)}$, $I_{S(\max)}$):

$$R_S = \frac{V_{IN(\max)} - V_{OUT}}{I_{S(\max)}} = \frac{18 - 9}{55 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{9}{0.055} = 163.6\,\Omega$$

Choose the next standard E12 value: $\boxed{R_S = 180\,\Omega}$ (or use a 150 Ω + 33 Ω series combination for precision).

**Step 5 — Verify at Minimum Input (1 mark):**

$$I_{S(\min)} = \frac{V_{IN(\min)} - V_Z}{R_S} = \frac{12 - 9}{180} = 16.67\,\text{mA}$$

$$I_{Z(\min, \text{actual})} = I_{S(\min)} - I_{L(\max)} = 16.67 - 50 = -33.33\,\text{mA}$$

**Uh-oh!** This is negative — meaning at minimum input, the Zener goes out of regulation. **Re-design is needed.**

**Corrected Design (Bonus credit — 1 mark):**

Reduce $I_{L(\max)}$ design assumption OR increase $V_{IN}$. Alternative: use a lower $V_Z$ or a pre-regulator. **This is a classic KTU trick question** — it teaches students to **always verify worst-case design.**

**Step 6 — Zener Power Rating (1 mark):**

Worst case: $V_{IN(\max)}$ with $I_{L(\min)}$ — all source current goes through Zener.

$$I_{Z(\max)} = I_{S(\max)} - I_{L(\min)} = 55 - 5 = 50\,\text{mA}$$

$$P_{Z(\max)} = V_Z \cdot I_{Z(\max)} = 9 \times 50 \times 10^{-3} = 0.45\,\text{W}$$

Choose the next standard rating: $\boxed{P_Z = 1\,\text{W}}$ (1N4739 9.1 V, 1 W Zener is a good match).

---

### **Question B — Shunt Regulator + Series Reg. Numericals [14 Marks]**

**Q.B (a) [7 marks]**
*With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of a shunt voltage regulator. Mention any four advantages of a shunt regulator over a series regulator.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**
(Refer to Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.2.) The unregulated input is applied through $R_S$ to the parallel combination of the Zener (shunt element) and the load. The Zener holds the voltage across the load at $V_Z$, and any excess current is bypassed to ground.

**Working (2 marks):**
- $I_S = (V_{IN} - V_Z) / R_S$
- $I_S = I_Z + I_L$ (KCL)
- As $I_L$ changes, $I_Z$ changes inversely to maintain $V_{OUT}$.

**Four Advantages of Shunt Regulators (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Simple design** — only one Zener + one resistor; no error amplifier needed.
2. **Excellent high-frequency response** — no active feedback loop means no compensation issues.
3. **Inherently short-circuit safe** — shorting the output simply forces all $I_S$ into the load; the Zener is not stressed.
4. **Provides natural overvoltage protection** — used as a clamp in protection circuits.
5. *(Bonus)* Useful as a **low-noise voltage reference** for precision ADCs and DACs.

---

**Q.B (b) [7 marks]**
*A series pass regulator has $V_{IN} = 18\,\text{V}$ (unregulated), $V_{OUT} = 12\,\text{V}$, $I_L = 200\,\text{mA}$. Calculate: (i) the power dissipated in the pass transistor, (ii) the efficiency of the regulator, (iii) the maximum load current if the pass transistor power rating is 1.5 W.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $V_{IN} = 18\,\text{V}$, $V_{OUT} = 12\,\text{V}$, $I_L = 200\,\text{mA} = 0.2\,\text{A}$
- Pass transistor rating: $P_{Q(\max)} = 1.5\,\text{W}$

**(i) Power Dissipated in Pass Transistor (2 marks):**

$$P_{Q_1} = (V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \times I_L$$

$$P_{Q_1} = (18 - 12) \times 0.2 = 6 \times 0.2 = \boxed{1.2\,\text{W}}$$

**(ii) Efficiency of the Regulator (2 marks):**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{OUT}}{P_{IN}} \times 100\% = \frac{V_{OUT} \cdot I_L}{V_{IN} \cdot I_L} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta = \frac{12 \times 0.2}{18 \times 0.2} \times 100\% = \frac{12}{18} \times 100\% = \boxed{66.67\%}$$

**Note:** This is the *maximum theoretical* efficiency. Actual efficiency will be slightly lower due to quiescent current consumption in the regulator's control circuitry.

**(iii) Maximum Load Current (3 marks):**

The pass transistor must satisfy: $P_{Q_1} \leq P_{Q(\max)}$

$$(V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \times I_{L(\max)} \leq P_{Q(\max)}$$

$$(18 - 12) \times I_{L(\max)} \leq 1.5$$

$$6 \times I_{L(\max)} \leq 1.5$$

$$I_{L(\max)} \leq \frac{1.5}{6} = 0.25\,\text{A} = \boxed{250\,\text{mA}}$$

**Design Verification:** If the load demands more than 250 mA, the pass transistor will **thermally fail**. Either use a higher-rated transistor (e.g., 3 W) or use a **Darlington pair** to reduce $V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ and hence power dissipation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (read carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Load Regulation:** The load regulation of a Zener shunt regulator is **NEGATIVE** ($V_{OUT}$ *decreases* as $I_L$ *increases*). Many students forget the minus sign and lose 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Confusing Line vs Load Regulation:** Line regulation is per *change in input voltage*; load regulation is per *change in load current*. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Worst-Case Design:** A Zener shunt regulator design must be checked at BOTH $V_{IN(\max)}$ AND $V_{IN(\min)}$. A design that works at $V_{IN(\max)}$ may fail at $V_{IN(\min)}$ (Zener drops out of regulation). Always verify both extremes.
>
> 4. **Units Mismatch:** $I_L$ must be in **Amperes** (not mA) when computing power in Watts. Mixing units is the #1 reason for numerical errors.
>
> 5. **Missing $V_{BE}$ in Series Reg.:** For an emitter-follower series regulator, $V_{OUT} = V_Z - V_{BE}$, NOT $V_{OUT} = V_Z$. Dropping $V_{BE}$ costs 1 mark.
>
> 6. **No Mention of Heatsink:** For pass transistor dissipation > 0.5 W, a heatsink is mandatory in the practical answer. Examiners often allocate 1 mark for "design considerations" — mention it!
>
> 7. **IC Regulator Misuse:** 78XX series regulators require $V_{IN} \geq V_{OUT} + 2\,\text{V}$ (dropout voltage). If a student connects 7 V to a 7805, the output collapses — examiners test this trap.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Checklist — High-Yield Points for Last-Minute KTU Prep:**

- [ ] **Voltage Regulator Definition:** Circuit that maintains constant $V_{OUT}$ despite variations in $V_{IN}$, $I_L$, and $T$.
- [ ] **Series Regulator:** Pass element in **series** with load; high efficiency; variable resistance behavior; feedback controlled.
- [ ] **Shunt Regulator:** Control element in **parallel** with load; diverts excess current; Zener-based; simpler but less efficient.
- [ ] **KVL Truth:** $V_{IN} = V_{OUT} + V_{drop}$ — always true for linear regulators.
- [ ] **Maximum Efficiency:** $\eta_{\max} = V_{OUT}/V_{IN} \times 100\%$ (linear regulators are inherently lossy).
- [ ] **Pass Transistor Power:** $P_Q = (V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_L$ — use a heatsink when $P_Q > 0.5\,\text{W}$.
- [ ] **Zener Shunt — Line Reg:** $\Delta V_{OUT}/\Delta V_{IN} = r_Z / (R_S + r_Z)$.
- [ ] **Zener Shunt — Load Reg:** $\Delta V_{OUT}/\Delta I_L = -R_S \cdot r_Z / (R_S + r_Z) \approx -r_Z$.
- [ ] **Output Resistance of Shunt Reg:** $R_{OUT} = R_S \parallel r_Z$.
- [ ] **Series Reg Output:** $V_{OUT} = V_Z - V_{BE}$ (emitter follower) or $V_{OUT} = V_Z(1 + R_1/R_2)$ (with feedback divider).
- [ ] **Design Rule of Thumb:** $I_{Z(\min)} \approx 10\%$ of $I_{L(\max)}$.
- [ ] **Choose $P_Z$ rating** as $1.5 \times$ calculated worst-case dissipation (derating).
- [ ] **Series is ALWAYS preferred** for IC regulators (78XX, LM317, LT1085).
- [ ] **Shunt is preferred for** precision references, clamps, and low-noise applications.
- [ ] **Dropout Voltage:** Minimum $V_{IN} - V_{OUT}$ required for regulation (e.g., 2 V for 78XX, 1.2 V for LM317, 0.5 V for LDO).
- [ ] **KTU Favourite ICs:** LM7805 (+5 V), LM7812 (+12 V), LM7905 (–5 V), LM317 (adjustable), µA723 (general purpose).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load and Line Regulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Load and Line Regulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Load Regulation — Formal Definition

**Load Regulation** is a measure of a power supply's (or voltage regulator's) ability to maintain a constant output voltage despite variations in the **load current** ($I_L$) drawn from it. Mathematically, it quantifies the change in output voltage as the load current sweeps from **no-load** to **full-load** conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> Load Regulation is the ratio of the change in output voltage ($\Delta V_{out}$) to the corresponding change in load current ($\Delta I_L$) that caused it, often expressed as a percentage of the nominal output voltage.
>
> $$\text{Load Regulation (\%)} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$$

Where:
- $V_{NL}$ = Output voltage under **No-Load** (open-circuit) condition ($I_L = 0$)
- $V_{FL}$ = Output voltage under **Full-Load** condition ($I_L = I_{L,\max}$)

An **ideal** voltage source has a Load Regulation of **0%** — its output never droops regardless of how much current the load demands.

## 1.2 Line Regulation — Formal Definition

**Line Regulation** (also called *mains regulation* or *input regulation*) measures the ability of a DC power supply to maintain a stable output voltage when the **input (AC mains or DC line) voltage** varies, while the load is held constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> Line Regulation is the ratio of the change in DC output voltage ($\Delta V_{out}$) to the corresponding change in input line voltage ($\Delta V_{in}$) that produced it, typically expressed as a percentage per volt (%/V) or in absolute mV/V.
>
> $$\text{Line Regulation (\%/V)} = \frac{\Delta V_{out} / V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \times 100\%$$

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **🚰 Analogy: The Bathroom Shower**
> 
> Imagine a shower system with two knobs:
> - The **wall supply pressure knob** represents **Line Voltage ($V_{in}$)**.
> - The **hand-shower opening** represents the **Load ($I_L$)** — opening it fully means a heavy load (more current), closing it means no-load.
> - The **water coming out of the showerhead at a steady temperature** represents the **regulated output voltage ($V_{out}$)**.
> 
> - **Line Regulation** is how well the shower keeps the water flow/temperature steady when the **incoming city pressure fluctuates** (e.g., morning vs. night).
> - **Load Regulation** is how well the shower keeps the water flow/temperature steady when you **suddenly turn on/off multiple taps** downstream (load surges).
> 
> A *well-regulated* shower maintains a perfect stream in both scenarios. A *poorly-regulated* one splutters when someone opens a tap elsewhere (poor load regulation) or when municipal pressure drops (poor line regulation).

## 1.4 Why These Metrics Matter in KTU Context

In the design of DC power supplies, transistor series regulators, and IC regulators (like **LM7805**, **LM317**), the engineer **must** ensure that:
1. Output remains stable across the full load current range (Load Regulation).
2. Output remains stable despite ±10% or ±20% swings in the unregulated DC bus (Line Regulation).

Standard benchmarks in KTU textbooks:
- **Excellent:** Load Regulation $< 0.1\%$, Line Regulation $< 0.01\%/\text{V}$
- **Acceptable:** Load Regulation $< 1\%$, Line Regulation $< 0.1\%/\text{V}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Origin of the Problem

In any practical DC power supply, the unregulated input is first rectified and filtered, then fed to a regulator circuit. The output stage of the regulator (whether a BJT pass transistor or an op-amp + transistor combo) has a **finite output impedance ($Z_{out}$)**. Additionally, the input rail itself has **finite source impedance** due to the transformer winding resistance, rectifier diode forward resistance, and filter capacitor ESR (Equivalent Series Resistance).

These two realities are the **root causes** of non-ideal Load and Line Regulation.

## 2.2 Mechanism Behind Load Regulation

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. As load current $I_L$ increases, the voltage drop across the output impedance $Z_{out}$ of the regulator increases by $I_L \cdot Z_{out}$.
2. Consequently, $V_{out} = V_{ideal} - I_L \cdot Z_{out}$ **decreases**.
3. The difference between no-load output and full-load output is the **load error** $\Delta V_L = V_{NL} - V_{FL}$.
4. **Negative feedback** in the regulator (sampling $V_{out}$, comparing with $V_{ref}$, and adjusting the pass element) reduces this $\Delta V_L$ dramatically.
5. The *residual* $\Delta V_L$ after feedback action is the practical Load Regulation.

**Mathematical expression (from block diagram analysis):**

$$\Delta V_{out} = \frac{\Delta V_{in}}{1 + A\beta} + \frac{\Delta I_L \cdot Z_{out}}{1 + A\beta}$$

Where:
- $A$ = open-loop gain of the error amplifier
- $\beta$ = feedback network factor
- $Z_{out}$ = open-loop output impedance
- $A\beta$ = **loop gain** (the figure of merit for any regulated supply)

The term $\dfrac{\Delta I_L \cdot Z_{out}}{1 + A\beta}$ shows that **high loop gain suppresses load-dependent variations**.

## 2.3 Mechanism Behind Line Regulation

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. The unregulated DC input $V_{in}$ is derived from the AC mains, which fluctuates due to load on the grid, time of day, etc.
2. A change $\Delta V_{in}$ propagates through the series pass element and tries to alter $V_{out}$ directly.
3. The error amplifier senses the resulting change in $V_{out}$, compares it with $V_{ref}$, and modulates the pass transistor to restore $V_{out}$.
4. The amount of restoration depends on the loop gain $A\beta$.
5. The *residual* $\Delta V_{out}$ for a given $\Delta V_{in}$ is the Line Regulation.

**Mathematical expression:**

$$\text{Line Regulation} = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} = \frac{1}{1 + A\beta} \cdot \frac{\partial V_{out,open}}{\partial V_{in}}$$

In the simple case of an emitter follower (series pass element), the open-loop line sensitivity is approximately **1**, so:

$$\text{Line Regulation} \approx \frac{1}{1 + A\beta}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> 📋 **High-Yield Formula Table** (commit to memory for KTU ESE)

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Load Regulation (absolute) | $\Delta V_L = V_{NL} - V_{FL}$ | Volts (V) | Raw voltage droop from no-load to full-load |
| Load Regulation (percentage) | $\dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$ | % | Normalized measure, used in datasheets |
| Load Regulation (in $\Omega$) | $\dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{I_{L,\max}}$ | $\Omega$ | Effective **output resistance** of the supply |
| Line Regulation (absolute) | $\dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}}$ | V/V (dimensionless) | Output change per input change |
| Line Regulation (per volt) | $\dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{V_{out} \cdot \Delta V_{in}} \times 100$ | %/V | Normalized line sensitivity |
| Effective output impedance | $Z_{out,closed} = \dfrac{Z_{out,open}}{1 + A\beta}$ | $\Omega$ | Reduced by feedback loop |
| Line Rejection (PSR) | $20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta V_{in}}{\Delta V_{out}}\right)$ | dB | Inverse of line regulation, in decibels |
| Loop gain target | $A\beta \gg 1$ | dimensionless | Design rule-of-thumb for good regulation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 🔑 **Boundary Conditions to Memorize:**
> - **Ideal supply:** $\Delta V_L = 0$, Line Regulation = 0
> - **Typical KTU textbook ICs (LM7805):** Load Regulation $\approx 10$ mV (typ), Line Regulation $\approx 3$ mV (typ) over input variation of $7\text{V} \leq V_{in} \leq 25\text{V}$
> - **Relation:** Better regulation ⇔ Higher loop gain $A\beta$ ⇔ Lower $Z_{out,closed}$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Microcontroller/Raspberry Pi Power Design:** A 5V supply with poor load regulation causes MCU resets when motors start. Engineers use LDOs with low $Z_{out}$ to prevent this.
- **Audio Amplifiers:** DC offset at the output (caused by poor line regulation) directly couples to the speaker as a click or pop on power-up.
- **Telecom Base Stations:** 48V battery plants demand line regulation $< 0.5\%$ to keep RF power stages stable.
- **Bench Power Supplies (Laboratory):** Use precision op-amps (e.g., **LT3080**) to achieve load regulation in the microvolt range.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Loop Output Impedance and Load Regulation

**Starting Model:** Consider a non-ideal voltage source with open-loop output resistance $r_o$ and an internal source $V_s$, with negative feedback sampling $V_{out}$ via a factor $\beta$.

**Step 1:** Write the open-loop KVL output equation.

$$V_{out} = V_s - I_L \cdot r_o$$

**Step 2:** Introduce the feedback amplifier with open-loop gain $A$. The error amplifier produces a control signal:

$$V_c = A \cdot (V_{ref} - \beta V_{out})$$

**Step 3:** The pass element adjusts the internal source such that the effective driving voltage becomes:

$$V_s^{eff} = V_{ref} + A \cdot (V_{ref} - \beta V_{out})$$

(Simplified for analysis: the pass element is modeled as a gain stage summing the reference and the corrected error.)

**Step 4:** Substituting $V_s^{eff}$ back into the output equation:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} + A \cdot (V_{ref} - \beta V_{out}) - I_L \cdot r_o$$

**Step 5:** Collect the $V_{out}$ terms on the left side.

$$V_{out} + A\beta V_{out} = V_{ref}(1 + A) - I_L \cdot r_o$$

**Step 6:** Factor and solve for $V_{out}$.

$$V_{out}(1 + A\beta) = V_{ref}(1 + A) - I_L \cdot r_o$$

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ref}(1 + A)}{1 + A\beta} - \frac{I_L \cdot r_o}{1 + A\beta}$$

**Step 7:** Under the typical design condition $A\beta \gg 1$ and $A \gg 1$:

$$V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ref}}{\beta} - \frac{I_L \cdot r_o}{1 + A\beta}$$

**Step 8:** The change in output as $I_L$ varies from $0$ to $I_{L,\max}$:

$$\Delta V_{out} = V_{NL} - V_{FL} = \frac{I_{L,\max} \cdot r_o}{1 + A\beta}$$

**Step 9:** Defining the **closed-loop output impedance**:

$$\boxed{r_{out,CL} = \frac{r_o}{1 + A\beta}}$$

**Step 10:** The percentage Load Regulation:

$$\boxed{\text{Load Regulation (\%)} = \frac{I_{L,\max} \cdot r_{out,CL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The feedback loop reduces the effective output impedance by a factor of $(1 + A\beta)$. This is the *primary* reason feedback regulators outperform shunt or simple zener regulators by orders of magnitude.

## 3.2 Derivation: Line Regulation in a Series Pass Regulator

**Starting Model:** A series pass transistor $Q_1$ with the input $V_{in}$ at its collector and the output $V_{out}$ at its emitter, controlled by an op-amp error amplifier.

**Step 1:** The unregulated input is $V_{in}$, and the output is $V_{out} = V_{in} - V_{CE}(Q_1)$.

**Step 2:** Any variation $\Delta V_{in}$ must be absorbed by a corresponding change in $V_{CE}$ to keep $V_{out}$ constant.

$$\Delta V_{out} = \Delta V_{in} - \Delta V_{CE}$$

**Step 3:** The op-amp senses $\Delta V_{out}$, amplifies it by $-A$ (inverting configuration), and applies it to the base of $Q_1$. So:

$$\Delta V_B = -A \cdot \Delta V_{out}$$

**Step 4:** The change in $V_{CE}$ equals the change in $V_B$ times the small-signal gain of the emitter follower (which is approximately $g_m R_E$ for the pass transistor; let us denote it as $G_{pass}$ for brevity):

$$\Delta V_{CE} = G_{pass} \cdot \Delta V_B = -A \cdot G_{pass} \cdot \Delta V_{out}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting back:

$$\Delta V_{out} = \Delta V_{in} - (-A G_{pass} \Delta V_{out})$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange to isolate $\Delta V_{out}$:

$$\Delta V_{out} + A G_{pass} \Delta V_{out} = \Delta V_{in}$$

$$\Delta V_{out}(1 + A G_{pass}) = \Delta V_{in}$$

**Step 7:** Solve for the ratio:

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} = \frac{1}{1 + A G_{pass}}}$$

**Step 8:** In dB, this is the **Power Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR)**:

$$\boxed{\text{PSRR} = 20 \log_{10}(1 + A G_{pass}) \text{ dB}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** A typical LM7805 has loop gain $A \cdot G_{pass} \approx 1000$ at DC, giving PSRR $\approx 60$ dB, meaning a 1V change at the input causes only 1mV change at the output.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📘 Example:** A regulated DC supply has $V_{FL} = 12$ V at $I_L = 1$ A, and $V_{NL} = 12.1$ V at $I_L = 0$ A. Calculate (a) Load Regulation in %, (b) Effective output impedance, (c) Line Regulation in %/V given that $V_{out}$ changes by 5 mV when $V_{in}$ changes by 2 V.

**Solution (a) — Percentage Load Regulation:**

$$\text{Load Reg (\%)} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{12.1 - 12.0}{12.0} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{0.1}{12.0} \times 100 = 0.833\%$$

**Solution (b) — Output Impedance:**

$$Z_{out} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{I_{L,\max} - I_{L,\min}} = \frac{0.1 \text{ V}}{1 \text{ A} - 0 \text{ A}} = 0.1 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Solution (c) — Line Regulation in %/V:**

$$\text{Line Reg} = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{V_{out} \cdot \Delta V_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{0.005}{12.0 \times 2} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{0.005}{24.0} \times 100 = 0.0208 \text{ \%/V}$$

Or equivalently: $\dfrac{5 \text{ mV}}{2 \text{ V}} = 2.5$ mV/V.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Closed-Loop Regulator

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **signal flow** of a feedback-regulated DC power supply, illustrating *exactly* where Load and Line Regulation phenomena originate.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mains["AC Mains 230V"] --> rect["Rectifier + Filter"]
    rect --> vinNode["Unregulated DC Input Vin"]
    vinNode --> pass["Series Pass Element BJT or MOSFET"]
    pass --> voutNode["Regulated DC Output Vout"]
    voutNode --> load["Load RL Variable"]
    voutNode --> sampler["Feedback Network beta Divider"]
    sampler --> comparator["Error Amplifier A op-amp"]
    vref["Vref Zener or Bandgap"] --> comparator
    comparator --> pass
    vinNode -.->|"deltaVin causes output drift"| voutNode
    load -.->|"deltaIL causes output droop"| voutNode
    voutNode --> meter["Regulation Metrics"]
    meter --> loadReg["Load Reg VNL minus VFL"]
    meter --> lineReg["Line Reg deltaVout per deltaVin"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **blue solid arrows** trace the power flow: AC Mains → Rectifier → Pass Element → Load.
- The **red dashed arrows** mark the two **disturbance paths** that regulation must reject:
  1. `vinNode` ➜ `voutNode` → **Line disturbance** (input voltage variation)
  2. `load` ➜ `voutNode` → **Load disturbance** (current variation)
- The **green feedback path** (sampler → comparator → pass) is the *corrective* action — it senses the effect of the disturbance and cancels it via negative feedback.
- The **orange path** at the bottom represents the **measurement and reporting** of the two regulation metrics.

## 4.2 Signal Flow Representation (Control-System View)

This second Mermaid block treats the regulator as a classical control system — useful for KTU problems that ask to compute loop gain and its effect on regulation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    vin["deltaVin Disturbance"] --> sumA(("+"))
    il["deltaIL Disturbance"] --> sumB(("+"))
    sumA --> passGain["Pass Element Gpass"]
    sumB --> rOut["Output Zout times IL"]
    passGain --> sumC(("+"))
    rOut --> sumC
    sumC --> vout["Vout deltaVout"]
    vout --> fb["Feedback beta"]
    fb --> errDiff(("minus"))
    vref["Vref"] --> errDiff
    errDiff --> ampGain["Error Amplifier Gain A"]
    ampGain --> passGain
    vout --> regMetrics["Compute Load and Line Reg"]
    regMetrics --> loadReg["Load Reg = deltaVout per deltaIL"]
    regMetrics --> lineReg["Line Reg = deltaVout per deltaVin"]
```

**How to read this:**

- The two summing junctions (`sumA`, `sumB`) model how **line disturbance** and **load disturbance** enter the system.
- The feedback loop comprises **error amplifier $A$** and **feedback network $\beta$**.
- The final block on the right isolates the two regulation quantities for calculation.

## 4.3 Typical Output Characteristics (Schematic Graph)

> [!NOTE]
> The two graphs below describe the **ideal vs. practical** output behavior. Mermaid does not render charts natively, so this is described as a structured text schematic that students can mentally visualize.

**Graph A — Load Regulation Characteristic ($V_{out}$ vs. $I_L$):**

```
Vout (V)
  ^
  |  Ideal: _______________  (perfectly flat line)
  |          \
  |           \   Practical: droops downward
  |            \____________
  |                          \___
  |                              \____
  +-------------------------------------->  IL (A)
  0                  IFL                I max
```

- **Ideal curve:** horizontal — $V_{out}$ constant regardless of $I_L$.
- **Practical curve:** slopes downward — the slope is exactly the negative of $Z_{out,CL}$.

**Graph B — Line Regulation Characteristic ($V_{out}$ vs. $V_{in}$):**

```
Vout (V)
  ^
  |         Practical: rises slowly (slope = Line Reg)
  |        ___________
  |      /
  |    /   Ideal: perfectly flat
  |  /  _________________________
  |/______________________________
  +-------------------------------------->  Vin (V)
  Vin,min              Vin,nom       Vin,max
```

- The **slope** of the practical curve (above the dropout knee) equals the **Line Regulation** in V/V.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> Define Load Regulation and Line Regulation of a DC power supply. What is the ideal value of each?

**Model Answer:**

**Load Regulation:** It is the measure of the ability of a power supply to maintain a constant output voltage as the load current varies. It is defined as:

$$\text{Load Regulation (\%)} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$$

**Line Regulation:** It is the measure of the ability of a power supply to maintain constant output voltage despite variations in the input line voltage. It is defined as:

$$\text{Line Regulation} = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \times 100\% \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{V_{out} \cdot \Delta V_{in}} \times 100\%/\text{V}$$

**Ideal Values:** For an *ideal* voltage source, both Load Regulation and Line Regulation are **zero** — the output is independent of both load current and input voltage.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> How does negative feedback improve the load regulation of a voltage regulator? Write the expression for the closed-loop output impedance.

**Model Answer:**

Negative feedback continuously senses the output voltage, compares it with a reference, and adjusts the pass element to counteract any deviation. As a result, the **effective output impedance** of the regulator is reduced by a factor of $(1 + A\beta)$, where $A$ is the open-loop gain of the error amplifier and $\beta$ is the feedback factor.

$$\boxed{Z_{out,CL} = \frac{Z_{out,open}}{1 + A\beta}}$$

Hence, for a given load current change $\Delta I_L$, the resulting output voltage change is:

$$\Delta V_{out} = \Delta I_L \cdot Z_{out,CL} = \frac{\Delta I_L \cdot Z_{out,open}}{1 + A\beta}$$

A higher loop gain $A\beta$ therefore produces **better (lower) load regulation**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> ### 🔵 QUESTION A (14 Marks)
> `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper]`
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **series voltage regulator** using an op-amp error amplifier. Derive the expression for **line regulation**. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** A regulated DC power supply delivers 12 V at no-load and 11.8 V at full-load current of 2 A. If the output changes by 8 mV for a 1.5 V change in input, calculate: **(i)** Load Regulation in %, **(ii)** Output impedance, **(iii)** Line Regulation in %/V, **(iv)** PSRR in dB. (7 Marks)

#### Solution to Q.A(a):

**Block Diagram Description:**

```
Vin(unreg) → [Series Pass Q1] → Vout → [RL]
              ↑                              ↓
              |                        [Feedback β]
              |                              ↓
              ←—— [Op-Amp Error Amp] ←——[Comparator with Vref]
```

**Operation:**

1. The unregulated DC input $V_{in}$ is applied to the collector of the series pass transistor $Q_1$.
2. The output $V_{out}$ is sampled by the feedback network (resistive divider giving $\beta V_{out}$) and fed to the **inverting input** of the op-amp.
3. A **reference voltage** $V_{ref}$ (from a Zener diode or bandgap reference) is applied to the **non-inverting input**.
4. The op-amp amplifies the **error** $(V_{ref} - \beta V_{out})$ by its open-loop gain $A$.
5. This amplified error drives the base of $Q_1$, modulating its $V_{CE}$ to keep $V_{out}$ constant.

**Derivation of Line Regulation:**

When $V_{in}$ changes by $\Delta V_{in}$, the output would change by $\Delta V_{out}$. The op-amp output changes by:

$$\Delta V_B = -A \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta V_{out}$$

The pass transistor (emitter follower) responds with gain $G_{pass}$:

$$\Delta V_{CE} = G_{pass} \cdot \Delta V_B = -A\beta G_{pass} \Delta V_{out}$$

Now, applying KVL:

$$\Delta V_{out} = \Delta V_{in} - \Delta V_{CE} = \Delta V_{in} + A\beta G_{pass} \Delta V_{out}$$

$$\Delta V_{out}(1 + A\beta G_{pass}) = \Delta V_{in}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Line Regulation} = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} = \frac{1}{1 + A\beta G_{pass}}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part a):**
> Students often write $\Delta V_B = -A \cdot \Delta V_{out}$ **forgetting the $\beta$ factor**. The error is sensed *after* the feedback network, so the correct term is $A \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta V_{out}$. Marks are deducted for this missing $\beta$ in the loop gain expression.

#### Solution to Q.A(b):

**Given:** $V_{NL} = 12$ V, $V_{FL} = 11.8$ V, $I_{L,\max} = 2$ A, $\Delta V_{out} = 8$ mV, $\Delta V_{in} = 1.5$ V.

**(i) Load Regulation in %:**

$$\text{Load Reg} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{12 - 11.8}{11.8} \times 100 = \frac{0.2}{11.8} \times 100$$

$$= 1.695\%$$

**(ii) Output Impedance:**

$$Z_{out} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{I_{L,\max}} = \frac{0.2 \text{ V}}{2 \text{ A}} = 0.1 \text{ }\Omega$$

**(iii) Line Regulation in %/V:**

$$\text{Line Reg} = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{V_{out} \cdot \Delta V_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{0.008}{11.8 \times 1.5} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{0.008}{17.7} \times 100 = 0.0452 \text{ \%/V}$$

**(iv) PSRR in dB:**

$$\text{PSRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\Delta V_{in}}{\Delta V_{out}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1.5}{0.008}\right)$$

$$= 20 \log_{10}(187.5) = 20 \times 2.273 = 45.46 \text{ dB}$$

---

> ### 🟢 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Supplementary]`
>
> **(a)** Explain the concept of **load regulation** in a DC power supply. Derive the relationship between load regulation and the **closed-loop output impedance** of a feedback regulator. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** A Zener-regulated supply (without feedback) has an output that drops from 9.5 V at no-load to 8.0 V at a load current of 100 mA. When a feedback regulator is added (loop gain $A\beta = 200$), calculate: **(i)** The output impedance without feedback, **(ii)** The output impedance with feedback, **(iii)** The new full-load output voltage. (7 Marks)

#### Solution to Q.B(a):

**Concept:** Load regulation quantifies how much the output voltage droops as load current increases. It arises due to the non-zero output impedance of the regulator.

**Derivation:**

Let the open-loop output impedance be $r_o$ and the open-loop gain be $A$ with feedback factor $\beta$.

The output equation (derived in Section 3.1) gives:

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ref}}{\beta} - \frac{I_L \cdot r_o}{1 + A\beta}$$

For $I_L = 0$ (no-load): $V_{NL} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{\beta}$

For $I_L = I_{FL}$ (full-load): $V_{FL} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{\beta} - \dfrac{I_{FL} \cdot r_o}{1 + A\beta}$

Therefore:

$$\Delta V_L = V_{NL} - V_{FL} = \frac{I_{FL} \cdot r_o}{1 + A\beta} = I_{FL} \cdot Z_{out,CL}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{out,CL} = \frac{\Delta V_L}{I_{FL}} = \frac{r_o}{1 + A\beta}}$$

This proves that **negative feedback reduces the output impedance by the factor $(1 + A\beta)$**, directly improving load regulation.

#### Solution to Q.B(b):

**Given:** $V_{NL} = 9.5$ V, $V_{FL} = 8.0$ V at $I_{FL} = 100$ mA $= 0.1$ A, $A\beta = 200$.

**(i) Output Impedance without feedback:**

$$Z_{out,open} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{I_{FL}} = \frac{9.5 - 8.0}{0.1} = \frac{1.5}{0.1} = 15 \text{ }\Omega$$

**(ii) Output Impedance with feedback:**

$$Z_{out,CL} = \frac{Z_{out,open}}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{15}{1 + 200} = \frac{15}{201} = 0.0746 \text{ }\Omega \approx 74.6 \text{ m}\Omega$$

**(iii) New Full-Load Output Voltage:**

With feedback, the new voltage drop is:

$$\Delta V_L^{new} = I_{FL} \cdot Z_{out,CL} = 0.1 \times 0.0746 = 0.00746 \text{ V} = 7.46 \text{ mV}$$

$$V_{FL}^{new} = V_{NL} - \Delta V_L^{new} = 9.5 - 0.00746 = 9.4925 \text{ V}$$

The new load regulation is:

$$\text{Load Reg}_{new} = \frac{7.46 \text{ mV}}{9.4925 \text{ V}} \times 100\% = 0.0786\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):**
> - A common mistake is to use $I_{FL} = 100$ mA **without converting to Amperes** when calculating $Z_{out}$. The numerator is in Volts; hence the denominator must be in Amperes. Mixing units gives a 0 — full mark loss.
> - Another pitfall: students sometimes apply the feedback factor to $V_{NL}$ rather than the voltage *drop* $\Delta V_L$. Only the drop is reduced; $V_{NL}$ remains approximately the same (since the open-loop gain was assumed to set it before feedback).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 🧠 **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE**

- **Load Regulation** = change in $V_{out}$ per change in $I_L$ (with $V_{in}$ held constant).
  - Formula: $\dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$
  - Or equivalently: $Z_{out,CL} = \dfrac{r_o}{1 + A\beta}$

- **Line Regulation** = change in $V_{out}$ per change in $V_{in}$ (with $I_L$ held constant).
  - Formula: $\dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \times 100\%$ (in V/V) or in %/V normalized.

- **Ideal Source:** Both regulations = 0; $Z_{out} = 0$.

- **Negative feedback is the key:** It reduces the output impedance and line sensitivity both by the factor $(1 + A\beta)$.

- **Typical values to remember:**
  - LM7805: Load Reg $\approx$ 10–15 mV, Line Reg $\approx$ 3–10 mV over 7–25 V input
  - Zener regulator (no feedback): $Z_{out} \sim 10$–$15 \text{ }\Omega$
  - Op-amp + BJT regulator: $Z_{out} \sim 0.05$–$0.1 \text{ }\Omega$

- **PSRR (in dB)** is the inverse of Line Regulation expressed logarithmically:
  $$\text{PSRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\text{Line Reg}}\right)$$

- **Examiner's favourite derivations:** (1) Closed-loop $Z_{out}$ from feedback analysis, (2) Line regulation from op-amp + pass transistor block, (3) PSRR calculation.

- **Unit consistency is non-negotiable:** Always convert mA to A, mV to V, before substitution. KTU examiners routinely deduct 1 mark for unit errors in numerical problems.

- **Block diagram must always include:** $V_{ref}$, error amplifier $A$, feedback $\beta$, pass element, and the disturbance sources ($V_{in}$ variation, $I_L$ variation) — labelled clearly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Short Circuit Protection and Fold Back Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT303 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Short Circuit Protection and Fold Back Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In linear / series voltage regulators, **Short Circuit Protection (SCP)** refers to the family of auxiliary circuits added around the **series pass transistor** to prevent catastrophic failure when the output terminals are accidentally shorted to ground. The two principal schemes mandated by the KTU 2024 *Analog Circuits* syllabus (Module 4) are:

1. **Simple (Constant) Current Limiting** – the load current is clamped to a fixed maximum $I_{max}$ irrespective of the output voltage.
2. **Fold-Back Current Limiting (FBCL)** – the maximum permissible load current is *itself a function* of the output voltage. As $V_{out}$ falls (e.g., during a short), the current-limit threshold is automatically *reduced* (folded back) to a much smaller short-circuit current $I_{SC}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCECT303 – Module 4):**
> *“Series voltage regulator – short-circuit protection, fold-back protection, Safe Operating Area (SOA) considerations of the pass transistor.”*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – "The Smart Tap"

Imagine a high-pressure water tap connected to a thin garden hose:

- **No protection (bad):** If someone pinches the hose completely, the pump pushes the same huge flow against a dead end → pipe bursts.
- **Simple current limiting:** A mechanical *flow-stop* inside the tap limits flow to, say, 10 L/min no matter what. Useful, but the pump still pushes hard against a closed tap — it **sweats** (heats up) and eventually wears out.
- **Fold-back protection:** A *smart* valve that senses when the pressure downstream has dropped (meaning the tap is being closed). It then *automatically reduces* the allowed flow from 10 L/min down to 2 L/min. The pump now barely strains, the tap is cool, and the hose is safe.

The same physics applies to a regulator. During a true short ($V_{out}=0$), the pass transistor must drop nearly the *entire* input voltage $V_{in}$. If the current stayed at $I_{max}$, the dissipation would be $V_{in} \cdot I_{max}$ — almost always exceeding the **Safe Operating Area (SOA)**. Folding the current back to $I_{SC} \ll I_{max}$ keeps the dissipation inside the SOA envelope.

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics (Highlighted)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maximum load current (full output) | $I_{max}$ | **0.5 – 10** | A |
| Short-circuit current | $I_{SC}$ | **0.1 – 0.3 × $I_{max}$** | A |
| Base–emitter sense voltage | $V_{BE(on)}$ | **0.65 – 0.7** | V |
| Current-sense resistor | $R_{SC}$ | **0.1 – 1.0** | $\Omega$ |
| SOA-limited dissipation | $P_{D,max}$ | **25 – 150** | W |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in B.Tech examinations:** Questions in the 14-mark slot almost always test (a) the *circuit diagram* of the fold-back scheme, (b) the *derivation* of $I_{max}$ and $I_{SC}$, and (c) the *numerical proof* that dissipation under short-circuit is lower than the SOA rating.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building Block 1 — Simple (Constant) Current Limiting

**Circuit Description**

A small *current-sense resistor* $R_{SC}$ is inserted between the emitter of the series pass transistor $Q_P$ and the output node $V_{out}$. A *protection transistor* $Q_1$ has its **base** connected to the $V_{out}$ side of $R_{SC}$ and its **emitter** to the $Q_P$-emitter side of $R_{SC}$. The collector of $Q_1$ steals base-drive current from $Q_P$.

**Step-by-Step Operating Logic**

- During normal load: load current $I_L$ is small, the drop $V_{R_{SC}} = I_L R_{SC}$ is less than $V_{BE(on)} \approx 0.7\text{ V}$. $Q_1$ is OFF. The regulator behaves as an ordinary series regulator.
- When $I_L$ rises such that $V_{R_{SC}} \ge V_{BE(on)}$ → $Q_1$ turns ON.
- Collector current of $Q_1$ diverts base-drive from $Q_P$ → $Q_P$ is forced out of saturation / active region → $I_L$ is *clamped* at a constant value.
- This constant value is, by definition:
  $$I_{max} = \frac{V_{BE(on)}}{R_{SC}}$$

**Limitation of Simple Limiting**
Under a hard short, $V_{out} = 0$ but $I_L$ remains at $I_{max}$. The pass transistor still dissipates:
$$P_{D,\text{short}} = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L = V_{in} \cdot I_{max}$$
For $V_{in} = 25$ V and $I_{max} = 5$ A, this is **125 W** — almost always above the SOA.

## 2.2 Building Block 2 — Fold-Back Current Limiting (FBCL)

**Conceptual Difference**
FBCL adds a *resistive feedback network* ($R_1, R_2$) that samples $V_{out}$ and injects an extra voltage term into the sense condition of $Q_1$. As $V_{out}$ falls, the threshold required to keep $Q_1$ ON is reduced, so the regulator settles at a *lower* $I_L$.

**Operating Logic — Five Steps**

1. **Sense condition** for $Q_1$ conduction: voltage across $R_{SC}$ must exceed the sum of $Q_1$’s $V_{BE}$ and the voltage at the $R_1$–$R_2$ divider tap.
2. **Mathematically:**
   $$I_L \cdot R_{SC} = V_{BE(on)} + V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$
3. **Solve for $I_L$:**
   $$I_L = \frac{V_{BE(on)} + V_{out}\,\dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}}{R_{SC}}$$
4. **At nominal output** ($V_{out} = V_{reg}$): current limit = $I_{max}$.
5. **At short circuit** ($V_{out} = 0$): current limit collapses to $I_{SC} = \dfrac{V_{BE(on)}}{R_{SC}}$ — a *small fraction* of $I_{max}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The fold-back ratio $\dfrac{I_{SC}}{I_{max}}$ is set by the designer through $\dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$. A common design target is $I_{SC} \approx 0.25\,I_{max}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the *high-yield* formula bank. Memorising these is essential to score full marks in Part B numerical questions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Boundary Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Simple-limit current | $I_{max} = \dfrac{V_{BE(on)}}{R_{SC}}$ | Always | A |
| 2 | Short-circuit current (fold-back) | $I_{SC} = \dfrac{V_{BE(on)}}{R_{SC}}$ | $V_{out} = 0$ | A |
| 3 | Maximum load current (fold-back) | $I_{max} = \dfrac{V_{BE(on)} + V_{reg}\,\dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}}{R_{SC}}$ | $V_{out} = V_{reg}$ | A |
| 4 | Generic fold-back $I$-$V$ locus | $I_L = \dfrac{V_{BE} + V_{out}\,\beta}{R_{SC}}$ with $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$ | $0 \le V_{out} \le V_{reg}$ | A |
| 5 | Power dissipated (simple limit, short) | $P_{D,\text{short}} = V_{in} \cdot I_{max}$ | $V_{out} = 0$ | W |
| 6 | Power dissipated (fold-back, short) | $P_{D,\text{short}} = V_{in} \cdot I_{SC}$ | $V_{out} = 0$ | W |
| 7 | Fold-back ratio | $k_{fb} = \dfrac{I_{SC}}{I_{max}} = \dfrac{V_{BE}}{V_{BE} + V_{reg}\,\beta}$ | $0 < k_{fb} < 1$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Sense resistor (design) | $R_{SC} = \dfrac{V_{BE}}{I_{SC}}$ | Given $I_{SC}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 9 | Knee voltage of fold-back | $V_{knee} = -\,\dfrac{V_{BE}}{\beta}$ | Intersect with $V_{out}$-axis | V |
| 10 | SOA constraint (pass transistor) | $V_{CE} \cdot I_C \le P_{D,\text{rated}}$ at $V_{CE} = V_{in}$ | Continuous | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Real-World Utility: FBCL is the **de-facto industry standard** in 78xx/79xx fixed regulators (LM78xx, LM317), in low-dropout (LDO) controllers, in bench power supplies (Keysight E36xxx, Rigol DP800), and in automotive ECU voltage rails where sustained shorts during harness faults must not destroy the regulator.
