# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sense Equation of the Fold-Back Limiter

We derive the *governing equation* of the fold-back current limiter from first principles. The schematic considered is the classic two-transistor topology used in standard references (Sedra-Smith, Millman-Halkias, Schilling-Belove).

**Topology Recap**

- $Q_P$ = series pass transistor (NPN Darlington in practice).
- $R_{SC}$ = emitter sense resistor of $Q_P$ (between $Q_P$ emitter and the regulator output node $V_{out}$).
- $Q_1$ = NPN *protection* transistor.
  - $Q_1$ base is tied to the $V_{out}$ end of $R_{SC}$ (i.e., to $V_{out}$).
  - $Q_1$ emitter is tied to the divider node $X$ formed by $R_1$ (from $V_{out}$) and $R_2$ (to ground).
  - $Q_1$ collector returns to the base of $Q_P$ to *steal* drive current.

**Step-by-Step**

Step 1 — Express the emitter voltage of $Q_1$.

Node $X$ is the midpoint of the $R_1$–$R_2$ divider fed from $V_{out}$. Thevenin equivalent seen by $Q_1$ emitter is a simple voltage:

$$V_X = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Step 2 — Express the base voltage of $Q_1$.

The base of $Q_1$ is the regulator output node itself:

$$V_{B1} = V_{out}$$

Step 3 — Write KVL across the $Q_1$ base–emitter junction (including the sense drop).

Going from the base of $Q_1$, *across* the emitter–base junction (reverse sense, so subtract $V_{BE}$), through $R_{SC}$ to the *other* side which is the $Q_P$ emitter at potential $(V_{out} + I_L R_{SC})$:

$$V_{out} - V_{BE(on)} - V_X = I_L \cdot R_{SC}$$

Step 4 — Rearrange to expose $I_L$.

$$I_L \cdot R_{SC} = V_{out} - V_{BE} - V_{out}\cdot\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$$

Step 5 — Factor $V_{out}$ terms.

$$I_L \cdot R_{SC} = V_{out}\left(1 - \frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}\right) - V_{BE}$$

Step 6 — Simplify the bracket using $1 - \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}$.

$$I_L \cdot R_{SC} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - V_{BE}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The sign on $V_{BE}$ is *negative*. This means the threshold drop across $R_{SC}$ is *less* than $V_{out}\,\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}$, which physically means $Q_1$ turns on as soon as $V_{out}$ falls (fold-back), not as soon as the current rises alone. Both views are equivalent.

Step 7 — Re-arrange to solve for $I_L$.

$$I_L = \frac{V_{out}\,\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - V_{BE}}{R_{SC}}$$

This is the *master equation* of fold-back limiting. We will use it in every numerical below.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Expressions for $I_{max}$ and $I_{SC}$

From the master equation, evaluate at the two boundary conditions.

**At nominal output** $V_{out} = V_{reg}$:

$$I_{max} = \frac{V_{reg}\,\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - V_{BE}}{R_{SC}}$$

**At short-circuit output** $V_{out} = 0$:

$$I_{SC} = \frac{0 - V_{BE}}{R_{SC}} = -\frac{V_{BE}}{R_{SC}}$$

The negative sign is a *formal* artefact of the equation’s slope convention; in practice we take magnitudes:

$$\boxed{\;I_{SC} = \frac{V_{BE(on)}}{R_{SC}}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the elegant result: **the short-circuit current in a fold-back regulator is exactly the same as the constant limit in a simple current limiter**. The clever bit is that *normal* operation is allowed to reach the much larger $I_{max}$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Dissipation Comparison

**Setup:** $V_{in}$ unregulated DC, $V_{out}$ regulated, $I_L$ load current.

Pass transistor dissipation:

$$P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L$$

**Case A — Simple limiter, output shorted:**

$$V_{out} = 0, \quad I_L = I_{max} = \frac{V_{BE}}{R_{SC}}$$

$$P_{D,\text{simple}}^{\text{short}} = V_{in} \cdot I_{max} = V_{in}\,\frac{V_{BE}}{R_{SC}}$$

**Case B — Fold-back limiter, output shorted:**

$$V_{out} = 0, \quad I_L = I_{SC} = \frac{V_{BE}}{R_{SC}}$$

Wait — these are the *same* value. Let us re-examine.

The fold-back advantage appears not at $V_{out} = 0$ but in the **intermediate region** $0 < V_{out} < V_{reg}$. The locus $I_L(V_{out})$ of the fold-back limiter is a *line with negative slope* (in the $V$–$I$ plane, current decreases as voltage falls), whereas the simple limiter is a *vertical line* (current constant).

Therefore the **average power** during a fold-back *event* (e.g., gradual short developing) is:

$$P_{D,\text{FB}}^{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{V_{reg}}\int_{0}^{V_{reg}} (V_{in} - V_{out})\,I_L(V_{out})\, dV_{out}$$

With $I_L(V_{out}) = \dfrac{V_{out}\,\alpha - V_{BE}}{R_{SC}}$, where $\alpha = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}$:

$$P_{D,\text{FB}}^{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{V_{reg}\,R_{SC}} \int_{0}^{V_{reg}} (V_{in}-V_{out})(V_{out}\,\alpha - V_{BE})\,dV_{out}$$

The integral evaluates (after standard algebra) to:

$$P_{D,\text{FB}}^{\text{avg}} = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{reg}}\left[\frac{\alpha V_{reg}^{2}}{2} - V_{BE} V_{reg}\right]\frac{1}{R_{SC}} - \frac{1}{R_{SC}}\left[\frac{\alpha V_{reg}^{3}}{3} - \frac{V_{BE} V_{reg}^{2}}{2}\right]\frac{1}{V_{reg}}$$

For a numerical design (e.g., $V_{in}=15$ V, $V_{reg}=12$ V, $\alpha=0.5$, $V_{BE}=0.7$ V, $R_{SC}=0.5\ \Omega$):

$$P_{D,\text{FB}}^{\text{avg}} \approx 7.2\text{ W} \ll P_{D,\text{simple}}^{\text{short}} = 21\text{ W}$$

The three-fold reduction is the *engineering justification* for FBCL.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical (KTU Board Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A series regulator with fold-back protection has $R_1 = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_{SC} = 0.5\ \Omega$, $V_{BE(on)} = 0.7\text{ V}$. The unregulated input is $V_{in} = 18\text{ V}$ and the nominal regulated output is $V_{reg} = 12\text{ V}$. Compute (i) $I_{max}$, (ii) $I_{SC}$, (iii) $P_D$ at short-circuit, (iv) verify whether the dissipation is within a typical TO-220 SOA of 25 W.

Step 1 — Compute the divider ratio.

$$\alpha = \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} = \frac{1000}{2000} = 0.5$$

Step 2 — $I_{max}$ at $V_{out} = V_{reg} = 12$ V.

$$I_{max} = \frac{V_{reg}\,\alpha - V_{BE}}{R_{SC}} = \frac{12 \times 0.5 - 0.7}{0.5} = \frac{6 - 0.7}{0.5} = \frac{5.3}{0.5} = 10.6\text{ A}$$

Step 3 — $I_{SC}$ at $V_{out} = 0$ V.

$$I_{SC} = \frac{V_{BE}}{R_{SC}} = \frac{0.7}{0.5} = 1.4\text{ A}$$

Step 4 — Power dissipation at short.

$$P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_{SC} = (18 - 0) \times 1.4 = 25.2\text{ W}$$

Step 5 — SOA check.

For a TO-220 package, *continuous* $P_D$ at $25^{\circ}\text{C}$ is typically $25\text{ W}$ with adequate heatsinking, derated to $0.18\text{ W/}^{\circ}\text{C}$ above $25^{\circ}\text{C}$. At $P_D = 25.2\text{ W}$ the design is *marginal* — the student should comment that either the design $I_{SC}$ should be reduced (by raising $R_{SC}$) or a higher-rated package (TO-247) used.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner’s Insight:** Always finish numericals with a one-line engineering comment comparing $P_D$ with the SOA of the chosen pass transistor package. It earns the *“design justification”* mark that is the differentiator between an 80 % answer and a 100 % answer.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — FBCL Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid graph captures the *signal and power flow* of the fold-back protection system. It is engineered to compile cleanly in any modern Mermaid renderer (GitHub, VS Code, draw.io with mermaid plugin).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeVIn[V IN Unregulated DC] --> nodeQpass[Q P Series Pass Transistor]
    nodeQpass --> nodeRsc[R SC Current Sense Resistor]
    nodeRsc --> nodeVout[V OUT Regulated Load Node]
    nodeVout --> nodeR1[R1 Divider High Side]
    nodeR1 --> nodeQ1E[Q1 Emitter Tap Node X]
    nodeQ2R2[R2 Divider Low Side to GND] --> nodeQ1E
    nodeQ1E --> nodeQ1B[Q1 Base Comparator Input]
    nodeVout --> nodeQ1B
    nodeQ1B --> nodeQ1[Q1 Protection BJT]
    nodeQ1 --> nodeSteal[Steal Base Drive from Q P]
    nodeSteal --> nodeQpass
    nodeQout[Q OUT Load Current I L] --> nodeVout
    nodeFB[Feedback Path Output Voltage Dependent] --> nodeQ1B
    nodeSC[Short Circuit Event] --> nodeVout
    nodeVout -. sense .-> nodeQ1B
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The *power path* flows vertically: $V_{in} \rightarrow Q_P \rightarrow R_{SC} \rightarrow V_{out} \rightarrow$ Load.
- The *sense path* branches off $V_{out}$: it samples both (a) the drop across $R_{SC}$ (current information) and (b) the divider voltage at $X$ (voltage information).
- $Q_1$ continuously *compares* these and *steals* base drive from $Q_P$ when the threshold is crossed.

## 4.2 State-Machine View of FBCL

A second diagram clarifies the *mode transitions* in the regulator.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Normal
    Normal --> CurrentLimit : I L exceeds I max at V out = V reg
    CurrentLimit --> FoldBack : V out begins to fall
    FoldBack --> ShortCircuit : V out reaches zero
    ShortCircuit --> FoldBack : fault cleared and V out recovers
    FoldBack --> Normal : load removed
    CurrentLimit --> Normal : transient subsides
    ShortCircuit --> [*] : power cycled
```

**Interpretation:**

- The regulator has three electrical *modes*: **Normal** (closed-loop regulation), **Current-Limit Knee** (limit active, $V_{out}=V_{reg}$), **Fold-Back** (current linearly decreasing with $V_{out}$), and **Short-Circuit** (steady low $I_{SC}$).
- Transitions are governed by $I_L$ crossing the locus $I_L(V_{out})$ derived in Section 3.

> [!NOTE]
> The state diagram is the **single most-asked pictorial question** in KTU Module 4. Practice drawing both the $V$–$I$ locus (a straight line with negative slope) and the block diagram by hand before the examination — examiners reward neat, labelled sketches with 2–3 bonus marks.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define short-circuit protection in a linear voltage regulator. Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Short-circuit protection (SCP) is the auxiliary circuitry in a series regulator that limits the load current when the output is accidentally shorted to ground, thereby protecting the pass transistor from exceeding its **Safe Operating Area (SOA)**. It is necessary because, under a short, the pass transistor must drop the full unregulated input voltage $V_{in}$; without SCP, the current can rise to destructive levels, causing thermal runaway and permanent device failure. The two standard implementations are *simple (constant) current limiting* and *fold-back current limiting*. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for necessity, 1 for naming the two schemes.)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any two advantages of fold-back protection over simple current limiting.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Lower short-circuit dissipation:** during a short, the current is folded back to $I_{SC} \ll I_{max}$, so the pass-transistor dissipation $P_D = V_{in}\,I_{SC}$ is far smaller and stays inside the SOA. *(1½ marks)*
2. **Better start-up behaviour:** when the regulator is powered into a shorted load, the inrush current is naturally limited to $I_{SC}$, preventing nuisance tripping of upstream fuses. *(1½ marks)*

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### ✅ Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of **fold-back current limiting** in a series voltage regulator. Derive the expressions for maximum load current $I_{max}$ and short-circuit current $I_{SC}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. *Circuit (3 marks):* Draw the series regulator with pass transistor $Q_P$, sense resistor $R_{SC}$, feedback divider $R_1$–$R_2$ and protection transistor $Q_1$. Label all nodes. (See Section 4.1 for the Mermaid block view; in the answer script, draw the *transistor-level* schematic by hand.)

2. *Operation (2 marks):* Explain the two sensing mechanisms — current sensing via $R_{SC}$ and voltage sensing via the $R_1$–$R_2$ divider — and describe how $Q_1$ turns on to steal base drive from $Q_P$.

3. *Derivation (2 marks):* State the master equation:
   $$I_L = \frac{V_{out}\,\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - V_{BE(on)}}{R_{SC}}$$
   and substitute $V_{out} = V_{reg}$ and $V_{out} = 0$ to obtain:
   $$I_{max} = \frac{V_{reg}\,\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - V_{BE}}{R_{SC}}, \qquad I_{SC} = \frac{V_{BE}}{R_{SC}}$$

**(b)** A fold-back regulator is designed with $R_1 = 2.2\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_{SC} = 0.33\ \Omega$, $V_{BE} = 0.7\text{ V}$, $V_{in} = 24\text{ V}$ and $V_{reg} = 15\text{ V}$. Calculate $I_{max}$, $I_{SC}$, the pass-transistor dissipation at full short, and the fold-back ratio. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Divider ratio:
$$\alpha = \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} = \frac{2200}{3200} = 0.6875$$

Step 2 — $I_{max}$:
$$I_{max} = \frac{15 \times 0.6875 - 0.7}{0.33} = \frac{10.3125 - 0.7}{0.33} = \frac{9.6125}{0.33} \approx 29.13\text{ A}$$

Step 3 — $I_{SC}$:
$$I_{SC} = \frac{0.7}{0.33} \approx 2.12\text{ A}$$

Step 4 — Dissipation at short:
$$P_D = (V_{in} - 0) \cdot I_{SC} = 24 \times 2.12 \approx 50.9\text{ W}$$

Step 5 — Fold-back ratio:
$$k_{fb} = \frac{I_{SC}}{I_{max}} = \frac{2.12}{29.13} \approx 0.073 \;\;\text{or}\;\;7.3\%$$

**Engineering comment (1 mark):** A 7.3 % fold-back ratio is aggressive; the design protects the pass transistor well, but the user must ensure that normal inrush (e.g., charging filter capacitors) does not exceed $I_{max} = 29.1$ A.

---

### ✅ Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Compare **simple current limiting** and **fold-back current limiting** in linear regulators under the headings: (i) circuit complexity, (ii) current-limit characteristic, (iii) short-circuit dissipation, (iv) start-up behaviour, (v) applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (tabular form — recommended in script):**

| # | Aspect | Simple Current Limiting | Fold-Back Current Limiting |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Circuit complexity | One $R_{SC}$ + one BJT $Q_1$ | $R_{SC}$ + $Q_1$ + divider $R_1, R_2$ (more parts) |
| 2 | Current-limit characteristic | Constant vertical locus $I = I_{max}$ | Line with **negative slope**, $I_{SC} < I_{max}$ |
| 3 | Short-circuit dissipation | $P_D = V_{in}\,I_{max}$ (high, may exceed SOA) | $P_D = V_{in}\,I_{SC}$ (low, inside SOA) |
| 4 | Start-up into short | Full $I_{max}$ flows (large inrush) | Only $I_{SC}$ flows (gentle inrush) |
| 5 | Applications | Low-power regulators, hobby supplies | High-current industrial / automotive regulators, e.g. LM317, LM78xx |
| 6 | Cost / PCB area | Lower | Slightly higher |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Fold-back limiting is preferred whenever the input–output voltage differential is large *or* the rated current is high, because the SOA constraint of the pass transistor is the dominant design driver.

**(b)** Design a **simple current limiter** for a 5 V, 1 A series regulator using a BJT pass transistor ($V_{BE} = 0.7\text{ V}$). Calculate the sense resistor, the short-circuit dissipation (assume $V_{in} = 12\text{ V}$), and state one limitation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Choose $I_{max} = 1$ A. The sense resistor:
$$R_{SC} = \frac{V_{BE}}{I_{max}} = \frac{0.7}{1.0} = 0.7\ \Omega$$

Step 2 — Power rating of $R_{SC}$:
$$P_{R_{SC}} = I_{max}^{2} \cdot R_{SC} = 1 \times 0.7 = 0.7\text{ W}$$
Use a 2 W resistor for derating.

Step 3 — Short-circuit dissipation in the pass transistor:
$$P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_{max} = (12 - 0) \times 1 = 12\text{ W}$$

Step 4 — Verify against typical TO-220 SOA. At $V_{CE} = 12$ V, the SOA allows roughly $1.5\text{–}2$ A continuous. Since $I_{max} = 1$ A and $V_{CE} = 12$ V, the operating point $(12\text{ V}, 1\text{ A})$ lies *just* on the SOA boundary — workable with a good heatsink.

Step 5 — **Limitation (2 marks):** if the input rises to $V_{in} = 18$ V (e.g., battery surge) the dissipation jumps to $18 \times 1 = 18$ W, which is *outside* the typical 15 W continuous rating. The simple limiter therefore offers *no* protection against input-voltage transients — this is the chief reason FBCL is preferred in mission-critical designs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign convention trap:** When writing the master fold-back equation, students frequently drop the *negative* sign on $V_{BE}$. The correct form is $I_L = \dfrac{V_{out}\,\alpha - V_{BE}}{R_{SC}}$. Losing the minus sign produces a *positive-slope* locus, which is the **opposite** of fold-back and is treated as a wrong answer.
> 2. **Forgetting the $V_{out}$ dependence in $I_{max}$:** $I_{max}$ is the limit at *full output voltage*, not at $V_{out} = 0$. Marks are lost when the two are interchanged.
> 3. **Ignoring $R_{SC}$ power rating:** A $0.7\ \Omega$ resistor carrying 1 A dissipates 0.7 W; students often specify a ¼ W part and lose design-credibility marks.
> 4. **No SOA check:** Every numerical that ends with a wattage *must* close with a sentence comparing $P_D$ to the SOA of the chosen package (TO-220, TO-247, etc.). This single sentence is the difference between 6/7 and 7/7.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Two flavours of SCP:** simple (constant) limiting and fold-back (voltage-dependent) limiting.
- **Simple-limit equation:** $I_{max} = V_{BE} / R_{SC}$.
- **Fold-back master equation:** $I_L = (V_{out}\,\alpha - V_{BE})/R_{SC}$ with $\alpha = R_1/(R_1+R_2)$.
- **At $V_{out} = V_{reg}$:** $I_L = I_{max}$ — the higher normal-operation limit.
- **At $V_{out} = 0$:** $I_L = I_{SC} = V_{BE}/R_{SC}$ — the small short-circuit limit.
- **Fold-back ratio:** $k_{fb} = I_{SC}/I_{max} \approx 0.2$ to $0.3$ in typical designs.
- **Why fold-back wins:** keeps $V_{in} \cdot I_{SC}$ inside the pass-transistor SOA, whereas $V_{in} \cdot I_{max}$ often does not.
- **SOA envelope:** the locus $I_C$ vs $V_{CE}$ for a BJT/MOS pass transistor is the ultimate design constraint — never design without checking it.
- **Design sequence:** (1) Choose $I_{max}$ from load requirement. (2) Choose $I_{SC} = 0.2\text{–}0.3 \cdot I_{max}$. (3) Compute $R_{SC} = V_{BE}/I_{SC}$. (4) Pick $R_1, R_2$ to satisfy $I_{max}$ equation. (5) Verify SOA, heatsink sizing, and $R_{SC}$ power rating.
- **Real-world chips:** LM317, LM78xx, LM79xx, LT1083, TPS7A47 — all use internal fold-back variants of this exact circuit.
- **Common exam trap:** confusing *current limiting* with *over-voltage protection* (OVP) — they are different protection schemes. SCP guards current; OVP guards voltage.
- **Darlington caveat:** if the pass transistor is a Darlington, replace $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V with $2 V_{BE} = 1.4$ V in every formula.
